CALL
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Call command to communicate online with other
users. Any user can start a call session.
You can also display a directory of users and a log of the call
session. See Call Directives.
When you send a message, a beeper sounds at the station
you are calling. When the party answers the call (by
pressing Message Wait), the system opens a line between
you and the other party and sends the message HELLO. The
Call command has priority over all other queued messages.
If there is no response within 30 seconds, you get the
message: NO ANSWER. The Call command is then ended
and the other party does not receive the message you sent.

Call Control Line Format
CALL [user-idsn|sn]
where user-id and sn are the user-id, station number, or
both, of a user who is currently signed on to the system.

Call Control Line Format Details
The following message prompt is displayed:
Call% Dir% Exit% Log% Abrt% Call Request
Enter USER-ID and/or station %

Call Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the CALL command:
To ensure that the message you send remains intact
when received by the user you are calling, press the
Erase to EOD key before sending your message to
clear any previously displayed ﬁeld control characters
(FCC).
When typing your message, avoid typing in the
rightmost column; the software uses this column to
deﬁne screen attributes.
Depending on what you want to do, you can tab to any
of the ﬁve control positions on the ﬁrst line and press
Transmit, or you can press any of the corresponding
function keys for the action.

Call Directives
Directive Description

Call

Redisplay the Call input
screen. Enter the user-id or
station number of the user
you are sending a message
to. You can enter as few as
three characters to
abbreviate the user-id.

Dir

Display a directory input
screen. Enter a user-id (or as
little as one character of a
user-id) to display the user-id
searched for, station number
that the user is signed on to,
organization number,
department name, and time
of their last input.

Direct

Exit

Terminate the Call command.

Log

Log all information and
display it after the call
session. With this option, you
receive a result list of the
complete conversation

beginning when the log was
ﬁrst enabled until you exit.
The log contains the time of
the call, user-id, station
number, department,
description of the process,
and actual messages sent.

Abrt

Abort the Call command. If
you decide not to make the
call, before or after the
person to call is speciﬁed, tab
to Abrt and press Transmit.

Lock

Lock the call until a response
is sent back to the sender.

Ü Ü 2200

@CALL (Call Subroutine)
Use the Call Subroutine (CALL) statement to save the
contents of all currently deﬁned variables and pass control
to an internal or external subroutine.
You can pass speciﬁc variables and manipulate them within
the subroutine and pass them back to the calling run
without aﬀecting any of the other currently deﬁned
variables. You can also specify that the passed variables
should not be changed by the system on returning to the
calling run. You can pass up to 80 variables to the
subroutine.
In addition to passing variables, a run also passes the
current -0 to the subroutine. Note that -0 can be either a
result or a permanent report (by deﬁnition, -0 is the most
recently referenced report). The subroutine returns its
current -0 (either result or permanent report) to the calling
run.
To retain the contents of -0 in the calling run, perform any of
the following commands:
@RSL, if -0 is a permanent report.
@RNM followed by @RSL, if -0 is a result prior to the
@CALL statement.
Also you can use @RNM and @RSL alternatively based on
the application requirement.

Business Information Server Subroutine CALL
Statement Format
Calls a Business Information Server subroutine.
@CALL[,c,d,r] lab ([p,...,p]) .Unregistered
subroutine call
@CALL,"name" lab ([p,...,p]) .Registered
subroutine call

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the external
subroutine.

"name"

Name of the registered
subroutine. The subroutine
name must be enclosed in
double quotes.

lab

Label where the subroutine
begins.

Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ

p,...,p

Parameters to pass to the
subroutine. Parentheses are
required (even if you do not

pass any parameters). See
Passing Variables and
Renames Using Call
Subroutine.
Maximum number of
parameters = 80.
Enter one of the following
kinds of parameters:
v-variable (system returns
the new value from the
subroutine).
*v-variable (system retains
the original value from the
calling run).
'string'-literal character
string, up to the maximum
size of a string variable
(MAXRW$ reserved word).
Enclose each string in
apostrophes ('). To pass an
apostrophe, use two
apostrophes ('').
The following are
characteristics of literal
character strings:
At the label where the
subroutine begins, enter
variables to receive the
literal character strings.

The system initializes
these variables as String
(S-type) variables of the
same size as the strings.
If the literal character
string size is less than 18
characters, then it is an H
type variable.
If you pass a String (S-type)
variable in a CALL statement,
you cannot modify its deﬁned
size.

Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
2200 Þ Þ

p,...,p

Parameters to pass to the
subroutine. Parentheses are
required (even if you do not
pass any parameters). See
Passing Variables and
Renames Using Call
Subroutine.

Maximum number of
parameters = 80.
Enter one of the following
kinds of parameters:
variable— passed-byreference. (subroutine
returns the new value to
the caller’s variable).
*variable — passed-byvalue. (caller’s variable is
not modiﬁed).
'string' — literal character
string up to the maximum
size of a string variable
(MAXRW$ reserved word).
Enclose each string in
apostrophes ('). To pass an
apostrophe, use two
apostrophes ('').
RNM(num) — Send and
receive a rename report
where num is a rename
number from -1 to MAXRNM$. The report
identiﬁer is sent to the
subroutine. The current
report identiﬁer from the
subroutine is placed back
into the caller’s rename
item.

RNMSND(num) — Sendonly a rename report
where num is a rename
number from -1 to MAXRNM$ or 0 to specify a
void report. The caller’s
rename item is not
modiﬁed when the
subroutine returns.
RNMRCV(num) — Receiveonly a rename report
where num is a rename
number from -1 to MAXRNM$. The caller
sends a void report
identiﬁer to the
subroutine. The current
report identiﬁer from the
subroutine is placed back
into the caller’s rename
item.
At the label where the
subroutine begins, enter
variables to receive the
variable and literal data and
enter RNM(num) to receive the
renames. These parameters
must be enclosed in
parentheses.
Variables returned to the caller
will receive the same variable
deﬁnition and data of the
subroutine variable. One

exception is when you pass an
array member. The caller's
array member always remains
the same type and the size
can be adjusted downward but
never upward.
The following are
characteristics of literal
character strings:
At the label where the
subroutine begins, enter
variables to receive the
literal character strings.
The system initializes
these variables as String
(S-type) variables of the
same size as the strings.
A void rename is a rename
report that does not point to
any report. Void renames can
be passed to subroutines and
returned from subroutines.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200 Þ Þ

JavaScript Subroutine CALL Statement Format
Calls a JavaScript subroutine.
@CALL,c,d,r [function] ([p,...,p]) .Unregistered
subroutine call
@CALL,"name" [function] ([p,...,p]) .Registered
subroutine call

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the external
subroutine.

name

Name of the registered
subroutine. The subroutine
name must be enclosed in
double quotes.

function

Name of the entry point
function to be executed. The
function name can be up to
50 characters long. This ﬁeld
is case sensitive and optional.
If no function is speciﬁed for
an unregistered subroutine,
no entry point function is
called.

If no function is speciﬁed for a
registered subroutine, a
function with the same name
(all uppercase) as the
registered subroutine name
will be executed, if it exists.
Otherwise, no entry point
function is called.

Note: If parameters are
speciﬁed, an entry point
function is required.

Ü Ü Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200
Windows Server Linux Windows Client Þ Þ

p,...,p

Parameters to pass to the
subroutine. Parentheses are
required (even if you do not
pass any parameters).
Maximum number of
parameters = 80.
Enter one of the following
kinds of parameters:
v-variable (system returns
the new value from the
subroutine).
*v-variable (system retains
the original value for the

calling run).
'string'-literal character
string, up to the maximum
size of a string variable.
Enclose each string in
apostrophes ('). To pass an
apostrophe, use two
apostrophes ('').
At the entry point in the
subroutine, enter the
arguments to receive the
parameters being passed, for
example, (arg1, arg2, &arg3).
The ampersand indicates that
this value is returned.
All variable parameters are
handled as strings.
If you pass any variable in a
CALL statement, you cannot
modify its deﬁned size.
You cannot redeﬁne any
passed variable as a String (Stype) variable. However, you
can redeﬁne the passed Htype variable as a String (Stype) up to size of 18.
If a string variable is passed by
reference, the value returned
by the subroutine can only be
modiﬁed up to the originallydeﬁned size.

If a non-string variable is
passed by reference, the value
will be redeﬁned up to the
maximum size for that
variable type.

Ü Ü Windows Server Linux Windows Client
2200 Þ Þ

p,...,p

Parameters to pass to the
subroutine. Parentheses are
required (even if you do not
pass any parameters).
Maximum number of
parameters = 80.
Enter one of the following
kinds of parameters:
variable — passed-byreference. (subroutine
returns the new value to
the caller’s variable)
*variable — passed-byvalue. (caller’s variable is
not modiﬁed)
'string' — literal character
string up to the maximum
size of a string variable.
Enclose each string in

apostrophes ('). To pass an
apostrophe, use two
apostrophes ('').
At the entry point in the
subroutine, enter the
arguments to receive the
parameters being passed, for
example, (arg1, arg2, &arg3).
The ampersand indicates that
this value is returned.
All variable parameters are
handled as strings. JavaScript
subroutines consider spaces
as signiﬁcant characters, so
you may want to pack your
parameter variables before
passing them.
If the return variable size is
smaller than the original
variable size, the return data is
placed in the original variable
and padded with spaces.
If the return variable size is
larger than the original
variable size, the original
variable is expanded to the
necessary size. If this size
exceeds the maximum size for
the variable type, then it is
converted to a String (S-type)
variable and the return data is
placed in this new variable.

Array members do not change
type or size.
If the return variable size
exceeds MAXCHR$, the
original variable is converted
to a String (S-type) variable of
length MAXCHR$ and the
return data is placed in this
new variable.

Ü Ü 2200

Call Subroutine Guidelines
Follow these general guidelines when using the Call
Subroutine statement:
For information speciﬁcally regarding variables and
renames, see Passing Variables and Renames Using Call
Subroutine.
Use the Return Call Routine (RETURN) statement to exit
the subroutine and return to the line following the CALL
statement.
You can pass only one -0 result to the subroutine. If you
need to use more than one result from the calling run,
use the Yank Line (LNY) and Put Line (LNP) statements
to pass a result as a buﬀer or use RNM(-n), RNMSND(-n)
or RNMRCV(-n).
Do not place other statements on the same line after a
CALL statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
Error routines registered in called routines override
previously registered error routines; however, when
control returns to the calling run, the system
automatically reregisters the original error routine.
Use CALL$ reserved word to obtain the number of CALL
levels remaining.
You can nest up to 40 CALL subroutine levels (internal or
external).

A registered subroutine has the same characteristics as
an unregistered subroutine, except that it has its own
set of security settings. The security of the registered
subroutine replaces the security of the calling
application. The caller's security settings are restored
when you return from the registered subroutine. This
capability allows subroutines to expose controlled
interfaces to sensitive data without requiring the caller
to have direct access rights.
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
Include error routines in each called routine; otherwise,
in case of an error, control goes to the error routine in
the calling run.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
2200 Þ Þ
If the run fails, the system cancels all active subroutines
unless overridden by the C-option on the RER
statement.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200 Þ Þ

Call Registered Business Information Server
Subroutine Guidelines
Follow these additional guidelines when using the Call
Registered Business Information Server Subroutine
statement:
The registered subroutine will continue using the I/O and
the logic lines processed (LLP) limits of the calling script.
2200 Þ Þ
A foreground script can only call registered foreground
subroutines. A background script can only call registered
background subroutines. Contact your administrator for
naming and registering scripts and subroutines.
The Business Information Server RER error handling
routine (2F0) has been enhanced to handle large error
messages that are returned from JavaScript subroutines.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Server Linux Windows Client Þ Þ
You can issue only one CALL Registered Subroutine (no
nesting).
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux Windows Client

Call JavaScript Subroutine Guidelines
Follow these additional guidelines when using the Call
JavaScript Subroutine statement:
A JavaScript subroutine uses the ‘ReturnDataset’ global
function to stage the desired dataset for the return to
the calling run. The returned dataset can be referenced
as -0 upon return. If ReturnDataset is not called, or the
subroutine terminates with an uncaught exception, no
-0 will exist.
You can issue only one CALL Registered Subroutine (no
nesting).
I/O limits are counted, but not checked when executing
a CALL to a JavaScript subroutine.
LLP limits are not counted when executing a CALL to a
JavaScript subroutine.
If an error occurs in a JavaScript subroutine:
JavaScript Syntax Errors:
If a JavaScript subroutine contains a syntax error (for
example, a missing semi-colon), the Business
Information Server script will err on the CALL
statement. In this case, a Business Information
Server RER routine must be registered in the calling
script to handle the error.
@.
@. <<< Calling a JavaScript function
requires a >>>

@. <<< registered RER routine to handle
syntax >>>
@. <<< errors detected in a JavaScript
subroutine. >>>
@.
@IF SYSNAM$(1-1) eq 'M' RER,0,F,2 0001 ; .
2200 RER
@IF SYSNAM$(1-1) ne 'M' RER,0,E,2 0001 ; .
non-2200 RER
@.
@CALL,"JVS_Script" JVS_Function() .
@.
JavaScript Errors (not handled in a JavaScript
subroutine):
If a JavaScript subroutine does not contain exception
handling (throw, try, catch, and ﬁnally statements)
to properly handle error recovery, the Business
Information Server script will err on the CALL
statement when exceptions occur. In this case, a
Business Information Server RER routine must be
registered in the calling script to handle the error.
@.
@. <<< Calling a JavaScript function that
does not >>>
@. <<< contain exception handling
statements to >>>
@. <<< handle error conditions will require
a >>>
@. <<< registered BIS RER routine to handle
errors. >>>
@.
@IF SYSNAM$(1-1) eq 'M' RER,0,F,2 0001 ; .
2200 RER

@IF SYSNAM$(1-1) ne 'M' RER,0,E,2 0001 ; .
non-2200 RER
@.
@CALL,"JVS_Script" JVS_Function() .
@.
JavaScript Errors (handled in a JavaScript
subroutine):
If a JavaScript subroutine contains exception
handling (throw, try, catch, and ﬁnally statements)
to properly handle error recovery, then the Business
Information Server script will not be notiﬁed when
exceptions occur. In this case, if the Business
Information Server script needs to know that an
exception occurred, the JavaScript subroutine must
pass the error message back to the calling Business
Information Server script in one of the calling
statement parameters. Then, the returned error
message can be displayed using a registered library
routine.
@.
@. <<< Calling a JavaScript function that
contains >>>
@. <<< exception handling statements to
handle >>>
@. <<< error conditions may require an
additional >>>
@. <<< parameter to pass the error message
back to >>>
@. <<< the calling script. >>>
@.
@CALL,"JVS_Script"
JVS_Function(<jvsmsg>sMAXCHR$) .
@.

@. <<< The returned error message can be
displayed >>>
@. <<< by calling a registered BIS Library
Routine. >>>
@.
@CALL,"LIB$JVSMSG" 0001* (<jvsmsg>,'1',
<sta>i3) .
@IF <sta> ne 0,(ErrLab) ; .
@.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200

Passing Variables and Renames Using Call
Subroutine
Follow these general guidelines regarding variables:
After the label where the subroutine starts, place
parentheses around the variables and renames that
correspond to the variables and renames that you are
passing with the CALL statement. Use the following
format:
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
@lab:(variable,variable,...)
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
2200 Þ Þ
@lab:(variable,rnm(-n),variable,...)
Ü Ü 2200
Variables and renames that you pass with the CALL
statement have a one-to-one relationship with the
variables and renames speciﬁed at the label. The
software equates these variables and renames by their
position in the statement, not by their name. That is, the
ﬁrst variable or rename in parentheses is equated with
the ﬁrst variable or rename of the CALL statement, and
so on.
Receiving variables in the called routine cannot be
single members of an array.

Any changes you make to variables you passed to the
subroutine using the CALL statement still exist when
control returns. Variables you did not pass to the
subroutine remain unchanged when control returns to
the calling run.
If the subroutine modiﬁes or clears the value of a
receiving variable in the parentheses, the system
passes the new value to the equivalent variable in the
CALL statement when control returns to the main
routine, unless you precede the variable with an asterisk
(*) in the CALL statement.
2200 Þ Þ
Renames are received in the subroutine by a
corresponding rnm(-n) parameter. The rename pointer
will be returned to the CALL statement unless passed
through the rnmsnd(-n) parameter.
Using a CLV statement in the subroutine clears the
deﬁnition and content of the variable in both the
subroutine and the calling run.
If no variables are passed to the subroutine and no
parentheses are included at the label, no error is
returned.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
Using a Clear Variables (CLV) statement in the
subroutine clears the content of the variable for the
remainder of the subroutine, but passes the latest
manipulated value of the variable to the calling run.

Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
Each subroutine can use the conﬁgured limit of variable
resources; however, when control returns to the calling
run, the system returns only the variables speciﬁed on
the CALL statement. The system releases all variables
initialized within the subroutine.
To pass all the variables in an array to a called
subroutine, specify only the name of the array in the
Load Variable Array (LDA) statement. An entire variable
array counts as one variable of the total number
allowed; to pass a speciﬁc member of the array, give its
name and number. For more information, see @LDA
(Load Variable Array).
When executing a CALL statement within a Network Run
(NRN) statement, you can pass only literal data. The
CALL statement does not pass variables. For information
on passing variables, see @NRN (Network Run).
Windows Server Windows Client 2200 Þ Þ

Passing Variables To a Registered Business
Information Server Subroutine
Follow this additional guideline regarding variables:
When formulating the CALL statement within an NRN
statement, load an ‘H1’ variable with the double quotes
and use that variable in the statement.

Passing Variables To a JavaScript Subroutine
Follow these additional guidelines regarding variables:
The variables are dynamically processed and can be
used or referenced at any time during the execution. If
an input argument should be returned to the calling
Business Information Server run, the argument must be
preceded by an ampersand which indicates that this
argument value should be returned.
If the JavaScript script modiﬁes the value of a receiving
argument in the parentheses, the system passes the
new value to the equivalent variable in the CALL
statement when control returns to the main routine,
unless you precede the variable with an asterisk (*) in
the CALL statement or do not use an ampersand for the
JavaScript argument.
Only individual members of an array can be passed. If
the member number is not speciﬁed, the ﬁrst member
of the array is assumed.
2200 Þ Þ
If the return variable length exceeds the current size of
the array member, the return data will be truncated. If it
is smaller, the size of the member will be adjusted
downward accordingly.
Ü Ü 2200
Ü Ü Windows Server Windows Client 2200

Passing Variables Example
This example transfers control to label 054 and passes only
v102 and v103 to the subroutine. The subroutine uses the
values of v102 and v103 for <drw> and <line>,
respectively.
@ldv v101i4=1234,v102h1=b,v103i4=5 .
@call 054 (v102,v103) .
.
. (other processing)
.
@054:(<drw>,<line>) .
@fnd,0,<drw>,,<line> a 'cust' |,amco <rpt>i4,
<line> .
@return .
In the example, when control returns, the variables used in
the main routine and subroutine are aﬀected as follows:
V101 still contains 1234 because it was not passed to
the subroutine.
V102 (<drw> in the subroutine) still contains the letter
b because the subroutine did not change it.
V103 (<line> in the subroutine) contains a new value
because the Find (FND) statement in the subroutine
changed it.
<rpt> is not deﬁned any longer because it was used
only in the subroutine.

Passing and Referring to Variables Example
This example transfers control to label 99 and passes v102,
v103, and v104. The subroutine uses the values of v102,
v103, and v104 for <scan>, <item>, and <qty>,
respectively.
@call 099 (v102,v103,v104) .
.
. (other processing)
.
@099:(<scan>,<item>,<qty>)
.
. (other processing using <scan>, <item>, and
<qty>) .
.
@srh,0,b dha 'product type' |,<item> <qty>,<scan>
.
.
. (other processing)
.
@return .
In this example, the system returns v102, v103, v104, and
the -0 result created in the subroutine. All other variables
remain unchanged, since you did not pass them to the
subroutine.

Passing Renames Example
This example transfers control to label 99 and passes
variable <status> and rename reports -2 and -3. It also
passes the rename placeholder -4 to receive a report back
from the subroutine. The subroutine uses the values of
<sts>, -12, -13 and -14 for <status>, -2, -3 and -4,
respectively.
@srh,0,b,2 d ‘CustCode’ TAB$,’AMCO’ rnm -2 .
@srh,0,b,3 d ‘CustCode’ TAB$,’FEDS’ rnm -3 .
@call 099
(<status>i6,rnmsnd(-2),rnm(-3),rnmrcv(-4)) .
.
. (other processing)
.
@099:(<sts>, rnm(-12),rnm(-13),rnm(-14))
.
. (other processing using -12, -13) .
.
@add,-12,-13 rnm -12 .
@sor,-12 ‘’ ‘StCd’,’ProductType’ TAB$,1,2 rnm -14
.
@tot,-13 ‘’ ‘SpcCod’ TAB$,’=NEW’ rnm -13 .
.
. (other processing)
.
@ldv <sts>=0 return .
In this example, the subroutine changes the contents
reports -12, -13 and -14. When the RETURN is performed,
the subroutine will pass back the value of <sts> to
<status>, -13 to -3 and -14 to -4. Report -2 will remain
unchanged in the calling routine.

Calling an External Subroutine Example
This example transfers control to an external subroutine in
report 15E0 beginning at label 123:
@call,0,e,15 123 () .
Since the statement does not pass variables, all variables in
the calling run are unchanged when control returns to the
calling run.

Retaining Current Values on Nonreturnable
Parameters Example
This example transfers control to label 100 and passes v101
and v102. The asterisk (*) before v102 speciﬁes that its
current value be retained on returning to the calling run,
even if it is changed in the subroutine. The subroutine
changes both variables.
@call 100 (v101,*v102)
.
. (other processing)
.
@100:(<data1>,<data2>) .
@ldv <data1>=5000,<data2>=2500 .
@return .
The system returns 5000 in v101, but does not return the
new value for v102 because it was preceded with an
asterisk when passed with the CALL statement.

Nonreturnable Parameters Example
This example transfers control to label 100 and passes v101
and strings hokey and pokey. The subroutine automatically
initializes <data2> and <data3> as S type variables of the
same size as the strings.
@call 100 (v101,'hokey','pokey')
.
. (other processing)
.
@100:(<data1>,<data2>,<data3>) .
The system returns the current value of v101, but does not
return any variable comparable to <data2> or <data3>.

Calling a Registered Business Information
Server Subroutine Example
This example calls a registered Business Information Server
script named "BIS_Script" and passes it two parameters.
.BIS Script
*=======================================
@CALL,"BIS_Script" 0001 (*<p1>,*<p2>) .
.
. (continue)
.
.External Registered BIS Script
*=======================================
@0001 (<d1>,<d2>) .
.
. (subroutine processing)
.
@RETURN .

Calling a Registered JavaScript Subroutine
Example
This example calls a registered JavaScript subroutine named
"JVS_Script" that contains a function called "JVS_Function".
.Registered Business Information Server Script
*================================================
============
@CALL,"JVS_Script" JVS_Function(<msg>s80) .
@MBX,ok,i,'Message Returned from JavaScript'
<msg> o,(lin1) .
.Registered JavaScript Script
*================================================
============
function JVS_Function(&msg)
{
msg = "Hello World!"
}
Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200 Þ Þ

Calling a Registered JavaScript Subroutine
Example
Here is another example that calls a registered JavaScript
subroutine. It passes a -0 result and arguments to the
JavaScript subroutine.
.@Call example
*================================================
=============================
@ RSL,0,b,2 . Create a result of 2B0.
@ . Load a variable to pass as an argument to the
JavaScript.
@ LDV <Argument2>i5=12345 .
@ . The function name is different from the
registered JavaScript subroutine.
@ CALL,"CJSTEST1" JSTEST('test',<Argument2>) .
@ DSP,-0,,,,,,'F1 to continue example' .
'<Argument2>' After returning from the
JavaScript.
The value of '<Argument2>' should be '54321'<Argument2>
@ GTO END .
Here is the JavaScript syntax used to process, change, and
return received information from a Business Information
Server run script. Basic in/out arguments and return Dataset
object are displayed. The ‘&’ character preceding argument
two indicates that argument two is passed back. The
keyword ‘InputDataset’ is used to open the passed -0 result.
You can only use this keyword once for temporary results.
The value for argument two will be changed. Use
ReturnDataset to pass a -0 result back to the calling run.

.JavaScript example
*================================================
=============================
function JSTEST(arg1, &arg2)
{
try {
var ds = new Dataset();
ds.open(InputDataset, dsReadOnly);
arg2 = "54321";
ReturnDataset(ds);
} catch (e) {
throw(e);
}
return;
} //end of main function
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux Windows Client 2200
Windows Server Linux Windows Client Þ Þ

Calling Examples of a JavaScript Subroutine
that Fails
This report contains examples showing error handling
between a calling Business Information Server script and a
JavaScript script.
.DATE 12 JAN 04 14:47:58 RID 168A 06 JAN 04
MAPCOORD
.Error processing examples from a registered
JavaScript subroutine.
*================================================
===============================
@CHG INPUT$ <Label>i1 .
@LDV <Error>sMAXCHR$ .
@GTO <Label> .
This is an error test for a syntax error. Control is transferred
to the error routine (RER).

Note: If try/catch/ﬁnally blocks are used, they will not
change the outcome because the syntax error prevents the
JavaScript script from being executed.
@0001: .
@RER,0,e,2 0001 . Register an error routine
@CALL,"ERRORTESTS" ERROR1(<Error>) . 169a Error1 syntax error
@LDV <Status>h6=Failed gto 0049 .
@gto end .
This is an error test for an invalid database name. If the
JavaScript subroutine incorporates a try/catch/(with
throw)/ﬁnally blocks and the ﬁnally block contains a return,
no error is returned to the calling run. The return in the
ﬁnally block negates the throw - this is not a recommended

practice. The error is processed by the JavaScript script and
returned to the calling run in the variable <Error>.
@0002: .
@RER,0,e,2 0001 . Register an error routine
@CALL,"ERRORTESTS3" ERROR2(<Error>) . 171a Invalid database name 1C2
@LDV <Status>h6=Passed gto 0049 .
This is an error test for an invalid database name.
Try/catch/(with throw)/ﬁnally blocks are used. The global
error is set and the error is processed by the RER routine.
@0003: .
@RER,0,e,2 0001 . Register an error routine
@CALL,"ERRORTESTS3" ERROR3(<Error>) . 171a Invalid database name 1C2
@LDV <Status>h6=Failed gto 0049 .
This is an error test for an invalid database name. If the
JavaScript subroutine incorporates a try/catch/ﬁnally blocks
(no throw) and the ﬁnally block contains a return, no error is
returned to the calling run. The error is processed by the
JavaScript script and returned to the calling run in the
variable <Error>.

Note: The outcome is the same if there is no return in the
ﬁnally block.
@0004: .
@RER,0,e,2 0001 . Register an error routine
@CALL,"ERRORTESTS3" ERROR4(<Error>) . 171a Invalid database name 1C2
@LDV <Status>h6=Passed gto 0049 .
This is an error test for an invalid database name.
Try/catch/ﬁnally blocks (no throw or return) are used. The

error is processed by the JavaScript script and returned to
the calling run in the variable <Error>.
@0005: .
@RER,0,e,2 0001 . Register an error routine
@CALL,"ERRORTESTS3" ERROR5(<Error>) . 171a Invalid database name 1C2
@LDV <Status>h6=Passed gto 0049 .
@.
*************************************************
***************************
@0049: .
@ LCV,0100 '' <Error> Error <CharPos>i3 .
@ BRK,0,i .
Error Text Passed Back for test number
<Label>,this test <Status>!
@ . The error message may contain embedded line
feed characters.
@ CHG <LineFeed>H1 CHR$ 12 . Load <LineFeed> with
the line feed character.
@ LDV <Index>i3=1 .
@ . Locate any line feed characters contained
within error text and skip them.
@0050:LCV,0051 '' <Error>(<Index>-0) <LineFeed>
<CharPos>i3 .
@ LDV <GRT80>i1=0 .
@ CHG <HoldChars>i3 <CharPos> -<Index> .
@ IF <HoldChars> > 80 . ; GTO 0061 .
@ LDV <GRT80>=1 .
@ LDV <HoldChars>=80 .
@0058:CHG <CharPos> <Index> + <HoldChars> .
@ IF <Error>(<CharPos>-1) EQ ' ' GTO 0061 .
Looking for end
@ DEC <HoldChars> .
@ IF <HoldChars> < 60 LDV <HoldChars>=80 GTO 0061

. ; GTO 0058 .

<Error>(<Index>-<HoldChars>)
@ CHG <Index> <Index> + <HoldChars> .
@ INC <Index> GTO 0050 .
@0051:IF <Error>(<Index>-1) EQ ' ' . ;GTO 0059 .
@ IF <Index> = 900 GTO 0059 . ; INC <Index> GTO
0051 .
@0059:CHG <CharPos> 900 - <Index> IF <CharPos> =
0 GTO 0070 .
<Error>(<Index>-<CharPos>)
@0070:BRK DSP,-0 .
@ GTO END .
@0100: .
No Global error and no return error text received
for test number <label>.
@ GTO END .
@0150:REL .

function ERROR1(&errortext)
{
var oDs = new Dataset();
var oDb = new Database();

oDb.logon("northwind", "sa", "123456"); // "sa"
user-id is valid

FirstName, Country FROM

function ERROR2(&errortext)
{
try {
var oDs = new Dataset();
var oDb = new Database();
var numRecords, numColumns;
oDb.logon("bbbthwind", "sa", "123456"); //
invalid database name - error
} catch (e) {
errortext = e.message;
if (oDb.errorText.length > 0) {
errortext += formatErrorMessage(oDb.errorText);
}
throw(e); // the throw is ignored due to the
return
} finally {
oDb.logoff();
return;
}
}

{

var oDb = new Database();
oDb.logon("bbbthwind", "sa", "123456"); //
invalid database name - error
} catch (e) {
errortext = e.message;
if (oDb.errorText.length > 0) {
errortext += formatErrorMessage(oDb.errorText);
}
} finally {
oDb.logoff();
}

}
// The following function will format a
relational database error
function formatErrorMessage(msg) {
var s = new String(); //formatted version of
"msg" to be returned to caller
var pat = /[\t\n\r\f]/g; //list on nondisplayable characters to be removed
var arr = msg.split("\n"); //split message into
elements based on each line
for (var i=0; i<arr.length; i++ ) { //for each
line of message
//remove all non-displayable, and reconstruct as
string
s += arr[i].replace(pat, "") + " \n";
}
do {
var arr = s.split(" "); //attempt to remove
extraneous whitespace
s = "Relational Database Error \n";
for (var i=0; i<arr.length; i++ ) { //for each
line of message
s += arr[i]; //reconstruct as string
}
}
while(arr.length > 1);
return(s);
}

CALU and @CAU (Calculate Update)
See CAL, @CAL (Calculate), CALU, @CAU (Calculate Update)
for more information.

@CAR (Clear Abort Routine)
Use the Clear Abort Routine (CAR) statement to cancel an
abort routine previously registered by a Register Abort
Routine (RAR) statement. See @RAR (Register Abort
Routine).
Use this format:
@CAR .

@CAU (Calculate Update)
See CAL, @CAL (Calculate), CALU, @CAU (Calculate Update)
for more information.

@CBX (Deﬁne Combo Box)
Use the Deﬁne Combo Box (CBX) statement to deﬁne a
combo box and display it on the user’s screen. The CBX
statement provides a choice between a drop-down list or a
list which is always visible to the user. The CBX statement
allows the software to closely simulate a Microsoft
environment.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.

Deﬁne Combo Box Statement Format
@CBX[,c,d,r,l,tf?,oname,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,f
c/bc,o,hl] [vch] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the list of
items to be displayed within
the combo box.

l

Line within the speciﬁed
report at which to start
transferring list items. Default
= 3.

tf?

Unconditionally transfer the
report (c,d,r) to the
workstation before processing
the CBX statement? Y or N.
Default = N.

oname

Name of the object that
contains the list items. The
object may specify either a PC
ﬁle name or a version
controlled name. If you do not
specify a name in this ﬁeld,
then you must specify the

name on line 2 (the title line)
of the report speciﬁed in the
c,d,r ﬁeld.

Notes:
This command fails if you
specify a version
controlled name and
version control naming is
not supported.
For more information on
version control availability,
see the Developer’s
Guide.

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window handle.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the combo box
using a positioning unit (or
row number). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on
positioning units. When you
specify a row number, the
position is based on the main
run font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the vertical position, you must

also specify values in the hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or row number).

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the combo
box using a positioning unit
(or column number). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on positioning units. When you
specify a column number, the
position is based on the main
run font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the horizontal position, you
must also specify values in the
vert, vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or column number).

vsiz

Vertical size of the combo box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on
positioning units. When you
specify row numbers, the size
is based on the text run font.
See @FON (Font) for more

information on fonts. If you are
specifying a value for the
vertical size, you must also
specify values in the vert,
hort, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the combo box
extends to the bottom of the
window in which you place it
and expands and contracts
with that window.

hsiz

Horizontal size of the combo
box speciﬁed in positioning
units (or column numbers).
See @WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on positioning units. When you
specify column numbers, the
size is based on the text run
font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the horizontal size, you must
also specify values in the vert,
hort, and vsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the combo box
extends to the right side of the
window in which you place it
and expands and contracts
with that window.

fc/bc

Foreground and background

colors for this combo box. See
Set Default Color Codes for
the available colors.

o

Options for the combo box.
See Deﬁne Combo Box
Options for available options.

hl

Number of the item to
highlight in the combo box.

vch

Variable to capture the combo
box handle.

Deﬁne Combo Box Options
Option

Description

A

Causes text typed in the
combo edit box to
automatically be sent to the
host after a brief delay. Allows
the application to dynamically
validate or modify dialog
content as the user enters
data. See also the I option.

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability:
DWCAP$(15-1) = 1 if
available.

D

Produces a drop-down list
(default option). Do not use
the D option with the F option.

E

This value can be edited
(default option). Do not use
the E option with the P option.

F

List is always visible. Do not
use the F option with D or P
options.

I

Causes the combo list box
selection to automatically be
sent to the host after a brief
delay. Allows the application
to dynamically validate or
modify dialog content as the
user selects an item. See also
the A option.

L

List contents are converted to
lowercase. User input is also
lowercase. Do not use the L
option with the U option.

P

List contents are protected.
You cannot change the list
contents, and you must select
from the list. This option also
allows automatic positioning
by keystroke within the list.
Do not use the P option with E
or F options.

S

List contents are sorted
alphabetically prior to display.

U

List contents are converted to
uppercase. User input is also

uppercase. Do not use the U
option with the L option.

Y

Allows the sizing of the control
to be in row and column units,
independent of the units used
for placement.

Deﬁne Combo Box Colors
See Set Default Color Codes for a list of valid colors that can
be used with the Deﬁne Combo Box statement.

Deﬁne Combo Box Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the CBX statement: You
can have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT, LST,
PIC, TXT, and WIN controls concurrently open.

Note: The actual number of handles created can be lower
depending on workstation PC memory conﬁguration and
usage.

Deﬁne Combo Box Example
This example uses the CBX statement to display a protected
drop-down combo box, and the INP statement to determine
where to continue the run after the user makes a selection
from the box.
@cbx,0,a,155,6,,list.dat,
<win1>,5600,9600,12000,24000,bla/whi,dp <cbx1>I6
.

.

at line 6.

where the data for the

is placed into <name>.

CC (Column Count)
Use the Column Count (CC) command to display the column
numbers at the top of the screen or report.
The column numbers are displayed in lines 2 and 3 of the
screen. The CC command displays the correct column
numbers, regardless of the format on display.

Column Count Control Line Format
CC

Column Count Guidelines
With the column counts on display, you can perform SOE
updates or roll through your report.
If you shift or change formats, the column counts are
removed from the display.

@CCC (COM Client Create Instance)
Windows Þ Þ
Use the COM Client Create Instance (CCC) statement to
create an instance of a component. The CCC statement is
one of a group of statements that allow you to integrate the
functionality of a Microsoft Component Object Model (COM)
component into your application. See the following
statements for related information:
@CCG (COM Client Get Property Value)
@CCI (COM Client Invoke Method)
@CCP (COM Client Put Property Value)
@CCR (COM Client Release Instance)
You should be familiar with the Microsoft Component Object
Model (COM) and have a thorough understanding of the
properties and methods of the components you plan to use
before using this statement.

COM Client Create Instance Statement Format
@CCC[,lab] prog-id vch [vmsg] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the
component cannot be created.

prog-id

Name of the component to be
created.

vch

Variable receiving the instance
handle. Must be an I type
variable.

vmsg

Variable to capture the COMrelated error message text, if
the operation is unsuccessful.
Must be an S type variable.

COM Client Create Instance Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the COM Client Create
Instance statement:
Windows 2000 or later operating system is required.
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the run errors. You should specify both
the lab and vmsg parameters to assist in evaluating
errors. The STAT1$ value should also be obtained at the
contingency label.
A primary instance handle is a handle returned by the
CCC statement. A secondary instance handle is a
component method or property returning IDispatch
pointers (handles) to other component instances. A
combined total of ﬁve primary and secondary instance
handles can exist concurrently in your session.
Instance handles exist and may be passed across
subroutine boundaries, but not across application
boundaries. For example, an application may pass a
handle as a CALL (Call Subroutine) statement
argument, or the subroutine may create an instance
handle and return it to the caller. When the current
application terminates or executes a LNK (Link to
Another Script) statement, all component instances are
released and their associated handle variables are set
to zero.
Components are created using the current session user
identity. For local Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server sessions, this is the Windows login
speciﬁed in your Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server connection script. For all other

session types, it is the conﬁgured guest login (a default
of, "CoolICEGuest" or "MAPPERGuest"). Component
access and starting permissions (default and classspeciﬁc) must be set accordingly.
When creating an instance of a component that is
hosted on a remote machine, the local Windows login
and password must be valid on the remote machine.

COM Client Create Instance Outcome
If the speciﬁed component is registered, and the access and
starting permissions allow creation by the local Windows
login, a new instance of the component is created. The
handle to the instance is set in vch and STAT1$=0.
If an instance cannot be created, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label. STAT1$ contains the HRESULT or Windows
error code value generated by the operation. The vmsg
variable contains a description of the error. If a label is not
speciﬁed, the run errors.

Scalability Failure
If a "Server execution failed" error occurs while utilizing CCC
within a run, check the System Event Viewer for the
following Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM)
event:
Event ID: 10010
Description: "The server {class id} did not register with
DCOM within the required timeout". The {class id} is the
globally unique identiﬁer (GUID) of the component
object class.
If the outlined failure occurs, see the Business Information
Server for Microsoft Windows Administration Help for
information on COM scalability.

COM Client Create Instance Example
This example creates an instance of a component called
"Prog.Mail". The instance handle is captured in the variable "
<handle>". If an instance is not created, the run continues
at label 199, and the variable "<vmsg>" contains a
description of the error.
@ccc,199 ‘Prog.Mail’ <handle>i2 <vmsg>s80 .
See @CCI (COM Client Invoke Method) for additional
examples.
Ü Ü Windows

@CCG (COM Client Get Property
Value)
Windows Þ Þ
Use the COM Client Get Property Value (CCG) statement to
get the value of an object property. The CCG statement is
one of a group of statements that allow you to integrate the
functionality of a Microsoft Component Object Model (COM)
component into your application. See the following
statements for related information:
@CCC (COM Client Create Instance)
@CCI (COM Client Invoke Method)
@CCP (COM Client Put Property Value)
@CCR (COM Client Release Instance)
You should be familiar with the Microsoft Component Object
Model (COM) and have a thorough understanding of the
properties and methods of the components you plan to use
before using this statement.

COM Client Get Property Value Statement
Format
@CCG[,lab] vch,property rtn [vmsg] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the operation
is unsuccessful.

vch

Variable containing the
instance handle of the
component instance whose
property is to be accessed.

property

Component property name.
See Specifying Property
Names.

rtn

Variable or report receiving
the property value. See
Specifying Argument and
Return Values in the @CCI
(COM Client Invoke Method)
statement.

vmsg

Variable to capture the COMrelated error message text, if

the operation is unsuccessful.
Must be an S type variable.

Specifying Property Names
The syntax of a property name is:

property[(index)]
The property name is speciﬁed by property and is required.
If property is deﬁned by the component as an indexed
property, the index values are enclosed in parentheses
immediately following the property name. You are only
allowed numeric indexes of up to two dimensions. An index
value must be an integer. Named indexes are not supported.
For example:

property

Non-indexed property
name

property(2)

Element 2 of the property

property(1,2) Element "1,2" of the
property

COM Client Get Property Value Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the COM Client Get
Property Value statement: If a label is not speciﬁed and the
operation cannot be completed successfully, the run goes
into error mode. You should specify both the lab and vmsg
parameters to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$ value
should also be obtained at the contingency label.

COM Client Get Property Value Outcome
If no error occurs, the property value is placed into the rtn
container and STAT1$=0.
If the property value request errors, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label. STAT1$ contains the HRESULT or Windows
error code value generated by the operation. The vmsg
variable contains a description of the error. If a label is not
speciﬁed, the run errors.

COM Client Get Property Value Example
This example gets the value of the "NumberOfRecords"
property of the component instance whose handle is
contained in the variable "<handle>". If the property value
is not returned, the run continues at label 199. The variable
"<vmsg>" contains a description of the error.
@ccg,199 <handle>,’NumberOfRecords’ <numrecs>i6
<vmsg>s80 .
See @CCI (COM Client Invoke Method) for additional
examples.
Ü Ü Windows

@CCI (COM Client Invoke Method)
Windows Þ Þ
Use the COM Client Invoke Method (CCI) statement to invoke
an object method. The CCI statement is one of a group of
statements that allow you to integrate the functionality of a
Microsoft Component Object Model (COM) component into your
application. See the following statements for related
information:
@CCC (COM Client Create Instance)
@CCG (COM Client Get Property Value)
@CCP (COM Client Put Property Value)
@CCR (COM Client Release Instance)
You should be familiar with the Microsoft Component Object
Model (COM) and have a thorough understanding of the
properties and methods of the components you plan to use
before using this statement.

COM Client Invoke Method Statement Format
@CCI[,lab] vch,method dt arg [rtn vmsg] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the operation
is unsuccessful.

vch

Variable containing the
instance handle of the
component instance whose
method is to be invoked.

method

Name of the component
method to be invoked.

dt

A comma-separated list
(maximum of 20 items)
indicating the data types of
the method arguments. See
Specifying Data Types.

arg

A comma-separated list of
method arguments. See
Specifying Argument and
Return Values. The number of
arguments speciﬁed must

match the number of items in
the dt ﬁeld exactly.

rtn

Variable or report receiving
the method return value. See
Specifying Argument and
Return Values.

vmsg

Variable to capture the COMrelated error message text, if
the operation is unsuccessful.
Must be an S type variable.

Specifying Data Types
A data type value speciﬁes the direction and data type of a
method argument, and consists of a direction indicator, a type
indicator, or both. The general syntax of a data type value is as
follows: [&][type]
An argument passes a value ("[in]"), returns a value ("[out]"),
or both ("[in,out]") in Interface Deﬁnition Language (IDL)
syntax.
If the leading & is present, the argument returns one or more
data items.
The type, if present, indicates the data type of the argument
only if it passes a value from the client. Do not specify a data
type if the argument only returns a value, because the
component includes the data type with the value. For example:

Data
Type

Returned Value

type

[in]

&

[out]

&type

[in,out]

A variant container is used to specify method arguments.
Variants are a chameleon data type that holds many types of
values. The basic data types allowed for type are as follows:

DT
Variant
Field Data Type

Description Range

UI1

Unsigned
Not applicable. The size of
integer - one the variable cannot be
byte
greater than 1.

VT_UI1

BOOL VT_BOOL

Boolean
Range: 0 to 1
value - zero
or one

I2

VT_I2

Signed
integer - 2
bytes

Range: -32,768 to 32,767

I4

VT_I4

Signed
integer - 4
bytes

Range: -2,147,483,648 to
2,147,483,647

R4

VT_R4

Real
(ﬂoating
point) - 4

Negative values:
-3.402823E+38 to
-1.175494E-38
Positive values: 1.175494E38 to 3.402823E+38

R8

VT_R8

Real
(ﬂoating

bytes

Double-precision, ﬂoatingpoint range:
Negative values:
-1.79769313486231E+308

to -2.22507385585072E308
Positive values:
2.22507385585072E-308 to
1.79769313486231E+308

See Date/Time Rules.
Not applicable

VT_VARIANT Variant
Not applicable
(report only)

Date/Time Rules:

Format must be DATE16$ TIME$ (HH:MM:SS). Any other
format may return unexpected results.
For example: @LDV <DateTime>s20=01/01/2000
12:00:00.
A time value is always returned. If only a date value is
requested, size your return variable to 10 characters.
Format can be date only, but must be DATE16$
(MM/DD/YYYY). Leading zeros are required for MM and DD.
For example:

BSTR

A BSTR (binary string) is
passed to the component, a
value is not returned.

&R8

An R8 (8-byte real number) is
passed to the component and
returned by the component.

&

A value is returned by the
component. The data type is
speciﬁed by the component.

The type syntax supports arguments deﬁned as arrays
(VT_ARRAY). The presence of paired square bracket characters
immediately following the basic data type, indicate an array of
the speciﬁed type. The number of elements in the array is
determined by the actual data source. See Specifying
Argument and Return Values.
For example:

BSTR[]

An array of BSTRs (binary
strings)

I4[]

An array of I4s (signed integer
- 4 bytes)

When the data type is VAR (variant), an array must be
speciﬁed. You must specify the array element deﬁnition within
the square brackets. An array element deﬁnition consists of a
comma-separated list of one or more basic data types. The
maximum number of array elements is 20.
For example:

VAR[BSTR,R8]

An array of variants.
The ﬁrst element in
the array is type
BSTR, and the
second is type R8.

VAR[I4,DATE,CY]

An array of variants.
The ﬁrst element in
the array is type I4,
the second is type
DATE, and the third
is type CY.

VAR[I4]

An array of variants.
Each element in the
array is type I4. This

is equivalent to I4[],
but is not identical.
The format is an
array of type VAR,
with elements of
type I4. The later is
simply an array of
type I4.

VAR[VAR[BSTR,R8]] An array of variant
arrays. The ﬁrst
element in the array
is also an array. The
sub-array contains
two elements. The
ﬁrst element is type
BSTR, and the
second element is
type R8. The
sequence repeats
for all remaining
elements in the
array.

Note: The DISP data type can only be a variable. It cannot be
used in a VAR data type.

Specifying Argument and Return Values
The argument list is used with the preceding dt list to specify
the actual data values of the method arguments.
The ﬁrst item in the arg list passes its data type from the ﬁrst
item in the dt list. The second item in the arg list passes its
data type from the second item in the dt list, and so on. The
number of items in the argument must match the number of
items in the data type list.
If a methods return value is deﬁned, it does not receive its type
from the data type list because the component supplies this
information when the value is returned. The script designer
should know this data type so a receiving container can be
speciﬁed. If a value is returned in a variable, the following are
the recommended variable types and the corresponding data
types.

Data
Type

Return Variable Type

UI1

S-type or H-type

BOOL

I-type

I2

I-type

I4

I-type

R4*

F-type

R8*

F-type

CY

F-type

BSTR

S-type or H-type

DATE

S-type or H-type

DISP

I-type (returns in variable only)

VAR

NA (returns in report only)

*

R4 and R8 array argument
values will be converted to
scientiﬁc notations, if their
magnitude exceeds the
number of signiﬁcant digits
that can be held in the R4 or
R8 container.

The COM Client facility supports the following three forms of
arguments:
Variable/literal data - any concatenation of script
variables, literal data, or reserved words. Can be used for
non-array arguments that do not return a value.

Simple variable - a single script variable speciﬁcation. Can
be used for non-array arguments.
Report - a single report speciﬁcation. Use with type BSTR
or array arguments. If the argument returns a value, the
report must be a result.

Specifying a Report as an Argument
The syntax for a report argument is as follows:
(c,d,-r[,rs,options])

Field

Description

c,d,-r

The cabinet, drawer, and
temporary report containing
the argument data. If the
argument passes a value, the
report must exist. If the
argument returns a value and
the report does not exist, c,d
must be speciﬁed.

rs

Record separator used by the
component (0-2 characters).

options

Report handling options. See
Report Handling Options for
available options.

Report Handling Options
Option

Description

B

Binary report. Valid only for
UI1[] arguments that return a
value. Not valid with any other
option.

C

Concatenate array elements
to or from a single report line.
Valid only for UI1[] type.
Without the W option:
Without a record
separator, values are
concatenated on one line
and truncation occurs if
the array exceeds the
drawer width.
With a record separator,
values are concatenated
on a line until a record
separator value (a new
line is started). If the end
of the line is reached
without a record separator
value, truncation occurs
until the next record
separator is reached.

With the W option, values are
concatenated across multiple
lines starting at column 2 and
truncation does not occur.

H

Include report headers.
Applies to arguments that
pass a value using the [in]
argument. If set, start at line 1
(.DATE). If headers are present
(default), start at the ﬁrst line
after the header divider. Valid
only for BSTR, BSTR[], and
UI1[] data types.

T

Include trailing blanks. Valid
for BSTR, BSTR[], and UI1[]
(with the C option) arguments
that pass a value.

W

Line wrap data. Allows
argument values that exceed
the drawer width to continue
across multiple lines. A tab
code line type indicates the
ﬁrst line of a new value.
Extension lines have an
asterisk (*) line type. Without
the W option, a line type is not
used and values that exceed
the drawer width are
truncated.

Report Argument Example Using VAR Data Type
This is a sample of the data format necessary for using the
VAR[VAR[&]] format. This example does not reference a
speciﬁc COM component.
Each record uses 3 lines. The ﬁrst line of each record is a BSTR
data type. The second line is a DATE data type. The third line is
an I4 data type. When using the VAR[VAR[&]] format, the
report can contain as many 3-line records as will ﬁt into a
result.
@ BRK .
*==========================
This is the first BSTR record
12/31/2000
30000000
This is the second BSTR record
01/01/2001
-20000000
@ BRK . Created the 0
@ CCI,label handle,method VAR[VAR[BSTR,DATE,I4]]
(-0) rtn vmsg .

Note: No other information (such as comments) should follow
a data item in the report.

COM Client Invoke Method Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the COM Client Invoke Method
statement: If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation cannot
be completed successfully, the run errors. You should specify
both the lab and vmsg parameters to assist in evaluating
errors. STAT1$, STAT2$, and STAT3$ values should also be
obtained at the contingency label.

COM Client Invoke Method Outcome
The speciﬁed named method is invoked. Argument values are
passed to the method in accordance with the direction
information speciﬁed in the dt ﬁeld. Argument and method
return values are placed into the speciﬁed container
(STAT1$=0).
If the speciﬁed method errors, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label. STAT1$ contains the HRESULT or Windows error
code value generated by the operation. If the error is from a
problem with a method argument, STAT2$ contains the
sequence number of the argument value that caused the
failure (the ﬁrst argument listed is number 1, the second
argument number listed is number 2, and so on). STAT3$
indicates truncation. When truncation occurs, STAT3$ contains
the argument number of the ﬁrst report argument. The
statement label is ignored and the run continues. The vmsg
variable contains a description of the error. If a label is not
speciﬁed, the run errors.

Sending an E-mail Message Example
To send an e-mail message, perform the following tasks:
1. Create a component instance.
2. Specify "From," "To," and "Subject" properties. The
message body is built in a result and speciﬁed using the
"textBody" property.
3. Call the "Send" method. This sends the message.

Notes:
The argument value speciﬁes the report containing the
body text, as well as the record separator (<lf>) that is
used.
The data type (dt) and argument (arg) ﬁelds are empty,
because the method does not pass any arguments.
@ CCC,0100 CDO.Message <Handle>i1 <Vmsg>s80 .
@ CCP,0100 <Handle>,From BSTR From.User@Company.com
<Vmsg>s80 .
@ CCP,0100 <Handle>,To BSTR To.User@Company.com
<Vmsg>s80
@ CCP,0100 <Handle>,Subject BSTR CCX NewMail'
<Vmsg>s80 .
@ BRK .
Greetings,
You have just received an e-mail that was sent using
the CCx statements.
@ BRK .
@ LDV <Lf>h1=chr$ 12 .
@ CCP,0100 <Handle>,textBody BSTR (-0,<Lf>)
<Vmsg>s80 .
@ CCI,0100 <Handle>,Send <Vmsg>s80 .
@ CCR <Handle> .

@ GTO END . **** Success ****
@0100 .
STAT1 = STAT1$, STAT2 = STAT2$, STAT3 = STAT3$
-<Vmsg>@ GTO END . **** Error occurred ****

Using the COM Client Invoke Method to Pass Data
Example
This example shows the NewMail program using the CCI to
pass data.

Note: CDONTS remains on your server when you upgrade to
Microsoft Windows Server 2003, because Cdonts.dll is not
removed during an upgrade to Microsoft Windows Server 2003.
CDONTS is not installed when you perform a new installation of
Microsoft Windows Server 2003.
@. This CCX example shows the NewMail program using
the CCI to pass data.
@.
@. To run this, fill in a valid
From.User@Company.com and To.User@Company.com
@.
@ CHG <Lf>h1 CHR$ 012 .
@ LDV <Importance>i1=2 . 2 = High Importance
@ CCC,0199 'CDONTS.NewMail' <Handle>i2 <Vmsg>s80
@ CCI,0100 <Handle>,Send bstr,bstr,bstr,bstr,i4
From.User@Company.com,\
To.User@Company.com,'Subject Text','Body Text',
<Importance> '' <Vmsg>s80 .
@ CCR <Handle> .
@ GTO END . ***** Success *****
@0100:.
@ CCR <Handle> .
@0199:.
@ BRK .
stat1: stat1$
stat2: stat2$
stat3: stat3$
-<Vmsg>
@ BRK DSP,-0 .
@ GTO END . ***** Failed *****

Ü Ü Windows

@CCP (COM Client Put Property
Value)
Windows Þ Þ
Use the COM Client Put Property Value (CCP) statement to
put a value into an object property. The CCP statement is
one of a group of statements that allow you to integrate the
functionality of a Microsoft Component Object Model (COM)
component into your application. See the following
statements for related information:
@CCC (COM Client Create Instance)
@CCG (COM Client Get Property Value)
@CCI (COM Client Invoke Method)
@CCR (COM Client Release Instance)
You should be familiar with the Microsoft Component Object
Model (COM) and have a thorough understanding of the
properties and methods of the components you plan to use
before using this statement.

COM Client Put Property Value Statement
Format
@CCP[,lab] vch,property dt value [vmsg] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the operation
is unsuccessful.

vch

Variable containing the
instance handle of the
component instance whose
property is to be updated.

property

Component property name.
See Specifying Property
Names in the @CCG (COM
Client Get Property Value)
statement.

dt

Property data type. See
Specifying Data Types in the
@CCI (COM Client Invoke
Method) statement

value

Variable or report that
contains the property value.

See Specifying Argument and
Return Values in the @CCI
(COM Client Invoke Method)
statement

vmsg

Variable to capture the COMrelated error message text, if
the operation is unsuccessful.
Must be an S type variable.

COM Client Put Property Value Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the COM Client Put
Property Value statement: If a label is not speciﬁed and the
operation cannot be completed successfully, the run errors.
You should specify both the lab and vmsg parameters to
assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$ value should also be
obtained at the contingency label.

COM Client Put Property Value Outcome
The value of the speciﬁed property is replaced by value and
STAT1$=0.
If the operation errors, the run continues at the speciﬁed
label. STAT1$ contains the HRESULT or Windows error code
value generated by the operation. The vmsg variable
contains a description of the error. If a label is not speciﬁed,
the run errors.

COM Client Put Property Value Example
This example puts the value of the "Subject" property to the
value contained in the "<subject>" run variable. The
property data type is "bstr" (binary string). The component
instance handle is contained in the variable "<handle>". If
the property value cannot be set, the run continues at label
199. The variable "<vmsg>" contains a description of the
error.
@ccp,199 <handle>,’Subject’ bstr <subject>
<vmsg>s80 .
See @CCI (COM Client Invoke Method) for additional
examples.
Ü Ü Windows

@CCR (COM Client Release Instance)
Windows Þ Þ
Use the COM Client Release Instance (CCR) statement to
release an instance of a component. The CCR statement is
one of a group of statements that allow you to integrate the
functionality of a Microsoft Component Object Model (COM)
component into your application. See the following
statements for related information:
@CCC (COM Client Create Instance)
@CCG (COM Client Get Property Value)
@CCI (COM Client Invoke Method)
@CCP (COM Client Put Property Value)
You should be familiar with the Microsoft Component Object
Model (COM) and have a thorough understanding of the
properties and methods of the components you plan to use
before using this statement.

COM Client Release Instance Statement Format
@CCR vch[,vch…] .

Field

Description

vch

A comma-separated list of
variables containing the
instance handles to be
released.

COM Client Release Instance Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the COM Client Release
Instance statement:
If one or more of the speciﬁed component instances
cannot be released, the run continues at the next
statement.
A primary instance handle is a handle returned by the
CCC statement. A secondary instance handle is a
component method or property returning IDispatch
pointers (handles) to other component instances. A
combined total of ﬁve primary and secondary instance
handles can exist concurrently in your session. The CCR
statement can be used to release both types of
handles.

COM Client Release Instance Outcome
For each valid instance handle speciﬁed, the associate
component instance is released (STAT1$=0).

COM Client Release Instance Example
This example releases the component instance whose
handle is contained in the variable "<handle>". The run
continues at the next statement.
@ccr <handle> .
See @CCI (COM Client Invoke Method) for additional
examples.
Ü Ü Windows

@CER (Clear Error Routine)
Use the Clear Error Routine (CER) statement to cancel an
error routine previously registered by a Register Error
Routine (RER) statement. See @RER (Register Error
Routine).
Use this format:
@CER .

CHART
Use the Chart command to create color charts for screen
display or for output to a printing device.
You can produce the following types of charts, using the
Chart command.

Bar

Radar

Block

Scatter

Line

Target

Mixed Bar and
Line

Text

Pareto

3D Bar

Pie

Time Line

Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Chart Control Line Format
CHART[,call,report]
or

call[,report]

Field

Description

call

Call for the type of chart being
produced (such as pieg).

report

Chart report in the current
cabinet. Default = current
report.

Chart Procedures
To see examples of the types of charts available, enter
chart,e on the control line. Select a chart, then press
Chart to display the chart on the screen.
To create a chart, using the Chart command, enter data
in a chart report and then enter chart.
To display a chart from a completed chart report, press
Chart.

Note: If you require a plottable version of your chart, do
not use the graphics primitive code generated after
pressing Chart. Instead, see Sending Graphics Primitive
Code to a Plotter.
To display a chart from a graphics primitive code report,
enter g - or chart,c.
To display a chart with no report displayed, enter
chart,c,report, where report is the report containing
graphics primitive code.
To use a chart in a run, use the Execute a Script (RUN)
or Link to Another Script (LNK) statement. To display the
chart, save the chart as a graphics primitive code report
and use the Display Graphics (DSG) statement.
To remove a chart from the screen using Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server, close the
chart window using standard Microsoft Windows
procedures.
2200 Þ Þ

To remove a chart from the screen, press Resume to
continue. To toggle to the previously displayed text
screen before you continued
PC: Press Shift + ESC twice. On PCs with STEP/PEP,
press F1.
UTS 30 and UTS 60: Press Shift + DISP 1-2 twice Ü Ü
2200
To display graphics primitive code, press Resume after
removing a chart from the screen. You can save and use
graphics primitive code in the MULTI, Display, and
Graphics Scaler commands. To save the code, use the
Replace Report or Duplicate Report commands.
To use an apostrophe in text on a chart, enter two
apostrophes (for example, Chris’’s). To avoid data
corruption in charts, do not use single apostrophes.
