Sending Graphics Primitive Code to a Plotter
The Send Code to Plotter selection of the Chart menu
enables you to design and plot your own charts, using
graphics primitive code.
Your terminal or a remote terminal must be attached to a
plotter. To send code to a local plotter, transmit from the
Send Code to Plotter selection of the Chart menu or enter
chart,p
To send code to a remote plotter, enter
chart,p,report,sn
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet
(default = current result), and sn is the station number of
the terminal attached to the plotter.

@CHD (Command Handler)
Use the Command Handler (CHD) statement to register a
routine to be executed whenever the user of the run enters
information on the control line after a Display Report (DSP),
Display Message (DSM), Output (OUT), or Screen Control
(SC) statement.

Note: Use the CHD statement only if you are an advanced
application designer. It is intended primarily for intercepting
and interpreting commands that are normally processed by
the software.

Command Handler Statement Format
@CHD[,c,d,r,rel?] lab .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report that contains a
command handler routine.
(Specify only if the routine is in
another run control report.)

2200 Þ Þ

c,d,r

Report that contains a
command handler routine.
(Specify only if the routine is in
another run control report.)
This report must be in the
same character set type as
the calling run control report.

Ü Ü 2200

rel?

Transfer release control to the
run? Y or N. If you specify Y,
the run user cannot execute
the Release Display (^)

command because the caret
(^) is intercepted by the run.
Default = N.

lab

Label where the command
handler routine starts. Use 0 to
cancel the currently registered
command handler routine. To
begin the routine at the ﬁrst
line of the external run control
report, use LIN1 in this ﬁeld.

Command Handler Outcome
Normally, the software interprets all entries made in the
control line. With a CHD statement in the run, however, the
run itself interprets and processes input from the control
line, except for the Release Display (^) command. The
software continues to perform the Release Display
command unless you enter y in the rel? subﬁeld to give
control to the run.
If the run user presses a function key, the run continues at
the next line. If function key mapping is in eﬀect, the run
does not continue unless the function key pressed is
mapped to "rsm" or "KEY." For information on key mapping,
see @SC (Screen Control) and @FKY (Function Key).
Whenever a run user transmits from the control line, the run
continues at the speciﬁed CHD routine and cancels any
active subroutines.
When you use the CHD statement with mapped function
keys, the function key is processed and results are returned
to CHD.
Any SOE updates of reports while using the CHD statement
resume the run and no updates occur to the displayed
report.

Command Handler Reserved Words
ICVAR$ and FKEY$ capture information about the user input
whenever the run user transmits from the control line.
ICVAR$ captures user input from the control line. Follow
these guidelines:
Use only with a CHD statement.
Place ICVAR$ before the variable in the Change Variable
(CHG) statement, and put the CHG statement before the
DSP, DSM, OUT, or SC statement.
Use a string variable (type S). You can also use an
alphanumeric (type A) or Hollerith (type H) variable if
the data ﬁts in it. The system copies all input (except
leading tab characters) into the speciﬁed variable up to
the end of the variable.
FKEY$ captures the number of the function key pressed.
Whenever the run user presses a function key, the run
continues executing at the statement following the DSP,
DSM, OUT, or SC statement - not in the CHD routine.
FKEY$ contains a number indicating the function key
pressed (contains 0 if the run user pressed Transmit with
the cursor positioned below the control line).

Command Handler Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Command Handler
statement:
Use the CHD statement only in an application’s initial
run control report, and do not invoke it from within a
subroutine (CALL or RSR). Using the CHD statement
within subroutines leads to unpredictable results.
The Accept Input (INP) statement intercepts and
interprets data entered on the control line, overriding
the eﬀect of the CHD statement.

Command Handler Examples
If the run user transmits from the control line, this example
transfers control to label 52 in this run control report:
@chd 052 .
If the run user enters a caret in the control line and
transmits, this example transfers control to label 100:
@chd,,,,y 100 .
This example cancels the currently registered command
handler routine:
@chd 0 .
This example uses ICVAR$, INVAR$, and FKEY$:
@chd 010 .
@chg icvar$ <controlln>s80 .
@chg invar$ <input1>a5,<input2>a5 .
@brk .
data1| data2|
@brk out,-0,2,2,,1,y .
@if fkey$ = 0,(030),1,(040),2,(050) ; .
Following is the outcome:
If the run user presses Transmit from the control line,
the run goes to label 10, with <controlln> capturing
control line input (from the last SOE character or home
position).

If the run user presses Transmit with the cursor below
the control line, the run goes to label 30, with <input1>
and <input2> capturing data input.
If the run user presses F1, the run goes to label 40.
If the run user presses F2, the run goes to label 50.

@CHG (Change Variable)
Use the Change Variable (CHG) statement to set the value
of a variable or capture input data into one or more
variables. Use format 1 to set the value of a variable; use
format 2 to capture input data.

Change Variable Statement Format
@CHG v {exp|vld} .
or
@CHG rw v[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

v

Variable you are changing. To
deﬁne or redeﬁne the variable,
include the variable type and
size.

exp

Expression by which you want
to set the value of the
variable.

vld

Value to place into the
variable. You can specify the
value using a literal, constant,
variable, reserved word, or
any combination of these.

rw

Reserved word that
determines the kind of input
data you want to capture in
the variable.

Using Expressions in Change Variable
You can use variables containing numbers, constants,
reserved words, and the following operators in a CHG
statement expression:
Addition (+)
Subtraction (-)
Multiplication (*)
Division (/)
Integer division (//)
Precede each operator with a space, but a space following
the operator is optional. If you include a variable in the
expression, the variable must be deﬁned prior to the CHG
statement.
The system evaluates the expression from left to right,
following no precedence rules. Therefore, use only simple
expressions with the CHG statement.

Change Variable Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the CHG statement:
To simply increase or decrease the numeric value of a
variable, use the Increment Variable (INC) or Decrement
Variable (DEC) statement.
If you need to perform simple arithmetic operations on
values in a variable, deﬁne the variable as an I or F type
variable. Use I for whole numbers (positive and
negative); use F for fractions (positive and negative).
Here is an example of using the CHG statement to set
the value of a variable with a simple arithmetic
operation:
@chg <total> <subtot1> + <subtot2> .
Performing arithmetic operations on type A, H, or S
variables causes unpredictable results and is not
recommended.
Each time a run receives input, the system stores it as
the current input. Refer to the current input with INPUT$.
To capture the current input into one or more variables,
issue a CHG statement with INPUT$ immediately after
the input is received.
For example, to capture the input entered on a screen
displayed by an OUT statement, use these statements:
@out,-0,2,5,1 .
@chg input$ <name>,<address>,<phone> .

To capture the initial input to a run (for example, if the
run user types runit,6,1,90), issue a CHG statement
similar to the following as the ﬁrst statement in the run:
@chg input$ <month>i2,<day>i2,<year>i2 .
2200 Þ Þ
When you use arithmetic operators on type O variables,
the CHG statement produces octal answers. See
Processing Octal Variables Using Change Variable.
Commas immediately following constants, variables,
and reserved words are ignored.
Ü Ü 2200

Change Variable Reserved Words
To capture upcoming input, issue the CHG statement
immediately before you solicit input. Use one of these
reserved words:

ICVAR$

Captures the next input
entered on the control line.

INMSV$

Captures the next input
entered in a function mask
displayed by the Output Mask
(OUM) statement.

INSTR$

Captures the next input
delimited by the end of each
line on the screen.

INVAR$

Captures the next input
delimited by tab characters or
the size of the variable,
whichever the system
encounters ﬁrst.

INVR1$

Captures the next input
delimited by the size of the
speciﬁed variables, including
intervening tab characters, if
any.

CHR$

Loads a speciﬁc ASCII
character into an H type
variable.

To set the value of a variable to a speciﬁc ASCII character,
use the CHR$ reserved word.
@CHG <variable>h1 CHR$ octal .
where

variable is an H type variable of length 1.
octal is an octal number from 000 to 377 corresponding to
an ASCII character.
Note: Use the CHR$ reserved word with caution. ASCII
control characters (000 to 037) and extended ASCII
characters (177 to 377) should not be written into Business
Information Server reports because they may cause data
corruption.

Processing Octal Variables Using Change
Variable
2200 Þ Þ
You can use the following operators with octal (type O)
variables:

A

Logical AND

O

Logical OR

X

Exclusive OR

L

Left shift (also -)

R

Right shift (also +)

C

Circular shift

A shift of a type O variable is actually a bit-count shift. If the
data being loaded is a literal value, the software assumes an
octal value. If the data resides in another variable, the
software assumes a decimal value and converts it to octal
before processing.
Ü Ü 2200

Change Variable Examples
This example sets the value of <commission> with the
solution of a simple expression:
@chg <commission> <sales> * <percent> .
This example captures the current input in <name>,
<address>, and <phone>:
@chg input$ <name>,<address>,<phone> .
This example captures the next input entered on the control
line in <controlln>:
@chg icvar$ <controlln> .
This example captures the line feed ASCII character
speciﬁed by using CHR$ and the number 12:
@chg <LineFeed> chr$ 12 .

CHG and @LCH (Locate and Change)
Use the Locate and Change (CHG) function or the LCH
statement to ﬁnd and replace character strings anywhere
within one report and to create a result containing both
changed and unchanged lines. (You can use the O option to
create a result containing only changed lines.)

Locate and Change Control Line Format
CHG [report f];/tgtstr/replstr/[o]

Field

Description

report

Report in which to locate and
change data. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
search for data (lets you look
for data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 if you do not
already have those columns
on display).

;

Required character.

/

Delimiter identifying the
character string boundaries.
Can be any character except
those used in the target or
replacement string, or a caret
(^).

tgtstr

Character string to locate.

replstr

Replacement string. This
string does not have to
contain the same number of
characters as the target
string.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Locate and
Change Options). In this
function format, the A, F, and
M options are always
assumed.

Locate and Change Statement Format
@LCH,c,d,r[,l,lab] o cc tgtstr/replstr
[,vlines,vrpt] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report in which to locate and
change data. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

l

Line number at which to start
the search. (The LCH
statement processes data
lines only; if you specify a
heading line, the scan begins
with the ﬁrst data line.)

lab

Label to go to if no ﬁnds are
made.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Locate and
Change Options). Use the A, F,
and M options to change
strings, regardless of line type.

cc

Column-character positions or

names of the ﬁelds to search.

tgtstr

Character string to locate.

replstr

Replacement string. This
string does not have to
contain the same number of
characters as the target
string. If this ﬁeld contains '', a
single space will be assumed.
The following two statements
have the same result:
@ lch,-0 afm 1-80 TAB$/'' .
@ lch,-0 afm 1-80 TAB$/' ' .

,

Skipped subﬁeld, not currently
used in LCH statements.

vlines

Variable to capture the
number of lines located that
contain the target string.

vrpt

Variable to capture the
number of the report where
the target string is located.

Locate and Change Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types,
ignoring the character in
column 1 of the function mask
(or the ﬁrst character of the
tgtstr subﬁeld of the LCH
statement). Changes the line
type of all lines containing
ﬁnds to the ﬁrst character of
the replacement string.
When you specify the target
and replacement strings,
using the control line
procedure, the A, F, and M
options are always assumed.

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

2200 Þ Þ

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

This option applies only to full
character set (FCS) reports.

Processes all line types and
locates and changes the entire
string. It does not locate
strings that start in column
one.
When you specify the target
and replacement strings, using
the control line procedure, the
A, F, and M options are always
assumed.

G

Searches for data backwards
through the report.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Processes all line types and
uses the ﬁrst column under the

character of the tgtstr and
replstr subﬁelds of the LCH

target and replacement
strings.

When you specify the target
and replacement strings, using
the control line procedure, the
A, F, and M options are always
assumed.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

M

Designates the ﬁrst character
of the target string as the line
type designator. Used
primarily in runs to locate
speciﬁc line types. When you
specify the target and
replacement strings, using the
control line procedure, the A,
F, and M options are always
assumed.

O

Creates a result containing
each line on which strings
were changed.

OU

Creates an update result
containing each line on which
strings were changed. This
update result enables you to
Delete (DEL) or Extract (EXT)
the formal lines from the
report or Update (UPD) the

found lines and blend them
back into the report.

S

Starts the search at the ﬁrst
line on the screen. This option
is not available with the LCH
statement.

Sx

Starts the search at line x,
where x is a positive number.

Sx-y

Searches lines x through y.

Sx,n

Starts the search at line x and
searches n lines.

Tx

Speciﬁes any character as a
wildcard character for tgtstr.
Default wildcard character =
blank. For an example, see
Using Space As Wildcard
Example. Do not use the
wildcard character as the ﬁrst
character in the character
string.

2200 Þ Þ

Tx

Ü Ü 2200

If the replacement string is
shorter than the target string,
the command ﬁlls the
characters at the far right with
the speciﬁed transparent
character.

Locate and Change Outcomes
Executing the Locate and Change command as a manual
function causes the following actions to occur:
If the data is found
A result is displayed that contains all the lines of the
report. In the result, the new string replaces the
target string at each occurrence.
If you used the O option, the result contains only the
lines where the target string was found and
changed. The OU option creates an update result.
If the replacement string is larger than the target
string, the command shifts the remaining characters
visible in the current format to the right on the lines
where changes were made. If the replacement
string is smaller than the target string, the
characters move to the left on the line.
If the data is not found, an unchanged result is
displayed.
Executing the statement causes the following actions to
occur:
If the speciﬁed data is found, the statement
Creates a -0 result containing all the lines of the
report. Wherever the target string was found, it is
changed to the replacement string in the result.

Loads variables as described under the format ﬁeld
descriptions.
If you used the O option, the statement creates a
result containing only the lines where the target
string was found and changed. The OU option
creates an update result.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, the statement
Loads the variables with 0.
Gives control to the speciﬁed label.

Locate and Change Procedures
To ﬁll in the function mask

1. On the ﬁrst line under the mask, type the line type to
process in the ﬁrst column, followed by the target.

2. Press the Erase to End of Line key.
3. Move the cursor to the next line.
4. Type the line type to process in the ﬁrst column followed
by the replacement string.

5. Press the Erase to End of Line key.
6. Move the cursor to the next line and transmit.
To change a string in a speciﬁc ﬁeld, erase all asterisks in
the function mask except those in the ﬁeld you want to
change.
To quickly ﬁnd a string in freeform lines, use the following
format with a report on display: CHG ;/tgtstr/replstr/

Locating and Changing a String Example
This example changes all occurrences of blackbox to
greenbox.
Function Mask
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.
** ****** ** ********* ****** ****** *****
blackbox
greenbox
Equivalent Statement
@lch,'report2b' afm 2-79 blackbox/greenbox ,
<lines>i4 .
Partial Result
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.
IP 831224 LS greenbox 436767 84389
IP 831225 LS greenbox 436768 84390
IP 831219 LS greenbox 637071 84353
OR 840110 LS greenbox 94754
SC 840110 LS greenbox 675281 97441
IP 831222 LS greenbox 737582 84040
SH 831203 LS greenbox 746327 54237
SH 831202 LS greenbox 368061 54438

Using the Control Line Example
This example illustrates a quick way of changing all
occurrences of ip to sh. Enter the necessary change
parameters along with the CHG call.
Control Line Entry
chg 2b0;/ip/sh/
Equivalent Statement
@lch,'report2b' afm 2-79 ip/sh ,<lines>i4 .
Partial Result
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.Cus
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Cod
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.===
sh 831224 LS BLACKBOX1 436767 84389 AMC
sh 831225 LS BLACKBOX1 436768 84390 AMC
sh 831219 LS BLACKBOX2 637071 84353 INT
OR 840110 LS BLACKBOX4 94754 ARC
SC 840110 LS BLACKBOX5 675281 97441 FED
sh 831222 LS BLACKBOX5 737582 84040 AMC
SH 831203 LS BLACKBOX0 746327 54237 FED
SH 831202 LS BLACKBOX6 368061 54438 FED

Using Space As Wildcard Example
This example changes the string a o (the letter A, two
spaces, and the letter O) to the string abcd. Because the
spaces in the character string are wildcards, possible target
strings in report 2B0 include both AMCO and ARCO.
Control Line Entry
chg 2b0;/a o/abcd/
Equivalent Statement
@lch,'report2b' afm 2-79 'a o'/abcd ,<lines>i4 .
Partial Result
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.====.
IP 831224 LS BLACKBOX1 436767 84389 abcd
IP 831225 LS BLACKBOX1 436768 84390 abcd
IP 831219 LS BLACKBOX2 637071 84353 INTR
OR 840110 LS BLACKBOX4 94754 abcd
SC 840110 LS BLACKBOX5 675281 97441 FEDS
IP 831222 LS BLACKBOX5 737582 84040 abcd
SH 831203 LS BLACKBOX0 746327 54237 FEDS
SH 831202 LS BLACKBOX6 368061 54438 FEDS
SH 831209 LS BLACKBOX6 777324 54232 DICO
SH 831203 LS BLACKBOX6 785367 52833 abcd
IP 831216 LS BLACKBOX6 926581 89381 INTR

@CLK (Clear Link)
Use the Clear Link (CLK) statement in a run that was started
by a Link to Another Script (LNK) statement in order to clear
the original link. This enables you to link to another run from
within the called run.
Use this format:
@CLK .

CLOCK
Use the Clock command to display the current date and a
running time clock.
Use this format:
CLOCK

@CLS (Close Control)
Use the Close Control (CLS) statement to close a control or
end a Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE) conversation.
The CLS statement closes the speciﬁed control. A control
can be: BTN (Button), CBX (Combo Box), EDT (Edit Box), LST
(List Box), PIC (Picture), TXT (Text Box), or WIN (Window). If
the control is a window, any controls within that window are
closed. The CLS statement can be used to end a DDE
conversation. See also @HID (Hide Control) and @SHW
(Show Control).
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Close Control Statement Format
@CLS[,vwh,...,vwh] .
where vwh is the variable that contains the handle of the
control to close. When you close a control handle, the run
sets its corresponding variable to 0. Default = all controls.
Maximum = 80 entries. When you specify a control handle
variable in a CLS statement, the statement closes that
control and sets its corresponding control handle variable to
0. Control handles closed indirectly (their variables are not
speciﬁed in the CLS statement but were contained within a
window that was closed) are not set to 0.

Close Control Example
This example closes the active controls that have control
handles in <tools> and <shapes>.
@cls,<tools>,<shapes> .

CLT and @CLT (Clear Label Table)
Use the Clear Label Table (CLT) function and CLT statement
to clear label tables from run control reports.
When you intend to add or delete lines in a run control
report or modify labels, clearing the label table beforehand
prevents you from having to rebuild the label table after
every change to the run before you can execute it.
The command removes the label table and creates a result.
If you are using the Clear Label Table manual function, if the
run control report does not contain a label table, the
command redisplays the report as a result.
For the Clear Label Table manual function, the run control
report must be on display.

Clear Label Table Control Line Format
CLT

Clear Label Table Statement Format
@CLT,c,d,r[,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Run control report in which to
clear label tables. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

lab

Label to go to if an error
condition exists (such as a
nonexistent report).

Clear Label Table Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Clear Label Table
command:
Remember to save the result, using the Replace Report
or Duplicate Report commands.
Normally you would use the manual CLT function to
process one run control report (enter clt).
If you need to clear label tables in several diﬀerent run
control reports quickly, use a CLT statement in a loop.
For example, you might have one whole drawer or
several drawers containing run control reports you wish
to process.
For an example of processing label tables in several
reports, see BLT and @BLT (Build Label Table).

@CLV (Clear Variables)
Use the Clear Variables (CLV) statement to clear the
deﬁnitions and contents of a set of numbered variables. Use
the CLV statement with care to avoid inadvertently clearing
named variables.
2200 Þ Þ
When you use the CLV statement on a named variable, it
clears only the size, type, and contents of the variable, not
its name.
Ü Ü 2200

Note: The statement uses numbers to deﬁne which
variables to clear; therefore, use only numbered variables
when clearing a partial set of variables.

Clear Variables Statement Format
@CLV[{,o | ,startv,q}] .

Field

Description

startv,q

The set of variables to clear. In
startv, specify the number of
the ﬁrst variable in the set. In
q, specify the number of
consecutive variables in the
set.
If you omit both subﬁelds, the
system clears all the variables
in your run. If you specify only
startv, the system clears all
the variables beginning at
startv.

o

Options subﬁeld. See Clear
Variable Options.

Clear Variable Options
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Option

Description

E

Clears all assigned
environmental session
variables.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

G

Clears all assigned global run
variables.

Clear Variables Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the CLV statement:
Typically, you use a CLV statement to release string
variable space, where only a limited amount is
available. After releasing the string space, you can use it
for other variables.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The CLV statement clears any named variable that is
associated with a numbered variable through the USE
statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
For information on variable arrays, see @LDA (Load
Variable Array) and @CALL (Call Subroutine).

CMP and @CMP (Compare Data)
Use the Compare Data (CMP) function or the CMP statement
to compare data in two reports. It performs a character-tocharacter comparison of speciﬁed ﬁelds in the two reports.
For the Compare Data manual function, one of the reports
must be on display.

Compare Data Control Line Format
CMP report [f,l]

Field

Description

report

Report to compare. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
compare data (lets you
compare data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 in the second
report).

l

Line number of the reports
where the comparison is to
start.

Compare Data Statement Format
@CMP,c1,d1,r1,[lin1],c2,d2,r2[,lin2,q,lab] o cc1
ltyp1,p1 cc2 ltyp2, p2 [vlno1,vcol1,vlno2,vcol2]
.

Field

Description

c1,d1,r1

Report to compare. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

lin1

Number of the line in the ﬁrst
report at which to begin the
comparison. Default = line 2.

c2,d2,r2

Report to compare with the
ﬁrst report.

lin2

Line number in the second
report at which to begin
comparison. Default = line 2.

q

Number of lines to compare.
Default = all.

lab

Label to go to if the statement

detects a diﬀerence.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Compare
Data Options).

cc1

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to
compare in the ﬁrst report.

ltyp1

Line type to compare in the
ﬁrst report. If you specify the
A option, leave this subﬁeld
blank, but enter the comma.

p1

Parameters ﬁeld (see
Compare Data Parameters).

cc2

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to
compare in the second report.

ltyp2

Line type to compare in the
second report. If you specify
the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank, but enter the
comma.

p2

Parameters ﬁeld (see
Compare Data Parameters).

vlno1

Variable to capture the line
number in the ﬁrst report
where the ﬁelds are not
identical.

vcol1

Variable to capture the
column number in the ﬁrst
report where the ﬁelds are not
identical.

vlno2

Variable to capture the line
number in the second report
where the ﬁelds are not
identical.

vcol2

Variable to capture the
column number in the second
report where the ﬁelds are not
identical.

Compare Data Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types. Each
line is compared regardless of
line type.

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

2200 Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.
This option applies only to full
character set (FCS) reports.

Ü Ü 2200

Compare Data Parameters
Parameter Description

1 - 25

Speciﬁes the sequence of
ﬁelds to compare, beginning
with 1. In the function mask,
type each parameter in the
ﬁrst column of the ﬁeld.
Erase or add asterisks in the
function mask, if necessary,
to make the ﬁelds you are
comparing the same size.
In the CMP statement, the
size of ﬁelds you specify for
comparison in the cc1 and
cc2 subﬁelds must be the
same.
Maximum ﬁeld size = 997
characters.

Compare Data Outcomes
If the manual CMP function detects a diﬀerence, it
redisplays the report you had on display with the cursor
located in the column where the diﬀerence occurs. A
message states that a diﬀerence was found.
Otherwise, a message states that the ﬁelds are identical.
2200 Þ Þ
Press F1 to display each subsequent diﬀerence.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
To display other occurrences, you must execute CMP again
and specify a starting line number greater than the line
number where the previous ﬁnd happened.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Executing the statement causes the following actions to
occur:
If the statement detects a diﬀerence, it loads variables
with line numbers and column numbers of the reports
where it detected the diﬀerence and gives control to the
label.
If you do not use a label or the statement does not
detect a diﬀerence, the run continues at the next
statement.

Compare Data Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Compare Data
command:
To continue comparing ﬁelds in the report, restart the
Compare Data command. In the Compare Data request,
include the line number at which the comparison should
start.
Since you can compare ﬁelds that are up to 997
characters wide, you can compare whole lines of data.
(Use the CMP statement in a run to compare lines that
extend beyond your screen display.)
Add asterisks in the function mask to make a solid line
of asterisks. Use the A option if you want to process all
line types.
You cannot compare non-displayable reports (for
example, encrypted, binary, and runtime).

Compare Data Example
This example compares data in the Product Type and
Product Cost ﬁelds in reports 2B and 1C. To display this
function mask, ﬁrst display 2b, then enter cmp 1c: Function
Mask
COMPARE
* Product . Sub .Produc. Whole . Retail . Sales .
* Type . Key . Cost . Sale$ . $$$$ .Commiss.
*=========.=====.======.=======.========.=======.
********* ***** ****** ******* ******** *******
1 2
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.====.
** ****** ** ********* ****** ****** ***** ****
1 2
Equivalent Statement
@cmp,'report1c',,'report2b' '' 2-9,18-6 |,1,2 159,\
32-6 |,1,2 <line1>i4,<col1>i3,<line2>i4,<col2>i3
.

@CMU (Commit Updates)
Use the Commit Updates (CMU) statement to save all
updates made to all reports for which updates were deferred
by a preceding Defer Updates (DFU) statement.
The reports for which you want to save updates must be
speciﬁed in a preceding DFU statement.

Commit Updates Statement Format
@CMU .

Commit Updates Outcome
The CMU statement saves all the updates made to all
reports for which updates were deferred by the preceding
DFU statement.
It also releases update control for the reports speciﬁed by
the preceding DFU statement.

CNT and @CNT (Count)
Use the Count (CNT) function or CNT statement to analyze
and summarize data. Based on key ﬁelds, the command
computes subtotals, percentages, averages, entry counts,
and more.
You can use the Iterative Count (ICNT) run to save and reuse
parameters and options and to create CNT statements. The
ICNT run helps make the CNT function easier to learn and
use because you do not need to retype parameters and
options each time you try something diﬀerent.
The Count command has several advantages over other
commands. These include the ability to
Subtotal ﬁelds without having to sort them ﬁrst.
Perform calculations on a single report or across an
entire drawer.
Process more than one distinctly diﬀerent set of
analyses with one function request.
Reduce processing time in two ways. You can replace a
sequence of two or more other commands with the
Count command. The command scans the report in one
pass, no matter how many analyses you request.
With the Count command, you can
Count the number of unique entries in key ﬁelds.
Calculate the subtotal, average, minimum, or maximum
value for each unique key.

Compute the percentage of entries in a certain key of
the total number of entries.
Compute the percentage that each subtotal ﬁeld is of
the grand total.
Numerically round each requested ﬁeld separately.
Perform calculations based on numeric, date, or time
intervals.
Display only speciﬁed ﬁelds in the result.

Count Control Line Format
CNT [report f]

Field

Description

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
process data (lets you process
data in ﬁelds beyond column
80 if those columns are not
already on display).

Count Statement Format
@CNT,c,d[,r] o cc ltyp,p [vrslts] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report or drawer to process.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Count
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to process.

ltyp

Line type to process. If you
specify the A option, you can
leave this subﬁeld blank, but
enter the comma.

p

Parameters ﬁeld (see Count
Parameters).

vrslts

Variable to capture the
number of lines for the

corresponding output result.
Up to eight variables can be
returned by the statement.

Count Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.

B

Extends the boundaries for
scaling intervals to the
minimum and maximum
values deﬁned in the scaling
option. Since the B option
assumes the Z option, every
interval between those
boundaries is displayed, even
intervals with no entries. Any
key ﬁelds that are scaled must
be the most signiﬁcant (the
lowest numbered keys) when
the B option is speciﬁed. See
Extending Intervals to the
Boundaries.

C

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters. Default = the system
processes and displays the
keys as uppercase.

D[n]

Processes only those keys that

occur n or more times, where
n is an integer greater than 1.
Default = 2.

E

Extracts result ﬁelds from the
last occurrence of each unique
key. Default = ﬁrst occurrence.

F

Extracts result ﬁelds from the
ﬁrst occurrence of each unique
key. When using a minimum
(<) or maximum (>) operator,
default = minimum or
maximum ﬁeld.

H

Displays only the ﬁrst set of
headings in the result when
you process more than one set
of parameters in a single
function request. See Omitting
Headings in Multiple Analyses.

I

Includes out-of-range keys.
Substitutes the minimum or
maximum boundary value,
when applicable, for an out-ofrange key. The key ﬁeld is then
considered valid, and all its
values are counted. Default =
the line is discarded for values
outside the boundaries.

J(x)

Justiﬁes numeric result values
in ﬁelds to x (x is one of the
values listed in the following
list). Leading zeros appear to
the left of a value,
nonsigniﬁcant zeros to the
right. Neither aﬀects the
value. The individual J
parameters override the J on
the option line.
C - eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros. Inserts
commas every three digits in
the integer portion of the
result. Right-justiﬁes values.
L - eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas. Left-justiﬁes values.
R - eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas. Right-justiﬁes values.
X - eliminates leading zeros
and commas, left-justiﬁes
values, and ﬁlls ﬁelds with
nonsigniﬁcant zeros.
Z - eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas, right-justiﬁes values,

and ﬁlls leading spaces with
zeros.

Nn

Substitutes invalid numeric
data with the value n. Default
= 0.

O

Omits data lines from the
result. Only heading lines,
grand total summary, and
warning messages are
included in the output result.
The T and W options are
automatically implied when
the O option is speciﬁed.

P

Packs the result by removing
all nonreferenced ﬁelds from
the result and reorders the
ﬁelds, from left to right,
according to this hierarchy: 1.
Key ﬁelds in numeric order
2. Extraction ﬁelds in
alphabetical order
3. Computational ﬁelds
(subtotals, averages, and so
on)
4. Minimum ﬁelds
5. Maximum ﬁelds
6. Entry counts
7. Percentages

For an example using the P
option, see Vertical Cumulation
and Selecting Fields. For an
example of how to reorder
ﬁelds using the P option, see
Reordering Speciﬁed Result
Fields.

Rx-y

Processes a range of reports
from x through y.

Rx,y

Processes reports x and y. The
reports are processed in the
order you specify.

Rx-y,z

Processes reports x through y
and also z. The reports are
processed in the order you
specify.

Sn

Creates intervals or scales in
key ﬁelds.
Sn(intv[/min/max]) (scales
numeric values)
SnDf(intv[/min/max/strt])
(scales dates)
SnTf(intv[/min/max/strt])
(scales times) n is the number
of the key ﬁeld to scale (1
through 9).

f is the date or time format.
See Date Format Numbers and
Time Format Numbers.
intv is the interval size.
Here are the date intervals:
nD n = number of days
(default)
nW n = number of weeks
nM n = number of months
nY n = number of years
Here are the time intervals:
nS n = number of seconds
nM n = number of months
nH n = number of hours

min is the minimum value at
which to begin scaling.
max is the maximum value at
which to end scaling.
strt is the starting value. Must
be same date format as the
key ﬁeld or must be a special
value listed here. The strt
value depends on the intv
value.
If intv is nW, the strt value is a
three-character day name, for
example, SUN
If intv is nM, the strt value is a
three-character month name,

for example, MAY
If intv is nY, the strt value is a
Two- or four-digit year, for
example, 96 or 1996
If intv is nS,nM,nH, the strt
value is the same format as
key ﬁeld.
For example, if you create
weekly intervals and choose a
starting point of WED, weeks
begin on Wednesday and end
on Tuesday. Using a day of the
week for a date scaling start
value is useful only for weekly
intervals.
Defaults = January 1, 1944 (for
year or month scaling);
January 2, 1944, Sunday (for
day or week scaling). See
Scaling¾Arranging Keys into
Fixed-Size Intervals.

T

Displays total, overall
summary values for each
computed ﬁeld at the end of
each result. For example,
subtotaling shows a grand
total; averaging shows a grand
average; entry counting shows
total entries; minimum and
maximum operators show the

smallest or largest values in
the ﬁeld. You can also
calculate the overall standard
deviation of values in a ﬁeld.

U

Displays the upper limit of the
interval for scaled results
rather than the lower limit.

V

Does not count lines with
invalid numeric, date, or time
ﬁelds. Default = 0 for numeric
operations; January 1, 1944,
for date calculations; or
00:00:00 for time operations.

W

Adds messages at the end of
the result showing the number
of lines skipped due to invalid
key or data ﬁelds.

Z

Displays all intervals in the
result, even those with no
entries, creating a scale with
no gaps. Any key ﬁelds that
are scaled must be the most
signiﬁcant (the lowest
numbered keys) when the Z
option is speciﬁed. Use this
option only with the S option.

*

Flags invalid subtotals,
averages, standard deviations,
maximums, and minimums if
invalid data were used in the
calculations.

Count Parameters
Maximum number of individual parameters allowed = 63.

Parameter Description

1-9

Speciﬁes key ﬁelds (at least
one key ﬁeld is required).

N

Speciﬁes numeric key ﬁelds
(1N).

Df

Speciﬁes date key ﬁelds
(see the Sn Option for
formats). Example: 1D0,
speciﬁes key ﬁeld 1 in date
format 0.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS
ÞÞ
f = 0-20 to specify date
format.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX
OS
2200 Þ Þ
f = 0-21 to specify date
format.

Ü Ü 2200

Tf

Speciﬁes time key ﬁelds.
(See the Sn Option for
formats.) f = 0-5 to specify
time format. Example: 2T5,
speciﬁes key ﬁeld 2 in time
format 5.

A-Z

Speciﬁes label ﬁelds. The
value displayed is the ﬁrst
value associated with the
key in the key ﬁeld. Label
ﬁelds are especially useful
with the P (Pack) option.

J ... x

Justiﬁes numeric result
value to x. J must be in the
ﬁrst column and the
justiﬁcation option letter x
must be the ﬁrst character
in the ﬁeld to which it
applies. The individual J
parameters override the J on
the option line. Both J and R
parameter lines can be used
for a ﬁeld; the order does
not matter.

R ... n

Rounds number to the
nearest n. R must be in the

ﬁrst column of a line on the
mask (using the CNT
statement, designate R as
the line type). The rounding
parameter (n) must be in
the appropriate ﬁeld. See
Rounding. Both J and R
parameter lines can be used
for a ﬁeld; the order does
not matter. The range for n
is .0000000000000001 to
100000000000.
For example, if you type
r.001, the values are
rounded to the nearest
thousandth. If you type
r10000, the values are
rounded to the nearest 10
thousand.
Examples:
With option r.001, the value
8.3224 gives the value of
8.322
With option r.001, the value
8.7719 gives the value of
8.772
With option r10000, the
value 12345 gives the value
of 1
With option r10000, the
value 16345 gives the value
of 2

For information on operators you can use with Count, see
Count Operators.

Count Outcomes
Executing the Count command causes the following actions
to occur:
The system creates a result containing the completed
calculations. If you specify more than one set of
parameters, the result contains a result for each set of
parameters, separated by heading lines and an .EJECT
command.
2200 Þ Þ
If there is no period line after the date line, the Count
command adds one to ensure there is always a title line
in the result.
Ü Ü 2200
The result contains a one-line summary of each unique
key.
The result is sorted by key ﬁelds in ascending order.
All nonreferenced ﬁelds are ﬁlled with spaces.
When an invalid key is encountered in the input report,
the entire line is omitted from calculations. When invalid
data is encountered in a nonkey ﬁeld, the value is
considered to be zero for numeric operations; January 1,
1944, for date calculations; or 00:00:00 for time
operations.
In addition to the outcome just described, up to eight
variables can be returned by the CNT statement. Each

variable contains the number of data lines for the
corresponding output result.

@CNT Reserved Words
STAT2$ contains the number of lines ignored in all results for
invalid or out-of-range key conditions. STAT3$ contains the
number of lines ignored in all results for invalid numeric,
date, or time values. The value is meaningful only if you
speciﬁed the V option.

How to Use the Count Command
The Count command groups identical values in the key ﬁeld
together and performs calculations for each group, or key.
Specify key ﬁelds by placing parameters, 1 through 9, in the
appropriate ﬁelds in the function mask. You must specify at
least one key ﬁeld for each set of parameters. The Count
command allows a maximum of 63 individual parameters.
Specify ﬁelds to operate on by placing operators in
appropriate ﬁelds in the function mask. For example, put an
equal sign (=) in the ﬁeld you want to load with the number
of times a unique key value occurs.

Key Field Parameters
Key ﬁelds contain data to be grouped together. You perform
calculations on ﬁelds based on the key parameters you
enter in the key ﬁeld.
Key ﬁeld parameters are the numbers 1 through 9, where 1
is the most signiﬁcant. You can begin with a number other
than 1. Always specify at least one key ﬁeld for each set of
parameters. The following paragraphs describe the types of
key ﬁelds.
Alphanumeric Key Fields
Use alphanumeric key ﬁelds to specify character data. Enter
a single digit, 1 through 9. By default, uppercase and
lowercase characters are considered equal.
Numeric Key Fields
Use numeric key ﬁelds to specify numeric ﬁelds. Enter a key
number and the parameter N (for example, 1N). Numbers
can be left justiﬁed or right justiﬁed.
Date and Time Key Fields
Use date and time key ﬁelds to specify date or time ﬁelds.
Use a key number, the parameter D or T, and a date or time
format number. For example, 1T0 indicates a key ﬁeld
containing times in format 0, HH:MM:SS. Following are the
date and time format numbers.
Date Format Numbers
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between 1944
and 2043.

Four-digit year dates must represent dates between 1600
and 2299.
One-digit year dates must represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from the current date. For
example, 1 January 1996 through 31 December 2005 (if the
current day was 1 November 2000).

Number Format

0

YMMDD

1

YYMMDD

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

2

DD MMM YY

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

2

DD MMM YY (Spaces
are optional)

Ü Ü 2200

3

YDDD

4

YYDDD

5

DDMMYY

6

MM/DD/YY

7

MONTH DD, YYYY

8

MMDDYY

9

DD/MM/YY

YYYYMMDD

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

12

DD MMM YYYY

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

2200 Þ Þ

12

DD MMM YYYY
(Spaces are optional)

21

Ü Ü 2200

16

MM/DD/YYYY

Time Format Numbers
2200 Þ Þ

For elapsed time formats T4 and T5, valid times are
between -9544371:46:07 and 9544371:46:07.
Ü Ü 2200

between -9544371:59:59 and 9544371:59:59.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Number Format

1

4

* Hours (H) can be from one to seven digits. If negative
values are used, the minus sign can be an eighth character.

Additional Key Field Parameters
The Count command provides additional key ﬁeld
parameters that group data based on criteria other than the
contents of the key ﬁeld. For more information, see
Advanced User.

Extraction Parameters
Ordinarily, only key ﬁelds and ﬁelds involved in operations
are included in a Count result. If you use extraction
parameters, however, you can include the contents of other
ﬁelds as well. An extraction parameter is a single letter
placed in a ﬁeld, called an extraction label. Do not label
more than one ﬁeld with the same letter. By default,
extraction ﬁelds are loaded from the report line where each
unique key occurs the ﬁrst time.
You can use Count operators with extraction labels (for
example, +L, where L is an extraction label); however, the
following operators are not available for use with extraction
labels:

-

Delete ﬁeld

%

Percentage

%+

Cumulative
percentage

=

Entry count

=+

Cumulative count

See Selecting Fields for Result Values and Performing
Multiple Operations on a Field for more uses of extraction
parameters.

Exclusion Parameter
The exclusion parameter is a minus sign ( - ) following the
extraction parameter and indicates that the ﬁeld should be
excluded from the result. Use this operator only with labeled
ﬁelds. For Example, A- labels a ﬁeld as A, but does not
include the contents of the ﬁeld in the result.

Count Operators
Operator Description

=

Entry count: counts the
number of entries for each
unique key.

=+

Cumulative count: displays
cumulative count of entries.

+

Subtotal: subtotals values for
each unique key in the key
ﬁeld.

++

Cumulative subtotal: displays
cumulative subtotals in the
key ﬁeld.

%

Percentage: provides the
entry counts for unique keys
expressed as a percentage of
the total entries in the ﬁeld.

%+

Cumulative percentage:
displays cumulative entry

counts expressed as a
percentage of total entries in
the ﬁeld.

+%

Subtotal percentage:
expresses the subtotal for
each unique key as a
percentage of the grand total
value of the ﬁeld. See
Computing Percentages of
Grand Totals.

+%+

Cumulative subtotal
percentage: displays
cumulative subtotals
expressed as a percentage of
the grand total value of the
ﬁeld.

/

Average: computes the
average by subtotaling values
and dividing by the number of
entries.

-

Delete ﬁeld: excludes values
in a ﬁeld from a result.

< or <Dn
or <Tn

Minimum: computes
minimum value for each
unique key. For date or time

entries, use <Dn or<Tn; n is
the date or time format. See
Date and Time Key Fields. For
example, <D1 ﬁnds the
earliest date for each key (in
format number 1, YYMMDD).

> or >Dn
or >Tn

Maximum: computes
maximum value for each
unique key. For date or time
entries, use >Dn or >Tn; n is
the date or time format. See
Date and Time Key Fields. For
example, >T0 ﬁnds the latest
time for each unique key (in
format 0, HH:MM:SS).

!- or !+

Standard deviation: computes
standard deviation for a
numeric ﬁeld. Following are
the operators and formulas
used in calculating standard
deviation (n = entry count,
and x = numeric ﬁeld).
For sample (!-):
SQRT((SUM(x**2)/(n-1))(SUM(x)*SUM(x)/(n*(n-1))))
For population (!+):
SQRT((SUM(x**2)/(n))(AVG(x)*AVG(x)))

!!- or !!+

Variance: computes the
variance for a numeric ﬁeld.
Following are the operators
and formulas used in
calculating variance: n =
entry count and x = numeric
ﬁeld.
For sample (!!-):
((SUM(x**2)/(n-1))(SUM(x)*SUM(x)/(n*(n-1))) For
population (!!+):
((SUM(x**2)/(n))(AVG(x)*AVG(x))

Additional Operators
The :R, :L, and :M operators load ﬁelds with report numbers,
line numbers, or analysis numbers, respectively, which they
calculate at the time of execution.

Operator

Description

Report
number
(:R)

Places the input report
number into the ﬁeld. Use
the :R operator only when
processing either an entire
drawer or a range of reports.

Line
number
(:L)

Places the input line number
into the ﬁeld. Use the :L
operator when you are
processing just one report, or
use it along with the :R
parameter when processing
an entire drawer or a range
of reports.

Multiple
analyses
number
(:M)

Places the corresponding
analysis number into the
ﬁeld. If you use more than
one set of parameters
(perform more than one
analysis) in a function
request, the command
creates a single result
containing output from all of
the analyses.
The :M operator loads the
speciﬁed ﬁeld with the
number of the analysis that
is the source of the output
line. For example, the
command loads output lines
created by the ﬁrst set of
parameters with a value of
one.

Scaling¾Arranging Keys into Fixed-Size
Intervals
Scaling creates intervals of values within the key ﬁeld. For
example, you can scale the values in a date key ﬁeld to
perform calculations based on weekly intervals. To scale a
key ﬁeld, specify the appropriate scaling (S) option. See
Scaling Types.

Scaling Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when scaling key ﬁelds:
Each scaled key ﬁeld must be deﬁned by its own scaling
option.
Each interval shown in the result has one output line
associated with it.
By default, the lower value in the interval is displayed.
By default, only intervals containing values are included.

Scaling Types
You can scale three types of data: numbers, dates, and
times. Each scaling type uses a diﬀerent format for the S
option. The topics that follow describe each scaling type and
option format.
Use numeric scaling to group values together within the
same numeric interval and treat them as a single key.
Sn(intv[/min/max])

Field

Description

S

Options letter.

n

Number of the key ﬁeld to be
scaled.

intv

Size of the interval

min

Minimum scaled value. Key
ﬁeld values smaller than this
value are ignored.

max

Maximum scaled value. Key
ﬁeld values larger than this
value are ignored.

Date Scaling
By scaling date keys, you group dates together within the
same interval and treat them as a single key.
SnDf(intv[/min/max/strt])

Field

Description

S

Options letter.

n

Number of the key ﬁeld to be
scaled.

D

Indicator of date scaling.

f

Number indicating the date
format of the data in the key
ﬁeld. See Date Format
Numbers.

intv

Size of the interval. This ﬁeld
consists of a positive integer
number (n) and a letter
describing the type of interval.
Formats of the ﬁeld follow: nD
where n = number of days
(default)

nW where n = number of
weeks
nM where n = number of
months
nY where n = number of years
For example, 2M creates an
interval size of two months.

min

Minimum scaled date in the
same date format as the data
in the key ﬁeld. Earlier dates
are ignored.

max

Maximum scaled date in the
same date format as the data
in the key ﬁeld. Later dates
are ignored.

strt

Interval starting date. Must be
same date format as the key
ﬁeld or must be a special
value listed here. The strt
value depends on the intv
value.
If intv is nW, the strt value is a
three-character day name, for
example, SUN.
If intv is nM, the strt value is a
three-character month name,
for example, MAY.

If intv is nY the strt value is a
two- or four-digit year, for
example, 96 or 1996.
For example, if you create
weekly intervals and choose a
starting point of WED, weeks
begin on Wednesday and end
on Tuesday. Using a day of the
week for a date scaling start
value is useful only for weekly
intervals.
Defaults = January 1, 1944,
(for year or month scaling);
January 2, 1944, Sunday (for
day or week scaling).

Time Scaling
By scaling time keys, you group times together within the
same interval and treat them as a single key.
SnTf(intv[/min/max/strt])

Field

Description

S

Option letter.

n

Number of the key ﬁeld to be
scaled.

T

Indicator of time scaling.

f

Number indicating the time
format of the data in the key
ﬁeld. See Time Format
Numbers.

intv

Size of the interval. This ﬁeld
consists of a positive integer
number (n) and a value
describing the type of interval.
Formats of the ﬁeld follow: nS
where n = number of seconds
(default)

nM where n = number of
minutes
nH where n = number of hours
For example, 2M creates an
interval size of two minutes.

min

Minimum scaled time in the
same time format as the data
in the key ﬁeld. Times in the
key ﬁeld that are earlier than
this time are ignored.

max

Maximum scaled time in the
same time format as the data
in the key ﬁeld. Later times
are ignored.

strt

Starting time for intervals. This
ﬁeld must be in the same time
format as the data in the key
ﬁeld. Default = 00:00:00.

hours that the systems were down, the cause code, and the
cost of the downtime.

Counting Keys and Calculating a Percentage
This example counts the times each unique key exists in the
key ﬁeld and calculates the percentage of total downtime
events for each location.
Function Mask
*Location.Date .Time .Down. . . .Count. %
*========.======.======.====. . . .=====.=====
1 = %
Equivalent Statement

@cnt,'report5a' '' 2-8,60-5,66-5 |,1,=,% .
Parameters Used

1

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

=

Identiﬁes where to place the
calculation of the number of times
a city had downtime.

%

Identiﬁes where to place the
percentage of times the city had a
downtime event.

Result
*Location.Date .Time .Down. . . .Count. % .
*========.======.======.====. . . .=====.=====.
BOSTON 4 44.4
CHICAGO 4 44.4
NEW YORK 1 11.1

Packs the result so only requested
ﬁelds are displayed in the result.

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

=+

Identiﬁes where to place the
cumulation of the number of times
a city had downtime.

the city had a downtime event.

example builds on the previous example of subtotaling and

Multiple Analyses in a Single Pass
Using more than one set of parameters (performing more
than one analysis) in a single function request creates a
single result containing output from each analysis.

Note: You can specify eight sets of parameters and
analyses.
Function Mask
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost . . . .
*========. . . .====.======.==========.=========.
. . .
1 a/ +a
= 1
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'report5a' '' 2-8,27-4,32-6,50-9
|,1,a'/',,+a/ |,=,,1 .
First set of parameters used:

1

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

a/

Labels the Down ﬁeld as A,
calculates the average hours
down for each location, and places
the values in the Down ﬁeld.

+a

Calculates the total number of
hours down for each location and
places the values in the Cost ﬁeld.

Second set of parameters used:

=

Counts the number of times each
cause occurred. In other words,
counts the number of times each
unique key exists in the key ﬁeld.

1

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

Result
This is the result of the previous calculation. Note that there
is an .EJECT command between each analysis output.
*Location. Date . Time .Down. Cause. Value . Cost
.Count. %
*========.======.========.====.======.==========.

.EJECT

NEWUSER

Scaling a Numeric Key Field

This example demonstrates how to specify intervals and
group data within them, and computes the average cost for
each interval.
Function Mask
s1(.5/1/4)
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .
*========. . . .====.======.==========.=========.
1 /
r 1
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'report5a' s1(.5/1/4) 27-4,50-9 |,1,'/'/r,,1
.
Scaling Option

s1

Creates intervals based on the
Down ﬁeld.

.5

Deﬁnes an interval size of .5.

/1/4

Deﬁnes the minimum and
maximum values to process (1
and 4, respectively).

Extending Intervals to the Boundaries
Using scaling with the B option, you can include numeric
time and date intervals that contain no occurrences within
the boundaries you speciﬁed. This option is useful to identify
intervals where data may be missing or to identify patterns
of occurrences.
Function Mask
s1(1/0/5)b
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .
*========. . . .====.======.==========.=========.
1 +
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'report5a' s1(1/0/5)b 27-4,50-9 |,1,+ .
S Option

s1

Creates intervals in the key ﬁeld 1.

1

Sets the interval size at 1.

0

Begins the ﬁrst interval at 0.

5

Begins the last interval at 5.

B Option

b

Extends the intervals to the limits
as set by the scaling option (0 and
5 in this example). The B option
assumes the Z option so all
intervals are displayed in the
result, even those with no entries.

Parameters Used

1

Speciﬁes the Down ﬁeld as key
ﬁeld 1.

+

Calculates subtotal for each
unique key. In other words, ﬁnds
the cost per interval.

Result
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .
*========. . . .====.======.==========.=========.
0 124
1 3810
2 550
3 0
4 7500
5 0

Note: If the B option had not been included, the intervals
that contained no entries (3 and 5) in the input result would
have been omitted from the result.

Computing Total Summarizations

This example uses the T option to calculate a total for each
computed ﬁeld. Subtotaling shows a grand total; averaging
shows a grand average; entry counting shows total entries;
minimum and maximum operators show the smallest or
largest values in the ﬁeld.
Function Mask
t
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .Count.
*========. . .
.====.======.==========.=========.=====.
1 +% = >
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'report5a' t 2-8,27-4,32-6,50-9 |,1,+%,=,> .
T Option

t

Displays a total for each computed
ﬁeld.

Parameters Used

1

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

+%

Speciﬁes subtotal percentage.

=

Speciﬁes an entry count.

>

Speciﬁes the maximum value.

Result
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .Count.
*========. . .
.====.======.==========.=========.=====.
BOSTON 19. 4 900
CHICAGO 73. 4 7500
NEW YORK 9. 1 10
* ---- ------ --------* 100. 9 7500
.
.OUTPUT LINES = 3
..... END REPORT .....
Interpretation
The Chicago location accounted for 73 percent of the total
downtime for all locations. There were a total of 9 downtime
events. The maximum cost in Boston was $900 and in New
York was $10. The maximum cost at any location was in
Chicago at $7500. Three locations were included in the
report.
Reordering Speciﬁed Result Fields
In addition to including in the result only the ﬁelds for which
you speciﬁed a parameter, the P option also reorders the
ﬁelds according to a preset hierarchy:
1. Key ﬁelds
2. Extraction ﬁelds

3. Computational ﬁelds
4. Minimum ﬁelds
5. Maximum ﬁelds
6. Entry counts
7. Percentages
Any ﬁelds without a letter or number specifying the priority
in which they are to be displayed, are displayed as the last
ﬁelds in the output. To maintain order for the displayed
output, use the P option and specify the order of the key
ﬁelds with a number and precede the order of the other
parameter ﬁelds with a letter. For example, use "A+" rather
than "+."
Function Mask
p
*Location. . . .Down. Cause. Value . Cost .Count.
*========. . .
.====.======.==========.=========.=====.
1 b+ /b /a a+
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'report5a' p 2-8,27-4,32-6,39-10,50-9
|,1,b+,'/'b,'/'a,a+ .
P Option

p

Packs the result so that only
requested ﬁelds are displayed in

the result and reorders the ﬁelds
according to the preset hierarchy.

Parameters Used

1

Indicates the key ﬁeld.

b+

Labels the Down ﬁeld as B and
subtotals the downtime for each
key in the key ﬁeld.

/b

Calculates the average value for
each unique key in the ﬁeld
labeled B.

/a

Calculates the average value for
each unique key in the ﬁeld
labeled A.

a+

Labels the Cost ﬁeld as A and
subtotals the cost for each key in
the key ﬁeld.

Result
*Location. Cost . Value .Down. Cause
*========.=========.==========.====.======

BOSTON 1004 251 2.10 0.5250
CHICAGO 10970 2742.50000 8.20 2.0500
NEW YORK 10 10 1 1
Advanced User
Two additional key ﬁeld parameters are available for
specialized processing:
Report key parameter (R)
Line number key parameter (L)
Though these parameters look a lot like the :R and :L
operators, the R and L parameters are much more powerful
and work quite diﬀerently because they specify subtotaling
by report and by line.
The following descriptions outline possible uses for each of
these parameters and include a brief explanation of how to
set up your database to take advantage of them.
Report Key
Use report key parameters (the key number followed by the
letter R, such as 1R) only when processing either an entire
drawer or a range of reports.
Use the R parameter to perform calculations and obtain a
resulting value based on each whole report, regardless of
key ﬁeld changes within the reports. Each report is
considered the key, not the data within the ﬁelds of the
reports.
Enter this parameter in any ﬁeld that can hold the value of
the highest report number and enter other operators as
usual. The command groups the resulting values by report.

Setting Up the Database for Using Report Keys
The data in the ﬁelds of one report should be logically
related to the same ﬁelds in the other reports.
An example of this would be a set of reports with data for
one month in one report, the data for the next month in
another report, and so on.
For example, report 2B contains sales data, along with other
data, for all products for the month of January 1996:
.January 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
running 100.00
cross trainer 200.00
aerobics 300.00
Report 3B contains sales data, along with other data, for all
products for February 1996:
.February 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
running 111.11
cross trainer 222.22
aerobics 333.33
The operators and parameters in the following function
mask calculate sales subtotals by report (not by content of
the ﬁeld) and place the report number in the ﬁeld
designated 1R. The R option speciﬁes the reports to process;
the R parameter rounds the result to the nearest hundredth.
Function Mask

r2,3
*January, 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
************* ******
1R +
r .01
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'drawerb' r2,3 2-13,16-6 |,1R,+/r,,.01 .
Result
January, 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
2 600.00 (January figures--report 2B)
3 666.66 (February figures--report 3B)
Line Number Key
Use line number parameters (the key number followed by
the letter L) only when processing either an entire drawer or
a range of reports.
Use the L parameter to perform calculations and obtain a
resulting value based on each line number, regardless of
key ﬁeld changes within the reports. Each line number is
considered the key, not the data within the ﬁelds of the
reports.
Setting Up the Database for Using Line Number Keys
The database must be set up so that each line number in a
report is directly associated with the same line number in
the other reports in the drawer. Also, the data in the ﬁelds of

one report must be logically related to the same ﬁelds in the
other reports.
An example of this would be a set of reports with data for
one month in one report, the data for the next month in
another report, and so on.
For example, Report 2B contains sales data, along with
other data, for all products for the month of January 1996: 2
January 1996
3 *Shoe Products.Sales . ...
4 *=============.======.
5 running 100.00
6 cross trainer 200.00
7 aerobics 300.00
Report 3B contains sales data, along with other data, for all
products for February 1996, in the same order as 2B: 2
.February 1996
3 *Shoe Products.Sales . ...
4 *=============.======.
5 running 111.11
6 cross trainer 222.22
7 aerobics 333.33

Note: In the preceding example, speciﬁc products exist on
the same line number in report 3B as they are do in report
2B.
The operators and parameters in the following function
mask calculate sales subtotals by line number (not by
content of the ﬁeld) and place the line number in the ﬁeld
designated 1L. The R option speciﬁes the reports to process.
This procedure works only when your database is set up as
just described.
Function Mask

r2,3
*January, 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
************* ******
1L +
Equivalent Statement
@cnt,'drawerb' r2,3 2-13,16-6 |,1L,+ .
Result
January, 1996
*Shoe Products.Sales . ...
*=============.======.
5 211.11 (running shoe figures)
6 422.22 (cross trainer shoe figures)
7 633.33 (aerobics shoe figures)

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

CS (Cabinet Switch)
See C, CS, and @CAB (Cabinet Switch) for more information.

@CSR (Clear Subroutine)
Use the Clear Subroutine (CSR) statement in an external
subroutine to clear the return path to the calling run so that
a Run Subroutine (RSR) statement can call another external
subroutine. (Once you use a CSR statement, you can no
longer return to the calling run.)
Use a CSR statement in conjunction with the corresponding
RSR statement.

Clear Subroutine Statement Format
@CSR .

Clear Subroutine Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the CSR statement:
You must use a CSR statement in the external
subroutine before calling another external subroutine.
You can also use it instead of an Exit Subroutine (ESR)
statement if you do not want to return to the calling run.
When registering your run, inform the administrator of
any other run control reports containing external
subroutines.

CUT
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Cut command to transfer sections of data from one
report to another report, or within the same report.
The Cut command extracts data from a report or result and
enables you to paste the data into the same report or into a
diﬀerent report, even if the report is located in another
cabinet or drawer. You can also move the data in the report
or delete it from the report. The Cut command creates a
result, so you must use the REP command to make the
changes permanent in the original report.

Note: The Cut command applies if the Word Processing
feature is available.

Cut Control Line Format
CUT [report]
PASTE [report]
PASTE C npsw report

Field

Description

report

Report to cut data from or to
paste data into.

npsw

Cabinet number and cabinet
password.

Cut Control Line Format Details
CUT
Displays the input screen.
CUT report
Was a report on display?
Yes: Assumes the report you request is the CUT RID and the
report on display is the PASTE RID-after cutting, simply enter
paste.
No: Places you in cut control in the report requested.
PASTE (while under cut control)

Redisplays the CUT RID (the report you were cutting from),
now labeled as PASTE RID, ready to be pasted into.
PASTE report
Displays the report to paste into.
PASTE C npsw report Displays the report in
another cabinet to paste into.

Cut Guidelines
Once you identify a report to cut, you are under cut control
and can enter only Cut directives; therefore LINE and RL are
the only positions on the control line you may use.
To get out of cut control at any time, press Exit WP.
If you call the Cut command while in interactive word
processing and press Exit WP, you are returned to
interactive word processing at the line that was on display
when you called the Cut command.

Cut Directives
Directive

Description

CLR

Clears out any previous cuts.

DEL

Deletes cut data from the
report.
To delete the cut data from
the report, enter del.
Remember that the CUT
command creates a result,
so the report being deleted
from is not actually being
updated.

LOOK

Displays the data cut from
the report.
To display a result containing
just the cut data, enter look.
With the LOOK RESULT on
display, you can perform
one of the following
operations: DEL-delete the
cut data
CUT-cut more data

PASTE-place the data into
another report PREP,x-use
the word processing PREP
directive CHG-use the
Change (CHG) command
(control line format with
parameters speciﬁed only)
WC-use the Word Change
(WC) command (control line
format with parameters
speciﬁed only) Exit WP keyBuild a standard result

LOOKX

Displays the data cut from
the report and exits cut
control.
To exit cut control
automatically when you
request the display of the
cut data, enter lookx. You
do not need to press Exit
WP before using another
command.

MOVE

Copies the cut data to
another portion of the report
and then deletes the
information from where it
was cut.
To place cut data in another
part of the same report and

remove it from the original
location
1. Enter move
2. Move the cursor just
beyond the point where
you want to move the
data and press
Transmit
The cut data is copied to the
new area and removed from
the old area. Remember that
the Cut command creates a
result, so the report being
manipulated is not actually
being updated.
To move data to another
report
1. Paste the data into the
other report as
described under PASTE
2. Enter rep to make the
paste permanent
3. Enter del to delete the
cut data from the
original report
4. Enter rep to make the
deletion permanent in
the original report

PASTE

Places cut data into a report.
To paste cut data in the
same report
1. Type paste and press
Transmit
2. Move the cursor just
beyond the point where
you want to paste the
data and press
Transmit
Remember that the Cut
command creates a result,
so the report being pasted is
not being updated. To create
a permanent copy of the
updated report, perform one
of the following operations:
Type rep and press
Transmit (replaces the
PASTE RID with the
PASTE RESULT-the PASTE
report itself is now
updated).
Press Exit WP to exit
cut control. Use the
Replace Report or
Duplicate Report
commands to save the
result.

PREP,x

Prepares LOOK result for
word processing (WP),
inserting control character x
on each line.

RECUT

Clears the most recent cut.
To clear the most recent cut,
enter recut. This directive is
similar to the CLR directive,
except that it clears only the
most recently marked cut.
Enter recut again to clear
the next most recent cut,
and so on.

REPX

Replaces report and exits
cut control.
To exit cut control
automatically when you
request that the report be
replaced, enter repx. You do
not need to press Exit WP
before using another
command.

Other Commands Allowed Under Cut Control
You can also use the following manual functions while under
cut control:
Locate and Change (CHG) control line format with
parameters speciﬁed only, and valid only on LOOK result
Locate (LOC), control line format with parameters
speciﬁed only
Replace Report (REP)
Word Change (WC), valid only on LOOK result
Word Locate (WL)
How to Cut Data
After you get into cut control, mark the data you want to cut
with the cursor: Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to cut and
press Transmit. Then position the cursor after the data to
cut and press Transmit. You can identify up to 10 cut items.
A cut item can be a single character or several lines. To cut
from any point in the report all the way to the end of the
report, mark only the beginning of the cut.
To cut whole lines, place the cursor at the beginning of the
ﬁrst line and press Transmit. Then position the cursor in
column 1 of the line following the last line to cut and press
Transmit.
Once you are under cut control, a fast way to cut several
blocks of data is to enter cut x (where x is any special
character, for example, an asterisk or semicolon, not

included in the data). This deﬁnes the special character as a
cut marker.
Type the special character at the beginning and end of each
block of data you want to cut. Press Transmit after marking
the blocks on each screen. If you need to roll the report, be
sure to press Transmit to mark the cuts on the present
screen before you roll the report to mark more data.
How to Paste Data
To paste an entire report into another report, you do not
need to mark any cuts. Display the report you want to copy
and enter paste report, where report is the report to paste
into.
To paste cut data in the same report, type paste and press
Transmit. Move the cursor just beyond the point where you
want to paste the data and press Transmit.
To paste cut data into another report, enter paste report,
where report is the report, including the cabinet number, to
paste into. The PASTE report is displayed at line 1. If the line
where you want to place the data is not on display, roll the
report. Paste as described in the previous paragraph.
When you position the cursor in any character position other
than column 1 or the last column on the line, the existing
line is split into two parts and the cut lines are inserted
starting at the column where the cursor was.
To paste cut data into a report located in another cabinet,
using the cabinet password access method, enter paste c
npsw report.

Field

Description

C

Cabinet Switch call.

npsw

Cabinet number and
password.

report

Report to paste into.

Outcome
The system expands the report and adds the data. Only
whole lines are added, so if you cut part of a line, the whole
line is actually pasted and the unused characters of the line
are blank. (If you are working in a word processing report,
the next ADJ command you perform removes the extra
spaces.)
Cut Control Line
The rightmost ﬁeld of the control line indicates what kind of
report or result is currently on display:

Field

Description

CUT RID

Cutting from report on
display.

CUT
RESULT

Cutting from result on
display, or result after
deleting.

Result showing cut blocks of
data.

MOVE RID

Moving with report on
display.

MOVE
RESULT

Result after moving.

PASTE RID

Pasting into report on
display.

PASTE
RESULT

Pasting into result on display,
or result after pasting.

Ü Ü 2200

CVT and CVZ (Convert Variable Table)
Use the Convert Variable Table (CVT) command to convert
numbered variables (such as V1) into named variables (such
as <name>) and vice versa, using the N option. The
command uses a previously built variable table at the end of
the run control report. For more information, enter cvt,help.
Use the CVZ command to convert all numbered variables to
three-character variables (for example, from V1 to V001).
For more information, enter cvz,help.

Convert Variable Table Control Line Format
CVT[,o]
CVZ

Convert Variable Table Option
The N option converts named variables to numbered
variables.
The N option converts all occurrences of data strings, line by
line, that meet the deﬁnition of a named variable, even if
the string is not intended to be a named variable (for
example, "<COMMENT>" in the comments area of your
run).

D and @DSP (Display Report)
Use the Display Report (D) function or DSP statement to
display a report.
The Display Report command displays the report you
specify. The new report on display is renamed -0.
The Display Report command displays the report and clears
the output area. If you speciﬁed a noninterim display, the
command resets I/O, LLP, and DLP counts.
If a report contains a user-private or department-private
password, you must use the same user-id or department
number with which the password was set.
2200 Þ Þ
If a report is in a drawer that has a drawer password, you
must unlock the drawer before displaying the report.
Ü Ü 2200

data in ﬁelds beyond column

those columns on display).

Ü Ü 2200

@DSP Statement Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when executing the DSP statement:
The screen display contains a function key bar. You can
customize the function key bar so that you can include
operations not available with the standard function key
bar. For more information, see @FKY (Function Key) and
@SC (Screen Control) in this document.
Use the DSP statement as a debugging aid. You can
display the output of statements in a run as they are
processed to see whether they are producing the
desired results. If they are, you can then remove the
DSP statements.
Use the DSP statement to display reports for manual
updating during a run. If manual updating is an integral
part of the run, leave in the DSP statements used for
this purpose.
To continue a run after the display, the run user presses
Resume.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the DSP statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
You can customize the report format by preceding the
DSP statement with a Format (FMT) or Set Format
Characters (SFC) statement.
If an odd-numbered cabinet is speciﬁed, you will be
unable to update the report.

Display Report Examples
This example displays report 2B0, format 3:
2b0 3
Equivalent Statement
@dsp,0,b,2,,,3 .
This example displays the report named "Inventory" in
format 2, beginning at line 15: d inventory 2,15
Equivalent Statement
@dsp,'inventory',15,,2 .

@DSP Statement Examples
This example displays report 1C0 beginning at line 8 and
continues the run after the display: @dsp,0,c,1,8,,,y .
This example displays format 5 of report 1D0 beginning at
line 3 and includes a message: @dsp,0,d,1,3,,5,,,` This line
displayed on control line. '

DATE and @DAT (Date)
Use the Date function or the DAT statement to perform
operations on dates in reports.
You can use the Iterative Date (IDAT) run to save and reuse
Date function masks containing options and parameters and
to create DAT statements.
You can use the Date command to
Compare dates in two ﬁelds and put the diﬀerence in
another ﬁeld
Compare dates in a ﬁeld to the current date
Compute new dates by adding or subtracting a number
of days
Change the format of a date ﬁeld (for example, from
YYMMDD to MM/DD/YY)
Compute the day of the week
Change the format of a time ﬁeld to decimal hours
Produce end-of-month dates based on values input from
a report
Executing the Date command causes the following actions
to occur:
The command processes the dates and times and
creates a result.

If you compare two dates for which the diﬀerence is a
negative number, a minus sign precedes the calculated
diﬀerence.
If you process a blank date ﬁeld, asterisks ﬁll the result
ﬁeld.
2200 Þ Þ
If the receiving ﬁeld is too small to contain the date
format you specify, the command removes the spaces
from the format, if possible (DDMMYYYY). If the date is
still too large for the ﬁeld, the command overwrites a
portion of the adjacent ﬁeld.
Ü Ü 2200

Date Control Line Format
DATE [report f]

Field

Description

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

f

Report format in which to
process dates (enables you to
process dates in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 if you do not
already have those columns
on display).

Date Statement Format
