Field

Description

c,d,r

Report on which to perform
computations. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Date
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of ﬁelds containing
dates to process.

ltyp

Line type to process. If you
specify the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank but enter the
comma.

p

Parameters ﬁeld (see Date
Formats). If you do not specify
a format in either a + or ﬁeld, format B is assumed in

both ﬁelds. If you specify a
format for either a + or - ﬁeld
(but not both), that format is
assumed for both ﬁelds. If you
do not specify a format for the
= ﬁeld, the format speciﬁed
for the + ﬁeld (if one exists) is
assumed; otherwise, the
format speciﬁed for the - ﬁeld
is assumed.

Date Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.

2200 Þ Þ

If the size of the receiving ﬁeld
is too small to hold the date
conversion, ﬁlls the receiving
ﬁeld with asterisks (*). If you
do not use the F option, the
command places the result of
the date conversion in the
speciﬁed ﬁeld even if the ﬁeld
is not large enough, thus
overwriting a portion of the
adjacent ﬁeld.

Ü Ü 2200

Converts a time ﬁeld to

to convert and an = parameter

diﬀerent date format
parameter and an equal sign in
another ﬁeld so that the date
is converted to another format.
Enter the : parameter in the
ﬁeld in which to place the 3letter day of the week.
See Determining the Day of
the Week Example.

n

Deﬁnes the number of days in
a workweek, where n is 1 to 7.
Default = 7.
See Adding a Constant Number
to a Date Example and
Calculating Number of Weeks
Example.

Date Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Date command:
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between
1944 and 2043.
Four-digit year dates must represent dates between
1600 and 2299.
2200 Þ Þ
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between
1964 and 2063.
Ü Ü 2200
Always place the date format before other parameters.
To change the dates in a ﬁeld to a new format, you must
copy them to a diﬀerent ﬁeld, deﬁning the new ﬁeld
with the new format parameter. See Date Formats.
If you specify a format for only one ﬁeld, all ﬁelds are
assumed to be in that format. To use two or more date
ﬁelds in diﬀerent formats, specify the formats on all
ﬁelds you use.
One-digit year dates must represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from the current date. For
example, 1 January 1996 through 31 December 2005 (if
the current day was 1 November 2000).

Date Procedures
To add or subtract a constant ﬁeld

1. Type + or - in the ﬁrst column of the date ﬁeld.
2. Type k in the ﬁeld to add or subtract.
3. Type = in the ﬁeld to contain the computed result.
See Adding a Constant Number Contained in a Field
Example.
To add or subtract a constant number

1. Type these values in the ﬁeld to add to or subtract from:
The date format (see Date Formats).
A plus or minus sign.
The number of days to add or subtract.

2. Type = in the ﬁeld to store the new date.
For example, to add 30 days to a date ﬁeld in format A, type
a+30 in that ﬁeld and = in the ﬁeld to store the new date.
See also Adding a Constant Number to a Date Example.
To add or subtract the current date, use a plus or minus sign
and zero (0). For example, a+0.

Comparing a Date to the Current Date Example
This example compares the date in the Produc Actual ﬁeld
with the current date and places the diﬀerence into the Ship
Date ﬁeld. If the date in the Produc Actual ﬁeld is in the
past, a minus sign precedes the calculated diﬀerence. If a
date is blank, the system places asterisks in the Ship Date
ﬁeld.
Function Mask
.Produc. Ship .
.Actual. Date .

current date.

Identiﬁes the Ship Date ﬁeld as
the ﬁeld to store the diﬀerence
between the current date and
the date in the Produc Actual
ﬁeld.

Calculating Number of Weeks Example
This example compares the dates in the Produc Actual ﬁeld
to the current date and places the diﬀerence (in weeks) into
the Ship Date ﬁeld.
When calculating the diﬀerence (in workdays) between two
dates, and the number of days per week is fewer than seven
(speciﬁed with the n option), the Date command uses the
following rules:
The workweek is assumed to begin on Monday.
If the starting (earlier) date does not fall on a workday,
the last workday before the starting date is used in
calculating the diﬀerence.
If the ending (later) date is not a workday, the next
workday after the ending date is used in calculating the
diﬀerence.
If both the starting and ending dates are not workdays,
the next workday after the starting and after the ending
date are used in calculating the diﬀerence.
Function Mask
1
.Produc. Ship .
.Actual. Date .
.======.======.
******.******.
+ =
Equivalent Statement

@dat,'report2b' 1 57-6,64-6 |,+,= .
Option Used

1

Speciﬁes a 1-day workweek in
order to calculate the number of
weeks (using the n option).

Parameters Used

+

Identiﬁes Produc Actual as the
ﬁeld to compare to the current
date.

=

Identiﬁes Ship Date as the ﬁeld
in which to place the diﬀerence
in the number of weeks
between the Produc Actual date
and the current date.

Partial Result
*St.Status.By. Product
.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.Produc.Produc. Ship .
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
Plan .Actual. Date .
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.====.
======.======.======.

831224 -468

IP 831225 LS BLACKBOX1 436768 84390 AMCO 831223
831225 -468
IP 831219 LS BLACKBOX2 637071 84353 INTR 831218
831219 -469

SC 840110 LS BLACKBOX5 675281 97441 FEDS 840131

831222 -468

Producing End-of-Month Dates
This example produces the end-of-month dates, when you
run the DATE function on a report. Assume that you are
running the DATE function on the following report: .EOM Test
*In .Out1 .
*======.==================.=======
|070112|
|070212|
|070312|
|070412|
|070512|
|070612|
|070712|
|070812|
|070912|
|071012|
|071112|
..... END REPORT .....
Function Mask .EOM Test
*In .Out1 .
*======.==================.=======
****** ****************** *******
|b |gm= |
Equivalent Statement @dat,'report2b' '' 'In','Out1'
|,b,gm= .
Option Used

b

Identiﬁes the input in DATE1$
format.

g

Identiﬁes the output in DATE6$
format

m

Speciﬁes that a date ﬁeld is to
be converted to
end-of-month date

Parameters Used

to place the converted dates.

*In .Out1 .

|070212|02/28/07
|070312|03/31/07
|070412|04/30/07
|070512|05/31/07
|070612|06/30/07
|070712|07/31/07
|070812|08/31/07
|070912|09/30/07
|071012|10/31/07
|071112|11/30/07

Note: If the column containing the end-of-month option is
smaller than the size required for the date format
requested, the date overwrites adjacent ﬁelds.

@DC (Date Calculator)
Use the Date Calculator (DC) statement to perform
arithmetic calculations on dates or times that reside in
variables and constants, or on literal dates or times.
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between 1944
and 2043.
Four-digit year dates must represent dates between 1600
and 2299.
One-digit year dates must represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from the current date. For
example, 1 January 1996 through 31 December 2005 (if the
current day was 1 November 2000).
2200 Þ Þ
For elapsed time formats T4 and T5, valid times are
between -9544371:46:07 and 9544371:46:07.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
For elapsed time formats T4 and T5, valid times are
between -9544371:59:59 and 9544371:59:59.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Date Calculator Statement Format
@DC eq vrslts .

Field

Description

eq

Equations separated by
semicolons (;).

vrslts

Variables to capture the
results of the equations in
sequential order.

Date Calculator Outcome
Executing the DC statement causes the following actions to
occur:
Each equation in a DC statement sequentially creates a
label, A through Z, that you can use in subsequent
equations in the same statement. You cannot deﬁne
your own labels, as you can with the Arithmetic (ART)
statement.
The resulting date or time is in the same format as the
original, unless you specify a format change. You do not
need to specify the format of a date or time represented
by a label.
If the resulting value is an invalid date or time, the DC
statement ﬁlls the variable with asterisks (*). The
statement then treats the asterisks as zeroes in
subsequent calculations.
2200 Þ Þ
Also, if the resulting value is a numeric value calculated
from an invalid date or time, the DC statement loads the
variable with a zero.
Ü Ü 2200

Date and Time Equations
To perform arithmetic calculations on dates or times that
reside in variables and constants, or on literal dates or
times, use the following equations: Dn=expression
Tn=expression
Mn=expression

Field

Description

Dn

Date operation where n
indicates the date format.

Tn

Time operation where n
indicates the time format.

Mn

Month operation where n
indicates the date format.

expression Calculations using literal data,
variables, constants, labels
created previously in the same
DC statement, constant labels,
or any combination of these.

Date and Time Parameters
Use the following date and time parameters for all dates
and times you process with the DC statement: Dn(x)
Tn(x)

Field

Description

Dn

Date operation where n
indicates the date format and
(x) is the date or time in a
variable, constant, or literal.

Tn

Time operation where n
indicates the time format and
(x) is the date or time in a
variable, constant, or literal.

For example, this date format speciﬁes that the date in
<date> is in the format DD MMM YY (DATE2$ format):
d2(<date>) .

Constant Parameter Labels
You can use the TODAY, TIME, MINDAT, and MAXDAT
constant parameter labels in your equations to process the
current date, time, minimum supported date, and maximum
supported date:
TODAY contains the current date, expressed in the
DATE7$ format (MONTH DD, YYYY).
TIME contains the current time, expressed in TIME0$
format (HH:MM:SS).
MINDAT contains the minimum supported date in the
selected output format. The default format is DATE7$.
MAXDAT contains the maximum supported date in the
selected output format. The default format is DATE7$.

Calendar Equation Parameters
Use calendar parameters to perform addition and
subtraction of a number of years, months, and days to a
date. When you use calendar parameters, a valid date must
be the ﬁrst expression in an equation:

nY speciﬁes the number of years to use in the equation.
nM speciﬁes the number of months to use in the
equation.
nD speciﬁes the number of days to use in the equation.
n speciﬁes the number of days to use in the equation
(default).

Adding Days to the Current Date Example
This example loads <future> with the current date plus 90
days, expressed in DATE7$ format (the original format of
TODAY): @dc today+90 <future>h18 .

Obtaining the Current Date and Time Example
This example loads <datenow> with the current date,
expressed in DATE11$ format and <timenow> with the
current time, expressed in TIME0$ format (the original
format of TIME): @dc d11=today;time <datenow>h8,
<timenow>h8 .

Validating Date Input Example
This example validates date input. If <input> is not a valid
date in DATE11$ format, the run goes to label 100: @dc
d11(<input>) <check>H1 if <check> EQ * gto 100 ; .

Performing Several Calculations Example
This example performs several date calculations and uses
one of the internal labels created by the DC statement: @dc
dw=d7('june 4, 1996');d7=d1(960626)+5;b-today
<day>s10,\
<date>s18,<numdays>i4 .

dw=d7('june 4,
1996')... <day>s10

Load <day> with
the day of the
week on which June
4, 1996, fell. D7 is
the input format.

d7=d1(960626)+5... Load <date> with
<date>s18
960626 plus ﬁve
days, but change
to DATE7$ format.
D1 is the input
format. This
equation also
creates label B,
which the next
equation uses.

b-today...
<numdays>i6

Load <numdays>
with the number of
days diﬀerence
between the date
in label B and the
current date. No

input format is
needed to use label
B.

This example uses the Load Variable (LDV) statement to
load <olddate> with 960604. The DC statement performs
several date calculations and uses internal labels created in
earlier equations.
@ldv <olddate>h6=960604 .
@dc d1(<olddate>)+60;dw=a;d7=a;c-2 <newdate>h6,
<day>s10,\
<datea>s18,<datec-2>s18 .

d1(olddate)+60...
<newdate>h6

Load <newdate>
with the contents of
<olddate> (960604)
plus 60 days, in
DATE1$ format (the
original formatÄÄd1
is the input format).

dw=a... <day>s10 Load <day> with the
day of the week on
which the date in
label A fell.

d7=a...
<datea>s18

Load <datea> with
the date in label A,
in DATE7$ format.

c-2... <datec2>s18

Load <datec-2> with
the date in label C
minus two days, in
DATE7$ format (the
original format of
label C).

Obtaining the Minimum Valid Date Example
This example loads <min7> with the minimum supported
DATE7$ format date and <min1> with the minimum
supported DATE1$ format date: @dc mindat;d1=mindat
<min7>h18,<min1>i6 .

Adding One Year to Today’s Date Example
This example adds one year to today’s date.
@dc d7=today+1y <newdate>h18 .

Adding Two Months to Today’s Date Example
This example adds two months to today’s date.
@dc d7=today+2m <newdate>h18 .

Using Multiple Equation Date Parameters on a
Date Example
This example subtracts one year, 11 months, and 30 days
from the speciﬁed date.
@dc d7=d14(2003060)-1y-11m-30d <newdate>h18 .

Producing the End-of-Month Date
This example produces the end-of-month date.
@dc m11=d7('February 12, 2007') <out1>S18
The expression “d7('February 12, 2007')” converts the given
date from DATE7$ format into a day number. Using “m11=”
before it converts the day number into the end-of-month
date in DATE11$ (YYYYMMDD) format. In this example, this
value is saved in the variable <out1>. as "20070228."

Note: If the variable receiving the End-of-Month function is
smaller than the size required for the Mn format, the value
is truncated.

@DCPY (DDP Copy)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the DDP Copy (DCPY) statement to copy an OS 2200
program ﬁle or element, or data ﬁle, from one host to
another using DDP 1100.
For more information about each ﬁeld of the DCPY
statement, refer to the DDP-FJT Operations Guide, Vol. 1: IPF
Interface.

DDP Copy Statement Format
@DCPY[,lab]
host,fn,rmhost,rmfn,type[,pos,transl,user,
pw,cllv,acct,projid,qual,rmu,rmpw,rmcllv,rmacct,
rmprojid,rmq,tmo] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error
status is returned by DDP
1100.

host

Name of the host conﬁgured
in DDP 1100 from which the
ﬁle is to be copied. Specify the
host here as you specify it in
the IPF environment.

fn

Name of the ﬁle contained on
the host. If the ﬁle name
contains special characters,
enclose the ﬁeld in
apostrophes ( ' ).

rmhost

Name of the host conﬁgured
in DDP 1100 that is to receive
the ﬁle.

rmfn

Name of the ﬁle on the
receiving host. If the ﬁle name
contains special characters,
enclose the ﬁeld in
apostrophes ( ' ).

type

Type of ﬁle or element to copy.
If you need more than one
type, separate them with
commas and enclose the ﬁeld
in apostrophes ( ' ). Enter one
of the following types in this
subﬁeld: DDP-Copy the entire
ﬁle ALL-Copy all elements of
the program ﬁle SYM-Copy a
symbolic element REL-Copy a
relocatable element ABS-Copy
an absolute element OMNCopy an omnibus element

ADD to add to the end of a
ﬁle, or REP to replace the

that you cannot use ADD if
you are copying to an OS
2200 ﬁle.)

transferred between two OS
2200 sites must use

TRAnsparent = Do not

element to ASCII
EBCdic = Translate ﬁle or
element to EBCDIC.

User-id used to sign on to a

remote host.

rmcllv

Two-character value indicating
the clearance level of a
remote host.

Remote account number used
on a run card.

Remote project-id used on the

The timeout value (in
seconds).

DEFAULT-60 seconds
0-Return immediately
NEG-Wait for completion
MAX-86400 seconds (24
hours)

DDP Copy Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the interface error. STAT2$ contains the
CLASS-CODE. STAT3$ contains the DETAIL-STATUS code.
The values for these codes are listed in the DDP-PPC/DDPFJT Messages Reference Manual.

DDP Copy Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the DCPY statement:
The system implements the DDP ﬁle transfer capability
asynchronously. When the system executes the DCPY
statement, it transfers the copy request to DDP.
DDP 1100 then does preliminary error checking and
returns a status to the system indicating that the copy is
in progress. If the preliminary error checking does not
pass, however, DDP 1100 does not return an error
condition. In either case, control returns to the system.

DDP Copy Example
This example copies all symbolic elements from the ﬁle A*B.
located on host RSL2 to the ﬁle D*E. on host TOC. The
elements replace any existing elements on host TOC. If
there was an error in the statement or DDP conﬁguration,
STAT1$, STAT2$, and STAT3$ contain the status code and
the label 1 exit is taken.
When the DCPY is in progress, the ﬁle is not completely
copied before control is returned. The software waits up to
one minute for a status message from the other host.
Depending upon the system usage, it may take some time
for the copy to complete.
@dcpy,1 rsl2,'a*b.',toc,'d*e.',sym,rep .
Ü Ü 2200

@DCR (Decode Report)
See DECODE and @DCR (Decode Report) for more
information.

@DCRE (DDP Create)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the DDP Create (DCRE) statement to create a ﬁle on a
DDP 1100 host. Before a ﬁle is copied with DDP Copy
(DCPY), it must be created on the remote host with DCRE.
Refer to the DDP-FJT Operations Guide, Vol. 1: IPF Interface
for more information about each ﬁeld of the DCRE
statement.

DDP Create Statement Format
@DCRE[,lab]
host,fn[,dev,init,gran,maxfs,vol,access,
user,pw,cllv,acct,projid,qual] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error
status is returned by DDP
1100.

host

Name of the host conﬁgured
in DDP 1100 where the ﬁle is
to be created or allocated.
Specify the host here as you
specify it in the IPF
environment.

fn

Name of the ﬁle to be created
or allocated. If the ﬁle name
contains special characters,
enclose the ﬁeld in single
quotation marks (").

dev

Name of the device where the
ﬁle is to be created. See the
DDP-FJT Operations Guide, Vol.

1., for a list of valid device
types.

init

Initial ﬁle size in units of
granularity. Default = 0. Note
that on an OS 2200 host, this
number multiplied by the
granularity and divided by
7168 gives the number of
tracks to allocate.

gran

Granularity of the ﬁle that
speciﬁes the size in bytes
based on the values speciﬁed
on the init and maxfs
subﬁelds. Default = 7168
bytes (for OS 2200 systems),
which is one track.

maxfs

Maximum ﬁle size in units of
granularity. Default = system
default size.

vol

Volume-id for a removable or
system disk pack. Default =
system ﬁxed mass storage
volume.

access

Access type for the ﬁle (you
can specify only the ﬁrst three

characters): PUBlic-is a public
(shared) ﬁle.
PRIvate-is a private
(nonshared) ﬁle (default).

user

User-id used to sign on to a
speciﬁed host.

pw

Password used, along with the
user-id, to sign on to a
speciﬁed host.

cllv

Two-character value indicating
the clearance level of a
speciﬁed host.

acct

Account number used for a
speciﬁed host.

projid

Project-id used for a speciﬁed
host.

qual

Qualiﬁer used when a ﬁle
name contains a leading
asterisk (*).

DDP Create Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the interface error. STAT2$ contains the
CLASS-CODE. STAT3$ contains the DETAIL-STATUS code.
STAT1$, STAT2$, and STAT3$ contain error codes. These
error codes are listed in the DDP-PPC/DDP-FJT Messages
Reference Manual.

DDP Create Example
This example creates the ﬁle A*B in host RSL2 on an 8450
disk with a movable head. The ﬁle is public and has an initial
size of 10 tracks with a maximum of 500 tracks.
@dcre,1 rsl2,'a*b.',f50m,10,,500,,pub .
This Exec control statement creates the same ﬁle in a batch
or demand program: @cat,p a*b.,f50m/10/trk/500 .
Ü Ü 2200

@DCU (Decommit Updates)
Use the Decommit Updates (DCU) statement to discard all
updates made to all reports for which updates were deferred
by a preceding DFU statement.
The reports for which you want to discard updates must be
speciﬁed in a preceding DFU statement.

Decommit Updates Statement Format
@DCU .

Decommit Updates Outcome
The DCU statement discards all the updates made to all
reports for which updates were deferred by the preceding
DFU statement. This does not aﬀect updates made to other
reports.
The statement also releases update control for the reports
speciﬁed by the preceding DFU statement.

@DDE (Dynamic Data Exchange
Interface)
Use the Dynamic Data Exchange Interface (DDE) statement
as a front-end interface to the Microsoft Windows Dynamic
Data Exchange feature. This Windows feature enables you
to conduct "conversations" between the system, acting as a
client application, and a Windows application that supports
the DDE protocol acting as a server application (such as
Excel or Word for Windows). DDE enables you to use the
power of the most appropriate application for the task at
hand.
The application (client) initiates the conversation, and the
server application (such as Excel or Word for Windows)
responds to the initiation by providing data or services as
requested by the client. Be aware that a client can request
data and services from multiple servers simultaneously, and
a server can be conversing with multiple clients
simultaneously.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.

This statement is restricted to foreground runs.
You must be very familiar with the syntax and usage of the
Windows application with which you are conversing,
because the requests you submit must be valid for that
application. The information in this topic is not intended as a
tutorial for writing an application using DDE. It is intended
only to describe how to use the DDE statement as part of
such an application.
Make sure the Windows application is running before the
DDE statement executes. You can start the Windows
application as you would normally, or you can use the Run
PC Program (PC) statement to start it before executing the
DDE statement.

Dynamic Data Exchange Interface Statement
Format
@DDE,{"string"|c,d[,r,l],filename},vcon,cmd,
{conversation_topic| item},datatype,o,lab vcon .

Field

Description

"string"

Data to send or
a command to
execute in the
server
application,
depending on
the DDE
operation you
request in the
cmd subﬁeld.
Enclose the
string in
quotation marks
("). If the string
itself contains
quotation
marks, enclose
the quotation
marks in
apostrophes (').
Maximum =
1280
characters, or
the maximum

size for any
statement.
When using the
DDE statement
for the ﬁrst time
with the I
command to
initiate the
conversation,
string should
contain the
name of the
application. This
is normally the
ﬁle name
without the .EXE
extension;
however, some
applications
could vary, so
check the
documentation
for the
application. Do
not include the
extension in
string.

c,d

Cabinet and
drawer to hold
the result, if
using the R
(request)
command;

otherwise,
speciﬁes the
cabinet and
drawer of the
report to send
to the server.
Do not use
these subﬁelds
if you use the
"string"
subﬁeld.
See Dynamic
Data Exchange
Interface
Guidelines for
information on
data size limits.

r,l

Report number
to send to the
server
application and,
optionally, the
line number (l)
at which to
start. Use these
subﬁelds to
send a larger
amount of data.
Do not use
these subﬁelds
when using the I
or R commands.

If you use these
subﬁelds, you
must also enter
a ﬁle name in
the ﬁlename
subﬁeld for use
as a temporary
ﬁle to pass to
the DDE
interface.

ﬁlename

File to send to
the server
application.
Specify as much
of the path as is
necessary to
deﬁne the exact
location of the
ﬁle. This
subﬁeld is
required
whenever you
send a report to
the server.
Do not use this
subﬁeld when
using the I or R
commands.
For security
reasons and for
eﬃcient use of
mass storage,

the system
deletes the ﬁle
after it sends
data to the
server.

vcon

Variable
containing a
window handle
identifying the
conversation.
This value is
returned after
using the DDE
statement the
ﬁrst time with
the I command
to initiate the
conversation.

cmd

Command
specifying the
type of DDE
operation to
perform. See
Dynamic Data
Exchange
Interface
Commands.

conversation_topic|item Conversation
topic, when

specifying the I
command for
initiation;
otherwise,
conversation
item. The entry
for this subﬁeld
varies
depending on
the application
and the
operation or
sequence of
operations you
request with
subsequent DDE
statements.
For example, in
Word for
Windows, this
subﬁeld might
contain a
document name
in one DDE
request and a
bookmark name
within that
document in the
next DDE
request. In
Excel, this
subﬁeld might
contain a
spreadsheet
name in one

DDE request
and a named
range of cells
within that
spreadsheet in
the next DDE
request.
For more
information on
conversation
topics and
conversation
items, see the
documentation
for the Microsoft
Software
Development
Kit (SDK).

datatype

Keyword
designating the
type of data
being sent. The
only valid entry
at this time is
text.

o

Options ﬁeld.
See Dynamic
Data Exchange
Interface
Option.

lab

Label to go to if
the DDE
operation
cannot be
completed
successfully.

vcon

Variable to
capture the
window handle
identifying the
conversation.
Use this ﬁeld
only with the I
command.

Dynamic Data Exchange Interface Option
Option

Description

T

Speciﬁes special handling for
text type data. When sending
a report to the server, only
tab lines are sent, not
including the tab character in
column 1. The statement
skips over any other line
types. This option has no
eﬀect when using the "string"
subﬁeld.
If you use the T option when
data is being returned from
the server, the statement
attempts to line up the ﬁelds
to match those deﬁned in
report 0 of the drawer you
specify in the c,d subﬁelds, or
in the current drawer when
using the "string" subﬁeld.
Fields are delimited with a
zero (0) in the input edit
codes line of report 0. The
statement adds extra spaces
or truncates characters that
do not ﬁt into the ﬁelds.
In addition, the statement
right-justiﬁes numeric values

within the ﬁeld. It considers
the ﬁeld numeric if the ﬁrst
character is a digit (for
example, 0 9). If report 0
does not contain input edit
codes, the statement ignores
this option and returns the
result as it existed on the
server.

Dynamic Data Exchange Interface Commands
Command Description

A

Advises the run regarding
topic. With this command,
you are establishing a
"warm" link to a data item
on the server and waiting for
input. The server notiﬁes the
client whenever the
particular data item
changes. Upon notiﬁcation,
the client can request the
new value of the data item.
Use the Accept Input (INP)
statement following a DDE
request with the A
command.

Note: Be aware that if other
input is simultaneously being
processed by the system,
the system rejects the DDE
interface request with a busy
status. Handling of a busy
status varies among
Windows applications. Also
be aware that some servers
send only one advisory, even
if they receive a busy status,
so it is extremely important

to ensure that the system is
always receptive when an
advisory is active.
If you need an advisory set
up for more than one data
item, use a diﬀerent
conversation because there
is no way to distinguish
which data item changed.
Use a DDE statement with
the U command to turn oﬀ
the Advise request.

E

Executes the command,
normally an applicationspeciﬁc macro, being
passed. Commands and
macros are totally
application-speciﬁc and
require a high degree of
familiarity with the receiving
application.

I

Initializes a DDE
conversation. Use this
command with the ﬁrst DDE
statement executed. Leave
the vcon subﬁeld blank when
using the I command.
The statement creates a
two-line result containing

information about the
connection. The ﬁrst line
contains the name of the
application; the second line
contains the conversation
topic. This is provided for
those cases where either a
NULL topic or application
was given. A NULL, or
empty, topic or application is
useful when the application
or topic is either unknown or
does not matter.
The statement also returns a
value in the vcon variable,
which contains the window
handle identifying the
conversation. Use this
variable in the vcon subﬁeld
of subsequent DDE
statements during the
current conversation.

P

Pokes (sends) data to the
server application. You must
send data in a format the
receiving application is
designed to handle.

R

Requests data from the
server application. If you
specify a cabinet and drawer

in the c,d subﬁelds, the DDE
interface returns the data in
-0 of that cabinet and
drawer; otherwise, it returns
the result in the current
drawer. To return the data to
the current drawer, use the
"string" subﬁeld with an
empty string ("") and do not
specify a report or ﬁle name
in the c,d,r,l, and ﬁlename
subﬁelds.
Remember that the drawers
have ﬁxed line lengths. The
system truncates data if it
exceeds the length of the
drawer.
See Dynamic Data Exchange
Interface Guidelines for
information on data size
limits.

U

Turns oﬀ (unadvises) a
previous Advise request.

Dynamic Data Exchange Interface Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the DDE statement:
Data sent and received, macros and commands
executed, and syntax of topics are totally applicationspeciﬁc. To use the DDE statement to its fullest
advantage, you should be very familiar with the
Windows application with which you are conversing. You
should also be familiar with how the application handles
the DDE interface.
The statement does not perform language translation on
any of the strings used in the DDE statement, for
example, application names, topic names, and data
item names. You are responsible for ensuring that the
DDE server application is a version that uses the same
language as the run was written in.
Leading, trailing, and embedded spaces may be
signiﬁcant for any of the DDE strings. For example, an
application may respond to the name "EXCEL" but will
not respond to the name " EXCEL " (with leading and
trailing spaces).
Tabs have special meaning to some applications.
Generally, they delimit or separate data items. Keep this
in mind when moving data that may contain tabs to a
DDE server.
You must place data obtained from a DDE application
into a report. The system truncates any data item
exceeding the line length of the current drawer. To
minimize the possibility of data truncation, use the
Break (BRK) statement to direct output to the widest
available drawer.

If you intend to use an advisory (A) command, be sure
the run is ready for the advisory when it is sent. The run
must not be performing other tasks or the advisory may
be missed. If the run is not ready, the DDE interface
notiﬁes the server application that it is busy and the
server application may not try again later to deliver the
advisory. The run is ready to accept an advisory when it
is waiting on an Input (INP) statement.
Normally, the DDE client controls when the conversation
ends. It is also permissible for a DDE server to terminate
the conversation on its own.
Following are the limits regarding data sizes:

Data Transfer From
Method
Client to
Server

From
Server to
Client

"string"

1,280
characters

6,000
characters

64,000
characters

6,000
characters.
(The data
is returned

The limits
shown are for
the entire DDE
statement,
including the
string.

as a result,
not in a
ﬁle.)

Using the PCR
statement or
the PCW
statement, or
both. See
Transferring a

of Data
Example.

No practical No
limit
practical
limit

DDE Tracing
Use DDE tracing to see the actual DDE messages and data
that are exchanged between the client and server. To enable
these DDE traces, add one tag to the INI ﬁle (Windows
conﬁguration ﬁle). Set the tag DDERunTraceLevel (not case
sensitive) to a positive integer, as indicated in the following
table. For Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client, the tag is under the section [MAPPER]. For Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server, the tag is under
the section [MPC]. The name of the INI ﬁle varies by
product, with Business Information Server for Microsoft
Windows Client using MAPPER.INI and Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server using MPC.INI.
The user must create the MAPPER.INI ﬁle in the windows
directory if it does not exist.

Trace
Level

Description

0

No tracing. Any existing trace
ﬁle is not altered.

1

Minimal detail. Makes an
entry every time a DDE
statement is executed. Each
trace entry has a timestamp
when the entry was made
and the real window handle of
the client DDE window. This

value is a decimal number
representing the number of
milliseconds that have
elapsed since Windows was
started.

2

Medium amount of detail.
Adds tracing for each of the
Windows DDE messages sent
and received. For more
information, see DDE
Messages.

3 or
larger

Maximum amount of detail.
Adds a complete dump in
hexadecimal and ASCII, of all
data exchanged between the
client and the server.

This information is most helpful when you understand the
DDE messaging system. See any Windows programming
environment reference manual for more detailed
information.

Note: Do not rely on the format or content of this trace ﬁle
since it may change with each subsequent release of
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows Client or
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server.
Outcome

The system creates a ﬁle called DDETRACE.TXT in the
default windows directory. If tracing is enabled, the system
initializes this ﬁle every time you launch Business
Information Server for Microsoft Windows Client or Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server.

DDE Messages
Message

Description

WM_DDE_ACK

Sent by one
application in
response to
another
applications
message during an
ongoing
conversation. It
can indicate
assent or dissent.

WM_DDE_ADVISE

Sent by a client
application to
request
continuously
updated data from
a server. This
establishes a
permanent data
link.

WM_DDE_DATA

Sent by a server
application in
response to a
client request.

WM_DDE_EXECUTE

Sends a string to
the server
application, which
is expected to
process it as a
series of
commands.

WM_DDE_INITIATE

Sent by the client
application to one
or more other
applications,
indicating that the
client wants to
start a DDE
conversation.

WM_DDE_POKE

Unsolicited data
sent by a client
application to a
server.

WM_DDE_REQUEST

Sent by a client
application
requesting a onetime transfer of a
data item from the
server. An item is
a location in the
topic, such as a
bookmark, cell
reference, named

range, or data
ﬁeld.

WM_DDE_TERMINATE Sent by either a
client or server
application to end
a DDE
conversation.

WM_DDE_UNADVISE

Sent by a client
application
indicating that
continuous
updates of linked
data are no longer
required from the
server.

The window handle of the receiving window appears as the
ﬁrst parameter of these calls. These values are the actual
window handles and not the host-generated handles that
the DDE statement uses. The second parameter contains
the message to be sent, the third parameter identiﬁes the
sending window, and the fourth parameter contains the
message-speciﬁc arguments.

Advise and Unadvise Example
This example sets up an advisory for row 1, column 1, waits
for a change, and handles the change:
@dde,"excel",,i,mysheet.xls,text <xl>i6 .
initiate conversation
@dde,"",<xl>,a,R1C1,text . set up advisory for
row 1 column 1
@inp . wait for something to happen
@if actinp$ ne <xl> gto lin-1 . was this the
EXCEL advisory?
@dde,"",<xl>,u,R1C1,text . cancel the advisory
@dde,"",<xl>,r,R1C1,text . yes, get the changed
data
@dsx,-0 . show the changed data

Poke and Execute Example
The following example manipulates a document in Microsoft
Excel:

Note: This example does not apply if any Microsoft Excel
document is already open.
@ BRK PCW,-0,2 'C:\TEMP\test.txt' . download the
file
@ DDE,"Excel",,i,,text,,0010 <DDE>i6 . see if
Excel is already awake
@ DDE,"[Open('"'C:\TEMP\test.txt'"')]",
<DDE>,e,,text .
@ CLS,<DDE> .
@ DDE,"Excel",,i,,text,,0010 <DDE>i6 .
@0005:DDE,"1234]5678",<DDE>,p,R1C1:R1C2,text .
@ REL .
@ . ~~~~ Start Excel ~~~~
@0010:PC 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft
Office\Office11\Excel
C:\TEMP\test.txt'.
@ DDE,"Excel",,i,,text <DDE>i6 . initiate a
conversation
@ GTO 0005 .

Note: The "]" character in the @0005 label represents the
tab character.
The following Microsoft Word example ﬁnds and changes a
string:
@ RSL,0,b,2 .
@ .Write few lines of 2B0 into a file ..
@ PCW,-0,45 'C:\TEMP\mydoc.rtf' .
@ BRK .

@ .Open the word document
@ PC 'Winword C:\TEMP\mydoc.rtf'
@ .Attach the document to the dde
@ DDE,"WinWord",,i,C:\TEMP\mydoc.rtf,text
<word>i4 .
@ .Ask Word to find a text string to be changed
@ DDE,"[EditFind.Find='"'GREENBOX8'"']",
<word>,e,,text .
@ .Replace GREENBOX with BLUEBOX in the file .
@ DDE,"[Insert '"'BLUEBOX'"']",<word>,e,,text .
@ REL .
The following statement will result in an error because a
report is speciﬁed but the ﬁle name is missing.
@dde,20,b,2,,,<dde>,p,SomeItem,text .
This will also result in an error because the P command
requires data either from a report or a string.
@dde,10,c,,,,<dde>,p,SomeItem,text .

Transferring a Large Amount of Data Example
This example ﬁrst uses the PCW statement to send a large
report (over 64,000 characters) to Word, then opens it in
Word, saves it after processing it, and ﬁnally, returns a
result using the PCR statement:
@dde,"WinWord",,i,system,text <word>i6 . initiate
conversation
@pcw,0,f,10 'c:'RSLANT$'large.fil' . data report
greater than 64000 characters
@. tell Word to bring in the data, which is now
in the file
@dde,"[FileOpen .Name ='"'c:\large.fil'"']",
<word>,e,,text .
@.
@. Word processes the file
@.
@. tell Word to save the changed file (as text)
@dde,"[FileSaveAs
.Name='"'c:\large.fil'"',.Format=2]",
<word>,e,,text .
@cls,<word> . terminate the conversation
@pcr,0,f,10,n,y 'c:\large.fil' . retrieve the
returned result

Transferring Medium Amounts of Data Example
This example processes a medium-sized (under 64,000
characters) ﬁle and displays the result:
@dde,"WinWord",,i,SomeWordFile,text <word>i6 .
initiate conversation
@. pass data to Word from a report with < 64000
characters of data
@dde,0,b,2,,MyFile,<word>,p,SomeWordItem,text .
@.
@. Word processes the file
@.
@dde,"",<word>,r,SomeWordItem,text . return the
processed data
@cls,<word> . terminate the conversation
@dsx,-0 . display the result

Conversation without a Topic Example
This example initiates a conversation without a topic in
order to request a speciﬁc cell in an Excel spreadsheet, then
starts a new conversation to process that spreadsheet:
@dde,"Excel",,i,,text <xl>i6 . initiate
conversation without a topic
@. ask Excel to open a specific cell in a
specific spreadsheet
@dde,"[Open('"'c:\mysheet.xls'"')]
[CALCULATE.NOW()]",<xl>,e,,text .
@cls,<xl> . close this conversation
@. now start a conversation about the just loaded
spreadsheet
@dde,"Excel",,i,mysheet.xls,text <xl>i6 .
@dde,"",<xl>,r,R1C1,text . grab a data item
@rdl,-0,2 2-10 <data>s10 . read the data
@dde,"[File.Close(False)][Quit()]",<xl>,e,,text .
done with EXCEL
@cls,<xl> .

Sending Data to an Application Example
This example sends a small amount of data to the Excel
spreadsheet expenses.xls. The data is inserted in two
adjacent cells in the active spreadsheet, starting at the
currently active cell. The tab character tells the spreadsheet
to put the data in adjacent cells.
@dde,"1234|5678",<comm>,p,expenses.xls,text .

Requesting Data from Application Example
This example requests data from the Excel spreadsheet
expenses.xls, starting at row 1, column 1 and ending at row
2, column 10.
@dde,"",<comm>,r,R1C1:R2C10,text .

Returning Data from the Server Example
Both of the following examples return SomeItem from the
server and create a result in drawer A. The ﬁrst method
does not use the c,d subﬁelds; instead it defaults to the
current drawer, set up with a previous BRK statement. The
second method uses the c,d subﬁelds.
@brk,0,a dde,"",<dde>,r,SomeItem,text .
@dde,0,a,,,,<dde>,r,SomeItem,text .

Handling Error Conditions Example
This example demonstrates how to handle error conditions:
@0010 .
@dde,"MyApp",,i,MyTopic,text,,0015 <app>i6 .
start conversation
@esr . Successful connection to MyApp
@.
@0015:if stat1$ = 13 gto lin+1 ; gto 0020 . MyApp
failed to respond?
@pc,b c:\MyPath\MyAppExe.exe . yes, it must not
be running. start it
@gto 0010 . and go back to try again
@0020:brk . Some serious problem that cannot be
recovered from
Failed to start conversation with MyApp
@brk dsx,-0 .

Ending a Conversation Example
This example starts a conversation and then ends it:
@dde,"MyApp",,i,MyTopic,text <app>i6 . start
conversation
@.
@. conversation ....
@.
@cls,<app> . close out this conversation

@DDI (Data Deﬁnition Information)
Use the Data Deﬁnition Information (DDI) statement to
retrieve a table description from a relational database and
place the information in a result.
For more information, see @LGN (Log On to Relational
Database) in this document.
Before using the DDI statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.

Data Deﬁnition Information Statement Format
@DDI[,c,d,lab,edsp?,d/p,db] 'id col tbl'
[vstat,vcol,vqty] .

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to hold
the result.

lab

Label to go to if an error
occurs. If you do not supply a
label and an error occurs, if
edsp? = Y, the error report is
built and the run continues; if
edsp? = N, no error report is
built and the run continues.

edsp?

Create a result if an interface
or database error occurs? Y or
N. The result contains the last
syntax sent to the database
manager and the text of the
system message. The table
and column where the error
occurred is identiﬁed, if
applicable. If you supplied a
label, the run continues at the
label and the error information
resides in the -0 result.

d/p

Eliminate period lines
containing descriptions of ﬁeld
abbreviations (d) or include
descriptions (p). Default = p.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

db

Database name. Default =
default database name on
your system.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

db

Database name. Default =
database running with MRIMID

set to 1. See your
administrator for more
information.

Ü Ü 2200

id

Unique identiﬁer for the cursor
name. This ﬁeld is required.

col

Columns included in the result.
If you specify more than one
column name, separate them
with commas. Use an asterisk
(*) to select all columns in the
table or tables.
If you specify an arithmetic
operation in this ﬁeld, the
result contains the operation
and sets the column status
(ST) to 2 (arithmetic result).

tbl

Name of the tables or views to
describe. If you specify more
than one table, separate the
names with commas.

vstat

Variable to capture the status
of the action performed.
Deﬁne this variable as type A,

seven characters in length (for
example, v1a7).

vcol

Variable to capture the
number of the character
(within the syntax generated
by the DDI statement) where
an error was detected. Deﬁne
this variable as type I, ﬁve
characters in length (for
example, v1i5).

vqty

Variable to capture the total
number of columns detected
in the selected tables.

Data Deﬁnition Information Outcome
The DDI statement generates a description of a relational
table in a result. The description includes the following
information for each column:
COLUMN NAME Column name.
TYPE

Type of data stored in the
column.

SIZE

Length of the ﬁeld.

DECPT

Number of positions to the right
of the decimal point in a ﬁxed
point number.

NF

Null value indicator (0 = nulls
not allowed; 1 = nulls allowed).

ST

Status of the ﬁeld (0 = not
primary key; 1 = part of primary
key; 2 = arithmetic result).

Data Deﬁnition Information Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the number of columns retrieved for the
deﬁnition. This value is valid even if the system detects an
interface error.
STAT2$ contains the total number of lines of data that were
written into the result below the heading lines, or zero if an
error occurred.
STAT3$ contains one of the following values:
The line number of the ﬁrst line following the heading
divider line in the result.
A system message number if an error occurred and you
supplied a label (contains zero if you did not supply a
label). Use the Load System Message (LSM) statement
to obtain the text of the message.

Data Deﬁnition Information Example
This example generates a description of the Houses and
Customers tables. If there is no error, the description is in
result -0, cabinet 0, drawer A.
@ddi,0,a,099,y,p,agency 'a * houses,customers' .

0,a,099,y

Goes to label 99 in
case of an error,
creates a -0 result
containing error
information, and
places it in cabinet 0,
drawer A.

p

Includes the period
lines containing
descriptions of ﬁelds
in the report.

agency

Speciﬁes the
database name.

a

Speciﬁes the cursor
name.

*

Speciﬁes that all
columns of the table

are to be included in
the result.

houses,customers Speciﬁes table
names, separated by
commas.

@DEC (Decrement Variable)
Use the Decrement Variable (DEC) statement to decrease
the value of a numeric variable.

Decrement Variable Statement Format
@DEC[,n] v[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

n

Number (ﬂoating point or
integer) by which to decrease
the value of the variable. You
can specify the number using
a literal, a variable, a
constant, or a reserved word.
Default = 1.

2200 Þ Þ

v

Name of the variable that
contains the value you want to
decrease. The variable you
specify must contain only
numeric data and cannot be a
string (type S) variable.
If the variable contains any
nonnumeric data, the value of
the variable is not changed.

Ü Ü 2200

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

v

Name of the variable that
contains the value you want to
decrease. The variable you
specify must contain only
numeric data and cannot be a
string (type S) variable.
If the variable contains any
nonnumeric data, the results
are undeﬁned.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Decrement Variable Examples
This example decreases the value of <counter> by 1:
@dec <counter> .
This example decreases the value of <counter> by 10:
@dec,10 <counter> .
This example decreases the value of <totalcost> by the
value of <discount>:
@dec,<discount> <totalcost> .

DECODE and @DCR (Decode Report)
Use the Decode Report (DECODE) function or DCR
statement to display report data previously encoded with
the Encode Report command. You must use the same
encryption key used to encode the report. For information
on encoding reports, see ENCODE and @ECR (Encode
Report) in this document.
The report must be encoded with the Encode Report
command.

Decode Report Control Line Format
DECODE
2200 Þ Þ
DECODE [key]
where key is a one- to eight-character code used to encode
the report.
Ü Ü 2200

Decode Report Statement Format
@DCR,c,d,r,key .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to decode. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

key

The one- to eight-character
key used to encode the report.
See ENCODE and @ECR
(Encode Report) for a
description of the encryption
keys.

Decode Report Outcome
Executing the Decode Report command creates a result
containing the restored report data. You can now save or
update the data.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
A message is displayed if one of the following occurs:
You speciﬁed the incorrect key or encryption level.
The encoded report is corrupted.
The report is not encoded.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The decoding process produces invalid characters under the
following circumstances:
You speciﬁed the incorrect key.
The encoded report is corrupted.
The report is not encoded.
Whenever the system produces these invalid
characters, a system message is displayed indicating an
error and the system replaces each invalid character in
the result with an SOE character.
If the encoded report is corrupted, characters in the
result shown as SOEs cannot be recovered.

Ü Ü 2200

Decode Report Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Decode Report
command:
To use the Decode Report manual function, you must
use the control line format. The menu selection is
available only for online help information.
Use caution when encoding and decoding reports. Do
not forget your key. A report cannot be decoded if you
lose or forget the key because no record of it is kept by
the system. Your administrator cannot tell you what it is.
Because of high processing overhead, extensive use of
this command may aﬀect system performance.
2200 Þ Þ
If you are using a normal ASCII terminal, you may not be
able to decode a report that was encoded from a
National Character Set (NCS) terminal.
Ü Ü 2200

Decode Report Example
This example decodes report 10B0, which was encoded
using the key jak: @dcr,0,b,10,jak .

DEFINE (Deﬁne Constant)
The Deﬁne Constant (DEFINE) statement is a special kind of
command you can use at the beginning of your run control
report to deﬁne values for any of the ﬁelds and subﬁelds in
a statement.
For more information, see the Developer’s Guide.

DEL and @DEL (Delete)
Use the Delete (DEL) function or DEL statement to remove
the lines in an update result from the original report.
The manual function deletes the lines and redisplays the
original report.
The statement removes the lines from the report and turns
the update result into a normal -0 result.
For the Delete manual function, an update result from a
previous function must be displayed. For the DEL statement,
the update result must be the -0 result.

Delete Control Line Format
DEL [psw]
where psw is the password required in order to perform a
delete operation on a write-protected report.

Delete Statement Format
@DEL .

Delete Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Delete command:
Only the lines in the update result are removed from the
original report when you execute the Delete command.
If you delete lines from the update result before you
execute the Delete command, those lines are not
removed from the original report.
To remove the lines from the original report and retain
the lines in the update result, use the Extract command.
For more information, see EXT and @EXT (Extract) in
this document.
To replace lines in a report with the lines in an update
result, use the Update command. For more information,
see UPD and @UPD (Update) in this document.

Delete and Yank Line and @LND
Use the Delete and Yank Line function or LND statement to
delete lines from a report and then copy them into a
temporary buﬀer. Compare this command to the Delete Line
command:
The Delete and Yank Line command holds your deleted
lines in a buﬀer so that you can put them in other parts
of the report or in other reports.
The Delete Line command just deletes the lines from the
report.
For the Delete and Yank Line manual function, the report
that you are deleting and copying from must be on display.
For the LND statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LND statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.

Delete and Yank Line In-Report-Line Format
>][n]D[b]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

n

Number of lines to be deleted
and yanked. Default = 1.

D

Delete and Yank Line call.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100. Default = unlabeled
buﬀer.

Delete and Yank Line Statement Format
@LND,c,d,r,l[,q,b] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which to delete
and copy lines. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

l

Line number of the ﬁrst line to
delete and copy.

q

Number of lines to delete and
copy. Default = 1. If you
specify more lines than the
report contains, the statement
deletes and copies all lines
from line l to the end of the
report.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100. Default = unlabeled
buﬀer.

Delete and Yank Line Outcome
Executing the Delete and Yank Line command causes the
following actions to occur:
The command deletes the lines from the report and
copies them into the buﬀer. If the buﬀer does not exist,
the command creates it.
When using the Delete key or the Delete lines selection
of the Line Change menu, the system executes the
Delete and Yank Line command, holding your deleted
lines in a buﬀer.

Delete and Yank Line Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Delete and Yank Line
command:
To move the lines from the buﬀer to any current report,
use the Put Line command.
If you specify a buﬀer label, remember it for copying
using the Put Line command.
For information on buﬀers, see Temporary Buﬀers in
Append Line and @LNA.

Delete and Yank Line Procedures
To delete and yank just one line or a few lines

1. Place the cursor on the line you want to delete and
yank.

2. Press Edit.
3. Press Delete once for each line to be deleted.
Or

1. Place the cursor at the beginning of the ﬁrst line to be
deleted and copied.

2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Delete and Yank Line in-report-line format.
To delete and yank several lines

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to delete and yank.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed. Type
the number of lines in the Delete lines ﬁeld and
transmit. If you specify more lines than the report
contains, the command deletes all lines following the
request. Default = 1.

Delete and Yank Line Example
This example deletes lines 12 through 19 from report 1C0
and copies them into buﬀer 33. Position the cursor on line
12, erase to the end of the line, and enter the following:
>]8d
Equivalent Statement
@lnd,0,c,1,12,8,33 .

Delete Line and @LNUse the Delete Line function or LN- statement to delete lines
below the date line from a report.
For the Delete Line manual function, the report must be on
display.
For the LN- statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LN- statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.

command.

Delete Line Outcome
Executing the Delete Line command causes the following
actions to occur:
The command deletes the speciﬁed lines from the
report; it does not create a -0 result.
If you use the menu procedure, the command also
copies the deleted lines to a buﬀer so that you can put
them in another report using the Put Line command. For
more information, see Put Line and @LNP in this
document.

Delete Line Procedures
To delete just one line or a few lines

1. Place the cursor on the line you want to delete.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press Delete once for each line to be deleted.
Or

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to be deleted.
2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Delete Line in-report-line format.
To delete several lines

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to delete.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed. Type
the number of lines in the Delete lines ﬁeld and
transmit. If you specify more lines than the report
contains, the command deletes all lines following the
request. Default = 1.
To undo a delete (to regain lines immediately after deleting
them), press Undo.

Delete Line Example
This example deletes two lines from report 1B0, starting at
line 15. Position the cursor on line 15, erase to the end of
the line, and enter the following: >]2Equivalent Statement
@ln-,0,b,1,15,2 .

@DEV Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the number of devices of the type speciﬁed

Device Outcome
Executing the Device command as a manual function
causes the following actions to occur:
The command displays a screen listing any devices
conﬁgured for the speciﬁed station. If there are no
conﬁgured devices, the command displays a message
indicating that a device is not conﬁgured for that
station.
To return to the current report, press Paint.

@DEV Statement Outcome
Executing the DEV statement causes the following actions
to occur:
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
If devices are conﬁgured for the speciﬁed station, the
command lists them in a drawer A -0 result; however, if
you use variables, the command does not create a -0
result.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
If no devices are conﬁgured, the run goes to the
speciﬁed label.
2200 Þ Þ
If devices are conﬁgured for the speciﬁed station and no
unit is speciﬁed, the run lists them in a result in drawer
A.
In addition, if the speciﬁed unit is not conﬁgured, the
run also goes to the label.

DEVMAP (Device Mapping)
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the Device Mapping (DEVMAP) command to
Temporarily change your terminal or auxiliary printer
type.
Immediately reconﬁgure your terminal or auxiliary
printer after the administrator makes changes to your
device conﬁguration.

Note: This command is not available on workstations using
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
software.
Here are some of the situations in which you might want to
use the Device Mapping command:
The administrator has made changes to your device
conﬁguration and you want the changes to take eﬀect
immediately.
You want to have a diﬀerent set of colors displayed on
your terminal.
You want to switch auxiliary printers.
You want to sign on as a nonconﬁgured station and
would like to switch to a speciﬁc terminal type.

Device Mapping Control Line Format Fields
DEVMAP
DEVMAP [ttype,ptype]

Field

Description

ttype

Type of terminal, PC, or
workstation (deﬁned in the
administrator database-see
your administrator for more
information).

ptype

Type of auxiliary printer
(deﬁned in the administrator
database-see your
administrator for more
information).

Device Mapping Outcome
Executing the Device Mapping command causes the
following actions to occur:
The command immediately reconﬁgures your terminal
or printer.
If you specify the same device that you signed on with,
the command picks up any changes made in the
administrator's Device Conﬁguration report since you
signed on.
If you specify a new device, the command reconﬁgures
your terminal or printer. Note that if you do this, but the
administrator does not change the Device Type or Aux
Device ﬁelds in the Station Registration report, the
device change is eﬀective only for the current session.
The system uses the original conﬁguration once you exit
the session and start a new session.

Device Mapping Examples
This example changes the terminal type to PCEG (a PC with
an EGA card) and the auxiliary printer to a model 115.
devmap pceg,115
This example changes the terminal type to PCEG (a PC with
an EGA card) and removes the auxiliary printer: devmap
pceg,
This example changes the printer type to a model 115
printer: devmap ,115
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

@DFA (Defer Additive)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Defer Additive statement to add a report to an
existing deferred-update list, or to create a new one. Like
other deferred updates, these updates will be held until a
Commit Updates (CMU) or a Decommit Updates (DCU)
statement is encountered. See the Defer Updates (DFU)
statement for background and guidelines.

Defer Additive Statement Format
@DFA,c,d,r[,timeout,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

The report on which to defer
updates. The total number of
reports is subject to the same
limits as for DFU.

timeout

The number of seconds to wait
for the update to become
available. Defaults to zero,
which may result in an
indeﬁnite wait. Specify -1 to
avoid a wait altogether.

lab

Label to go to when a request
fails to acquire the lock. There
is no default action from BIS:
the script will fail if the request
fails and there is no label.

When successful, execution will resume with the next
statement with STAT1$ = 0. The following table summarizes
the return values:

Condition

STAT1$

STAT2$

STAT3$

Success

0

0

0

Timeout

1

MGUPIN

Unit #

Deadlock

2

MGDEAD
or
MGAUDT

Unit #

Other
error

3

MGxxxx

0

Stall

4

MGSTAL

0

Notes:
The ﬁrst deferred update may be @DFU or @DFA;
subsequently deferred updates can only be @DFA
@DFA attempts on a result or a report that is already
locked are ignored.
Timeout defaults to <forever> if zero or unspeciﬁed;
any negative value will force an immediate return with
no wait at all. A station abort condition (kill, msg wait)
will penetrate the wait-for-update condition (MGABRT)
and abort the script.

The ‘deadlock’ status (MGDEAD) indicates that waiting
for the requested lock would create a deadlock
condition. It is useless to retry this lock after receiving
this status, and usually best to decommit any updates
already made and start over.
The ‘audit’ status (MGAUDT) indicates a condition where
the system has been waiting for some time to start a
Cycle or a Pack while this script has been waiting to lock
another report, and the system has waited too long.
Subsequent requests are likely to fail with this same
status. It is generally best to decommit any updates
already made and start over.
The ‘update in progress’ status (MGUPIN) is the usual
indication of a timeout return where the target report
was locked for update by another unit. It is not a good
idea to try to delay and try again because:
it will eﬀectively defeat the system’s ability to
detect deadlock conditions;
it will most likely be futile.
Other errors may include various illegal operations,
Index ﬁle full, Update ﬁle full, etc
The ‘update stall’ status (MGSTAL) indicates that this
initial DFA request was made when deferred updates are
suspended. Unlike the others, do try again later.
Ü Ü 2200

@DFC (Set Default Color)
Use the Set Default Color (DFC) statement to set the default
foreground and background colors of a Graphical User
Interface (GUI) run. If a run does not specify a color within
the GUI control statement, the default foreground and
background colors are used.
The DFC statement provides the colors for the following
statements, if a color is not speciﬁed within the statement:
Deﬁne Button (BTN)
Deﬁne Combo Box (CBX)
Deﬁne Edit Box (EDT)
Deﬁne List Box (LST)
Deﬁne Text Box (TXT)
Deﬁne Window Display (WIN)
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
This statement does not set colors for the main window.

If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
If you specify only one color (omitting the slant), that
color is used for both foreground and background.
If you specify a color as one space with single quotes,
that color is the default color. The default color is
deﬁned to be the colors speciﬁed in the previous DFC
statement. If there are no DFC statements, the default
colors are black foreground and white background.

Set Default Color Statement Format
@DFC,fc/bc .

Field

Description

fc/bc

Sets the default foreground
and background colors for the
following controls while the
run is active:
BTN, CBX, EDT, LST, TXT, WIN
See Set Default Color Codes
for available colors.

Set Default Color Codes
You designate the choice of colors for the run or the
individual controls. You can specify the default colors for a
run by using the DFC statement. You can also specify the
colors of a particular control within the controls statement.
The following is a list of controls that allow for color
speciﬁcation: BTN, CBX, EDT, LST, TXT, and WIN. You can
select from three categories of colors: Microsoft Windows
deﬁned colors, MAPPER colors, or red, green, and blue (RGB)
colors. You can mix and match these selections throughout
a run, or within an individual control.
You can designate the colors of the user’s PC conﬁgured
default colors. These are the colors the user has speciﬁed
within their Windows Display Properties on their PC. The
color WIN or 3D uses the Windows or 3D objects colors
speciﬁed within the user’s Windows Display Properties on
their PC. You can choose the colors of the runs display,
based upon your users PC conﬁguration.
The run can specify the colors in the MAPPER color codes
(see Color Code Table). These colors allow for basic color
speciﬁcation.
A run can also specify the colors of red, green, and blue
(RGB) color codes. The RGB color codes are used to specify
the colors in current Windows and Web applications. The
RGB color codes are three numbers that deﬁne the amount
of red, green, and blue in a color. Each of the numbers can
have the hexadecimal value of 0 to FF, allowing for a color
palette of over 16 million colors.
The following table lists all of the MAPPER colors and a small
number of the possible RGB color codes.

Notes:

For win to be a valid color, check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if available.
For RGB color codes to be valid colors, check DWCAP$
for availability: DWCAP$(13-1) = 1 if available.
For 3D to be a valid color, check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(13-1) = 1 if available.
For 3DB, 3DD, and 3DL to be valid colors, check
DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(14-1) = 1 if available.
When specifying win or 3D for either the foreground or
background color, the Windows-conﬁgured
foreground/background color pair will be used. For
example, red/win produces the same aﬀect as win/win
or just win.
Magenta is the same color as dark pink.

Set Default Color Examples
This example sets the default colors to white text on a blue
background.
@dfc,whi/blu .
This example sets the default colors to dark gray text on a
light yellow background.
@dfc,606060/FFFFC0 .

@DFU (Defer Updates)
Use the Defer Updates (DFU) statement to put an update
lock on one or more reports and defer (that is, hold) all
subsequent updates made to the reports until a Commit
Updates (CMU) or Decommit Updates (DCU) statement is
encountered.
If a run fails or aborts, or if the system aborts, the system
decommits all updates, restoring the reports to the state
they were before the DFU statement was processed.

Defer Updates Statement Format
@DFU[,lab] c,d,r[,c,d,r,...,c,d,r] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an update lock
cannot be granted for any of
the speciﬁed reports.

c,d,r

Reports on which to defer
updates. The maximum
number of reports that can be
speciﬁed is site conﬁgurable
(10-64). You cannot lock
results, and you cannot lock
an entire drawer by specifying
only a cabinet and drawer.

Defer Updates Guidelines
Whenever you use a DFU statement, you must also use a
CMU or DCU statement later in the run to either commit or
decommit the updates.
To perform update functions such as Match Update (MAU)
and Search Update (SRU) while in deferred updating, you
must list the reports to process in the DFU statement.
If any of the reports to be locked by the DFU statement have
already been locked by the LOK statement, the DFU
statement ﬁrst releases them before locking them.
To minimize update lock conﬂicts between users, always try
to minimize the length of time that reports are kept locked.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You cannot execute the following statements between a DFU
statement and a CMU or DCU statement: DFU, DSF, DSG,
DSM, DSP, DSX, GOC (when display requested), GS (when
display requested), INP, LNK, LOK, NET, NOF, NRM, NRT,
OUM, OUT, REL, RRN, RTN, RUN, SC, WAT, XIT, or XUN.
When you use the DFU statement, all reports deferred must
be either audited or non-audited. The function cannot be
performed on both kinds of reports at the same time. The
purpose of the DFU statement is to ensure that all reports
are maintained accurately. If some of the deferred reports
are backed up and the audit tapes are used to restore the
database, the deferred reports may not be correct.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

You cannot execute the following statements between a DFU
statement and a CMU or DCU statement: BTN, CBX, DDE,
DFC, DFU, DSF, DSG, DSM, DSP, DSX, EDT, FON, GOC (when
display requested), GS (when display requested), HELP, HID,
INP, LNK, LOK, LST, MBX, MNU, NET, NOF, NRD, NRM, NRN,
NRT, NWR, OUM, OUT, PC, PCF, PIC, REL, RRN, RTN, RUN, SC,
SHW, SIZ, TIP, WAT, or XIT.
Compared to traditional LOK/WRL sequences for updating,
deferred updating causes extra lines to be processed by the
system and may increase response time. Follow these
guidelines to minimize the impact on the system:
Keep all reports you are updating as small as possible.
When networking to Business Information Server for
ClearPath OS 2200, the initiating run is not allowed to
perform a DFU via the Network Run (NRN) statement.
If possible, limit the number of Write Line (WRL)
statements to one or two.

Note: If you must use several WRL statements, consider
using the ccpy subﬁeld in the WRL statement, which
forces the system to write a complete copy of the
report. Though this may cost additional I/O overhead to
update the report, it can considerably decrease the cost
of other users attempting to access the same report.
See also: @DFA
Ü Ü 2200
Some functions place internal update locks on reports
before processing them because there is a possibility for
update. If you intend to use these functions while deferring

updates, you must include the reports they will be
processing on the DFU statement.
Following is an example of statements that place internal
locks on reports:
Binary Find (BFN) with the U option
Calculate Update (CAU)
Delete Report (DLR)
Locate (LOC) with the O and U options
Locate and Change (LCH) with the O and U options
Match Update (MAU)
Replace Report (REP)
Search Update (SRU)

Defer Updates Examples
This example defers updates to a report and goes to a label
if update control cannot be granted for the report:
@dfu,100 0,b,2 .
This example defers updates and then executes either a
CMU or DCU statement: @ dfu 0,b,2,0,c,1 .
.
. (updates to reports 2B0 and 1C0)
.
@ sru,0,b,2,,100 ad 15-9 |,blackbox2 ext .
.
. (other processing)
.
@ cmu .
@ rel .
@100:dcu .

Defer Updates Reserved Words
Reserved
Word

Description

DFULOK$

The number of
reports locked
under deferred
updates

DFUACT$

The number of
reports locked
under deferred
updates that
have actually
been updated

DH (Display and Hold Headings)
Use the Display and Hold Headings (DH) command to hold
the headings at the top of the screen as you roll through,
shift, or change the format of the report. You can use the
command from any position in the report.
The report in which to hold headings must be on display.

Display and Hold Headings Control Line Format
DH

Display and Hold Headings Outcome
The command displays the heading lines at the top of the
screen.

@ DIAG (Generating Diagnostic
Reports)
Use the Generating Diagnostic Reports ( DIAG) statement to
generate reports containing detailed script information.
When the script is in error mode (processing inside of an
RER routine), the DIAG statement reports any error
information captured at the time of the error, and each
section heading of the report clearly states if the
information was derived from error capture data.

Generating Diagnostic Reports Statement
Format
@ DIAG,c,d[,lab] o[,levels,lines] .

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer into which
the data should be placed. The
data is returned as a -0 result.

lab

Label to go to if an error
condition is encountered.

o

Options for generating
diagnostic reports. See
Generating Diagnostic Reports
Options for available options.
You must specify at least one
option.

levels

Number of call levels to
process for options R and V.
The DIAG processes from the
current call level down to call
level zero. Default = 1.

lines

Number of lines to dump from

each rename report if the R
option is speciﬁed. Default =
no report dump. Limited to
5000 lines for each report.

Generating Diagnostic Reports Options
Option

Description

A

All available information for all
options.

C

Call and RSR stack
information.

G

General information.

2200 Þ Þ

I

Index of the Session Global
Report Table, which contains
the names of the reports
placed in one of the Session
Global slots.

J

Jump History information. If
enabled by the RER option.

Ü Ü 2200

N

Narrow display. Limit variable
dump to an 80-column report
even though the output report
is wide enough to handle 132
characters.

R

Renamed report information
and optionally dumping of
those reports if lines is
nonzero.

S

Dump a segment of the erred
script starting 10 lines before
the erred line and ending 10
lines after the erred line.

V

Dump global variables and
local variables. If executed in
an RER routine, it dumps the
local variables that were
captured at the time of the
error if access to them is
allowed. Access to error
capture local variables is
allowed if one of the following
is true:
The RER routine is at the
same Call Level as the
error.

The executing user-id has
RDB access to the erred
RCR.

Generating Diagnostic Reports Example
This example shows general diagnostic information.
.DATE 03 JAN 05 12:39:59 RID MAPPER
.Diagnostic Information
*================================================
===
.----- General Information -----------------------** G option information is placed here **
.----- Failing Script Dump -----------------------** S option information is placed here **
.----- Call Stack Information --------------------** C option information is placed here **
.----- Jump History ------------------------------** J option information is placed here **
.----- Local Variables ---------------------------** V option information is placed here **
.----- Rename Information ------------------------** R option information is placed here **

@DIR (Directory)
Use the Directory (DIR) statement to load variables with
information about a report, drawer, or cabinet name from
the system directory.
With the DIR statement you can determine whether a data
name is valid. You can also determine what kind of data the
name represents (report, drawer, cabinet, or range of
reports).

Directory Statement Format
@DIR[,lab] name
[vcabinet,vdrawer,vrpt,vhirptr,vsec,vscript] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the name is
invalid. If the statement does
not contain a label, the script
fails.

name

Data name. This name is
usually a name in the System
Directory. It can also be a
standard report identiﬁer, in
the rdc format where r is the
report number, d is the
drawer letter, and c is the
cabinet number. For example,
1b218 is report number 1 in
drawer b of cabinet 218.

vcabinet

Variable to capture the
cabinet number.

vdrawer

Variable to capture the drawer
letter.

vrpt

Variable to capture the report
number.

vhirptr

Variable to capture the higher
report number if the data
name represents a range of
reports.
It will be -1 if name is a
Namelist.

vsec

Variable to capture the drawer
access permission for the user
executing the script. The user
security registration reports
contain the access level that
each user has for each
drawer. The variable is loaded
with a space (no access), R
(read only access), or W (full
write access).

vscript

Variable to capture the
cabinet access permission for
the executing script. The
script registration reports
contain the cabinets that each
script can access. The
variable is loaded with a
space (no access), R (read
only access), or W (full write
access).

Directory Outcome
The statement loads variables with information from the
system directory.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the vdrawer, vrpt, or vhirptr variable does not apply to a
name, it contains 0 if the variable is an I type variable;
otherwise, it contains spaces. For example, vhirptr does not
apply unless the name is for a range of reports.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If the vdrawer, vrpt, or vhirptr variable does not apply to a
name, it contains spaces. For example, vhirptr does not
apply unless the name is for a range of reports.
Ü Ü 2200

Directory Reserved Words
If the data name supplied is invalid and the script continues
at the label (the script fails if you do not use the label),
STAT1$ contains one of the following error codes:

1

Data name was not found in
the system directory.

2

Data name does not begin with
an alphabetic character (A to
Z).

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

3

Data name contains an invalid
character (^, ;, /, tab).

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

3

Data name contains no
alphanumeric characters (A to
Z or 0 to 9).

Ü Ü 2200

4

Data name contains more than
16 characters.

This example loads variables with information about the
name order-status: @dir,099 order-status <cabinet>i4,
<drawer>h1,<report>i4,\
<hireport>i4 .

Go to label 99 if the name
order-status does not exist.

order-status

obtain information.

cabinet number of orderstatus.

<drawer>h1 Load <drawer> with the
drawer letter.

<report>i4

Load <report> with the
report number.

<hireport>i4 Load <hireport> with the
higher range report
number.

This example loads variables with information about the
system-deﬁned name 2BO: @dir,099 2BO <cabinet>i4,
<drawer>h1,<rid>i4,,<sec>h1,<run>h1 .

0099

Go to label 0099 if the
name is not deﬁned.

'2BO'

System-deﬁned name to
obtain information about
'2BO'.
