cabinet number (0).

<drawer>h1 Load <drawer> with the
drawer letter (B).

<rid>i4

Load <rid> with the report
number (2).

<sec>h1

Load <sec> with the user
security access
(Read/Write).

<run>h1

Load <run> with the run
registration access
(Read/Write).

DISPLAY
Use the Display run to present a series of charts, like a slide
show. Use charts created using the Chart and Sign
commands.
Save the graphics primitive code for the charts you want to
display, and keep a list of these graphics primitive code
reports.
The run presents a continuous display of charts, which you
can run automatically or control manually.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Display Control Line Format
DISPLAY[,o,{report | drawer},l,cyc?,interval]

Field

Description

o

Options ﬁeld. See Display Options.

d

Drawer letter in which to place the
index list report. Use this subﬁeld with
the NEW option: DISPLAY,NEW,d

report

Index list report.

l

Line number within the index list
report at which to begin displaying
charts. Default = 5. Use this subﬁeld
with the RUN option:

DISPLAY,RUN,report[,l]
cyc?

Cycle continuously? Y, N, or number of
cycles (1 - 99). Default = N (cycles
once). Use this subﬁeld with the CYC
option:

DISPLAY,CYC,report[,,cyc?,interval]
Y = Cycles until you press Abort.

interval Number of seconds between displays,
up to 60 seconds. Default = 3. Use this
subﬁeld with the CYC option:

DISPLAY,CYC,report[,,cyc?,interval]

Display Options
Option

Description

ADD

Adds the displayed graphics
primitive code report to the
index list report.

CYC

Displays charts automatically.

NEW

Sets up an index list report.

RUN

Displays charts manually.

Display Guidelines
When using the RUN option in the Display run, you can
display each chart in the list by pressing Resume.
When using the CYC option in the Display run, you can press
Abort to display a selection screen. If you press Resume
with the selection screen displayed, the Display run resumes
at the next sequential line of the index list report. If you
press F2 (Back up), the Display run backs up one line in the
index list report and displays that chart.

Display Example
This example illustrates how to use the Display run to show
a sequence of charts. First, create an index list report in
drawer C: display,new,c
Then display, in turn, each report containing graphics
primitive code for the charts, and add each to the index,
using the following command: display,add,3c
Finally, display the charts, either manually, as in the ﬁrst
example, or automatically, as in the second example. The
second example cycles through the charts ﬁve times with
four-second intervals between each chart.
The charts are then displayed, either manually:
display,run,3c
Or automatically:
display,cyc,3c,,5,4

DL (Drawer List)
Use the Drawer List (DL) command to display a list of
drawers you can access. The Drawer List command is
similar to the Report key, with the exception that the
Drawer List control line procedure enables you to specify a
diﬀerent cabinet.
The command displays a list of all drawers you are
registered to access, from which you can select to display a
list of reports in that drawer. Or, if you specify a cabinet, this
command displays a list of drawers you can access in that
cabinet.

Note: If you specify another cabinet, you do not actually
switch to that cabinet.

Drawer List Control Line Format
DL [c]
where c is the cabinet number, if diﬀerent from your current
cabinet, from which to display a list of drawers.

DLC (Display Line Count)
Use the Display Line Count (DLC) command to display line
numbers along the right side of the screen. To remove the
line numbers from the screen, press Resume.
A report must be on display.

Display Line Count Control Line Format
DLC[,S]
where S displays the report line count and screen line count.
Default = displays report line numbers along the right side
of the screen.

@DLR (Delete Report)
See DR and @DLR (Delete Report) for more information.

@DPUR (DDP Purge)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the DDP Purge (DPUR) statement to delete a ﬁle on a
DDP 1100 host.

DDP Purge Statement Format
@DPUR[,lab]
host,fn[,user,pw,cllv,acct,projid,qual] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error status
is returned by the DDP 1100
host.

host

Name of the host conﬁgured in
DDP 1100 where the ﬁle is to
be deleted or backed up.
Specify the host here as you
specify it in the IPF
environment.

fn

Name of the ﬁle to be deleted
or backed up. If the ﬁle name
contains special characters,
enclose the ﬁeld in
apostrophes ( ' ).

user

User-id used to sign on to a
speciﬁed host.

pw

Password used along with the

user-id to sign on to a
speciﬁed host.

cllv

Two-character value indicating
the clearance level of the
speciﬁed host.

acct

Account number used for a
speciﬁed host.

projid

Project-id used for a speciﬁed
host.

qual

Qualiﬁer used when a ﬁle
name contains a leading
asterisk (*).

DDP Purge Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the interface error, STAT2$ contains the
CLASS-CODE, and STAT3$ contains the DETAIL-STATUS code.
These are error codes and are listed in the DDP PPC/DDP FJT
Messages Reference Manual.

DDP Purge Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the DPUR statement:
You can remove all evidence of the existence of a ﬁle
with the DPUR statement.
DDP 1100 host provides protection to ensure that
unauthorized deletion of ﬁles does not occur. Therefore,
the name you specify must include any read and write
keys.
You can delete only one ﬁle with each DPUR statement.
You can use the DPUR statement to delete entire
program or data ﬁles; you cannot delete elements of a
ﬁle.

DDP Purge Example
The DPUR statement deletes the ﬁle A*B. on host RSHS:
@dpur,1 rshs,'a*b' .
Ü Ü 2200

DPW (Drawer Password)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Drawer Password (DPW) function to access a drawer
locked with a password. The administrator assigns the
drawer password.

Note: Statements can overwrite reports with drawer
passwords. This function restricts only manual function
users.
You can maintain access for up to two drawers at the same
time until you display the active screen.

Drawer Password Control Line Format
DPW
When you enter DPW, the system displays DPW Enter
Drawer Password In the DPW ﬁeld, type the password for the
drawer and transmit.
You must use the control line procedure to access the
Drawer Password function. The function automatically
repeats transparent password prompting until you enter a
valid password.
Ü Ü 2200

DR and @DLR (Delete Report)
Use the Delete Report (DR) function to remove a report from
the database.
Use the DLR statement to delete a report, result, or
renamed report. Unlike the Delete Report manual function,
the DLR statement enables you to delete a report even if
you were not the last person to update it. You can also
delete reports with read or write passwords, as well as
encoded reports.
Executing this command as a manual function deletes the
report from the database and displays a message indicating
that the report was deleted.
For the Delete Report manual function, the report to delete
must be on display and your user-id must be the same as
the user-id of the person who created or last updated the
report.
Executing this statement deletes the speciﬁed report. If the
report does not exist, the run goes to the label, if speciﬁed.

Delete Report Control Line Format
DR [report]
where report is the report to delete. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process in this document.

Delete Report Statement Format
@DLR,c,d,r[,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to delete. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

lab

Label to go to if the report
does not exist. (To simplify
your run logic, if you want the
run to continue on the next
line, use LIN1 in this subﬁeld
instead of a label number.) If
the report number exceeds
the maximum number of
reports for the system, the
command limit will not process
the label.

Delete Report Guidelines
To regain a report immediately after you delete it with the
Delete Report manual function, press Undo. To regain a
report after you delete it and then execute other functions,
ask your administrator whether you can retrieve it from a
backup tape.

Delete Report Examples
This statement deletes report 3B0:
@dlr,0,b,3 .
This statement deletes the -3 result:
@dlr,-3 .

@DRW (Drawer)
Use the Drawer (DRW) statement to load variables with the
following information about a drawer: number of characters
per line, next available report to be added, highest report
number, and the report and line limits.

Drawer Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

vcpl,,,,vnxrd,vhirptd,vlnd,vrptsd,vrlmt,vllmt,
vrslt,vdrw] .

2200 Þ Þ
@DRW,c,d[,lab
vcpl,vcs,vmfno,vmfn,vnxrd,vhirptd,vlnd,vrptsd,
vrlmt,vllmt,vrslt,vdrw] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to
process.

lab

Label to go to if the drawer
does not exist.

vcpl

Variable to capture the
number of characters per line.

2200 Þ Þ

vcs

Variable to capture the
character set type: 0 LCS
1 FCS
2 FCSU

vmfno

Variable to capture the MAPER
ﬁle number (for example, 1, 2,

resides. (MAPER ﬁles are OS

Variable to capture the

vdrw

Variable to capture the drawer
description.

Drawer Outcome
If the drawer exists, the DRW statement loads the speciﬁed
variables. STAT1$ contains the number of the highest report
in the drawer.
If the drawer does not exist, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label. If there is no label, the run continues at the
next statement. STAT1$ = 0.

Drawer Example
This example loads <chrs> with the number of characters
per line in cabinet 0, drawer B. If the drawer does not exist,
the run continues at label 5.
@drw,0,b,005 <chrs>i3 .

@DSF (Display Form)
Use the Display Form (DSF) statement to display a report
containing screen commands (a form) and return control to
the run. From within a run, use the DSF statement instead of
the SC statement to display a form starting at a particular
page number.

Display Form Statement Format
@DSF,c,d,r[,pn,tabp,stack] o [fldtxt] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the form.

pn

Page number within the form
at which to start the display.
This is valid only if the
speciﬁed form uses paged
data.

tabp

Tab position at which to place
the cursor. A positive number
represents a forward tab
position (up to 100) and a
negative number represents a
backward tab position (up to
-100).
Default = 1. 0 = no
positioning.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

stack

Return stack action. Use one

of these values to specify the
action for the return stack:
0-puts this form on the top of
the stack (default).
1-clears the stack and puts
this form on the bottom.
2-marks the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
3-saves the context of the
current form so a return
restores the screen as it
currently exists. This is useful
for help displays.
4-does not put an entry on the
return stack for this form.
5-overwrites the current entry
on the return stack with this
form.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

stack

Return stack action. Use one
of these values to specify the
action for the return stack:

0-puts this form on the top of
the stack (default).
1-clears the stack and puts
this form on the bottom.
2-marks the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
3-saves the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
The screen itself is not saved.
4-does not put an entry on the
return stack for this form.
5-overwrites the current entry
on the return stack with this
form.

Ü Ü 2200

o

T-ﬁeld text option. Inserts text
supplied in the ﬂdtxt ﬁeld into
unprotected screen ﬁelds.
Using this option, you can add
values to menu ﬁelds. See
Field Attribute Commands.

ﬂdtxt

Field values to be passed to
the form when the T option is

present. This ﬁeld is a list of
items separated by commas.
The ﬁrst item is placed in the
ﬁrst ﬁeld, the second item in
the second ﬁeld, and so on.

Display Form Example
This example displays the form in report 36E6 starting at
page 100, placing the cursor at tab position 1. It passes the
data values "redeem" and the current user-id to the form
and does not put the form on the return stack.
@dsf,6,e,36,100,1,4 't' redeem,user$ .

@DSG (Display Graphics)
See G and @DSG (Display Graphics) for more information.

@DSM (Display Message)
Use the Display Message (DSM) statement to display your
own one-line message at the top of the screen. The
message must reside in a report or result. You display it by
specifying its line number.
There are two ways to use the DSM statement:
Display a message at the top of an existing screen.
Display a report or result with the selected message at
the top of the screen. The displayed report or result can
begin at line 1 or at a speciﬁed line number.

Display Message Statement Format
@DSM,c,d,r,lmsg[,tabp,erase?,interim?,pdq,dc,dd,d
r,dspl,dspf] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the
message.

lmsg

Line number of the message
in the report.

tabp

Tab position after which to
place the cursor. Use a
negative number to indicate
the number of tab positions to
move backwards. Maximum =
100. Default = home position.

erase?

Erase the screen? Y or N.
Default = N.

interim?

Interim display? Y or N. If Y,
the run continues
automatically when the
display is complete. Default =
N.

pdq

Number of lines to push down
on the screen. Default = 0.
The system ignores this
subﬁeld if you use the dc, dd,
or dr subﬁelds.

dc

Cabinet of the report to
display.

dd

Drawer of the report to
display.

dr

Report to display.

dspl

Line number of the speciﬁed
report at which to start the
display. Default = 1.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

dspf

Format of the report to display.
Omit this subﬁeld if you are
displaying a format deﬁned
with an FMT or SFC statement.

Format = 0-25. Default = 0,
basic format.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

dspf

Format of the report to display.
Omit this subﬁeld if you are
displaying a format deﬁned
with an FMT or SFC statement.
Format = 0-6. Default = 0,
basic format.

Ü Ü 2200

Display Message Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The DSM statement displays the speciﬁed message at
the top of the screen or report you speciﬁed.
Messages displayed using the DSM statement have the
following characteristics:
The system highlights the text in the same manner
as the system messages.
The system translates all less than (<) and greater
than (>) characters into blinking characters.
If you have a 132-character terminal, the system
automatically centers the message.
Executing the DSM statement clears the output area.

Display Message Reserved Words
If you display a message with no report on display, you can
use the following reserved words to capture input data the
run user enters:
INVAR$
INVR1$
INPUT$
INSTR$

Display Message Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the DSM statement:
You can customize the report format by preceding the
DSM statement with a Format (FMT) or Set Format
Characters (SFC) statement.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the DSM statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
The DSM statement stalls the run until the run user
presses Resume, unless you specify the interim display,
in which case, the run continues automatically. Note
that the DSM statement clears the output area.
The text displayed by the DSM statement appears as it
does for system messages.
The screen display contains a function key bar. You can
customize the function key bar so that you can include
operations not available with the standard function key
bar. For more information, see @FKY (Function Key) in
this document.
2200 Þ Þ
If you intend to display a report using the dc,dd,dr
subﬁelds and your message to display is in a -0 result,
use the Rename (RNM) statement to rename the
message beforehand.

Note: If an error routine is registered (RER), and the
DSM statement errors because a non-existent drawer or

report was speciﬁed, or it exceeded the largest report
for the drawer, or the line requested was beyond the
end of the report, the run will terminate, and the RER
will not be executed.
Ü Ü 2200

Display Message Examples
This example displays a message (located on line 4 of the
current result) at the top of the current screen: @dsm,-0,4 .
This example displays report 2D0, places a message
(located on line 4, report 12A0) on the top line of the screen,
and positions the cursor at the tenth tab position:
@dsm,0,a,12,4,10,,,,0,d,2 .

Use the Display Report and Exit (DSX) statement to display

statement.

Line number at which to start

100. Default (position 0) is the
Roll ﬁeld on the control line.

Report format. Default = 0,
basic format. Omit this ﬁeld if

deﬁned with a Format (FMT) or
Set Format Characters (SFC)
statement.

hold on the screen. The
requested report display
begins on the ﬁrst nonheld
line.

Message to display on the

Display Report and Exit Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the DSX statement:
You can customize the report format by preceding the

If an odd-numbered cabinet is speciﬁed, you will be
unable to update the report.

Display Report and Exit Examples
This example displays the current result, the renamed result
-3, and report 2B0, and then exits the run.
@dsp,-0 .
@dsp,-3 .
@dsx,0,b,2 .
This statement displays format 5 of report 1D0, beginning at
line 3, and includes a message: @dsx,0,d,1,3,,5,,,` This run
has ﬁnished processing. '

Use the Defer Unlock (DUL) statement to remove a nonupdated report from the deferred-update list.

Unlock Deferred Statement Format

@DUP (Duplicate Report)
See XR and @DUP (Duplicate Report) for more information.

Duplicate Line and @LNX
Use the Duplicate Line function or LNX statement to copy
lines in a report. Compare this command with the Insert Line
command:
The Duplicate Line command copies the lines at their
current location in the report.
The Insert Line command copies the lines to another
part of the report.
The command duplicates the speciﬁed lines; it does not
create a -0 result.
For the Duplicate Line manual function, the report must be
on display.
For the LNX statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LNX statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.

Duplicate Line In-Report-Line Format
>]xX[q]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

x

Number of times to duplicate
the line or lines.

X

Duplicate Line call.

q

Number of lines to duplicate.
Default = 1.

Duplicate Line Statement Format
@LNX,c,d,r,l,x[,q] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report in which lines are to be
duplicated. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process in this
document.

l

Number of the ﬁrst line to
duplicate.

x

Number of times to duplicate
the lines. No default.

q

Number of lines to duplicate.
Default = 1.

Duplicate Line Procedure
To duplicate lines

1. Press Edit.
2. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to be duplicated.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
4. In the Duplicate multiple lines ﬁeld, enter the
number of lines to duplicate.
Or

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to be duplicated.
2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Duplicate Line in-report-line format.

Duplicate Line Example
This example duplicates three lines, starting at line 6, ﬁve
times. Position the cursor on line 6, erase to the end of the
line, and enter the following: >]5x3
Equivalent Statement
@lnx,0,b,3,6,5,3 .

@DVS (Deﬁne Variable Size)
Use the Deﬁne Variable Size (DVS) statement to create
variables equal in size to report ﬁelds.
2200 Þ Þ
The statement creates a -0 result and releases any previous
-0 result.
Ü Ü 2200

Deﬁne Variable Size Statement Format
@DVS[,c,d,r,lab] field[,field,...,field]
v[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report that contains the ﬁelds
for which you want to deﬁne
corresponding variables.
Default = -0.

lab

Label at which to continue
execution if the speciﬁed ﬁelds
are not found.

ﬁeld

Name of the report ﬁeld for
which you want to deﬁne a
corresponding variable.

v

Name and type of the variable
you want to deﬁne. The
system assigns a size to the
variable that equals the size of
the corresponding report ﬁeld.
The ﬁrst variable you specify
corresponds to the ﬁrst ﬁeld
you specify, the second

variable you specify
corresponds to the second
ﬁeld you specify, and so on.

Deﬁne Variable Size Examples
This example deﬁnes variables equal in size to the Order
Number and Ord Qty ﬁelds in report 2B0: @dvs,0,b,2
'ordernumbr','ordqty' <num>s,<qty>i .
This example deﬁnes variables equal in size to the Order
Number and Ord Qty ﬁelds in the current result: @dvs
'ordernumbr','ordqty' <num>s,<qty>i .
This example uses the variables deﬁned by the DVS
statement to build an output area to capture user input:
@dvs 'ordernumbr','qty' <num>s,<qty>i .
@brk .
Enter Order Number: <num>,
Enter Quantity: <qty>,
@brk out,-0,2,23,1,1,y,,,p .
@chg input$ <num>,<qty> .

ECL and @ECL (Exec Control
Language)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Exec Control Language (ECL) function to submit an
@CAT or @SYM statement to the OS2200 Exec. The @CAT
command will catalog a mass storage or tape ﬁle. The
@SYM command will queue a ﬁle for printing or punching.
The @CAT statement must specify a sector-formatted disk
ﬁle or a tape ﬁle. It cannot have read or write keys. You may
specify a +1 cycle of an existing ﬁle. The @SYM command
must specify an existing data ﬁle. The qualiﬁer and ﬁle
name must conform to OS 2200 rules for standard names.

Element Control Line Format
ECL

Element Statement Format
@ECL[,lab] 'ECL-Command' [fac-stat] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go
to if the run
encounters
an error.

fac-stat

Facility
Request
Status Word.
If the
command
fails, this
variable will
capture the
36 bit (12digit) Facility
Request
Status. Refer
to the ECL
Operations
and
Programming
Reference for
complete
information
on the

Facility
Inventory
Status Bits.

ECLCommand

@CAT or
@SYM
command.

@ECL Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the following status codes:

2

File is already
assigned to the
system.

3

File is already
assigned
exclusively to
the system.

4

File is already
assigned to
another user.

5

File is already
assigned
exclusively to
another user.

7

Facilities
currently
unavailable.

12

File is a

MAPPER ﬁle.

13

System I/O
error.

14

Facility
warning or
reject.

15

Insuﬃcient or
improperly
formatted
statement.

17

Cycle
attempted on
nonexistent
ﬁle.

20

File creation
denied.

STAT2$ contains a line number identifying the system
message. Use a Load System Message (LSM) statement to
read the message. Place the number in STAT2$ in the
msgno subﬁeld in the LSM statement.

ECL Outcome
If you specify an @CAT command and the ﬁle is not
currently cataloged, the ECL function catalogs it. If you
specify an @SYM command, the ECL function will queue the
speciﬁed ﬁle for printing or punching.

ECL Guidelines
The qualiﬁer and ﬁle name must conform to OS 2200 rules
for standard names. Only alphabetic, numeric, and the
special characters dollar sign ($) and minus (-) can be
included. Refer to the ECL Operations and Programming
Reference for complete information on ﬁles and cataloging.
Do not specify read or write keys when cataloging ﬁles.
You can request a new cycle (+1) for an OS 2200 ﬁle on the
ECL command. If a new cycle is requested, the ﬁle is
cataloged with the same granularity, reserve, and pack-id (if
previously cataloged on a removable drive) as the original
ﬁle.

ECL Example
This statement creates MYQUAL*MYFILE (qualiﬁer*ﬁle) with
track granularity, maximum size of 999 tracks, on a
removable drive with a pack-id of J1D2. If an error occurs,
the script jumps to label 99, with variable V1 containing the
Facility Request Status Word bits.
@ECL,99 '@CAT,P MYQUAL*MYFILE.,f///999,J1D2'
V1H12 .
This example will queue MYQUAL*MYFILE (qualiﬁer*ﬁle) to
printer 'PR1', with a banner of MYPRNT. If an error occurs,
the script jumps to label 99, with variable V1 containing the
Facility Request Status Word bits.
@ECL,99 '@SYM,U MYQUAL*MYFILE.,,PR1,MYPRNT' V1H12
.
Ü Ü 2200

@ECR (Encode Report)
See ENCODE and @ECR (Encode Report) for more
information.

@EDT (Deﬁne Edit Box)
Use the Deﬁne Edit Box (EDT) statement to deﬁne an edit
box and display it on the user screen. Use the Accept Input
(INP) statement to accept input from the edit box.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
The statement creates an edit box that accepts input from
the user. The edit box remains on the screen until a CLS
statement closes it or until the run ends.

Deﬁne Edit Box Statement Formats
Format 1
@EDT,c,d,r[,l,q,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,fc/bc,o]
[veh] .
Format 2
@EDT[,"text",vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,fc/bc,o]
[veh] .

Note: In both formats, all tab characters are converted to
spaces.

Field

Description

c,d,r

(Format 1 only.) Report
containing the text to be
displayed.

l

(Format 1 only.) Line in the
report at which to start
reading lines. Default = 1.

q

(Format 1 only.) Number of
lines to read from the report.
Default = 1.

Note: Since the statement
places these lines in a buﬀer

and passes them to the
workstation, only about 6,500
characters can be displayed in
the edit box.

"text"

(Format 2 only.) Text to be
displayed in the edit box. Text
must be enclosed in quotation
marks. Variables are
translated to their contents. If
you do not specify text, place
two quotation marks in this
ﬁeld.

Note: The reverse slant (\) in
literal text may be interpreted
as a continuation character.
Use RSLANT$ (without
apostrophes) to avoid
misinterpretation or, place an
at sign (@) in column one of
the subsequent line.

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window or edit box handle.
Default = main window.
If you specify a window
handle, the run places the edit
box in that window, and all
vertical and horizontal
coordinates are relative to that
window.

If you specify an edit box
handle, the system assumes
that you are changing that edit
box in some way. If you do not
specify a value in the fc/bc,
typ, or text subﬁelds, the
system uses the original
values; however, you must
specify values in the vert, hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the edit box
using a positioning unit (or row
number). See @WIN (Deﬁne
Window Display) for more
information on sizing units.
When you specify a row
number, the position is based
on the main run font. See
@FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you are
specifying a value for the
vertical position, you must
also specify values in the hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit or
row number).

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the edit
box using a positioning unit (or

column number). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When you specify a
column number, the position is
based on the main run font.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you are
specifying a value for the
horizontal position, you must
also specify values in the vert,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit or
column number).

vsiz

Vertical size of the edit box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When you specify row
numbers, the size is based on
the text run font. See @FON
(Font) for more information on
fonts. If you are specifying a
value for the vertical size, you
must also specify values in the
vert, hort, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the edit box extends
to the bottom of the window in
which you place it and
expands and contracts with
that window.

hsiz

Horizontal size of the edit box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or column numbers). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on sizing units. When you
specify column numbers, the
size is based on the text run
font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the horizontal size, you must
also specify values in the vert,
hort, and vsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the edit box extends
to the right side of the window
in which you place it and
expands and contracts with
that window.

fc/bc

Foreground and background
colors for this edit box. See Set
Default Color Codes for the
available colors. If you specify
only one color (omitting the
slant), that color is used for
both foreground and
background.

o

Type of edit box being created.

See Deﬁne Edit Box Options
for available selections. Once
you create an edit box of a
certain type, you can change
that type only by closing the
box using the Close Control
(CLS) statement, and
recreating it, using the EDT
statement.
Default = left-justiﬁed, ﬁxed
text length (the user cannot
type past the borders of the
box.)

veh

Variable to capture the edit
box handle. This must be a sixcharacter variable.

Deﬁne Edit Box Options
Option

Description

A

Accepts alpha data only.

B

Places a border around the
edit box. If used, the row and
column sizes of the edit box
are one-half character larger
than the sizes speciﬁed in the
rsiz and csiz subﬁelds.

C

Centers input text. Use in
conjunction with the M option.
Do not use with the L or R
option. If you use this option
with the V and H options, the
system ignores the H option.

D

Produces a drop box (an edit
box with an arrow on the far
right). Click the arrow to
display a list of values
beneath the box. Click a
selection to place that
selection in the edit box. Do
not use the D option with the

M option. Minimum rows = 4.
Minimum columns = 7.
Supply the data to be
displayed in the list of values
in the "text" subﬁeld (format
2) or the c,d,r subﬁelds
(format 1). Separate multiple
items supplied in the "text"
subﬁeld with a slant (/).
A number (enclosed in
parentheses) following the D
option identiﬁes which item
(of those listed between the
quotation marks) is displayed
as the default value in the edit
box. If none is speciﬁed, no
value is displayed.

H

Scrolls horizontally. If a user
tries to type past the right
border of the edit box, the box
scrolls to the right, allowing
more room for text. The arrow
keys provide horizontal
movement within the box. Do
not use with the Z option.

J

Speciﬁes that when the ﬁeld is
full, the cursor moves to the
next ﬁeld. If you use a
variable pitch font, the J

option may not work as
expected. Do not use with the
H, T, V, or Z option.

L

Left-justiﬁes input text. Do not
use with the C or R option.

M

Accepts multiple lines of
input. The run places input
from this type of edit box into
a result with the same cabinet
and drawer as the current
output area. Do not use with
the H, T, V, or Z option.

N

Accepts numeric data only.

O

Displays input text in
lowercase characters. Do not
use with the U or P option.

P

Makes input text invisible on
the screen. Do not use with
the U or O option.

R

Right-justiﬁes input text. Use
in conjunction with the M
option. Do not use with the C

or L option. If you use this
option with the V and H
options, the system ignores
the H option.

S

Speciﬁes that the edit box is
to be sent immediately. If you
do not use this option, the
system does not send the box
until the current block is
transferred; this results in
increased performance. If you
do not want the Business
Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client or
Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server to
wait for the block transfer,
however, you must use the S
option.

T

Same as V, except that the
run places a scroll bar to the
right of the edit box, for
vertical movement within the
box. Do not use with the V
option.

U

Displays input text in
uppercase characters. Do not
use with the O or P option.

V

Scrolls vertically. If a user tries
to type past the bottom
border of the edit box, the box
scrolls down, allowing more
room in which to enter text.
The arrow keys provide
vertical movement within the
box. Do not use with the T
option.

Y

Allows the sizing of the control
to be in row and column units,
independent of the units used
for placement.

Z

Same as H, except that the
run places a scroll bar
beneath the edit box, for
horizontal movement within
the box. Do not use with the H
option.

3

Displays the edit box with 3D
eﬀects.

Notes:
Though the D option is still available, use the CBX
statement instead for added ﬂexibility.

Text in the Edit control box shows as selected and
highlighted at the time of creating and updating @EDT.
If a user selects a particular text with a mouse or
keyboard, then that many characters show as selected
and highlighted.
@EDT, J option follows the above two points at the time
of cursor movement (One @EDT control to another
@EDT control) when the current @EDT ﬁeld is full.
@INP follows the second and third points at the time of
making @EDT control as a focus window.

Deﬁne Edit Box Colors
See Set Default Color Codes for a list of valid colors that can
be used with the Deﬁne Edit Box statement.

Deﬁne Edit Box Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the EDT statement:
Use format 1 of the INP statement to highlight the text
at the insertion point while the run is suspended waiting
for input.
You can have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT,
LST, PIC, TXT, and WIN controls concurrently open.

Note: The actual number of handles created can be
lower depending on workstation PC memory
conﬁguration and usage.

Deﬁne Edit Box Examples
This example produces an edit box that allows input (a sixcharacter password) from the user. After the user enters a
password, the run continues processing at label 100.
Because the variable <key> contains the window handle for
the edit box, the insertion point is in the edit box while the
run waits for input.
@txt,"Enter password",<gate>,02,02,01,15 <pass>i6
.
@edt,"",<gate>,02,18,01,06,,p <key>i6 .
@inp,<key> <key>,(0100) .
@0100:inp,<key>,y .
@chg input$ <passwd>a6 .
This example produces a drop box. The values found within
the quotation marks are those that appear in the list of
values for the edit box, if requested. This list contains ﬁve
values (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5). The d(3) option means that the
edit box should appear with a default value of 3.
@edt,"1/2/3/4/5",v10,10,10,04,05,,d(3) v3i6 .
This example uses variables in a multiline edit box.
@ldv <txt1>s5=abc,<txt2>a3=ddd .
@edt,"
<txt1>/<txt2>",,004,010,005,0023,bla/whi,bam
<ed1>i6 .
@inp .

ELT and @ELT (Element)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Element (ELT) function or ELT statement to copy a
report to a standard OS 2200 data ﬁle or element (symbolic
or omnibus) of a program ﬁle.
A program ﬁle may be a standard program ﬁle (PF), a large
program ﬁle (LPF), or a large element program ﬁle (LEPF).

Note: Use the Retrieve File command to copy an OS 2200
ﬁle to a report.
The ﬁle must be a sector-formatted ﬁle with no read or write
keys, and it cannot be in read-only or write-only mode.
For the Element manual function, you must be in the
cabinet containing the report you wish to copy.

Element Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
If the ﬁle you are copying is not a currently assigned ﬁle
and the OS 2200 enables large ﬁles, the command
assigns it with a maximum of 262,143 positions.
Otherwise, the command assigns it with a maximum of
262,143 tracks.
When you request that headings not be included, the
outcome depends on whether you are processing a
report or a result.
Report: The command omits only the number of
heading lines deﬁned in report 0 of the drawer
where your report resides.
Result: The command omits only the date line.
The B option, in the MAPPER format ﬁeld, indicates
there are no heading lines.
To exclude all lines above and including the heading
divider line in all cases, use the Create File command
and request that headings not be included.
If the B option is used, the input report must be full
character set and is written as an omnibus element (no
standard data ﬁle control words) to the speciﬁed
program ﬁle.

Element Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
The qualiﬁer, ﬁle name, element, and version must all
conform to OS 2200 rules for standard names. Only
alphabetic, numeric, and the special characters dollar
sign ($) and minus (-) can be included. See the
Executive Control Language (ECL) and FURPUR
Reference Manual for complete information on ﬁles,
elements, and cataloging.
When you copy a report to the OS 2200 system, you can
control which data is copied. Use the same data control
commands you use with the Start command. You can
place these commands on any line in the report; the
commands take eﬀect from that point in the report.
Spaces at the right of data lines are not transferred.
Characters that cannot be translated are changed to
spaces.
Use the $INCL$ command to copy more than one report
or drawer.
You can request a new cycle (+1) for an OS 2200 ﬁle. If
a new cycle is requested, the ﬁle is cataloged public
with the same granularity and reserve as the original
ﬁle.
If you request to save an element, the ﬁle in which you
save it must be a program ﬁle, not a data ﬁle. If you do
not enter an element name, the ﬁle must be a data ﬁle.
If you request to save an existing element, the element
is marked for deletion and a new element is inserted. If

you request to save an existing data ﬁle, the ﬁle is
overwritten.
If you do not specify a ﬁle type, a large program ﬁle or a
large element program ﬁle is the default, depending on
the size of the element. If the ﬁle already exists, this
ﬁeld is ignored.

Element Example
This example creates MYQUAL*MYFILE (qualiﬁer*ﬁle) and
copies the data from report 2B0, to it; or the run goes to
label 99 if there is an error and the system cannot copy the
data.
@elt,0,b,2,99 myqual,myfile .
Ü Ü 2200

ELT- and @EL- (Element Delete)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Element Delete (ELT-) function or EL- statement to
delete a standard OS 2200 data ﬁle or symbolic element of
a program ﬁle.
A program ﬁle may be a standard program ﬁle (PF), a large
program ﬁle (LPF), or a large element program ﬁle (LEPF).
The system deletes the data ﬁle or symbolic element you
specify.
You cannot use this command to delete a ﬁle.
The ﬁle must be a sector-formatted ﬁle with no read or write
keys.

Ü Ü 2200

ENCODE and @ECR (Encode Report)
Use the Encode Report (ENCODE) function or ECR statement
to transform report data into unreadable code. You cannot
display the report data in a readable form until you supply
the correct key, using the Decode Report command.
This command is particularly useful for highly sensitive
reports and messages. It is more secure than read/write
passwords because no one, including the administrator, can
decode the report without knowing the key.
Because of high processing overhead, excessive use of this
command may aﬀect system performance.

Encode Report Control Line Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
ENCODE
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
ENCODE key
where key is a one- to eight-character code with which to
code the report. Valid characters are A through Z and 0
through 9.
Ü Ü 2200

Encode Report Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@ECR,c,d,r,key[,hdgs?,dsp?] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@ECR,c,d,r,key[,hdgs?,nodsp?] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to encode. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process in this
document.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

key

One- to eight-character key
with which to encode the
report.
Three encryption keys, that
are only eﬀective in a script,
are (literally):

key:user - limits decoding
to a script running under
the current user-id.
key:rcr - limits decoding to
a script in the same report
as that of the current
script.
key:site - limits decoding
to a script on the current
site.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

key

One- to eight-character key
with which to encode the
report. Valid characters are A Z and 0 - 9. The more
characters you use, the more
diﬃcult it is for an
unauthorized user to guess the
key.
Three encryption keys, that
are only eﬀective in a script,
are (literally):
key:user - limits decoding
to a script running under
the current user-id.

key:rcr - limits decoding to
a script in the same report
as that of the current
script.
key:site - limits decoding
to a script on the current
site.

Ü Ü 2200

hdgs?

Encode report headings? Y or
N. Default = N.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

dsp?

Create a displayable result? Y
or N. Default = N.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

nodsp?

Ü Ü 2200

Create a nondisplayable
result? Y or N. Default = N.

Encode Report Outcome
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
After executing the Encode Report command as a manual
function, the system displays a result containing only the
heading lines and a message at the top of the screen
indicating that the result is protected. (If you speciﬁed Y in
the Include Headers? ﬁeld, the headings are not displayed.)
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
After executing this command as a manual function, a result
is displayed with the data scrambled.
Ü Ü 2200

@ECR Statement Outcome
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Executing the Encode Report statement creates a result
containing encoded data. If you speciﬁed Y in the dsp?
subﬁeld, you can see the encoded data if you display or
print the report, though it is unreadable; otherwise, you
cannot see the encoded data.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Executing this statement creates a result with the data
scrambled. If you speciﬁed Y in the nodsp? subﬁeld, you
cannot see the encoded data.
Ü Ü 2200

Encode Report Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Encode Report
command:
Use caution when encoding reports. Do not forget your
key. A report cannot be decoded if you forget the key,
because no record of it is kept by the system. Your
administrator cannot tell you what it is.
Do not move encoded data between drawers of diﬀerent
report widths, because you will not be able to decode it.
Changing the width of the drawer causes all encoded
reports in that drawer to be corrupted and therefore
unreadable.
You must use the control line format or the ECR
statement to access the Encode Report command. The
menu selection is available only for online help
information.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You cannot display or update the result until you use the
Decode Report command.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Do not update an encoded report. Any change to the
encoded report corrupts the report and you will not be
able to decode it. Protect your report from updates by
using an update password.

You may not be able to decode data with a normal ASCII
terminal if the data was encoded with a National
Character Set (NCS) terminal. Also, if a report contains
special NCS characters, you cannot decode it with a
normal ASCII terminal.
Encoding a report approximately doubles its size.
Ü Ü 2200

Encode Report Example
This example encodes report 10B0 (including the report
headings), using the characters jak as the encryption key.
@ecr,0,b,10,jak,y .

@ESR (Exit Subroutine)
Use the Exit Subroutine (ESR) statement to exit a subroutine
and return to a speciﬁed line in the run control report
relative to the line containing the calling Run Subroutine
(RSR) statement.
Use an ESR statement in conjunction with the corresponding
RSR statement.

Note: When RSR calls another CALL function that has an
RER statement within it, any command failure encountered
inside the CALL function causes the ESR statement to fail.
This error occurs when an RSR, a CSR, or a RETURN
statement is encountered but does not match the current
subroutine type. For example, if an RSR is issued and that
subroutine contains a CALL statement, a RETURN statement
must be processed before an ESR or CSR statement can be
issued. Similarly, if a CALL subroutine contains an RSR
statement, an ESR or CSR statement must be processed
before a RETURN function can be performed.

Exit Subroutine Statement Format
@ESR[{,q|,-q}] .
where q is the relative number of lines after or before the
RSR statement to which the run should return. Use positive
numbers for lines following the RSR statement, negative
numbers for lines preceding the RSR statement. Default =
the line after the RSR statement.

Exit Subroutine Guideline
Do not place other statements on the same line after the
ESR statement. Other statements following it on the same
line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new line.

Exit Subroutine Example
This statement executes the subroutine at label 1. When the
subroutine ends, it returns to the line following the line
containing the RSR statement (run statement A):
@ rsr 001 .
. run statement A .
. run statement B .
. run statement C .
@ gto end .
@001: . (subroutine)
.
. (other processing)
.
@ esr .
To return to run statement B, enter the ESR statement as
follows:
@ esr,1 .

EXIT and @XUN (Exit MAPPER)
Use the Exit MAPPER (EXIT) function or XUN statement to
terminate the connection to the software. This terminates
your current run, terminates your active session, and
returns you to the previous environment.
Use the XUN statement to terminate the active run,
terminate your session, and return to the operating system.
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
For the XUN statement, if the run user executed the
software from the Linux or UNIX login, the Linux or UNIX logon prompt is displayed. If the run user executed the
software from a Linux or UNIX shell, the shell prompt is
displayed.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
If the user is connected to the system using Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server, the user session is
closed.
2200 Þ Þ
If the user is connected to the system using Uniscope or a
Uniscope emulator, the user session is closed and system
message MGCM02, “<MAPPER disconnected--enter
transaction name>” will be displayed.
Ü Ü 2200

Exit Control Line Format
EXIT

Exit Statement Format
@XUN .

Exit Procedures
To exit MAPPER, using the Exit key

1. Press the Return function key until you reach a screen
with SgnOﬀ on the function key bar.

2. Press SgnOﬀ to end your user session. (The inactive
logo is displayed.)

3. Press Exit (or type exit at the home position and
transmit)

EXT and @EXT (Extract)
Use the Extract (EXT) function to remove the lines displayed
in an update result from the original report and redisplay the
result as a normal result.
Use the EXT statement to remove the lines in an update
result from the original report and place those lines in an
ordinary -0 result.
You can then do other processing on the extracted lines,
such as duplicating the result to save the lines in a
permanent report.
For the Extract manual function, an update result from a
previous function must be displayed. For the EXT statement,
the update result must be the -0 result.

Note: Since the new result is no longer an update result,
you cannot use it to update the original report again.

Extract Control Line Format
EXT [psw]
where psw is the password required to perform an extract
operation on a write-protected report.

Extract Statement Format
@EXT .

Extract Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Extract command:
Only the lines in the update result are removed from the
original report when you execute the Extract command.
If you delete lines from the update result before you
execute the Extract command, those lines are not
removed from the original report.
To remove the lines from the original report and discard
the update result, use the Delete command. See DEL
and @DEL (Delete).
To replace lines in a report with the lines in an update
result, use the Update command. See UPD and @UPD
(Update).

F and @FND (Find)
Use the Find (F) function to ﬁnd character strings in
speciﬁed ﬁelds in a report or drawer and display the report
at the ﬁrst occurrence. To see more occurrences, press
Resume.
You can use the Iterative Find (IFND) run to save and reuse
Find function masks containing options and parameters and
to create FND statements.
Use the FND statement to search a report or drawer for data
and load variables with information about the search. Then
use a Read Line (RDL) statement, followed by a Read Line
Next (RLN) statement, to read the lines.
The Search (S), Find (F), and Binary Find (BF) manual
functions no longer require the minus sign (-) to indicate the
report or result currently on display. This method is
consistent with the Business Information Server for
ClearPath OS 2200. These functions still prompt the user for
a report if one is not on display. A minus sign is still
accepted on these functions.

Find Control Line Format
F {report|drawer} [f]

Field

Description

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

drawer

Drawer to process. Use this
ﬁeld to search all reports in a
given drawer.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

f

Report format in which to
search for data (enables you
to look for data in ﬁelds
beyond column 80 if you do
not already have those
columns on display).

Find Statement Format
@FND,c,d[,r,l,lab] o cc ltyp,p [vrpt,vlno,vcab,
vdrw] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report or drawer to search.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

l

Line number at which to start
the search.

lab

Label to go to if no ﬁnds are
made. If you do not specify a
label and no ﬁnds are made
(or the line or report does not
exist), the run continues.
(To simplify your run logic, if
you want the run to continue
on the next line, use LIN1 in
this subﬁeld instead of a label
number.)

o

Options ﬁeld (see Find
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to search.

Line type to search. If you
specify the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank but enter the
comma.

also Find Parameters.

Variable to capture the
number of the report where
the ﬁrst ﬁnd was made.

ﬁrst ﬁnd was made.

the ﬁrst ﬁnd was made.

Variable to capture the
number of the drawer where
the ﬁrst ﬁnd was made.

Find Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C(x)

Alters the ﬁnd process based
on the character set order.
Ordinarily the system
processes the report based on
the character set of the
drawer. The C option enables
you to choose the character
set type on which to base the
ﬁnd. Use one of the following:
C(F) for full character set (FCS)
C(L) for limited character set

(LCS)
C(S) for a strict comparison
(distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters)

Ü Ü 2200

F

Finds numeric data in report
ﬁelds regardless of justiﬁcation
or numeric format. The F
option is ignored for ﬁelds that
use a Y option parameter.

G

Searches for data backwards
through the report. If a
starting line number is not
speciﬁed, the search starts
with the last line in the report
and ends with the ﬁrst line in
the report.

N

Finds the ﬁrst data line not
meeting the search criteria.
For example, if you search a
column for the letter A, the
function ﬁnds the ﬁrst line that
does not contain the letter A in
the column. Note that when
using the N option, the target

line type still determines the
line types searched.

Rx-y

Searches a range of reports
from report number x through
report number y.

Rx,y

Searches reports x and y.

Rx-y,z

Searches reports x through y
and also z.

2200 Þ Þ

S

Starts the search at the ﬁrst
line on the screen. This option
is not available for statements.

Ü Ü 2200

Y[(x)]

Searches for dates in the ﬁelds
deﬁned by the date format
parameter line. (See the Y

Optionally speciﬁes the line

type of the parameter line.
The line type cannot be an R
and defaults to a Y. It should
be changed only if your target

Indicates that @ characters
found on the parameter line
specify a search for space
characters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Find Parameters
Parameter Description

Searches for a slant (/)
character when used with

Searches for spaces. Use

character in parentheses.

Find Outcome
Executing this command as a manual function causes the
following actions to occur:
If the data is found, the system displays the report
starting at the line where the data is located. When the
search is complete, a system message states that there
are no more ﬁnds.
Press Resume to continue the search. The system
displays the report at the next ﬁnd that occurs after the
current screen.

Note: If the string exists on the same screen as a
previous ﬁnd, the Find manual function does not display
that line as the next ﬁnd.
If the data is not found, a system message is displayed.

@FND Statement Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
If the speciﬁed data is found, the statement
Renames the report where the ﬁnd was made to -0.
Note that any previous -0 result is released.
Loads variables with information as described under
the format ﬁeld descriptions.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, the statement
Does not create a -0 result.
Loads the variables with 0.
Gives control to the speciﬁed label.

Find Guidelines
Follow these guidelines in using F and FND.
To update a single line, it is more eﬃcient to use the
FND statement followed by the Write Line (WRL)
statement than to use the Search Update (SRU)
statement with the Totalize (TOT) statement.
R line types should not be used because they conﬂict
with the Range (R) parameter.
For more examples of searching for data, see S and
@SRH (Search), SU and @SRU (Search Update). Since
the parameters available for the Search and Find
commands are very similar, you can try the examples
shown in the Search command discussion; simply
execute the Find command instead of the Search
command.

Note: Since the Find command does not create a result
containing ﬁnds, some of the Search command options are
not available with the Find command.

Find Procedures
To search for multiple strings in the same ﬁeld, enter each
character string on a separate line under the ﬁeld heading
in the mask. A ﬁnd is made when either of the requested
strings is found.
To search for multiple strings in diﬀerent ﬁelds, enter the
character strings under diﬀerent headings on the same line.
A ﬁnd is made when all speciﬁed ﬁelds contain the
requested strings.
To search for multiple strings in diﬀerent ﬁelds and include
either one string or the other or both, enter the character
strings under diﬀerent headings and on diﬀerent lines in the
mask. A ﬁnd is made when either of the speciﬁed ﬁelds
contains the requested strings.
To search for a range of character strings
1. Type the characters for the lower end of the range in the
ﬁrst line under the mask in the correct ﬁeld.
2. Type r in the ﬁrst character position in the next line.
3. On the same line as the R, type the characters for the
higher end of the range in the same ﬁeld as the lower
end.
4. Transmit. A ﬁnd is made when the ﬁrst data that ﬁts
within the range is found.
For example:
*St.Status.
*Cd.Date .

*==.======.
** ******
800101 <-- Lower end of the range. r
r 891231 <-- R parameter in column 1 designating
a
range search; higher end of the range.

Finding Data in a Range of Reports Example
This example displays the ﬁrst report where a ﬁnd was
made, at the line containing the data being searched for.
Function Mask
r2-10,14
*St.Status.By.
*Cd. Date .In.
*==.======.==.
** ****** **
ip
sh
Equivalent Statement
@fnd,'drawerb' r2-10,14 2-2 |,ip/|,sh <report>i4,
<line>i6 .
Option Used

r2-10,14

Search reports 2 through 10
and 14.

Parameters Used

ip
sh

Search for these parameters in
the St Cd ﬁeld.

Finding Spaces Example
This example uses the @ option and the @ parameter to
ﬁnd spaces in the Serial Number ﬁeld.
Function Mask

*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number.
*==.======.==.=========.======.
** ****** ** ********* ******
@@@@@@
Equivalent Statement
@fnd,'report2b' @ 25-6 |,@@@@@@ <report>i4,
<line>i6 .

This example ﬁnds the ﬁrst line of data within a range of
dates. The Y option indicates the presence of a date format
parameter line and the Y parameter identiﬁes the date
format parameter line. The YYMMDD date format in the
Status Date ﬁeld speciﬁes the alphabetic date format to use.

.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.Produc.Produc. Ship .
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
Plan .Actual. Date .

****** ****** ******
sh 970101
Rsh 101231
Y YYMMDD

FC and @FC (Format Columns)
Use @FC (Format Columns) function to perform justiﬁcation
of text within one or more columns of report. The following
are the available categories:
Column justiﬁcation
Case justiﬁcation
Column Justiﬁcation
The column justiﬁcation justiﬁes alphanumeric text.

Parameter

Description

L

Left justify

R

Right justify

C

Center

Case Justiﬁcation
The case justiﬁcation justiﬁes only alphabetic text.

Parameter

Description

U

Upper case.

O

Lower case.

F

Lower case
with ﬁrst
character of
ﬁrst word
upper case

W

Lower case
with ﬁrst
character of
each word
upper case

Note: Any column can contain a maximum of one Column
Justiﬁcation parameter and one Case Justiﬁcation
parameter.

Format Columns Options
Options

Description

A

Process all
line types.

C

Ignore case
parameters
(O F) if the
report is LCS
or FCSU.

I

Inhibit
processing
of invalid
data.

The I-option indicates to leave the column unformatted
when processing a text column and the data in that ﬁeld
begins with a plus-sign, minus-sign, period, or number.
The C-option indicates to ignore any O or F parameters if
the report is of type LCS or FCSU. If these reports contain
lower case characters, an error message is displayed.

@FCH (Relational Aggregate Fetch)
Use the Relational Aggregate Fetch (FCH) statement to
retrieve columns and rows from a relational table and place
them in a result.
Before using the FCH statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Statement Format
@FCH[,c,d,lab,o,adj,skip,q,edsp?,wrap,info,vert,s
ort,nchar,dst,sp,db, fs] 'id col tbl [Where
clause]'[,c,d,r,sel] [vstat,vcol,vqty] .

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to hold the
result. Default = 0,A.

lab

Label to go to if the status
returned by the relational
database is other than normal
completion or end of data, or if
an interface error occurs. The
error information resides in the
-0 result.

o

Options subﬁeld. See
Relational Aggregate Fetch
Options.

adj

Decimal ﬁeld width adjustment
options (ignored when you
specify D in the o subﬁeld).
See Relational Aggregate
Fetch Options for adjustment
options. Default = N. The
adjustment varies by database
manager.

skip

Number of rows in the table to
skip when retrieving data
(ignored when you specify D or
H in the o subﬁeld). If skip is
zero or less than zero, the
statement retrieves data,
beginning with the ﬁrst row in
the table.

q

Number of rows in the table to
retrieve for the result (ignored
when you specify D or H in the
o subﬁeld). If q is zero or less
than zero, or if it is greater
than the number of rows in the
table, the statement retrieves
all rows in the table.

edsp?

Create a result if an error
occurs? Y or N. The result
contains the last statement
sent to the database manager
and the text of the system
message. It also contains the
status code and column where
the error occurred, if
applicable. If you supplied a
label, the run continues at the
label and the error information
resides in the -0 result.

wrap

Column and data line
wrapping options. See
Relational Aggregate Fetch
Options for wrapping options.
Default = N.

info

Table information options.
Default = N, no additional
information required. See
Relational Aggregate Fetch
Options for information
options.

vert

Vertical display options.
Default = N, information from
columns is displayed as ﬁelds;
other options display columns
on separate lines. The data
items follow the heading

divider line and are also
displayed as one line per
column, or as multiple lines if
you specify a wrap option. A
period line precedes the data
records in the following
format, where xxx is the
record number (RECORD.xxx).
See Relational Aggregate
Fetch Options for vertical
display options.

sort

Speciﬁes the position of the
columns in the result. The
statement sorts columns by
primary key. Default = N.
Enter one of the following
options:
Y or F (positions the primary
key or unique index columns
ﬁrst)
N (positions columns in the
sequence in which they were
selected)

nchar

Any nonalphanumeric
character to ﬁll the null
character ﬁelds received from
the database manager (ﬁlls
null decimal ﬁelds with
blanks). Default = ﬁlls null
character ﬁelds with blanks

and null decimal ﬁelds with +0
characters.

dst

Enter d to eliminate duplicate
data rows in the result (SQL
DISTINCT). Default = result
can contain duplicate rows. If
the syntax is in a report,
specify DISTINCT in the SQL
statement instead of using this
subﬁeld.

sp

Alternate character to
designate a space. Use this
ﬁeld only when using the
syntax portion. Use spaces to
separate subﬁelds in the
syntax portion of the FCH
statement.
Some commands require a
space as part of a table name
or column name, such as a
table alias or built-in function.
Use an alternate character to
represent a space when
assigning such names. The
system translates alternate
characters into spaces when
the SQL syntax is created. If
the syntax is in a report, you
do not need to specify an

alternate space character.
Following are valid characters:
!"#${|?@}`

db

Database name.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

db

Default = default database
name on your system.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

db

Default = database running
with MRIMID set to 1. See your
administrator for more
information.

Ü Ü 2200

fs

Controls the number of spaces
between columns in the result.
(This is ignored if truncation or
wrapping would result from

adding the speciﬁed number of
spaces.) Enter a value
between 0 and 18. Default =
0.
The id, col, tbl, and Where
clause subﬁelds contain
information required by the
FCH statement in order to
create an SQL statement. You
must separate these subﬁelds
with spaces.

id

Unique identiﬁer for the cursor
name. This ﬁeld is required for
all relational databases;
however, you can leave it
blank if you specify a report in
the c,d,r subﬁelds.

col

Column names from which to
retrieve data. If you specify
more than one column name,
separate them with commas.
Use an asterisk ( * ) to select
all columns in the table or
tables.

tbl

Table names from which to
retrieve data. If you specify
more than one table, separate
them with commas.

Where
clause

Clause specifying one or more
conditions for retrieving rows.
The result contains only the
rows for which the condition is
true. Use the Where clause to
compare the values in a table
to one or more values stated
in the Where clause, or to join
the data in multiple tables.
If you need to use apostrophes
in the Where clause, assign a
variable to TIC$, as in the
following example:
@ldv,w <tic>h1=tic$ .
@fch... 'c * houses
location ='\
<tic>'Edina'<tic> .

2200 Þ Þ

c,d,r

Report containing a Select
statement. If you enter a
report location in these
subﬁelds, the statement
executes the Select statement
contained in the report and
ignores the previous four
subﬁelds. The maximum
number of syntax characters
for the basic mode MRIM is

7948 when the syntax is
supplied in a report. The
maximum number of syntax
characters for the extended
mode MRIM is 7500 when the
syntax is supplied in a report.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

c,d,r

Report containing a Select
statement. If you enter a
report location in these
subﬁelds, the statement
executes the Select statement
contained in the report and
ignores the previous four
subﬁelds. The maximum
number of syntax characters is
7500 when the syntax is
supplied in a report.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

sel

Select statement number. If a
report contains more than one
Select statement, this subﬁeld
deﬁnes which Select
statement to execute. Each

Select statement must end
with a semicolon ( ; ).

vstat

Variable to capture the status
of the action performed.
Deﬁne this variable as type A,
seven characters in length (for
example, v1a7).
Normal completion status =
0000 or 6001, depending on
the database.
Use the "GETERROR..." syntax
to obtain information relating
to other status values.

vcol

Variable to capture the
number of the character
(within the syntax generated
by the FCH statement) where
an error was detected. Deﬁne
this variable as a ﬁve-digit
type I variable (for example,
v2i5).

vqty

Variable to capture the actual
number of output columns.
This can be equal to the
number of columns selected if
you are using a wrap option to
truncate report lines.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Options
The following tables describe the relational aggregate fetch
options.
Relational Aggregate Fetch Options¾o Subﬁeld

Option

Description

D

Retrieves a description of the
columns and tables speciﬁed
in the col and tbl subﬁelds.
This option produces a result
identical to the result of the
DDI statement.

E

Retrieves an explanation of
the syntax generated by the
FCH statement and the access
path used to retrieve the data.
The information contained in
the explanation may vary
between relational databases
and is not available on all
relational databases.

H

Produces a result containing
headings corresponding to the
columns in the tables you
speciﬁed, the heading divider

line, and ﬁve blank lines with
tab characters. The vert
subﬁeld determines the
format of the heading display.
The value of the info subﬁeld
(T, I, or B) determines
additional information in the
report.

L

Left-justiﬁes any decimal
ﬁelds and suppresses trailing
zeros in any fractions.

R

Right-justiﬁes any decimal
ﬁelds and suppresses trailing
zeros in any fractions.

Windows Þ Þ

S

To access schema metadata
(OLE DB only), this option
causes the tbl ﬁeld to become
the input for a DBSchema name
and any restrictions. The
DBSchema name is the ﬁrst
table entry and the subsequent
comma-separated values are
the schema restrictions. In
addition, the col ﬁeld limits the
columns from which to retrieve

data. Otherwise, an asterisk (*)
retrieves all the columns.

Note: The size of DBSchema
metadata columns can be large.
These large column sizes can
be limited by setting the wrap
option to T. If necessary, the
data is truncated.
This example uses the FCH,S
option (OLE DB only) to retrieve
schema metadata.
Retrieves a list of every type of
table that exists in the
database:
@fch,0,i,099,s& 'c *
tables'
where tables is the schema.
Retrieves a list of user tables
that exist in the database:
@fch,0,i,099,s& 'c *
tables,,,,table'
where tables is the schema and
table is the table_type
restriction.
Limits the column returned as
follows:

@fch,0,i,099,s& 'c
table_type,table_name
tables,,,,table'
where table_type and
table_name are the columns in
the tables schema and table is
the table_type restriction.
Retrieves a list of views that
exist in the database:
@fch,0,i,099,s& 'c
table_type,table_name
tables,,,,view'
where table_type and
table_name are the columns in
the tables schema and view is
the table_type restriction.
Retrieves a list of stored
procedures that exist in the
database:
@fch,0,i,099,s& 'c
procedure_name procedures'
where procedure_name is the
column in the procedures
schema.

Ü Ü Windows

Z

Right-justiﬁes any decimal
ﬁelds and does not suppress
trailing zeros in any fractions.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Options¾adj Subﬁeld

Option

Description

B

Increases the width of any
decimal ﬁeld.

I

Increases the width of decimal
ﬁelds that contain integer
values (no fractional portion).

N

Makes no adjustment to
decimal ﬁeld width.

Linux/UNIX OS 2200 Þ Þ

F

Increases the width of decimal
ﬁelds that contain a fractional
part.

Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS 2200

Windows Þ Þ

F

Increases the width of decimal
ﬁelds that contain a fractional
part. For ODBC connections,
the ﬁeld is increased by two
columns, one for the sign (+ or
-) and one for the decimal
point.

Ü Ü Windows
Relational Aggregate Fetch Options¾wrap Subﬁeld

Option

Description

A

If the record length is greater
than the line, truncates the
report heading and places the
remainder of the record on
trailer (asterisk) lines.

B

If the column does not ﬁt on
the report line, places column
and remainder of record on
trailer (asterisk) lines.

N

Displays a system message if

the data does not ﬁt within
the line width of the selected
drawer or if a decimal value
does not ﬁt within its column
width.

T

Truncates the data to ﬁt within
the line width for a selected
drawer. Also truncates
decimal values that do not ﬁt
within their ﬁeld widths.

Y or W

Displays the data on multiple
lines if it is greater than the
line width for the selected
type. A blank line is added to
each header line that is
wrapped.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Options¾info Subﬁeld

Option

Description

B

Produces the information
speciﬁed by table information
options T and I.

D

Deletes period lines from the

result if o = D.

I

Produces a result that
identiﬁes the data type for
each column, the primary key
column or columns, and result
columns. Data types are
identiﬁed by the ﬁrst letter of
the data type, followed by the
column width. The formats for
the primary keys or result
columns are as follows:
Primary Key
PRIMARY KEY
PRIMARY
P
Result Columns
RESULT
R

N

No additional information is
produced.

P

Includes period lines in the
result if o = D.

T

Produces a list of tables, and
cross-references each column
with the table name (if o does
not = D). Each table is
identiﬁed by a unique
sequence number. Columns in
the table are identiﬁed by the
table sequence number in the
result.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Options¾vert Subﬁeld

Option

Description

C

Left-justiﬁes each column
name before the data item.
Column names are aligned by
the longest column name.

N

Produces a horizontal result
with all columns displayed
side by side.

R

Right-justiﬁes each column
name before the data item.
Column names are aligned by
the longest column name.

Y

Displays only the data record.
Column names are omitted.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Outcome
The FCH statement creates a result consisting of headings
corresponding to columns in the relational table and data
lines corresponding to rows in the relational table.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the FCH statement selects only one column and that
column is classiﬁed as a large object (normally a picture or
image), the statement places the data in a result and wraps
the data from line to line until all the data is returned.
If the statement selects multiple columns and one of the
columns is classiﬁed as a large object, the statement places
a message in that column indicating that a large object was
returned. Use the Display Picture (PIC) statement to display
the object, provided it is in a format supported by the PIC
statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Relational Aggregate Fetch Reserved Words
STAT1$ varies depending on the option used. The following
values are valid even if an error occurred:

Option
Used

Contents of STAT1$

L, R, or Z

Number of records retrieved
from the database.

D

Number of columns entered.

H

O.

STAT2$ contains the total number of lines of data written to
the result following the headings, or zero if the statement
detected an error.
STAT3$ contains one of the following values:
Line number of the ﬁrst line following the heading
divider line in the result.
Zero if the relational database returns an error (for
example, an invalid column or table name) and you
supplied a label.

A system message number if an interface error occurred
(for example, the database is inoperative) and you
supplied a label. Use the Load System Message (LSM)
statement to obtain the text of the message.
For ODBC databases, to view a list of user tables, system
tables, or views, execute one of the following SELECT
statements:

Statement

Description

SELECT * FROM
ODBC$API$SQLTABLES

Retrieves a
list of every
type of table
that exists in
the
database.

ODBC$API$SQLTABLES

Retrieves a
list of views
that exist in
the
database.

ODBC$API$SQLTABLES

Retrieves a
list of user
tables that
exist in the

SELECT SYSTEM TABLE
FROM
ODBC$API$SQLTABLES

Retrieves a

tables that
exist in the
database.

Relational Aggregate Fetch Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Normally, the FCH statement is the most eﬃcient way to
retrieve columns and rows from a table. In a few
situations, the Submit SQL (SQL) statement is more
eﬃcient. For example, when you are
Able to specify the exact format of the table headers
Retrieving fewer than ﬁve rows
Using a routine that retrieves one row at a time
If you need to use apostrophes in the Where clause,
assign a variable to TIC$, as in the following example:
@ldv,w <tic>h1 = tic$ .
@fch... 'c * houses location =
'<tic>'Edina'<tic> .
2200 Þ Þ
When using an FCH statement, enter the expected
number of lines in the q subﬁeld. This allocates
suﬃcient storage space for the data and improves
performance by reducing internal copying, even if you
overestimate the quantity.
Ü Ü 2200

Retrieving All Columns and Rows Example
This example retrieves all columns and rows from the
houses table:
@fch,0,a,099,l,,,,y,a,,,,,,,agency 'c * houses' .

0,a

Holds the result in cabinet 0,
drawer A.

099

Goes to label 99 in case of an
error.

l

Retrieves data and leftjustiﬁes decimal ﬁelds.

y

Creates an error report if
necessary.

a

Truncates the record and
places the remainder on
trailer lines if the record is
longer than the report width
(wrap option A).

agency

Speciﬁes the database name.

'c *
houses'

Speciﬁes the cursor, column,
and table name for the
retrieval operation. The
asterisk (*) indicates that all
columns are to be included in
the result.

Joining Two Tables Example
Use the FCH statement to perform a JOIN operation and
retrieve data into a result. You can include the syntax in the
statement or store it in a report.
The following FCH statement accesses report 0,a,262
(shown next), which contains the SQL statement for joining
the houses and customers tables. The syntax portion of the
statement contains only a cursor name (C). The remaining
syntax resides in the report.
@fch,0,a,099,,,,,y,a,,,,,,,rdms 'c',0,a,262 .
Report
Line> 1 Roll> - 262A
.DATE 29 JUN 91 13:01:10 RID 262A 20 JUN 91
MAPPER
.Join houses and customers
*================================================
====
SELECT cname,house.no,houses.price
FROM houses, customers
WHERE location=location_desired;
..... END REPORT .....

Retrieving a Binary Object Example
This example retrieves and displays a binary object that is
stored in a relational database. The FCH statement creates
an undisplayable result that the PIC statement can display,
provided the following conditions are met:
Only one row of data is returned from the relational
database.
Only one column is returned from the relational
database and must be either an INFORMIX data type of
byte or an ORACLE data type of long raw.
In this example, the FCH statement returns the row that has
a catalog_num of 10001 and the column cat_picture.
@ldv,p <db>h12=database,<id>h12=newuser,
<psw>h12=new* .
@lgn,1,y,2,i,<db> '',,<id>,<psw> .
@006:fch,02,a,,z,b,,,y,w,,,n,*,,,<db> 'q
cat_picture catalog WHERE \
catalog_num = 10001' .
@001:dsp,-0 .
@pic,-0,y,'dbblob.bmp()' 'INFX'.<infx>i6 .
@dsp,-0 .
@rel .

Find and Read Line Outcome
If the speciﬁed data is found, the statement
Renames the report where the ﬁnd was made to -0. Any
previous -0 result is released.
Loads variables with information as described under
statement ﬁeld descriptions.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, the statement
Does not create a -0 result.
Loads the variables with 0.
Gives control to the speciﬁed label.

Find and Read Line Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the FDR statement:
The FDR statement is diﬀerent from a Find (FND)
statement in that the FDR statement can be followed by
a Read Line Next (RLN) statement instead of an RDL
statement. It is generally more eﬃcient to use an
FDR/RLN combination instead of an FND/RLN
combination.
For more information, see @RDC (Read Continuous),
@RDL (Read Line), and @RLN (Read Line Next).

Find and Read Line Example
These statements search for the character string ip in the St
Cd ﬁeld and reads the number in the Order Number ﬁeld on
the found line:
@fdr,0,b '' 'stcd' |,ip <report>i6,<line>i6 .
@rln,<line>,099 'order' <ord>i .

Option

Description

0,b

Search drawer B in cabinet
0.

''

Use no options.

'stcd'

Search the St Cd ﬁeld.

|

Process tab lines only.

ip

Search for the character
string ip.

<report>i6 Load <report> with the
number of the report in
which the ﬁnd is made.

<line>i6

Load <line> with the line
number where the ﬁnd is
made.

rln,
Read the number from the
<line>,099 Order Number ﬁeld on the
'order'
found line (in the <line>
<ord>i
variable) and load <ord>
with the order number.

This example ﬁnds data within a range of dates. The Y

line and the Y parameter identiﬁes the date format

|,sh,970101/r,sh,101231/y,,YYMMDD .
