Create File Outcome
Executing the Create File command causes the following
actions to occur:
If the ﬁle does not exist, the command creates the ﬁle; if
the ﬁle already exists, the command overwrites it.
If you create a ﬁle in a format other than MAPPER
format, and the original report contains tab characters,
the command translates tab characters to spaces. For
information on retaining tabs or translating them to
other characters, use the $TABA$ data control
command.
If you create a ﬁle with headings, the ﬁle keeps
information such as the report identiﬁer and the date
the report was last updated. The command writes the
date line into the ﬁle so when you retrieve the ﬁle, the
date line of the original report is part of the result.
With the Create File manual function, the report you had
on display when you requested the function is
redisplayed.
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If your Linux or UNIX log-on does not have write
permission for the speciﬁed ﬁle, a prompt is displayed
that solicits a diﬀerent log-on.
The command creates the ﬁle with the following
permissions: read and write by the owner (the Linux or
UNIX log-on of the station), read by group, and read by
other.

Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If the B option is used, the input report must be full
character set and is written as an omnibus element (no
standard data ﬁle control words) to the speciﬁed
program ﬁle. The B option, in the MAPPER format ﬁeld,
indicates there are no heading lines. Date (line 1), title
(line 2), and End Report lines are not written to the ﬁle.
Ü Ü 2200

Create File Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Create File
command:
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You can create the ﬁle in MAPPER format or in standard
data ﬁle format. Avoid using Linux or UNIX edit utilities
on ﬁles in MAPPER format because the results may be
unpredictable; however, you can use Linux or UNIX text
editors on standard data ﬁles.
Do not use any Linux or UNIX metacharacters when
specifying the ﬁle name (for example, *, ^, [, ], and so
on). These characters are interpreted in a special way
by the Linux or UNIX shell, so you may not get the
results you are expecting.
For trusted mode installation:
In foreground sessions, the system prompts you for the
login and password if you attempt to create or copy an
existing ﬁle whose permissions do not allow access by
your current login.

Note: Background runs are executed using the mapper
login. If your current login does not allow access to an
existing ﬁle, the operation will terminate with an error
status.
For secure mode installation:
If you attempt to create or copy an existing ﬁle whose
permissions do not allow access by your current login,

the operation will terminate with an error status of This
ﬁle cannot be accessed
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
You can create the ﬁle in MAPPER format (which
contains control characters and may be unreadable by
text editors) or in standard data ﬁle format (which text
editors can process). Files in MAPPER format are useful
when transferring reports between systems. See your
system administration documentation for more
information.
Windows Þ Þ
Business Information Server does not allow the use of
the following characters in ﬁle names:
&[]*?,\^;
Ü Ü Windows
2200 Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Business Information Server does not allow the use of
the following characters in ﬁle names:
&[]()*?,\^;
Ü Ü 2200 Linux/UNIX OS
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If you used the Cabinet Switch (CS) run to switch to a
remote cabinet, you must specify the full path name.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

2200 Þ Þ
Use the Element (ELT) function to create data ﬁles or
symbolic elements of a program ﬁle.
Ü Ü 2200
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Sharing Native Files
You might be unable to read, overwrite, or remove a native
(Linux or UNIX) ﬁle that was created by another session
user. The ﬁle might have been created at the system level
or within the Business Information Server context using the
FILE or UNIX command or with the FIL or UNX statement. An
attempt to access the ﬁle for reading or writing can be done
at the system level or within the Business Information
Server context with the RET or UNIX command or with the
RET or UNX statement. Whether another user can access
the ﬁle in diﬀerent modes depends upon the active or
default permissions attached to the ﬁle.
All ﬁles created either at the system level or within the
Business Information Server context receive "rw-r--r"
permissions by default. These permissions allow the owner
to read and write to the ﬁle. Group members and others can
only read the ﬁle. This is because the default umask
provided by the operating system is 022, which removes
"group" and "other" write permissions. The ﬁle owner can
actively change permissions by doing one of the following:
Issuing the system "chmod" command which sets the
access permissions explicitly. This can be done either at
the system level or within the Business Information
Server context using the UNIX command or the UNX
statement. It can be done by all workstation and
terminal emulator users.
Modifying the umask for the system session. This can be
done only by terminal emulator users, including the
"mapper" user in Trusted sites.

Using a mapper login: add a umask command to the
mlogin script. This script resides in the M_ROOT/lbin
directory.
Using other logins: add a umask command to the
.proﬁle ﬁle, found in the user's home directory.
The argument to the umask is a three-digit octal number
that is bitwise subtracted from octal 666. For example:
umask 002
changes ﬁle creation mode to 664. Thereafter, all ﬁles are
created with "rw-rw-r--" permissions.
Files created by runs initiated by MAPQUE are owned by the
default user/group ("mapper"/"mapper" in Trusted
instances) and receive permissions "rw-rw-r--". (The default
user and group have read and write permissions, but others
have only read permission.) This also applies to scheduled
runs initiated by DISPATCHER. The software uses umask to
actively change the ﬁle creation mode when it starts
MAPQUE and DISPATCHER.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

Create File Examples
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
This example creates a standard Linux or UNIX ﬁle called
production in the test directory. It uses the data in report
2B0, including the report headings.
@fil,0,b,2,n,y /test/production .
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

MYQUAL*MYFILE. It uses the data in report 2B0, including
the report headings.
@fil,0,b,2,n,y myqual*myfile .
Ü Ü 2200

\TEST directory on drive C. It uses the data in report 2B0,
including the report headings.
@fil,0,b,2,n,y 'c:\test\prodstat.new' .
Ü Ü Windows

@FKY (Function Key)
Use the Function Key (FKY) statement to customize the
function key bar, which is displayed at the bottom of the
screen during a display by the Display (DSP), Display
Message (DSM), or Output Mask (OUM) statements.
Deﬁne the function keys in a separate report or in the
output area of your run, using Screen Control commands
such as FKEY. For more information, see @SC (Screen
Control).

Function Key Statement Format
@FKY[,c,d,r] .
where c,d,r is the report containing the Screen Control
commands that create a custom function key bar for the
following DSP, DSM, or OUM statement to display.

Function Key Outcome
The statement designates a Screen Control form that
subsequent DSP, DSM, or OUM statements use to display a
custom function key bar.
The DSP, DSM, and OUM statements continue to use the
report you speciﬁed until one of the following conditions
occur:
The run terminates.
The run links to another run via the Link to Another
Script (LNK) statement.
You designate another Screen Control report with the
FKY statement.
You use another FKY statement with no report speciﬁed.
To return control to your run, use the KEY command.

Function Key Example
This example sets up Screen Control commands in the
output area, performs Break (BRK) and Rename (RNM)
statements to make them accessible as -1, then uses FKY
and DSP statements to display reports with and without a
customized function key bar:
@brk,fftype$ .
fkey,1,'Resume',rsm
fkey,2,'Paint',pnt
fkey,4,'Return',KEY
fkey,10,'Quit',^
@brk rnm -1 . Save FKEY result
@srh,0,b,2 d 2-2 ,ip rnm -2 . Save search result
@fky,-1 . Customize func. key bar
@dsp,-2 . Display search result
@if fkey$ eq 4,(0010) ; . . (other processing)
@fky . Restore default func. keys
@dsp,0,b,2 . Display report

@FMT (Format)
Use the Format (FMT) statement to create a display format
for a following output display, such as that created by a
Display Report (DSP) or Output Mask (OUM) statement. You
can also select columns to print the next time you call the
Auxiliary (AUX) or Print (PRT) statements.
You can select which ﬁelds to display by specifying either
ﬁeld names or column characters.

Note: The FMT statement sets up the format for your
current -0 result or the report you specify in the c,d,r ﬁeld
(which then becomes the current result).

Format Statement Format
@FMT[,c,d,r] field[,field,....,field] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which to display
ﬁelds. Default = -0.

ﬁeld

Fields to display, either ﬁeld
names or column-character
positions.

Format Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The system displays the subsequent DSP, DSM, OUM, or
OUT screen with the speciﬁed ﬁelds.
If you name the ﬁelds, the display includes the columns
of each ﬁeld as well as the character immediately
following the ﬁeld.
If you use the column-character positions, the run
displays only the columns speciﬁed.
The system always includes column 1, the line type
designator, in the format.
Although you can list the ﬁelds in any order on the FMT
statement, the ﬁelds are always displayed in the same
order as they exist in the original report.

Format Examples
This example displays the St Cd, Ship Date, and Cust Code
ﬁelds of the current -0: @fmt 'stcd','shipdate','custcode' .
@dsp,-0 .
This example displays three characters beginning in column
2, ﬁve characters beginning in column 45, and seven
characters beginning in column 64: @fmt,0,b,2 2-3,45-5,647.
@dsp,0,b,2 .

Fn (Display Alternate Format)
Use the Display Alternate Format (Fn) command to display a
report in a diﬀerent format.
Report formats are deﬁned in report 0 of the drawer. Each
format is a variation on the way the report is displayed;
each includes a diﬀerent selection of data columns.
The report in which to display a diﬀerent format must be on
display.

Display Alternate Format Control Line Format
Fn

Field

Description

n

Format number in which to
display the report. (Enter 0 to
display the report in basic
format.) Note: Do not leave a
space between the F and the
format number. (For example,
enter f2, not f 2.)

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

n

Formats available are 0
through 25.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

n

Formats available are 0
through 6.

Ü Ü 2200

@FND (Find)
See F and @FND (Find) for more information.

@FON (Font)
Use the Font (FON) statement to change the character font
used to display objects on a screen.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
At any time, you can have three diﬀerent fonts deﬁned for
each Windows workstation session. The three fonts are the
Main Window font, the Run font, and the Text font.

Main Window Font
Here are the highlights of the Main Window font:
The Main Window font requires a nonproportional (ﬁxedpitch) font. TrueType fonts are required for portability of
applications.
The Main Window font is selected by one of the
following:
At startup time, a MAPPER Setup File (MSF) selects
the font that was saved when the Setup ﬁle was
created.
Anytime from a workstation by selecting File,
Display, Main Window Fonts, and then choosing a
font.
Anytime from a run by specifying a font statement
without any types (for example,
@FON,,name,size,opts).
The Main Window font remains active until another Main
Window font is selected.
If there are no active runs, the Main Window font is used
for:
Displaying text on function keys
Displaying text in reports and screens

Run Font (R type)
Here are the highlights of the Run font:
The Run font requires a nonproportional (ﬁxed-pitch)
font. TrueType fonts are required for portability of
applications.
The Run font is selected by one of the following:
At the start of a run, it is automatically set the same
as the Main Window font.
Anytime from a run by specifying a font statement
with the R type (for example,
@FON,R,name,size,opts).
The selected Run font remains active until either:
The run encounters a FON,R statement (with no
subﬁelds).
The run terminates with a DSX, REL, or GTO END
statement.
The Run font is used when a run is active for:
Displaying text on function keys
Displaying text in reports and screens (see the
following table)
If the W option is not set on the WIN statement, then the
size of the Run font is used for both:

Positioning objects on the screen (see the following
table)
Sizing objects on the screen (see the following
table)
If you deﬁne the window to use rows and columns (the W
option is not set on the WIN statement), then the
characteristics of the Run font determine the number and
size of those rows and columns. When you select the Run
font, the position and size of the BTN, PIC, and WIN objects
are aﬀected.
The following table shows the relationship of the Run font to
objects.

Object Position of
Object
Aﬀected by

Text Font (T type)
Here are the highlights of the Text font:
The Text font can be either a nonproportional (ﬁxedpitch) or a proportional font. TrueType fonts are required
for portability of applications.
The Text font is selected by one of the following:
At the start of a run, it is automatically set the same
as the Run font.
Anytime from a run by specifying a font statement
with the T type (for example,
@FON,T,name,size,opts).
The selected Text font remains active until either:
The run encounters another FON,T statement (with
or without subﬁelds).
The run terminates with a DSX, REL, or GTO END
statement.
The Text font is used when a run is active for displaying
text on objects (see the following table).
If the W option is not set on the WIN statement, then the
size of the Text font is used for sizing objects on the
screen (see the following table).
If you deﬁne the window to use rows and columns (the W
option is not set on the WIN statement), then the
characteristics of the Text font determine the number and

size of those rows and columns. When you select the Text
font, the size of the CBX, EDT, LST, and TXT objects are
aﬀected.
The following table shows the relationship of the Text font to
objects.

Generic Format
@FON[,type,name,size,o,lab]

Font Statement Formats
Sets the Main Window font to the name and size selected.
@FON,,name,size[,o,lab]
Sets the Run font to the name and size selected.
@FON,R,name,size[,o,lab]
Sets the Run font the same as the Main Window font.
@FON,R
Sets the Text font to the name and size selected.
@FON,T,name,size[,o,lab]
Sets the Text font the same as the Run font.
@FON
@FON,T

Field

Description

type

Identiﬁes the type of font.
R - set the Run font.
T - set the Text font.

Note: If you do not specify
any type, which is the default,
it sets the text of the main

window font without making
any changes in the function
button text.
To change the main window
and function keys text, use File
-> Display -> Main Window
Fonts.

name

Name of the font (for example,
Arial). Enclose font names
containing embedded spaces
in apostrophes ('). Available
fonts are listed under
File -> Display items (Fonts
selection) on the main menu
bar.

Note: if you do not specify the
font name, then the values in
size (font size) and o (options)
are ignored.

size

Font size. Use one of the
following values:
L - large version of the
speciﬁed font.
M - medium version of the
speciﬁed font.
N - size of characters in the
speciﬁed font.

S - small version of the
speciﬁed font.
When using the S, M, or L
sizes, a corresponding size is
selected by the software.
These sizes are portable
between PCs; however, be
aware that they are not
necessarily displayed
identically on all PCs, and the
small version (S) may be
unreadable on some PCs.
If you use the P option, this
subﬁeld is a point size;
otherwise, this subﬁeld
speciﬁes the height of the
character cell in pixels. For
portability, use both the P and
D options.

o

Options ﬁeld. See Font
Options.

lab

The run continues processing
at the label speciﬁed if the
Font statement fails; for
example, the designated font
cannot be located on the PC.
STAT1$ contains zero if
successful. If the command is
unsuccessful, STAT1$ contains
the status of the error. See

Graphical Interface Returned
Status Codes (STAT1$ Values)
for possible values returned in
the STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: For the FON statement
to return status codes, check
DWCAP$ for availability:
DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.

Font Options
Option

Description

B

Displays characters in bold
type.

D

If a font name or size,
speciﬁed in the name or size
subﬁeld, does not exist,
Windows font mapping
process creates an
appropriate font from among
those installed on the run
user's PC, which can vary from
user to user. See your
Windows documentation for
more details on font mapping.
If you are changing the main
window font, the Windows font
mapping process makes the
selection from nonproportional
(ﬁxed-pitch) fonts.
Check DWCAP$ for availability:
DWCAP$(9-1) = 1 if available.

I

Displays characters in italic
type.

P

Indicates that the size subﬁeld
contains a point size.
Check DWCAP$ for availability:
DWCAP$(9-1) = 1 if available.

S

Strikes through characters.

T

Changes the font immediately.
Without this option, the
system waits until the current
block is transferred.

U

Underlines characters.

Font Reserved Word
If the FON statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement does not complete, the run continues at the
label. The STAT1$ reserved word contains an error status.
See Graphical Interface Returned Status Codes (STAT1$
Values) for possible values returned in the STAT1$ reserved
word.

Font Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the FON statement:
Always use TrueType fonts for all of the font types in
your runs. TrueType fonts are scaleable. When GUI
objects are deﬁned in rows and columns, the size and
positioning of objects in GUI windows are determined by
the attributes of the fonts you select. The most reliable
presentation on diﬀerent workstations (which may have
diﬀerent local display settings) is attained by using
TrueType fonts.
You should have one Run font (@FON,R) per run, and it
must be a ﬁxed-pitch font.
To display a list of available TrueType ﬁxed-pitch fonts,
choose the Display submenu from the File Menu of your
workstation client. Then choose the Main Window Fonts
selection.
When deﬁning the position or size of objects using row
and column units, it is recommended that you use
MAPPER Standard for the Run font. The MAPPER
Standard font is:
An exact replacement for Terminal Font size 9
Installed with Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server and Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client.
You may have multiple text fonts (@FON,T) within a run.
Text fonts are usually deﬁned as a proportional font.
Arial is a good choice for a text font.

A proportional font uses the system average character
width, which is provided by the designer of the font.
There is a limit of 100 fonts per run. Any combination of
font face, font size, and attribute option each counts as
a diﬀerent font.
Load font names in variables for easy maintainability of
runs.
Use the P and D options for increased portability among
workstations.

Note: If these options are available, DWCAP$ (9-1) = 1.
However, all supported levels of the workstation client
maintain this capability.

Font Example
This example sets the Run font to MAPPER Standard, which
is a nonproportional (ﬁxed-pitch) TrueType font. Set the Run
font once at the beginning of the run and do not change it
for the duration of the run. Next, the Text font is set to Arial,
which is a proportional TrueType font. Later on, the Text font
is changed to Comic Sans MS, which is also a proportional
TrueType font.
@0001 FON,R,'MAPPER Standard',10,dp . First,
define a Run Font
@0002 FON,T,'Arial',10,dp . . Next, define a Text
Font
@. . Create your objects
@ WIN
@ BTN
@0003 FON,T,'Comic Sans MS',10,dp . Redefine a
Text Font
@. . Create more objects
@ CBX
@ EDT

Note: @FON does not change all the font related properties
of the main application font. For example if you use @FON
with default type, then it changes only the main window
font text without changing the function key font details. If
you like to change the main application font, change it
through the following GI-BIS menu option.
File -> Display -> Main Window fonts

FORM
Use the FORM script to display the format of statements
(ﬁelds and subﬁelds).

FORM Control Line Format
FORM
Enter the @ sign and run function call (for example, @srh) in
your run control report and enter form.

FORM Outcome
The script ﬁlls in all open function calls in the report with
abbreviations for the ﬁelds and subﬁelds.

FORM Example
Following is an example of starting the Form script with
some function calls entered: Line> 1 Roll> form 59E0
.DATE 01 JUN 90 05:02:00 RID 59E 01 JUN 90 MAPPER
.Run Function Data: Example of use of the FORM command
*===================================
=============================
@SRH
@SOR
@MCH
@DSP
..... END REPORT .....
The Form command inserts the statement formats directly
into the run control report, as shown: Line> 1 Roll> - 59E0
.DATE 01 JUN 90 05:03:00 RID 59E 01 JUN 90 MAPPER
*Run Function Data: Example of use of the FORM command
*===================================
=============================
@srh,c,d[,r,l,q,lab] o cc ltyp,p [vlines,vls,vrpt] .
@sor,c,d,r o cc ltyp,p .
@mch,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab] o icc iltyp,ip rcc rltyp,rp .
@dsp,c,d,r[,l,tab,f,interim,hold,msg] .

FORMG
Use the FORMG script to generate experimental reports.
Once an experimental report is completed, see the
administrator to generate a drawer for the report.

FORMG Control Line Format
FORMG
The script displays an input screen (FORMG worksheet)
where you deﬁne ﬁeld order, sizes, appearance in speciﬁc
formats, acceptable character types for the ﬁeld, and ﬁeld
headings. You can also select ﬁelds from speciﬁc
applications. (Enter formg and use the F8 help or enter
formg help.)

FORMG Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this script:
The FORMG worksheet accepts a variety of entries
before it requires processing. For example, you could
rearrange the FN numbers, deﬁne some ﬁelds, accept
ﬁelds from one or more applications, and accept
information from some speciﬁc ﬁelds within one or more
applications before entering the worksheet.
Press Show to see how the experimental report will be
displayed in ﬁnal form. The number 1 ﬁeld (determined
by its Field Number) is at the left. Headings are
displayed as they will exist in the report. The edit code
for the ﬁeld is displayed in the line below the heading
divider line.
The ﬁnal six lines of the experimental report show which
ﬁelds will be included in the various formats. If a series
of Xs is displayed beneath a ﬁeld, the ﬁeld will be
included in the format. The ﬁrst line of Xs represents the
ﬁrst format, the second line the second format, and so
on through the sixth line and sixth format.
You can deﬁne up to 99 ﬁelds.

FORMGEN
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the FORMGEN command to create experimental reports.

FORMGEN Control Line Format
FORMGEN

FORMGEN Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command displays an input screen where you
deﬁne or redeﬁne the placement of each ﬁeld, size,
format inclusion, edit codes, ﬁeld heading names, and
the total line length for the experimental report.
When the result is displayed, you can continue to
update it. The command either saves this result as a
report in cabinet 0, drawer I, or uses it to generate a
modiﬁed input screen.
The ﬁelds in the result are sorted by ﬁeld number.

FORMGEN Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the FORMGEN
command:
To divide the title of a ﬁeld into two parts, displayed one
above the other on two lines, mark the division with an
underscore character ( _ ).
To rearrange the order of the ﬁelds, renumber them in
the FN column of the input screen.
Use the Roll function key to toggle between the two
input screens. Each screen allows you to deﬁne up to 15
ﬁelds.
At any time during the design phase of your report
generation, you can look at the prospective report you
are building by pressing the DspRpt function key. From
that point, you can return to FORMGEN for more
processing or save the report permanently.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@DSG Statement Outcome
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Executing this statement displays the graphic and clears the

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The command displays the graphics, text, or both,
depending on your entry in the display subﬁeld, and
clears the output area.
If you specify interim display (Y in the interim? subﬁeld),
the run continues automatically; otherwise, the run user
must press Resume to continue the run.
Ü Ü 2200

@DSG Reserved Word
2200 Þ Þ
STAT1$ contains the following status codes if the system is
unable to display graphics at another station:
1

Station does not exist or is a batch
port, remote run, or background
station.

2

Station is not available because it
is not currently connected to the
system.

3

Either no one is signed on at the
speciﬁed station and interim
display was not speciﬁed, or the
terminal is not a graphics terminal.

4

There is no answer. The user at the
speciﬁed station did not respond to
the message wait signal within one
minute.

5

There has been a communications
timeout when plotting.

Ü Ü 2200

Display Graphics Guidelines
2200 Þ Þ
Follow these guidelines when using the DSG statement to
send graphics to another station:
If you do not specify a label and the system cannot
successfully display graphics at another station, the run
terminates with an error. See @DSG Reserved Word.
Use the sn and lab subﬁelds to display graphics on
stations other than the one executing the run. The
system activates the message wait signal on the station
receiving the graphics, and the run stalls. If the user
presses Message, the graphic is displayed.
If you specify interim display (Y in the interim? subﬁeld),
the run continues automatically; otherwise, it stalls until
the user at the receiving station presses any key.
If the user does not respond within one minute, the run
either continues at the speciﬁed label or terminates with
an error (STAT1$ = 4). If the run sends subsequent
graphics displays to the station, the user must respond
to each message wait signal.
If you know that no user is signed on at the speciﬁed
station, specify a Y in the interim? subﬁeld. In this case,
the system does not activate the message wait signal
but displays the graphic and continues the run. If you do
not specify an interim display, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label or terminates with an error (STAT1$ = 3).

Note: You cannot obtain exclusive use of any station.
When more than one run is sending graphics to the
same station, the displays may be intermixed and not
displayed in the same order as they were sent. In
addition, the one-minute time limit may elapse for the
second DSG request before the ﬁrst DSG request
ﬁnishes.
Ü Ü 2200

Display Graphics Examples
This example displays text from the primitive graphics code
in report 1A0. The run user must press Resume to continue.
@dsg,0,a,1 .
This example displays graphics from the primitive graphics
code in report 2I0. The run continues automatically.
@dsg,0,i,2,,y .
2200 Þ Þ
This example displays graphics on station 123 from the
primitive graphics code in report 3C0. The run continues
automatically:
@dsg,0,c,3,,y,,123 .
Ü Ü 2200

GOC and @GOC (Generate
Organization Charts)
Use the Generate Organization Chart (GOC) function or GOC
statement to create organization charts.

Generate Organization Charts Control Line
Format
GOC [report]
where report is the report containing Generate Organization
Chart commands. (Type goc - to indicate the current
report.) For more details, see Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

Generate Organization Chart Statement Format
@GOC,c,d,r[,lab] ulvl?[,notxt?,ige?,igcz?,fxcz?]
optmz?[,outrslt?,
unp? vlinesi,vlineso,vco,vbuffz] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing Generate
Organization Chart
commands. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

lab

Label to go to in case of error.

ulvl?

Create an upper level chart? Y
or N. If Y, produces a special
chart that contains only one
box at all levels except the
bottom level.

notxt?

Eliminate all text? Y or N. If Y,
produces a chart containing
boxes with no text (useful for
determining the size of the
boxes).

ige?

Ignore all error conditions? Y
or N.

igcz?

Ignore the character size? Y or
N. If Y, does not check
whether the resulting
character size is within the
correct range for the selected
display device.

fxcz?

Select a ﬁxed character size
to use? Y or N. This character
size is dependent on the
selected display device.

optmz?

Optimize the result? Y or N. If
Y, produces a result and links
to the Graphics Scaler
command with the optimize
options selected. (This may
reduce the number of
characters required to
generate a chart.) If you
select the FONT parameter,
this subﬁeld must be a Y.

outrslt?

Display the graphics result on
the screen? Y or N. If you
entered y in the optmz?
subﬁeld, optimizes the result
with the Graphics Scaler

command ﬁrst, then displays
it.

unp?

Unpack the result? Y or N. If Y,
displays the result in
unpacked format, where the
resulting primitive commands
are not split across successive
lines; ignored if optmz? = Y.

vlinesi

Variable to capture the
number of input lines scanned
following the heading divider
line.

vlineso

Variable to capture the
number of output lines in the
result following the heading
divider line.

vco

Variable to capture the
number of characters to send
out.

vbuﬀz

Variable to capture the size of
the buﬀer used to hold chart
information.

@GOC Reserved Words
If an error occurs in the report, the run continues at the
speciﬁed label and loads STAT1$ with the system message
number and STAT2$ with the line number of the error. Use
the Load System Message (LSM) statement to obtain the
message.

Generate Organization Chart Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command generates a result containing graphics
primitive code to draw an organization chart. It uses the
information speciﬁed with Generate Organization Chart
commands in the input report. To display the chart, use
the Display Graphics command.
It uses freeform data as the data appears, including
spaces. If data on a line exceeds the width of the
screen, type a semicolon (;) and continue the data on
the next line.
It detects commands only when the ﬁrst nonblank
character on a line is a dollar sign ($), or when the
command is in the global parameter section. Any other
occurrence of a dollar sign ($) is treated as literal data
for the chart.
When charts are printed on a plotter, the color white
prints as black.

Generate Organization Chart Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when creating a report containing
Generate Organization Chart commands:
Data commands provide text and other visible data.
Global parameters deﬁne where to place data, and
global commands enable you to insert comments,
specify ﬁller characters, and produce variations of
organization charts.
The term boxes refers to blocks of text that represent
names and titles, regardless of whether they have boxes
around them.
It is easier to enter commands in the proper order if you
ﬁrst make a sketch of how you want levels and boxes
organized and whose names are to be on each level.
Place global commands on any line between the
heading divider line and the $END command.
If you use a parameter or data command more than
once in a report, it uses only the last command given,
except for the $APPROVED and $SIGNATURE commands.
You can use a global command more than once in a
report.
Organization charts displayed on the screen do not
show actual character size. Use these displayed charts
to check correct placement of boxes. When you print the
chart, the character sizes are correct.

Generate Organization Chart Procedures
To create an organization chart, in a blank freeform report,
type commands in this order: $PARAMETERS command
Global parameters as needed
$DATA command
Data commands as needed
$END command
To enter names in the chart, start with the top level name.
Then enter one name per level until you get to the bottom
level. Return to the topmost level in the same branch.
Continue entering names, one per level, in this manner.
When you have entered all names in one branch, go to the
topmost name in the next branch and continue.
To enter command subﬁelds, enter command subﬁelds in
any order, using only the subﬁelds you want. Separate
subﬁelds and commands with commas, as in
conﬁdential,red,ur. The Generate Organization Chart
command ignores tab characters and leading spaces within
a subﬁeld.
To expand box width, use a row of ﬁller characters. See the
global command $FILLER for more information.
2200 Þ Þ
To return to the text screen,
For PC terminals: Press Shift + Esc twice.
For UTS 30 or UTS 60 terminals: Press Shift + Disp.
Ü Ü 2200

If you are using Business Information Server for Microsoft
Windows Client or Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server, the chart is displayed in a separate
window. Use the mouse to activate the main text window.

Generate Organization Chart Example
$parameters Begin global parameters.
horizontal Arrange bottom level of boxes
horizontally.
plotter,small Use 8-1/2 x 11 inch plotter paper.
level,1,2,red,blue Define level 1 with two lines
of text.
level,2,2,green,blue Define level 2 with two
lines of text.
$data Begin data commands.
$level,1 Use next two lines as level 1 text.
PRESIDENT
J. A. DOE
$level,2 Use next two lines as level 2 text.
DISTRICT 1 CHIEF
J. B. DOE
$level,2
DISTRICT 2 CHIEF
J. C. DOE
$level,2
DISTRICT 3 CHIEF
J. D. DOE
$end End of commands.

Generate Organization Chart Global Parameters
Begin the global parameter portion of the report with the
$PARAMETERS command. You can place more than one
global parameter on a line. Separate parameters with
spaces.

Parameter Description

Background Speciﬁes the background
color of the screen. If you
specify an item to be the
same color as the
background color, the item
is displayed in black. If you
specify PLOTTER, the system
ignores this parameter.
Format: BACKGROUND,color
where color is the
Background color. Default =
black. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.

Color

Eliminates all colors from an
existing chart. For black and
white plotter output from a
color chart, use this
parameter to change the
colors to black and white.

Format: COLOR,action
where action is On (to use
all colors as speciﬁed) or Oﬀ
(for black and white plotter
output). Default = On.

Columns

Arranges the bottom level
into a maximum of twovertical columns. This
parameter is valid for upperlevel management charts
only; these charts have only
one name at each level
except the bottom level.
When you use this
parameter, type y for the
Upper Level Chart option on
the Generate Organization
Chart input screen.
Format: COLUMNS

Conﬁdential Places the word
CONFIDENTIAL on the chart.
Format:
CONFIDENTIAL,color,position
where

color is the text color.
Default = black. See
Generate Organization Chart
Colors.

position is the two-character
abbreviation specifying
where to place the word.
Default = UR (upper right).
See Generate Organization
Chart Screen Position
Options.

Font

Speciﬁes the character font
to be used. To see examples
of font types, enter chart,e
on the control line.
Format: FONT,n
where n is the font number
(0 - 28). Default = 0. For any
number but 0, type y for the
Optimize Results option on
the Generate Organization
Chart input screen.

Horizontal

Arranges the bottom level of
boxes horizontally. This
parameter is not valid for
upper level charts.
Format: HORIZONTAL

Ignore

Ignores any errors
encountered in the $DATA
section.

Format: IGNORE

Level

Deﬁnes up to twelve levels
of boxes. Use one LEVEL
parameter for each level of
boxes.
Format:
LEVEL,n,qty,color1,color2,...
where

n is the level label. Enter
any character from 0 - 9 or
A - Z. The ﬁrst level
encountered by the
Generate Organization Chart
command is the top level on
the chart, the second level
encountered is next, and so
on.
qty is a number (0 - 128)
specifying the maximum
number of lines of text on
this level. Number 0 splits
the bottom level into more
than two columns if there
are three or more levels; the
bottom level must be
vertical, and 0 must be used
for the level next to the
bottom. 0 overrides the qty
speciﬁed in the Vertical
parameter.

color1,color2,... is the text
color for each line of text.
Default = white. See
Generate Organization Chart
Colors. To use the same
color for more than one line
of text, type a color followed
by the number of lines in
parentheses, as follows:
level,1,3,blue(1),red(2).

Page

Places the word PAGE and a
page identiﬁer on the chart.
Format:
PAGE,pageid,position,color
where

pageid is a one- to fourcharacter page identiﬁer,
such as a page number.
position is a two-character
abbreviation specifying
where to place the text.
Default = LR (lower right).
See Generate Organization
Chart Screen Position
Options.
color is the text color.
Default = white. See
Generate Organization Chart
Colors.

Plotter

Creates the code for
plotting.
Format:
PLOTTER,size,orientation
where

size is the paper size. SMALL
(8-1/2 x 11 inches) or LARGE
(11 x 15-1/4 inches). Default
= SMALL.
orientation is the paper
width. WIDE (11 inches) or
TALL (8 31/2 inches). This
ﬁeld is valid only if size is
SMALL. Default = WIDE.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Scope

Displays the chart.
Format: SCOPE

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

Scope

Displays the chart on a

graphics terminal. Parameter
is not valid on PCC, PCE, or
PCS terminals.
Format: SCOPE,type
where type is the terminal
type. UTS60 or AQUA (for
AQUASTAR). Default = UTS 60
for all terminals except
AQUASTAR.

Ü Ü 2200

Stagger

Displays the bottom level of
boxes as two horizontal,
staggered rows. This
parameter is valid for upperlevel management charts only;
these charts have only one
name at each level except the
bottom level. When you use
this parameter, type y for the
Upper Level Chart option on
the Generate Organization
Chart input screen.
Format: STAGGER

SubLevel

Deﬁnes a nonsupervisory staﬀ
position (secretary, technician,
and so on) as a sublevel of the

previous level. Enter this
parameter after its
corresponding LEVEL
parameter, one sublevel per
level. This parameter is
ignored if used for the bottom
level.
Format: SUBLEVEL,n,qty,color1,color2,...
where

n is the label speciﬁed in the
corresponding LEVEL
parameter. Enter any character
from 0 - 9 or A - Z. The ﬁrst
level encountered by the
Generate Organization Chart
command is the top sublevel
on the chart, the second level
is next, and so on. A minimum
of 1 level and a maximum of
12 levels are allowed.
qty is a number (1 - 128)
specifying how many lines are
in the sublevel. This number
must be less than or equal to
the number of lines in the
corresponding LEVEL
parameter.
color1,color2,... is the text
color for each line of text. See
Generate Organization Chart

Colors. Default = white. To use
the same color for more than
one line of text, type a color
followed by the number of
lines in parentheses, as
follows: sublevel,1,3,aqua(1),pink(3)
See Generate Organization
Chart Colors.

Vertical

Arranges the bottom level of
boxes vertically. This
parameter is not valid for
upper-level charts. Format 1
stacks boxes with no space
between them. Format 2
stacks boxes with spaces
between them.
Format 1: VERTICAL,STK,qty
where qty is the maximum
number of boxes to display
before automatically splitting
the boxes into two columns.
Type a number from 0 - 99.
Format 2:
VERTICAL,SPREAD,position
where position is an option
indicating where to place
boxes, left or right of the

connecting line. L (left) or R
(right).

Generate Organization Chart Data Commands
Data commands provide names and titles and other visible
data on the chart. Begin the data portion of the report with
the $DATA command. Then enter data commands as
needed. These commands are valid only within the portion
of commands following the $DATA command. Do not enter
more than one data command on a line.

Command

Description

Approved

Places on the chart the text on
the following lines (12
maximum), and draws a solid
signature line above the text.
Use no more than a total of two
$SIGNATURE and $APPROVED
commands per report.
Format:
$APPROVED,position,line?,color
where

position is a two-character
abbreviation specifying where to
place the text. Default = LL
(lower left). See Generate
Organization Chart Screen
Position Options.
line? means "Draw a line above
the signature?" Default = yes. If

no, enter nl (no-line). Line color
is determined by the $LINECOLOR command.

color is the text color. Default =
white. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.
This example places four lines
of red text in the upper right
corner of the chart,
accompanied by the characters
APPROVED.
$approved,ur,nl,red
J. Q. Public
Group Manager
Techno-Hi, Inc.
1/19/93

Blank

Inserts a blank line into text
following the $LEVEL, $SUBLEVEL, $SIGNATURE,
$APPROVED, $DUTIES,
$MISSION, $OBJECTIVE, or
$RESPONSIBILITY commands.
Format: $BLANK

Box

Draws boxes in a speciﬁed style
around each item for every level
or sublevel, until another $BOX
command changes the style, or
the $NO-BOX command is

encountered. All boxes at the
lowest level of a vertical chart
are drawn as $BOX,NORMAL or
$NO-BOX.
Format: $BOX,style
where style is the box style.
Default = NORMAL. The box
styles are
NORMAL-box outlined with a
single line.
OUTLINE-box outlined with
double lines.
BOLD-box outlined with double
solid ﬁlled lines.
3D-LEFT-box appears threedimensional from the left.
3D-RIGHT-box appears threedimensional from the right.

Box Color

Draws boxes around subsequent
items in a speciﬁed color.
Format: $BOX-COLOR,color
where color is the box color.
Default = white. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.

Conﬁdential

Places a message on the chart
regarding conﬁdentiality. Use
only one $CONFIDENTIAL

command per chart. It overrides
the CONFIDENTIAL global
parameter.
Format:
$CONFIDENTIAL,position,color
text
where

abbreviation specifying text
position. Default = UR (upper
right). See Generate
Organization Chart Screen
Position Options.
color is the text color. Default =
black. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.
text is the message text.
This example places the words
Restricted Distribution in red
text in the lower left corner of
the chart.
$confidential,ll,red
Restricted
Distribution

Dashed

Draws all connecting lines as
dashed lines until you use the
$SOLID command to change to
solid lines.

Format: $DASHED

Data

Indicates the beginning of the
data commands.
Format: $DATA

Date

Adds a date to a chart. Use only
one command per chart.
Two-digit year dates must
represent dates between 1944
and 2043.
Four-digit year dates must
represent dates between 1600
and 2299.
One-digit year dates must
represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from
the current date. For example, 1
January 1996 through 31
December 2005 (if the current
day was 1 November 2000).
Format: $DATEn,position,color
[date]
where

n is the date format. Default =
MONTH DD, YYYY. The date
formats are: $DATE MONTH DD,

$DATE1 YYMMDD
$DATE2 DD MMM YY
$DATE3 YDDD

$DATE9 DD/MM/YY
$DATE11 YYYYMMDD
$DATE12 DD MMM YYYY
$DATE14 YYYYDDD
$DATE15 DDMMYYYY
$DATE16 MM/DD/YYYY
$DATE18 MMDDYYYY
$DATE19 DD/MM/YYYY
$DATE20 YYYY-MM-DD

Ü Ü 2200

position is a two-character
abbreviation specifying where to

(lower left). See Generate

color is the text color. Default =
black. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.

date is a speciﬁc date; enter
literal date. Default = current
date.

Duties

Adds text lines (a maximum of
12) to the chart as a block of
information, with no boxes or
lines drawn around them. Use
only one of the following
commands for each
organization chart: $DUTIES,
$OBJECTIVE, $MISSION, or
$RESPONSIBILITY.
Format: $DUTIES,position,color
where

position is a two-character
abbreviation specifying screen
position. Default = UR (upper
right). See Generate
Organization Chart Screen
Position Options.
color is the text color. Default =
black. See Generate
Organization Chart Colors.

End

Signals the end of the
commands. The Generate
Organization Chart command
ignores anything following the
$END command.
Format: $END

Justify

Arranges text at levels and
sublevels left-justiﬁed, rightjustiﬁed, or centered.
Justiﬁcation remains the same
until you change it, using
another $JUSTIFY command.
Format: $JUSTIFY,type
where type is the justiﬁcation
type. The type values are: l or
left - left-justify the text
r or right - right-justify the text
c or center - center the text
(default)

Level

Text for the speciﬁed level.
Format 1 draws the level as
speciﬁed; Format 2 does not
draw the level.

Level (Format Format 1:
1)
$LEVEL,n,color1,color2,...
where

n is the label assigned to the
LEVEL global parameter.
color1,color2,... is the text color
for each line of text; these
colors override the colors
deﬁned in the LEVEL global
parameter for this level. Any
color set with the $LEVEL
command remains in eﬀect until
changed by another $LEVEL
command for the same level.
Format is the same as for the
LEVEL global parameter.
Enter the same number, or
multiples of the same number,
of lines of text as deﬁned by the
qty ﬁeld in the LEVEL global
parameter. (Use the IGNORE
global parameter to make the
Generate Organization Chart
command ignore any extra
text.) Missing text is
automatically displayed as a
blank line, provided there is at
least one valid text line.
If there are names at the
bottom level, you can enter the
names without typing $LEVEL,n
before each name.

Level (Format Format 2: $LEVEL,n,V
2)

where n is the label assigned to
the LEVEL global parameter.
This format indicates a missing
box at any level other than the
top or bottom. Do not type text
lines following this command
format. Type n for the Upper
Level option in the Generate
Organization Chart input screen.
If you type y, this command
format is treated as format 1.

the $LEVEL command with

level,3,1,blue
$data
$level,1
E. Johnson Level 1 text, Line 1
(green)
President Level 1 text, Line 2
(green)
$level,2,v No Level 2 text

J. Anderson Level 3 text (blue)
R. Olson

Solid

Draws all subsequent

See also the $DASHED
command.
Format: $SOLID

Sub-Level

Text for the speciﬁed sublevel.
The format for the text is the
same as the $LEVEL command,
except that you can enter a list
of names (without typing $SUBLEVEL,n before each name) at
any level but the bottom.
Format: $SUBLEVEL,n,position,color1,color2,...
where

n is the label assigned in the
SUB-LEVEL global parameter.
position indicates where to
place the sublevel box. Default
= directly below the main level
box. The position values are: l or
left - places the sublevel box to
the left of the main level. r or
right - places the sublevel box
to the right of the main level.

color1,color2,... is the text color.
These colors override the colors
deﬁned by the SUB-LEVEL
global parameter for this
sublevel. Any color set with the
$SUB-LEVEL command remains
in eﬀect until you change it with
another $SUB-LEVEL command
for the same level. The format is
the same as for the SUB-LEVEL
command.
The example below places the
title Manufacturing and
Production in red in the upper
center of the chart.
$title,red Manufacturing
and Production

Title

Places a title on the chart. Use a
maximum of two title
commands; the character size
of the second title is slightly
smaller than that of the ﬁrst.
Format: $TITLE,position,color
text
where

position is a two-character
abbreviation specifying where to
place the text. Default = UC
(upper center). See Generate

Organization Chart Screen
Position Options.

color is the color name for the
title. Default = black. See
Generate Organization Chart
Colors.
text is the title text.

Uppercase

Displays all alphabetic
characters as uppercase. See
also the $LOWERCASE
command.
Format: $UPPERCASE

Generate Organization Chart Global Commands
Place global commands on any line in the Generate
Organization Chart command report between the heading
divider line and the $END command.

Command Description

Comment

Changes the character used
to denote comments to
another character. Default =
period (.). To include
comments that are not
printed on the chart, begin
the command line with a
period (.) in column 1,
followed by a comment.
Format: $COMMENT,x
where x is any character
other than A - Z. Do not use
the dollar sign ($).

Endi

See the $SET command.
Format: @ENDI

Filler

Changes the ﬁller character.
Default = \. Because the
Generate Organization Chart

command ignores spaces,
arrange text by using either
the $JUSTIFY command or
ﬁller characters. This
command works with textoriented data commands.
Format: $FILLER,x
where x is any character
other than A - Z. Do not use
the dollar sign ($).
This example changes the
ﬁller character to #.
$level,3
\ this line has 31
characters \
$filler,# (Change
filler
character to #.)
$level,3
#this line has 29
characters#

Include

See the $SET command.
Format: $INCLUDE,name
where name is the name
deﬁned in the $SET
command.

Set

Selects which commands to
interpret. This is helpful for
making variations of a chart,
such as one with only
speciﬁc departments or only
upper-management
positions. Instead of
preparing separate reports,
include all information in one
report. Then select the
commands to include, using
the $SET, $INCLUDE, and
$ENDI commands.
Use the $INCLUDE and
$ENDI commands to select
names throughout the report
to include or exclude on
chart variations. Use the
$INCLUDE command at the
beginning of the selected
names to label the names,
and type the $ENDI
command at the end of the
selected names. Use the
$SET command to deﬁne the
assigned label as true or
false.
If you nest the $INCLUDE
commands, the outermost
$INCLUDE command with a
label deﬁned as false has
priority.
Format: $SET,label,condition

where

label is a string of up to eight
characters or numbers. Do
not use commas.
condition is either true (t) or
false (f). When you deﬁne a
label as true, all commands
assigned that label are
included on the chart. When
you deﬁne a label as false,
all commands assigned that
label are excluded from the
chart.
In this example, the
commands assigned label1
are included on the chart
because the $SET command
has deﬁned them as true.
The commands assigned
label2 are excluded from the
chart because label2 is false.
$set,label1,t Define
label1 as
true.
$set,label2,f Define
label2 as
false.
$include,label1 Label1
is true;
include next
. commands.
$level,1

Y. CARLSON
H. ANDERSON
$endi End label1
commands.
$include,label2 Label2
is false;
exclude next
. commands.
$level,1
F. JOHNSON
N. LARSON
$endi End label2
commands.

Generate Organization Chart Examples
To see an example of an organization chart generated by
Generate Organization Chart, enter chart,e. To return to the
CHART run menu, press Resume.
The following example generates an organizational chart,
using the Generate Organization Chart commands in report
2H0. The chart is not optimized and is not an upper-level
chart.
@goc,0,h,2 n n .

GR
Use the GR run to create a chart report from existing data in
column-formatted reports.
The report you want to chart must be on display. Your
terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

GR Control Line Format
GR

GR Options
Option

Description

L

Vertical line chart (default)

B

Vertical comparative bar chart

H

Horizontal line chart

BH

Horizontal comparative bar
chart

S

Stacked bar chart

F

Filled line chart

M

Mixed bar and line chart

C

Scatter chart

X

Pareto chart

P

Pie chart

GR Parameters
Bar and Line Chart Parameters
Row 1: Type l in the x-axis ﬁeld. Type x in each column (up
to six) containing data to be charted.
Row 2: Type the ﬁll pattern number for each column marked
with an X in the ﬁrst row. For patterns, see BARG (Bar
Chart).
Row 3: Type the color number for each ﬁeld marked with an
X in the ﬁrst row. For colors, see BARG (Bar Chart).
Pareto Chart Parameters
Row 1: Type l in the x-axis ﬁeld. Type x in the column
containing data to be charted.
Row 2: Type the ﬁll pattern number for each column marked
with an X in the ﬁrst row. For patterns, see PARETO (Pareto
Chart).
Row 3: Type the color number for each ﬁeld marked with an
X in the ﬁrst row. For colors, see PARETO (Pareto Chart).
Mixed Chart Parameters
Row 1: Type l in the x axis ﬁeld. Type b in each column
containing data to be represented by bars. Type x in each
column containing data to be represented by lines. The total
number of B and X ﬁelds must be at least two, with a
maximum of six.
Row 2: Type the ﬁll pattern number for each ﬁeld marked
with a B or X in the ﬁrst row. For patterns, see MIXED (Mixed

Chart).
Row 3: Type the color number for each ﬁeld marked with a B
or X in the ﬁrst row. For colors, see MIXED (Mixed Chart).
Pie Chart Parameters
Row 1: Type l in the x-axis ﬁeld. Type x in the column
containing data to be charted. If you specify more than one
X column, pie segments are constructed only from the
values on the ﬁrst line of each selected column.
Row 2: Type the ﬁll pattern number for each column marked
with an X in the ﬁrst row. For patterns, see PIEG (Pie Chart).
Row 3: Type the color number for each ﬁeld marked with an
X in the ﬁrst row. For colors, see PIEG (Pie Chart).
Scatter Chart Parameters
Row 1: Type x in the column containing x axis values. Type y
in the column containing y axis values.
Row 2: Type the marker symbol number for each column
marked with an X in the ﬁrst row. For available symbols, see
SCAT (Scatter Chart).
Row 3: Type the color number for each ﬁeld marked with an
X in the ﬁrst row. For colors, see SCAT (Scatter Chart).

GR Outcome
A function mask is displayed. Fill in the function mask with
the options and parameters as described in the sections
that follow. After transmitting the completed function mask,
the run creates a chart using the report data.

GRID
Use the Grid run to create a chart report from data in a
column-formatted report.
To display charts on your monitor, it must be conﬁgured for
color.

Grid Control Line Format
GRID[,report]
where report is the report to chart.

Grid Options
Option

Description

L

Vertical line chart (default).

B

Comparative bar chart.

C

Scatter chart.

P

Pie chart.

Grid Parameters
Pie, Bar, and Line Chart Parameters
Type l in the x axis ﬁelds (slice label ﬁelds for pie charts). If
you do not type an l, the system oﬀers a choice of labels,
such as WEEKS, YEARS, or FISCAL MONTHS.
Type numbers from 1 to 6 in the ﬁelds whose data you want
to represent on the chart. Place the numbers in the order
you want the lines, bars, or slices to be displayed. Field
headings become captions.
Scatter Chart Parameters
Type x in the column containing x axis values. Type y in the
column containing y axis values.

Grid Outcome
A function mask is displayed. Fill in the function mask with
the options and parameters as described in the sections
that follow. After transmitting the completed function mask,
the run creates a chart report containing data from the
report. The run places the result in the same cabinet and
drawer as your data report.

Grid Guidelines
Save the chart report produced by a Grid run in a freeform
report created before requesting a chart command. Use the
Duplicate Report or Replace Report command. The report
must have a heading divider line.

Grid Procedure
To subtotal data in the report (and create a line chart report)

1. Type s in the ﬁeld containing the caption for the chart.
The system detects up to six diﬀerences in the column
and uses them as captions.

2. Type + in the ﬁeld containing the data for the chart.
3. Type l in the ﬁeld containing labels.

GS and @GS (Graphics Scaler)
Use the Graphics Scaler (GS) function or GS statement to
Modify the appearance of a chart
Change the size of a chart
Rotate a chart on the screen or page
Oﬀset a chart on the screen or page
Convert expanded syntax into graphics primitive code
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics and you must
save the graphics primitive code for the chart you want to
modify. Remember the report number.

Graphics Scaler Control Line Format
GS [report]
where report is the report containing graphics primitive
code. To indicate the current report, type gs -. For more
details, see Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process.

Graphics Scaler Statement Format
@GS,c,d,r[,lab]
maxy[,o,ige?,unp?,aga?,expand?,ighitxt?,
outrslt?,plotter?] sfx[,offx,offy,]sfy
angle[,absx,
absy vci,vco,vminx,vmidx,vmaxx,vminy,vmidy,vmaxy]
.

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the
graphics primitive code. For
more details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to
Process.

lab

Label to go to in case of error.

maxy

Maximum Y value on which to
check the limits (ranging from
0 to 32767; you can type a 0
if you want 32767). See
Maximum Y Coordinate for
Various Devices to determine
the maximum Y.

o

Options ﬁeld for IN/OUT
format (see Graphics Scaler

Options for IN/OUT Format).

ige?

Ignore errors? Y or N. If Y,
truncates values that are out
of range and ignores invalid
commands and sequences. If
you use the S option, this
subﬁeld must be N.

unp?

Unpack the result? Y or N. If Y,
displays the result in
unpacked format with one
command per line. If you use
the S option or if outrslt? = Y,
this subﬁeld must be N.

aga?

Assume graphics active? Y or
N. If Y, assumes that a ZI
command preceded the data,
and does not require a closing
ZT command to end the data.
If you use the S option or if
outrslt? = Y, this subﬁeld
must be N.

expand?

Handle expanded syntax? Y or
N. If Y, converts expanded
syntax into graphics primitive
code. If you use the S option
or if outrslt? = Y, this subﬁeld
must be N.

ighitxt?

Ignore high text? Y or N. If Y,
never converts data to highquality text, even if the
statement encounters a CT2
command or rotated text.

outrslt?

Display the graphics result on
the screen? Y or N. If Y, both
unp? and aga? must be N.

plotter?

Optionally add or delete
plotter primitives. Select one
of the following: P - Add
ZO1ZS1R (commands for
small plotter).
L - Add ZO1ZS1 (commands
for large plotter).
D - Delete ZO1, ZS1, and
initial R command.
Any other character causes no
change.

sfx

A number (0.0 through
32767.0) to be used for
scaling appropriate X
parameters. Default = 1 (if
you enter 0, uses 1).

A scale factor of 2.0 produces
a result twice as large; a scale
factor of 0.5 produces a result
half as large, and so on.
To scale just the X axis, set sfx
to the desired value and set
sfy to 1.0.

oﬀx

Oﬀset value (-9999999999 to
9999999999) to add to the
absolute X components.

oﬀy

Oﬀset value (-9999999999 to
9999999999) to add to the
absolute Y components.

sfy

A number (0.0 through
32767.0) to be used for
scaling appropriate Y
parameters. Default = 1 (if
you enter 0, uses 1).
A scale factor of 2.0 produces
a result twice as large; a scale
factor of 0.5 produces a result
half as large, and so on.
To scale just the Y axis, set sfy
to the desired value and set
sfx to 1.0.

angle

Rotation angle (-360.0 to
360.0) used to rotate all X and
Y values and angles. Positive
angles produce
counterclockwise rotation;
negative angles produce
clockwise rotation.

absx

Absolute X rotation value
(-9999999999 to
9999999999).

absy

Absolute Y rotation value
(-9999999999 to
9999999999).

vci

Variable to capture the
number of characters
scanned.

vco

Variable to capture the
number of characters to send.

vminx

Variable to capture the
minimum X value.

vmidx

Variable to capture the
midpoint X value.

vmaxx

Variable to capture the
maximum X value.

vminy

Variable to capture the
minimum Y value.

vmidy

Variable to capture the
midpoint Y value.

vmaxy

Variable to capture the
maximum Y value.

characters.

Maximum Y Coordinate for Various Devices
To determine aspect ratio, see the table that follows, or
enter chart on the control line and select "Determine Type of
Terminal" from the Chart menu. The table that follows shows
the maximum y (vertical) coordinate available on each
device. The maximum x (horizontal) coordinate is 32767 for
all devices. The aspect ratio is the ratio of 32767 units in the
y direction. Aspect ratio = y/x. For example, 23999/32767 =
.73242.
