@GS Reserved Words
In case of an error in the data report, the run continues at
the label you specify and loads STAT1$ with the system
message number and STAT2$ with one fewer than the line
number in the report being processed where the error
occurred. Use the Load System Message (LSM) statement to
obtain the message.

Graphics Scaler Outcome
The command oﬀsets, scales, or rotates the chart as
requested, and processes expanded syntax. The command
always performs the ﬁrst move as an absolute move, even if
a move is not there or if you select the R option in the
IN/OUT Format ﬁeld.

Graphics Scaler Examples
This example processes report 1A0, creating an unscaled
and unrotated display.
@gs,0,a,1 20000 1 0 .

0,a,1

Process report 1A0.

20000

Create maximum Y values to
20,000.

1

Set scale factor to 1.

0

Rotate angle to 0.

This example processes report 1A0, creating a scaled,
oﬀset, and rotated display.
@gs,0,a,1,10 23999,o,n,n,n,n,n,y 0.75,2000,\
1000 45,16383,11399 <scanned>i5,\
<chars>i5,<minx>i5,<midx>i5,<maxx>i5,\
<miny>i5,<midy>i5,<maxy>i5 .

0,a,1

Process report 1A0.

10

Go to label 10 in case of

an error.

23999

Set maximum Y value.

o

Optimize the results.

n,n,n,n,n

Do not ignore errors,
unpack the result, assume
graphics code, handle
expanded syntax, or
ignore high text.

y

Display the result on the
screen.

0.75

Reduce to 3/4 size.

2000

Move to the right 2000.

1000

Move up 1000.

45

Rotate result 45 degrees
counterclockwise.

16383

Rotate around the X value

16383.

11399

Rotate around the Y value
11399.

<scanned>i5 Load <scanned> with the
number of characters
scanned.

<chars>i5

Load <chars> with the
number of characters in
the result.

<minx>i5

Load <minx> with the
minimum X value.

<midx>i5

Load <midx> with the
midpoint X value.

<maxx>i5

Load <maxx> with the
maximum X value.

<miny>i5

Load <miny> with the
minimum Y value.

<midy>i5

Load <midy> with the

midpoint Y value.

<maxy>i5

Load <maxy> with the
maximum Y value.

GTAD
Use the GTAD command to adjust the location of text on a
chart display. The command moves the text as speciﬁed.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

GTAD Control Line Format
GTAD [report]
where report is the report in the current cabinet containing
graphics primitive code. Default = current report.

GTAD Guidelines
Use text strings exactly as they appear in graphics primitive
code. Text in the graphics primitive code is located between
apostrophes. The command does not move partial character
strings.

@GTO (Go To)
Use the Go To (GTO) statement to branch unconditionally to
another location in the run or to another run control report
in the same cabinet and drawer.

Go To Statement Format
@GTO {lab|END[,n,n,n]|LIN[+]n|LIN-n|RPX r} .

Field

Description

lab

Label at which to continue
execution. You can specify the
label number or a variable
that contains the label
number.

END

Terminate the run and display
the output area.

[n,n,n]

Optional subﬁelds valid only in
runs that were started with
the LNK statement. You can
use the subﬁelds to specify up
to three status codes (must be
integers) to pass back to the
original run.
The original run can access
the codes, using STAT1$,
STAT2$, and STAT3$. For more
information, see @LNK (Link
to Another Script).

LIN

Current line.

[+]n

Jump to the line that is equal
to the current line plus n lines.
When the system encounters
continued lines (reverse slant
[\] at the end of a line), it
considers the last line of the
continuation sequence as the
current line.
Specify n using an integer or
using a variable that contains
an integer. When using a
variable, do not place a plus
sign in front of the variable
name. If the variable contains
a negative number, execution
continues at the current line
minus the absolute value of
the variable.

-n

Jump to the line that is equal
to the current line minus n
lines. When the system
encounters continued lines
(reverse slant [\] at the end of
a line), it considers the last
line of the continuation
sequence as the current line.
Specify n using an integer or
using a variable that contains
an integer. When using a

variable, it must contain the
negative integer. For example,
<count> would contain -1; do
not place a minus sign in front
of the variable name. Run
execution continues at the
current line minus the
absolute value of the variable.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Plus and minus signs are
allowed when named variables
are used.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

RPX r

Jump to a run control report in
the same cabinet and drawer
as the current run, where r is
the report number of the run
control report.
The run control report at which
you are continuing execution
need not be registered;
however, inform your
administrator that you are
using the report as part of your
registered run.

Go To Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Go To statement:
When you use the RPX ﬁeld to jump to another run
control report in the same cabinet and drawer as the
current run, consider the following:
All security checks for the ﬁrst run must be met by
the run control report to which you want to jump.
After jumping to the new run control report, all
variables and labels are still valid.
It is a poor programming practice to jump to line
numbers. Whenever you modify the run, you must also
check all line references.
Rather than using line numbers, use labels to tag line
locations; then use the Build Label Table command to
build a table of the labels and line numbers.
For information on computed IF/GO statements, see @IF
(If Conditional).

Go To Examples
This example jumps to the label speciﬁed by <start>:
@gto <start> .
This example jumps to label 010:
@gto 010 .
This example executes the statements in report 2 of the
same cabinet and drawer as the current report:
@gto rpx 2 .

GUIX (Graphical User Interface
Examples)
Use the Graphical User Interface Examples (GUIX) command
to see a dynamic demonstration of the graphical user
interface statements in action.
This command requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
Use this format:
GUIX

Hn (Hold Lines on Screen)
Use the Hold Lines on Screen (Hn) command to hold a
speciﬁed number of lines at the top of the screen as you roll
through the report.
The command displays the speciﬁed lines at the top of the
screen. When you roll back to the top of the report, the held
lines are still displayed above the heading lines.
The report in which to hold lines must be on display.

Hold Lines on Screen Control Line Format
Hn
where n is the number of lines to hold.

Note: Do not leave a space between the H and the number
of lines. (For example, enter h10, not h 10.)

HCn (Hold Characters on Screen)
Use the Hold Characters on Screen (HCn) command to hold
a speciﬁed number of characters on the left side of the
screen as you shift the report.
The command displays the speciﬁed characters on the left
side of the screen. When you shift the report, the held
characters are still displayed on the left side of the screen.
The report in which to hold characters must be on display.

Hold Characters on Screen Control Line Format
HCn
where n is the number of characters to hold.

Note: Do not leave a space between the HC and the
number of characters. (For example, enter hc10, not hc 10.)

HELP and @HELP (Help)
Use the Help (HELP) statement to display graphical help.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server.

Help Control Line Format
HELP[,topic] .

Field

Description

topic

The help topic title or keyword.

Help Statement Format
@HELP[,,lab] topic .

Field

Description

lab

The run continues processing
at the label number if the
HELP statement errors within
the workstation client.

topic

The help topic title or keyword.

Notes:
If a topic is not speciﬁed, place two apostrophes in the
topic ﬁeld.
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server, the reserved WS$ (workstation ﬂag)
is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(14-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.

Help Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Help statement:
If the topic is not found, the general index screen is
displayed.
The run continues when the help window is displayed.
You must close the help window when ﬁnished.

Help Reserved Word
If the HELP statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and
the statement receives an error from the workstation client,
the run continues at the label. The STAT1$ reserved word
contains an error status. See Graphical Interface Returned
Status Codes (STAT1$ Values) for possible values returned in
the STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: If a run syntax error is detected, the run errors; it
does not continue at the speciﬁed label.

Help Examples
To ﬁnd help on the CBX statement within a run, enter: @help
‘cbx’ .
To access the general help index within a run, enter: @help ‘
‘.
To ﬁnd help on the CBX statement, enter: help cbx .
To access the general help index, enter: help .

Help Function Key
Use the Help function key to display context-sensitive
online help.

Note: If you are using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server, the function key bar optionally is
displayed at the bottom of the screen. If it is not, click File
in the menu bar at the top of the screen, select Display and
click Fkeys Displayed to display the list of function keys
currently available.
The function keys may also be hidden if the window is too
small. You can correct this by manually increasing the
window size or by changing the main window font to a
smaller size.

@HID (Hide Control)
Use the Hide Control (HID) statement to remove a control
from the screen. A control can be: BTN (Button), CBX
(Combo Box), EDT (Edit Box), LST (List Box), PIC (Picture),
TXT (Text Box), or WIN (Window). If the control is a window,
any controls within that window are hidden. This statement
is similar to the Close Control (CLS) statement except that
the control still exists.
To display a control again, use the Show Control (SHW)
statement.
The HID statement temporarily removes the speciﬁed
controls from the screen.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Hide Control Statement Format
@HID[,vwh,...,vwh] .
where vwh is the variable that contains the control handle of
the control to hide. Default = all controls. Maximum = 80
entries.

Hide Control Outcome
The HID statement temporarily removes the speciﬁed
controls from the screen. This statement should not be used
with DDE conversation handles.

Hide Control Example
This example temporarily removes the active controls
whose handles reside in <shapes> and <sizes>.
@hid,<shapes>,<sizes> .

@HSH (Hash)
Use the Hash (HSH) statement to assign a number (hash
value) within a speciﬁed range to a given piece of input.
The statement performs a routine to assign a numeric value
to the input. As long as the range remains the same, the
routine produces the same numeric value for that piece of
input.

Hash Statement Format
@HSH v=vld,min-max .

Field

Description

v

Variable to receive the hash
number.

vld

Data to which the system
assigns a hash number.
Specify the data, using a
variable, constant, reserved
word, literal, or any
combination.

min-max Range in which the system
assigns a hash number, where
min is the lowest number in
the range and max is the
highest number in the range.
Specify numbers in the range
0 through 1,000,000.

Hash Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Hash statement:
To ensure the most even distribution of hashed
numbers, do not specify a vld ﬁeld larger than seven
characters in length.
The system uses the current value of variables and
reserved words when they get hashed. Therefore, if the
size or value of your vld ﬁeld changes (from day-to-day,
or system-to-system), the hashed result may also
change.
A hash value can serve as a numeric identiﬁer to index
information. Using a hash value rather than an
alphabetic or numeric scheme to index data has these
advantages:
Using hash values ensures a more even distribution
of data items among index identiﬁers.
For example, if you were indexing employee
information into reports based on the ﬁrst letter of
the last name, your "R" or "S" reports would more
than likely contain more records than your "Q" and
"Z" reports. Using a hash number solves this
unequal distribution.
The system determines and maintains the hash
value, so you need not know or remember the hash
value.

Hash Examples
This example indexes each user to a report number
between one and 100: @HSH <usrpt>i3=USER$,1-100 .
This example creates a random number between one and
999,999: @HSH <random>i6=MSEC$(6-0),1-999999 .

I and @IND (Index)
Use the Index (I) function or IND statement to create a
listing of a speciﬁed number of lines from all reports in a
drawer, starting at the date line of each report.

Index Control Line Format
I [qd]

Field

Description

q

Number of lines to display
from each report. Default =
heading lines (up to eight)
from each report.

d

Drawer to be indexed. For
more details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to Process.

Index Statement Format
@IND,c,d[,q,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to index.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

q

Number of lines to display
from each report. Default =
heading lines (up to eight).

lab

Label to go to if there are
either no reports in the drawer
or report 0 is the only report in
the drawer.

@IND Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the number of reports in the drawer.
STAT2$ contains the total number of lines in the drawer.

Index Outcome
Executing the Index command causes the following actions
to occur:
The command creates a result containing the following
lines:
The date line for the result.
A line containing the number of reports in the
drawer and the total number of lines in the drawer.
The speciﬁed number of lines from each report
starting with the date line in each report. The right
side of each date line contains the number of lines
in the corresponding report.
To help separate the information for each report, the
result also includes a blank line with a period in
column one between the information for each
report.
If you are indexing reports that are fewer than 80
characters wide, the command adds trailer lines that
contain the number of lines from each report.

Note: In the case of a limited-access report, such as a readprotected or encoded report, only the date line and the title
line are displayed. The contents of the report are not
included in the result.

@IND Statement Outcome
In addition, when executing the IND statement, if the drawer
does not exist, control goes to the label.

Index Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when executing the Index
command:
When determining the number of lines to include from
each report, remember that the control line is not
considered part of a report.
To see only the heading lines from each report, do not
specify a quantity (q).
To see all of the lines in each report, for quantity (q),
enter a number larger than the size of any report.

Note: There is no maximum number for the q ﬁeld;
however, since a request to index many lines from each
report can aﬀect system performance, use caution when
indexing large drawers.

@IND Statement Example
This example indexes the ﬁrst four lines from each report in
cabinet 0, drawer B, and goes to label 2 if drawer B does not
exist.
@ind,0,b,4,2 .

IBFN (Iterative Binary Find)
Use the Iterative Binary Find (IBFN) run to generate Binary
Find function masks that you can save, alter, and use again.
With the IBFN run, you can use any functionality available
with the Binary Find command, but with greater ease. For
example, you can do the following tasks:
Save parameters and options in the function mask and
use them again.
Recall a previously saved function mask instead of
retyping options and parameters into a new one.
Translate function masks into their equivalent statement
formats.
The report you intend to process must reside in the current
cabinet.

Iterative Binary Find Control Line Format
IBFN[*] {report|drawer}[,f,maskname,maskrep]

Field

Description

*

An asterisk to specify that
ﬁeld names are to be used in
place of column character
positions when a statement
is produced.

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to
Process.

drawer

Drawer to process. Use this
ﬁeld to process all reports in
a given drawer. For more
details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to
Process.

f

Report format in which to
process data (enables you to
process data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 if you do not

already have those columns
on display).

maskname The name of an existing
function mask, a new
function mask, or blank. (If
you leave this ﬁeld blank,
you name the new function
mask when you save it.) The
name can be 1 to 12
characters; it must begin
with a letter and contain no
spaces.

maskrep

Report containing previously
saved function masks. Use
this subﬁeld when the
function masks are in a
report other than the report
you are processing. Note that
if you leave the maskname
subﬁeld blank, you cannot
specify a report in the
maskrep subﬁeld.

ICAL (Iterative Calculate)
Use the Iterative Calculate (ICAL) run to create equation
sets that you can save, alter, and use again.
With the ICAL run, you can use any functionality available
with the Calculate command, but with greater ease. For
example, you can do the following tasks:
Save equations sets in the mask and use them again.
Recall a previously saved equation set instead of
retyping a new one.
Translate function masks into their equivalent statement
formats.
The ICAL run processes equation sets. An equation set is the
set of options, headings, and parameters from the
completed mask.
2200 Þ Þ
The report you intend to process must reside in the current
cabinet.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
A report must be on display.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

of column character positions
when a statement is produced.

2200 Þ Þ

f

Report format in which to
process data (enables you to

process data in ﬁelds beyond

Ü Ü 2200

eqnm

The name of an existing
equation set, a new equation
set, or blank. (If you leave this
ﬁeld blank, you name the new
equation set when you save
it.) The name can be 1 to 12
characters; it must begin with
a letter and contain no spaces.

r

CAL equations. Specify the
report when equations are in a
report other than the data
report on display. Note that if
you leave the eqnm subﬁeld
blank, you cannot specify a
report in the r subﬁeld.

@IF (If Conditional)
Use the If Conditional (IF) statement to test the relationship
between two or more values and specify the statements to
execute when the test condition is true or false.

If Conditional Statement Formats
Basic Format
@IF[,o] val1 op val2 stmt1 [ .] ; stmt2 .
Logical OR
@IF[,o] val1 op val2,val3[,val4,...,valn] stmt1 [
.] ; stmt2 .
Logical AND
@IF[,o] val1 op val2 & op val3
[& op val4 ... & op valn] stmt1 [ .] ; stmt2 .
Computed IF/GTO
@IF[,o] val1 op val2,(lab)[,val3,(lab),...,valn,
(lab)] ; stmt1 .

Field

Description

o

Options ﬁeld. See If
Conditional Options.

val1

Data to compare to another
value. You can specify the
data as a variable,
constant, literal, or reserved
word.

op

Relational operator. Use one
of the following:
= or EQ - Equal
GE - Greater than or equal
> or GT - Greater than
LE - less than or equal
< or LT - Less than
NE or NOT = or NOT EQ Not equal
NOT LT or NOT < - Not less
than
NOT GT or NOT > - Not
greater than

val2

Data to compare to val1.
You can specify the data,
using a variable, constant,
literal, or reserved word.

stmt1 [ . ]

Statements to execute
when the condition is true.
If you intend to execute
stmt2, do not include the
space-period-space after
the statement. For more
information, see If
Conditional Guidelines.

stmt2 .

Statements to execute
when the condition is false.
Include the space-periodspace after the statement.
For more information, see If
Conditional Guidelines.

,val3,...,valn A comma (,) to represent a
logical OR, followed by
another value, and so on.

& op
val3...& op
valn

An ampersand (&) to
represent a logical AND,
followed by a relational
operator, another value,
and so on.

,(lab)

Label or location at which to
continue execution when
the IF condition is true. In
addition to specifying any
valid label in the run, you
can also use any of the
following keywords:
END - end of run.
LIN +n - current line plus n
lines.

LIN -n - current line minus n
lines.
RPX r - run control report in
the same cabinet and
drawer, where r is the
report number.

stmt2

Statement indicating the
action to take if the IF
statement condition is not
met (FALSE).

If Conditional Date Formats
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between 1944
and 2043.
Four-digit year dates must represent dates between 1600
and 2299.
One-digit year dates must represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from the current date. For
example, 1 January 1996 through 31 December 2005 (if the
current day was 1 November 2000).

If Conditional Guidelines
Use the IF statement for conditional branching or the
execution of another statement.
If the condition is true, the statements are executed
until a branch is encountered or the equation is
terminated with a period. If the condition is false, the
statements following the semicolon are executed.
Include the space-period-space-semicolon-space to
terminate the IF statement, when you do not want to
execute stmt2 if the condition is true.
Syntax:
@IF {condition} {stmt1} . ; {stmt2} .
When the condition is true, stmt1 is executed and the
run continues at the next line.
When the condition is false, stmt2 is executed and the
run continues at the next line.
The system checks the content of A-type variables to
determine how to process the data. If the ﬁrst character
is numeric, the system scans for a space or nonnumeric. If a space is found, the variable is treated as
numeric, otherwise, the variable is treated as
alphanumeric. If the ﬁrst character is non-numeric, the
variable is treated as alphanumeric, and the entire
variable is scanned.
Example 1:
@LDV V1A13=’01 numeric value’.

@LDV V2A13=’01 numeric value1’.
@IF V1 NE V2 REL ;.
In this example, the system treats V1 and V2 as numeric
(I type), the system stops the scan when a space is
encountered, and the @IF returns an equal condition.
Example 2:
@LDV V1A13=’0a is not numeric value’.
@LDV V2A13=’0a is not numeric value1’.
@IF V1 NE V2 REL ;.
In this example, the system treats V1 and V2 as
alphanumeric (I type), and the @IF returns a not-equal
condition.
Example 3:
@LDV V1A26=’999E0 is not numeric value’.
@LDV V2A27=’999E0 is not numeric value1’.
@IF V1 NE V2 REL ;.
In this example, the system treats V1 and V2 as
numeric, and the @IF returns an equal condition.
Example 4:
@LDV V1A13=’I LOVE MAPPER’ .
@LDV V2A13=’I LOVE UNISYS’ .
@IF V1 NE V2 REL ; .

In this example, the system treats variables as
alphanumeric, and the system does not stop the scan
when a space is encountered. In this case, the @IF
returns a not-equal condition. Therefore, it is
recommended to use type H or type S variables when
doing string comparisons.
The IF statement treats the following characters as
zeros in type A variables when the type A variables are
treated as numeric:
. + In type H variables, characters must be identical to
satisfy a true condition.
2200 Þ Þ
The IF statement considers the contents of a type O
variable as a decimal value, not octal.
When you compare substrings of up to 18 characters,
the IF statement considers the characters in the
substring as type A variables.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
When you compare substrings, the IF statement
considers all characters in the substring as type A
variables.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

If Conditional Basic Examples
If USER$ equals newuser, the run branches to label 002;
otherwise, it branches to label 001:
@if user$ EQ newuser gto 002 ; gto 001 .
If <status> equals <done>, the run releases the screen
display; otherwise, the run continues execution with the
next line:
@if <status> EQ <done> rel ; .
If v1 equals A and if v2 equals B, the run branches to label
001; otherwise, it continues execution with the next line:
@if v1 EQ a if v2 EQ b gto 001 ; .
If v1 equals 2, the system executes the Load Variable (LDV)
statement (loads v3 with the value 4) and branches to label
001; if v1 does not equal 2, the run branches to label 002:
@if v1 EQ 2 ldv v3=4 gto 001 ; gto 002 .
If v1 equals 2, the system executes the LDV statement
(loads v3 with the value 4) and branches to label 002; if v1
does not equal 2, the run branches to label 002 without
loading any variable:
@if v1 EQ 2 ldv v3=4 ; gto 002 .
Note that in the previous example, the run goes to label 2
even if the condition of the IF statement is not met, because
the GTO statement is the next logical statement to execute.
If the value of <test1> is equal to <count>, the value of
<test1> is increased by 1; otherwise, <test1> is increased

by 2:
@if <test1> EQ <count> inc <test1> . ; inc,2
<test1> .

Logical OR Example
If <menu> equals either two or four, the run branches to
label 010; otherwise, the run continues execution with the
next line:
@if <menu> EQ 2,4 gto 010 ; .

Logical AND Example
If v1 is greater than 0 and less than 100, the run branches
to label 003; otherwise, it continues execution with the next
line:
@if v1 GT 0 & LT 100 gto 003 ; .

Computed IF/GTO Examples
If <total> equals 30, the run branches to label 001; if
<total> equals 40, the run branches to label 002; if <total>
equals either 50 or 60, the run branches to label 003;
otherwise, it continues execution with the next line:
@if <total> EQ 30,(001),40,(002),50,60,(003) ; .
If v2 equals 4, the run branches to the second line following
the current line; or, if v2 equals 5, the run goes to the end of
the run; otherwise, it continues execution with the next line:
@if v2 EQ 4,(lin +2),5,(end) ; .

Trailing Substring Examples
This example uses an unknown trailing substring. (The 0-3
speciﬁes the last three characters; the starting column
position is unknown.) If the last three characters of v1
contain the letters mon, the run branches to label 025:
@if v1(0-3) EQ mon,(025) ; .
This example uses a known trailing substring. (The 3-0
speciﬁes the known starting position of 3 for the remainder
of the ﬁeld.) If the characters beginning with character 3
through the end of the ﬁeld contain the letters fri, the run
branches to label 026:
@if v1(3-0) EQ fri,(026) ; .
This example displays the date format option. The d9 ﬁeld
corresponds to the date format number.
@LDV v1h8='11/01/95' .
@IF,d9 v1 LT date9$ rel ; .

@INC (Increment Variable)
Use the Increment Variable (INC) statement to increase the
value of a numeric variable.

Increment Variable Statement Format
@INC[,n] v[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

n

Number (ﬂoating point or
integer) by which to increase
the value of the variable. You
can specify the number, using
a literal, a variable, a
constant, or a reserved word.
Default = 1.

2200 Þ Þ

v

Name of the variable that
contains the value you want to
increase. The variable you
specify must contain only
numeric data and cannot be a
string (type S) variable.
If the variable contains any
nonnumeric data, the value of
the variable is not changed.

Ü Ü 2200

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

v

Name of the variable that
contains the value you want to
increase. The variable you
specify must contain only
numeric data and cannot be a
string (type S) variable.
If the variable contains any
nonnumeric data, the results
are undeﬁned.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Increment Variable Examples
This example increases the value of <counter> by 1:
@inc <counter> .
This example increases the value of <counter> by 10:
@inc,10 <counter> .
This example increases the value of <counter> by the value
of <currenttot>:
@inc,<currenttot> <counter> .

your current run.
For more information, see the Developer’s Guide.

@INP (Accept Input)
Use the Accept Input (INP) statement to temporarily
suspend a run, wait for input from a graphical control, form,
or DDE conversation, and specify where the run should
continue processing, depending on a selection made by the
user.

Note: Information in this topic regarding controls and
control handles also applies to DDE conversations and
conversation handles unless otherwise noted.
You must have one of the following combinations of software
installed and operational:
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client and Microsoft Windows Server.
Microsoft Windows and Business Information Server for
Linux and the UNIX Operating System or Business
Information Server for ClearPath OS 2200. See the
related Software Release Announcements for supported
levels.

Accept Input Statement Formats
Format 1 - Set Focus and Wait Format
@INP[,vfocus,,vinput vwh,(lab),...,vwh,(lab)] .
Format 2 - Single Data Input Format
@INP[,vwh,fxmt?] .
Format 3 - Multiple Data Input Format
@INP[,vwh,...,vwh] .
Format 4 - Use with the DDE Statement
@INP .

Field

Description

vfocus

(Format 1 only.) Variable that
contains a control handle. If
speciﬁed, this control
becomes the initial focus for
user input and response. If a
button is set in focus, it can be
selected with either the
mouse or from the Enter key
on the keyboard.

vinput

(Format 1 only.) Variable that
contains a window handle. If

speciﬁed, the system accepts
input only from controls within
this window. This subﬁeld does
not apply to DDE
conversations.

vwh

(Format 1 only.) Variable that
contains a control handle. If
the user selects this control,
run execution is transferred to
the label speciﬁed in the
following subﬁeld. Maximum =
80 handles.
If you select a control that is
not listed here, run execution
is transferred to the next line
(lin+1) in the report.

lab

(Format 1 only.) The label to
go to if the user selects the
preceding control handle.
Maximum = 80 entries.

vwh

(Format 2 only.) Variable that
contains a control handle. This
control becomes the focus of a
forced transmit speciﬁed in
the following subﬁeld. This
format is designed to transfer
data from an inactive control
to the INPUT$ reserved word.

fxmt?

(Format 2 only.) Transmit from
the control whose handle is
speciﬁed in the preceding
ﬁeld. Y or N. Default = N (no
action is performed). This
subﬁeld does not apply to DDE
conversations.

vwh

(Format 3 only.) Two or more
variables that contain control
handles. Maximum = 80
handles. The system forces a
transmit from every control
listed. This format is designed
to transfer data from a
number of inactive controls to
the INPUT$ reserved word
simultaneously.
For this format, only one
control handle can be that of a
multiple-item list or multiline
edit box. Specify that control
handle last.

Accept Input Outcome
Format 1 suspends the run until it receives input, after
which the run continues at the speciﬁed label. If a label is
not speciﬁed, control continues on the next line. The
statement places focus on the object speciﬁed in vfocus and
places the insertion point there. If the object is an edit box,
the statement highlights the text at the insertion point.
Formats 2 and 3 do not suspend the application. Control of
the application continues on the next line. These formats
are designed to transfer data from controls to the INPUT$
reserved word.
Format 4 suspends the run until it receives input, after
which the run continues at the next line.

Accept Input Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the INP statement:
If receiving input from a list box, and the input contains
tab characters, load the variable or variables with
INVR1$ before executing the INP statement.
Input from a multiline edit box is limited to 256
characters per line.
The total number of input characters cannot exceed
6500. A system message is displayed and the input is
truncated if the maximum is exceeded.
The INP statement overrides the eﬀect of the Command
Handler (CHD) statement. If you are using an INP
statement to receive input from a window, the system
ignores any registered command handler routine.
The INP statement should be used with a DDE
conversation only after setting up an advisory with the
DDE server program.
If a user presses Esc while the run is suspended on an
INP statement, the run aborts. Be sure to include an RAR
statement in your run to handle such an occurrence.
For more information, see @DDE (Dynamic Data Exchange
Interface) in this document.

Accept Input Reserved Words
You can use one of the following reserved words in the vwh
ﬁeld:
WND$

Contains the handle of the main
session window.

ACTWIN$ Contains the handle of the
active window.
ACTINP$

Contains the handle of the
control that last provided input.
If the control is not a window,
ACTWIN$ contains the handle
of this control’s parent window.

Accept Input Examples
This example uses the INP statement to put focus on the OK
button and speciﬁes input can only come from the
<win001> window. The application uses ACTINP$ to
determine which control handle the user selected and where
to continue the run. Also, the application uses INPUT$ to
determine if the user’s input was from the menu bar. When
a menu item is selected, the menu label is passed to the
application in INPUT$.
@0010 INP,<BTNOK>,,<WIN001> .
@. ------------ BTN input
@.
@ IF ACTINP$ eq <BTNOK>,(0100) ; . OK
@ IF ACTINP$ eq <BTNCAN>,(0199) ; . Cancel
@ IF ACTINP$ eq <BTNHLP>,(0195) ; . Help
@.
@. ------------ Menu inputs
@.
@ IF INPUT$ eq CANCEL,(0199) ; . Cancel
Application
@ IF INPUT$ eq HELP,(0195) ; . Help screen
@ IF INPUT$ eq ABOUT,(0190) ; . About screen
@ GTO 0010 .
@.
This example uses an INP statement that functions the
same as the previous INP example.
@0010 INP,<BTNOK>,,<WIN001> \
<BTNOK>,(0100),<BTNCAN>,(0199),<BTNHLP>,(0195) .
@ IF INPUT$ eq EXIT,(0199),HELP,(0195),ABOUT,
(0190) ;
@ GTO 0010 .

This example demonstrates the INP statement when
collecting data entered by the user. An item is selected from
a single-item list box <LST001>, and then saved in the
variable <LIST1>.
@.
@0100 INP,<LST001>,Y .
@ CHG INPUT$ <LIST1>S20 .
@.
This example demonstrates the INP statement when
collecting data entered by the user. Data is selected from a
multi-item list box <LST001>, and the selected items are
saved in the following variables: <LIST1>, <LIST2>,
<LIST3>, and <LIST4>.
@0105 INP,<LST001>,Y .
@ CHG INPUT$ <LIST1>S20,<LIST2>S20,<LIST3>S20,
<LIST4>S20 .
This example demonstrates the INP statement when
collecting data entered by the user. Data is entered or
selected in multiple controls, such as Edit boxes, Combo
boxes, or List boxes. Data is selected in the following control
handles: <EDT001>, <EDT002>, <CBX001>, <EDT003>,
and <CBX002>. Data is saved in the following variables:
<ITEM1>, <ITEM2>, <ITEM3>, <ITEM4>, and <ITEM5>.
@0110 INP,<EDT001>,<EDT002>,<CBX001>,<EDT003>,
<CBX002> .
@ CHG INPUT$ <ITEM1>S20,<ITEM2>S20,<ITEM3>S20,
<ITEM4>S20,<ITEM5>S20 .

Note: With this method, only one multi-item control can be
speciﬁed, and it must be the last control.

Insert Line and @LNI
Use the Insert Line function or LNI statement to copy lines
from one location in a report to another. Compare this
command with the Move Line command:
The Insert Line command copies the lines, retaining
them in their original location.
The Move Line command moves the lines, deleting them
from their original location.
The command copies the speciﬁed lines; it does not create a
result.
For the Insert Line manual function, the report must be on
display.
For the LNI statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LNI statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.

Insert Line In-Report-Line Format
>]xIl[,q|-LL]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

x

Number of times to insert the
line or lines.

I

Insert Line call.

l

Line number of the ﬁrst line to
insert.

q

Number of lines to insert.
Default = 1.

LL

Line number of the last line to
insert. Default = 1.

Insert Line Statement Format
@LNI,c,d,r,lb4,[x],l[,q] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report where lines are to be
copied. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

lb4

Line number preceding the
location where copied lines are
inserted.

x

Number of times to copy and
insert the lines. Default = 1.

l

Number of the ﬁrst line to
insert.

q

Number of lines to insert,
beginning with the line
speciﬁed in the l ﬁeld. Default
= 1.

Insert Line Procedure
To insert lines

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to be copied.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
4. In the Yank line ﬁeld, type the number of lines to copy
and transmit. Default = 1.

5. Move the cursor to the line above the line where the
copied lines are to be inserted.

6. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
7. Move the cursor to the Put line ﬁeld and transmit.
You can repeat steps 5 through 7 to copy lines to various
locations until you release the buﬀer or use it for another
yank operation.
Or

1. Place the cursor on the line above the line where the
copied lines are to be inserted.

2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Insert Line in-report-line format.

Insert Line Example
This example copies two lines, 8 and 9, one time and inserts
them after line 6. Position the cursor on line 6, erase to the
end of the line, and enter the following: >]1i8,2
Equivalent Statement
@lni,0,b,2,6,1,8,2 .

ISOR (Iterative Sort)
Use the Iterative Sort (ISOR) run to generate Sort function
masks that you can save, alter, and use again.
With the ISOR run, you can use any functionality available
with the Sort command, but with greater ease. For example,
you can do the following tasks:
Save parameters and options in the function mask and
use them again.
Recall a previously saved function mask instead of
retyping options and parameters into a new one.
Translate function masks into their equivalent statement
formats.
The report you intend to process must reside in the current
cabinet.

Iterative Sort Control Line Format
ISOR[*] [report,f,maskname,maskrep]

Field

Description

*

An asterisk to specify that
ﬁeld names are to be used in
place of column character
positions when a statement
is produced.

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to
Process.

f

Report format in which to
process data (enables you to
process data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 if you do not
already have those columns
on display).

function mask, a new
function mask, or blank. (If
you leave this ﬁeld blank,
you name the new function

mask when you save it.) The
name can be 1 to 12
characters; it must begin
with a letter and contain no
spaces.

this subﬁeld when the
function masks are in a
report other than the report
you are processing. Note that
if you leave the maskname
subﬁeld blank, you cannot
specify a report in the
maskrep subﬁeld.
