IU and @IDU (Index User)
Use the Index User (IU) function or IDU statement to create
a listing of a speciﬁed number of lines from selected reports
in a drawer.
You can choose any combination of these parameters:
Reports created or updated by a speciﬁc user or by any
user.
Reports with speciﬁc last update start dates, end dates,
or both.
Range of reports.

Index User Control Line Format
IU [qd]

Field

Description

q

Number of lines to display
from each report. Default =
heading lines (up to eight)
from each report.

d

Drawer to index. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

Index User Statement Format
@IDU,c,d[,q,user,sdate,endate,srpt,endrpt
vrpts,vlines,vrptsd,vhirptd] .

Field

Description

c,d

Drawer to index. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

q

Number of lines to display
from each report. Default =
heading (up to eight).

user

User-id to index or all to index
all users. Default = current
user-id.

sdate

First update date to be
included in the date range
(DDMMMYY format). Default =
all. This is either the date of
the most recent update to the
report or the creation date if
there are no updates. See also
Index User Guidelines for
information on user language.

Format DDMMMYYYY is also
valid.
Spaces are optional (for
example, DDMMMYY or DD
MMM YY is allowed).

endate

Last update date to be
included in the date range
(DDMMMYY format). Default =
current date. This is either the
date of the most recent
update to the report, or the
creation date if there are no
updates. See also Index User
Guidelines for information on
user language.
When specifying NOV for
November, enclose the date in
apostrophes so that the run
processor does not interpret
the V as a numbered variable
name.
Format DDMMMYYYY is also
valid.
Spaces are optional (for
example, DDMMMYY or DD
MMM YY is allowed).

srpt

First report number in a report
range. Default = 1.

endrpt

Last report number in a report
range. Default = highest
report number.

vrpts

Variable to capture the
number of reports found.

vlines

Variable to capture the total
number of lines in the reports
found.

vrptsd

Variable to capture the total
number of reports in the
drawer.

vhirptd

Variable to capture the highest
report number in the drawer.

Index User Outcome
Executing the Index User command causes the following
actions to occur:
The command creates a result containing a listing of the
speciﬁed reports.
A result is displayed containing the following lines:
The date line for the result.
Lines containing the number of reports that meet
the selection criteria and the user-id requested for
the index.
Lines containing headings that identify each ﬁeld,
such as USER and UPDATE DATE, and a heading
divider line.
The speciﬁed number of lines from each report,
starting with the date line in each report. The right
side of each date line contains the number of lines
in the corresponding report.
To help separate the information for each report, the
result also includes a blank line with a period in
column 1 between the information for each report.
If you are indexing reports that are fewer than 80
characters wide, the command adds trailer lines that
contain the number of lines from each report.

Note: In the case of a limited-access report, such as a readprotected or encoded report, only the date line and the title

line are displayed. The contents of the report are not
displayed on the screen.

@IDU Statement Outcome
In addition to creating a -0 result, the command loads
variables with information about the reports and the drawer,
as described under the statement format.

Index User Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Index User
command:
When determining the number of lines to include from
each report, remember that the control line is not
considered part of a report.
To see only the heading lines from each report, do not
specify a quantity (q).
To see all of the lines in each report, for quantity (q),
enter a number larger than the size of any report.

Note: There is no maximum number for the q ﬁeld;
however, since a request to index many lines from each
report can aﬀect system performance, use caution when
indexing large drawers.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
For the manual Index User function, always enter dates
in your language (the "user" language), not the
language of the report or drawer being processed.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
For the IDU statement, always enter dates in the
language of the report or drawer being processed.

Index User Examples
This example indexes cabinet 0, drawer A, and creates a
result containing the ﬁrst three lines from all reports
updated by the user-id newuser: @idu,0,a,3,newuser .

0,a

Index cabinet 0, drawer A.

3

Include three lines of data
from each indexed report.

newuser

Index reports created or last
updated by user-id newuser.

This example creates a listing of a range of reports that
were last updated by the user newuser:
@idu,0,b,7,newuser,01jan93,'30nov93',5,10
<numreports>i3,\ <numlines>i9,<repsdrwr>i4,
<hireport>i4 .

0,b

Index reports in cabinet
0, drawer B.

7

Include seven lines of
data from each indexed
report.

newuser

Include reports that
were last updated by
newuser.

01jan96

Index reports beginning
on January 1, 1996.

'30nov96'

Index reports ending
on November 30, 1996.
The date is enclosed in
apostrophes because
of the V in nov. This
distinguishes it from a
variable name (such as
v1, v2, and so on).

5,10

Start the index at
report 5 and stop at
report 10.

<numreports>i3 Load <numreports>
with the number of
reports found.

<numlines>i9

Load <numlines> with
the number of lines
found.

<repsdrwr>i4

Load <repsdrwr> with

the number of reports
in drawer B.

<hireport>i4

Load <hireport> with
the highest report
number created.

@JUV (Justify Variable)
Use the Justify Variable (JUV) statement to reformat the
contents of numeric variables.
2200 Þ Þ
Numeric variables can be up to 18 characters in length.
Ü Ü 2200
Normally, you reformat the contents of a variable before
displaying the value on the screen or writing it to a report.
To reformat the contents of variables that contain
nonnumeric data, you can use the options of the LDV
statement.

Justify Variable Statement Format
@JUV,o v[,v,...v] .

Field

Description

o

Option that determines how
the contents of the variable
are reformatted. Use only one
option. See Justify Variable
Options.

v

Variable to reformat. If the
variable you specify does not
contain numeric data, the
system does not change the
variable.

Justify Variable Options
Option

Description

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C

Inserts commas in the integer
portion of the variable every
third digit, eliminates leading
zeros, right-justiﬁes the
contents, and blank ﬁlls all
remaining characters to the
left of the number.
If inserting commas would
expand the variable beyond
the deﬁned size of the
variable, the variable remains
unchanged.
You must remove commas
inserted by the C option before
using the variable with the
CHG or LDV statements. See
the D option.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C

Inserts commas in the integer
portion of the variable every
third digit eliminates leading
zeros, right-justiﬁes the
contents, and blank ﬁlls all
remaining characters to the
left of the number.
If inserting commas would
expand the variable beyond
the deﬁned size of the
variable, the variable remains
unchanged.

Ü Ü 2200

D

Deletes all commas and
leading zeros, and rightjustiﬁes the contents within
the variable.

L

Eliminates leading and trailing
zeros, left-justiﬁes the
contents, and blank ﬁlls all
remaining characters to the
right of the number.

R

Eliminates leading and
insigniﬁcant trailing zeros,
right-justiﬁes the contents, and
blank ﬁlls all remaining

characters to the left of the
number.

X

Eliminates leading zeros, leftjustiﬁes the contents, and
zero-ﬁlls all remaining
characters to the right of the
number.

Z

Eliminates insigniﬁcant trailing
zeros, right-justiﬁes the
contents, and zero-ﬁlls all
remaining characters to the
left of the number.

2200 Þ Þ

CE

Inserts periods in the integer
portion of the variable every
third digit eliminates leading
zeros, right-justiﬁes the
contents, and blank ﬁlls all
remaining characters to the
left of the number. Changes a
decimal point to a comma in
European numeric notation.
If inserting periods would
expand the variable beyond
the deﬁned size of the

variable, the variable remains
unchanged.
You must change the number
back to a U.S. numeric
notation before using it in any
calculations. See the E option.

DE

Deletes all periods and leading
zeros, and right-justiﬁes the
contents within the variable.
Changes a comma decimal
point to a period in European
numeric notation.

E

Switches between U.S. and
European numeric notation.
(Periods are changed to
commas, and commas are
changed to periods.)

Ü Ü 2200

Justify Variable Examples
This example inserts commas in <number> (# = space):
@ldv <number>f12.3=5323.750 .
@juv,c <number> . <number> = ###5,323.750
This example inserts commas in <number> before
displaying the value, then uses the D option to delete the
commas (# = space):
@ldv <number>f10.3=5323.750 .
@juv,c <number> . <number> = #5,323.750
.
. (processing that displays <number>).
@juv,d <number> . <number> = ##5323.750
These examples justify the numeric value in <number>
(5323.750) in several diﬀerent ways, as shown (# = space):
@juv,l <number> . <number> = 5323.75###
@juv,r <number> . <number> = ###5323.75
@juv,x <number> . <number> = 5323.75000
@juv,z <number> . <number> = 0005323.75

@KEY (Function Key Input)
Use the Function Key Input (KEY) statement to obtain a
number via FKEY$ that indicates the function key the run
user pressed to move on from a noninterim DSP, DSM, OUT,
or SC display. Use a KEY statement before DSP, DSM, OUT,
or SC statements.

Note: A noninterim display is a screen displayed by a run
that remains on display until the run user transmits or
resumes.
Use the KEY statement only with output displays that do not
use a function key bar, since the functions on the function
key bar override the eﬀect of the KEY statement. For
information on using function key bars, see @SC (Screen
Control) and @FKY (Function Key) in this document.
When the run user presses a function key after a noninterim
display, the run continues executing at the statement
following the DSP, DSM, OUT, or SC statement.

Function Key Input Statement Format
@KEY .

FKEY$ Reserved Word
FKEY$ contains a number indicating the function key
pressed (contains 0 if the run user pressed Transmit with
the cursor positioned below the control line).

Function Key Input Example
This example requests function key input with the KEY
statement and displays a report using the OUT statement:
@key .
@out,-0,4,1 .
Following these statements, the run can test FKEY$ for its
contents and proceed accordingly.

KILL and @KLL (Kill)
Use the Kill command or KLL statement to terminate active
tasks (runs or functions).
2200 Þ Þ
The command also signs you oﬀ the system and terminates
the station session.
Ü Ü 2200
You may only terminate your own tasks.
The KILL command will not terminate an active task, station,
or user if that activity is held due to a suspended resource.
For example, if auditing is enabled, an activity (task, station,
or user) would be held when an audit trail ﬁle becomes full
(and suspends as a result). The KILL command will not
identify the activity being held as one that can be
terminated until the cause of the suspension is ﬁxed. In this
case, the administrator could start a PACK, add a ﬁle to the
audit trail, or disable auditing to resume the resource and
hence, the activity (task, station, or user). Once the
resource resumes, the KILL command must be executed
again to terminate the task, station, or user.

Kill Control Line Format
2200 Þ Þ
KILL {run|,sn|run,sn}
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
KILL {run|,sn|run,sn}[,terminate?]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Field

Description

run

Name of the run to terminate.

sn

Station number of the station
where the run is executing.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

terminate? Specify Y to terminate the
selected sessions. Specify
the default (N) to terminate
the active task.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@KLL Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@KLL,{run|,sn|run,sn}[,terminate?] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@KLL,{run|,sn|run,sn}[,,,terminate?,lab] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

run

Name of the run to
terminate.

sn

Station number of the station
where the run is executing.

terminate? Specify Y to terminate the
selected sessions. Specify
the default (N) to terminate
the active task.

2200 Þ Þ

lab

Label to jump to if an error
occurs.

If stations are stopped (or killed), the run continues at the
next statement with STAT1$=0 and STAT2$ set to the
number of stations that were aﬀected. If no stations are
found, the run jumps to label with STAT1$=1. If any other
errors are encountered, the run jumps to the error label with
STAT1$=2 and STAT2$ that contain the system error
message value which can be retrieved with the LSM
statement. If no error label is speciﬁed, the run errors.
Ü Ü 2200

Kill Guidelines
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the System command to locate the run name and
station number of the run you want to terminate.
A queued background run can be terminated only by the
administrator.
If any of the following unexpected conditions exist in a
target session, KILL has no eﬀect:
the underlying process has exited
a hung session caused by an internal software or
hardware defect that has rendered the recovery
information useless (expected to be a very small
percentage of hung sessions)
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Run Status (RS) command to locate the run name
and station number of the run you want to terminate.
Ü Ü 2200

Kill Procedures
To Terminate a Run Using the Run Name
Enter the run name alone to terminate all background runs
with that name that you started on any station.
To Terminate a Run Using the Station Number
Enter the station number alone to terminate the background
run that you started at that station.
To Terminate a Run Using Both
Enter both the run name and station number to terminate a
speciﬁc run that you started at the speciﬁed station.

L (Line Control)
Use the Line Control (L) command to restore the control line
when it is erased or overwritten (for example, when a
system message is displayed).
The command redisplays the control line, enabling you to
continue processing the report with any command.

Line Control Control Line Format
L

Line Control Procedure
To restore the control line

1. Move the cursor to the home position.
2. Type L and transmit.

LANG and @LNG (Language)
Use the Language (LANG) function or LNG statement to
change the text of system messages to another language.
For example, you can have the system display messages in
French, if French is available on your system.
Future system messages are displayed in the assigned
language until you (or the run user, in the case of the LNG
statement) either sign oﬀ or choose another language.

Language Control Line Format and Statement
Format
LANG [n]
@LNG,n .

Field

Description

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

n

Language number. Since valid
languages vary among sites,
refer to the input screen for a
list of languages speciﬁc to
your site.
1 = primary language at your
site.
Valid entries = 1 - 8.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

n

Language number. Since valid
languages vary among sites,
refer to the input screen for a

list of languages speciﬁc to
your site.
0 = primary language at your
site.
Valid entries = 0 - 7.

Ü Ü 2200

@LCH (Locate and Change)
See CHG and @LCH (Locate and Change) for more
information.

@LCV (Locate and Change Variable)
Use the Locate and Change Variable (LCV) statement to ﬁnd
and, optionally, replace data within a variable. With the LCV
statement, you can locate a string, change a string, count
the number of occurrences, or compare two variables
literally.
An LCV statement is to variables what a Locate (LOC) or
Locate and Change (LCH) statement is to data in reports.
An LCV statement is more eﬃcient than a comparable
IF/GTO sequence.

Locate and Change Variable Statement Format
@LCV[,lab] o v tgtstr[/replstr vpos,voccs] .

Field

Description

lab

Label at which to continue
execution when no ﬁnds are
made or when n occurrences
are not found (see the Bn
option). To continue execution
at the label when a ﬁnd is
made, use the N option. (To
simplify your run logic, if you
want the run to continue on
the next line, use LIN1 in this
subﬁeld instead of a label
number.)

o

Option that determines how to
search the variable for data
(see Locate and Change
Variable Options).

v

Variable to search. You can
specify any type of variable,
and you can specify a
substring or an array member.

tgtstr

The data you want to locate
within the variable (called the
target string). You can specify
the data, using a variable,
literal, constant, or reserved
word.
Within the target string, the
transparent character is a
wild-card character (that is,
the transparent character
matches any character in that
position).
The default transparent
character is the space.
Therefore, any spaces in the
data are treated as wild-card
characters. You can use the Tx
option to change the default
transparent character when
you need to locate spaces.

replstr

The data (from 0 to the
maximum line length) to
replace the target string. If
you do not use the Bn option,
the statement replaces all
occurrences of the target
string with the replacement
string.
To completely remove the
target string, enter nothing for

the replacement string (for
example, target/).

vpos

Variable to capture the
character position within the
variable of the ﬁrst occurrence
of the target string.
With the Bn option, vpos
captures the character
position of the nth occurrence
of the target string within the
variable.

voccs

Variable to capture the
number of occurrences of the
target string within the
variable. This is useful when
doing a change or locate with
the B option.

Locate and Change Variable Options
Option

Description

Bn

Locates only the nth
occurrence of the target string
(variable vpos captures the
character position within the
variable of the nth
occurrence) or locate and
change n occurrences (vpos
contains the character
position of the ﬁrst changed
occurrence).

Bn-x

Locates and changes from n
through x occurrences of the
target string, where n is the
ﬁrst occurrence to process
and x is the number of
subsequent occurrences from
n to process. Variable vpos
captures the character
position of the ﬁrst changed
occurrence.

C

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

Lx

Locates a speciﬁc line type
where x is the line type. The
ﬁrst character of the variable
must match the line type
designator or the statement
stops processing the variable.

M

Uses the transparent
character (see the Tx option)
as a mask to inhibit
replacements. Characters in
the variable that correspond
to the positions of the
transparent characters in the
target string are not replaced
with those in the replacement
string.

N

Goes to the speciﬁed label
when a ﬁnd is made rather
than when no ﬁnds are made.

Tx

Speciﬁes the character to use
as the transparent character.
The transparent character is a
wild-card character used to
match any character in the
corresponding position. You
can also use the transparent
character as a mask to inhibit
replacements (see the M

option). Default = space
character.

Locating Second Occurrence of an Item
Example
The contents of v1 for this example are as follows:
v1=doghorsecowpigbirdcatcatcatcatmouse
| |
column 15 column 34
| locate columns |
This example locates the second occurrence of the word cat
in v1 starting at column 15 for 20 characters: @lcv,001 b2
v1(15-20) cat v2i6 .

001

Goes to label 1 if fewer than
two occurrences of the word
cat are found.

b2

Bails out on the second
occurrence of the word cat.

v1(1520)

Scans v1, starting in column
15, for 20 characters.

cat

Locates target string cat.

v2i6

Loads v2 with the character
position where the second

occurrence of the word cat
begins.

After the statement executes, v2 contains 22 (the column in
v1 where the second occurrence of the word cat was found).

Counting Occurrences of an Item Example
The contents of v1 for this example are as follows:
CATdogCATDOGCATDogCATDOGCATdog
This example counts the number of times the word dog
occurs in v1: @lcv b99 v1 dog ,v3i6 .

b99

Bails out on the 99th
occurrence of the word dog.

v1

Scans v1 (the entire variable).

dog

Locates target string dog.

v3i6

Loads v3 with the number of
occurrences of the word dog.

In this example, the bailout count was set to a value higher
than the number of occurrences of the word dog because
we did not want to bail out. V3 continues to count each
occurrence of the word dog. After the statement executes,
v3 contains 5.

Changing Character String Example
The contents of v1 for this example are as follows:
*CATDOGCATDOGCATDOGCATDOGCATDOG
This example changes the second, third, and fourth
occurrences of the word DOG to cat in v1: @lcv l*b2-3 v1
DOG/cat v2i6 .

l*

Makes the change only if the
ﬁrst character of v1 is an
asterisk (the line type
indicator).

b2-3

Changes target string, starting
at the second occurrence, for
three occurrences.

v1

Scans v1.

DOG

Locates target string DOG.

cat

Changes target string DOG to
cat.

v2i6

Loads v2 with the character
position of the ﬁrst occurrence

of the target string changed.

Since the ﬁrst character of v1 matches the character
speciﬁed in the L option, the change is made. V2 equals 11
and v1 contains: *CATDOGCATcatCATcatCATcatCATDOG
1235
v2=11

Comparing Strings Example
The contents of <string1> for this example are as follows:
abc123
The contents of <string2> are as follows:
abc+++
This example compares <string1> to <string2>:
@lcv,001 '' <string1> <string2> .

001

Goes to label 1 if
<string1> is not equal to
<string2>.

''

Uses no options.

<string1>
<string2>

Compares <string1> with
<string2>.

Since <string1> is not equal to <string2>, the run goes to
label 1. Remember, if you use the N option, the run does not
go to the label unless the two variables are equal.

Masking Transparent Characters Example
The contents of v1 for this example are as follows:
blackbox1*blackcan1*blackbag1*blackcup1
This example uses the M option to locate each occurrence of
the string black followed by any three characters, followed
by the number 1. Each time the locate string is found, the
characters black are changed to green, the next three
characters remain unchanged, and the last character, 1, is
changed to 2.
@lcv m v1 'black 1'/'green 2' v2i6,v3i6 .

m

Speciﬁes the mask option.
Transparent characters in the
replacement string are not
inserted into the variable
being changed.

v1

Scans v1.

'black 1'/ Speciﬁes the target string and
change string delimiter (/).

'green 2'

Speciﬁes the replacement
string (characters with which
to replace each occurrence of
the target string).

v2i6

Loads v2 with the column of
the ﬁrst occurrence changed.

v3i6

Loads v3 with the number of
occurrences changes.

V1 now contains:
greenbox2*greencan2*greenbag2*greencup2

Using an Unknown Trailing Substring Example
The contents of v1 for this example are as follows:
feature010101
This example uses an unknown trailing substring and bails
out after the second ﬁnd: @lcv,001 b2 v1(0-6) 01 v2i3 .

001

Goes to label 1 if no ﬁnds are
made.

b2

Bails out after the second
occurrence.

v1(0-6)

Scans the last six characters of
v1 (the 0-6 speciﬁes the last
six characters-the starting
character position is
unknown).

01

Locates the target string 01.

v2i3

Loads v2 with the character
position where the second
occurrence of 01 begins in the
speciﬁed substring. (In this
example, v2 contains 10

because the second 01 begins
in the tenth column of v1.)

Using Known Trailing Substring Example
The contents of <money> for this example are as follows:
$$$dollars
This example uses a known trailing substring to scan
column 4 through the end of the ﬁeld and changes the word
dollars to the word yen in all occurrences: @lcv,001 ''
<money>(4-0) dollars/yen .

001

Goes to label 1 if no ﬁnds are
made.

''

Uses no options.

<money>
(4-0)

Scans <money> beginning
with column 4 through the
remaining characters (the 40 speciﬁes the known
starting position of 4 through
the end of the ﬁeld).

dollars/yen Changes the target string
dollars to yen.

@LDA (Load Variable Array)
Use the Load Variable Array (LDA) statement to deﬁne a
variable array and put data into the array. Each variable in
an array is referred to as a member of the array.
2200 Þ Þ
The run control report must be in full character set (FCS or
FCSU).
Ü Ü 2200

Load Variable Array Statement Formats
Format 1
@LDA[,o] v[n][=vld,...,vld] .
Format 2
@LDA[,o] v[n][,v[n],...,v[n]] .

Field

Description

o

Option that determines how
data is put into the variables
in the array. See Load Variable
Array Options.

v

Variable array in which you
want to put data. To deﬁne or
redeﬁne the variable, include
the variable type and size.

[n]

Number of members in the
array. The brackets are
required.

vld

Data you want to put in each
member of the array. You can
specify data using a literal,

constant, variable, reserved
word, or any combination of
these.
The ﬁrst data you specify is
loaded into the ﬁrst member
of the array, the second data
you specify is loaded into the
second member of the array,
and so on.

Load Variable Array Options
Option

Description

C

Centers data within each
variable.

L

Left-justiﬁes the data within
each variable.

O

Converts all uppercase
alphabetic characters to
lowercase characters.

P

Packs data into each variable
so that the variable contains
only signiﬁcant characters.
If you pack data that does not
contain any signiﬁcant
characters, the variable into
which you packed the data
becomes a variable of 0
length. Note that if you try to
use a variable of 0 length in a
statement, the results are
unpredictable.

To avoid creating a variable of
0 length, use the DEF
statement to determine the
packed size of a variable
before you pack it.

R

Right-justiﬁes the data within
each variable.

U

Converts all lowercase
alphabetic characters to
uppercase characters.

2200 Þ Þ

W

Loads variables with the
values of reserved words. (The
reserved word values are leftjustiﬁed. Make sure the
variables are large enough to
hold the values of the reserved
words.)

Ü Ü 2200

Z

Right-justiﬁes and zero-ﬁlls
each variable after the data is
loaded.

Load Variable Array Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the LDA statement:
Once you deﬁne an array (that is, speciﬁed the size,
type, and number of members), you cannot redeﬁne
any member of the array.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Once you deﬁne a variable, even one that is not a
variable array, you cannot redeﬁne it as an array.
Be aware that array type variables are padded with
control characters that require storage space and count
toward the variable character limit.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Array members consume string variable space,
regardless of the variable type.
Ü Ü 2200

Referring to a Member of an Array
You can refer to a speciﬁc member of an array, using this
format:

name[n]
where name is the name of the array and n is the position of
the member within the array. For example, to refer to the
third member of <bigarray>, you would use the following:
<bigarray>[3]
Notice that the brackets ([ ]) are a required part of the
format.

Changing the Value of Array Members
Each member of an array is a variable, and you can change
the value of an array member using the same statements
you use to change the value of a variable. However, you
must refer to the member using the proper format for
referring to array members.
For example, to change the value of the ﬁfth member of
<bigarray> to 65, you could use either of these statements:
@ldv <bigarray>[5]=65 .
@chg <bigarray>[5] 13 * 5 .

Array Members Count as Variables
The system counts each variable in the array as one
variable of the total number allowed on the system.
When you pass a variable array to a Call Subroutine (CALL)
statement without specifying the array size ([n]), you can
access all members of the array, but they count as only one
of the total number allowed to be passed. For example, this
statement passes the entire array <bigstring> to the
subroutine at label 005:
@call 005(<bigstring>) .

Passing an Array Member
You can pass individual array members to a called
subroutine as separate variables. To do so, include the
names of the members you want to pass in the CALL
statement. For example, this statement passes only the ﬁfth
member of <bigstring> to the subroutine at label 005:
@call 005(<bigstring>[5]) .

Load Variable Array Examples
This example deﬁnes an array of 10 string variables, each of
which is 5 characters in length:
@lda <bigstring>s5[10] .
This example loads the ﬁrst three members of <bigstring>
with the value of <name>, <address>, and <phone>:
@lda <bigstring>=<name>,<address>,<phone> .
This example initializes an array of variables called <qty>
as an integer array containing ﬁve array members, and
loads information into each member of the array:
@lda,r <qty>i6[5]=1,12,123,1234,12345 .
The members <qty>[1] through <qty>[5] contain the
following information:
<qty>[1] = 1
<qty>[2] = 12
<qty>[3] = 123
<qty>[4] = 1234
<qty>[5] = 12345

@LDV (Load Variable)
Use the Load Variable (LDV) statement to put data into a
new or existing variable or to reformat the contents of an
existing variable.
Use format 1 to put data into a new or existing variable; use
format 2 to reformat the contents of an existing variable.
For additional reformatting options that pertain only to
numeric data, see @JUV (Justify Variable).

Load Variable Statement Formats
Format 1
@LDV[,o] v=vld[,v=vld,...,v=vld] .
Format 2
@LDV,o v[,v,...,v] .
Format 3
@LDV,Q
rv=iv,n[(delim),rv=iv,n(delim),...,rv=iv,n(delim)
] .

Field

Description

o

Option that determines how
data is put into the variable or
variables. See Load Variable
Options.

v

Variable into which you want
to put data. To deﬁne or
redeﬁne the variable, include
the variable type and size.

vld

Data you want to put into the
variable. You can specify the
data using a literal, constant,

variable, reserved word, or
any combination of these.

Q

Q Option. See Loading
Variables Based on Content.

rv

Receiving variable.

iv

Issuing variable.

n

Number of delimiters in iv to
skip before loading data. This
subﬁeld must be a literal
value.

(delim)

Character to use as a
delimiter. Default = tab
character. The parentheses
are required if using this
subﬁeld.

Load Variable Options
Option

Description

C

Centers data within the
variable.

2200 Þ Þ

H

Returns the status of the
remote run link. Use the format
LDV,H v=rms, where v is the
variable to capture the result
(0 = oﬄine; 1 = online) and
rms is the remote site number.

Note: The H option only
indicates whether the remote
site is conﬁgured.

Ü Ü 2200

L

Left-justiﬁes the data within
the variable.

O

Converts all uppercase

alphabetic characters to
lowercase characters.

P

Packs the data in a variable so
that the variable contains only
signiﬁcant characters. Use this
option to remove unnecessary
leading and trailing spaces.
If you pack a variable that
does not contain any
signiﬁcant characters, the
variable will contain no
characters at all and other
statements may not be able to
use it. To avoid this condition,
use the DEF statement before
packing a variable to
determine its packed size.

Q

Loads delimited data from a
variable or reserved word into
another variable. See Loading
Variables Based on Content.

R

Right-justiﬁes the data within
the variable.

U

Converts all lowercase
alphabetic characters to
uppercase characters.

2200 Þ Þ

W

Loads the variable with the
value of the reserved word.
(The reserved word value is
left-justiﬁed. Make sure the
variable is large enough to
hold the value of the reserved
word.)

Ü Ü 2200

Z

Right-justiﬁes and zero-ﬁlls
each variable after the data is
loaded.

Load Variable Guidelines
Use an LDV statement to perform these tasks:
Initialize variables.
Load variables from other variables.
Load variables already initialized.
To load a substring of a variable that is already
initialized, use the format position characters.
(Parentheses are required.)
To load strings containing spaces or commas, enclose
them in apostrophes.
Use an LDV statement to load literal data, substrings,
and reserved words.
Load a variable with its own contents.
When you load a variable with its own contents, you
need only specify the receiving variable. This is
particularly useful for packing variables. You can use, for
example, @ldv,p <var1>,<var2> rather than @ldv,p
<var1>=<var1>, <var2>=<var2>. Loading variables
in this manner is also useful when centering, left
justifying, or right justifying variables, and initializing
space ﬁlled variables without justifying variables, and
initializing space-ﬁlled variables without setting the
characters equal to spaces.
Use options with LDV statements to load data in a
speciﬁc way, such as centered (C option).

Whenever an LDV statement encounters a space or
comma, it stops loading the variable with data. To place
a space or comma in a variable without placing either
character in a variable beforehand, enclose spaces and
commas in apostrophes.
Use a Change Variable (CHG) statement to do simple
arithmetic calculations on the contents of variables and
to capture user input.

Loading Multiple Variables
In the following example, the ﬁrst LDV statement loads v1
through v4; the second LDV statement then loads these four
variables into v5:
@ldv <str1>a5=This,<str2>a3=is,<str3>a3=an,
<str4>a8=example .
@ldv <string>s40=<str1><str2><str3><str4> .
<string> now contains the words This is an example.

Packing Variables
With the P option, you can delete leading or trailing spaces
from a variable. This is called packing a variable. Do not,
however, pack a variable to contain no characters at all. If
you do, other statements trying to use the variable may fail.
Once you pack a variable to contain fewer than its original
number of characters, you must reinitialize the variable to
make it larger. If you try to place more than the original
number of characters in a variable, you lose the extra
characters.

Loading Variables Based on Content
The Q option enables you to load variables based on tab
characters (or a speciﬁed delimiter) contained in the
variable. The receiving variable is loaded with the data from
the speciﬁed tab character (or delimiter) to the next tab
character in the variable. Use the Q option when processing
variables initialized using INVR1$ or INSTR$.

Load Variables Examples
This example centers the contents of <name> in
<heading1>:
@ldv,c <heading1>=<name> .
This example centers the data in <heading2>:
@ldv,c <heading2> .
This example packs the contents of <answer> into
<panswer>:
@ldv,p <panswer>=<answer> .
This example loads the current date into the last nine
characters of <dateline>:
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@ldv <dateline>(0-9)=date2$ .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@ldv,w <dateline>(0-9)=date2$ .
Ü Ü 2200
This example loads <cab> with the cabinet number,
<drawer> with the numeric drawer number, and <report>
with the report number of the last report or result processed
or on display:

@ldv,w <cab>i4=cab$,<drawer>i6=drw$,
<report>i4=rpt$.
If NEWUSER is on station 12, v4 contains abcNEW12xyz:
@ldv,pw v4s20='abc'user$(1-3)stnum$xyz .
This example uses an unknown trailing substring to load v2
with the minutes and seconds (MM:SS) substring from v1
(the 0-5 speciﬁes the last ﬁve characters, but the starting
character position is unknown):
@ldv,w v1h8=time$ . v1 now contains hh:mm:ss
@ldv v2i5=v1(0-5) . v2 now contains mm:ss
This example uses a known trailing substring to load v2 with
the seconds (SS) substring from v1 (from the previous
example, the 7-0 speciﬁes the substring beginning with the
seventh character through the end v1):
@ldv v2i2=v1(7-0) . v2 now contains ss
This example uses the LDV Q option with a protected
screen.
@1:brk.
Field 1 , Field 2 ,
@brk.
@chg invr1$ v1s80,v2s80,v3s80 .
@out,-0,2,23,1,1,y,,,p .
@ldv,q <field1>h6=v1,0,<field2>h6=v1,1 .
<field1>/
<field2>/

Legacy Run Debugger
The legacy run debugger can only run on a user
workstation. For better debugging performance, you should
use the powerful capabilities of the Developer Workshop to
perform your script debugging. For complete information on
using this tool, see the Business Information Server
Developer Workshop Help.
With the legacy debugger, you can step through your script
to do the following:
Display the contents of a variable or reserved word
Display reports and results
Display the run control report and make corrections
Pause at a speciﬁed variable, statement, or label
Dynamically execute statements, for example, to
change the contents of a variable
Change the value of a variable
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You must have write access to the drawer of the run control
report to debug scripts; otherwise the system ignores the
RDB request. The script must not be registered in the
Master Script Registration report if you want to execute RDB
manually. However, you can use the RDB statement in the
script.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

2200 Þ Þ
You must be registered as an application designer and you
must be the one who last updated the run control report;
otherwise the system ignores the RDB request.
Ü Ü 2200

Legacy Run Debug Outcome
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Starting the legacy debugger causes the following actions to
occur:
When you start the RDB utility, line 3 of your run control
report is highlighted as the current command line. Your
script is now in RDB mode.
When the script encounters the RDB statement, the
script is put into RDB mode at the line of the RDB
statement.
When the script encounters an error, it stops and
displays a system message but leaves you in RDB
mode. All variables and results are available for you to
examine.
When the script encounters a statement that displays
output, such as an Output (OUT) or Display Report (DSP)
statement, the RDB utility repaints the previous output
screen, then displays the output of the current
statement.
When the script encounters an Execute a Script (RUN)
statement, the RDB utility terminates. To continue the
RDB utility, the script started through the RUN
statement must contain an RDB statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Legacy Run Debug Outcome
2200 Þ Þ
Starting the legacy debugger causes the following actions to
occur:
When you start the RDB utility, it displays the RDB
prompt followed by a line containing the name and
location of the script. Below this information, line 3 of
your run control report appears (up to 70 characters of
the line on an 80-character screen; up to 122 characters
on a 132-character screen).

Note: If you request the RDB utility, using the RDB
statement, the utility displays the ﬁrst statement
following the RDB statement.
Your script is now under control of the RDB utility. The
next statement to execute is displayed at the lowest
displayed line on your screen.
When the script encounters an error, press Resume to
activate RDB error mode. In error mode, you can
examine all variables, reserved words, and renamed
reports and results as they existed when the script
erred. While in error mode, only the RDB commands
that display information (-n, R, Vv, reswd$) are allowed.
When the script encounters a statement that displays
output, such as an Output (OUT) or Display Report (DSP)
statement, the RDB utility displays the screen as
requested. When you resume the script, it clears the
screen and displays the RDB prompt.

When the script encounters an Execute a Script (RUN)
statement, if you were the last person to update the
newly started script, the RDB utility continues to be
active in that script.
When the script executes a Defer Updates (DFU)
statement, the RDB utility skips to either the Commit
Updates (CMU) or Decommit Updates (DCU) statement,
whichever comes ﬁrst. The system executes any
intervening statement and displays them in streaming
mode (that is, in a scrolling, uninterrupted manner).
Ü Ü 2200

Legacy Run Debug for the Graphical Interface
for Business Information Server
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If you are using the Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server, the RDB utility provides buttons for
performing the various debugging activities in place of
function keys and commands. It also displays scroll bars
(arrows and slider boxes) at the edge of any window
displaying information that extends beyond its current
boundaries.
For details, see Using the Legacy RDB on the Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
The JavaScript debugger is a component of the Business
Information Server Developer Workshop. This workshop
must be installed on your desktop system in order to debug
a JavaScript script. For installation information, see your
Business Information Server administrator.
2200 Þ Þ
The RDB utility provides a text based screen for debugging
BIS scripts. It provides function keys and commands for
performing the various debugging activities.

Legacy Run Debug Commands
Use RDB commands and function keys F1 through F4 to
view diﬀerent kinds of information in your script.

Command Description

B prm

Stops the script at the point
(breakpoint) speciﬁed in
prm. Only one breakpoint
can be active at a time, and
the previous breakpoint is
cleared when you set a new
one. Enter one of the
following commands or B to
clear the breakpoint:
B n - line number at which to
interrupt the script. The
script stops before executing
line n.
B Llab - letter L followed by
the label number at which to
interrupt the script. The
script stops before executing
the line at label lab.
B v - variable at which to
interrupt the script. The
script stops after executing
any statement that refers to

variable v (variable name or
number).
B @rfc - run function call at
which to interrupt the script.
The script stops before
executing run function call
rfc.

F1

Steps through the script by
executing a line at a time,
including any continuation
line.

F2

Steps through the script by
executing one statement at
a time (use when multiple
statements occur on one
line). Note that the utility
considers the period-space
terminator a statement.

F3

Returns the script to normal
execution speed. To stop the
script, press F3 a second
time.

F4

Sets streaming mode, which
means that the script
executes on logic line (or
statement, if you had been

pressing F2) at a time in a
scrolling, uninterrupted
manner. To stop the script,
press F4 a second time.

M v|$

Monitors a variable or
reserved word by displaying
its contents whenever the
script stops (between line
steps or statement steps).
Only one item can be
monitored at a time, and the
previous monitor is cleared
when you set a new one.
Enter one of the following
commands or M to clear the
monitor:
M v - variable to monitor.
The size, type, and contents
of variable v (variable name
or number) are displayed
whenever the script stops.
M reswd$ - name of the
reserved word to monitor
(for example, USER$ or
LLP$). The contents of reswd
whenever the script stops.

-n

Displays renamed report or
result n.

R

Displays your run control
report.
You can manipulate this
report or result on the screen
(for example, roll or shift it)
and you can update it with a
line change or SOE update.
However, you cannot use
manual functions, such as
Search (S) or Locate (LOC).
Press Resume to return to
the script.

reswd$

Displays the name of the
reserved word you specify
(for example, USER$ or
LLP$), followed by its
contents enclosed in slant
characters.

v

Displays the variable size
and type of variable v,
followed by its contents
enclosed in slant (/)
characters. You can enter a
variable name or number
(for example, <data> or v3).
You do not need to enter the
less than (<) and greater
than (>) symbols to display
a named variable.

@rfc

Dynamically inserts a
temporary statement to
execute. Processing
continues uninterrupted on
the current line.

rfc - run function call
followed by the remaining
ﬁelds needed in the
statement. For example,
@ldv v099=123. loads v099
with 123.

?

Displays a summary of RDB
commands. Press Resume
to return to the script.

^

Exits RDB mode, terminates
the script, and releases the
screen.

Using the Legacy RDB on the Graphical
Interface for Business Information Server
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Business Information Server Script Debugger
Interface
When entering RDB mode, the command line to be
executed appears in red below the buttons. The command
line can be modiﬁed to dynamically change the run
command line. Any valid run functions can be entered.
For example, change ‘@ldv v1i3=123’ to ‘@ldv v1i3=456’ or
change ‘@ldv v1i3=123’ to ‘@ldv v1i3=123 @ldv
v2i3=456’.
You must press the Enter key on the red command line for
any changes made to take eﬀect. The script continues at
the next line of the run control report.
Click the following buttons to view diﬀerent kinds of
information in your script.

Button

Description

NEXT

Steps through the script by
executing a line at a time,
including any continuation
line; steps around a CALL
routine. Note that the next
line to be executed is
displayed in a highlighted

display line just below the row
of buttons.

STEP

Steps through the script by
executing one statement at a
time (use when multiple
statements occur on one
line); steps into a CALL
routine. If a registered
JavaScript subroutine is being
called, the Business
Information Server Developer
Workshop must be active on
your desktop system. Note
that the next statement to be
executed is displayed in a
highlighted display line just
below the row of buttons.

WATCH

Displays the current values of
variables and reserved words.
To terminate your WATCH
selection, click the WATCH
button again.
The following buttons are
displayed when you click the
WATCH button:
MONITOR-enables you to
enter the variables and
reserved words you want to
monitor. Type these items on

the input line at the top of the
variables window and press
Enter. These items and their
current values are displayed
in the top portion of the
Variables window.
ALL-displays all variables
along with their current
values in the lower portion of
the Variables window. Use the
scroll bar to scan the list of
variables.
The variables and reserved
words you select with the
MONITOR button remain
displayed while you scroll
through the list of variables in
the lower portion of the
screen.

BKPT

Stops the script at the point
(breakpoint) you specify. In
methods one and two
described below, to set the
speciﬁed breakpoints, click
the OK button; to resume the
script, click either the NEXT,
STEP, or NORM buttons, found
in the main RDB window.
Method One
To stop the script at a

label, a variable, or a
statement (in the format
@call), after clicking the
BKPT button, type the
item (for example,
<vardata>), in the input
line below the button and
press Enter.
If you set your breakpoint
on a CALL statement and
then click NEXT, the script
continues at the next line
instead of going to the
called routine.
You can set breakpoints
simultaneously on one
label, one variable, and
one statement.
Method Two
Instead of clicking the
BKPT button, focus on the
line in the main window
you want to stop on and
click twice.
Method Three
Click the BKPT button,
then click each line in the
breakpoint window you
want to stop on.

DISP

Displays an input line where
you type the name of the
report or result to display (for
example, 2b0 or -0). Press
Enter. To locate a string in
the displayed report
1. Type the target in the
input line.
2. Press the LOCATE button.
3. To continue looking for
the target, press the
spacebar.

NORM

Continues the script normally
until one of these occurs:
Script encounters a
breakpoint or an RDB
statement
Script ﬁnishes
Script fails

QUIT

Terminates your RDB session.

STRM

Sets streaming mode, which
means that the script
executes one logic line (or
one statement if you had

clicked the STEP button) at a
time in a scrolling,
uninterrupted manner. You
can stop streaming mode in
the following ways:
If the script is waiting for
input from the main
window, you can click the
STRM button. When the
script continues after
accepting the input, it is
no longer in streaming
mode.
Make sure the main
window is your active
window; then press Esc.

Note: When the script is suspended and waiting for input,
as with a Display Report (DSP), Accept Input (INP), or Output
(OUT) statement, the RDB utility disables the RDB buttons
and does not reenable them until you supply input.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@LFC (Load Format Characters)
Use the Load Format Characters (LFC) statement to capture
the format of the current report or result (-0). Use it, for
example, when the run user may start your run with a
displayed report that was shifted or reformatted using the
Create Temporary Format (VIEW) command.
The LFC statement loads the variable with a string of Xs and
blank characters. The Xs indicate report columns displayed;
blanks are columns not displayed. The string is conceptually
the same as the format line in report 0.
The run must be registered as format sensitive. See your
administrator to register the run.

Load Format Characters Statement Format
@LFC v .
where v is the variable to capture the format of the current
result.

Load Format Characters Guideline
To display a report in the format captured by the LFC
statement, use the Set Format Characters (SFC) statement
before displaying the report.

Load Format Characters Example
This example captures the format of the current result in
<format>, processes the report, then redisplays it in the
previous format:
@lfc <format>s80 .
.
. (other processing)
.
@sfc <format> .
@dsp,-0 .

@LFN (Load Field Name)
Use the Load Field Name (LFN) statement to load variables
with the names of report ﬁelds that correspond to the
column-character positions supplied.
The LFN statement is especially useful for converting an
existing run to one that uses named ﬁelds. Use it also to
translate column position data, such as the columncharacter positions captured using the Output Mask (OUM)
statement, into ﬁeld names.

Load Field Name Statement Format
@LFN[,c,d,r,tics?,lab] cc v[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which to load ﬁeld
names. Default = -0.

tics?

Enclose the ﬁeld name in
apostrophes? Y or N. Default =
N. Enter y only if you intend to
build a complete statement in
a variable or in the output
area, to be executed at a later
time.

lab

Label to go to if the ﬁeld name
cannot be loaded.

cc

Column-character positions of
the ﬁelds.

v

Variables to load with ﬁeld
names.

Load Field Name Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
If a speciﬁed variable is not large enough to contain an
entire ﬁeld name, the trailing characters in the name
are truncated.
If the speciﬁed columns do not represent an entire ﬁeld,
the statement loads the name, followed by a partial ﬁeld
designation (for example 'CustCode(1-3)').
The run continues at the label if the ﬁeld name cannot
be loaded.
If you do not use a label and a ﬁeld name cannot be
loaded, the run continues at the next statement, loading
the variable with the actual column-characters (for
example, 2-2).

Load Field Name Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains one of the following codes in case of an
error:
1

A report heading is improperly
formatted for ﬁeld names.

2

Columns supplied do not fall
within ﬁeld boundaries.

3

A ﬁeld name in the report
heading has no signiﬁcant
characters.

4

A ﬁeld name is not unique in the
report heading.

5

A ﬁeld name was truncated
because of the variable size,
and the name is not unique in
the report heading.

STAT2$ contains the number of the ﬁeld in error.

Load Field Name Guidelines
Enter y in the tics? subﬁeld only if you intend to build a
complete statement in a variable or in the output area, to
be executed at a later time.

Load Field Name Examples
This example loads ﬁeld names from report 2B0:
@lfn,0,b,2,y 2-2,45-3 <field1>h18,<field2>h18 .

0,b,2

Load ﬁeld names from
report 2B0.

Y

Enclose ﬁeld names in
apostrophes.

2-2,45-3

Get the names of the twocharacter ﬁeld starting at
column 2 and the threecharacter partial ﬁeld
starting at column 45.

<ﬁeld1>h18 Load <ﬁeld1> with the
name of the two-character
ﬁeld starting at column 2
(<ﬁeld1> then contains
'StCd', including
apostrophes).

<ﬁeld2>h18 Load <ﬁeld2> with the
name of the threecharacter partial ﬁeld
starting at column 45

(<ﬁeld2> then contains
'CustCode(1-3)' ).

This example uses a variable as a ﬁeld indicator. The LFN
statement loads v1 with UNISYS.
@brk,fftype$ .
.
*Unisys
*======.
. 1
@brk lfn,-0 2-6 v1h8 .
@sor,-0 '' 'v1' .,1 .

@LGF (Log Oﬀ Relational Database)
Use the Log Oﬀ Relational Database (LGF) statement to end
a session on a relational database.
The Log On to Relational Database (LGN) statement must
have been issued to initiate communication with a
database.

Log Oﬀ Relational Database Statement Format
@LGF[,lab,rb?,db] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error
occurs. If you specify a label
and attempt to log oﬀ a
relational database before
logging on, the run continues
at the label and builds an error
report in the current output
area; otherwise, the system
ignores the error.

rb?

Issue a ROLLBACK command?
Y or N. May not be available
for all databases. If Y, the
statement decommits changes
made during this session or
since the last recovery point.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

db

Database name. Default =
default database name on
your system.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

db

Ü Ü 2200

Database name. Default =
database running with MRIMID
set to 1. See your
administrator for more
information.

Log Oﬀ Relational Database Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the status of the last syntax submitted to
the database manager. STAT2$ is always 0. If STAT3$ equals
zero, STAT1$ contains a status; otherwise, if you supplied a
label, STAT3$ contains a system message number. Use the
Load System Message (LSM) statement to obtain the text of
the message.

@LGN (Log On to Relational
Database)
Use the Log On to Relational Database (LGN) statement to
initiate a session on the relational database.
The LGN statement establishes communication with the
default database or the database named in the statement.
The session remains active until the statement receives a
Log Oﬀ Relational Database (LGF) statement, or until the
run process is complete.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Note: A maximum of 10 concurrent LGN statements is
allowed for any given run.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Format
2200 Þ Þ
@LGN[,lab,edsp?,c,d,db,inf,pkey,trnrpt]
o[,lang,user,pw vdbt] .
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

vdbt] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or UNIX
systems.

occurs.

occurred, if applicable.

Values and descriptions of the
Primary Key option ﬁeld.

Report that contains a

no translation is used. The
following network statements

(NRN), and reports returned
through Network Return (NRT).

Log On to Relational Database Thread
Management Options
Option

Description

INFO

Use the INFO option to obtain
the status of all active threads,
as in the following example:

@lgn,,,0,h info .
@dsp,-0 .
When you use the INFO option,
the run interpreter ignores the
db subﬁeld and all ﬁelds
following the INFO option. This
option does not activate a
thread.

KILL

Use the KILL option to
terminate any thread on the
current system for this user
and department. It uses the
following LGN format:

@LGN,,,c,d,databasename
KILL,station .
Where KILL is the option and
station is the station number
that created the thread. The
KILL option issues a logon

before it sends a MANAGE
THREAD to RDMS.

Ü Ü 2200

Log On to Relational Database Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the status of the last syntax submitted to a
database. STAT2$ is always 0, unless the database type is
ODBC. For ODBC database types, STAT2$ will only be set to
an ODBC database subtype if the inf or pkey options are set.
Otherwise, STAT2$ is set to 99 since the ODBC database
subtype is unknown. If STAT3$ equals zero, STAT1$ contains
a status; otherwise, if you supplied a label, STAT3$ contains
a system message number. Use the Load System Message
(LSM) statement to obtain the text of the message.
For ODBC databases, the STAT2$ contains an integer value
between 0 and 499 when the pkey parameter is set to Y. To
extract the Transaction Capability and ODBC subtype value,
divide STAT2$ by 100. The result corresponds to the
Transaction Capability, and the remainder value contains
the ODBC subtype value.
The following table displays transaction capability values,
SQL constants, and descriptions of the values for the STAT2$
variable.

Transaction SQL Constant
Capability
Values

Description

0

SQL_TC_NONE

Transactions
not
supported.

1

SQL_TLC_DML

Transactions

can contain
only Data
Manipulation
Language
(DML)
statements
(SELECT,
INSERT,
UPDATE,
DELETE).
Data
Deﬁnition
Language
(DDL)
statements
encountered
in a
transaction
causes an
error.
2

SQL_TC_DDL_COMMIT Transactions
can contain
only DML
statements.
DDL
statements
(for
example,
CREATE
TABLE,
DROP
INDEX)
encountered
in a

transaction
cause the
transaction
to be
committed.
3

SQL_TC_DDL_IGNORE Transactions
can contain
only DML
statements.
DDL
statements
encountered
in a
transaction
are ignored.

4

SQL_TC_ALL

Transactions
can contain
statements
in any order.

database types for the STAT2$ variable.

When an informational -0 result is created (information ﬁeld
= Y), the result contains 2 columns. The ﬁrst column
contains the InfoType. The second column contains the
InfoType value. If no value is returned, then a blank is placed
in the second column.
The following table displays information that is retrieved and
appears in the -0 informational result in the following order.

InfoType

InfoType Value

SQL_ACCESSIBLE_PROCEDURES

Character "Y" if
you can execute
all procedures
returned by
SQLPROCEDURES.
Character "N" if
there are
procedures that

you cannot
execute.

Character "Y" if
you are
guaranteed
SELECT privileges
to all tables
returned by
SQLTABLES.
Character "N" if
there are tables
returned that you
cannot access.

An
SQLUSMALLINT
value specifying
the maximum
number of active
environments
that the driver
can support. If
there is no
speciﬁed limit this
value is set to
zero.

An
SQLUSMALLINT
value indicating
how a COMMIT
operation aﬀects

if the data source
deﬁnes a named

"database".

accessed by the
driver.

indicating the

version of the
DBMS product
accessed by the
driver.

A character string
with the version
of the Driver
Manager.

SQL_DRIVER_NAME

A character string
with the ﬁle name
of the driver used
to access the
data source.

SQL_DRIVER_VER

A character string
with the version
of the driver and
an optional
description.

SQL_IDENTIFIER_QUOTE_CHAR

A character string
used as the
starting and
ending delimiter
of a quoted
(delimited)
identiﬁer in SQL
statements. If the
data source does
not support

quoted identiﬁers,
a blank is
returned.

An
SQLUSMALLINT
value specifying
the maximum
number of active
connections that
the driver can
support for an
environment. The
limitation reﬂects
a driver or data
source limit. If
there is no limit,
the value = 0.

A character string
"Y" if the data
source supports
more than one

same time. A
character string
"N" if the data
source does not
support more
than one
transaction at the
same time.

An
SQLUSMALLINT
value that
determines if
quoted identiﬁers
are necessary
and if they are
stored in
uppercase or
lowercase in the
system catalog.

SQL_SERVER_NAME

A character string
with the actual
data sourcespeciﬁc server
name.

SQL_SPECIAL_CHARACTERS

A character string
containing all
special characters
that can be used
in an identiﬁer
name, table
name, column
name, or index
name. If an
identiﬁer contains
one or more of
these characters,
the identiﬁer
must be a
delimited
identiﬁer.

SQL_TXN_CAPABLE

An
SQLUSMALLINT
value describing
the transaction
support in the
driver or data
source.

SQL_USER_NAME

A character string
that indicates the
name used in a
particular
database. This
name can be
diﬀerent from the
login name.

in the -0 result.

necessarily support the same level of functionality that an
ODBC driver returns.

LIMITS
Use the Limits command to display the highest report
number allowed and the maximum number of lines per
report allowed for the current cabinet and drawer
(determined by the administrator).

where ALL is for administrators only; displays the limits for
all reports on your system.

Note: The ALL parameter involves high overhead, so use it
sparingly.

LINEG (Line Chart)
Use the Line Chart (LINEG) run to create a line chart. The
line chart is displayed on the screen or drawn on a plotter. A
line chart consists of lines of data drawn to correspond to
values in a set of data.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Line Chart Control Line Format
LINEG[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

Line Chart Colors

Stall

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Line Chart Guideline
To produce a bar or a mixed (bar and line) chart from a line
chart report, enter barg or mixed on the control line. The
run ignores ﬁelds on the chart report that do not apply to
the requested chart, and uses default values in others.

centered in a small box on

existing text between the
tab characters; there are
four ﬁelds of 15 characters
each. If you use this ﬁeld,
delete unused note box
ﬁelds.

Note

Location of the note box.

position

Default = N (no note box).
L option - positioned in the
upper left
R option - positioned in the
upper right

Title

Optional chart title of 40
characters or fewer. This is
centered at the top of the
chart in the color speciﬁed
for text.

Optional subtitle of 60
characters or fewer.

characters or fewer,

(horizontal) axis.

characters or fewer, placed

in the lower left corner of

the chart. Y = Current date
in the format DD MMM YY.

values outside these values
and represents them
numerically.

base line drawn. Values
greater than this level are
displayed above the base
line; lesser values are
displayed below it. Default
= 0.

dots?

N. Fill is color and pattern
extending from the line to
the bottom of the chart.
Lines are drawn in order
from one to six, so some
ﬁlled lines may overlap
previously drawn lines with
lower data values.

Text for a legend describing

containing data values.

values for each line in the
appropriate caption column.
Enter at least two labels and
two data values.

@LLN (Last Line Number)
Use the Last Line Number (LLN) statement to load a variable
with the last line number of a report. It is especially useful
for determining whether a report or drawer exists or
whether a report contains any lines.

Note: You can also use the Line Zero (LZR) statement to
capture the number of lines in a report; for more
information, see LZ and @LZR (Line Zero).

Last Line Number Statement Format
@LLN,c,d,r[,lab] vlines .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report in which to locate the
last line number.

lab

Label to go to if the speciﬁed
report or drawer does not
exist.

vlines

Variable to capture the
number of the last line in the
report.

Last Line Number Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The LLN statement loads vlines with the last line
number in the report. It does not create a -0 result.
If the report or drawer does not exist, the run continues
at the label you specify, or if no label is speciﬁed, the
run continues at the next statement.
When you use the LLN statement with a result, the
number of heading lines is always equal to one.

Last Line Number Reserved Words
After the run executes an LLN statement, STAT1$ and
STAT2$ contain the following information:

If

Then

The report exists

STAT1$ = date of the
last update in DATE1$
format (YYMMDD).
STAT2$ = creation
date of the report in
DATE1$ format
(YYMMDD).

The report was
never updated

STAT1$ = 0

The report does
not exist

STAT1$ = highest
report number in the
drawer.

The drawer does
not exist

STAT1$ = 0

When using reserved words such as STAT1$ and STAT2$,
which may read date values from a report (for example, LZR

report), the application must load the values from the
reserved words into "H" type variables, right-justiﬁed, and
zero-ﬁlled to indicate the correct date.

Note: This is necessary for correctly processing year 2000
dates.

Last Line Number Example
This example captures the number of the last line in report
2B0: @lln,0,b,2,050 <line>i3 .

0,b,2

Obtain information from
report 2B0.

050

Go to label 50 if the report
does not exist.

<line>i3

Load <line> with the number
of the last line.

LM and @LMG (List Merge)
Use the List Merge (LM) function or LMG statement to
extract lines or parts of lines from a report and merge them
with another report, creating a result containing multiple
documents.
Use this command to produce several copies of a form letter
or other kind of document that requires varying data in
some places. You specify the data to extract from a standard
column formatted report, the issuing report. The text you
want to remain the same for each document resides in
another report, the receiving report, and contains List Merge
control characters that indicate the columns of special data
to extract.
The command creates a result containing one copy of the
receiving report for each tab line in the issuing report with
the speciﬁed data merged. The result is ready to be
processed with other word process function commands,
such as ADJPRT.

Note: The List Merge (LM) function is only available on 2200
systems.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the replacement string must expand beyond the report
width, it wraps to the next line. If it wraps to more than one
line, you receive the following message: (MGMW14) Word
and Phrase Change can only expand one line Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS
For the List Merge manual function, the issuing report
(containing standard column-formatted data) must be on
display.

List Merge Control Line Format
2200 Þ Þ
LM [report]
where report is the receiving report. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process.
Ü Ü 2200

List Merge Statement Format
@LMG,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report.

lab

Label to go to in case of an
error.

List Merge Control Characters
Enter the following control characters in the receiving report
where you want to merge data.

Control
Description
Character

~=x-y,z

Extract columns of data from
the issuing report. Insert an F
after the equal sign to freeze
the merged portion. (The
command does not delete
extra spaces found in the
speciﬁed columns.) ~= is the
control character for
extracting data

x is the starting column
number y is the number of
columns z is the number of
the trailer-type line past the
tab line from which to extract
data-must be fewer than 5
(optional-omit ,z to process
tab lines). Note that if you
specify ,z and no trailer type
line exists, the data
extraction ﬁeld is treated as
a ﬁeld of spaces.
For example, ~=2-4,3
extracts four characters

starting in column 2 of the
third trailer type line
following the tab line.

~=0,y-z

Extract whole lines starting
at line y past the tab line,
and place z lines into the
receiving report. Insert an F
after the equal sign to freeze
the merged portion (the data
is left-justiﬁed and spaces
are ﬁlled in the receiving
report). There must be
suﬃcient space for the
deﬁned size of the ﬁeld in
the receiving report or data
following the merged ﬁeld
will be overwritten.

~&x-y,z

Create a list containing
speciﬁed columns from each
of the lines speciﬁed in the
issuing report. The ~&
control character creates a
list that is identical for each
copy of the document in the
result. You can use a
maximum of nine ~& control
characters on a line.
~& is the control character
for creating a list x is the
starting column number y is

the number of columns z is
the number of lines past the
tab line from which to extract
data-must be less than 5
(optional-omit ,z to process
tab lines) For example, ~&217 extracts 17 characters
starting in column 2 of the
issuing report and places
them in the receiving report
at the location of this control
character. The command
generates one line of data
for each tab line in the
issuing report.

List Merge Example
This example merges lines and partial lines from the issuing
report, report 7H0, into the receiving report, report 8H0:
@lmg,0,h,7,0,h,8 .

@LNK (Link to Another Script)
Use the Link to Another Script (LNK) statement to start one
script from another script. A LNK statement diﬀers from an
Execute a Script (RUN) statement in that when it starts a
script that executes a GTO END statement, it returns control
to the calling script.

Link to Another Script Statement Format
@LNK[,lab] script[,vld] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the script you
want to start is not registered.

script

Registered name of the
Business Information Server
script to be started.

vld

Input parameters consisting of
variables, literals, reserved
words, constants, or any
combination of these. When
the script starts, it uses their
values to initialize variables
via INPUT$. Pack or rightjustify all variables.
Maximum number of input
parameters = 80.

Link to Another Script Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The calling script resumes when the called script
executes a GTO END statement.
If the called script terminates with a Release Display
(REL) or Display Report and Exit (DSX) statement, or
because of an error condition that does not execute an
error routine (RER), control does not return to the calling
script.
Miscellaneous error conditions can occur in which the
label is not used; for example, if the script report does
not exist or if the script report has an update lock.
The LNK statement clears the output area of the calling
script; the GTO END statement clears the output area of
the called script before control returns to the calling
script.
The system does not pass global script variables to the
called script, but it restores them to their previous state
when the script executes a GTO END statement.

Link to Another Script Reserved Words
LINK$ contains 0 if the script was not started by a LNK
statement; otherwise, contains 1. Examine LINK$ before
ending a script to see if you should execute a GTO END
statement to return to the calling script.
STAT1$, STAT2$, and STAT3$ contain integers used as status
codes sent from the called script via the GTO END
statement.

Link to Another Script Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the LNK statement:
The called script can return up to three numeric status
codes via the GTO END statement, which you can use to
signify whatever you like. The current -0 result is also
available to the calling script.
To capture the numeric status codes once control
returns to the calling script examine STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$.
A script started by a LNK statement cannot itself contain
another LNK statement unless you use the Clear Link
(CLK) statement to clear the original link.
You can link to various system utility scripts, but unless
the utility script contains a GTO END statement, control
does not return to your calling script.
If you set up function keys using the Function Key (FKY)
statement in the calling script, you must set them up
again in the called script, because the system does not
retain them once you link to another script.
You can send up to 80 input parameters and the current
-0 result to the called script for processing.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You cannot link to a script that is registered for a greater
number of variables, labels, or variable characters.

Do not put other statements on the same line after the
LNK statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
You cannot link to a JavaScript script using the LNK
statement. Use the CALL (Call Subroutine) statement for this
purpose.

Link to Another Script Examples
This example starts the TEST script and sends the contents
of <ﬁrst> and <second> and the literal sam as input
parameters to be initialized in the TEST script via INPUT$:
@lnk test,<first>,<second>,sam .
The following GTO END statement in the TEST script returns
control to the calling script and passes three numbers
indicating some sort of status:
@gto end,5,10,20 .
A statement, such as the following IF statement, follows the
LNK statement in the calling script to handle the numeric
status codes returned by the TEST script. This IF statement
checks the ﬁrst status code returned:
@if stat1$ EQ 5 gto 100 ; .

Linking to the SCHEDULE Script Example
You can also use the LNK statement to call the SCHEDULE
script from within a script. The SCHEDULE script schedules a
registered background script to start at a later time, using
the input parameters you supply.

Statement Format
Format 1:
@LNK
SCHEDULE,script,time[,date,sn,restart?,vld,...,vl
d]
2200 Þ Þ
Format 2:
@LNK
SCHEDULEX,script,time[,date,sn,restart?,uid,dpt,p
wd,vld,...,vld]
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

script

Name of the script to be
scheduled. Note that this
script must be registered as a
background script for the
person starting your script.

time

Time (format HHMM) the
script is to start, or NOW for
the current time.

date

Date (format YYMMDD or

YYYYMMDD) the script is to
start. Default = the current
date.

sn

Station number to be notiﬁed
when the SCHEDULE script is
complete, or N if no one is to
be notiﬁed. Default = user's
station number.

restart?

Restart the script if the
system goes down while the
script is processing? Y or N.
Default = N.

2200 Þ Þ

Field

Description

uid

User-id the script is executed
with.

dpt

Department the script is
executed with.

pwd

Password for the user-id
speciﬁed in the uid ﬁeld.

Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

vld,...,vld

Up to 35 variables, literals,
reserved words, constants, or
any combination of these, to
send to the scheduled script.

Outcome
The system schedules the script to be executed in
background at the time and date you request.

Reserved Word
STAT1$ contains one of the following status codes when
linking to the SCHEDULE script:
0

The background script has been
scheduled.

1

The background script name
speciﬁed is not a registered
background script for the person
executing your script.

2

The speciﬁed time is invalid.

3

The speciﬁed date is invalid.

4

The speciﬁed time and date has
already passed.

Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
5

The speciﬁed station number is
invalid.

6

DISPATCHER is not running or it
has been aborted; the script

was not scheduled.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
2200 Þ Þ
5

Only administrators can
schedule with the credentials of
another user.

6

The department number is
invalid.

Ü Ü 2200

Example
This example schedules the RUNIT script for execution at
1:30 P.M. on June 5, 1996. It notiﬁes station 123 when the
SCHEDULE script execution is complete.
@lnk schedule, runit,1330,960605,123 .

Linking to Chart Scripts
For a description of subﬁelds in the formats that follow and
for more information on chart scripts, see CHART.

Statement Formats
Format 1: (Use when a -0 result or a report you specify in
the report subﬁeld contains the name of the chart script to
execute on line 2.)
@LNK CHART[,,report] .
Format 2:
@LNK chartscript[,report,dev,sn,psiz?,transpcy?]
.

Outcome
If you use format 1, the statement links to the chart script
speciﬁed on line 2 of the -0 result or report you speciﬁed.
If you use format 2 and enter a chart report in the report
subﬁeld, the input report is displayed. After you press
Resume to continue the script, the -0 result contains the
graphics primitive code. If you do not enter a chart report in
the report subﬁeld, the chart script returns graphics
primitive code to the calling script in the -0 result. Use the
Display Graphics (DSG) statement to display the chart.

Guideline
The default for the report subﬁeld is the current result (-0),
so if you do not specify a chart report in the report subﬁeld,
be sure the current result is a chart report.

Reserved Word
STAT1$ contains nonzero if an error occurs in the chart
script, so you can check STAT1$ before executing the DSG
statement to handle any errors in your script. Line 8 of the
current result contains the system message, so you can also
check line 8 or start a display at line 8 of the current result.

Example
This example starts the BARG script using data in a
completed chart report in -0. The DSG statement uses the
graphics primitive code in the -0 result returned from the
BARG script to display the chart:
@lnk barg .
@dsg,-0 .
