@LNP (Put Line)
See Put Line and @LNP for more information.

@LNX (Duplicate Line)
See Duplicate Line and @LNX for more information.

@LNY (Yank Line)
See Yank Line and @LNY for more information.

@LN+ (Add Line)
See Add Line and @LN+ for more information.

@LN- (Delete Line)
See Delete Line and @LN- for more information.

LOC and @LOC (Locate)
Use the Locate (LOC) function to ﬁnd a character string
anywhere within one report and display the report at the
ﬁrst occurrence. (Press Resume to see more occurrences.)
Use the LOC statement to search for a character string
within a report and load variables with information about
the data.

Locate Control Line Format
LOC [report f;/tgtstr/o]
LOC tgtstr

Field

Description

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
search for data (enables you
to look for data in ﬁelds
beyond column 80 if you do
not already have those
columns on display).

;

Required character.

/

Delimiter identifying the
character string boundaries.
Can be any character except
those used in the target string
or a caret (^).
See Using Hyphen as Delimiter
Example.

tgtstr

Character string to locate.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Locate
Options). In this function
format, the A, F, and M options
are always assumed.

Use the following format in the control line to locate strings
quickly and easily: LOC tgtstr
When you are locating a character string that resembles a
report identiﬁer (RID), for example 2b0, or if a named report
exists on your system and you locate text that matches the
name of the report followed by a semicolon (;), use this
format: LOC ;/tgtstr/
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the character string you are locating is a negative
number, use this format: LOC ;/-tgtstr/
If the character string you are locating is preceded by a plus
(+) and contains data that resembles a RID, for example
+2b0, use this format: LOC ;/+tgtstr/
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Locate Statement Format
@LOC,c,d,r[,l,lab] o cc tgtstr
[vcol,vlno,vrpt,vcab, vdrw] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to search. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

l

Line number at which to begin
the search. (The LOC
statement processes data
lines only; if you specify a
heading line, the scan begins
with the ﬁrst data line.)

lab

Label to go to if no ﬁnds are
made. (To simplify your run
logic, if you want the run to
continue on the next line, use
LIN1 in this subﬁeld instead of
a label number.)

o

Options ﬁeld (see Locate
Options). Use the A, F, and M
options to locate strings,
regardless of line type.

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to search.

tgtstr

Character string to be located.

vcol

Variable to capture the
number of the column
preceding the column where
the ﬁnd starts.

vlno

Variable to capture the
number of the line where the
ﬁnd is made.

vrpt

Variable to capture the
number of the report where
the ﬁnd is made.

vcab

Variable to capture the
number of the cabinet where
the ﬁnd is made.

vdrw

Variable to capture the
number of the drawer where
the ﬁnd is made.

Locate Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types,
ignoring the character in
column 1 of the function mask
(or the ﬁrst character of the
tgtstr subﬁeld of the LOC
statement). For example, if
the target string is |abc (|
represents a tab character),
the A option looks for the
string abc on all line types.
When you specify the target
string using the control line
procedure, the A, F, and M
options are always assumed.

B[n]

Includes n (1 to 9) lines above
the data match when the
report is displayed. Do not use
this option with the O option.
This option does not apply to
the LOC statement. Default =
1.

C

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase

letters.

2200 Þ Þ

C

This option applies only to full
character set (FCS) reports.

Ü Ü 2200

F

Processes all line types and
includes in the target the
character in column 1 of the
function mask (or the ﬁrst
character of the tgtstr subﬁeld
of the LOC statement). When
you specify the target string
using the control line
procedure, the A, F, and M
options are always assumed.

2200 Þ Þ

G

Searches for data backwards
through the report. If a starting
line number is not speciﬁed,
the search starts with the last
line in the report and ends with
the ﬁrst line in the report.

Ü Ü 2200

M

Designates the ﬁrst character
of the target string as the line
type designator. Use with the F
option to ﬁnd the string on all
line types, even when the
string starts in column 1. You
can also use the M option in
runs to specify the line type to
process. For example, if the
target string is |abc (|
represents a tab character),
the M option looks for the
string abc on tab lines.
When you specify the target
string using the control line
procedure, the A, F, and M
options are always assumed.

O

Creates a result containing
each line on which the
character string occurs.
If you use this option with the
LOC statement, the statement
loads vcol, vlno, vrpt, vcab,
and vdrw with the following
information: vcol - 0

vlno - number of lines
containing the target string
vrpt - number of the report
where target string is found
vcab - number of the cabinet
where target string is found
vdrw - number of the drawer
where target string is found

OU

Creates an update result
containing each line on which
the character string occurs.
This update result enables you
to Delete (DEL) or Extract
(EXT) the formal lines from the
report, or to Update (UPD) the
found lines and blend them
back into the report. This
option can be used only with
reports, not with results.

S

Starts the search at the ﬁrst
line on the screen. This option
is not available for statements.

Sx

Starts the search at line x,
where x is a positive number.

Sx-y

Searches lines x through y. If
the G option is used, x must
be greater than y.

Sx,n

Starts the search at line x and
searches n lines.

Tx

Speciﬁes any character as a
wildcard character. Default
wildcard character = blank. Do
not use the wildcard character
as the ﬁrst character in the
character string. The Tx option
is useful for locating spaces.
For example, if you use the T$
option with the character
string "A D," since the wildcard
character is no longer a space,
the command looks for two
spaces between A and D (Aspace-space-D).
See Locating Strings
Containing Spaces Example.
Without the T option, with the
character string "A D," the
command locates all fourcharacter strings with A in the
ﬁrst character position and D
in the fourth character
position, such as ABCD, A 2D,
and A%CD.
See Locating Strings Using a
Space as a Wildcard Example.

U

Resumes the search beyond
the lines on display, instead of
on the next line. This option
does not apply to the LOC
statement.

Locate Outcome
Executing the Locate command as a manual function causes
the following actions to occur:
If the data is found
The system displays the report starting at the ﬁrst
data line containing the speciﬁed string. If you use
the O or OU option, a result or update result is
displayed.
To continue the search, press Resume.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, a system message is
displayed.

@LOC Statement Outcome
Executing the LOC statement causes the following actions to
occur:
If the speciﬁed data is found, the LOC statement
Loads variables as described under the format ﬁeld
descriptions.
Leaves the previous -0 result intact unless you used
the O or OU options, which create a new result.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, the LOC statement
Loads the variables with 0.
Gives control to the speciﬁed label.

Locate Procedures
To ﬁll in the function mask

1. On the ﬁrst line under the mask, type the line type to
process in the ﬁrst column, followed by the target string.

2. Press the Erase to End of Line key.
3. Move the cursor to the next line and transmit.
To ﬁnd a string in a speciﬁc ﬁeld, erase all asterisks in the
function mask except those in the ﬁeld you want to search.
To ﬁnd a string quickly, use the following format:
LOC tgtstr

Locating Strings in the Current Report Example
This example locates the character string arco in the current
report.
Control Line Entry
loc arco
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report2b',,99 afm 2-79 arco <col>i2,
<line>i4 .

Locating Strings Starting at a Speciﬁc Line
Example
This example locates the character string green in report
2B0, using the S option to begin at line 30.
Control Line Entry
loc 2b0;/green/s30
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report2b',,99 afms30 2-79 green <col>i2,
<line>i4 .
Or
@loc,'report2b',30,99 afm 2-79 green <col>i2,
<line>i4 .

Using Hyphen as Delimiter Example
This example creates a result containing all occurrences of
the character string 89/11, using a hyphen (-) instead of the
slant (/) as the target string delimiter, since a slant is part of
your target string. This example also uses the O option to
create a result containing all ﬁnds.
Control Line Entry
loc ;-89/11-o
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report3a',,99 afmo 2-79 '89/11' .

Locating Strings Using a Space as a Wildcard
Example
This example locates the string a o (the letter A, two spaces,
and the letter O) in report 2B0. Because the spaces in the
character string are wildcards, possible target strings in
report 2B0 include both AMCO and ARCO.
Control Line Entry
loc 2b0;/a o/
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report2b',,99 afm 2-79 'a o' <col>i2,
<line>i4 .

Locating Strings Containing Spaces Example
This example locates all dates in DATE1$ format (YYMMDD)
where the year 1996 is ﬁlled in along with any month
number, but no day (where there are spaces in the DD
portion of the date). The T option changes the wildcard
character to a dollar sign ($) so that the request can ﬁnd
spaces.
Control Line Entry
loc ;/96$$ /t$
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report3a',,99 afmt$ 2-79 '93$$ ' <col>i2,
<line>i4 .

Locating Strings Containing Negative Numbers
Example
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
This example locates the character string ‘-4’ in report 2b0.
Control Line Entry
loc ;/-4/
Equivalent Statement
@loc,'report2b',,99 afm 2-79 '-4' <col>i2,<line>i4 .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@LOG (Log for Analysis)
Use the Log for Analysis (LOG) statement to log each
statement executed in a script to create a result that can
help you evaluate the general quality of your script.
Enter the LOG statement ahead of all other statements,
except for label table (:L) statements (these must always be
at the beginning of the script). Delete the LOG statement
from your script when ﬁnished evaluating the script.

Log for Analysis Statement Format
@LOG .
@LOG,SUB .
where SUB provides an intermediate level of logging. If you
select Subroutine Logging (SUB), statistics are accumulated
until a call to a subroutine (@CALL, @RSR, or @GTO RPX) or
a return from a subroutine (@RETURN or @ESR) is
encountered. When one of these statements is executed, an
entry is written to the log. This entry shows the resources
used since the previous log entry.

Log for Analysis Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the LOG statement:
With the LOG statement in the script, start the script.
When the script ﬁnishes, wait a few seconds and then
press Resume to see the log result.
If your script contains a Release (REL) statement,
replace it with a Go To (GTO END) statement. This keeps
the log result intact after the script completes. (If you
leave the REL statement in the script, the log result is
not available when you press Resume.)
When you are ﬁnished analyzing the script, place the
REL statement back in the script and remove the GTO
END statement.
If your script does not terminate normally because of a
loop, or if you have other problems and cannot get a log
result, ask your administrator to obtain the log result for
you.

@LOK (Update Lock)
Use the Update Lock (LOK) statement to establish update
control of a report. This prevents other users from updating
a report until the run releases update control with the
Unlock (ULK) statement.
The following statements require a LOK statement before
executing:
LNI, LNM, LNP, LNX, LN+, LN-, WRL

Update Lock Statement Format
@LOK,c,d,r[,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report on which to set an
update lock.

lab

Label to go to if another user
has update control of the
speciﬁed report.

Update Lock Reserved Word
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the run goes to the label, STAT1$ contains either:
The station number of the user updating the report.
Zero indicating the report being locked is an executing
run control report (RCR).
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If the run goes to the label, STAT1$ contains the station
number of the user who has the report locked.
Ü Ü 2200

Update Lock Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the LOK statement:
Specify a label on the LOK statement so that the run
continues at the label if another user has update control
of the report; otherwise, the run stalls.
2200 Þ Þ
When not using the label, the run stalls for the amount
of time speciﬁed in the UPWAIT start parameter (see
your administrator for more information) and then
retries the LOK statement.
Ü Ü 2200
In addition to the Unlock (ULK) statement or another
LOK statement, the following statements automatically
release update control:
ADR, DEL, DFU, DLR, DUP, EXT, GTO END, REP, UPD.
2200 Þ Þ
In addition, the AUX, SEN, SNU, and SOR statements
release update control.
Ü Ü 2200
If the run is a linked run, using the GTO END statement
does not release update control.
The system also releases update control whenever a run
terminates.

Update Lock Example
This example requests update control of report 3B0. If
another user has update control of that report, the run
continues at label 99, where it checks STAT1$ to see if it is a
certain station number.
@ lok,0,b,3,099 .
.
. (Other processing)
.
@099:if stat1$ EQ 1 gto 100 ; .

0,b,3

Lock report 3B0.

099

Go to label 99 if another user
has update control of the
speciﬁed report.

stat1$

Reserved word containing the
station number of the user
who has update control of the
speciﬁed report.

@LSM (Load System Message)
Use the Load System Message (LSM) statement to load a
variable with the contents of a system message. This is
useful in error routines when you want to see the actual text
of a system message. XERR$ contains only the message
number, not the text of the message.

Load System Message Statement Format
@LSM,msgno[,lab] vmsg .

Field

Description

msgno

Message number. If you are
using this statement in an
error subroutine, you can use
XERR$ in this subﬁeld to
capture the message number.
For more information, see
@RER (Register Error Routine).
Message numbers between 1
and 9999 are traditional
Business Information Server
messages.
Message numbers between
10000 and 10999 are
JavaScript messages. These
messages are very large and
require special processing.

lab

Label to go to if the message
number does not exist.

vmsg

Variable in which to place the
message.

Specify 80 characters for the
variable size if the message is
generated from a traditional
Business Information Server
application. Business
Information Server messages
do not require any additional
processing.
Specify MAXCHR$ characters
for the variable size if the
message is generated from a
JavaScript application.
JavaScript messages require
additional processing to
eliminate line feed characters.

Capturing a System Message Number Example
This example uses the system message number in XERR$
and loads <error> with the text of the message:
@lsm,xerr$ <error>sMAXCHR$ .

Parsing a JavaScript Message Example
This example calls a JavaScript subroutine and receives an
error. The RER is registered to go to label 0199 if there are
any errors. The call to the library routine removes the line
feed characters from the JavaScript error message and
displays the message for the user.
.Calling a JavaScript subroutine that produces an
error.
*================================================
========
@.
@ RER 0199 .
@ CALL,"CJSTEST" JSTEST2() .
@.
@.
@0199 LSM,XERR$ <jvsmsg>sMAXCHR$ .
@ CALL,LIBDRW$,11 0001 (<jvsmsg>,'1',<sta>i3) .
@.
Here is the JavaScript syntax used to create an error
condition which detects an undeclared variable within the
JavaScript logic.
.CJSTEST
*================================================
=========
function JSTEST2()
{
var = arg1
try {
} catch (e) {
// arg2 += "*** UNEXPECTED ERRORS ***\n" + e;

throw(e);
}
return;
} //end of main function

@LST (Deﬁne List Box)
Use the Deﬁne List Box (LST) statement to deﬁne a list box
and display it on the user screen.
Use the Accept Input (INP) statement to accept input from
the List Box.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Deﬁne List Box Statement Format
@LST[,c,d,r,l,tf?,oname,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,f
c/bc,o,hl] caption [vlh] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the list of
items to be displayed within
the list box.

l

Line within the speciﬁed
report at which to start
transferring list items. Default
= 3.

tf?

Unconditionally transfer the
report (c,d,r) to the
workstation before processing
the LST statement? Y or N.
Default = N.
If c,d,r is a result, specify Y to
ensure up-to-date information,
since the data is dynamic.

oname

Name of the object that
contains the list items. The
object may specify either a PC

ﬁle name or a version
controlled name. If you do not
specify a name in this ﬁeld,
then you must specify the
name on line 2 (the title line)
of the report speciﬁed in the
c,d,r ﬁeld.

Notes:
This command fails if you
specify a version
controlled name and
version control naming is
not supported.
For more information on
version control
availability, see the
Developer’s Guide.

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window or list box handle.
Default = main window.
If you specify a window
handle, the list box is placed
in that window. The
coordinates speciﬁed in the
vertical and horizontal ﬁelds
are relative to that window.
If you specify a list box
handle, the system assumes
that you are changing that list

box in some way. If you do not
specify a value in the fc/bc, or
title ﬁelds, the system uses
the original values; however,
you must specify values in the
vert, hort, vsiz, and hsiz
subﬁelds.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the list box using
a positioning unit (or row
number). See @WIN (Deﬁne
Window Display) for more
information on positioning
units. When you specify a row
number, the position is based
on the main run font. See
@FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you
are specifying a value for the
vertical position, you must
also specify values in the hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or row number).

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the list
box using a positioning unit
(or column number). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information

on positioning units. When
you specify a column number,
the position is based on the
main run font. See @FON
(Font) for more information on
fonts. If you are specifying a
value for the horizontal
position, you must also specify
values in the vert, vsiz, and
hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or column number).

vsiz

Vertical size of the list box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on
positioning units. When you
specify row numbers, the size
is based on the text run font.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you
are specifying a value for the
vertical size, you must also
specify values in the vert,
hort, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the list box extends
to the bottom of the window in
which you place it and
expands and contracts with
that window.

hsiz

Horizontal size of the list box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or column numbers). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on positioning units. When
you specify column numbers,
the size is based on the text
run font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the horizontal size, you must
also specify values in the vert,
hort, and vsiz subﬁelds.
Default = the list box extends
to the right side of the window
in which you place it and
expands and contracts with
that window.

fc/bc

Foreground and background
colors for this list box. See Set
Default Color Codes for the
available colors. If you specify
only one color (omitting the
slant), the statement uses
that color for both foreground
and background.

o

Type of list box to display. See
Deﬁne List Box Options for

available options. Default = S
(single item select.)

hl

Item(s) to highlight in the list
box. Default = 1 (highlight the
ﬁrst line of the list box) for the
F, H, I, L,S, X, and Y options.
Default = no highlighting for
the M, N, R, or D options.
To pass multiple line numbers,
load multiple line numbers
into an array variable. Valid
values for the hl ﬁeld or array
variable include:
0 or blank no lines highlighted
1-n highlight the appropriate
line number
-1 highlight all lines in the list
You can select multiple
highlighted lines with the M,
N, or U options.

Note: Multiple highlighted
lines are only available when
DWCAP$(14-1) = 1.

caption

The caption of the list box.
Enclose captions containing
embedded spaces in
apostrophes ('). If you do not
specify a caption, place two

apostrophes in this ﬁeld. List
boxes without captions cannot
be moved on the screen. The
maximum character length is
255 characters. If this limit is
exceeded, the caption is
truncated to 255 characters.

vlh

Variable to capture the list box
handle. This must be a sixcharacter variable.

Note: The maximum number of lines in a list box is 32767.

Deﬁne List Box Options
Option

Description

D

MS-DOS ﬁle list box. All
directories are denoted by
[directory name]. Double-click
the item to go into the
directory. Double-click [--] to
back up a directory. The
directory name is displayed in
the caption if speciﬁed.
Similar to the M option.

F

Creates a thick frame around
the window, enabling the user
to expand or contract the
window.

H

Creates a list box without a
horizontal scroll bar. The U
option task is ignored for the
H option.

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability:
DWCAP$(15-1) = 1 if
available.

I

Allows the user to respond
with a single-click. (Cannot
use the I option with D, M, or
N options.)

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(12-1) =
1 if available.

L

Adds a 'Locate' button and an
edit box to the top of the list
box, enabling the user to
locate a target within the list
box. When found, the target is
highlighted (unless used with
the M option) and centered
within the list box. Do not use
this option if you speciﬁed a
window or list box handle in
the vwh subﬁeld.

M

Allows the user to select more
than one item. As each item is
selected, it is highlighted.
When all items have been
selected, the statement
passes those values back to
the run as a result in the same
cabinet and drawer as the
current output area.

N

Similar to option M, but

passes the item line numbers
back to the run as a result in
the same cabinet and drawer
as the current output area.

R

Creates a read-only list box.
No item is highlighted. If the
user uses the M or N options
with the R option, then the
lines are highlighted, but the
box still works as a read-only
list box.

Notes: Check DWCAP$ for
availability:
DWCAP$(15-1) = 1 if
available.

S

Allows the user to select one
item. The statement passes
back to the run the value in
the ﬁeld following the selected
item.

U

Updates an existing list box.
This indicates that something
is being changed in an
existing list box (for example,
color or size). In this case, you
must use the same variable in
the vwh and vlh ﬁelds.

X

Enables the user to enlarge
the window to full size.

Y

Allows the sizing of the control
to be in row and column units,
independent of the units used
for placement.

Deﬁne List Box Colors
See Set Default Color Codes for a list of valid colors that can
be used with the Deﬁne List Box statement.

Deﬁne List Box Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the LST statement:
You can have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT,
LST, PIC, TXT, and WIN controls concurrently open.

Note: The actual number of handles created can be lower
depending on workstation PC memory conﬁguration and
usage.

Deﬁne List Box Examples
This example uses the LST statement to display a list box,
and the INP statement to determine where to continue the
run after the user makes a selection from the box.
@lst,0,a,155,6,,list.dat,<win1>,07,12,10,20,,s,4
\
’List of Names’ <list2>I6 .
@inp <list2>,(0100) .
.
.

List data in report 155a.

Line number within the
report to start transferring
list items.

list.dat

Name of the PC ﬁle where
the data for the list box is
stored.

<win1>

Specify the handle of the
window in which to place
the list box.

07,12,10,20 Display the list box at line 7,

column 12. Make the list
box 10 lines long and 20
characters wide.

s

Option to allow you to select
one item.

4

Highlight the fourth item in
the list box.

List of
Names

Give the list box the caption
'List of Names.'

<list2>i6

Place the list box handle in
variable <list2>i6.

@inp

If the INP statement detects
that this window was used,
the run continues
processing at label 0100,
where the input is placed
into <name>.

This example uses the LST statement to display a list box
with multiple items highlighted.
@LDA <select>i5[3]=2,4,6 .
@LST,0,a,420,3,y,'LIST.DAT',

<win001>,01000,02000,10000,15000\
,,fm,<select> 'Multiple Selection' <lst001>i6 .

Report containing the list
of items to be displayed
within the list box.

Line within the speciﬁed
report to start transferring
list items.

y

Line within the speciﬁed
report to start transferring
list items.

‘LIST.DAT’

File name on the
workstation where the
report data is stored.

<win001>

Variable containing the
parent window handle.

01000,02000 Vertical and horizontal
position (in positioning
units) of the list box
relative to the parent
window.

10000,15000 Vertical and horizontal size
(in positioning units) of the
list box.

fm

The F option (frame) and
the M option (multiple
selection).

<select>

Array variable containing
three members (2,4,6).
Each member is a line
number that is highlighted
in the list box.

‘Multiple
Selection’

The caption on the list box.

<lst001>i6

Variable to capture the list
box handle.

LZ and @LZR (Line Zero)
Use the Line Zero (LZ) function to display information about
a report, such as its number of lines or number of
characters. See Line Zero Information Screen for an
example of the kinds of information this command displays.
Use the LZR statement to load variables with information
about a report, such as its number of lines or number of
characters. The LZR statement is especially useful for
ﬁnding out whether a report exists. Use a label in the
statement, and if the speciﬁed report does not exist, the run
continues at the label.

Line Zero Control Line Format
LZ [report]
where report is the report about which to display
information. For more details, see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

Line Zero Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@LZR,c,d,r[,lab
vlines,vcpl,vhdgs,,,vdept,vuser,vrpw,vwpw,vlgn,vr
typ] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@LZR,c,d,r[,lab
vlines,vcpl,vhdgs,vcs,vupds,vdept,vuser,vrpw,
vwpw,vlgn,vrtyp] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report about which to obtain
information. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

lab

Label to go to if the report or
drawer does not exist. To ﬁnd
out whether the report or
drawer exists, use this subﬁeld
alone (for example,
@lzr,0,b,3,099). (To simplify
your run logic, if you want the

run to continue on the next
line, use LIN1 in this subﬁeld
instead of a label number.)

vlines

Variable to capture the
number of lines.

vcpl

Variable to capture the
number of characters per line.

vhdgs

Variable to capture the
number of heading lines.

2200 Þ Þ

vcs

Variable to capture the
number of the character set
type: 0 = LCS, 1 = FCS, and 2
= FCSU.

vupds

Variable to capture the
number of updates to the
report since it was created.

Ü Ü 2200

vdept

Variable to capture the
department number if the
report has a departmentprivate read password.

vuser

Variable to capture the user-id
if the report has a user-private
read password.

vrpw

Variable to capture the read
password or the word LOCKED
if the report has a read
password.

vwpw

Variable to capture the write
password or the word LOCKED
if the report has a write
password.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Variable to capture the
report’s language number.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

vlgn

This variable always contains
zero.

Ü Ü 2200

vrtyp

Variable in which the system
returns one of the following
values identifying the type of
report: 0 Normal report
1 Binary report
2 Encoded report
3 Source-protected report (for
a runtime application)
4 Namelist

@LZR Statement Outcome
Executing the LZR statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The Line Zero command does not create a result. The
previously existing -0 result remains after the LZR
statement executes.
If the report or drawer does not exist, the run continues
at the label or, if you did not specify a label, the run
continues at the next statement.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The vrpw and vwpw variables contain the word LOCKED
if the report has a read or write password and if the user
is not signed on in department 2 (the Business
Information Server coordination department).
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The vrpw and vwpw variables contain the word LOCKED
if the report has a read or write password and if the user
is not signed on with the key user sign-on accessible to
administrators.
Ü Ü 2200

@LZR Reserved Words
If the report exists:
When using reserved words such as STAT1$ and
STAT2$, which may read date values from a report
(for example, LZR report), the application must load
the values from the reserved words into "H" type
variables, right-justiﬁed, and zero-ﬁlled to indicate
the correct date.

Note: This is necessary for correctly processing
year 2000 dates.
STAT1$ contains the date of the last update in
DATE1$ format YYMMDD.
STAT2$ contains the creation date of the report in
DATE1$ format YYMMDD.
STAT3$ contains the save ﬂag date, if one exists. If
the save ﬂag contains an invalid date, STAT3$
contains zero.
If the report was never updated:
STAT1$ contains zero.
STAT2$ contains the creation date of the report in
DATE1$ format YYMMDD.
If the report does not exist, STAT1$ contains the highest
report number in the drawer.
If the drawer does not exist, STAT1$ contains zero.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

password exists.

Line Zero Example
This example captures information about report 2B0:
@lzr,0,b,2,099 <lines>i4,<chars>i3,<hdgs>i1 .

0,b,2

Obtain information about
report 2B0.

099

Go to label 099 if the report
does not exist.

<lines>i6

Load <lines> with the
number of lines.

<chars>i3 Load <chars> with the
number of characters per
line.

<hdgs>i6 Load <hdgs> with the
number of heading lines.

MA and @MCH (Match), MAU and
@MAU (Match Update)
Use the Match (MA) function or the MCH statement to
compare the data in one or more ﬁelds of two reports and,
optionally, copy data from an issuing report to a receiving
report, creating a result.
Use the Match Update (MAU) function or MAU statement to
create an update result, which enables you to Delete (DEL)
or Extract (EXT) the found lines from the report or Update
(UPD) the found lines and blend them back into the report.

1. The command compares data in ﬁelds of the receiving
report to the corresponding ﬁelds in the issuing report
and creates a result containing the same headings as,
and with the data lines in the same order as, the
receiving report.

2. If you speciﬁed data to be copied, the copied data
appears in the result. Whenever a matched item occurs
in multiple lines of the issuing report, the data copied to
the receiving report ﬁeld is from the last matched line of
the group.

3. During the match process, the system locks both reports
to prevent changes by other users.

4. The command inserts the following information at the
top of the result:

a. Number of matches or nonmatches (depending on
the options you selected).

b. Number of lines compared.

Use the D option to omit this information from the
result.
When using the MA or MAU function, the receiving report
must be displayed. Use the I option if the issuing report is
displayed.
See Match Guidelines for information about displaying
masks for reports wider than 80 characters.

Match Control Line Format
MA [report f]
MAU [report f]

Field

Description

report

Issuing report or, if you use
the I option, the receiving
report. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

f

Report format in which to
match data (enables you to
match data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 in the second
report).

Match Statement Format
@MCH,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab] o icc iltyp,ip rcc
rltyp,rp .
@MAU,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab] o icc iltyp,ip rcc
rltyp,rp .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report.

lab

Label to go to if either the
issuing or receiving report is
not sorted (valid only with the
P option).

o

Options ﬁeld (see Match
Options). The D option is
assumed.

icc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds in the
issuing report.

iltyp

Line type in the issuing report
to match against. If you
specify the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank, but enter the
comma.

ip

Parameters in the issuing
report. See Match Parameters.

rcc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds in the
receiving report.

rltyp

Line type in the receiving
report to match against. If you
specify the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank, but enter the
comma.

rp

Parameters in the receiving
report. See Match Parameters.

Match Options
Option

Description

A

Matches all line types.

B

Blends issuing and receiving
report lines in a result. Data
must be presorted. Do not use
this option with the M or N
options.
The B option is not valid with
the Match Update Function.

2200 Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters. This option applies
only to full character set (FCS)
reports.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

D

Deletes match information
lines from the result. The D
option is always assumed with
the MCH statement.

E

Does not move blank ﬁelds
from the issuing report. For
matched items occurring in
multiple lines of the issuing
report, if the last item of the
group is blank, the command
does not move the data.

F

Does not ﬁll move ﬁelds in a
no-match condition. The F
option is invalid when used
with either the M or N options.

I

Speciﬁes that an issuing report
instead of a receiving report is
displayed. With the MCH
statement, reverses the
positions of the issuing report

and receiving report in the
statement syntax. This option
is useful only with the Match
manual function.

L(x)

Omits lines of type x from the
result. Note that you can use
any line type here. For
example, L(*.A) indicates that
asterisk lines, period lines, and
A-type lines are to be omitted
from the result. If the line type
being omitted is the same as
the line type being searched,
the command includes these
lines in the total number of
ﬁnds. If the C(S) option is
speciﬁed, any letters speciﬁed
as line types are case
sensitive.

M

Includes in the result only lines
containing ﬁelds that match
between the compared
reports. Do not use this option
with the B option. When using
Match Update, the M option is
the default.
See Moving Columns in Lines
That Match Example.

N

Includes in the result only lines
containing ﬁelds that do not
match between the compared
reports. Do not use this option
with the B option.
See Finding Lines That Do Not
Match Example.

P

Speciﬁes that issuing and
receiving reports are presorted
by ﬁelds to be matched. Do
not use this option with the Q
option. See Match Guidelines
for more information.

Q

Speciﬁes quick execution of
match data by assuming the
data is presorted. When you
specify the Q option, you
guarantee that the reports are
sorted, so the system does not
check for unsorted ﬁelds. Do
not use this option with the P
option.

S

Includes in the result only lines
containing ﬁelds that match,
appearing in the same order
as the issuing report.

Match Parameters
Since the length of non-date ﬁelds must be identical, adjust
ﬁeld sizes by erasing or adding asterisks in the function
mask when using the Match manual function. Date ﬁelds
must be at least as wide as the size deﬁned for the selected
date format.

Parameter Description

1-5

Speciﬁes the sequence of
ﬁelds to match, beginning
with 1. Type each parameter
in the ﬁrst column of the
ﬁeld.

A[n] - Z[n]

Speciﬁes the sequence of
ﬁelds to copy, beginning
with A. Type each parameter
in the ﬁrst column of the
ﬁeld.
The optional [n] ﬁeld deﬁnes
the characters that get ﬁlled
into the receiving ﬁeld if the
match parameters (1 - 5) do
not match.
The [n] ﬁeld is not valid with
Match Update.

Yn

Deﬁnes date ﬁelds to be
processed. n is the number
corresponding to the date
format of 0 to 20 The Y
parameter immediately
follows the parameter used
to identify the ﬁelds to
match in each report. Note
that invalid dates do not
match other invalid dates.
Blank ﬁelds are considered
invalid dates
2200 Þ Þ
Deﬁnes date ﬁelds to be
processed. n is the number
corresponding to the date
format of 0 to 21. The Y
parameter immediately
follows the parameter used
to identify the ﬁelds to
match or move in each
report. Note that invalid
dates do not match other
invalid dates. Blank ﬁelds
are considered invalid
dates. Issuing date ﬁelds
containing invalid dates will
appear as asterisks in the
receiving ﬁelds when
moved.
Note that a receiving move
ﬁeld may specify either ﬁll
characters (n) or a date

format (Yn) following the
ﬁeld letter, but not both.
When characters follow the
ﬁeld letter, if the
corresponding issuing ﬁeld
speciﬁes a date format, then
the receiving ﬁeld must
contain a date format,
otherwise the characters are
considered to be ﬁll
characters.
Ü Ü 2200
For a complete list of date
formats, see Entering Dates
and Times in the DC
Statement.

@MCH Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the number of lines in the receiving report
that are matched or not matched, depending on the
selected options.
STAT2$ contains the total number of lines in the receiving
report against which a match was attempted.

Match Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
Use the P option to specify that ﬁelds to match in the
issuing and receiving reports are presorted. Presorting
and using the option increases processing eﬃciency. You
must presort the data if you are matching two diﬀerent
line types.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the match ﬁelds are not presorted, the system
performs an internal sort on both reports. After
processing, the receiving report is restored to its original
order. Use the P or Q option whenever possible.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If the match ﬁelds are not presorted, the system
performs an internal sort on both reports. After
processing, the receiving report is restored to its original
order. (The maximum number of characters sorted
internally is 156.) Use the P or Q option whenever
possible.
Ü Ü 2200
To ﬂag ﬁelds where no matches occur, enter characters
such as asterisks or zeros after the alphabetic
parameters in the receiving mask (or in the rp subﬁeld
of the MCH statement). See Flagging a No-Match
Occurrence Example.

You can use the Search and Sort commands in ways
similar to the Match command. Refer to those
commands to be certain you are using the best one.
The displayed report can be in any format, including a
temporary format selected with the Create Temporary
Format command, but the only way to request a
function mask containing columns beyond column 80 in
the other report is to specify a standard format (deﬁned
in report 0) in the input screen.
If you must match or copy data in the issuing report
beyond column 80, either display the columns you need
using the Create Temporary Format command and use
the I option (issuing report on display), or use a format
containing the columns you need so that you can
request it in the input screen.
If you need more formats deﬁned in report 0, see your
administrator.

Creating an Update Result
Use the Match Update command to create an update result.
The MAU statement uses the same ﬁelds and subﬁelds as
the MCH statement.
You can process the update result in the following ways:
Delete the update result lines from the original report,
using the Delete command.
Delete the update result lines from the original report
and redisplay the result, using the Extract command.
Merge updated lines in the update result into the
original report, using the Update command. (For faster
updating, use the Totalize command to ﬁll ﬁelds with
dates, status codes, and so on before calling the Update
command.)

Matching and Moving Items in Fields Example
This example copies a ﬁeld when items in two other ﬁelds
match.
Function Mask
* Product . .Cust. .Sale.
* Type . .Code. .Rep . <-- Issuing report
*=========. . . . .====. . . . .====. (1D0)
********* **** ***
1 2 a
* Product . .Cust. .Spc.
* Type . .Code. .Cod. <-- Receiving report
*=========. . . . .====. . . . .===. (2B0)
********* **** ***
1 2 a
Equivalent Statement
@mch,'report1d','report2b' ''
'product','cust','sale(1-3)' |,1,\
2,a 'product','cust','spc' |,1,2,a .
Parameters Used

1 and 2

Identiﬁes the ﬁelds to match in
each report.

a

Identiﬁes the ﬁeld to copy and
where it is to be placed in the
receiving report whenever

items in both the Product Type
and Cust Code ﬁelds match.

and

whenever items in

@MBX (Deﬁne Message Box)
Use the Deﬁne Message Box (MBX) statement to deﬁne a
message box and display it on the user screen. The user
must respond to each message before continuing with the
application.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Deﬁne Message Box Statement Format
@MBX[,btyp,ityp],caption message code,(lab)
[,code,(lab),...,code,(lab)] .

Field

Description

btyp

Type of button (or buttons)
displayed in the message box.
See Deﬁne Message Box
Options for the available
buttons. Default = OK.

ityp

Type of icon displayed in the
message box. See Deﬁne
Message Box Options for the
available icons. Default = no
icon.

caption

The text lines that appear in
the header box of the
message box can be a literal
or a variable. The caption of
the message box is limited to
100 characters. If this limit is
exceeded, the caption is
truncated to 100 characters.
The default value is the name
of the application.

message

The text lines of the message
to be displayed in the
message box. The lines are
speciﬁed as a parameter or
set of parameters which may
be any combination of literals
or variables. When the
message box is displayed,
each parameter value starts
on a new line. The slant (/) is
the syntax character which
separates multiple
parameters. The total
message text displayed is
limited to 800 characters.

code,(lab) Label to go to when the user
selects the speciﬁed button.
The ﬁrst code listed is the
default button. See Deﬁne
Message Box Options for the
available codes.

Deﬁne Message Box Options
Options for the btyp Subﬁeld:

Option

Description

OK

OK

OC

OK and Cancel

RC

Retry and Cancel

YN

Yes and No

YNC

Yes, No, and Cancel

ARI

Abort, Retry, and Ignore

Options for the ityp Subﬁeld:

Option

Description

E

Exclamation point

I

Informational

S

Stop sign

Q

Question mark

Options for the code,(lab) Field:

Option

Description

O

OK

C

Cancel

A

Abort

R

Retry

I

Ignore

Y

Yes

N

No

Deﬁne Message Box Example
This example creates a message box.
@LDV V1S5='FIRST'
@LDV V2S7='SECOND'
@mbx,OC,E,Continue? 'You may Continue'/'or
Cancel' O,(0005),C,(0199) .

Field

Value

Description

btyp

OC

Display two
buttons¾OK
and
Cancel¾within
the message
box.

ityp

E

Display an
exclamation
point icon
within the
message box.

caption

Continue?

The message
box title.

message ‘You may
Continue’/‘or

This is the
message

code,
(lab)

Cancel’

within the
message box.
The slant (/)
character
displays the
text on two
separate lines.

O,(0005),C,
(0199)

If the user
selects OK, the
run continues
at label 0005.
If the user
selects Cancel,
the run
continues at
label 0199.
The user must
respond before
doing any
further
processing.

Notes:
A forward slash (/) is used to split the message into
multiple lines. The statement after the forward slash (/)
begins in a new line.
When using single quotation for the message text, no
space is allowed between variables and the forward
slash (/) which is outside the single quote.
Example

@mbx,OC,E,Continue? 'You may 'V1'Continue'/'or
'V2'Cancel' O,(0005),C,(0199)
When using double quotation for the message text, the
variables and the forward slash (/) are used without
quotation.
Example
@mbx,OC,E,Continue? "You may V1 Continue/or V2
Cancel" O,(0005),C,(0199)

MIXED (Mixed Chart)
Use the Mixed Chart (MIXED) run to create charts with both
bars and lines representing data. A mixed chart is displayed
on the screen or drawn on a plotter.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Mixed Chart Control Line Format
MIXED[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

Mixed Chart Colors

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Mixed Chart Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this run:
You can place one set of bars and up to three lines on a
single chart. Each row of data located under the heading
divider line represents one bar and one point on each of
the lines. You do not have to specify information for both
the line and bar portions.
To produce a line chart or a bar chart from a mixed chart
report, enter barg or lineg on the control line. The
system ignores ﬁelds on the chart report that do not
apply to the requested chart, and uses default values in
others.
The types of bars available include
Stacked - Values stack on one bar, producing one
bar for each label.
Comparative - Each y value has its own bar.

Field

Description

Text color

Color of the text. Default =

Colors.

= black. See Mixed Chart
Colors.

the chart (screen display
only). Default = screen
color. See Mixed Chart
Colors.

tab characters; there are

ﬁelds.

Level at which you want a

greater than this level are

= 0. Do not specify a base

or 3D bar charts.

or a marker number.

= 4 (circle). See Mixed

below the word Labels.
Enter data values for each
bar and line in the
appropriate caption
column. Each data value is
graphed as a bar. Enter at
least two labels and two
data values.

@MNU (Deﬁne Menu Bar)
Use the Deﬁne Menu Bar (MNU) statement to deﬁne a menu
bar and display it on the user screen.
The statement creates a menu bar, which provides available
selections.

Note: The number of items displayed in the menu bar
depends on the run user's monitor; on some monitors, not
all of the items you requested to be displayed may be
visible.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Deﬁne Menu Bar Statement Format
@MNU,type[,vwh] keyword[,"label",o,action/val] .

Field

Description

type

Speciﬁes type of menu bar, P
(permanent) or T (temporary).
Leave this ﬁeld blank when
using the RESET keyword.
Default = T.

vwh

Speciﬁes a window handle.
When you specify this handle,
the statement attaches the
menu bar to that window
rather than defaulting to the
main window.

keyword

Speciﬁes attributes of the
menu bar:
MENUBAR ¾ Names each
position on the menu bar,
from left to right. You can
specify multiple MENUBAR
keywords per MNU statement.
All menu bar keywords must
specify an action to perform

or immediately be followed by
a SUBMENU keyword.
RESET ¾ restores the
previous permanent menu
bar. When using this keyword,
omit the type, label, o, and
action/val ﬁelds.
SUBMENU ¾ speciﬁes that
there is a pop-up menu
associated with this pull-down
menu item, and that the lines
deﬁning the pop-up menu
immediately follow this
subﬁeld.
You can specify multiple
SUBMENU keywords for used
MENUBAR keyword. Terminate
SUBMENU keywords with an
END command.
END ¾ signals the end of a
SUBMENU or MENUBAR
keyword. If there is a
SUBMENU keyword that has
not been closed with an END
keyword, the statement
considers END to be the end
of that SUBMENU. If there are
no open SUBMENU keywords,
END signals the end of the
MENUBAR keyword.

"label"

Names each position on the
menu bar. Use with the
MENUBAR and SUBMENU
keywords only.

o

See Deﬁne Menu Bar Options
for the available options.

action/val Action to perform or value to
provide. If you do not specify
an action or value, provide
one or more submenu
deﬁnitions immediately
following the MENUBAR
command.
If this is a permanent menu
bar and there are no pulldown selections associated
with this label, this subﬁeld
must contain an action that is
valid from the control line.

Deﬁne Menu Bar Options
Use the following options only on pull-down or pop-up menu
deﬁnition lines. They are not valid on MENUBAR or submenu
keywords that specify an action. Default = E and U.

Option

Description

A

Add a hot key for the menu
item. The menu item must be
a pop-up menu item. The label
ﬁeld must hold the following
format <Menu Name>\<any
Printable Character>.

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability:
DWCAP$(15-1) = 1 if
available.

C

Displays item as checked. Do
not use with the U or S option.

D

Displays item as disabled
(grayed). Do not use with the
E or S option.

E

Displays item as enabled. Do
not use with the D or S option.

S

Displays a separator bar. The
label ﬁeld must contain two
quotation marks ("") only. Do
not use in combination with
any other option.

M

Marks the end of one column
of pull-down selections. The
next item begins a new
column. Do not use with the S
option.

U

Displays item as unchecked.
Do not use with the C or S
option.

Deﬁne Menu Bar Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
A permanent menu bar is active until the statement
encounters another menu bar. It does not terminate
when the run terminates.
A temporary menu bar is active until one of the
following occurs:
You replace it with another menu bar.
The run terminates.
You restore a previous menu bar (see keyword
subﬁeld).
The MENUBAR, RESET, and SUBMENU commands that
deﬁne the menu bar also control the contents of the
menu bar and the action taken by each selection.
A MENUBAR keyword is terminated by any at sign (@)
command, or by an END keyword not paired with a
SUBMENU keyword. Also, a MENUBAR (with its
SUBMENU) closes when another MENUBAR keyword
begins.
Specify one menu bar label per MENUBAR keyword. You
can deﬁne up to 500 labels within one MNU statement,
up to 100 per menu bar item. Maximum number of
characters per label = 79.
To tell the user which character to use (in conjunction
with the ALT key) when selecting an item via the

keyboard rather than the mouse, place an ampersand
(&) immediately preceding the character. When the user
displays the menu bar, the character (called the hot
key) is underlined. If multiple menu bar entries exist
with the same hot key, the statement uses only the ﬁrst
entry.
For permanent menu bars, if there are no pull-down
selections associated with the menu bar name, you
must provide an action in the action/val ﬁeld.
For temporary menu bars, use the INP statement to
receive the contents of the action/val ﬁeld. You can then
reference the contents in INPUT$, INVAR$, and so on.
If the run suspends via a DSP, OUM, OUT, or SC
statement, and the user selects a menu bar item, the
statement considers the contents of the action/val ﬁeld
to be input from the home position of the screen and
processes it accordingly.
If you add a menu bar to a window, the menu bar takes
space within that window and reduces the number of
lines available within the window. The actual height of
the menu bar depends on the type of monitor (VGA or
EGA).

Deﬁne Menu Bar Example
This example uses the MNU statement to create a
temporary menu in the window <win1>. The menu bar
includes three separate main items. The ﬁrst item is 'border'
which is a drop-down menu item containing the selections of
‘None’ and ‘Frame’. The third item, ‘Color’, lists the colors of
Blue, Red, and Yellow followed by a separator bar, and then
the color menu option of ‘More Colors’. Selecting ‘More
Colors’ generates a submenu with the colors of Orange,
Green, and Purple. The color ‘Purple’ is disabled; thus, you
cannot select this color. Depending on which menu item you
choose determines where the run continues.
@win,,2,2,30,30,,o 'Menubar Example'<win1>i6 .
@mnu,t,<win1> MENUBAR,"&Border"
"&None",,NONE
"&Frame",,FRAME
MENUBAR,"&Font"
"&Courier New",,CN
"&Lucinda Console",,LC
"&MAPPER Standard",,MS
MENUBAR,"&Color"
"&Blue",,blu
"&Red",,red
"&Yellow",,yel
"",s,0
SUBMENU,"&More Colors"
"&Orange",,ora
"&Green",,gre
"&Purple",d,pur
@0002: INP .
@ IF INPUT$ eq NONE,(0010),FRAME,(0020),CN,
(0030),LC,(0040),\

MS,(0050),BLU,(0060),RED,(0070),YEL,(0080),ORA,
(0090),GRE,(0100),\
PUR,(0110) ; gto 0002

Move Line and @LNM
Use the Move Line function or LNM statement to remove
lines from one location and place them in another. Compare
the Move Line command with the Insert Line command:
The Move Lines command copies the lines, deleting
them from their original location.
The Insert Line command copies the lines, retaining
them in their original location.
The command copies the lines and deletes them from their
original location; it does not create a result.
For the Move Line manual function, the report must be on
display.
For the LNM statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LNM statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.

Move Line In-Report-Line Format
>]xMl[,q|-LL]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

x

Number of times to move the
line or lines.

M

Move Line call.

l

Line number of the ﬁrst line to
move.

q

Number of lines to move.
Default = 1.

LL

Line number of the last line to
move. Default = 1.

Move Line Statement Format
@LNM,c,d,r,lb4,[x],l[,q] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report in which to move lines.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

lb4

Line number preceding the
location where lines are to be
moved.

x

Number of times to move the
lines. Default = 1.

l

Number of the ﬁrst line to
move.

q

Number of lines to move,
beginning with the line
speciﬁed in the l subﬁeld.
Default = 1.

Move Line Guideline
With the Delete and Yank Line command, you can move
lines by deleting them and copying them to a buﬀer so that
you can put them elsewhere. For more information, see
Delete and Yank Line and @LND.

Move Line Procedure
Use the following procedure to move lines:

1. Place the cursor on the ﬁrst line to be moved.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
4. Type the number of lines to move in the Delete line
ﬁeld and transmit. Default = 1. The system yanks the
deleted lines so that you can use the Put lines ﬁeld to
copy them.

5. Move the cursor to the line where you want to copy the
previously deleted lines.

6. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
7. Move the cursor to the Put lines ﬁeld and transmit.
Or

1. Place the cursor on the line above the line where the
copied lines are to be moved.

2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Move Line in-report-line format.

Move Line Example
This example moves two lines, 8 and 9, one time and places
them after line 6 of report 2B0. Position the cursor on line 6,
erase to the end of the line, and enter the following:
>]1m8,2
Equivalent Statement
@lnm,0,b,2,6,1,8,2 .

@MQL (MAPPER Query Language)
Use the MAPPER Query Language (MQL) statement to issue
SQL statements against a database.
The statement produces a -0 result containing the retrieved,
updated, inserted, or deleted records as speciﬁed by the
SQL statement, or an error result. When you issue multiple
SQL statements, a separator line is displayed in the -0 result
showing the result of each statement.

MAPPER Query Language Statement Format
@MQL[,c,d,lab,o,skip,q,ib,ltyp,dspfmt,sbf,dtchar,
wrap,info,vert,adj,
nchar] 'syntax'[,sc,sd,sr,sqln,sq]
[vstat,vcol,vrpt,vsel,vsq,vinsrt,
viq,vdel,vdq,vupd,vuq,vcre,vcq,vdrop,vdrq,valt,va
lq] .

Note: If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or UNIX
systems.

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer for the
result. Specify a full character
set (FCS) drawer. Default =
0,a.

lab

Label to go to if the MQL
statement detects errors or a
MAPPER interface error
(standard MAPPER system
message). When the
statement detects an error,
the STATUS ﬁeld in the error
result is a positive MQL
system message number.
When the statement detects

an interface error, any STATUS
ﬁeld in the error result
contains the negative MAPPER
system message number.

o

Case-sensitivity option for
updates, inserts, or character
compares. Default = no
option. All compares, inserts,
or updates on character data
are case-sensitive. This is the
ANSI standard for SQL
character strings.

o (C
option)

Internally converts all
character data to uppercase
when performing character
compares.

o (D
option)

Converts all character strings
to uppercase and then inserts
or updates.

o (B
option)

Same as C and D options
combined.

o (L
option)

Uses the exact case of ﬁeld
names when creating a table
or getting a table description.

Default = changes ﬁeld names
to uppercase.

o (S
option)

Uses the exact case of ﬁeld
names when creating a table
or getting a table description
and places a space where
heading lines split. For
example: *ST.

*CD.
*==.
becomes ST CD.

o (Z
option)

Generates CREATE TABLE
syntax, using the information
from the DDI-style syntax
report you supply. The
statement converts only valid
DDI information lines, and if it
ﬁnds none, creates a result
containing only a header line
and a heading divider line. If
you do not supply an input
report, the statement ignores
this option.

skip

Number of rows in the table to
ignore when retrieving data.
Default = all data retrieved,
beginning with the ﬁrst row.
Use this ﬁeld only with SQL

SELECT statements. This ﬁeld
applies to all SELECT
statements encountered.

q

Number of records. Use this
subﬁeld only with SQL SELECT
statements. This subﬁeld
applies to all SELECT
statements encountered.
If q = 0, all records are
returned.
If q > 0, the statement stops
returning records when it
reaches the quantity speciﬁed
or it encounters the End of
Data (end of report).
If q < 0, only the column
headings and the data type
line are inserted into the
result. No records are
selected.

ib

Blank column option. Type y to
have columns with blank
column names included in the
selected result. Default =
ignore columns with blank
column names. Use this ﬁeld
only with SQL SELECT
statements. This ﬁeld applies

to all SELECT statements
encountered.

ltyp

Line type option. Type y to
have a data type inserted
after the headings in the
result. Y also includes data
lines encountered in the
database that do not begin
with a tab code (nondata
lines), if the SELECT statement
references a single table (no
JOINS) and the COUNT
function is not invoked. Use
this ﬁeld only with SQL
SELECT statements. This ﬁeld
applies to all SELECT
statements encountered.

dspfmt

Format for display. Currently
aﬀects only the results of the
SELECT or CREATE TABLE
statements.

dspfmt
(B
option)

Add ﬁve blank lines to created
table.

dspfmt
(C
option)

Produce CREATE TABLE syntax
from description.

dspfmt
(D
option)

Display column names, types,
and sizes.

dspfmt
Same as P, plus detailed
(F option) cabinet, drawer, and report for
all tables.

dspfmt
(H
option)

Produce only table headers.
The statement inserts ﬁve
blank tab lines after the
heading divider line (valid for
horizontal headers only) and
space for one vertical header.

dspfmt
Same as D, plus trailing
(P option) information lines.

dspfmt
Left-justify numeric ﬁelds.
(L option)

dspfmt
(R
option)

Right-justify numeric ﬁelds.

sbf

Size of blank ﬁelds between
columns of selected data. Use

this ﬁeld only with SQL
SELECT statements. This ﬁeld
applies to all SELECT
statements encountered. If
the width of the resulting line
would exceed the width of the
result drawer, the statement
reduces the size of the ﬁeld to
any value (down to 0).

dtchar

Data typing line type
character. Default = asterisk
(*) character. If N, no data
type determination occurs (all
columns are treated as
character type). Do not use
characters A - Z, 0 - 9, space,
tab, or period.

wrap

Option to control wrapping of
columns and data lines that
exceed the length of the line
in the report. Default = N. Use
this ﬁeld only with SQL
SELECT statements. This ﬁeld
applies to all SELECT
statements encountered.

wrap
(A
option)

If the record length is greater
than the line, the report
heading truncates and places

the remainder of the record on
trailer lines.

wrap
(B
option)

If the column does not ﬁt on
the report line, places column
and remainder of record on
trailer lines.

wrap (Y
or W
option)

Displays the data on multiple
lines.

wrap
Truncates data to the length of
(T option) the line.

wrap
(N
option)

Displays a system message.

information about the tables
for the result. Default = N.

sequence number.

info (I

side.

against a MAPPER database.

MAPPER Query Language Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the status (positive or negative). STAT2$
contains the line number of the ﬁrst data line found with the
ﬁrst SELECT statement. This line number represents the ﬁrst
data line found in the ﬁrst report if you selected a Join
operation. If an error occurs, STAT3$ contains the system
message number.

SQL Terminology
Following are SQL terms and their software equivalents:

SQL
Terms

Terms

Column

Field

Table

Report

Row

Tab line (special lines are
treated as comments)

Referencing Tables
Because MQL tables are reports, the MQL statement uses
methods such as those in the following examples to
reference the tables:

32B

Report 32B in current cabinet

32B110

Report 32B in cabinet 110

"22e"

Report 22E in current cabinet

ABC

Searches the system directory
for report ABC

"USER"

Searches the system directory
for report USER

$R2

Renamed report -2

$R-4

Renamed report -4

"-1"

Renamed report -1

You can use quotation marks around an item to override its
explicit meaning. For example, the quotation marks around
items "22e" and "-1" are required since 22e and -1, are
normally considered numeric entities. Quotation marks are
also required around "USER" since USER is an SQL reserved
word. You can also use quotation marks in cases where the
meaning does not change (ABC is the same as "ABC").

Referencing Columns
SQL requires that you specify a full column name to
reference a column. The MQL statement references columns
as follows:
Full column name (same as SQL).
Partial column name (specify enough of the name to
make unique distinction among columns).
A column selection method ($Cxx) where xx refers to a
logical ﬁeld numbered 1 to n (left to right).
If a column name is a reserved word, such as
Description, enclose it in quotation marks (").

Boolean Expressions
A Boolean expression is an expression that results in a value
of either true or false. You use Boolean expressions to select
rows in a table for which the Boolean expression is true. The
simplest kind of Boolean expression consists of two
operands separated by a comparison operator, as follows: a
<= b c = d You can also use the BETWEEN..AND.. operator
to create a Boolean expression:

x BETWEEN y AND z You can create more complex
Boolean expressions by using NOT, AND, and OR
operators:
a <= b AND c = d
a = b OR NOT (c BETWEEN d AND e) The valid
comparison operators are <> (not equal), <, >,
<=, >=, =, IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, and LIKE.
The valid Boolean operators are NOT, AND, OR, and
BETWEEN..AND..
Each comparison operator must have an expression on each
side of it, except for IS [NOT] NULL. Precede the IS [NOT]
NULL operator with a single expression.
If one of your operands in an expression is null, such as the
value in a column of your table, the result is null. If you
compare a nonnull value with a null value, the result is
unknown; use the IS [NOT] NULL operator to look for null
values.

Data Types
The MQL statement treats all data as either a null, numeric,
or character data type. The MQL statement determines the
data type for each ﬁeld (column) in each report (table).
Null data is a ﬁeld (column) that is all blanks. Likewise, if
a ﬁeld (column) is all blank, it is considered null.
Numeric data can contain fractional parts or be
speciﬁed using scientiﬁc notation.
Character data is data that is not null or numeric. In SQL
statements, enclose character data in apostrophes (' ').
To specify an apostrophe as part of a character data
item, use two successive apostrophes (for example,
'cat''s').
The MQL statement looks for a data type line that follows
the headings and precedes the data lines. If a data type line
is found, the MQL statement uses information from this line
to determine the data type for each ﬁeld (column) of the
report. If it ﬁnds no data type line, each line of the report is
read and selected columns are examined to determine their
data type.
For a column to be null type, all lines must contain blanks in
that column position. For a column to be numeric type, all
lines must contain a valid numeric representation in that
column position. If a column is neither null nor numeric, it is
determined to be character type.

Working with Large Amounts of Data
When selecting a number of records such that the result
occupies more than 200 lines, use the q subﬁeld. This
assigns an area large enough for the entire result and
reduces internal copying of data, which can occur when the
size is not deﬁned.
Normally, the MQL statement assigns an area in MAPER0
large enough to hold about 200 lines of data and then
selects records until the area is full. If there is more data to
select, the MQL statement allocates an area of space (size
already determined), plus 4000 lines. It then copies all
selected data to the new area and continues selecting. This
process of ﬁlling up the assigned area plus 4000 lines
repeats until all data is selected.
When you use the q subﬁeld to specify the number of
records to select, the statement assigns an area large
enough to hold the data, and selects and writes the data
only once to MAPER0.

SQL Statements
Following are formats and descriptions of SQL statements.
SELECT
Format 1
SELECT select-list FROM table-name-list [WHERE
Boolean-expression] [ORDER BY order-by-list];
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Format 2
SELECT select-list FROM table-name1 join-type
table-name2 [ON Boolean-expression] [join-type
table-name3] [ON Boolean-expression] [join-type
table-name4] [ON Boolean-expression] [join-typ
table-name5] [ON Boolean-expression] [WHERE
Boolean-expression] [ORDER BY order-by-list];

Field

Description

select-list

Columns to be selected. To
ensure that a column name is
unique, qualify it with the
name or alias of a table. If
you do not qualify the column
name, the system matches
the column with the ﬁrst
table in table-name-list that
contains the column name.
The format for a column

name is one of the following:
column
table.column
alias.column

select-list can contain one of
the following:
Column names,
computed columns,
numeric literals, or string
literals separated by
commas.
An asterisk (*) to indicate
all columns from the
speciﬁed tables.
The COUNT(*) function,
which produces a single
value representing the
number of rows that
match the selection
criteria in the WHERE
subclause.
The AVG(<columnname>) function, which
produces a single value
representing the average
of NUMERIC ﬁeld values
in the column <columnname> that match the
selection criteria in the
WHERE subclause.

The MAX(<columnname>) function, which
produces a single value
representing the
maximum of CHARACTER
or NUMERIC ﬁeld values
in the column <columnname> that match the
selection criteria in the
WHERE subclause.
The MIN(<columnname>) function, which
produces a single value
representing the
minimum of CHARACTER
or NUMERIC ﬁeld values
in the column <columnname> that match the
selection criteria in the
WHERE subclause.
The SUM(<columnname>) function, which
produces a single value
representing the sum of
NUMERIC ﬁeld values in
the column <columnname> that match the
selection criteria in the
WHERE subclause.

Note: When executing
the MAX and MIN
aggregate functions on
CHARACTER ﬁelds, the

ASCII values of those
characters are used in
making a character-forcharacter comparison of
data in that ﬁeld.
See the Using Aggregate
Function Example to view
the results for the
Aggregate Functions.

tablename-list

Table or tables to be used in
the selection. List one to ﬁve
entries separated by
commas, using the following
format: table-name [alias]
where table-name is the
report to be used and alias is
the identiﬁer to reference the
given table.
When you specify more than
one table, the number of
rows available is the cross
product of all rows in all
tables.

Join-type

Type of ANSI SQL style of JOIN
to be executed. MQL
supports INNER JOIN, CROSS
JOIN, LEFT OUTER JOIN,
RIGHT OUTER JOIN, and FULL
OUTER JOIN.

BooleanRow or rows to select. If you
expression do not use the WHERE or ON
subclauses, the statement
selects all rows of the tables.
The Boolean-expression of
each ON clause may only
reference columns from
tables that have been
referenced so far in the
SELECT statement (for
example, the ﬁrst ON clause
may only reference columns
from the ﬁrst two tables, and
so on.) Also, when executing
a CROSS JOIN, an ON clause
with a Boolean-expression is
not allowed. This is by
deﬁnition, since a CROSS
JOIN cannot have an ON
clause. See Boolean
Expressions.

order-bylist

Column or columns to use to
sort the selected rows. You
can use the ORDER BY clause
to sort the selected rows. The
ORDER BY list contains 1 to 5
sort speciﬁcations of one of
the following formats,
separated by commas:
column-name [direction]

selected-column-number
[direction]
where column name is a
column selected in select-list.
selected-column-number is a
numeric reference (left to
right) to the column to use in
select-list. direction is the
direction to sort by: ASC
(ascending) or DESC
(descending). Default = ASC.

Joining Two Reports Example
This example uses MQL to join two reports. In this example,
report 2B0 contains information about products ordered, but
does not contain the sales commission. Report 1C0 contains
the sales commission. This SELECT statement joins the
reports so that the sales commission from report 1C0 is
combined with information from report 2B0.
The FROM statement deﬁnes tables with aliases, the WHERE
statement speciﬁes that the product types are equal, and
the ORDER BY statement sorts by status and product type.
Result -1 contains the syntax.
@BRK .
*========================
SELECT stcd,
statusdate,byin,t1.producttype,serialnumber,
salescommiss,ordernumbr,custcode,producplan,produ
cactual
FROM 2b0 t1,1c0 t2
WHERE t1.producttype = t2.producttype
ORDER BY stcd,producttype;
@brk rnm -1 .
@mql,0,a '',-1 dsp,-0 .

Creating a Report Example
This example uses SQL syntax to create a report and adds
blank tab lines. The report is an A type report. Variable v3
contains the report number. The column types are used
when you access the report via MQL. In addition, a title is
added to the report.
@BRK .
*========================
CREATE TABLE yourtable in a (
"ProductType" CHARACTER(9), "Subkey"
CHARACTER(5),
"ProducCost" NUMERIC(6), "Wholesale" NUMERIC(7),
"Retail" NUMERIC(8),
"SalesCommiss" NUMERIC(7.2),
"SpaceReq" NUMERIC(5),
"DemoQuantity" NUMERIC(8),
"DemoResults" NULL(15)
) ''Optional 1-43 Character Title'';
@brk rnm -1 .
@mql,0,a,99,,,,,,b '',-1 v1a4,v2i5,v3i5 .
@dsp,0,a,v3
@99:dsp,-0 .
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Using Aggregate Function Example
This example uses SQL syntax to execute Aggregate
Functions (AVG, MAX, MIN, and SUM) on NUMERIC and
CHARACTER data in a Business Information Server report
using a SELECT statement executed via the MQL statement.
This example uses the EMP table that is pulled from an
Oracle database and saved into a Business Information
Server report. The name of the report is EMP and that name
can be referenced as a table name via MQL, as the following
example shows.
@brk .
*=================
SELECT MAX(sal), MIN(sal), AVG(sal), SUM(sal),
MAX(DEPTNO), \
MAX(ENAME), MIN(ENAME) FROM EMP;
@brk rnm -1 .
@mql,0,i,100,,,,,,N '',-1 dsp,-0 .
@100:
@dsp,-0 .
When you execute the above script, the results are
displayed in a Business Information Server rid and appear as
follows: * . . . .[M. .
* . . . .AX. .
* . . . .(D. .
* . . . .EP. .
* . . . .TN. .
*[MAX(SAL).[MIN(SAL). . .O).[MAX(ENAME.[MIN(ENAME
*] .] .[AVG(SAL)] .[SUM(SAL)] .] .)] .)]
*=========.=========.=================
=.==================.==.==========.===
=======

| 5000.00| 800.00| 2073.21| 29025.00|30|WARD |ADAMS
..... END REPORT ....

Using ANSI SQL JOIN Syntax With MQL Example
This example uses ANSI SQL join syntax to demonstrate how
the syntax is used to join data in multiple Business
Information Server reports using a SELECT statement
executed via the MQL statement. This example uses the
EMP and DEPT tables that are pulled from an Oracle
database and saved into Business Information Server
reports. The names of the reports are EMP and DEPT,
respectively. Those names can be referenced as table
names via MQL, as the following example shows.
@brk .
*=================
SELECT * FROM emp RIGHT OUTER JOIN dept ON
emp.DEPTNO = dept.DEPTNO;
@brk rnm -1 .
@mql,0,i,100,,,,,,R '',-1 dsp,-0 .
@100:
@dsp,-0 .
When you execute the above script, the results are
displayed in a Business Information Server rid and appear as
follows: * . . . . . . .DE.DE. .
*EMPN. . . . . . .PT.PT. .
*O .ENAME .JOB .MGR .HIREDATE .SAL .COMM
.NO.NO.DNAME .LOC
*====.==========.=========.====.=======
==.=========.=========.==.==.==========
====.=============
|7369|SMITH |CLERK |7902|17-DEC-80| 800|
0|20|20|RESEARCH |DALLAS
|7499|ALLEN |SALESMAN |7698|20-FEB-81| 1600|
300|30|30|SALES |CHICAGO
|7521|WARD |SALESMAN |7698|22-FEB-81| 1250|

500|30|30|SALES |CHICAGO
|7566|JONES |MANAGER |7839|02-APR-81| 2975|

|7654|MARTIN |SALESMAN |7698|28-SEP-81| 1250|
