|7698|BLAKE |MANAGER |7839|01-MAY-81| 2850|
0|30|30|SALES |CHICAGO
|7782|CLARK |MANAGER |7839|09-JUN-81| 2450|
0|10|10|ACCOUNTING |NEW YORK
|7788|SCOTT |ANALYST |7566|19-APR-87| 3000|
0|20|20|RESEARCH |DALLAS
|7839|KING |PRESIDENT| 0|17-NOV-81| 5000|
0|10|10|ACCOUNTING |NEW YORK
|7844|TURNER |SALESMAN |7698|08-SEP-81| 1500|
0|30|30|SALES |CHICAGO
|7876|ADAMS |CLERK |7788|23-MAY-87| 1100|
0|20|20|RESEARCH |DALLAS
|7900|JAMES |CLERK |7698|03-DEC-81| 950| 0|30|30|SALES
|CHICAGO
|7902|FORD |ANALYST |7566|03-DEC-81| 3000|
0|20|20|RESEARCH |DALLAS
|7934|MILLER |CLERK |7782|23-JAN-82| 1300|
0|10|10|ACCOUNTING |NEW YORK
| 0| | | 0| | 0| 0| 0|40|OPERATIONS |BOSTON
..... END REPORT .....
The above result shows that the EMP table has been rightjoined with the DEPT table. Since the record in the DEPT
table where DEPTNO equals 40 did not have a match in the
EMP table, it was joined together with a null-extended row
and included in the result of the outer-join operation. The
RIGHT OUTER JOIN requires all records from the right table
(in this case, the DEPT table) to be returned. If they do not
have a matching record in the left table, then they are
joined together with a null-extended row.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

MSG (Message Waiting)
Use the Message Waiting (MSG) command to display a
message sent to your station or user-id.
The command displays the message as a result in the
cabinet and drawer it was sent from.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0, drawer
I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides
in cabinet 0, drawer I.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0, drawer
I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides
in cabinet 0, drawer I. If a message from an OS 2200 remote
site is an 80-character or less result, it resides in drawer A. If
you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides in
drawer A.
Ü Ü 2200
An incoming message is signaled with a beep. There may or
may not be a visual indicator, depending on the type of
terminal or terminal emulator you are using.

Message Waiting Control Line Format
MSG
If the message came from the local site, you can press the
Message key to display the message. If the message came
from a remote site, press Message to stop the beeping and
enter msg to display the message.

Message Waiting Guidelines
To delete a message from the queue, type ok on the control
line of the displayed message and transmit, then press the
Return function key.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
To display the message following the one displayed, request
the Message Waiting command again.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client 2200 Þ Þ
To display the message following the one displayed, ﬁrst
acknowledge the current message (type ok and press
Transmit), then request the Message Waiting command
again.
Ü Ü 2200

@MSG (Message to Console)
Use the Message to Console (MSG) statement to send a
message to the system console.
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The system console displays the message preceded by the
following identiﬁer: MAPPER-<sl> *nnn*
where nnn is the run user's station number, and <sl> is the
site letter.
The message is also recorded in the device log ﬁle.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
Windows Þ Þ
The message is recorded in the Windows application event
log, preceded by the following identiﬁer: MAPPER-<sl>
*nnn*
where nnn is the run user's station number, and <sl> is the
site letter.
Ü Ü Windows 2200 Þ Þ
The system console displays the message preceded by the
following identiﬁer: MAPPER*4101: MESSAGE FROM user-id
AT MAPPER STATION nnn: where user-id and nnn are the run
user's user-id and station number.
The message itself is displayed on the next line of the
console, preceded by the characters MAPPER*4101+.

Note: The system allows a maximum of three outstanding
messages at any given time. Additional messages are
queued until the previous outstanding messages are
answered.
Ü Ü 2200

Message to Console Statement Format
@MSG[,o] vld [vrsp] .

Field

Description

2200 Þ Þ

o

The N option suppresses the
ﬁrst line of the console
message to the operator's
console. (This line contains the
author's user-id and station
number.) The ﬁrst line of the
console message goes into the
system print ﬁle. If the vrsp
response ﬁeld is used, the N
option is ignored.

Ü Ü 2200

vld

Variables, constants, literals,
reserved words, or any
combination of these to
compose a message of up to
48 characters. If your literal
data includes spaces, enclose
the message in apostrophes.

2200 Þ Þ

vrsp

Ü Ü 2200

Variable to capture the
operator response (up to 60
characters). If you use this
ﬁeld, the run waits for a
response before continuing.

Message to Console Examples
This example produces a message at the console or log:
@msg 'Please reload rpt 2B0 from 891103 tape.' .
2200 Þ Þ
This example asks the operator whether to continue, and
loads <response> with the response: @msg 'Should my run
continue?' <response>h3 .
@if <response> EQ no gto end ; .
Ü Ü 2200

MULTI
Use the MULTI run to display two, three, or four charts on
the same screen or page. The charts are displayed or
plotted on the same screen or page. For each chart in the
group of charts, save its graphics primitive code as a report.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

MULTI Control Line Format
MULTI[,q,f,sn,psiz,transpcy?],c,d,r,c,d,r,
[,c,d,r,c,d,r]

Field

Description

q

Number of charts (2, 3, or 4)
to arrange on one screen.

f

Format number. See Chart
Layouts.
For two charts: 1, 2, or 3
For three charts: 1 or 2
For four charts: leave blank

sn

Printing device station
number. Fill in this ﬁeld only if
you want to print the chart.

psiz

Paper size. Y = 11 x 17
inches. Default = N (8 1/2 x
11 inches). Enter only if you
entered a printing device
station number.

transpcy? Are you making
transparencies? Y or N.
Default = N. Enter only if you
entered a printing device
station number.

c,d,r

Report containing the
graphics primitive code.
Specify at least two reports,
but no more than four.

MULTI Guidelines
Due to size limitations, use the fewest number of characters
possible for text on charts.
For best results, use a black background for your charts; the
MULTI command ignores all background colors but black.

Chart Layouts
Following are layout selections for the format ﬁeld.

@NAMDMP (Namelist Dump)
Use the @NAMDMP (Namelist Dump) statement to create a
result showing the contents of a pre-deﬁned Namelist in a
readable form.

Format
@NAMDMP,ic,id,ir,rc,rd[,fmt]

Outcome
The statement creates a new result in the receiving drawer
that shows the issuing Namelist's contents:
Deﬁnition Report that deﬁnes the headers
Drawers and Reports that comprise the body of the
Namelist
The fmt parameter allows the list to be built vertically for
readability, or horizontally for compactness.

Note: Either format can be used as input to @NAMLST.

Namelist Dump Options
@NAMDMP,ic,id,ir,rc,rd[,fmt]

Option

Description

ic, id, ir

Namelist Result or
directory name.

rc, rd

Output (receive)
drawer.

fmt

Format option, V
(vertical) or H
(horizontal).

Namelist Dump Example
Example 1
This example creates a result showing the contents of the
System Data Directory name 'MYNAMELIST' in drawer 'A'.
@NAMDMP,'MYNAMELIST',0,A Result .DATE
.MYNAMELIST
* NAME .MODE.T.RID .RANG.
*================.====.=.====.====.
|MYNAMELIST 0 A 14 LIST
+2b0-7b0 2c0-20c0 72b2 80b2-89b2
Outcome The Namelist Deﬁnition Report is 14A and
the body of the namelist includes reports in diﬀerent
drawers, B0 and C0, and one in B2.
This is the default horizontal representation, useful for its
compactness.

Example 2
This example creates a result showing the same content,
but from an ad-hoc namelist contained in result -3.
@NAMDMP,-3,0,A,V
Result .DATE
.
* NAME .MODE.T.RID .RANG.
*================.====.=.====.====.
| 0 A 14 LIST
+ 2B0 - 7B0
+ 2C0 - 20C0
+ 72B2
+ 80B2 - 89B2
Outcome The same Namelist Deﬁnition Report as in
Example 1, but there is no name associated with it.

Namelist Dump Format
These formats are compatible with the System Data
Directory so that the output from @NAMDMP can be used as
input to the @NAMLST Run Statement. The following column
positions are used:

1-1

A tab code.

2-16

The Name of the
Namelist.

19-4

Cabinet number of
Deﬁnition Report.

24-1

Drawer letter of
Deﬁnition Report.

26-4

Report number of
Deﬁnition Report.

31-4

The letters LIST to
identify a Namelist.

NAME
Use the Name command to create a data name for a
cabinet, drawer, or report. You can use data names in place
of cabinet numbers, drawer letters, and report numbers to
make it easier to identify your data.
You can also use the Name command to change or delete an
existing name.
You can change or remove the cabinet, drawer, or report
name only if you originally named it.
The data name you deﬁne can also be a Namelist, which
lets your data set span drawers, cabinets, or discontinuous
lists of reports.

NAME Control Line Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
NAME [nm,c,d,r,dept,user-id,act,desc]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
NAME [nm,c,d,r,dept,user-id,func,upd?,namelist?]
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

nm

Name you wish to use, up to
16 characters long, beginning
with a letter (A - Z). The
system uses A through I for
drawers, so you cannot specify
a single character name of A
through I.
Use more than one character
for the name. You cannot use a
caret (^), semicolon (;), slant
(/), comma (,), or space.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

nm

Name you wish to use, up to
16 characters long, beginning
with a letter (A - Z). The
system uses A through I for
drawers, so you cannot specify
a single character name of A
through I.
The command stores only
alphanumeric characters (A to
Z and 0 to 9). It ignores all
other characters.

Ü Ü 2200

c

Number of the cabinet to
name.

d

Letter of the drawer to name.
Leave this ﬁeld blank when
naming a cabinet.

r

Number of the report or range
of reports to name. To name a
range of reports, enter the ﬁrst

and last report numbers,
separated by a hyphen.
For example, 8-33 speciﬁes
reports 8 through 33. Leave
this ﬁeld blank when naming a
cabinet or drawer.

dept

Number of the department
that can use the name, or all,
for all departments. Default =
your department.

user-id

User-id of whoever can use the
name, or all, for all user-ids.
Default = your user-id.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

act

Action. Must be one of the
following:
C ¾ change a name.
D ¾ display the directory
information about an existing
name.
I ¾ insert a new name into the
system directory.
Q ¾ quit the NAME routine
and return to the active
screen.

R ¾ remove a name (leave the
cabinet, drawer, and report
ﬁelds blank).

desc

Brief description (25
characters maximum) of the
named cabinet, drawer, or
report.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

func

Action. Default = ADD. Must
be one of the following:
ADD ¾ add a new name into
the system directory.
CHG ¾ change a name
(cabinet, drawer, or report
deﬁnition only).
DEL ¾ delete a name (leave
the cabinet, drawer, and
report ﬁelds blank).

upd?

Update names in the system
directory? Y or N. Default = Y.
If you are naming several
reports, it is more eﬃcient to
enter n for all but the last
report. You must enter y for

the last report to update all
the reports you named
previously.

Namelist? Create a Namelist? Y or N;
default = N.
If Y, c,d,r is taken to be the
Namelist Deﬁnition Report.
NAME will display a screen
where you will be able to
enter the information for the
body of the namelist.

Ü Ü 2200

Name Examples
This example assigns the name corpstat to report 2B0 for
your user-id and department:
name corpstat,0,b,2
You can now access report 2B0 by any of the following
names: corpstat, 2B0, or 2B (if you are signed on in cabinet
0).
This example assigns the name newuser to cabinet 0 for all
departments and all users:
name newuser,0,,,all,all

NAMELIST
Use the NAMELIST command to deﬁne your current
session’s Active Namelist. You can then use Search, Locate,
or other functions to process your namelist data by using
NAMELIST as if it were a data name.

Format
NAMELIST,c,d,r
This will take c,d,r as the Namelist Deﬁnition Report and will
then display a screen where you will be able to enter the
information for the body of the namelist.

NAMES
Use the Names command to display the data names
currently registered in the system directory.
The Names command is especially useful for seeing the
data names that are deﬁned and for verifying changes you
made to the system directory.
Use the following format:
NAMES

@NAMLST (Namelist)
Use the @NAMLST (Namelist) Run Statement to deﬁne an
ad-hoc Namelist. For more information about Namelist, see
Namelist.

Format
@NAMLST,c,d,r[,[opt],lab] vld This generates a
binary Namelist Result that can be used as input
to various functions that support Namelists.

Normal Outcome
Input c,d,r become the Namelist Deﬁnition Report (must be
a permanent report); vld identiﬁes the reports in the body of
the Namelist.
I-Option The I-Option allows input from a report or
result rather than from vld. The input c,d,r provide all
information for the Namelist. Even though empty, vld
must still be present.

Namelist Options
@NAMLST,c,d,r,I[opt][,lab] ''

Option

Description

Make the
output of
this function
the Unit's
Active
Namelist so
that it's
available to
at directly to
manual
users.

C

Clear the
Active
Namelist.

I

Input from a
report.

@NET (Network Sign-On)
Use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to sign on to a
remote site.

Network Sign-On Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

] \
[nertxt,nercod] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

[domain\]rmu,rmd,rmpw['/'newrmpw],trnrpt,mtr?,lab
,con?,slrq] \
[nertxt,nercod] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

net-id

Network identiﬁer of the
remote site. This ﬁeld
corresponds to the Network
Name ﬁeld in the Network
Conﬁguration Report.

site-id

Site letter of the remote
site. Default = ﬁrst site
conﬁgured for the speciﬁed
network identiﬁer. (For a list

of sites, press Remote
from the active screen.)
This ﬁeld corresponds to
the sl ﬁeld in the Network
Conﬁguration Report.

domain

An optional domain name
used to qualify the user-ID.

rmu

User-id registered on the
remote site. Default = userid of the user executing the
run.

rmd

User department number
for the remote sign-on.
Default = department
number of the user
executing the run.

rmpw/newrmpw User-id password for the
remote sign-on. Default =

executing the run. If
Network Sign-On Encryption
is enabled, you can specify

However, if Network SignOn Encryption is not

enabled, this ﬁeld cannot
specify a new password.

2200 Þ Þ

If a remote department (rmd ﬁeld) is
speciﬁed without specifying a remote
user or remote password (rmu and rmpw
ﬁelds respectively), Business Information
Server attempts to read the user
registration report for the department
number speciﬁed in the rmd ﬁeld. If the
user executing the NET statement does
not exist in that department, the rmu,
rmd, and rmpw values passed to the
remote site are as follows:

rmu = user executing the NET
statement
rmd = value speciﬁed in the rmd ﬁeld
rmpw = null
If the user-id in the rmd department on
the remote site has a password, the NET
statement errors because the rmpw
value and the user registration password
ﬁeld contents do not match.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

The password value sent in the rmpw
ﬁeld matches the password for the user
executing the NET statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

trnrpt

Report that contains a
translation table used to
translate characters between
the local and remote sites. The
translation reports must be in
drawer F of the user's
language cabinet. Default =
no translation is used. The
following network statements
use this translation report:
Network Read (NRD), Network
Write (NWR), Network Run
(NRN), and reports returned
through Network Return (NRT).

2200 Þ Þ

mtr?

Ü Ü 2200

Monitor the transferred data
on a terminal screen? Y or N.
Default = N.

lab

Label to go to if the
connection cannot be
established.

con?

Keep the network connection
open after the Network Return
(NRT) terminates the Network
Remote (NRM) session? Y or
N. Default = N. This feature
must also exist on the remote
site. If the remote site does
not support this feature, this
ﬁeld is ignored. After the NRM
session has been terminated,
the run can determine if the
session remained open by
testing the NETOUT$ reserved
word.

slrq

The security level requested
for this connection. If no slrq
value is speciﬁed, then the
system-deﬁned security level
is used for the connection. If
an slrq value is speciﬁed, it
must be at least as strict as
the system-deﬁned security
level, or else the systemdeﬁned security level is used
instead.
Allowable values are:

NN = Logon and data
encryption are not
requested.
LN = Logon encryption is
requested.
LY = Logon encryption is
required.
DN = Logon and data
encryption are requested.
DY = Logon and data
encryption are required.

nertxt

Error text provided by the
remote site if the connection
cannot be established. This
consists of the actual error
message in columns 1
through 80 and the 6-digit
error message-id in columns
82 through 87. This ﬁeld is
only returned for Network
Sign-On Encryption attempts.

nercod

A 2-digit network error code
set if the connection cannot
be established.
Network Error Codes:

1 Site does not exist
2 Site is down
3 Encryption error
4 Invalid userid/dept/password
5 Password change required
6 Invalid characters or pattern
in new password
7 User-id has been disabled
8 Internal error or validation
server is down
9 INTER-RUN not registered
for this user-id
10 Maximum sessions already
active
11 Invalid command code
12 Password may not be
changed
13 Terminal conﬁguration
error
14 User conﬁguration error
15 Could not negotiate
common protocol
16 Network conﬁguration
error

Network Sign-On Reserved Words
2200 Þ Þ
If a label is speciﬁed on the NET statement and an error
occurs, STAT1$ contains the following status codes.

Field

Description

0

Successful NET.

1

Remote site does not
recognize the user-id,
department number,
password, or the user is not
registered for the Inter-Run. If
using a workstation, the user
is not registered for the WS
run.

2

Cannot ﬁnd speciﬁed networkid in Network Conﬁguration
Report.

3

Cannot access the Network
Conﬁguration Report.

4

Remote site does not respond.

5

Remote site responded with
unexpected data.

Network Translation Report is
not correct.

7

Remote site does not support
remote message queuing.

8

Could not negotiate a common
protocol.

9

Network conﬁguration report
in error.

Ü Ü 2200

Network Sign-On Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
The run user must be registered to execute the INTERRUN run on the remote system (the server).
If the server is a Business Information Server for
ClearPath OS 2200, the run user must be registered to
execute the INTER-RUN run.
Contact the administrator to verify all of these access
requirements.
You must specify either the network identiﬁer or the site
identiﬁer. If you do not specify the site identiﬁer, the
software defaults to the ﬁrst site conﬁgured for the
speciﬁed network identiﬁer.
If you specify only the site identiﬁer, the software reads
the Network Conﬁguration report for the correct network
identiﬁer. Contact your administrator for the network
and site identiﬁers conﬁgured on your system.

Network Sign-On Example
This example signs on to the remote host SYS15, MAPPER
site K:
@net,SYS15,k,newuser,7,newuserpassword .

@NOF (Network Oﬀ)
Use the Network Oﬀ (NOF) statement to sign oﬀ the remote
site after executing other network statements, such as
Network Read (NRD) and Network Write (NWR). The NOF
statement closes the communications connection with the
remote host.
You must use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to
connect to the remote site.

Network Oﬀ Statement Format
@NOF .

@NRD (Network Read)
Use the Network Read (NRD) statement to read a report
from the remote site.
You must use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to
connect to the remote site.

Network Read Statement Format
@NRD,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab vmsg] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report on the remote
site. If you specify a result,
you must also specify the
cabinet and drawer (for
example, 0,b,-2). Use the
cabinet number, drawer letter,
and report number. Cabinet,
drawer, and report names are
not allowed.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report on the local
site. If you leave the rr
subﬁeld blank or enter 0, the
system returns the issuing
report as a -0 result. Use the
cabinet number, drawer letter,
and report number. Cabinet,
drawer, and report names are
not allowed.

lab

Label to go to if the remote
site returns an error.

vmsg

Variable to contain an 80character system message if
control goes to the label.

Network Read Example
This example reads report 2B0 on the remote site and
places the data into report 5B0 on the local site. In case of
an error, the run goes to label 99.
@nrd,0,b,2,0,b,5,099 <error>s80 .

@NRM (Network Remote)
Use the Network Remote (NRM) statement to execute
manual functions and runs on a remote site.
With the NRM statement, since the terminal remains under
local control, the screen and keyboard retain the look and
feel of the local site regardless of the kind of system to
which the user is connected. This enables you to design
distributed applications that provide users with a way to
process data on diﬀerent systems transparently.
The run containing the NRM statement remains active while
the command executes at the remote site.
If a Reserved Station Number agreement is conﬁgured for
the receiving site, the receiving session has the same
station number as the initiating session. See your
administrator for more information.
You must use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to
connect to the remote site.
RDB is turned oﬀ before starting the run on the remote
Business Information Server site. If you want to continue
RDB into the remote run, it is possible to start RDB in the
remote run. It will be necessary to restart RDB, if you want
to continue RDB when returning from the remote run
initiated by NRM.

Network Remote Statement Format
@NRM[,c,d] "cmd" .

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer at the
local site in which to place the
report returned by an NRT
statement. Default = cabinet
and drawer speciﬁed in the
NRT statement on the remote
system.

2200 Þ Þ

"cmd"

Run name or manual function
call to execute at the remote
site, including input
parameters to be sent to the
run or manual function.
Enclose the request in
quotation marks ("). The
length of the request cannot
exceed 255 characters.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

"cmd"

Run name or manual function
call to execute at the remote
site, including input
parameters to be sent to the
run or manual function.
Enclose the request in
quotation marks ("). The
length of the request cannot
exceed 998 characters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Network Remote Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
To return to the local site, the run user can type two
release characters (^^) and press Transmit.
If the NRM statement started a run, you can have the
remote run execute a Network Return (NRT) statement
to return to the local site.
To maintain the transparency of a run executing at a
remote site, be sure to include error and abort routines
in the remote run.
The routine should return the run user to the local site
via an NRT statement in case of an error.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Note: Do not place other statements on the same line after
the NRM statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new line.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Network Remote Examples
This example signs on to the remote host SYS15, MAPPER
site K, then uses the NRM statement to execute the ABC run
on site K:
@net,SYS15,k,newuser,7,newuser .
@nrm "abc" .
This example in a run at the remote site returns the run user
to the local site and returns report 2B0:
@nrt,0,b,2 .

@NRN (Network Run)
Use the Network Run (NRN) statement to pass statements
to the remote site for execution.
You must use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to
connect to the remote site.

Network Run Statement Format
@NRN[,lab] "run statements" [vmsg] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error
occurs on the remote
system.

2200 Þ Þ

"run
Statements to be executed
statements" on the remote site. Enclose
the statements in quotation
marks ("). Include the at
sign (@) as the ﬁrst
character of the statement.
You can pass up to 1280
characters in the
statements. For more
information, see Network
Run Outcome and Network
Run Guidelines.
Since the local system
translates variables within
quotation marks before
sending them to the remote
system, use the Load

Variable (LDV) statement
with the P option to pack
the variables (remove
signiﬁcant spaces) before
using them in an NRN
statement.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

"run
Statements to be executed
statements" on the remote site. Enclose
the statements in quotation
marks ("). Include the at
sign (@) as the ﬁrst
character of the statement.
You can pass up to 998
characters in the
statements. For more
information, see Network
Run Outcome and Network
Run Guidelines.
Note: If the server system
does not support 998
characters, the statement is
truncated.
Since the local system
translates variables within
quotation marks before
sending them to the remote
system, use the Load

Variable (LDV) statement
with the P option to pack
the variables (remove
signiﬁcant spaces) before
using them in an NRN
statement.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

vmsg

Variable to contain an 80character system message if
control goes to the label.

Note: The system message
variable may need to be
much larger (up to 900
characters) if the run
statement speciﬁes an
@CALL to a JavaScript
subroutine. JavaScript
messages can be very large
and contain line feed
characters.

Note: If the NET statement speciﬁes a network translation
table, the "run statements" ﬁeld of the NRN statement is
translated. For more information on the translation report,
see @NET (Network Sign-On).

Network Run Outcome
The remote system executes the statements you passed.

Network Run Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Variable and reserved word references in the statements
are passed to the remote environment. This is true even
if they are enclosed in apostrophes ('). Apostrophes are
passed as literal data. Example 4 shows how to specify
variable and reserved word references that you want to
resolve only in the remote environment.
The statements you specify are executed inside a
special system run that was started when you speciﬁed
the NET statement. It is a separate run environment
from your local run. Variables you create in the remote
environment cannot be directly referenced by your run.
You cannot use labels in the statements you are
passing. In the following example, the statement is not
valid:
@lzr,0,b,2,199 .
To retrieve the values of variables from the remote
system, you must place them in the remote output area,
turn it into a result, and pass the result back to the local
system. See Network Run Examples.
To process the results generated from an NRN
statement, rename the results.
You cannot use the NRN statement to execute
statements that terminate control of the INTER_RUN
run. For example, the RUN and GTO RPX statements
terminate the INTER_RUN run.

If you use the NRN statement to execute statements
that load variables, conﬂicts may occur in the INTERRUN run that processes NRN requests. Do not use
variables v1 through v15 in the NRN statement because
they are reserved for the INTER-RUN run.

Network Run Examples
Example 1
This example performs a TOT statement in the remote
system and renames the result for further processing:
@nrn,0099 "@tot,0,c,1 '' 18-6,25-7,65-8 |,+,-,\
= rnm -2" <error>s80 .
Example 2
This example performs an SRH statement in the remote
system and renames the result -1 for further processing.
Note that DATE6$ is enclosed in apostrophes (') so when the
system substitutes the actual date in the format MM/DD/YY,
the embedded slants (/) do not interfere with the parsing of
the data by the remote system.
@nrn,0099 "@srh,0,a,3 dh 2-8 |,'date6$' rnm -1"
v100s80 .
You can then use a Network Read (NRD) statement to read
the result into a report on the local system.
Example 3
This example retrieves values of variables from the remote
system.
@ldv v1a5='v10i6',v2a3='v10' .
@nrn,0099 "@brk,0,a srh,0,b,2 'dh' 'custcode'
|,amco v1 rnm -2" .
@nrn,0099 "v2" . put value loaded by SRH into
remote output area
@nrn,0099 "@brk" .

@brk,0,a .
@brk .
@nrd,0,a,-0,0,a,-0,0099 . read result back to
local system
@rdl,-0,2 1-6 v10i6 . read value from returned
result
Example 4
This example shows how to specify a variable reference that
is to be satisﬁed in the remote environment.
@ldv,p <vlineinit>h12='<line>i6'
<vline>h12='<line>' .
@ldv,p <vdatainit>h12='<data>s80',
<vdata>h12='<data>' .
@nrn,0099 "@fnd,0,b,2 '' 2-2 |,IP ,<vlineinit>"
<msg>s80 .
@nrn,0099 "@rdl,0,b,2,<vline> 1-80 <vdatainit>
brk,0,b" <msg>s80 .
@nrn,0099 "<vdata>" <msg>s80 .
@nrn,0099 "@brk" <msg>s80 .
@nrd,0,b,-0,0,b,-0,0099 <msg>s80 .
The LDV statements load local variables with the names of
variables that will be created and referenced in the remote
environment. The FDR statement initializes and loads
variable <line> with the line number where a ﬁnd was made
in a report on the remote system. The RLN statement then
references <line> to read the item and load it into variable
<data> in the remote environment. The variable <data> is
then placed into the output area in the remote environment.
A BRK is done, and -0 read back to the local system using
the NRD statement.
Example 5

This example executes a CALL statement to a named
JavaScript subroutine on a remote Business Information
Server system.
The NRN statements must be within double quotes.
Therefore, the CALL statement must ﬁrst load the double
quotes into a variable that does not interfere with the
interpretation of the NRN statement.
@LDV,p <jvsnam>s10='"JVS_Name"' .
@NRN,0199 "@CALL,<jvsnam> JVS_Function()"
<jvsmsg>sMAXCHR$ .
Since JavaScript error messages can be very large, the error
message variable is now set to MAXCHR$ characters. To
process this large message, do the following:
@0199 CALL,LIBDRW$,11 0001 (<jvsmsg>,'1',<sta>i3)
.
Example 6
This example executes operating system commands on
remote systems.
To execute an @NRN of an OS statement on a remote
system (Windows), tic$ markers are necessary around the
options. Tic$ markers are also necessary around any blanks
within the statement submitted to the remote operating
system command prompt.
@ net,<net-id>,<site-id>,<user-id>,<dpt>,<pswd>
@ ldv,p <cmd>s62=\
'@os,0,f,user,passwd 'tic$'-f -d'tic$' dir'tic$'
'tic$'c:\temp . ' .
@ nrn,0001 "<cmd>" <msg1>s80 .
@ nrd,0,f,-0,0,f,-0,0001 <msg2>s80 .

@ dsp,-0 .
@ nof .
@ gto end .
@0001:nof .
@ brk .
Error in Network run:
<msg1>
Error in Network read:
<msg2>
@ brk dsp,-0 .
Example 7
This example executes operating system commands on
remote systems.
To execute an @NRN of a UNX statement on a remote
system (Linux or UNIX), tic$ markers are necessary around
any blanks within the command submitted to the remote
operating system shell.
@ net,<net-id>,<site-id>,<user-id>,<dpt>,<pswd>
@ ldv,p <cmd>s62='@unx,0,f,usr,password -f
ls'tic$' 'tic$'/work/user/' .
@ nrn,0001 "<cmd>" <msg1>s80 .
@ nrd,0,f,-0,0,f,-0,0001 <msg2>s80 .
@ dsp,-0 .
@ nof .
@ gto end .
@0001:nof .
@ brk .
Error in Network run:
<msg1>
Error in Network read:
<msg2>
@ brk dsp,-0 .

@NRT (Network Return)
Use the Network Return (NRT) statement to close the
connection and return to a local site from the remote site.
The connection may remain open if Y is speciﬁed in the con?
subﬁeld of the Network Sign-on (NET) statement.
You can also return a report to a local site with this
statement.
You must use the Network Remote (NRM) statement to start
a run or manual function at the remote site.

Network Return Statement Format
@NRT[,c,d,r] {"cmd"|vld,...,vld} .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to return to the local
site. Use the cabinet number,
drawer letter, and report
number. Cabinet, drawer, and
report names are not allowed.

"cmd"

Run name or manual function
call to execute at the local
site, including input
parameters to be sent to the
run or manual function.
Enclose the request in
quotation marks ("). The local
run will be terminated before
cmd is executed.

vld,...,vld

Variables, literal data,
reserved words, constants or
any combination of these to
return to the local run. The
returned values are captured
by the local run using the
input$ reserved word.

Network Return Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
You can specify both a report and a command. After the
system returns the report to the local site, it executes
the command.
Use the NRT statement in an error routine of a remote
run to maintain the transparency of executing a run at a
remote site. The NRT statement returns the run user to
the local site in case of an error.

Note: Do not place other statements on the same line after
the NRT statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new line.

Network Return Example 1
A run named ABC was executed from another site. The last
line of run ABC includes an NRT statement to return control
to the local site. Once control is returned to the local site,
the run that executed ABC via an NRM statement continues
executing at the next line:
@nrt .
This example passes variable data to the issuing run.
@net,mmsxxx,a,newuser,2,xyz .
@nrm "runvar" .
@chg input$ v6i4,v7i4,v8i4,v9i4 .
v6/v7/v8/v9 .
@gto end .
Here is the remote run named RUNVAR:
@ldv v1i4=9999,v2i4=8888,v3i4=7777,v4i4=1111 .
@nrt v1,v2,v3,v4 .
@gto end .
The data from the remote run appears in the variables v6,
v7, v8, and v9 after the NRM statement. Notice the
receiving variables in the issuing run correspond to the
position of the variables on the NRT statement.

Network Return Example 2
This example returns a report to your local system by way of
the NRT statement.
@nrt,-0 .

@NWR (Network Write)
Use the Network Write (NWR) statement to copy a local
report to the remote site.
You must use the Network Sign-On (NET) statement to
connect to the remote site.

Network Write Statement Format
@NWR,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,lab vmsg] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report on the local
site. Use the cabinet
number, drawer letter, and
report number. Cabinet,
drawer, and report names
are not allowed.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report on the
remote site. If you leave the
rr subﬁeld blank or enter 0,
the system duplicates the
report within the drawer.
STAT2$ contains the new
report number. Use the
cabinet number, drawer
letter, and report number;
cabinet, drawer, and report
names are not allowed.

lab

Label to go to if the remote
site returns an error.

vmsg

Variable to contain an 80-

character system message if
control goes to the label.

Entries for rr Subﬁeld
Entry

Description

0

Duplicates the report within
the drawer. STAT2$ contains
the new report number.

Receiving
report
number
(positive)

Replaces the receiving
report with the issuing
report.

-0 through
-16

Creates a renamed result
that you can use with the
Network Run (NRN)
statement.

Network Write Examples
This example writes report 2B0 from the local system, to
report 3B0 on the remote system. If the remote system
returns an error, the run continues at label 99:
@nwr,0,b,2,0,b,3,099 <error>s80 .
This example sends report 1C0 to the remote system and
renames it -1 in cabinet 0, drawer C. The example uses the
NRN statement to execute a Match (MCH) statement
between the renamed result and report 1D0, and renames
the result -2. It then sends the -2 result back to the local
system via the Network Read (NRD) statement and displays
the result: @nwr,0,c,1,0,c,-1,099 v1s80 .
@nrn,099 "@mch,0,d,1,-1 '' 12-9,31-8 ,1,\
a 2-9,33-8 ,1,a rnm -2" v2s80 .
@nrd,0,c,-2,0,c,,099 v3s80 .
@dsp,-0 .

OK and @OK (Acknowledge Message)
Use the Acknowledge Message (OK) function or OK
statement to acknowledge a message sent to your station
or user-id. You can optionally return a response to the
sender.
The command acknowledges the message and removes it
from the message queue. If the sender requested an
acknowledgment, the message is transmitted back to the
sending station.
A message must be on display. See MSG (Message Waiting).

Acknowledge Message Control Line Format
OK [response]
where response is a response of up to 70 characters to be
sent to the user who sent you the message.

Acknowledge Message Statement Format
@OK[,lab,vrsp] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if no message is
queued to your station.

vrsp

Variable containing a response
to return to the sender.

Acknowledge Message Examples
This example acknowledges an outstanding message and
continues the run at the next statement (if there are no
queued messages, goes to label 3): @ok,003
As an example of how you might use an OK statement in a
run, suppose your terminal beeps and you start a run that
saves the message in a report.
First the run executes a WAT statement with the M option to
suspend the run while the system passes information to it
that a message is waiting. If you started this run without a
message waiting, the run waits for 1000 milliseconds,
expecting the message, but then continues at the next line;
otherwise, the run continues at the label, where it
acknowledges the message and saves it in a report. Then
the run displays information about the saved message. Here
is the run: @65:wat,m,60 1000 .
@brk .
No message was received.
@gto end .
@60:ok dup,-0 ldv,pw v1i4=cab$,v2H1=adrw$,v3i4=rpt$ .
@brk .
The message is in report v3v2v1.
@brk out,-0,2,5,1,1,y .

OS and @OS (Operating System
Interface)
Windows Server Windows Client Þ Þ
Use the Operating System Interface (OS) function and OS
statement to execute native commands and programs on
the host system.

Operating System Interface Control Line
Format
OS [o cmd]

Field

Description

o

Options ﬁeld (see Operating
System Interface Options).
Enter options in lowercase
characters.

cmd

Command to be performed.
The system performs standard
path searching to ﬁnd the
command. The system cannot
determine whether the
operating system syntax
speciﬁed in this ﬁeld is valid or
if it encountered an error
during execution. You need to
analyze the generated output
to determine whether the
execution was successful.

Operating System Interface Statement Format
Windows Þ Þ
@OS[,c,d,user,psw,lab] o '[cmd]' .
Ü Ü Windows

Field

Description

c,d

Drawer into which the result is
to be returned. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

Windows Þ Þ

user

Windows Server user login.

psw

Windows Server user
password.

Note: A remote user is required to provide a user-id and
password.
Ü Ü Windows

lab

Label to go to if the command
fails.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Operating
System Interface Options).
Enter options in lowercase
characters. If you do not
specify options, you must
designate the options ﬁeld
with two apostrophes (‘’).

cmd

Command to be performed.
The system performs standard
path searching to ﬁnd the
command.
Enclose commands containing
embedded spaces in
apostrophes ('). If you do not
specify a command, designate
its position with two
apostrophes ('').

Operating System Interface Options
Option

Description

-d

Executes the command using
the CMD.EXE shell program.
You must use this option to
specify any command that is
built into the CMD.EXE shell
(for example, DIR).

-f

The -f option is always applied
to the OS statement and the
OS command. It causes the
output of the command to be
returned as a result. If you
enter a ﬁle name immediately
following the -f option, the
Operating System Interface
command places a copy of the
output from the command into
that ﬁle.

Operating System Interface Guidelines
Any command executed using the OS command must work
correctly as a background program. There is no interactive
connection to the command.
If you use both options, make sure a hyphen precedes each
option and that a space separates them. Do not combine
both options on one hyphen.

Operating System Interface Examples
This example obtains a directory listing and retrieves the
output as a result. The equivalent statement also places the
directory listing in a result in cabinet 0, drawer B (0,b).
os -d -f dir c:\
Equivalent Statement
@os,0,b '-d -f' 'dir c:\' .

-d

Uses the CMD.EXE shell
interface to access the DIR
command.

-f

Places the output in a
temporary ﬁle.

c:\

Executes the DIR command in
the root directory of the C
drive.

Ü Ü Windows Server Windows Client

OTA (Octal to Alpha)
Use the Octal to Alpha (OTA) command to convert an octal
drawer number to the corresponding cabinet number and
drawer letter. Use this format:
OTA octal-number

@OUM (Output Mask)
Use the Output Mask (OUM) statement to display a blank
function mask containing the report headings for a speciﬁed
drawer. In the mask, the run user can enter options and
parameters, which your run can capture.
The OUM statement displays the function mask and
suspends the run until the user transmits. The run then
continues, loading the variables speciﬁed with INMSV$ with
information about the user's entries in the function mask.

Output Mask Statement Format
@OUM,ic,id[,ir,if,rc,rd,rr,rf,title] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

For a single mask: report that
contains the headings for the
mask. For a double mask:
issuing report. Default =
report 0.

if

For a single mask: format in
which to display the mask. For
a double mask: format of the
issuing report. Default = basic
format.

rc,rd,rr

For a double mask: receiving
report. Default = report 0.

rf

For a double mask: format of
the receiving report mask.
Default = basic format.

title

Title (up to 12 characters) to
be displayed above the mask.

Output Mask Guideline
The screen display contains a function key bar. You can
customize the function key bar so that you can include
operations not available with the standard function key bar.
See @FKY (Function Key) for more information.

Output Mask Reserved Words
INMSV$ captures user input from the function mask screen.
Use these guidelines:
Use INMSV$ only with an OUM statement.
Place INMSV$ before the variable names in the Change
Variable (CHG) statement.
Place the CHG statement before the OUM statement.
Use string (type S) variables. You can use alphanumeric
(type A) or Hollerith (type H) if the data ﬁts in them.

Output Mask Variables
Use the blank function mask to load run parameter data
into a variable. For a single mask, an OUM statement
returns the option line and up to four decoded
parameter lines. For a double mask, it returns the option
line and up to two decoded parameter lines.
The ﬁrst variable initialized by INMSV$ contains the
entire option line. In a single mask, you can capture up
to four parameter lines. In a double mask, you can
capture the two parameter lines allowed (one each for
the issuing and receiving masks). Each parameter line
requires three variables:
The ﬁrst variable contains the starting column
numbers of the ﬁelds where the run user entered
parameters. Each starting column designation in the
variable is three characters long, right-justiﬁed, and
ﬁlled with zeros. The three-character designations
for all starting columns are packed to the left in the
variable. For example, parameter entries starting in
columns 2 and 45 in the mask produce a variable
containing 002045.
Whenever you use a format other than basic, the
column numbers are those deﬁned in report 0 for
the format. For example, if format 1 displays a ﬁeld
that actually starts in column 126, the variable
contains 126 for the starting column number, not
the column number where the ﬁeld was displayed
on the screen.
Use a 120-character variable to hold the maximum
possible, 40 ﬁelds. The system ﬁlls unused

character positions with blanks.
The second variable contains the corresponding
ﬁeld sizes, three characters each, packed to the left
in the variable.
The third variable contains the entire parameter line
expanded according to the format of the mask.
The run stalls until the user presses Transmit, then
it continues with the data in the speciﬁed variables.

Note: To use ﬁeld names rather than column positions in
your run, use the ﬁrst and second parameter line variables
with the Load Field Name (LFN) statement.

Using a Single Mask Example
This example displays a single function mask:
@chg inmsv$
v1s80,v2s120,v3s120,v4s132,v5s120,v6s120,v7s132 .
@oum,0,d,1,1,,,,,'TEST' .

@chg
inmsv$

Load INMSV$ with variables.

v1s80

Load v1 with the option line.

v2s120

Load v2 with the ﬁrst
parameter line ﬁeld starting
columns.

v3s120

Load v3 with the ﬁrst
parameter line ﬁeld sizes.

v4s132

Load v4 with data the run
user entered in the ﬁrst
parameter line.

v5s120

Load v5 with the second
parameter line ﬁeld starting
columns.

v6s120

Load v6 with the second
parameter line ﬁeld sizes.

v7s132

Load v7 with data the run
user entered in the second
parameter line.

oum,0,d,1,1 Display a blank function
mask derived from report
1D0, format 1, to receive
user input.

'TEST'

Display the title TEST above
the mask.

Extracting Parameters Example
This example captures the parameters entered from a
function mask displayed by an OUM statement: 1. @ ldv
v10i3=1 .
2. @ def,c v11i3,v2 .
3. @001:ldv v12i3=v2(v10-3) .
4. @ ldv v13i3=v3(v10-3) .
5. @ ldv v14s18=v4(v12-v13) .
.
. Save variables here so loop
. can capture more information.
.
6. @ INC,3 v10 .
7. @ IF V10 LT V11 GTO 001 ; .
Explanation

1. Initialize the column-character position.
2. Determine the number of ﬁelds selected based on the
number of characters in v2.

3. Obtain the starting column number.
4. Obtain the ﬁeld size.
5. Obtain the ﬁeld from the parameter line.
6. Increase the column-character position by 3.
7. Loop back if there are more columns.
You now have all the option, column-character positions,
and parameter entries in variables. Use these variables in

statements such as Sort (SOR), Search (SRH), and Totalize
(TOT), depending on the kinds of parameters the run user
enters in the mask. An SOR statement does not get far, for
example, if your variables contain search parameters.

Using a Double Mask Example
This example displays a double function mask:
@chg inmsv$
v1s80,v2s120,v3s120,v4s132,v5s120,v6s120,v7s132 .
@oum,0,b,,,0,c .

v1s80

Loads v1 with the option
line.

v2s120

Loads v2 with the issuing
mask parameter line ﬁeld
starting columns.

v3s120

Loads v3 with the issuing
mask parameter line ﬁeld
sizes.

v4s132

Loads v4 with the issuing
mask parameter line.

v5s120

Loads v5 with the receiving
mask parameter line ﬁeld
starting columns.

v6s120

Loads v6 with the receiving
mask parameter line ﬁeld

sizes.

v7s132

Loads v7 with the receiving
mask parameter line.

oum,0b,,,0,c Displays a double function
mask derived from reports
0B0 and 0C0, basic format
(using default report 0 and
basic format for both
reports).

@OUT (Output)
Use the Output (OUT) statement to display lines from a
report on the screen. For example, you can display a menu
or input screen you created that resides in the -0 result. You
can display information on the entire screen or part of the
screen; you can start with a blank screen or overlay an
existing one.

Note: It is recommended that you use the Screen Control
(SC) statement to display data on the screen. The SC
statement provides many features not available with the
OUT statement.

Output Statement Format
@OUT,c,d,r,l,q[,outl,tabp,erase?,interim?,pdq,pro
tect,fxmt?,outsp,blink?,sn,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which to display
data.

l

Line in the report from which
the output is to start.

q

Number of lines to display.

outl

Line on the screen where the
output is to start. Default =
top line of the screen.

tabp

Number of tab positions from
home position after which to
place the cursor. Use a
negative number to indicate
the number of tab positions to
move backwards starting from
the home position. Maximum
= 100.
Default = home position.

erase?

Erase to the end of the screen
starting at the line speciﬁed in
the outl subﬁeld? Y or N.
Default = Y.

interim?

Interim display? Y or N. If Y,
the run continues even if the
user does not respond by
pressing Resume or
Transmit. Default = N.

pdq

Number of lines to push down
on the screen.

protect

Protected format option. See
Output Options.

fxmt?

Force transmit? Y or N. If Y,
the run continues and you can
load variables with ICVAR$,
INPUT$, INSTR$, INVAR$, or
INVR1$. Default = N.

outsp

Determine how the spaces
and data are sent to the
screen. A, B, or space. Default
= space.

A - send the data, including all
spaces.
B - send the data, but not
spaces. (If data exists on a
line, only the columns
containing new data are
overwritten. The old and new
data are blended together on
the same line.)
Space - send the data,
including spaces, if fewer than
six consecutive spaces appear
between the characters. If six
or more spaces appear
between the characters, send
the data, but not the spaces.
This enables the screen to be
painted faster.

blink?

Change the less-than (<) and
greater-than (>) characters to
left and right blink characters?
Y or N. Default = N. Hardware
capabilities of supported
terminals may not allow
blinking.

2200 Þ Þ

sn

Station number where output

is to be displayed. If you omit
this ﬁeld or specify zero,
output is displayed at the
station executing the run.

lab

Ü Ü 2200

Label to go to if the output to
another station cannot be
completed successfully. If you
omit this ﬁeld and the run
cannot be successfully
completed, it is terminated
with an error.

Output Options
Option

Description

E

Erases only unprotected ﬁelds
on the screen (for use in
clearing input ﬁelds of a
displayed menu).

2200 Þ Þ

F

Allows full-screen input. Use
only with IBM Series 3270
terminals.

Ü Ü 2200

I

Edits input characters in
unprotected ﬁelds by character
type. Use one of the following
edit codes in the ﬁrst character
position of the unprotected
ﬁeld in the output area. The
numeric code enables users to
see the data as they type it.
The letter code makes the
entered data transparent.

Space - any data, no editing
0 or A - any data, no editing
1 or B - alphabetic data, no
editing
2 or C - numeric data, leftjustiﬁed (including +, -, and
period)
3 or D - no data
4 or E - any data, right-justiﬁed
5 or F - alphabetic data, rightjustiﬁed
6 or G - numeric data, rightjustiﬁed (including +, -, and
period)
Numeric data includes plus and
minus signs and the period.
The presence of the I option
assumes output protect
features. See the P option.

P

Protects ﬁelds when displaying
data. Enter one of the following
protect codes in column 1 of
the output lines or immediately
after the comma that ends an
unprotected ﬁeld.

0 or space - normal intensity
1 - no intensity (characters are
not displayed)
2 - low intensity
3 - blinking characters,
alternating low and normal
intensity
If protected and unprotected
ﬁelds are displayed on the
same line, start the
unprotected ﬁeld with a tab
character and end it with a
comma. To use diﬀerent
intensities on the same line,
place an unprotected ﬁeld
between the protected ﬁelds.

4

Speciﬁes that ﬁelds are deﬁned
on four lines for one line of
output, as follows:
Line 1 contains M attribute
characters.
Line 2 contains N attribute
characters.
Line 3 contains emphasis
characters.
Line 4 contains the data to
display.

5

Speciﬁes that ﬁelds are deﬁned
on ﬁve lines for one line of
output, as follows (see Fourand Five-To-One Output):
Line 1 contains M attribute
characters.
Line 2 contains N attribute
characters.
Line 3 contains a color
code.
Line 4 contains emphasis
characters.
Line 5 contains the data to
display.

Output Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The statement displays the speciﬁed lines on the screen
and clears the output area.
The run waits until the run user transmits or resumes,
unless you specify a Y in the interim? or fxmt? subﬁelds.
If you specify N in the interim? subﬁeld, the statement
resets the I/O, LLP, and DLP counts.
The statement clears any currently mapped function
keys.

Output Reserved Words
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
To capture user input, load input variables using INPUT$,
INSTR$, ICVAR$, INVAR$, and INVR1$. If the user presses
Resume instead of Transmit, the data entered is not
captured.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
To capture user input, load input variables using INPUT$,
INSTR$, ICVAR$, INVAR$, and INVR1$. If the user presses
Resume instead of Transmit, the data entered is not
captured.
If you use the lab subﬁeld and the OUT statement cannot be
completed, the run goes to the label. Examine STAT1$ for
the status code:

1

Station does not exist or it is a
batch port, remote run, or
background station.

2

Station is not available because it is
not currently connected to the
system.

3

No one is signed on at the speciﬁed
station and interim? was not

speciﬁed.

4

User at the speciﬁed station did not
respond to the message wait signal
within one minute.

5

User answered the signal and
received the screen, but did another
operation rather than supply input.

Ü Ü 2200

Output Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Do not use an OUT statement in a run started with the
Background Run (BR) statement or from a remote site using
a Remote Run (RRN) statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Do not use an OUT statement in a run started with the
Background Run (BR) statement or from a remote site
using a Remote Run (RRN) statement. You can use an
OUT statement in a run from a remote site using a
Remote Run (RRN) statement if you send the output to
another terminal using the sn subﬁeld.
Ü Ü 2200
When the output is displayed, you can load input
variables using INPUT$, INSTR$, ICVAR$, INVAR$, and
INVR1$. To resume the run after the output is displayed,
press Transmit. You can also press Resume to resume
the run, but the data entered on the screen is not
captured.
Put reserved words before the variable name in the
Change Variable (CHG) statement, and be sure the CHG
statement precedes the OUT statement (INSTR$,
INVAR$, INVR1$).

Initialize variables with INPUT$ following the OUT
statement.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the OUT statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.

Output Examples
This example displays ﬁve lines of the current result,
starting at report line 2, on the ﬁrst line of the screen:
@out,0,2,5,1 .
This example displays one line from the current result,
pushing the currently displayed data down on the screen:
@out,-0,4,1,1,,n,y,2 .

-0,4

Displays lines from the current
result, starting at report line 4.

1

Displays one line.

1

Starts the display on the ﬁrst line
of the screen.

n

Do not erase the screen.

y

Interim display; the run continues
even if the user does not respond.

2

Pushes the currently displayed
data down two lines before
displaying this line.

2200 Þ Þ
Displaying Information at Another Terminal
Use the sn and lab subﬁelds to display information and
obtain input from terminals other than the one executing
the run. You can use background, batch port, and remote
runs to solicit and obtain input just as you can with normal
runs, but you must send the output to a real terminal.
You cannot obtain exclusive use of any station. When more
than one run is sending output to the same station, the
output may be intermixed and may not be displayed in the
same order as they were sent.
If the User Is at the Terminal
Sending information to a terminal where a user is signed on
activates a message wait signal on that terminal and stalls
your run. If the user responds to the signal, the information
is displayed on the user's screen.
If you specify N or blank in the interim? subﬁeld, your run
stalls until the user at the terminal presses any key. If you
specify Y in the interim? subﬁeld, your run continues
automatically.
If the user at the other terminal does not respond to the
signal within one minute, your run either continues at the
speciﬁed label or terminates with an error (STAT1$ = 4). If
the user does respond to the signal, but enters a new
command rather than supplying input, your run continues at
the speciﬁed label or terminates with an error (STAT1$ = 5).
If the User Is Not at the Terminal

If no user is signed on at the speciﬁed station, you must
specify a Y in the interim? subﬁeld. In this case, the
message wait signal is not activated, the output information
is displayed on the screen, and your run continues.
This is particularly useful if you want to use a terminal as a
system monitor, with a scheduled background run
periodically updating the display. If you do not specify an
interim display, your run continues at the speciﬁed label or
terminates with an error (STAT1$ = 3).
Ü Ü 2200
Four- and Five-To-One Output
If your display terminal lets you control emphasis for special
editing and presentation techniques, you can use either the
four- or the ﬁve-to-one output. Four-to-one is for
monochrome displays and ﬁve is for color.
The software ignores the color feature on a monochrome
terminal.
Four-to-one output consists of three edit code lines and one
data line for each output line. Five-to-one output contains
four edit code lines and a data line for each output line.
The following tables describe all possible combinations of
the M and N characters for the four- and ﬁve-to-one
displays.

Note: On the same line, you cannot combine an M code
with an N code or vice versa.
The following table shows the characteristics for the fourand ﬁve-to-one displays. Each line of code in a four-to-one

or ﬁve-to-one output may contain an edit code character.
These characters are

Emphasis Characters

Emphasis characters consist of the column separator, the
underscore, and the strikethrough.
Because of hardware limitations of the supported terminals,
the software ignores all emphasis characters. The emphasis
characters are described here because the code character
line is required for four-to-one or ﬁve-to-one displays.
Emphasis characters are sent to the terminal, but hardware
capabilities vary among terminal types. See your display
terminal documentation for more information on controlling
emphasis.
Emphasis characters are normally displayed as white text
on a black background. Foreground and background colors
are ignored.
The following table lists the applicable codes for emphasis
characters.

Code Strike
Underscore Column
Through
Separator

space -

-

-

!

-

-

Yes

$

-

Yes

-

%

-

Yes

Yes

(

Yes

-

-

)

Yes

-

Yes

,

Yes

Yes

-

-

Yes

Yes

Yes

For example, if you want your input ﬁelds to be displayed
with underscores, select a dollar sign ($) from the previous
table. If you wish to create a vertical line, select a series of
exclamation points (!).
Now that you have had a brief look at edit codes and
emphasis characters, you are ready to see how the software
deﬁnes screens that use them.
Four-to-One Screens
These screens make full use of edit codes and emphasis
characters. They allow you to specify the following:
An M code
An N code
An emphasis character
A displayable character
All can be speciﬁed for the same screen position.

You must place each of the codes and characters in the
same column of four successive lines of source code. The M
code must be on the ﬁrst line, the N on the second, the
emphasis character on the third, and the displayable
character on the fourth.

Output Examples
For example, suppose you want the name of a ﬁeld to be
low intensity, reverse video, with protected ﬁelds. First,
select an N from the M table, and S from the N table, and
code the four lines as follows:
N (M byte)
S (N byte)
(Emphasis character)
State:__ (Displayable character)
If you want the input ﬁeld to contain a tab stop, have normal
intensity, be left-justiﬁed, and allow alpha characters only,
select a lowercase d from the M table and an uppercase A
from the N table:
N d (M byte)
S A (N byte)
(Emphasis character)
State:__ (Displayable character)
When the software receives a four-to-one screen, it scans
the source lines four at a time, vertically combines the four
codes or characters of each column, and generates one line
of output. The output line contains the edit codes, emphasis
characters, and displayable characters ready for
transmission to the terminal.
The following is an expanded edit code sequence:
D L L (M byte)
A B C (N byte)
Changing an unprotected ﬁeld causes initialization of a new
variable, even though no tab character was speciﬁed.

Note: When you redeﬁne the editing code, even though no
tab character was speciﬁed, the contents are placed into the
next variable.
If you intend to display a four-to-one screen, enter a 4 as
the protected format option on the OUT statement, as
follows:
@brk out,-0,2,16,1,1,y,,,4 .
Now you are ready to code a simple data entry screen.
Suppose you need to enter the name, street, city, zip, and
phone of a new customer. The state ﬁeld should allow only
alphabetic input. The input ﬁelds should be normal intensity,
have tab stops, and be ﬁlled with underscores. The ﬁeld
labels should be low intensity and protected.
This example illustrates the four-to-one coding for a data
entry screen.
1. @brk .
2. N d N
3. S @ S
4. $$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$
5. Name:
6. d N
7. @ S
8. $$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$
9. Street:
10. d N
11. @ S
12. $$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$
13. City:
14. d N d N d Nd Nd N
15. A S B S B SB SB S
16. $$ $$$$$ $$$ $$$ $$$$$

17. State: Zip: Phone:( ) 18. @brk out,-0,2,4,1,1,y,,,4 .
When the OUT statement executes, source lines 2 through 5
combine to form the ﬁrst output line. Lines 6 through 9
combine to form the second output line, and so on. The
following data entry screen is displayed:
Name: ___________________________________
Street: ___________________________________
City: ___________________________________
State: __ Zip: _____ Phone (___)___-______
The underscored areas are unprotected and of normal
intensity. Because the underscores are protected emphasis
characters, they are continuous and are not overwritten by
input from the keyboard. The ﬁeld labels are protected and
of low intensity. The State ﬁeld allows only alphabetic input.
The Zip and Phone ﬁelds allow only numeric input.

PARETO (Pareto Chart)
Use the Pareto Chart (PARETO) run to create a pareto chart
to be displayed on the screen or drawn on a plotter. A
pareto chart is a combined bar and line chart on which the
line represents the sum of the bar values.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

PARETO Chart Control Line Format
PARETO[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

Pareto Chart Colors

5

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Labels for the x axis,

entered in the column below
the word Labels. Enter data
values for each bar in the
Bar column. Each data value
is graphed as a bar. Enter at
least two labels and two
data values.

PASTE
2200 Þ Þ
See the Cut command for more information.
Ü Ü 2200

PC (Phrase Change)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Phrase Change (PC) command to locate a word or a
sequence of words and change it to the speciﬁed
replacement string. It is similar to the Locate and Change
command, but does not locate words that are contained
within other words.
If the change causes a line to expand to a length greater
than that allowed for the report, an extra line is added for
the extra characters. In reports wider than the screen, extra
characters are moved into nondisplayed columns.

Phrase Change Control Line Format
PC tgtstring,replstring,[o]

Field

Description

tgtstring

Word or sequence of words
(separated by spaces) to
locate. tgtstring can contain
only alphanumeric characters
A through Z and 0 through 9,
hyphens, and apostrophes.
The Phrase Change command
ignores all special characters
except hyphens and
apostrophes.

replstring Word or sequence of words
(separated by spaces) that
replaces the target phrase.

O

The Rn option is the report
number you want to process
(must be in the same drawer
as the report on display). You
cannot use this from a result.

Phrase Change Guidelines
The Phrase Change command is not available in interactive
word processing. Remember to press the Exit WP key to
exit interactive word processing before using the PC
command.
Use the Phrase Change command on adjusted reports,
unless you used the Return key when you typed the text
and did not let the text wrap from line to line. The command
does not change words that wrap from one line to the next,
but it changes phrases that wrap if a word in the phrase
ends in the last column on display (or is followed by at least
one space on the line) and the next word continues on the
next line.
Ü Ü 2200

@PC (Run PC Program)
Use the Run PC Program (PC) statement to execute a
speciﬁed local PC application.
Use format 2 to access Windows WinHelp; however, see the
prerequisites listed below. Since the PC statement performs
only a limited syntax check before passing the information
to WinHelp, have your run check the accuracy of WinHelp
parameters before executing the PC statement.
The command string is composed of two parts: the
command name and the optional parameters. The Microsoft
Windows operating system imposes a limit of 256
characters on the combined length of these two strings. If
this limit is exceeded, the PC statement will fail. There is
also a 127-character limit on the length of the parameter
string. If this limit is exceeded, the operating system will
truncate any excess characters and not return an error
message. Exceeding this limit does not cause the PC
statement to fail.
The PC statement releases all reports opened by a Find and
Read Line (FDR) statement. Any subsequent Read Line
(RDL) or Read Line Next (RLN) statements are then invalid.
A DOS program can cause a Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or a Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client session to end unexpectedly. If the
DOS program was conﬁgured to have exclusive use of the
PC, it prevents the Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or the Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client session from communicating with
the host. The host then assumes that the session was
terminated and it initiates a cleanup procedure.

This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
This statement is restricted to foreground runs.

Run PC Program Statement Formats
Format 1
@PC[,o,lab] command .
Format 2
@PC ‘WinHelp help-file help-command [parameter]’
.
Format 3
@PC[,o,lab] application parameter .

Note: With format 3, you can launch Winhlp32.exe.

Field

Description

o

Action option. See Run PC
Program Options for
available options.

lab

The run continues processing
at the label number if the PC
statement fails; for example,
if the request cannot be
found or the destination is
not available. STAT1$
contains zero if successful. If
the statement is
unsuccessful, a nonzero

value is returned. See
Graphical Interface Returned
Status Codes (STAT1$
Values) for possible values
returned in the STAT1$
reserved word.

command

Name of the application to
be executed. Enclose the
name in quotation marks if
there are embedded spaces.

WinHelp

Speciﬁes access to WinHelp.

help-ﬁle

Help ﬁle name for WinHelp to
use. Specify as much of the
path name as necessary to
identify the ﬁle.

helpcommand

The help command to
perform. Supported
commands include:
CONTEXT
CONTENTS (default)
SETCONTENTS
CONTEXTPOPUP
KEY
PARTIALKEY
COMMAND
FORCEFILE

HELPONHELP
QUIT

parameter (Format 2.) Parameter
speciﬁc to each command, if
required.

application An application to be started.

parameter (Format 3.) A list of
command line options to be
passed to the application.

Note: Winhelp.exe and Winhlp32.exe are Microsoft
products. For documentation on these products, see article
115183 at the Microsoft.com Website.

Run PC Program Options
Option

Description

B

Executes the speciﬁed
program and minimizes it as
an icon.

C or N

Identiﬁes the speciﬁed
program as not being a
Windows program (such as an
MS-DOS command).

W

Waits for the main window of
the speciﬁed program to close
before continuing.

Note: Some programs may
not be designed to support
this option, for example,
WinWord.

R

(Format 3 only.) Designates an
application and a parameter.
Check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(12-1) =
1 if available.

Note: For more information
on DWCAP$, see the
Developer’s Guide.
MAPPER-API recognizes DOS
environment variables. The
format for an embedded
environment variable is
%variable%, as in PC,R
"winword"
"%TEMP%\mywords.doc". On
the Business Information
Server for ClearPath OS 2200,
use the reserved word
RSLANT$ for the backslash.
MAPPER-API deﬁnes a special
variable, DATADIR, to
represent the default data
directory.

If the C option is used and an error label is provided,
MAPPER goes to the error label
If CMD.EXE in Windows Server or COMMAND.COM in
Windows is not present on the client system
If CMD.EXE or COMMAND.COM returns an error status
MAPPER does not go to the error label when the program
named in the command argument fails or errors.

Run PC Program Reserved Word
If the PC statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement does not complete, the run continues at the
label. The STAT1$ reserved word contains an error status.
See Graphical Interface Returned Status Codes (STAT1$
Values) for possible values returned in the STAT1$ reserved
word.

Printing Data or Bitmap Images
To print whatever is in the main window as data, use
@pc ‘windowprint’ .
To print another window as a bitmap, use
@pc ‘windowprint var’ .
where var is the variable containing the window handle of
another window.

Run PC Program Examples
This example executes the PC program called "solitaire."
@pc ‘sol.exe’ .
This example executes the WinHelp CONTENTS command.
@pc ‘WinHelp c:\mydir\myhlpfil contents’ .
@.
This example combines the application and parameter
options.
@pcw,100,b,5 'mywords.rtf' . Writes to MAPPERAPI's data directory
@pc,rw 'wordpad' '%datadir%'rslant$'mywords.rtf'
. W suspends run until Word closed
@pcr,100,b,5,n 'mywords.rtf' . Reads from PC data
directory
@pc,n 'del %datadir%'rslant$'mywords.rtf' .
Clean-up

@PCF (PC File)
Use the PC File (PCF) statement to retrieve workstation ﬁle
and directory attributes. The status is returned in the
STAT1$ and STAT2$ reserved words.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.

PC File Statement Format
@PCF[,opt] oname [vSize] .

Field

Description

opt

Option ﬁeld (see PC File
Option).

oname

Name of the object containing
the ﬁle name. The object may
specify a PC ﬁle name.

vSize

Holds the ﬁle information.
vSize holds the byte count of
the ﬁle.

Note: 0 is returned if GI-BIS
does not support the feature.

PC File Option
Option

Description

S

Returns basic existence and
access information on the
speciﬁed ﬁle.

PC File Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the following values:

0

File or directory does not exist.

1

File exists.

2

Directory exists.

STAT2$ contains the following values:

0

File or directory does not exist.

1

File or directory is read-only.

3

File or directory has read and
write capabilities.

PC File Example
This example requests attribute status on the speciﬁed ﬁle
from your workstation.
@pcf,s 'c:'rslant$'rose.bmp' .
@if STAT1$ eq 1 gto 0120 . does the file exist?
@pcw,0,a,153 'c:'rslant$'rose.bmp' . No, write it
to the PC
@0120 .

(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
This statement is not allowed in background runs.

Transfer from PC Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The statement transfers speciﬁed data from a Business
Information Server for Microsoft Windows Client or a
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
workstation to a report.
If oname is a drive or subdirectory (ending with a
reverse slant), the report contains a list of ﬁles in that
drive or directory.
Example:
@PCR,0,a,-0 ‘C:\mydirectory\’ .
@DSP,-0 .
If the retrieved data is wider than the receiving report,
the data is continued on a new line.

Transfer from PC Examples
This example copies a Repository object named rose into
report 63C32. The full object name ROSE.BMP( ) is written to
line 2 of the report and becomes its title.
@pcr,32,c,63,,y 'rose.bmp()' .
This example retrieves an MS-DOS bitmapped ﬁle, creates a
result, and then displays the result.
@pcr,32,c,-0,y,y 'f:\bmp\demo.bmp' .
@dsp,-0 .

(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
This statement is not allowed in background runs.

PCX
The PCX utility transfers data between a report and a DOS
ﬁle. Using the PCX utility, you can transfer data in either
direction-MS DOS to Business Information Server or
Business Information Server to MS DOS.
This utility requires a workstation session that is using one
of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
For additional information, follow this procedure:

1. Enter PCX and press Enter.
2. From the menu bar or the help button, select Help.

@PIC (Display Picture)
Use the Display Picture (PIC) statement to display a picture
or a graph within a window.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
The statement displays a picture on the screen, within the
speciﬁed window.
For more information, see the Developer’s Guide.

Display Picture Statement Format
@PIC[,c,d,r,tf?,oname,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,,o,
lab] caption [vph] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the picture
to be displayed.

tf?

Transfer the report to the
workstation before processing
the PIC statement? Y or N.
Default = N.

oname

Name of the object containing
the picture. The object may
specify either a PC ﬁle name
or a version controlled name.
If you do not specify a name in
this ﬁeld, then you must
specify the name on line 2
(the title line) of the report
speciﬁed in the c,d,r ﬁeld.
The following graphical ﬁle
formats are supported: .BMPbitmap

.GIF-Graphical Interchange
Format
.JPG-Joint Photographic
Experts Group standard image
ﬁle format (JPEG) .MGLMAPPER graphics primitives
.PCX-PCX image

Notes:

controlled name and
