not supported.
For Image on Button to
function correctly, check
DWCAP$ for availability:
DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on
DWCAP$, see the
Developer’s Guide.

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window or picture handle.
Default = main window.
If you specify a window
handle, the statement places
the picture in that window. All
vertical and horizontal

coordinates are relative to
that window.
If you specify a picture handle,
the system assumes that you
are changing that picture in
some way. If you do not
specify a value in the fc/bc, o,
or caption ﬁelds, the system
uses the original values;
however, you must specify
values in the vert, hort, vsiz,
and hsiz subﬁelds.
Once deﬁned, you can change
only the vert, hort, vsiz, hsiz,
fc/bc, o, and caption ﬁelds.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the picture using
a positioning unit (or row
number). See @WIN (Deﬁne
Window Display) for more
information on sizing units.
When you specify a row
number, the position is based
on the main run font. See
@FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you
are specifying a value for the
vertical position, you must
also specify values in the hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.

Default = 1 (positioning unit
or row number).

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the picture
using a positioning unit (or
column number). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When you specify a
column number, the position
is based on the main run font.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you
are specifying a value for the
horizontal position, you must
also specify values in the vert,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or column number).

vsiz

Vertical size of the picture
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When you specify row
numbers, the size is based on
the main run font. See @FON
(Font) for more information on
fonts. If you are specifying a
value for the vertical size, you

must also specify values in the
vert, hort, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Actual = the picture is
displayed with the actual
dimensions (a size of -1) as it
was stored.
Default = 0 (positioning unit
or row number). The picture
extends to the bottom of the
window in which you place it
and expands and contracts
with that window.

hsiz

Horizontal size of the picture
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or column numbers). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on sizing units. When you
specify column numbers, the
size is based on the main run
font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for
the horizontal size, you must
also specify values in the vert,
hort, and vsiz subﬁelds.
Actual = the picture is
displayed with the actual
dimensions (a size of -1) as it
was stored.
Default = 0 (positioning unit
or 0 column number). The

picture extends to the right
side of the window in which
you place it and expands and
contracts with that window.

o

Type of window to display. See
Display Picture Options for
available options.

lab

The run continues processing
at the label number if the PIC
statement fails within the
workstation client; run syntax
errors do not take the label.
STAT1$ contains zero if
successful. If the statement is
unsuccessful, a nonzero value
is returned. See Graphical
Interface Returned Status
Codes (STAT1$ Values) for
possible values returned in the
STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: Adding a label has the
same aﬀect as the S option,
you may notice a minor
performance degradation.

caption

Caption for the pictures.
Enclose captions containing
embedded spaces in
apostrophes ('). If you do not

specify a caption, place two
apostrophes in this ﬁeld. The
system translates variables to
their corresponding values.
Pictures without captions
cannot be moved. The
maximum character length is
255 characters. If this limit is
exceeded, the caption is
truncated to 255 characters.

Note: If DWCAP$(13-1) is not
set, the maximum character
length is 79 characters.

vph

Variable that contains the
picture handle. This must be a
6-character variable.

Display Picture Options
Option

Description

B

Displays a border around the
window. Do not use with the F
option.

F

Creates a thick frame around
the window, enabling the user
to expand or contract the
window. Do not use with the B
option.

I

Input picture. If you click this
picture, the statement
captures the x and y
coordinates where you clicked
and the run continues. Use
INPUT$ to load variables with
the coordinates. These
coordinates are actually a
percentage of the width and
height of the picture, from 1
to 100, so the values remain
the same even if the size of
the picture changes.

P

Places a horizontal and

vertical scroll bar on the
picture, if required. These
scroll bars enable the user to
pan horizontally and tilt
vertically through the picture.
This option is ignored when
oname is .MGL.

S

Send immediate. This option
controls whether the run
sends the picture data
immediately or transfers it in
blocks. If you specify a report
in the c, d, r subﬁelds, the run
always sends the data
immediately. If you do not
specify a report, data is sent
in blocks unless you use the S
option.

X

Enables the user to enlarge
the window to full screen size.

Display Picture Reserved Word
If the PIC statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement does not complete, the run continues at the
label. The STAT1$ reserved word contains an error status.
See Graphical Interface Returned Status Codes (STAT1$
Values) for possible values returned in the STAT1$ reserved
word.

Display Picture Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the PIC statement:
You can also use the PIC statement to display a large
binary object retrieved from an Informix or Oracle
database using the FCH statement.
Use the JPEG image ﬁle format only if you have a highperformance PC and a high-quality monitor (more than
256 colors). The JPEG format gives you smaller ﬁle sizes
and shorter transfer times; however, it increases display
times and does not provide an acceptable picture if your
monitor displays fewer than 256 colors.
You can have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT,
LST, PIC, TXT, and WIN controls concurrently open.

Note: The actual number of handles created can be
lower depending on workstation PC memory
conﬁguration and usage.

Display Picture Example
This example uses the PIC statement to display a picture.
@pic,0,a,3,y,'rose.bmp()',<win>,1,1,-1,-1 'Rose'
<petals>i6 .

0,a,3

Take the picture from report
3A0.

y

Download the object before
processing the PIC
statement.

'rose.bmp()' Specify the object name of
the picture.

<win>

Specify the handle of the
window where the picture is
placed.

1,1,-1,-1

Display the picture in the
upper left corner of the
window. The picture is
displayed with the actual
dimensions as it was stored.

'Rose'

Display the picture with a

caption of 'Rose'.

<petals>

Load <petals> with the
picture handle.

PIEG (Pie Chart)
Use the Pie Chart (PIEG) run to create a pie chart. A pie
chart consists of a circle divided like a sliced pie, each slice
proportional to a value.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Pie Chart Control Line Format
PIEG[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

Pie Chart Colors

Pen
Stall

Aqua

8

1

2

Gray

1

Green

4

3

Lime

6

Magenta

7

Pink

3

Red

3

Tan

5

Turquoise 8

Violet

7

White

1

Yellow

5

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Pie Chart Fill Patterns
Code

Pattern

N

None (just a border)

0

Solid

1

45-degree left (\\\)

2

45-degree right (///)

3

Vertical lines

4

Horizontal lines

5

Vertical/horizontal crosshatch

6

45-degree crosshatch

over 6

Varies by device

Pie Chart Guidelines
The order in which you enter the slices on the chart report
determines their order in the pie chart. Using a clock for a
reference point, slices start at the three o'clock position and
proceed counterclockwise.
There is no limit to the number of slices you can have in
your pie, but after a certain point the slices become too
small to be eﬀective. To avoid this problem, rearrange the
order of the slices, or separate small slices by putting large
slices between them.

Pie Chart Report Fields
Field

Description

Text color

Color of the text. Default =
white. See Pie Chart
Colors.

Screen color

Color of the screen.
Default = black. See Pie
Chart Colors.

Title

Optional title of 40
characters or fewer,
centered at the top of the
chart in the color speciﬁed
for text.

Subtitle

Optional subtitle of 60
characters or fewer.

Date

Optional date of 18
characters or fewer, placed
in the lower left corner of
the chart. Y = Current date
in the format DD MMM YY.

Include
leading '$'?

Place a dollar sign ($) in
front of each value? Y or N.
Default = N.

Display
percentage

whole rather than actual

(display value and
percentage).

in the lower right corner of

= N.

Segments ﬁeld, the
command places the six
smallest values in a bar
regardless of what you
type in the Comb ﬁeld.
This selection is not
available for 3D pie charts.

PL (Phrase Locate)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Phrase Locate (PL) command to locate a word or a
sequence of words within a report. The Phrase Locate
command is similar to the Locate command except that it
does not locate words that are contained within other words.
Use the Phrase Locate command on adjusted reports unless
you used the Return key when you typed the text and did
let not a word wrap from line to line.

Phrase Locate Control Line Format
PL tgtstring[,o]

Field

Description

tgtstring

Word or sequence of words
(separated by spaces) to
locate. tgtstring can contain
only alphanumeric characters
A through Z and 0 through 9,
hyphens, and apostrophes.
The command ignores all
special characters except
hyphens and apostrophes.

o

Options ﬁeld. See Phrase
Locate Options.

Phrase Locate Options
Option

Description

B

Stops the scan after the ﬁrst
ﬁnd in each report (for
multiple reports).

Ln

Scans the ﬁrst n lines in each
report. If used with the S
option, the S option overrides
the L option.

Q[n]

Quick locate scans only the
ﬁrst n lines in each report and
displays each report at line 1
instead of the line where the
phrase is located. Default =
50. If used with the S option,
the line number in the S
option overrides the number
of lines (or the default, 50) in
the Q option. However, the S
option does not aﬀect the
display of the Q option
because the command scans
only once in each report.

Rx-y

Scans a range of reports

within the same drawer as the
report on display from report x
to report y. The command
skips reports with standard,
user, or department read
passwords. It does not skip
those with your own
password.

S[n]

Starts the scan at the ﬁrst line
on display, or the line n you
specify. If used with the L
option, the S option overrides
the L option. See also Q
Option.

Phrase Locate Outcome
The report is displayed at the line where the ﬁnd was made,
and the cursor is placed just ahead of the ﬁrst word of the
phrase. To continue locating the phrase, press F1 or type
rsm and transmit.
The Phrase Locate command does not locate single words
that wrap from one line to the next. It does locate phrases
that wrap if a word in the phrase ends in the last column on
display (or is followed by at least one space on the line) and
the next word continues on the next line.

Phrase Locate Guideline
If you are using the Phrase Locate command in a word
processing report, adjust the report ﬁrst. When you are done
locating the phrases in your report, adjust the report again
to realign the text within the margins.
Ü Ü 2200

PLOTIT
2200 Þ Þ
Use the PLOTIT command to print a chart at a remote
plotter.
The command prints the chart at the plotter you specify. If
the station you send to is busy, a message oﬀers the option
to retry.
The plotter must be assigned to the system, and it must
have automatic paper feed. The PLOTIT command may
require a special setup for your site. Your administrator can
refer to HELP,COORD,GRAPHICS for information on special
setups for the PLOTIT command.

PLOTIT Control Line Format
First save the graphics primitive code as a report, then enter
the following format on the control line:
PLOTIT[,plotstation,sendingstation]

Field

Description

plotstation

Number of the station to
send the graphic to.

sendingstation Number of the station
from which to execute the
PLOTIT command.

PR and @PRT (Print)
Use the Print (PR) function or PRT statement to print a report
on a system printer.
The manual function redisplays the report on your screen
and places it in a queue for the requested printer. The
statement places the report in the print queue for the
requested printer. It does not create a result.

2200 Þ Þ
When you specify a label on the PRT statement and an error
is encountered, STAT1$ returns one of the following values:

1

Device name speciﬁed is not
conﬁgured.

2

Pre-deﬁned form deﬁnition is
invalid.

3

Line, page, or margin deﬁnition is
invalid.

4

Any other error encountered
(usually system or ﬁle errors).

STAT2$ returns the line number of the system error
message. Use the Load System Message (LSM) statement to
get the message for display purposes.

Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To move the printer paper to the top of the next page,
type the .EJECT command in the report where you want
the page break. The command must begin in column 1.

shell and if the Linux or UNIX commands lp, rm, lpstat,
and cancel are in your Linux or UNIX path (both lp and
rm are required in the Linux and UNIX path for printing),
you can perform the following operations:
Send prints to the system printer
Cancel prints sent to the system printer
Check the status of the system printer

Print Guidelines
2200 Þ Þ
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To move the printer paper to the top of the next page,
type the .EJECT command in the report where you want
the page break. The command must begin in column 1.
You can use the PRINTFORM command to create, edit,
and delete banner reports and predeﬁned printer forms.
For information about using this command, and for
information on banners and predeﬁned forms, enter
printform,help.
To print a device across a network, you must enter both

the line for Destination Printer. The node name and
queue name together cannot be more than 14
characters.

System Printer Control Commands
The following is a list of commands you can type in a report
that controls printing on system printers. A command must
begin in column 1 and can be typed in uppercase or
lowercase letters.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Command

Description

.EJECT

Advances the paper to
the top of the next
page before
continuing to print.
The paper is advanced
to the Home position if
the ﬁrst nonblank
string on the line is
".EJECT".

$HOM$

Advances the paper
(same as .EJECT, but
does not have to
begin in column 1).

$KIP$ n

Skips n lines on this
page. If n is greater
than the number of
lines remaining on the

page, the printer
advances to the top of
the next page.

$LPP$ n

Prints n lines per
page. Default = 60,
including header and
footer margins.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

Command

Description

.EJECT

Advances the paper
to the top of the
next page before
continuing to print.
The paper is
advanced to the
Home position if the
ﬁrst nonblank string
on the line is
".EJECT".
The period can be
any character except
a blank.

$HOME$

Go to the top of the
next page (same as
.EJECT).

$MARG$
lpp,tm,bm,lpi,lsp

Set the page
parameters:

1 - 192

0 - 99

or 8

The parameters are
optional. If you leave

current value
remains in eﬀect.

instead of a value if

value.

Control page
o,pgn,ejct?,pghdtxt heading:

Change the page
number.

characters of data

Skip n lines in the

or begin skipping
report lines until an

character x, where x

is any nonnumeric
character.

Enables Output
Manager control
images to be placed
in the output print
ﬁle. Any text
beginning in column
7 of the $EOM$
command is
appended to the
“<Esc>60<Esc>S ”
escape sequence
and written to the

End skipping lines
begun by $SKIP$ x,
where x matches the
nonnumeric
character in $SKIP$
x. To end skipping
lines in any case,

use $ESKP$ without
a nonnumeric
character following
it.

$SKPL$ n

Skip n lines on this
page, where n is a
number. If n is
greater than the
number of lines
remaining on the
page, go to the top
of the next page.
To use the $SKPL$
command following
a $HOME$ or .EJECT
command, place a
blank line before the
$SKIP$ command to
ensure proper paper
positioning.

$BLOK$ x

Block-print x, where
x is from one to
eight alphanumeric
characters, hyphens,
and colons (for
example,
NEWUSERS). If the
block does not ﬁt on
this page, go to the

top of the next page
before printing it.

Block-print the

eight characters).

Block-print the
current date
(MM/DD/YY).

Block-print the
current time
(HH:MM:SS).

Block-print the
user's department

Block-print the

PRINTFORM
2200 Þ Þ
Use the PRINTFORM command to create, edit, and delete
banner reports and predeﬁned printer forms. For more

PRINTFORM
Ü Ü 2200

PSW and @PSW (Password)
Use the Password (PSW) function to assign, change, or clear
the write password assigned to a report, or to unlock a
write-protected report for updating.

Note: Statements can overwrite reports with write
passwords. This command restricts only manual function
users. Also note that when a run replaces a report
containing a write password, the password is removed.
2200 Þ Þ
Use the PSW statement to enable your run to update reports
that have write passwords assigned to them. You can
specify only one write password with each PSW statement in
your run.
When you use the statement, the system retains one
password per session and allows updates to any report that
has that write password until the run ﬁnishes.

Note: The PSW statement is available only if your system is
set up for it. For more information, see your administrator.
Ü Ü 2200
For the Password manual function, a report must be on
display. You must have update control of the report to
assign, change, or clear a write password.

Password Control Line Format
PSW [psw]
where psw is the password currently assigned to the
displayed report, a new password for the report, or clear to
remove password protection from the report. The password
can be up to six alphanumeric characters; no spaces are
allowed. This ﬁeld is transparent, preventing your password
from being displayed.

Password Statement Format
2200 Þ Þ
@PSW,psw .
where psw is the write password.
Ü Ü 2200

Password Guidelines
If you forget the password, follow these guidelines:
If you were the last person to update the report, create
an unprotected copy of the report using the Create
Result Copy command. Use the Replace Report
command to replace the result into the original report.
The original report no longer has a password.
If you were not the last person to update the report,
contact the report owner (last person to update the
report) or your administrator.

Password Examples
This example sets a password to "lockit" or unlocks a report
that has "lockit" as its current password: psw lockit
This example removes a password from the displayed
report:
psw clear

Put Line and @LNP
Use the Put Line function or the LNP statement to copy lines
from a temporary yank buﬀer to a report.
For information on buﬀers, see Temporary Buﬀers in Append
Line and @LNA.
The buﬀer must contain lines received from the Yank Line
command or the Append Line command.
For the Put Line manual function, the report you are copying
lines into must be on display.
For the LNP statement, unless you are processing a result,
you must precede the LNP statement with an Update Lock
(LOK) statement.
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command inserts the speciﬁed lines into the report
and truncates the copied lines or ﬁlls them with spaces
to equal the line length in the current report.
The report expands to receive the added lines.

Put Line In-Report-Line Format
>]P[b]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

P

Put Line call.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100 as speciﬁed by the Yank
Line or Append Line
command. Default =
unlabeled buﬀer.

Put Line Statement Format
@LNP,c,d,r,lb4[,b] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to which lines are to be
copied. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

lb4

Line number preceding the
line where the buﬀer lines will
be placed.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100 as speciﬁed by the LNY,
LNA, or LND statement.
Default = unlabeled buﬀer.

Put Line Procedure
To copy lines from a temporary yank buﬀer
1. Press Edit.
2. Place the cursor on the line above the line where the
buﬀer lines are to be copied.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
4. Tab to the Put lines ﬁeld.
5. Enter the buﬀer number, if you assigned one with a
previous Yank Line, Append Line, or Delete and Yank
Line command.
6. Transmit.
Or
1. Place the cursor on the line preceding the lines where
the buﬀer lines are to be copied.
2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Put Line in-report-line format.

Note: To copy numbered buﬀers, you must use the inreport-line format or the LNP statement.

Put Line Example
In this example, the Yank Line command copies lines 8
through 10 from report 2B0 into buﬀer 10. The Put Line
command inserts the lines from buﬀer 10 into report 13B0
following line 8. Position the cursor on line 8, erase to the
end of the line, and enter the following: >]3y10
Equivalent Statement
@lny,0,b,2,8,3,10 .
Position the cursor on line 8, erase to the end of the line,
and enter the following: >]p10
Equivalent Statement
@lnp,0,b,13,8,10 .

@QCLS (Close Message Queue Object)
Use the Close Message Queue Object (QCLS) statement to
close a message queue object. The QCLS statement is one
of a group of statements that allow you to work with a
message queue. See the following statements for related
information:
@QGET (Get Message from Message Queue)
@QINQ (Inquire on Object Attribute)
@QOPN (Open Message Queue Object)
@QPUT (Put Message on Message Queue)
You should be familiar with IBM WebSphere MQ and have a
thorough understanding of message queues before using
this statement.

Close Message Queue Object Statement Format
@QCLS[,lab] vobjhandle[,o] .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error or
warning message is received
(see Returned Status). The
label is ignored when the A
option is speciﬁed.

vobjhandle Variable containing an
existing handle.

o

Option ﬁeld (see Close
Message Queue Object
Option).

Close Message Queue Object Option
Option

Description

A

Closes all open objects.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Note: The A option also disconnects the Queue Manager.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Close Message Queue Object Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Close Message
Queue Object statement:
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the script errors. You should specify a
label to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$ values should be obtained at the
contingency label.
When a script terminates for any reason, the system
closes all objects that are in use by that script.
If the A option is speciﬁed, all errors are ignored when
closing objects.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the A option is speciﬁed, all objects are closed and the
queue manager is disconnected. Use this option when a
connection to a second queue manager is necessary.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Close Message Queue Object Outcome
If the close is successful, control is returned to the script. All
returned status reserved words are set to zero.
If an error occurs when attempting the close, the script
continues at the speciﬁed label. The STAT1$, STAT2$, and
STAT3$ values are set as described in Returned Status. If a
label is not speciﬁed, the script errors.
Returned Status
STAT1$ returns one of the following completion codes:
0 = OK
1 = Warning Message
2 = Command Failed
STAT2$ returns one of the following status codes:
0 = No Error
7 = WebSphere MQ Close error
100 = The WebSphere MQ interface is down
101 = Invalid Object Handle speciﬁed
200 = URTS NPESTAT error (2200 only)
201 = URTS TPENPE error (2200 only)
202 = URTS NPESETUP error (2200 only)
STAT3$ returns one of the following reason codes:

For STAT2$ values between 1 and 99, see the
WebSphere MQ Application Programming Reference for
a complete list of Reason Codes.
For STAT2$ values between 100 and 199, use the LSM
script statement to get the actual message.
For STAT2$ values between 200 and 299, contact the
Unisys Support Center for more information.

Close Message Queue Object Examples
This example closes a single object.
The object handle <vObjHandle> was captured on a
previous Open statement.
If successful, the Object is closed and the script
continues.
If an error occurs, the script continues at label 0199.
@QCLS,0199 <vObjHandle> .
This example closes all the objects opened by this session.
When closing all objects (A option), the Object Handle
ﬁeld is left blank.
If successful, all objects are closed and the script
continues.
If an error occurs, it is ignored (because of the A option)
and the script continues.
@QCLS ,A .

@QCTL (Queue Control)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Queue Control (QCTL) statement to obtain various
information about the Data Transfer Module (DTM).

Queue Control Statement Format
@QCTL[,c,d,r,lab] func[,p] v[,...,v] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

DTM conﬁguration report. For
use by administrators.

lab

Label to go to in case of error.

func

INFO or STAT, depending on
the kind of information you
need. For additional
administration functions, see
the Administration Guide.

p

Parameters (up to 10). As an
application designer, the only
parameter you can specify is a
queue name. All other
parameters are speciﬁed by
the administrator.

v

Variables. See Functions for
Loading Variables.

Queue Control Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the error status corresponding to standard
system messages. Use a Load System Message (LSM)
statement to read the message.
STAT2$ contains the line number in the DTM conﬁguration
report where the error occurred (if applicable). For use by
administrators.
STAT3$ contains the ﬁeld number in the DTM conﬁguration
report where the error occurred (if applicable). For use by
administrators. If the system encounters an error condition,
it returns an error status and stops processing. All action
completed before the error remains in place.

Queue Control Example
This statement uses the INFO function to request general
information about the message.
@qctl info <mtyp>i1,,<oque>h12,,<sn>i5 .

<mtyp>i1

Load <mtyp> with the
message type.

<oque>h12 Load <oque> with the
name of the originator.

<sn>i5

Load <sn> with the unit
number of the originator.

The next statement follows the ﬁrst. It examines the
message type in v1 and sends a response if necessary. The
response report is the current result: @if <mtyp> eq 1
qrsp,-0 ; .

Functions for Loading Variables
The following two functions of the QCTL statement load
variables with information:
The INFO function loads up to 13 variables containing
general information about the message. This
information is deﬁned only if the run is on a station
processing input from DTM, (DTM$ = 2).
The STAT function loads up to 15 variables with status
information about the queue speciﬁed in the parameter
ﬁeld. (You must specify exactly one queue.) You can use
the STAT function at any terminal, as long as the system
is conﬁgured with DTM.
The following table lists variables loaded when you specify
the INFO function:

Variable

Type/Size Content

Message
Type

following

0-The message

(QSND)

Ü Ü 2200

@QGET (Get Message from Message
Queue)
Use the Get Message from Message Queue (QGET)
statement to retrieve a message from a message queue.
The QGET statement is one of a group of statements that
allow you to work with a message queue. See the following
statements for related information:
@QCLS (Close Message Queue Object)
@QINQ (Inquire on Object Attribute)
@QOPN (Open Message Queue Object)
@QPUT (Put Message on Message Queue)
You should be familiar with IBM ® WebSphere ® MQ and
have a thorough understanding of message queues before
using this statement.

Get Message from Message Queue Statement
Format
@QGET,c,d[,hdgs?,lab]
vobjhandle[,o,correlid,msgid,waittime] |
[vmsgtyp,vcorrelid,vmsgid,vreplytoquemgr,vreplyto
quenam] .

Field

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer into
which the data should
be placed. The data is
returned as a -0 result.

hdgs?

Add headings to the
data from the receiving
drawer? Y or N. Default
= N.

lab

Label to go to if an
error or warning
message is received
(for example, if no
message is found).

vobjhandle

Variable containing an
existing object handle.

o

Reserved for future use.

correlid

Correlation identiﬁer
(24 character
maximum). Used in
conjunction with msgid
to select a speciﬁc
message from the
queue. If correlid and
msgid are blank, the
ﬁrst message on the
queue is selected.

msgid

Message identiﬁer (24
character maximum).
Used in conjunction
with correlid to select a
speciﬁc message from
the queue. If correlid
and msgid are blank,
the ﬁrst message from
the queue is selected.

waittime

Time (in milliseconds)
the script waits for a
message to arrive. If no
message is selected in
the time allowed, the
script errs or takes the
label exit. Values
allowed are:

-1 = Unlimited (wait
forever)
0 = Check once and
return (default)
1-9999999 =
Milliseconds to wait (up
to 2 hours and 45
minutes)

vmsgtyp

Variable to capture the
message type
associated with the
message (I9). Expected
values:
1 = Requesting a reply
from the receiver (use
the vreplytoquemgr
and vreplytoquenam
variables to send the
reply).
2 = Replying to a
received message.
8 = A simple message
with no reply expected
(default).
# = Application deﬁned
(65536 - 999999999).

vcorrelid

Variable to capture the
correlation identiﬁer
found in the message
(S24).

vmsgid

Variable to capture the
message identiﬁer
found in the message
(S24).

vreplytoquemgr Variable to capture the
queue manager used to
send a reply (S48).

vreplytoquenam Variable to capture the
queue name used to
send a reply (S48).

Get Message from Message Queue Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Get Message from
Message Queue statement:
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the script errors. You should specify a
label to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$ values should be obtained at the
contingency label.
The Queue Manager, Queue, and Business Information
Server each have a deﬁned maximum message size
(based on the number of characters). There are three
maximums and the smallest maximum is the deciding
factor in determining the maximum message size.
The vcorrelid and vmsgid ﬁelds may contain nondisplayable characters. Do not attempt to display the
contents of this variable or use it with any other script
statements except the QPUT statement.
If the waittime expires before any message is found for
retrieval, the script continues at the speciﬁed label. In
this case, STAT1$=2, STAT2$=3, and STAT3$ = 2033
(MQRC_NO_MSG_AVAILABLE).

Get Message from Message Queue Outcome
If the message is successfully retrieved from the message
queue, the text of the message becomes the -0 result and
information about the message is returned in vmsgtyp,
vcorrelid, vmsgid, vreplytoquemgr, and vreplytoquenam. All
returned status reserved words are set to zero.
If a warning message is received (STAT1$=1), the text of the
message may become the -0 result but it may not be
complete. For example, the message may be truncated or
invalid characters may be converted to SOE characters.
If an error occurs when attempting the get, the script
continues at the speciﬁed label. The STAT1$, STAT2$, and
STAT3$ values are set as described in Returned Status. If a
label is not speciﬁed, the script errors.
Returned Status
STAT1$ returns one of the following completion codes:
0 = OK
1 = Warning Message
2 = Command Failed
STAT2$ returns one of the following status codes:
0 = No Error
3 = WebSphere MQ Get error
100 = The WebSphere MQ interface is down
101 = Invalid Object Handle speciﬁed

104 = Non-string data received
106 = Data exceeds report width - Lines have been
truncated (warning message)
107 = Invalid characters have been substituted
(warning message)
200 = URTS NPESTAT error (2200 only)
201 = URTS TPENPE error (2200 only)
202 = URTS NPESETUP error (2200 only)
STAT3$ returns one of the following reason codes:
For STAT2$ values between 1 and 99, see the
WebSphere MQ Application Programming Reference for
a complete list of Reason Codes.
For STAT2$ values between 100 and 199, use the LSM
script statement to get the actual message.
For STAT2$ values between 200 and 299, contact the
Unisys Support Center for more information.

Get Message from Message Queue Examples
This example gets any message from a message queue.
Drawer A0 is speciﬁed to receive the message.
<vObjHandle> contains the object handle returned from
a previous Open statement.
A waittime of 0 indicates that QGET searches once for
any available message and then immediately returns.
If a message is found, the text of the message becomes
the -0 result and the variables <vmsgtyp>, <vcorrelid>,
<vmsgid>, <vReToMgr>, and <vReToQue> are loaded
with information from the message.
The script continues at label 0199 if an error or warning
message is received (for example, if no message is
found).
@QGET,0,a,,0199 <vObjHandle>,,,,0 <vmsgtyp>i9,
<vcorrelid>s24,\
<vmsgid>s24,<vReToMgr>s48,<vReToQue>s48 .
This example gets a speciﬁc message from a message
queue.
Drawer A0 is speciﬁed to receive the message.
<vObjHandle> contains the object handle returned from
a previous Open statement.
The <CorrelId> and <MsgId> are provided to get a
speciﬁc message.

A waittime of -1 indicates that the QGET waits for the
speciﬁc message forever.
If the message is found, the variables <vmsgtyp>,
<vcorrelid>, <vmsgid>, <vReToMgr>, and
<vReToQue> are loaded with additional information.
The script continues at label 0199 if an error or warning
message is received.
@QGET,0,a,,0199 <vObjHandle>,,<CorrelId>,
<MsgId>,-1 \
<vmsgtyp>i9,<vcorrelid>s24,<vmsgid>s24,\
<vReToMgr>s48,<vReToQue>s48 .
This example gets any message and checks for common
errors.
@QOPN,0195 '','MY.LOCAL.Q',G <vHanG>i6 . Open
Queue
@QGET,0,I,,0195 <vHanG> . . Get Message
@DSX,-0 . . Display Message
@0195 IF STAT1$ eq 1,(0197),2,(0199) ; . Label
Exit Check Status
@0197 . STAT1$ = Warning Message
@IF STAT3$ eq 2079,(xxxx) ; . Message Length
Truncated by WebSphere
@IF STAT2$ eq 106,(xxxx) ; . Data exceeds report
width - Lines have been truncated
@IF STAT2$ eq 107,(xxxx) ; . Invalid characters
have been substituted
@GTO xxxx . . Jump to Error Routine
@0199 . STAT1$ = Command Failed

@IF STAT3$ eq 2033,(xxxx) ; . No Messages
Available at this time
@IF STAT2$ eq 100,(xxxx) ; . The WebSphere MQ
interface is down
@IF STAT2$ eq 101,(xxxx) ; . Invalid Object
Handle specified
@IF STAT2$ eq 102,(xxxx) ; . Maximum open
Handles/Connections exceeded
@IF STAT2$ eq 104,(xxxx) ; . Non-String Data
Received
@IF STAT2$ eq 108,(xxxx) ; . BIS resources are
unavailable at this time
@GTO xxxx . . Jump to Error Routine

@QINQ (Inquire on Object Attribute)
Use the Inquire on Object Attribute (QINQ) statement to
retrieve selector attributes from a queue manager or queue.
The QINQ statement is one of a group of statements that
allow you to work with a message queue. See the following
statements for related information:
@QCLS (Close Message Queue Object)
@QGET (Get Message from Message Queue)
@QOPN (Open Message Queue Object)
@QPUT (Put Message on Message Queue)
You should be familiar with IBM WebSphere MQ and have a
thorough understanding of message queues before using
this statement.

Inquire on Object Attribute Statement Format
Format 1 - Use this format to inquire on a single selector
within an object type.
@QINQ,"valid_selector"[,lab] vobjhandle,o
vselectordata .
Format 2 - Use this format to inquire on all selectors within
an object type.
@QINQ,c,d[,lab] vobjhandle,o .

Field

Description

valid_selector Enter a valid selector (for
a Queue Manager or
Queue Name) enclosed in
quotation marks. See the
IBM WebSphere MQ
Application Programming
Reference or execute a
format 2 inquire to view a
list of all valid Queue
Manager or Queue Name
selectors.

c,d

Cabinet and drawer into
which the data should be
placed. To capture all
possible selector values,

the drawer width must be
at least 289 characters.

lab

Label to go to if an error or
warning message is
received (for example, if
selector is not for type).
See Returned Status.

vobjhandle

Variable containing an
existing handle.

o

Option ﬁeld (see Inquire
on Object Attribute
Options). You must specify
at least one option.

vselectordata Variable to capture the
format 1 selector
attribute. The maximum
size of selector attributes
is 256 characters. Most
selector values are much
smaller than 256 and can
be stored in small integer
variables.

Inquire on Object Attribute Options
Option

Description

M

Inquire on a Queue Manager

Q

Inquire on a Queue

Note: The option on the Inquire statement (QINQ) must
match the option on the Open statement (QOPN).

Inquire on Object Attribute Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the Inquire on Object
Attribute statement:
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the script errors. You should specify a
label to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$ values should be obtained at the
contingency label.

Inquire on Object Attribute Outcome
If the inquiry is successful, the selector attributes are
returned in the -0 result or returning variable. All returned
status reserved words are set to zero.
If an individual selector is not applicable to the type of the
queue whose attribute is being inquired:
integer attributes are set to -1.
character attributes are set to all asterisks (*).
(Format 1) - The label exit is taken and STAT1$=1,
STAT2$=6, and STAT3$=2068.
(Format 2) - The label exit is not taken and STAT1$=0,
STAT2$=0, and STAT3$=0.
If an error occurs when attempting the inquiry, the script
continues at the speciﬁed label. The STAT1$, STAT2$, and
STAT3$ values are set as described in Returned Status. If a
label is not speciﬁed, the script errors.
Returned Status
STAT1$ returns one of the following completion codes:
0 = OK
1 = Warning Message
2 = Command Failed
STAT2$ returns one of the following status codes:
0 = No Error

6 = WebSphere MQ Inquire error
100 = The WebSphere MQ interface is down
101 = Invalid Object Handle speciﬁed
200 = URTS NPESTAT error (2200 only)
201 = URTS TPENPE error (2200 only)
202 = URTS NPESETUP error (2200 only)
STAT3$ returns one of the following reason codes:
For STAT2$ values between 1 and 99, see the
WebSphere MQ Application Programming Reference for
a complete list of Reason Codes.
For STAT2$ values between 100 and 199, use the LSM
script statement to get the actual message.
For STAT2$ values between 200 and 299, contact the
Unisys Support Center for more information.

Inquire on Object Attribute Examples
This example performs an inquiry on all the attributes in the
queue manager.
The inquiry is executed on ‘my.qmgr’ using the I(M)
option on the QOPN.
If successful, a -0 is returned to the script.
If an error occurs in the QOPN or QINQ, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 'my-qmgr',,I(M) <vObjHandle>i6 .
@QINQ,0,I,0199 <vObjHandle>,M .
@DSP,-0 .
.WebSphere MQ Inquire on Queue Manager Selectors

If successful, a -0 is returned to the script.
If an error occurs in the QOPN or QINQ, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 '','MY.LOCAL.Q',I(Q) <vHanQ>i6 .
@QINQ,0,I,0199 <vHanQ>,Q .
@DSP,-0 .
.WebSphere MQ Inquire on Queue Selectors
* Selector . Selector Attribute
*==============================.=================

==============
MQCA_ALTERATION_DATE 2003-03-20

This example performs an inquiry on a speciﬁc selector.
The inquiry is executed on MY.LOCAL.Q using the I(Q)
option on the QOPN.
The selector (MQIA_CURRENT_Q_DEPTH) returns the
current number of messages on the queue.
If successful, the <vAttribute> variable contains the
current number of messages on the queue.
If an error occurs in the QOPN or QINQ, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 '','MY.LOCAL.Q',I(Q) <vObjHandle>i6 .
@QINQ,"MQIA_CURRENT_Q_DEPTH",0199 <vObjHandle>,Q
\
<vAttribute>i9 .

@QOPN (Open Message Queue
Object)
Use the Open Message Queue Object (QOPN) statement to
open a message queue object for subsequent work. The
QOPN statement is one of a group of statements that allow
you to work with a message queue. See the following
statements for related information:
@QCLS (Close Message Queue Object)
@QGET (Get Message from Message Queue)
@QINQ (Inquire on Object Attribute)
@QPUT (Put Message on Message Queue)
You should be familiar with IBM WebSphere MQ and have a
thorough understanding of message queues before using
this statement.

Open Message Queue Object Statement Format
@QOPN[,lab] [quemgr],[objname],o vobjhandle .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if an error or
warning message is
received (see Returned
Status).

quemgr

Queue Manager name (48
character maximum).
Default = blank (default
Queue Manager). Queue
Manager names are case
sensitive.

objname

Object name. The object
name must be a valid
Queue name (48 character
maximum). This ﬁeld must
be blank if the Queue
Manager is being opened for
an Inquire. Object names
are case sensitive.

o

Option ﬁeld (see Open
Message Queue Object

Options). You must specify
at least one option.

vobjhandle The object handle that must
be speciﬁed on subsequent
Message Queue statements.
This variable is deﬁned as
an I6.

Open Message Queue Object Options
Option

Description

G

Open for input from a queue
using the default access
type deﬁned by the queue
(either shared or exclusive
use).

G(S)

Open for input from a queue
using shared access.

G(E)

Open for input from a queue
using exclusive use.

I(M)

Open to inquire on a Queue
Manager. Do not use this
option in combination with
any other option.

I(Q)

Open to inquire on a Queue
name.

P

Open for output to a queue.

Open Message Queue Object Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Open Message
Queue Object statement:
The executing script must be a full character set (FCS)
report due to case-sensitivity of WebSphere naming
conventions.
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the script errors. You should specify a
label to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$ values should be obtained at the
contingency label.
Object handles may be passed across subroutine
boundaries, but not across application boundaries. For
example, an application may pass a handle as a CALL
(Call Subroutine) statement argument, or the subroutine
may create an object handle and return it to the caller.
When the current application terminates, all object
handles are released.
A maximum of 32 objects may be open at one time.
2200 Þ Þ
If Queue Manager names get externally re-deﬁned, the
Business Information Server MQS interface may need to
be re-initialized so the changes take aﬀect in Business
Information Server.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

In an executing script, only one Queue Manager may be
connected at a time. To disconnect a Queue Manager,
use the QCLS statement with the A option.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Open Message Queue Object Outcome
If the object can be opened, a handle to the object is
returned in the vobjhandle variable. All returned status
reserved words are set to zero.
If an error occurs when attempting the open, the script
continues at the speciﬁed label. The STAT1$, STAT2$, and
STAT3$ values are set as described in Returned Status. If a
label is not speciﬁed, the script errors.
Returned Status
STAT1$ returns one of the following completion codes:
0 = OK
1 = Warning Message
2 = Command Failed
STAT2$ returns one of the following status codes:
0 = No Error
1 = WebSphere MQ Connect
2 = WebSphere MQ Open
8 = WebSphere MQ Disconnect
100 = The WebSphere MQ interface is down
102 = Maximum open Handles/Connections exceeded
108 = Business Information Server resources are
unavailable at this time

200 = URTS NPESTAT error (2200 only)
201 = URTS TPENPE error (2200 only)
202 = URTS NPESETUP error (2200 only)
300 = Windows error code
STAT3$ returns one of the following reason codes:
For STAT2$ values between 1 and 99, see the
WebSphere MQ Application Programming Reference for
a complete list of Reason Codes.
For STAT2$ values between 100 and 199, use the LSM
script statement to get the actual message.
For STAT2$ values between 200 and 299, contact the
Unisys Support Center for more information.
For STAT2$ values between 300 and 399, see the
Windows System Error Codes for more information.

Open Message Queue Object Examples
This example opens a message queue to Put a Message.
The default queue manager (blank) is selected.
The "BISTestQueue" message queue is selected.
The P option (Put Message) is selected.
The object handle is captured in the variable
<vObjHandle> for use in subsequent statements.
If the message queue cannot be opened, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 '','BISTestQueue',P <vObjHandle>i6 .
This example opens a message queue to Get a Message.
The "my.qmgr" queue manager is selected.
The "MY.LOCAL.Q2" message queue is selected.
The G option (Get message) is selected.
The object handle is captured in the variable
<vHandle2> for use in subsequent statements.
If the message queue cannot be opened, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 'my.qmgr','MY.LOCAL.Q2',G <vHandle2>i6
.
This example opens a queue manager for an Inquire.

The "BISTestQueueManager" queue manager is
selected.
The message queue is left blank (inquire on Queue
Manager).
The I(M) option (inquire on Queue Manager) is selected.
The object handle is captured in the variable
<vObjHandle> for use in subsequent statements.
If the queue manager cannot be opened, the script
continues at label 0199.
@QOPN,0199 'BISTestQueueManager',,I(M)
<vObjHandle>i6 .

@QPUT (Put Message on Message
Queue)
Use the Put Message on Message Queue (QPUT) statement
to send a message to a message queue. The QPUT
statement is one of a group of statements that allow you to
work with a message queue. See the following statements
for related information:
@QCLS (Close Message Queue Object)
@QGET (Get Message from Message Queue)
@QINQ (Inquire on Object Attribute)
@QOPN (Open Message Queue Object)
You should be familiar with IBM WebSphere MQ and have a
thorough understanding of message queues before using
this statement.

Put Message on Message Queue Statement
Format
@QPUT[,c,d,r,l,q,tabs?,lab]
vobjhandle[,o,correlid,msgid,expiry,msgtyp,
replytoquemgr,replytoquenam] [vcorrelid,vmsgid].

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to send.

l

Line number at which to
start processing. Default
= line 2.

q

Number of lines to
process. Default = all
lines from the starting
line to the end of the
report.

tabs?

Convert tab characters
to space characters? Y or
N. Default = N.

lab

Label to go to if an error
or warning message is

received (see Returned
Status).

vobjhandle

Variable containing an
existing object handle.

o

Reserved for future use.

correlid

Correlation identiﬁer (24
character maximum). If
blank, Business
Information Server
generates a unique
character value (see Put
Message on Message
Queue Guidelines).

msgid

Message identiﬁer (24
character maximum). If
blank, Business
Information Server
generates a unique
character value (see Put
Message on Message
Queue Guidelines).

expiry

Time (in tenths of
seconds) the message
stays on queue before it
can be discarded.

Default = 0 (it stays on
the queue forever or
until it is removed by
another application).
Values can range
between 0 and
999999999
(approximately 3.17
years). Expired
messages are not sent to
the dead-letter queue.

msgtyp

Message type to send to
the queue. Values are:
1 = Requesting a reply
from the receiver (must
include the
replytoquemgr and
replytoquenam ﬁelds to
receive a reply).
2 = Replying to a
received message.
8 = A simple message
with no reply expected
(default).
# = Application deﬁned
(65536 - 999999999).

replytoquemgr If requesting a reply, this
ﬁeld must contain the
Queue Manager to
receive the reply (48
character maximum).

Default = blank (default
Queue Manager).

replytoquenam If requesting a reply, this
ﬁeld must contain the
Queue Name to receive
the reply (48 character
maximum).

vcorrelid

Variable to capture the
correlation identiﬁer sent
with the message (S24).

vmsgid

Variable to capture the
message identiﬁer sent
with the message (S24).

Put Message on Message Queue Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Put Message on
Message Queue statement:
If a label is not speciﬁed and the operation is
unsuccessful, the script errors. You should specify a
label to assist in evaluating errors. The STAT1$, STAT2$,
and STAT3$ values should be obtained at the
contingency label.
Trailing spaces are removed from each line in the report.
A carriage return (CR) and line feed (LF) are added to
the end of each line as termination characters. If a
report is not speciﬁed, a message length of zero is put
on the message queue.
Applications that are not Business Information Server
applications should never allow the Queue Manager to
generate unique correlation and message identiﬁers
because the generated values contain binary data that
is not displayable in the Business Information Server
system.
The Queue Manager, Queue, and Business Information
Server each have a deﬁned maximum message size
(based on the number of characters). There are three
maximums and the smallest maximum is the deciding
factor in determining the maximum message size. The
Business Information Server maximum message size is
999,999 characters (including control characters but not
including trailing spaces).

Put Message on Message Queue Outcome
If the message is successfully placed on the message
queue, the correlation and message identiﬁers are returned
in vcorrelid and vmsgid. All returned status reserved words
are set to zero.
If an error occurs when attempting the put, the script
continues at the speciﬁed label. The STAT1$, STAT2$, and
STAT3$ values are set as described in Returned Status. If a
label is not speciﬁed, the script errors.
Returned Status
STAT1$ returns one of the following completion codes:
0 = OK
1 = Warning Message
2 = Command Failed
STAT2$ returns one of the following status codes:
0 = No Error
4 = WebSphere MQ Put error
100 = The WebSphere MQ interface is down
101 = Invalid Object Handle speciﬁed
103 = Report does not exist or line is beyond the end of
the report
105 = Maximum data bytes exceeded

200 = URTS NPESTAT error (2200 only)
201 = URTS TPENPE error (2200 only)
202 = URTS NPESETUP error (2200 only)
STAT3$ returns one of the following reason codes:
For STAT2$ values between 1 and 99, see the
WebSphere MQ Application Programming Reference for
a complete list of Reason Codes.
For STAT2$ values between 100 and 199, use the LSM
script statement to get the actual message.
For STAT2$ values between 200 and 299, contact the
Unisys Support Center for more information.

Put Message on Message Queue Examples
This example puts a message on a message queue.
The text of the message is found in report 1A0.
Since no line or line quantity is speciﬁed, the entire
report (starting at line 2) is sent.
All tab characters (Y) are converted to space characters.
The system creates unique values for the correlid and
msgid (because these ﬁelds are blank) and returns the
system generated values in <vcorrelid> and <vmsgid>.
A msgtyp of 8 indicates a datagram message (send
message and expect no reply).
If the message cannot be sent, the script continues at
label 0199.
@QPUT,0,A,1,,,Y,0199 <vobjhandle>,,,,,8 \
<vcorrelid>s24,<vmsgid>s24 .
This example puts a message on a message queue.
The text of the message is in report 2A0 starting at line
1000 for 2000 lines.
Tab characters (N) are not converted to space
characters.
The user-deﬁned correlid (BIS-CorrelID-01) and msgid
(BIS-MsgId-01) are sent with the message.
An expiry of 5000 indicates that the message can be
deleted from the queue after 500 seconds.

A msgtyp of 1 indicates a request message (send
message and expect a reply).
If the message cannot be sent, the script continues at
label 0199.
@QPUT,0,A,2,1000,2000,N,0199 \
<vobjhandle>,,'BIS-CorrelID-01','BIS-MsgId01',5000,1 .
This example opens a queue, gets a message oﬀ a queue,
and replies to that message.
@QOPN,0199 '','MY.LOCAL.Q',G <vHanG>i6 .
@QGET,0,A,,0199 <vHanG> \
<vMsgTyp>i9,,<vMsgId>s48,<vReToMgr>s48,
<vReToQue>s48 .
@IF <vMsgTyp> ne 1,(xxxx) ; . Jump if No Need to
Reply
@QOPN,0199 <vReToMgr>,<vReToQue>,P <vHanP>i6 .
@QPUT,-0,,,,0199 <vHanP>,,<vMsgId>,,,2 . Send
Reply Note: By inserting the MsgId from the QGET
statement in the CorrelId field of the QPUT
statement, the originator of the message can
search for the reply.

@QREL (Release Message)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Release Message (QREL) statement to indicate that
Data Transfer Module (DTM) processing is complete. The
QREL statement is never required; however, you may want
to use it if, for example, your run reaches a point in which
you would not want to reprocess the input in the case of a
system failure.

Release Message Statement Format
@QREL .

Release Message Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Use the QREL statement only if the report was sent with
a Send Message, No Response (QSND) statement (from
another run) or a CALL 'QSND' (from a batch COBOL
program or COBOL TIP transaction). Do not use the
QREL statement if the report was sent with a Send
Message, Response Expected (QSNR) statement.
To determine how the report is sent, examine the
message type. For more information, see Functions for
Loading Variables.
Ü Ü 2200

@QRSP (Send Response Message)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Send Response Message (QRSP) statement, through
Data Transfer Module (DTM), when a run is initiated by a
Send Message, Expect Response (QSNR) statement (from
another run) or a CALL 'QSNR' (from a batch COBOL
program or COBOL TIP transaction). Only one response per
run is permitted.

Send Response Message Statement Format
@QRSP[,c,d,r,l,q,,eol,tabs?,err,,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to send.

l

First line to send. Default = 2.

q

Number of lines to send.
Default = all.

eol

End-of-line character. The
system normally truncates
lines after the last nonspace
character in reports passed to
DTM.
If a message is sent to a
Business Information Server
system, the system space-ﬁlls
lines to the width of the
receiving report. If a message
is sent to a system other than
a Business Information Server
system, you may need to send
spaces beyond the last
nonblank character on a line.

Place any graphic character in
this subﬁeld; the system
truncates the data line at the
ﬁrst occurrence.

tabs?

Translate tabs or other control
characters to spaces? Y, N, or
C. To remove them from the
data, enter C (for compress).
Default = N. This ﬁeld is
particularly useful for sending
a Business Information Server
report to a system other than
a Business Information Server
system.

err

Error status code to be
returned to the message
originator (1 to 8 characters;
no error = all spaces).

lab

Label to go to in case of error.

Send Response Message Outcome
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
Once the response is made, the system discards the
QSNR message that initiated the run, in which case the
message is no longer available for processing.
If an input process run owes a response and does not
make one, the system automatically invokes the DTERR
command to send a system message.
The drawer on the QSNR statement determines the
receiving drawer if you use the SU, SQ, or SR response
functions and the response returns while the run is
waiting for it. Otherwise, the system uses the default
input drawer. The drawer used on the QRSP statement
has no eﬀect on the drawer in which the response
message resides.
For more information on the SU, SQ, and SR response
functions, see @QSNR (Send Message, Expect
Response).

Send Response Message Examples
This example is a response to a QSNR statement or call. It
sends the current result and goes to label 50 in case of an
error.
@qrsp,-0,,,,,,,,0050 .
This example is a response to a QSNR statement or call. It
does not return a current result; it returns an error status
code.
@qrsp,,,,,,,,<error> .

Note: If you are not sending a report but are sending an
error status, there are only 8 commas.
Ü Ü 2200

@QSND (Send Message, No
Response)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Send Message, No Response (QSND) statement,
through Data Transfer Module (DTM), to send a message to
a named queue and expect no response.

Send Message, No Response Statement Format
@QSND[,c,d,r,l,q,,eol,tabs?,,sn,oq,acct,,lab]
dnfunc, dnqu[,[domain\]user,dept,pw,dnrun,vld] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to send. The cabinet
and drawer also deﬁne where
to place the response, if any.

l

First line to send. Default = 2.

q

Number of lines to send.
Default = all.

eol

End-of-line character. The
system normally truncates
lines after the last nonspace
character in reports passed to
DTM.
If a message is sent to a
Business Information Server
system, the system space-ﬁlls
lines to the width of the
receiving report. If a message
is sent to a system other than
a Business Information Server

system, you may need to
send spaces beyond the last
nonblank character on a line.
Place any graphic character
in this subﬁeld; the system
truncates the data line at the
ﬁrst occurrence.

tabs?

Translate tabs or other control
characters to spaces? Y, N, or
C. To remove them from the
data, enter C (for compress).
Default = N. This ﬁeld is
particularly useful for sending
a Business Information Server
report to a system other than
a Business Information Server
system.

sn

Station number of the sender
(if diﬀerent from your own).

oq

Name of the originating
queue, up to 12 characters.
Default = the value in
DTNAM$. Must be a valid
input queue.

acct

Accounting department
number, up to 12 characters.

Default = your department or
the value in DEPT$.
If you are sending a message
to another run, the system
captures the number in ﬁeld
6 (Chargeback Account
Number) when you specify
the INFO function of the
Queue Control (QCTL)
statement. If you are sending
a message to a TIP
transaction, the system
captures the number in the
MTQ-CONTROL-LINE (MCLACCOUNT) ﬁeld. See the
SCHDLR Reference for more
information.

lab

Label to go to in case of error.

dnfunc

Destination function:
Q-call the default input
process run of the destination
queue. (The Q function is not
supported by SCHDLR.) R-call
the named run or TIP
transaction.

dnqu

Name of the destination
queue. Must be a valid send

queue.

domain

An optional domain name
used to qualify the user-ID.

user

User-id at the destination.
Default = user-id of the user
executing the run, if
conﬁgured.

dept

Department number at the
destination (required only
when the destination is
another Business Information
Server system).

pw

Password at the destination.

dnrun

Name of the run or name of
TIP transaction to call (dnfunc
must equal R; not applicable
if dnfunc equals Q).

vld

Data to send. The data can
be a variable, constant,
reserved word, literal, or any
combination of these. Enclose
literal data containing spaces,
commas, or reverse slants in

apostrophes. Maximum = 80
items, up to 18 characters
each.
Capture data via INPUT$ in a
run or in the MTQTURNAROUND-LINE (MTLVBL) if the called run is a TIP
transaction. See the SCHDLR
Reference for more
information about capturing
variables in a TIP transaction.

Send Message, No Response Examples
This example sends a report to a queue named SCHDLR and
starts a TIP transaction called DXDTST: @qsnd,-0,,,,,,,,,,,50
r,schdlr,,,,dxdtst .

-0

Send the current result.

50

Go to label 50 in case of error.

r

Call the named run.

schdl

Send the report to a queue
named SCHDLR (default
name for SCHDLR).

dxdtst

Start TIP transaction DXDTST.

This example sends a report to a queue named MAPPER and
starts the MAPPER run called DXDTST: @qsnd,-0,,,,,,,,,,,50
r,mapper,newuser,7,psw,dxdtst .

-0

Send the current result.

50

Go to label 50 in case of

error.

r

Call the named run.

mapper

Send the report to the
queue named MAPPER.
Note that this is sending
to itself.

newuser,7,psw Use NEWUSER,7,PSW for
the sign-on.

dxdtst

Ü Ü 2200

Start the MAPPER run
DXDTST.

2200 Þ Þ

(if diﬀerent from your own).

subﬁeld.

each.
Capture data via INPUT$ in a
run or in the MTQTURNAROUND-LINE (MTL-VBL)
if the called run is a TIP
transaction. See the SCHDLR
Reference for more information
about capturing variables in a
TIP transaction.

rspfunc

The response function is a
required ﬁeld:
Q-give control to the default
input process run of the queue.
R-give control to rsrun.
SQ-wait for a response; if the
run times out, give control to
the default input process run of
the queue.
SR-wait for a response; if the
run times out, give control to
rsrun.

SU-wait for a response; if the
run times out, discard the
response.

rspqu

Queue to receive the response.
It must be currently initialized
as an input queue on this
Business Information Server
system. Default = the value in
DTNAM$.

domain

An optional domain name used
to qualify the user-ID.

user

User-id at the originating
queue (rspfunc must equal R
or SR).

dept

Department number at the
originating queue (rspfunc
must equal R or SR).

pw

Password at the originating
queue (rspfunc must equal R
or SR).

rsrun

Name of the run to call
(rspfunc must equal R or SR).

vld

Data to send. The data can be
a variable, constant, reserved
word, literal, or any
combination of these. Enclose
literal data containing spaces,
commas, or reverse slants in
apostrophes. Maximum = 80
items, up to 18 characters
each.

vstat

Variable to capture the status
code supplied by the responder
in case of error, from 1 to 8
characters long. If the variable
contains all spaces, there was
no error; otherwise, the run
goes to the label.

Notes:
The system derives the
error status codes from the
err subﬁeld in the Send
Response Message (QRSP)
statement. If the QRSP
statement is prepared by
the DTERR run, this is the
mnemonic for the error
code that caused the error.
Enter help,err-code for
information about the
error.

If the QRSP statement is
prepared by a user run (not
by the DTERR run), this
variable is deﬁned by the
user. A TIP transaction
cannot specify a value for
this ﬁeld.

Send Message, Expect Response Example
This example sends a report to a queue named SCHDLR,
starts a TIP transaction called DXDTST, and waits for a
response:
@qsnr,-0,,,,,,,,30,,,50 r,schdlr,,,,dxdtst su
<cod>h8 .

-0

Send the current result.

30

Wait up to 30 seconds for the
response.

50

Go to label 50 in case of error.

r

Call the named run.

schdir

Send the report to a queue
named SCHDLR.

dxdtst

Start the TIP transaction
DXDTST.

su

Wait for a response. If it times
out, discard the response.

<cod>h8 Load variable <cod> with
spaces or an error code.

Following this statement, if control returns at the next
statement, you received a normal response in the new -0
result in drawer A, and the variable <cod> contains spaces.
Note that the system returns the response in the same
drawer as the original report. If, however, control returns at
label 50, you received an abnormal response.
Ü Ü 2200

RADAR (Radar Chart)
Use the Radar Chart (RADAR) run to create a radar chart.
A radar chart is displayed on the screen or drawn on a
plotter. A radar chart consists of spokes radiating from a
center point and lines corresponding to values in a set of
data.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Radar Chart Control Line Format
RADAR[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

Radar Chart Colors
Color

Pen
Stall

Aqua

8

Black

1

Blue

2

Cyan

2

Gray

1

Green

4

Hot pink

3

Lime

6

Magenta

7

Pink

3

Red

3

Tan

5

Turquoise 8

Violet

7

White

1

Yellow

5

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Radar Chart Guidelines
Each label in the RAYS column represents one ray on the
chart. Each ray is made up of a scale and a series of points.
The run graphs, as one line, the data values in a column
under a particular caption.
To compare the captions, use a clock as a reference point.
The ﬁrst ray is at the three o'clock position, and the rest of
the rays proceed counterclockwise.

Radar Chart Report Fields
Field

Description

Text color

Color of the text and border.
Default = white. See Radar
Chart Colors.

Screen
color

Color of the screen. Default
= black. See Radar Chart
Colors.

Title

Optional title of 40
characters or fewer. This is
placed at the top of the chart
in the color speciﬁed for text.

Subtitle

Optional subtitle of 60
characters or fewer.

Date

Optional date of 18
characters or fewer, placed
in the lower left corner of the
chart. Y = Current date in
the format DD MMM YY.

Percentage Place symbols on each ray
markers?
that clarify the value of data
points? Y or N. Default = Y
(markers placed at 25, 50,
and 75 percent of the
distance from the center).

Captions

Text for a legend describing
the data. Type up to six
captions (at least one). Two
rows are available. Captions
are required for any column
containing data values. Each
data value you place in the
column below each caption
is graphed as a line; see the
Rays ﬁeld.

Color

Color of each line. See Radar
Chart Colors. Defaults are
shown on the report.

Rays

Label for each ray on the
chart, describing what it
represents. Enter names in
the column under the word
Rays on the report.

Scale

Maximum value for the Y
scale of each ray on the
chart.

@RAM (Relational Aggregate Modify)
Use the Relational Aggregate Modify (RAM) statement to
create tables, insert rows, or update a table with data stored
in a report. For more information, see @LGN (Log On to
Relational Database).
The RAM statement modiﬁes the database depending on the
option speciﬁed in the statement.
Before using the RAM statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.
The RAM statement supports the UPDATE, INSERT, and
DELETE options for access through the Open Database
Connectivity (ODBC) interface.
The RAM statement does not support the CREATE option for
access through the Open Database Connectivity (ODBC)
interface. You must use the SQL statement to create tables
through the ODBC interface.
The RAM statement supports the UPDATE, INSERT, and
DELETE options for access through the OLEDB interface.
The RAM statement does not support the CREATE option for
access through the OLEDB interface. You must use the SQL
statement to create tables through the OLEDB interface.

Relational Aggregate Modify Statement Format
@RAM[,c,d,r,lab,o,adj,dup/col,iqty,cqty,edsp?,cst
at,istat,
nchar,db] 'table[,options]'
[vstat,vcol,vins,vcom, vnbq,vdpk] .

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report used to create a table
or to insert, delete, or update
rows in the table.

lab

Label to go to if the status in
the vstat variable is other than
normal completion and does
not equal the value of the
cstat or istat subﬁelds.

o

Options subﬁeld. Default = I.
See Relational Aggregate
Modify Options.

adj

Options for adjusting the
decimal ﬁeld width (applicable
only when you specify B or C
in the o subﬁeld). See
Relational Aggregate Modify
Options. Default = N. The
adjustment varies by database
manager.
When creating a table, the
statement ignores the adj ﬁeld
for any ﬁeld that was deﬁned
using the UDyy.xx and the
UDxx formats. The database
manager returns a system
message if a decimal column
exceeds the maximum length
of decimal ﬁelds in the
database.

dup/col

Manages duplicate primary
key columns or invalid
characters in column names
derived from report headers.
See Relational Aggregate
Modify Options. Default = N.

iqty

Number of rows to delete,

update, or insert. Default =
deletes or updates all rows in
a report derived from a
speciﬁed table or inserts all
data lines from a speciﬁed
report into a table as rows.
This subﬁeld is meaningful
only when inserting, deleting,
or updating rows (when the o
subﬁeld = B, I, F, R, S, U, or
M).

cqty

Controls the frequency of data
commits.
If cqty is < 0, the COMMIT
command is never issued.
If cqty is = 0, the COMMIT
command is issued after
successful completion of the
operation by MRI.
If cqty is > 0 and o subﬁeld =
C or B, the COMMIT command
is issued after creating the
table, and before inserting any
rows.
If cqty is > 0 and o subﬁeld =
E or D, the COMMIT command
is issued after dropping the
table or deleting rows.

If cqty is > 0 and o subﬁeld =
B, I, F, R, S, U, or M, the
COMMIT command is issued
after each insertion, deletion,
or update of the value of cqty
rows; the ﬁnal COMMIT
command is issued after the
operation is complete.
Use caution when cqty > 0. It
is possible for an error to
occur after some changes are
committed but before all
changes are made.
Some database managers
automatically issue a COMMIT
command after certain
operations, such as creating a
table; therefore, cqty < 0 may
not apply.

edsp?

Create a result if an error
occurs? Y or N. The result
contains the last syntax sent
to the database manager and
the text of the system
message. It also contains the
status code and column where
the error occurred, if
applicable.
If you supplied a label, the run
continues at the label and the

error information resides in
the -0 result.

cstat

Alternate valid status if the o
subﬁeld = D, E, R, S, U, M, I, F,
or B (create table); otherwise,
it is ignored.

istat

Alternate valid status if the o
subﬁeld = D, E, R, S, U, M, I, F,
or B (insert table); otherwise,
it is ignored. (This overrides
the dup/col subﬁeld = N or
blank.)

nchar

Controls treatment of null
values. If you use a special
(nonalphanumeric) value, the
statement treats character
ﬁelds ﬁlled with the speciﬁed
value and blank decimal ﬁelds
as null.
C - treats blank or nonexistent
character ﬁelds as null.
D - treats blank or nonexistent
decimal ﬁelds as null.
B - treats blank or nonexistent
character and decimal ﬁelds
as null.

db

Database name.

table

Name of the relational table.

options

Contains optional information
for creating, deleting, or
updating the table, including
storage area, data access
clause, Where clause, and
column names (col,col,...). See
Relational Aggregate Modify
Options.

vstat

Variable to capture the status
of the action performed.
Deﬁne this variable as type A,
seven characters in length (for
example, v1a7).

vcol

Variable to capture the
number of the character
(within the syntax generated
by the RAM statement) where
an error was detected. Deﬁne
this variable as type I, ﬁve
characters in length (for
example, v2i5).

vins

Variable to capture the actual

number of rows inserted (if o
= B, I, or F), the actual
number of rows deleted (if o =
D, E, R, or S), or the actual
number of rows updated (if o
= U or M). This value may
diﬀer from the actual number
of rows committed to the
database, depending on the
values of the cqty and vcom
subﬁelds.

vcom

Variable to capture the actual
number of COMMIT commands
issued to the database. This
variable is valid for all options.

vnbq

Variable to capture the actual
number of nonblank columns
inserted into the table. If o = R
or S, the variable contains the
number of primary key
columns in the table used to
perform the speciﬁc delete. If
o = U or M, the variable
contains the number of
columns in the table that were
updated.

vdpk

Variable to capture the
following information if the
dup/col subﬁeld contains Y, D,

R, or B. If o = B, I, or F, the
variable contains the number
of duplicate primary keys. If o
= R or S, the variable contains
the number of rows not
deleted. If o = U or M, the
variable contains the number
of rows not updated.

Relational Aggregate Modify Options
Options for the o Subﬁeld

Option

Description

B

Creates a table using the
heading and information lines
from the report and inserts all
data lines from the report as
data rows in the new table.
See Relational Aggregate
Modify Reserved Words for
more information. For ODBC
databases, this option may
work but is not supported for
some column types.

C

Creates a table using the
heading and information lines
from the report. Place
additional information
required to create the table in
the heading divider line and
the three information lines
following it. See Relational
Aggregate Modify Guidelines
for more information. For
ODBC databases, this option
may work but is not supported
for some column types.

D

Drops the table from the
database.

E

Deletes rows in the table. You
can specify the condition for
deletion in the Where clause
in the options subﬁeld. Default
= deletes all rows in the table.

I

Inserts all data lines from the
report as data rows in the
table. The sequence of
columns in the report must
match the sequence of
columns in the table
deﬁnition.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the I option.

Ü Ü 2200

F

Inserts all data lines from the
report as data rows in the

table. The report must be
sorted so that all primary key
columns are listed ﬁrst.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the F option.

Ü Ü 2200

R

Deletes rows in the table
where the primary key or
unique index matches the
primary key in the report. The
report must contain all
columns that are part of the
primary key, and the sequence
of columns in the report must
match the sequence of
columns in the table deﬁnition.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the R option.

Ü Ü 2200

S

Deletes rows in the table
where the primary key or
unique index matches the
primary key in the report. The
report must contain all
columns that are part of the
primary key, and the report
must be sorted so that all
primary key columns are listed
ﬁrst.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the S option.

Ü Ü 2200

T

Deletes rows in the table,
using all report ﬁelds as the
key for deletion. This option
compares values of all ﬁelds in
the report to values in the
table and deletes rows where a
match is found. Use this option

when the table does not
contain a primary key or
unique index.

Note: If your table does not ﬁt in the report, use this option
with caution. Rows may be deleted unexpectedly.

U

Updates nonprimary key
columns for rows matching the
primary key columns in the
report. Use this option only if a
primary key or unique index is
deﬁned for the table. If you do
not specify the optional
column names (col,col...in the
options subﬁeld), the ﬁelds in
the report must exactly match
the columns in the table you
are updating. The number of
columns updated in the table
is equal to the number of
nonprimary key ﬁelds in the
report.
If you want to skip columns, or
if the columns are out of order,
you must use the optional
column clause. All columns in
the report, including the
primary key columns, must be
listed in the order that they
exist in the report. The
statement ﬁlls any columns

listed in the column clause that
do not exist in the report with
default values of blanks, zeros,
or nulls.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the U option.

Ü Ü 2200

M

Updates nonprimary key
columns for rows matching the
primary key columns in the
report. The report must be
sorted so that all primary key
columns are listed ﬁrst. If you
do not specify an optional
column clause, the ﬁelds in the
report must exactly match the
columns in the table you are
updating. The number of
columns updated in the table
is equal to the number of
nonprimary key ﬁelds in the
report.
If you want to skip columns, or
if the columns are out of order,

you must use the optional
column clause. All columns in
the report, with the exception
of the primary key columns,
must be listed in the order that
they exist in the report. The
statement ignores any primary
key columns listed in the
column select clause and ﬁlls
any columns listed in the
column clause that do not
exist in the report with default
values of blanks, zeros, or
nulls.

2200 Þ Þ

You must specify a full
character set (FCS) report
when using the M option.

Ü Ü 2200

J

Issues only a COMMIT
command to the database
manager (ignores the value in
the cqty subﬁeld). You must
enter a value in the table
subﬁeld, even if it is only a
space enclosed in apostrophes.

Option

Description

Name of the storage area or

(applicable if the o subﬁeld

managers).

create
(ignored if the o subﬁeld
clauses

additional create clause from

Clause identifying rows for

Optional column names for

all columns to be updated
and primary key columns. List

to be updated. The statement
