Relational Aggregate Modify Reserved Words
If the option is B, I, or F, STAT1$ contains the number of
rows inserted into the table. If the option is D, E, R, or S,
STAT1$ contains the number of rows deleted. If the option is
U or M, STAT1$ contains the number of rows updated.
STAT2$ contains the actual number of COMMIT commands
issued to the database (valid for all options, and valid even
if an error occurs).
STAT3$ contains one of the following values:
Status of the last syntax sent to the database manager
if no error occurs.
Zero if an error occurs.
A system message number if an interface error occurred
(for example, the database is inoperative) and you
supplied a label. Use the Load System Message (LSM)
statement to obtain the text of the message.

Relational Aggregate Modify Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
The RAM statement derives the column names in the
new table from the headings on the asterisk lines of the
report. The sequence of columns in the new table is
identical to the sequence of headings of the asterisk
lines in the report.
If the o subﬁeld contains B or C, the report identiﬁed in
the c,d,r subﬁelds must contain one or more asterisk
lines preceding the heading divider line.
For RDMS databases, if you create a table without
information lines, the PK consists of the ﬁrst 16 ﬁelds in
the report.
For RDMS databases, the report must also contain some
additional information for creating the table, including
data types, primary key indicators, and null indicators.
The following illustration identiﬁes the lines in a sample
report that the RAM statement uses to create tables:
Asterisk lines *ST.STATUS. . PRODUCT
.SERIAL.PRODUC .ORDER.CUST.PRODUC...
*CD. DATE . . TYPE .NUMBER. COST .NUMBR.CODE.
PLAN ...
Heading divider
*==.======.==.=========.======.=========.=====.==
==.======...
Info line 1 *C UC7 C D D2 UD8.2 X C
Info line 2 * p A D
Info line 3 * N N
IP 831224 LS BLACKBOX1 436767 1234.95 84389 AMCO

831223
IP 831225 LS BLACKBOX1 436768 84390 AMCO 831223
IP 831219 LS BLACKBOX2 637071 77.66 84353 INTR
831218
Data lines OR 840110 LS BLACKBOX4 94754 ARCO
SC 840110 LS BLACKBOX5 675281 97441 FEDS 840131
IP 831222 LS BLACKBOX5 737582 84040 AMCO 831222
SH 831203 LS BLACKBOX0 746327 54437 FEDS 831201
SH 831202 LS BLACKBOX6 368061 54438 FEDS 831201

Creating a Table with the RAM Statement
You can assign the data type and size for a column on
information line 1. The available data types vary by
database manager.
If you omit information line 1, the default data type is
character type.

Note: You can omit all the information lines or have a blank
information line. You cannot have information lines 2 and 3
without an information line 1, or an information line 1 and 3
without an information line 2.
You can let the database manager assign the column size
internally for certain data types. If the column size must be
deﬁned, you can specify the size on information line 1
following the data type. If you do not specify the column
size on information line 1, the RAM statement determines
the column size by the number of positions occupied by the
column on the heading divider line.
To omit a column, place an X in the column or omit the
column name.
You can identify primary keys on information line 2. The
following values are valid on information line 2:
P (primary key - ascending)
A (primary key - ascending)
D (primary key - descending)
You can identify columns in which null values are permitted
on information line 3. Type an n in line 3 in the ﬁrst position

occupied by the column. If you do not identify the null
indicator, null values will not be permitted in this ﬁeld.

Relational Aggregate Modify Examples
This example creates an empty table, using the RAM
statement with the C option. The RAM statement speciﬁes a
table name and identiﬁes a report that contains column
names and information lines:
@RAM,0,a,599,,c,,,,,y,,,,agency
'houses,storage.area' <error>a5 .

0,a,599

Creates the table from the
ﬁeld headings and
information lines in report
599A.

c

Creates a table.

y

Creates an error report if
necessary.

agency

Speciﬁes the database
name.

houses

Creates a table named
houses in the database.

storage.area Speciﬁes the schema and
storage area for the table

(required only for RDMS).

<error>a5

Loads <error> with the
status of the action
performed.

Example using the D option
This example drops the table RAM_TEST from the database
<db>.
@RAM,,,,199,d,,,,,y,,,,<db> 'RAM_TEST' <error>a5
.
Example using the C option
This example creates a table RAM_TEST that only includes
the column names from the input report. No additional data
lines are added to the table, regardless of the input report
contents. No Primary Key column is identiﬁed.
@RAM,0,b,2,199,c,,,,,y,,,,<db> 'RAM_TEST'
<error>a5 .
The following illustration shows the input report.
*St.Status.By.ProductTy.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.
Produc.Produc.ShipDa.ShipO.Spc.
*Cd.Date .In.pe .Number.Cost .Numbr.Code.Plan
.Actual.te .rder .Cod.

831224

831225

The RAM statement creates the table RAM_TEST in database
<db> with the same column headers as in the input report.
The data from the input report is not included in the output
report.
*St.Status.By.ProductTy.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.
Produc.Produc.ShipDa.ShipO.Spc.
*Cd.Date .In.pe .Number.Cost .Numbr.Code.Plan

Example using the B option
This example creates a table RAM_TEST that builds the
column names and inserts the data lines from the report
into the table. The column “OrderNumbr” is the Primary Key
column.
@RAM,0,b,2,199,b,,,,,y,,,,<db> 'RAM_TEST'
<error>a5 .
The following illustration shows the input report.
*St.Status.By.ProductTy.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.

@RAR (Register Abort Routine)
Use the Register Abort Routine (RAR) statement to register a
routine to be executed if the user aborts a run by pressing
Abort. Since all variables, temporary results, and the
current output area remain unaltered and are accessible to
the abort routine, the routine can capture information
currently being processed even though the user aborted the
run. Use the RAR statement in a background run to register
an abort routine for use with the Stop function.
If a user aborts a run and the run has an abort routine
registered, the system runs the abort routine and cancels
any previously registered abort and error routines and
update locks.
2200 Þ Þ
External abort routines must be in the same character set
type as the calling run control report.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Abort Routine Statement Format
@RAR[,c,d,r] lab .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the external
routine. (Default is current
c,d,r).

lab

Label in the report where an
abort routine begins.
To begin the routine at the ﬁrst
line of the external run control
report, use LIN1 in this ﬁeld.

Register Abort Routine Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Place the RAR statement as close as possible to the
beginning of the run. If the user aborts the run before
the run executes an RAR statement, the run has no
abort routine to go to.
The system cancels the abort routine when the run
terminates, but you can also use the Clear Abort Routine
(CAR) statement to cancel the routine.
If a user presses Abort while an abort routine is
executing, the run aborts.
Every called routine (through the Call Subroutine [CALL]
statement) should have an abort routine registered or
the results may be unpredictable.
Abort routines registered in called routines (through the
Call Subroutine [CALL] statement) override previously
registered abort routines. When control returns to the
calling run, the system automatically reregisters the
original abort routine.
If you do not register an abort routine in the called
routine and a user aborts the run, control goes to the
abort routine in the calling run.
Cabinet restrictions from the run also apply to the abort
routine.
A noninterim display speciﬁed by any of the following
statements in your abort routine continues normal run

processing when a user tries to abort a run: DSG, DSM,
DSP, INP, MBX, OUT, SC.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The values of IO$ and LLP$ reﬂect the number of I/Os
and LLPs used in the abort routine and the run.
The abort routine cannot contain the statements GTO
RPX, RAR, RER, or RUN. You can use an RSR statement
only if it starts an internal subroutine.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The values of IO$ and LLP$ reﬂect the number of I/Os
and LLPs used in the abort routine itself.
The abort routine cannot contain the statements GTO
RPX, LNK, RAR, RER, or RUN. You can use an RSR
statement only if it starts an internal subroutine.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Abort Routine Reserved Words
The following reserved words contain zero until a run aborts.

ACTINP$

The handle of the main
window (WND$).

AXDRW$

Drawer letter of the run
control report where the run
aborted.

XDRW$

Drawer number of the run
control report where the run
aborted.

XFUN$

Run function call last executed
before the run aborted.

XLINE$

Line number in the run control
report where the run aborted.

XRPT$

Report number of the run
control report where the run
aborted.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

The system resets the reserved words to zero whenever the
run executes a noninterim display with the DSG, DSM, DSP,
OUT, or SC statements.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The system resets the reserved words to zero whenever the
run executes a noninterim display with the DSG, DSM, DSP,
INP, MBX, OUT, or SC statements.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Abort Routine Example
This example registers the external abort routine at label 5
in report 4E0:
@RAR,0,E,4 0005 .

RDB and @RDB (Run Debug)
Use the Run Debug (RDB) function or RDB statement to
locate and correct any errors in a script while it is executing.
Use the RDB statement in a script instead of the manual
function request to start the debugging at a speciﬁc point in
a script. This is especially useful for debugging large or
complex scripts.

Business Information Server Developer
Workshop Debugger Interface
The Developer Workshop provides a comprehensive
debugger for applications and subroutines, including those
written in JavaScript. It allows you to establish conditional
and line breakpoints, track and evaluate expressions during
execution, evaluate variables, and examine execution ﬂow.
For complete information on using this powerful tool, see
the Business Information Server Developer Workshop Help.
By default, BIS attempts to connect to the Developer
Workshop that might be executing on your workstation. This
connection is attempted only if you are initiating the script
from the Graphical Interface. If BIS is unable to connect with
the Developer Workshop or if you are not initiating the script
from a Graphical Interface then it reverts to the legacy run
debugger.

Note: The BIS administrator has the capability to disable
the default action of connecting to the Developer Workshop.
For details on the Legacy Debugger, see the Legacy Run
Debugger topic.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You must have write access to the drawer of the run control
report to debug scripts; otherwise the system ignores the
RDB request. The script must not be registered in the
Master Script Registration report if you want to execute RDB
manually. However, you can use the RDB statement in the
script.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

2200 Þ Þ
You must be registered as an application designer and you
must have write access to the drawer of the run control
report to debug scripts; otherwise the system ignores the
RDB request.
Ü Ü 2200
RDB is turned oﬀ before starting the script on the remote
Business Information Server site. If you want to continue
RDB into the remote script, it is possible to start RDB in the
remote script. It will be necessary to restart RDB, if you
want to continue RDB when returning from the remote script
initiated by NRM.

Run Debug Control Line Format
RDB script[,v,...,v] .

Field

Description

script

Name of the script to debug.

v,...v

Data supplied to the script to
be initialized through INPUT$.

Run Debug Statement Format
Use this command to locate and correct any errors in a
script while it is executing.
Format 1 - Default
@RDB .
By default, it ﬁrst attempts to connect with the Developer
Workshop client. If it is unable to connect with the Workshop
then it reverts to the legacy debugger, if permitted,
otherwise the command is ignored. If the script is already
debugging, then it generates a breakpoint halt.
Format 2 - Developer Workshop
@RDB[,WorkshopHostAddress,WorkshopPortNumber] .
This format allows debugging to a speciﬁed remote
Developer Workshop client, which gives you the capability
of debugging background scripts or any other script that
does not have a user workstation associated with it. If it is
unable to connect with the Workshop, then the command is
ignored. If the script is already debugging, then the format
is ignored and it generates a breakpoint halt.

Field

Description

WorkshopHostAddress The name or IP
address of the
host on which the
Developer

Workshop is
running. (The
default value is
the host
associated with
the current
session.)

WorkshopPortNumber The port number
on which the
Developer
Workshop client is
listening. (The
default port
number is
57006.)

Debugging Background Runs and ICE Services
These examples start a background run or ICE service
debug session on the Developer Workshop. Ensure the
Developer Workshop is running on the
WorkshopHostAddress speciﬁed system where you intend to
start debugging, otherwise the debug session does not
start. A WorkshopHostAddress must be speciﬁed to debug
background runs and ICE services. The
WorkshopPortNumber is only needed if the Developer
Workshop listener port has been changed. Do not add a
value for this ﬁeld unless required. If a WorkshopPortNumber
is not speciﬁed, the default port number will be used.
Example 1 – Debugging to localhost (BIS and the Developer
Workshop running on the same system)
@RDB, localhost .
Example 2 – Debugging to a speciﬁc system (my-testsystem) specifying the Developer Workshop default port
(port number is not required)
@RDB, my-test-system, 57006 .

@RDC (Read Continuous)
Use the Read Continuous (RDC) statement to read report
lines or line segments and load variables with the data.
The statement generates one line in the output area for
each line it reads from the input report.

Note: Use only one line in the output area for the variables
to be written by the RDC statement.

Read Continuous Statement Format
@RDC,c,d,r[,l,q,ltyp,lab,contin?] cc vdata .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which lines are to
be read.

l

Line number at which to start
reading. Default = line 1.

q

Number of lines to read.
Default = all lines.

ltyp

Line type to read. Default = all
types.

lab

Label to go to if the starting
line or report does not exist. If
you do not specify a label and
the starting line or report does
not exist, the run continues.

contin?

Recognize a backslash (\) as a
continuation character on the

output line? Y or N. Default =
N.

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to be read.
You do not have to specify
ﬁelds in order from left to
right, but be sure to assign the
variables that capture the
data in matching sequence.

vdata

Variables to capture the data.
Maximum number of variables
= 80.

Read Continuous Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RDC statement:
You must follow the RDC statement with an output line
because an RDC statement does the following in
sequence:
Reads a line
Places the data read in variables
Writes the data to the output area
Reads the next line
To have the run automatically initialize a variable to the
size of a ﬁeld, specify only the variable name and type
(for example, <data1>h, <data2>s, and so on). The
RDC statement initializes the variables to the
appropriate size for the ﬁelds it reads.
When you load variables of a speciﬁed size, the size of
each variable determines the number of columns the
statement reads. For example, in the following
statement <data> contains ten characters of data
starting with column 5, even though you speciﬁed 2
characters in the cc ﬁeld:
@rdc,0,b,2 5-2 <data>h10 .
2200 Þ Þ

Note: The RDC statement ignores columns that exceed the
line size of the report being read and is not considered an
error condition.

Ü Ü 2200

Reading All Lines Example
This example reads all lines of the current result, captures
the data in <data>, and places the variable in the output
area: @rdc,-0 1-256 <data>s256 .
<data>

Reformatting Data Example
This example uses an RDC statement to read the heading
lines deﬁned for drawer D from report 0D0, which start at
line 18, uses another RDC statement to read data from
report 0B2, then reformats the data in the output area:
@brk,0,d .
@rdc,0,d,0,18,4 1-132 <heading>s132 .
<heading>
@rdc,0,b,2,6 1-80 <data>s80 .
<data>(1-3)<data>(38-6) <data>(14-11) <data>(44-6)
@brk .

Placing Fields under Headings Example
This example places the headings Shipping Order and Date
Last Status Change in the output area. Then it reads data
from two ﬁelds, capturing the data in <order> and <date>,
and places the data in the output area under the speciﬁed
headings: Shipping Order Date Last Status Change
@rdc,0,b,2,6,50,|,099 'statusdate','shiporder' <date>i,
<order>h .
<order> <date>

0,b,2

Read lines in report 2B0.

6

Start reading at line 6.

50,|

Scan 50 lines, but read tab
lines only.

099

Go to label 99 if line 6 or the
report does not exist.

'statusdate' Read data from the Status
Date ﬁeld.

'shiporder'

Read data from the Ship
Order ﬁeld.

<date>I

variable to the size of the
Status Date ﬁeld, to capture
the data read from the ﬁeld.

<order>h

Initialize <order> as a type
H variable to the size of the
Ship Order ﬁeld, to capture
the data read from the ﬁeld.

<order>
<date>

Place the data in the output
area in this format under the
headings Shipping Order
and Date Last Status
Change.

RDI (Relational Database Interface)
Use the Relational Database Interface (RDI) command to
interface between the system and several relational
database management systems. The Relational Database
Interface command enables software users to easily access
data stored in relational databases by responding to
prompts on menus.
One or more databases must be registered. See your
administrator for more information.

Relational Database Interface Control Line
Format
RDI[,command,drawer,db,user-id,psw]

Field

Description

command Displays a command-speciﬁc
menu, where command is one
of the following commands or
utilities: select create

destroy
join sql cview
insert utility dview
delete alter update
restrict
See Relational Database
Interface Commands and
Relational Database Interface
Utilities.

drawer

Drawer to contain the result.

db

Database name (if diﬀerent
from the default).

user-id

User-id for database access (if

diﬀerent from the default).

psw

Password for database
access. This is only required
for some database managers.

Relational Database Interface Example
A relational model can be described as data being
represented by two-dimensional tables of rows and
columns, providing a simple view of data for users. The twodimensional tables are called relational tables. Through the
MRI interface, you can retrieve and update data in relational
tables. Following is a sample relational table containing real
estate information: *HOUS. . .
*E_NO. LOCATION .PRICE.HOUSES_DESCRIPTION
*====.================.=====.=========
===============
H101 Minneapolis 150 3 bedroom rambler
H125 Minneapolis 65 2 bdrm-good starter home
H134 Minneapolis 265 eightplex
H180 Minneapolis 75 Duplex - 2 bdrm a side
H224 Minneapolis 95 3 bdrm rambler

RDI and SQL
Use the SQL to retrieve data in a relational database. SQL
commands enable you to perform the following operations:
Create and alter the tables in which relational data is
stored
Retrieve data from single or multiple tables
Insert, change, or delete data stored in relational tables
You can issue SQL commands in several ways. For example,
you can formulate an SQL statement to retrieve data and
submit the statement directly to the database. SQL
statements can also be embedded in an application
program, such as a run, that requires access to data stored
in a relational database. These methods require knowledge
of programming and expertise in the SQL language.
RDI enables you to submit standard SQL statements in a
familiar and convenient format. RDI eliminates the need to
formulate complete SQL statements through the use of
menus and context-sensitive help screens.
To save an SQL command in a report
1. Select the desired SQL task from the RDI menu and
respond to the series of prompts on the assistance
screen.
2. After the information is entered, RDI translates this
information into an SQL command and submits it to the
database.

3. The data selected by the SQL command is then returned
as a report, or the relational operation is performed.
4. Press ShwSQL to see the SQL statement generated by
RDI.
5. Press SveSQL to save the SQL statement in a report.

Relational Database Interface Commands
Command Description

Select

Retrieves rows from a single
relational table into a report.

Join

Retrieves rows from two or
more relational tables into a
report.

Insert

Adds new rows to a
relational table.

Delete

Deletes rows from a
relational table.

Update

Updates relational tables
with changes you request.

Create

Deﬁnes new tables in the
database.

SQL

Submits SQL syntax directly
to the database. You can also

save SQL syntax generated
by RDI for any operation for
use at a later date.

Relational Database Interface Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To select a command from the RDI menu, tab to the
selection you want and transmit.
RDI menu commands not accessible with your user-id
are displayed in blue on a color monitor, or in low
intensity on some black and white monitors. See your
system administrator for information about privileges
assigned to your user-id.
If a database requires a password, the DBMS menu is
displayed. To accept the default database and user-id,
enter a password, if required, in the appropriate ﬁeld.
You can display the DBMS menu manually and select a
database, by pressing DBMS from the RDI main menu.
To change the default parameters used during RDI
operations, press Envmnt and enter the new
information.
The RDI session will be read-only, when a RDMS 2200
user is conﬁgured for multiple logons = Y and runs RDI
in one of the following ways:
"RDI,SELECT,F0,rdms2200db,userid,password"
or
"RDI,JOIN,F0,rdms2200db,userid,password"
or
"RDI,,F0,rdms2200db,userid,password" where the ﬁrst
action is a SELECT or JOIN

The choice of specifying "SELECT" or "JOIN" as the ﬁrst
action within RDI puts the RDI session into a read-only
thread. This only applies to RDMS 2200 database
models and when the user is conﬁgured for multiple
logons = Y.

@RDL (Read Line)
Use the Read Line (RDL) statement to read a report line or
segments of a line and load variables with the data.

Notes:
You can use either the RDL statement or the Read Line
Next (RLN) statement in a loop to read several lines;
however, the RLN statement is slightly more eﬃcient
because the run interpreter does not need to determine
the report from which to read.
Generally, the report opened by RDL is closed by all
functions except the following functions: BRG, BRK,
CHG, DEF, ESR, GTO, IF, LDV, LOK, RDC, RLN, RNM, RSR,
ULK, LOG, RER, RAR, CAR, CER, DEC, INC, MSG, RDB,
JUV, DC, CAB, DRW, LNG, XQT, LCV, RPW, PNT, KEY,
NOF, SCH, CLK, USE, NRT, LDA, FKY, and HSH.
The report opened by RDL is closed to prevent
deadlocks from occurring.

Line number to read.

not specify a label and the line
or report does not exist, the
run fails.

conﬁgured to be compatible
with how the Windows Server,

handle the absence of an error

LINE$ contains the next line number to read. If the last line
read was beyond the end of the report, LINE$ contains a
zero.

Release Display and @REL
Use the Release Display (^) function or REL statement to
release the current screen and display the active screen.
With the REL statement, the command also terminates the
run.
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The active screen is displayed.
The system releases the current report or result.
The system clears any line buﬀers.
The system releases any runs, temporarily saved
reports or results, and previous error conditions.

Note: The Release Display command does not sign you oﬀ
the system. For more information on signing oﬀ, see X and
@XIT (Sign Oﬀ User Session).

Release Display Control Line Format
^
To release the screen, type ^ (a caret) on the control line
and press Transmit.
More ways to release the screen include the following:
Press Quit if it is displayed on the function key bar.
Press Return until the active screen is displayed.

Release Display Statement Format
@REL .

@RELRNM (Release Rename)
2200 Þ Þ
Use this command to disassociate a report from a rename
value.

Release Rename Statement Format
@RELRNM rename_list where rename_list is one or
more rename values in the range of -0 to MAXRNM$, separated by commas. Each rename value
can optionally specify a quantity after a colon
separator. If you do not specify a quantity, one
is assumed.
A rename can be released even if there is no current report
associated with that rename. You can also specify a rename
value multiple times.
The entire rename_list is parsed ﬁrst before any renames
are released. If there is a syntax error in the rename_list.
If variables are used, specify one variable for the rename
value and another variable for the quantity. You cannot use
one variable to specify both subﬁelds.

Release Rename Examples
@RELRNM -5
@RELRNM -5,-2:3,-14
Ü Ü 2200

REP and @REP (Replace Report)
Use the Replace Report (REP) function or REP statement to
replace a report, referred to as the receiving report, with the
current, or issuing report. The receiving report can be either
an existing report or a new report. You can use the Replace
Report command to save results as permanent reports.
For the Replace Report manual function, the current, or
issuing, report (containing the replacement data) must be
on display. Also, you can replace the receiving report only if
you were the last person to update it.
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command replaces the report you specify with the
current report and displays the newly replaced report.
The REP statement replaces the receiving report with
the issuing report.
If the receiving report does not exist, the command
creates the report with the number you specify.
If you do not specify a title, the command adds the title
of the current report to the receiving report.
2200 Þ Þ
If your system is set up to retain save dates when you
replace reports, to avoid having the save date
overwritten by the title, either do not enter a title or
include a save date (@YYMMDD) at the beginning of the
title.
Ü Ü 2200

If the reports have unequal line length, the command
space-ﬁlls or truncates the lines of the current report to
match the line length of the receiving report.
If the issuing report contains non-displayable data
(binary, encoded, or run-time), the line length must
match the receiving report.
The command clears any read and write passwords that
exist on the report being replaced.
When using the Replace Report manual function, to
regain the original report immediately after replacing it,
press Undo.

Replace Report Control Line Format
REP [report ,title]

Field

Description

report

Receiving report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

title

The new title of the receiving
report (up to 43 alphanumeric
characters).

Replace Report Statement Format
@REP[,ic,id,ir],rc,rd,rr[,rt] [vtitle] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report. If the issuing
report is the current (-0)
result, these subﬁelds need
not be speciﬁed.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report.

rt

Place a Y in this subﬁeld to
create a source-protected
version of the report being
replaced.

vtitle

A variable containing the title
of the receiving report (up to
43 alphanumeric characters).

Replace Report Example
This example replaces the contents of report 3B20 with
report 2B0:
@ldv <title>s43=`this is the title' .
@rep,0,b,2,20,b,3 <title> .

@RER (Register Error Routine)
Use the Register Error Routine (RER) statement to register a
routine to be executed if the script encounters an error.
Since all variables, temporary results, and the current
output area remain unaltered and are accessible to the error
routine, use the routine to help debug the script.
If an error occurs, the system runs the registered error
routine and cancels any previously registered abort and
error routines and update locks. See also Register Error
Routine Reserved Words.
2200 Þ Þ
External error routines must be in the same character set
type as the calling run control report.
See Generating Diagnostic Reports (DIAG) for collecting
diagnostic information.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Error Routine Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@RER[,c,d,r] lab .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@RER[,c,d,r] lab[,opts] .
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the external
routine. (Defaults to the
current c,d,r).

lab

Label in the report where an
error routine begins.
To begin the routine at the ﬁrst
line of the external run control
report, use LIN1 in this ﬁeld.

2200 Þ Þ

opts

See Diagnostic Options.

Diagnostic Options
Option

Description

C

Call stack compatibility with
BIS for Windows. When a
script error occurs, do not
unwind the call stack and local
variables back to the call level
of the original RER
registration.

J

Enable Jump History
collection. See Jump History
Collection.

H

Halt Jump History collection.

P

Protect the variables at this
call level from examination by
the DIAG statement at higher
call levels.

Jump History Collection
Jump History collection keeps track of the most recent
jumps that took place in the script. Jumps are deﬁned as a
redirection from sequential processing which includes jumps
to labels, jumps to lines, calls to subroutines, jumping to
error routines (RER target), or abort routines (RAR target).
The number of entries in the Jump History depends on your
level of BIS. However, the number of entries is never less
than 100.
Jump History can be retrieved at any time by the DIAG
statement. If the DIAG statement is executed inside of an
RER routine, the Jump History that it reports is a snapshot of
the Jump History at the time of the error.
Once Jump History is activated it stays active until the script
exits the CALL level where it was activated. For example, if
you are at CALL level 2 when you activate Jump History, it
stays active as you CALL levels 3 and above. If CALL level 3
has an RER statement you do not need to specify the Joption because CALL level 2 has already activated the Jump
History collection. When the script exits CALL level 2 (by
way of RETURN) the Jump History collection is halted
because CALL level 1 did not have collection active.
Applications are typically written with an RER in the main
body (CALL level 0) of the script and with an RER in each
CALL subroutine. In order to enable Jump History for the
entire application (script), you only need to add the J-option
to the RER in the main body of the script.
Enabling Jump History aﬀects the performance of your script
to some degree. For example, a script executing the
following line 500,000 times:

@DEC <cnt> IF <cnt> GT 0,(LIN-0)
Showed a performance degradation of 2.2 percent with
Jump History enabled. Since most scripts include database
access (SRH/CAL/SOR/TOT/) which consume larger
quantities of wall time, the actual performance impact
should be much less than this.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Error Routine Reserved Words
The following reserved words contain zero until a script fails.

AXDRW$

Drawer letter of the run
control report where the script
erred.

XDRW$

Drawer number of the run
control report where the script
erred.

XERR$

Message number of the error.
Use this number in the LSM
statement to retrieve the text
of the message.

XFUN$

Run function call of the
statement that erred.

XLINE$

Line number in the run control
report where the script erred.

XRPT$

Report number of the run
control report where the script
erred.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The system resets reserved words to zero whenever the
script refers to CERR$ or executes a noninterim display with
the DSG, DSM, DSP, OUT, or SC statements.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The system resets reserved words to zero whenever the
script executes a noninterim display with the DSG, DSM,
DSP, INP, MBX, OUT, or SC statements.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Error Routine Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Place the RER statement as close as possible to the
beginning of the script. If an error occurs before the
script executes the RER statement, the script has no
error routine in which to go.
The system cancels the error routine when the script
terminates, but you can also use the Clear Error Routine
(CER) statement to cancel the routine.
If an error occurs while an error routine is executing, the
script terminates with a normal system message.
Every called routine (through the Call Subroutine [CALL]
statement) should have an error routine registered or
the results may be unpredictable.
Error routines registered in called routines (through the
Call Subroutine [CALL] statement) override previously
registered error routines. When control returns to the
calling script, the system automatically reregisters the
original error routine.
If you do not register an error routine in the called
routine, control goes to the error routine in the calling
script if an error occurs.
You can use an RSR statement in an error routine only if
it starts an internal subroutine.
Cabinet restrictions from the script also apply to the
error routine.

A noninterim display speciﬁed by any of the following
statements in your error routine continues normal script
processing: DSG, DSM, DSP, INP, MBX, OUT, SC.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The error routine cannot contain the statements GTO
RPX, RAR, RUN, or RER.
The values of IO$ and LLP$ reﬂect the number of I/Os
and LLPs used in the error routine and the script.
Any references to CERR$ clears CERR$ and XERR$ and
continues normal script processing.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The error routine cannot contain the statements GTO
RPX, LNK, RAR, RTN, RUN, or RER.
The values of IO$ and LLP$ reﬂect the number of I/Os
and LLPs in the error routine itself.
Ü Ü 2200

Register Error Routine Example
This example registers the external error routine at label 5
in report 2E0:
@RER,0,E,2 0005 .

Using the RUNERR Routine
Use the RUNERR error subroutine as a debugging tool during
development of your scripts or as an error notiﬁcation
routine in production scripts.
Following is a sample of the information provided:
System message
Failing script name and location of the run control report
Failing line number and statement
User-id, station, and department of the script user
Date and time of the failure
Variable deﬁnition and contents at the time of failure
First 25 lines of the output area, -0, and all existing
renamed reports
2200 Þ Þ
Since the subroutine uses variables v192 through v199, do
not use these variables in your script.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The RUNERR is designed to use only global variables to
insure that it will not fail when processing an error. The RER
obtains the variable processing space when the RER gets
control if it has not been reserved in advance by the RSR in
the example below.

The global variable requirement for RUNERR is 25 variables
and 2000 bytes. The administration conﬁguration default for
the value of global script variables (maximum number of
bytes) is 5000 bytes, and the maximum number of global
script variables is 100.
The RER refers to variables v190 and v191 as identiﬁed
below. The reserved global variables have a dedicated
purpose in RUNERR. The RER uses these global variables:
<*calldate>, <*calltime>, <*callmsec>, <*Sessionid>,
<*lgc> and many variables with an RER# preﬁx, such as
<*RER#...>. If you are using RUNERR, these names should
be avoided in your scripts.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Registering the System Error Routine
To set up your scripts to access the RUNERR subroutine
Register the routine. Place one of the following
statements at the beginning of your script:
2200 Þ Þ

@RER,0,F,2 0001 . (Business Information Server full
character set version or
JavaScript script)

@RER,0,E,2 0001 . (Business Information Server
limited character set version)

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

@RSR,0,E,2
0100 .
@RER,0,E,2
0001 .

Optional, reserves the necessary
space.
Required, registers the RER.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Load v190 to send an error dump to either a station
number or user-id. If v190 is not loaded, an error dump
is displayed on the user's screen.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
When sending an error dump to a user, load v190 with
user-id[,dept,sl]. (Department and site letter are
optional.) There cannot be any spaces in v190.
Example: @LDV,wp v190H18='mapcoord,2' or
'mapcoord,2,Z' .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
When sending an error dump to a user, load v190 with
user-id.
Example: @LDV,wp v190H18='mapcoord' .
Ü Ü 2200
When sending an error dump to a station number, load
v190 with a station number indicating where the error
dump should be sent. If v190 is not loaded, an error
dump is displayed on the user's screen.
Example: @LDV v190I5=999 .
Load v191 with a text string containing information
about the script designer, such as name, location, and
phone number. This is displayed to the user so the
person responsible can be contacted.
Example: @LDV v191s80='J.Doe -- Ext 7418' .
Outcome
The error dump is displayed in either of the following two
ways:

It is sent to the station speciﬁed by v190, or
If v190 is blank, it is displayed on the screen that
executed the script; or if v190 contains a valid user-id or
station number, then the RER error dump is sent to the
user-id or station number.
To toggle the display between the error dump and the failing
run control report, press Resume.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

has exceeded the maximum LLP limit.) in the form of an RER

run registration for error dump processing.

Retrieve File Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command creates a result containing the retrieved
ﬁle.
If a ﬁle was created with no headings and you retrieve it
with no headings, the result contains no headings
except a new date line reﬂecting information for the
result.
To ensure that all retrieved ﬁles contain only one date
line, this command does not add a date line if the ﬁle
already contains one, even if you request it; however, if
the retrieved ﬁle does not have a date line, the
command adds one regardless of how you answer
hdgs?.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The command always adds a date line to a ﬁle that is
not in MAPPER format. If you create a ﬁle as a native
data ﬁle with headings and retrieve it without headings,
the result contains two date lines.
If you retrieve a ﬁle in MAPPER format that is wider than
the drawer into which it is being retrieved, the
command truncates (drops) any extra characters at the
end of each line. If the ﬁle is not in MAPPER format,
extra characters wrap to the next line. You can however,
conﬁgure whether you want this command to wrap or
truncate extra characters using the MAPPER
Administration Menu Utility.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If you retrieve a ﬁle that is wider than the drawer into
which it is being retrieved, the command truncates
(drops) any extra characters at the end of each line.
If you retrieve a binary ﬁle (omnibus element from an
OS 2200 program ﬁle) using the B option in the MAPPER
format ﬁeld, the command sizes the data to the
receiving drawer size. The data must be in full character
set (FCS) format. The omnibus element is one
continuous line that wraps from line to line until an endof-element is detected. The last line in the report is ﬁlled
with a binary end-character. This report is marked as
non-displayable but includes a date, title (line 2), and
End Report line. The date and title lines are visible when
the report is displayed.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Þ Þ
If you retrieve a ﬁle that is wider than the drawer into
which it is being retrieved, the command wraps extra
characters to the next line. You can conﬁgure whether
you want this command to wrap or truncate extra
characters using the MAPPER Administration Menu
Utility.
You cannot retrieve a binary ﬁle into a drawer wider
than 256 characters.

Sharing Native Files
You might be unable to read, overwrite, or remove a native
(Linux or UNIX) ﬁle that was created by another session
user. The ﬁle might have been created at the system level
or within the Business Information Server context using the
FILE or UNIX command or with the FIL or UNX statement. An
attempt to access the ﬁle for reading or writing can be done
at the system level or within the Business Information
Server context with the RET or UNIX command or with the
RET or UNX statement. Whether another user can access
the ﬁle in diﬀerent modes depends upon the active or
default permissions attached to the ﬁle.
All ﬁles created either at the system level or within the
Business Information Server context receive "rw-r--r"
permissions by default. These permissions allow the owner
to read and write to the ﬁle. Group members and others can
only read the ﬁle. This is because the default umask
provided by the operating system is 022, which removes
"group" and "other" write permissions. The ﬁle owner can
actively change permissions by doing one of the following:
Issuing the system "chmod" command which sets the
access permissions explicitly. This can be done either at
the system level or within the Business Information
Server context using the UNIX command or the UNX
statement. It can be done by all workstation and
terminal emulator users.
Modifying the umask for the system session. This can be
done only by terminal emulator users, including the
"mapper" user in Trusted sites.

Using a mapper login: add a umask command to the
mlogin script. This script resides in the M_ROOT/lbin
directory.
Using other logins: add a umask command to the
.proﬁle ﬁle, found in the user's home directory.
The argument to the umask is a three-digit octal number
that is bitwise subtracted from octal 666. For example:
umask 002
changes ﬁle creation mode to 664. Thereafter, all ﬁles are
created with "rw-rw-r--" permissions.
Files created by runs initiated by MAPQUE are owned by the
default user/group ("mapper"/"mapper" in Trusted
instances) and receive permissions "rw-rw-r--". (The default
user and group have read and write permissions, but others
have only read permission.) This also applies to scheduled
runs initiated by DISPATCHER. The software uses umask to
actively change the ﬁle creation mode when it starts
MAPQUE and DISPATCHER.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

Retrieve File Examples
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
This example retrieves the /work/prod/status ﬁle, which is
not in MAPPER format. The statement does not add
headings to the cabinet 0, drawer B result.
@ret,0,b,n,n /work/prod/status .

@ret,0,b,n,n '\work\prod\status.new' .

RETN (Return Result from Remote)
Use the RETN run to return a result from a remote site to the
local site.

Example
Use the remote function key to connect to a remote
MAPPER. You need to generate a result report and with it
displayed enter RETN on the command line. This returns
both control and the result to your local system which
appears in your current cabinet as a result report.

RETR and @REH (Retrieve Report
from History)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Retrieve Report from History (RETR) function or REH
statement to retrieve a version of a report that existed at
the time of the last purge or merge process. It is especially
useful if you accidentally delete a report.
If you are unable to retrieve the version of the report that
you need, call your administrator, who might be able to
retrieve an earlier version of the report for you.
The command creates a result containing the retrieved
report.

RETR

occurs.

@REH Reserved Words

error codes listed below. The codes indicate the reason, if
any, why the data cannot be retrieved by the system. If you
do not use the lab subﬁeld and an error occurs, the run fails.

4

Drawer was added since last
purge (prior version does not
exist).

13

Prior version does not exist.

@RETURN (Return Call Routine)
Use the Return Call Routine (RETURN) statement to exit a
called subroutine and return to the calling run on the line
following the Call Subroutine (CALL) statement.

Return Call Routine Outcome
The statement returns the current result (-0) created in the
subroutine and any variables passed in the CALL statement.
Renamed results (-1 to -16) in the calling run remain
unchanged, even if the subroutine creates -1 to -16 results.

Return Call Routine Statement Format
2200 Þ Þ
@RETURN .
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
@RETURN,lab .
where lab is the label to go to instead of returning to the
line following the CALL statement. The label can be in a
variable, so you can load the variable based on activities
occurring in the run.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client

RF and @RFM (Reformat Report)
Use the Reformat Report (RF) function or the RFM statement
to copy columns of data between reports or across drawers.
The Reformat Report command is especially useful for
rearranging the report columns; simply create a new report
containing the newly arranged headings, then use the
Reformat Report command to copy the columns from the
existing report to the new report in the proper columns.
For the manual Reformat function, the receiving report must
be on display in the desired format. See Reformat Report
Guidelines for information about displaying masks for
reports wider than 80 characters.
The receiving report must contain only heading lines.
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
A result is created that includes all the data copied from
the issuing report to the receiving report.
In addition to copying columns, the command copies
line type designators from one report to another.

Note: If the issuing report contains period lines, the
entire line is moved to the receiving report (so that the
receiving report contains the entire comment line).

Reformat Report Control Line Format
RF [report f]

Field

Description

report

Issuing report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format of the issuing
report (enables you to copy
data in ﬁelds beyond column
80 of the issuing report.)

Reformat Report Statement Format
@RFM,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr o icc ,ip rcc ,rp .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report.

o

Options. As no options are
available for this statement,
designate no options with ' '.

icc

Column-character positions or
ﬁeld names in the issuing
report of the data to be
moved.

ip

Parameters for the issuing
report (A - Z). See Reformat
Report Parameters.

rcc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds in the

receiving report where the
data is to be placed.

rp

Parameters for the receiving
report (A - Z). See Reformat
Report Parameters.

Reformat Report Parameters
The A - Z parameters indicate the data you want to move
from the designated ﬁelds in the issuing report and the
ﬁelds you want to move the data to in the receiving report.
You can specify a maximum of 26 ﬁelds (A - Z). Enter the
parameters in order (A, B, C, and so on) in the ﬁrst column
of each ﬁeld.

Reformat Report Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
For each parameter you enter in the issuing report, be
sure to enter a corresponding parameter in the
receiving report. Enter the parameter in the ﬁrst column
of the ﬁeld.
Be sure that the issuing and receiving ﬁelds are the
same size. If they are not, erase or add asterisks in the
asterisk line to adjust the size. For example, if you are
copying an eight-character ﬁeld to a ten-character ﬁeld,
erase the ﬁrst or last two asterisks in the ten-character
ﬁeld.
If you want to match ﬁelds and optionally move data
between two diﬀerent reports, use the Match command
instead of the Reformat Report command. For more
information, see MA and @MCH (Match), MAU and
@MAU (Match Update).
The receiving report can be in any format, including a
temporary format selected with the Create Temporary
Format (VIEW) command, but the only way to request a
function mask containing columns beyond column 80 in
the issuing report is to specify a standard format
(deﬁned in report 0) in the input screen.
If you must copy data in the issuing report beyond
column 80, use a format containing the columns you
need so that you can request it in the input screen. If
you need more formats deﬁned in report 0, see your
administrator.

Reformat Report Example
In the following example, the line type designators and
ﬁelds speciﬁed with the parameters a through d in the
issuing report are copied into the corresponding ﬁelds of the
receiving report.
Function Mask
*St.Order . Product .Ord.Cust.
*Cd.Number. Type .Qty.Code.
*==.======.=========.===.====.
** ****** ********* *** ****
a b c d
*Cust.St.Order .Serial.Ord.
*Code.Cd.Number.Number.Qty.
*====.==.======.======.===.
**** ** ****** ****** ***
d a b c
Equivalent Statement
@rfm,'report1d','report4a' '' 'St','Order','Ord
Qty','Cust' |,a,b,c,\
d 'Cust','St','Order','Ord Qty' |,d,a,b,c .
Partial Result
*Cust.St.Order .Serial.Ord.
*Code.Cd.Number.Number.Qty.
*====.==.======.======.===.
AMCO OR 99951S 2
AMCO OR 99951S 1
AMCO OR 99951S 1
ARCO OR 96652S 1

ARCO OR 96652S 2
ARCO OR 96652S 1
DICO OR 99753S 1
FEDS OR 99842S 1
FEDS OR 99842S 1

RL (Report List)
Use the Report List (RL) command to display a list of reports
you can access.
The Report List command is similar to the Report key, with
the exception that the Report List control line procedure
allows you to specify a diﬀerent drawer and cabinet.
The command displays a list of drawers in your current
cabinet from which you can select to display a list of reports
in that drawer. Or, if you specify a drawer, and optionally, a
cabinet, it displays a list of reports you can access in that
drawer.

Note: If you specify another cabinet, you do not actually
switch to that cabinet.

Report List Control Line Format
RL [dc]
where dc is the drawer letter and cabinet number, if
diﬀerent from current cabinet, from which to display a list of
reports.

@RLN (Read Line Next)
Use the Read Line Next (RLN) statement to continue reading
from the report from which a Read Line (RDL) or Find and
Read Line (FDR) statement was just executed.
An RLN statement must follow a successful RDL or FDR
statement.

Read Line Next Statement Format
@RLN[,l,lab] cc vdata .

Field

Description

l

Line number to read. Default
= next line number.

lab

Label to go to if the speciﬁed
line does not exist.

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to read.
You do not have to specify
ﬁelds in order from left to
right, but be sure to assign the
variables that capture the data
in matching sequence.

vdata

Variables to capture the data.
Maximum number of variables
= 80.

Read Line Next Reserved Word
LINE$ contains the next line number to read. If the line read
is beyond the end of the report, LINE$ contains a zero.

Read Line Next Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RLN statement:
To have the run automatically initialize a variable to the
size of a ﬁeld, specify only the variable name and type
(for example, <data1>h, <data2>s, and so on). The
RLN statement initializes the variables to the
appropriate size for the ﬁelds it reads.
When you load variables of a speciﬁed size, the size of
each variable determines the number of columns the
statement reads.
For example, in the following statement, <data>
contains ten characters of data starting with column 5,
even though you speciﬁed 2 characters in the cc ﬁeld:
@rln,,099 5-2 <data>h10 .
2200 Þ Þ
The RLN statement ignores columns that exceed the line
size of the report being read. This is not considered an
error condition.
Ü Ü 2200
Since the RLN statement continues processing a
previously speciﬁed report, the only statements allowed
between RDL and RLN (or FDR and RLN) statements are
those that do not end the processing on the report, such
as IF, CHG, and LDV. You can, however, place an RDC
statement, between RDL and RLN statements provided
the RDC is processing a diﬀerent report.

When most other statements, such as those with a
manual function counterpart, begin processing, they
close the report previously being used, making it
unavailable. Some of these statements include ART, DC,
DEV, FND, LZR, MCH, SRH, SOR, TOT, and WRL.

Reading Data from a Field Example
This example reads data from the Cust Code ﬁeld in the
report previously processed by an RDL or FDR statement
and captures the data in <data>: @rln 'custcode' <data>h .

'custcode' Read data from the Cust Code
ﬁeld.

<data>h

Initialize <data> as a type H
variable to the size of the
Cust Code ﬁeld to capture the
data read from that ﬁeld.

Reading Data from Two Fields Example
This example shows both the RDL and RLN statements
reading data from two ﬁelds in report 2B0. Notice that the
RLN statement does not specify the report to process. It
continues processing the report speciﬁed in the RDL
statement.
@rdl,0,b,2,<line>,099 'statusdate','shiporder'
<date>i,<order>h .
. (other processing such as IF and GTO)
.
@rln,,099 'statusdate','shiporder' <date>,<order>
.

0,b,2

Read a line in report 2B0.

<line>

Read the line number in
<line> (the following RLN
statement automatically
reads the line in <line> +
1).

099

Go to label 99 if the line or
the report does not exist.

'statusdate' Read data from the Status
Date ﬁeld.

'shiporder'

Read data from the Ship
Order ﬁeld.

<date>I

Initialize <date> as a type I
variable to the size of the
Status Date ﬁeld to capture
the data read from the ﬁeld.

<order>h

Initialize <order> as a type
H variable to the size of the
Ship Order ﬁeld to capture
the data read from the ﬁeld.

@RNM (Rename)
Use the Rename (RNM) statement to establish a new
reference for a report or result, using names -0 through -16
in any order. The RNM statement is useful for temporarily
saving multiple results. If you do not rename a result, the
system releases it when it creates a new -0 result.
Renaming a report to -0 through -16 does not make it a
result. It simply gives the report another name;
consequently, the system applies any processing or
updating on the renamed report to the report itself.

Rename Statement Format
@RNM[,c,d,r] -n .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to rename. If none is
speciﬁed, the RNM statement
renames the current -0 result
with the number speciﬁed in
the -n ﬁeld.

-n

New name of the report or
result, -0 through -16.

Rename Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RNM statement:
If you rename a -0 result to -1, you can process the
result using either name; however, once you create
another -0 result, you can process the previous result
only as -1.
You can rename reports more than once. For example,
you could rename report 2B0 as -2 and -3, and then
process the report using -2, -3, or 0,2,b. Remember, if
you update -2, you are actually updating report 2B0.

Rename Example
In this example, the ﬁrst RNM statement saves the CAL
result in -1 and the second statement saves the MCH result
in -2. The last MCH statement matches the saved -1 and -2
results and creates a new -0 result.
@cal,0,c,1 c 'sales' |,a if:a>2500;then:a=a-a*.05
.
@rnm -1 .
.
. (Current result is -0 and -1.)
.
@mch,-0,0,c,2 b 'product' |,1 'product' |,1 .
@rnm -2 .
.
. (Current result is -0 and -2; -1 remains
intact.)
.
@mch,-1,-2 n 'sales' |,1 'sales' |,1 .
.
. (Current result is -0; -1 and -2 remain
intact.)
.

RPSW and @RPW (Read Password)
Use the Read Password (RPSW) function to limit access to
individual reports. With the Read Password manual function,
you can
Assign a standard read password.
Assign a user-private read password.
Assign a department-private read password.
Clear a read password.
Use the RPW statement to enable your run to access reports
that have standard read passwords assigned to them. You
can specify one or two read passwords with each RPW
statement in your run.

Note: The only statements that can overwrite reports
containing read passwords are Transfer from PC (PCR),
Delete (DEL), and Replace Report (REP). All other
statements and all manual functions cannot overwrite
reports containing read passwords. Also note that when a
PCR, DEL, or REP statement updates a report containing a
read password, the password is removed.
For the Read Password manual function, you must have
update control of a report before you can assign, change, or
clear a read password.
The manual function assigns or clears the password. When
attempting to display a report with a read password, the
system automatically repeats transparent password
prompting until you enter a valid password.

As a result of using the statement, the system retains up to
two passwords per session and allows access to any report
that has one of those read passwords until the run ﬁnishes.

Read Password Control Line Format
RPSW {psw | keyw}

Field

Description

psw

The one- to six-character
password.

keyw

One of the following keywords:
Clear - clears an existing
password.
USER$ - assigns a user-private
password. (The report can be
displayed only by a user with
your user-id in your
department.) DEPT$ - assigns
a department-private
password. (The report can be
displayed only by users with
your department number.)

Read Password Statement Format
@RPW{,pw1|,pw1,pw2|,,pw2} .

Field

Description

pw1

First read password.

pw2

Second read
password.

Read Password Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RPSW command as
a manual function:
You can supply only two passwords on the @RPW
statement. These passwords are replaced with each
@RPW statement.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
A run can access a report that has a read password if
the password is supplied on the @RPW statement or if it
was one of the last two read passwords supplied on the
RPSW command in manual mode.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
You can maintain access to a report as long as you have
that report displayed. If you release the report and try to
redisplay it, you must enter the password again.
Ü Ü 2200
You cannot assign a read-access password to report 0 or
to results.
When a command that processes multiple reports in a
drawer encounters a locked report, it considers the
locked report nonexistent.
When the Index command encounters a locked report, it
displays only the date line and title line; it does not
display the contents of the report.

You must use the control line format to access the
manual Read Password command. The menu selection is
available only for online help information.

@RPW Statement Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RPW statement:
You can use RPW statements throughout your run to
access diﬀerent reports. For example, this statement
speciﬁes two read passwords:
@rpw,xxx,yyy .
This statement speciﬁes a diﬀerent second read
password (from the previous example) so the run can
access a diﬀerent report. The ﬁrst read password
remains xxx.
@rpw,,zzz .
For greater security, you can solicit the password from
the run user and conceal the password in the run, as in
this example:
@chg input$ v1h6 .
@rpw,v1 .

Read Password Examples
This example sets the read password to readme:
rpsw readme
This example sets a read-access lock for your user-id:
rpsw user$
This example accesses one report containing a read
password: @rpw,readme .
@srh,0,b,2 '' 'custcode' |,amco .
This example accesses two reports containing read
passwords: @rpw,readme,readit .
@mch,0,b,2,0,c,1 n 'product' |,1 .

RR and @RRN (Remote Run)
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The Remote Run (RR) manual function is available on
Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, and UNIX operating
systems.
Use the Remote Run (RR) function or RRN statement to start
a run in background at a remote site or to start a
background run using a diﬀerent user-id.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
The Remote Run (RRN) statement is available on Windows
Server, Windows Client, Linux, UNIX, and OS 2200 systems.

Note: Though the RRN statement is still available in this
level of the software, use the following network statements
instead for added ﬂexibility and compatibility among future
levels of the software: NET, NOF, NRD, NRM, NRN, NRT, and
NWR.
For information on executing manual functions and runs at
remote sites in an interactive mode instead of background
mode, see @NRM (Network Remote). This is a convenient
way to process data on diﬀerent systems.
Your site must have remote sites conﬁgured and connected.
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The speciﬁed run starts at the remote site.

If you enter an invalid value in the rmu, rmd, or rmpw
subﬁelds or if the remote run terminates abnormally,
the run user receives a message.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The remote run must not contain the following statements,
which communicate with a terminal: GOC (when display
requested), GS (when display requested), OUM, SC, XIT,
XUN, graphical user interface statements.
The remote run must not contain the following statements if
they suspend the run: DSM, DSP, DSX, OUT.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Using the Remote Run (RRN) Statement
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Remote Run (RRN) statement to start a run at a
remote site.
The remote run must not contain the following statements,
which communicate with a terminal: DSG, DSM, DSP, DSX,
GOC (when display requested), GS (when display
requested), OUM, OUT, REL, RSI, SC, XIT, graphical user
interface statements.
You can use the DSG, OUT, and SC statements in the remote
run if you send the output to another terminal.
Ü Ü 2200

Using the Remote Run (RR) Manual Function
Executing this command as a manual function on Windows
Server, Windows Client, Linux, and UNIX systems causes the
following actions to occur:
The run starts in background at the remote site and an
active screen is displayed on your terminal.
If you enter an invalid user-id, department number, or
password in the input screen, or if the remote run
terminates abnormally, you receive a message as a
station-to-user message.

Remote Run Control Line Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
RR run[,d1,d2...]

Note: If you do not send a report to the remote run, no data
needs to be entered in the SEND or RECEIVE ﬁelds of the
input screen.

Field

Description

run

Name of the run to start at the
remote site.

d1,d2

Literal data, separated by
commas, to pass to the
remote run (captured by the
remote run using INPUT$).

Ü Ü 2200

@RRN Reserved Word
STAT1$ contains 0 if the remote run was successful, or
nonzero if the remote run was unsuccessful.

Remote Run Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the RRN statement:
When using the manual function, press Remote from the
active screen for a list of sites.
Do not place other statements on the same line after the
RRN statement. Other statements following it on the same
line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new line.
2200 Þ Þ
Use the rmf and lf subﬁelds to specify the report formats to
condense the amount of information being sent by the
remote site to the local site. Only the columns speciﬁed in
the format are sent, and are received at the local site in the
same format as sent.
Ü Ü 2200

Remote Run Example
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
This example starts a run at a remote site and transfers
data to the run.
@rrn thisrun,ABC b,newuser,7 .

thisrun

Start the THISRUN run.

ABC

Transfer the literal ABC
(captured by INPUT$ in the
THISRUN run at the remote
site).

b

Start the run at remote site B.

newuser

Use the user-id NEWUSER
registered at the remote site.

7

Use the sign-on department
number 7 registered at the
remote site.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Remote Run Example
2200 Þ Þ
This example starts a run at a remote site and transfers
data to the run.
@rrn thisrun,ABC 2,newuser,7,psw .

thisrun

Start the THISRUN run.

ABC

Transfer the literal ABC
(captured by INPUT$ in the
THISRUN run at the remote
site).

2

Start the run at remote site
number 2.

newuser

Use the user-id NEWUSER
registered at the remote site.

7

Use the sign-on department
number 7 registered at the
remote site.

psw

Use the password PSW to sign
on to the remote site.

Ü Ü 2200

RS and @RS (Run Status)
Use the Run Status (RS) function or the RS statement to
create a result showing the status of runs started on the
system, including background and remote runs.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
You can request status of all runs or just your own.
Do not place other statements on the same line after an RS
statement. Other statements following it on the same line
are ignored. Put the next statement on a new line.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
You can choose to see the status of all runs started with
your user-id, including background and remote runs.
Ü Ü 2200
A result is created showing run status. When using the
manual Run Status function, press Resume to display a new
result with updated status.
