Ü Ü 2200

RSI and @RSI (Remote Symbiont
Interface)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Remote Symbiont Interface (RSI) function so you
can use a terminal in demand mode. Use the RSI statement
to initiate an interactive demand program through a run.
You must have access to demand processing through the
Remote Symbiont Interface manual function. Call your
administrator to determine if you have access.
When executing a run containing the RSI statement, if the
run user is not a valid RSI user (as deﬁned in the user
registration report), no @ or @@ commands are allowed
(except for @@TERM and @@X).
Executing this command as a manual function causes a
series of OS 2200 sign-on messages to be displayed; answer
them and start your demand processing. The wait light
remains on as long as output to the display terminal is
pending.
Executing this statement causes the run to terminate; then
the system signs the user on to the operating system in
demand mode, and gives the user manual control of the
display terminal. The wait light remains on as long as output
to the display terminal is pending.

Note: The RSI interface of the Exec is text oriented.
Therefore, the system cannot determine whether the
parameters in the RSI statement are causing an error. For
example, an invalid user-id, password, or a nonexistent ADD
ﬁle can cause an error.

Remote Symbiont Interface Control Line Format
RSI

Remote Symbiont Interface Statement Format
@RSI[,site-id,tmo]
user,pw[,acct,qual,fn,cyc,elt,ver,q] .

Field

Description

site-id

Site identiﬁer (six characters)
for this display terminal.

tmo

Number of seconds to wait for
demand input at the terminal.
If left blank, no timeouts
occur. Pressing Abort before
receiving manual control
aborts the RSI statement.

user

Demand mode user-id.

pw

Demand mode password that
validates the user-id.

acct

Account number. If speciﬁed,
the system submits an
executive control language
(ECL) RUN statement in this
format: @RUN run-

id,acct/,proj-id run-id active site-id.

acct - account number.
proj-id - qualiﬁer (qual from
the RSI statement). If you do
not specify a qualiﬁer in the
RSI statement, this ﬁeld is left
blank.

qual

File name qualiﬁer.

fn

File name. If speciﬁed, the
system submits an ECL ADD
statement after demand
mode sign-on is complete.

If omitted from the RSI
statement, the following ﬁlerelated subﬁelds are left
blank.

cyc

File cycle number.

elt

Element name.

ver

Element version name.

q

Number of text lines to
display before giving manual
control to the user. Default =
1 (minimum). Maximum =
one fewer than the vertical
screen size of the display
terminal. If a demand
program executes a control
command that the
Communications Control
Routine (CCR) for the system
interprets before it solicits
input from the terminal, it
discards everything that
preceded the control
command.

Remote Symbiont Interface Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To submit an unsolicited image, press Message. The
CCR responds by returning an SOE character and
turning oﬀ the wait light.
If an auxiliary printer is conﬁgured for the display
terminal in the terminal conﬁguration report, you can
designate the printer for both input and output as it is
passed between the OS 2200 operating system and the
display terminal. You can turn the auxiliary printer on or
oﬀ during the time that the Business Information Server
for ClearPath OS 2200 CCR is soliciting input, indicated
by the SOE displayed at the bottom of the screen and
the wait light oﬀ.
The auxiliary print device turns oﬀ the wait light after
printing a line. Also, the tape cassette unit turns oﬀ the
wait light after a read or write operation.

Business Information Server CCR Commands
The user CCR interface is similar to the OS 2200 UNISCOPE
100 display terminal CCR interface with the following
restrictions.

Command Description

@@END

Turns oﬀ escape mode, fullscreen mode, and any other
modes normally canceled by
@@END commands in the
OS 2200 system.

@@ESC

Turns on escape mode for
input and output.

@@ESC I

Turns on escape mode for
input.

@@ESC O

Turns on escape mode for
output.

@@FRZ n

Rolls normally from line n
and changes no data. If n =
0 (assumed at sign-on time),
it rolls the entire screen.

@@FUL

Turns on full-screen mode
(implied @@INQ).

@@INS n

Positions the SOE character
for input solicitation. If n = 0,
the SOE is positioned at the
next-to-last line of the
screen. This command is
ignored if n is larger than
screen size.

@@NOPR

Turns oﬀ the auxiliary print
device. (Cancels all previous
@@PMOD commands.)

@@PMOD
A

Turns on the special mode
for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PMOD
C

Turns on the special mode
for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PMOD
F

Turns on the special mode
for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PMOD

Turns on the special mode

P

for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PMOD
V

Turns on the special mode
for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PMOD
X

Turns on the special mode
for UTS 400 and UTS 40
display terminals.

@@PRNT
dvn

Turns on the speciﬁed
auxiliary print devices,
where dvn is the device
name or number in the
terminal conﬁguration report
(for example, COP or 001).
The auxiliary print device
must be directly connected
to the display terminal or its
controller unit.

@@RLD

Rolls the screen downward.

@@RLU

Rolls the screen upward.

@@TCI
dvn,n

Enters n blocks. (Both
minimum and default values
= 512; it continues receiving

input until n blocks are read
or an end-of-ﬁle mark is
encountered).

@@TCM

Writes an end-of-ﬁle mark on
output and terminates
output, or terminates input
from cassette.

@@TCS
trk,adr

Searches for address adr on
track trk, or on the current
track if trk is not speciﬁed.

@@TERM

Terminates the RSI interface.

@@TCO
dvn,n

Processes a block of data
having n images per block.
The n defaults to 1 and must
be less than or equal to the
vertical screen size of the
display terminal in use.

@@TCT
dvn,n

Positions tape cassettes with
device name or number dvn
(for example, TC1 or 002) to
the top of track n, or to track
1 if n is not speciﬁed.

Note: When using the transparent tape cassette commands
(TCO and TCT), specify the dvn parameter on the ﬁrst call
only; all subsequent calls assume dvn. The dvn parameter
can be either the software name for the device or the logical
unit number. The order of appearance of the auxiliary
devices in the terminal conﬁguration report determines
logical unit numbers.
Ü Ü 2200

RSLT and @RSL (Create Result Copy)
Use the Create Result Copy (RSLT) function to create and
display a copy of a report as a result. Use the RSL statement
to create a copy of a report as the new -0 result. You can
also use the RSL statement to create a copy of a previously
renamed result.
To replace the original report with the updated data in the
result, use an LOK statement to lock the original report for
the exclusive use of your run before creating a result with
the RSL statement.
With the new result, you can do the following tasks:
Make changes to the result without disturbing the data
in the original report.
Save the result as a new report, using the Duplicate
Report command.
Replace the original report or another existing report
with the result data, using the Replace Report
command.

Create Result Copy Control Line Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
RSLT [report]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
RSLT [report,output-dc]
Ü Ü 2200
where report is the report to be created as a result. For
more details, see Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process.

output-dc is the drawer and cabinet for the output result. It
defaults to the drawer of the input report.

Create Result Copy Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@RSL,c,d,r .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@RSL,c,d,r[,rc,rd]
Ü Ü 2200
where c,d,r is the report to copy as a result. For more
details, see Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process.

rc,rd is the cabinet and drawer for the output result. It
defaults to the drawer of the input report. STAT1$ is loaded
with one of the following values:

0

No warnings.

1

Output report
lines have been
truncated to
accommodate
the output
drawer.

2

The report has
been converted

to a diﬀerent
character set.
(LCS, FCS, FCSU)

3

The report lines
have been
truncated and
the character set
has changed.

Create Result Copy Example
This example locks report 3B0, creates a result copy, and
replaces report 3B0 with the updated result:
@lok,0,b,3 .
@rsl,0,b,3 .
.
.
.
@rep,0,b,3 .

RSM (Resume)
Use the Resume (RSM) command to continue execution of a
command after it is suspended by a display. The Resume
command is particularly useful when you use the Find and
Locate commands, since it enables you to continue the
search after the system displays each ﬁnd.
Use the following format:
RSM
Or
Press Resume, if it is displayed on the function key bar.
(The Resume key is displayed when you are using a
command for which processing can be resumed.)

@RSR (Run Subroutine)
Use the Run Subroutine (RSR) statement to execute an
external or internal subroutine starting at a speciﬁed label.
To save the contents of all currently deﬁned variables and
pass control to a subroutine, use the Call Subroutine (CALL)
statement. You can pass variables to the subroutine,
manipulate them, and pass them back to the calling run
without aﬀecting any of the other currently deﬁned
variables.
The system transfers control to a subroutine within the
same run control report (an internal subroutine) or to one in
another report (an external subroutine).
2200 Þ Þ
External subroutines must be in the same character set type
as the calling run control report.
Ü Ü 2200

Run Subroutine Statement Format
@RSR{,c,d,r lab | lab} .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the external
subroutine.

lab

Label where the subroutine
starts. This ﬁeld is required.
To begin the routine at the ﬁrst
line of the external run control
report, use LIN1 in this ﬁeld.

Run Subroutine Reserved Words
Use ACDRW$ and CRPT$ in an external subroutine to
determine the drawer and report number of the calling run
control report.

Run Subroutine Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
You can refer to all variables and results in the calling
run from subroutines. When you return to the calling
run, newly created variables and results in the
subroutines become available. However, if in the
subroutine you reinitialize variables or rename results
from the calling run, the subroutine overwrites them.
You can nest subroutines in a run. Internal subroutines
can call subroutines that call other subroutines, up to 10
levels; however, you can call only one external
subroutine.
Use an Exit Subroutine (ESR) statement to exit the
subroutine and return control to a speciﬁed line in the
run control report relative to the line containing the
calling RSR statement.
Use a Clear Subroutine (CSR) statement in an external
subroutine to execute another external subroutine. A
CSR statement allows an external subroutine to call
another external subroutine but does not allow a return
to the original calling run control report with an ESR
statement.
When registering your run, inform the administrator of
any other run control reports containing external
subroutines.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the RSR statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.

Internal Subroutine Example
This example executes the subroutine at label 1. When the
subroutine ends, it returns to the line following the line
containing the RSR run statement (run statement A):
@ rsr 001 .
. run statement A .
. run statement B .
. run statement C .
@ gto end .
@001: . (subroutine)
.
. (other processing)
.
@ esr .

External Subroutine Example
This example executes an external subroutine at label 2 in
report 2E0:
@rsr,0,e,2 002

Nesting Subroutines Example
The ﬁrst RSR statement executes the subroutine at label 1.
That subroutine uses another RSR statement to execute a
subroutine at label 2.
When the second subroutine completes, it uses an ESR
statement to return to run statement B. When that
completes, it uses another ESR statement to return to the
original run statement (run statement A).
@ rsr 001 . (go to first subroutine)
. (run statement A)
@ gto end .
@001: . (first subroutine)
.
@ rsr 002 . (go to second subroutine)
. (run statement B)
.
@ esr . (returns to run statement A)
@002: . (second subroutine)
.
@ esr . (returns to run statement B)

@RTN (Return Remote)
Use the Return Remote (RTN) statement to return a report
from a remote site to the local (calling) site as a -0 result.

Note: Though the Return Remote (RTN) and Remote Run
(RRN) statements are still available in this level of the
software, use the following network statements instead for
added ﬂexibility and compatibility among future levels of
the software: NET, NOF, NRD, NRM, NRN, NRT, and NWR.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The user running the calling run (from the RRN statement)
receives a result as a station-to-user message.
You can use the RTN statement only in a background run or
a remote run. The remote or background run continues at
the instruction that follows the RTN statement.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The user running the calling run (from the RRN statement)
receives a result.
You can use the RTN statement only in a remote run. The
remote run terminates after sending the result back to the
run that executed the RRN statement.
Ü Ü 2200

Return Remote Statement Format
@RTN,rmc,rmd,rmr[,rmf] lc,lcpw,ldr[,lf] .

Field

Description

rmc,rmd,rmr Remote report to return.

2200 Þ Þ

rmf

Remote report format.

lc

Local cabinet.

lcpw

Local cabinet password.

ldr

Local drawer.

lf

Local report format.

Ü Ü 2200

Return Remote Guidelines
2200 Þ Þ
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the RTN statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
Use the rmf and lf subﬁelds to specify the report formats
to condense the amount of information being sent by
the remote site to the local site. Only the columns
speciﬁed in the format are sent, and are received at the
local site in the same format as sent.
Ü Ü 2200

Return Remote Example
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
This example sends report 2B0 back to the user who started
the Remote Run (RRN) statement: @rtn,0,b,2 .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
This example sends report 2B0 back to the user who started
the Remote Run (RRN) statement: @rtn,0,b,2 0,open,b .
Ü Ü 2200

@RUN (Execute a Script)
Use the Execute a Script (RUN) statement to terminate the
current script and start another script on the same station.
You can also start Business Information Server manual
functions with the RUN statement.
To start scripts manually, type the name of the script on the
control line and transmit, or select the script from the Apps
menu.
To start functions manually, type the function call on the
control line and transmit, or select the function from the
Select Task menu.

Execute a Script Statement Format
@RUN {script[,vld]|"cmd"} .

Field

Description

script

Registered name of the
Business Information Server
script or JavaScript to be
started.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

vld

Variables, literals, reserved
words, constants, or any
combination of these, to
transfer to the script. When
the script starts, it uses their
values to initialize variables
through INPUT$.
You can identify up to 80
items; however the maximum
total number of characters for
the vld ﬁeld is approximately
920 characters. The scan is
terminated after a space.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

2200 Þ Þ

vld

Variables, literals, reserved
words, constants, or any
combination of these, to
transfer to the script. When
the script starts, it uses their
values to initialize variables
through INPUT$.
You can identify up to 80
items; however the maximum
total number of characters for
the vld ﬁeld is approximately
1280 characters. The scan is
terminated after a space.

Ü Ü 2200

"cmd"

Command (Business
Information Server manual
function call) to execute,
including input parameters to
be sent to the manual
function. Enclose the request
in quotation marks (").
If the function speciﬁed
requires input parameters,
separate them with commas.

Execute a Script Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
You can identify up to 80 variables to capture in the
started script with INPUT$.
If the script being started uses input variables as
parameters for report processing commands, such as
Binary Find or Search, use the Load Variable (LDV)
statement with the P option beforehand to pack the
variables.
If the name of the script to start is in a variable, pack
that variable.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the RUN statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored.
A RUN statement does not clear a Link to Another Script
(LNK) statement.
2200 Þ Þ
If the command is a manual function call, the "cmd"
ﬁeld is not available in limited character set (LCS) run
control reports.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OSÞ Þ
You cannot execute a JavaScript script using the RUN
statement if the script containing the RUN statement was
started with the LNK (Link to Another Script) statement.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Execute a Script Example
This example starts the TEST script and sends v1, v2, and
the character string SAM to that script. (The TEST script
initializes these as variables using INPUT$.) The current -0
result is also available to the script.
@run test,v1,v1,SAM .

Starting Chart Scripts Using RUN
You can start chart scripts using a RUN statement. With the
chart scripts, you can specify various parameters. All
parameters above the heading divider line are optional,
except for the CAPTIONS parameter in line and bar charts.
Set up properly formatted input data for the chart script in
the output area. Once you have the chart data in the output
area, you can place the output area in a result, using a
Break (BRK) statement, then execute the chart scripts, using
the current result (-0).
You can also use a script to write data to a chart report.
Simply read lines from a standard report, using a statement
such as Read Continuous (RDC), then write the lines to a
chart report in the proper format, using the Write Line (WRL)
statement.
In addition to using the RUN statement, you can call a chart
script, using the Link to Another Script (LNK) statement.
Quick Reference
@RUN
chartscript,report[,dev,sn,psiz?,transpcy?,script
,vld] .
For a description of subﬁelds in this format and for more
information on chart scripts, see CHART.
Example
This example starts the LINEG script, which makes a line
chart, using data in report 51B of the current cabinet.

After displaying the graph, and after the user resumes the
script, it starts the TEST script and sends the values 1, 2,
and 3 to the TEST script, which initializes variables with
them, using INPUT$:
@run lineg,51b,,,,,test,1,2,3 .

Starting the MULTI script using RUN
2200 Þ Þ
With the MULTI script, you can display two, three, or four
charts on the screen and plot them.
Quick Reference
@RUN
MULTI,q[,f,sn,psiz?,transpcy?],c,d,r,c,d,r[,c,d,r
,..] .
Ü Ü 2200

RUN
Use the Automatic Run Generation (Run) run to generate
statements automatically from functions you perform at
your terminal. Run generates these statements
transparently as you execute the functions.
Run is especially useful for users who do a sequence of
repeated tasks on the same reports. By using Run, a user
who has no knowledge of run design can easily automate
these basic tasks.
For example, suppose that every day you need to search a
report for a particular item, sort the result, add the values in
a column, and print the result. You can have Run generate
statements for each of these tasks so that all you need to
type in is one word, the run name.
Your terminal is placed under control of Run. If no report is
on display, the Automatic Run Generation active screen is
displayed; otherwise the run redisplays the report.

Run Control Line Format
RUN[*]
where * creates statements using ﬁeld names. Default =
creates statements using column-character positions.

Procedures
After you start the Run command, follow these steps to
continue the processing:
1. Perform the commands for which you want statements
generated.
2. Use the keys listed on the Run function key bar to
control the operation of the Run command.
3. Press Exit or type a caret (^) and transmit when you
are ready to register the statements as a run or when
you are ready to execute the statements.
To register your statements as a run, in the Automatic Run
Generation Terminated screen, type the letter of the drawer
where you want the run to reside and transmit.

Note: You may be required to register the run in a drawer
designated for run control reports (with a drawer name
beginning with "Run"); however, your administrator can
change the Run command to allow registration of the run in
any kind of drawer. See your administrator if you have
questions.
The Run command
Duplicates the result containing the statements in the
drawer you specify in your current cabinet.
Registers the run with a run name of your user-id. The
run is registered so that only your user-id can execute it.
Writes over the run registration of any previously
generated run with the same run name. To retain the old

run, have the administrator reregister the old run with a
new name before you execute the run again.
2200 Þ Þ
If your user-id begins with a number (for example,
7NEWUSER), you can still execute the run by entering run
7newuser.
Ü Ü 2200

To Display Reports and Results in Your
Generated Run
The Run command does not generate a Display (DSP)
statement for each result generated by a command, even
though you see the results as you execute the commands. It
generates a DSP statement only after the ﬁnal result in the
run.
If you want to display each result in the process, use the
manual Display Report (D) function. For example, if you
want to search and sort a result and display both results,
type d - after each result is displayed and then transmit.

Run Limitations
Be aware of the following limitations of the Run command:
You can use the following commands while under control
of the Run command, but the Run command does not
generate statements:
L - restores the control line; same as typing pnt and
transmitting.
RSM - resumes the run; same as pressing DspRun.
If you select a command that is not available to the Run
command, a system message is displayed.
The Run command generates statements only for
statements that have equivalent manual functions. For
example, the Load Variable (LDV) statement does not
have an equivalent manual function.
The following commands generate statements but
terminate automatic run generation:
X generates a Sign Oﬀ User Session (XIT) statement
and displays the Automatic Run Generation
Terminated screen.
^ generates a Release Display (REL) statement. The
run executes, releases the screen, and displays the
Automatic Run Generation Terminated screen.
You cannot generate output screens or capture user
input from any other source (for example, by using
INPUT$ or INVAR$).

You cannot reuse a function mask after a system
message is displayed on the control line. Instead, you
must reenter the call, complete the function mask a
second time, and transmit.
You cannot use the SOE Update command during run
generation. However, when the generated run is
executing, you can update reports in the usual way.
To update displayed reports, use the Change command or
an updating command, such as the Search Update
command, followed by the Update command. You can also
use a line change command, such as the Add Line
command.

Run Function Key Bar
Key

Description

DspRun

Displays the generated
statements in a result.

Re-Dsp

Displays the report you were
processing. Use this key after
you press DspRun to
continue executing other
commands after looking at
the generated statements.

AcScrn

Displays the Automatic Run
Generation active screen.

Exit

Displays the Automatic Run
Generation Terminated
screen, where you can
register the run or use the
following function keys to
display or execute the
statements:
DspRun-displays the
generated statements in a
result.

Run-executes the statements.
For each Display (DSP)
statement in the run, press
Resume to continue
processing the next
statement.

Quit

Terminates the Run command
and displays the active
screen.

S and @SRH (Search), SU and @SRU
(Search Update)
Use the Search (S) function or SRH statement to ﬁnd a
character string in speciﬁed ﬁelds of one or more reports in
a drawer and to create a result containing all lines on which
the string is located.
You can use the Iterative Search (ISRH) run to save and
reuse Search function masks containing options and
parameters and to create SRH statements.
Use the Search Update (SU) function or SRU statement to
create an update result, which enables you to Delete (DEL)
or Extract (EXT) the found lines from the report or Update
(UPD) the found lines and blend them back into the report.
The Search (S), Find (F), and Binary Find (BF) manual
functions no longer require the minus sign (-) to indicate the
report or result currently on display. This method is
consistent with the Business Information Server for
ClearPath OS 2200. These functions still prompt the user for
a report if one is not on display. A minus sign is still
accepted on these functions.

Search Control Line Format
S {report|drawer}[ f]
SU {report|drawer}[ f]

Field

Description

report

Report to search.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

drawer

Drawer to process. Use this
ﬁeld to search all reports in a
given drawer. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
process data (lets you process
data in ﬁelds beyond column
80 if you do not already have
those columns on display).

Search Statement Format
@SRH,c,d[,r,l,q,lab] o cc ltyp,p
[vlines,vls,vrpt] .
@SRU,c,d[,r,l,q,lab] o cc ltyp,p
[vlines,vls,vrpt] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report or drawer to search.
For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

l

Line number at which to start
the search.

q

Number of lines to search.

lab

Label to go to if no ﬁnds are
made.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Search
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to search.

ltyp

Line type to search. If you
specify the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank but enter the
comma.

p

Character string to ﬁnd (see
also Search Parameters). The
maximum number of
parameters allowed in the
same ﬁeld is 11.

vlines

Variable to capture the
number of lines found.

vls

Variable to capture the
number of lines that were
searched. If the l subﬁeld is
speciﬁed, all lines searched
are included (not just the line
type speciﬁed).

vrpt

Variable to capture the
number of the report where
the ﬁnd was made. With the
SRH statement, this variable is
useful only when using the B
option when searching
multiple reports. If you do not

use the B option with the SRH
statement, vrpt = 0.
Since the SRU statement
processes multiple update
results one at a time, this
variable is always useful when
searching more than one
report with the SRU
statement.

Search Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types. Do
not use this option with the T
or U options.

B[(n)]

Stops a search after the nth
ﬁnd. Default = ﬁrst ﬁnd. The
search continues until items
are found or until the end of
the report.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C(x)

Alters the search process
based on the character set

order. Ordinarily, the system
processes the report based on
the character set of the
drawer. This option enables
you to choose the character
set type on which to base the
search. Use one of the
following: C(F) - full character
set (FCS).
C(L) - limited character set
(LCS).
C(S) - strict comparison;
distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters

Ü Ü 2200

D

Omits search information lines
from the result.

2200 Þ Þ

En

Estimates the number of lines
in the result, where n is an
integer. This option improves
the eﬃciency of the search
when the output result
contains more than 500 lines.

F

Searches for a numeric value
instead of a character string.
To search for a range of
negative or ﬂoating point
numbers, use this option. The
F option is ignored for ﬁelds
that use a Y option parameter.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

F

Searches for a numeric value
instead of a character string.
To search for a range of
negative or ﬂoating point
numbers, use this option.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

H

In a multiple report search,
includes the heading lines of
the ﬁrst report only.

L(x)

Omits lines of type x from the
result. Note that you can use
any line type here. For
example, L(*.a) indicates that

asterisk lines, period lines, and
A-type lines are to be omitted
from the result. If the line type
being omitted is the same as
the line type being searched,
the command includes these
lines in the total number of
ﬁnds.

N

Includes only those lines not
meeting the search criteria.
For example, if you search the
column for the letter A, the
result contains all lines that do
not contain the letter A in the
column. Note that even when
using the N option, the target
line type still determines the
hierarchy of line types
searched. Do not use this
option with the T or U options.

P

Extracts paragraphs from the
report. A paragraph consists of
data on a speciﬁed line type
and all subsequent lines until
the next occurrence of that
line type.
For example, a normal search
of asterisk lines creates a
result listing asterisk lines as
well as the trailer lines (period

lines, in this case) that follow.
With this option, the result
also includes tab lines that
follow.

Q[(n)]

Extracts the ﬁrst n paragraphs
from the report; stops the
search after the nth
paragraph. Default = ﬁrst
paragraph.

Rx-y

Searches a range of reports
from report number x through
report number y. For example,
specify r2-10 to search reports
2 through 10.

Rx,y

Searches reports x and y. For
example, specify r2,5 to
search reports 2 and 5.

Rx-y,z

Searches reports x through y
and also z.

S

Groups lines found in search
parameter order. (Use this
option with the H option to
avoid repeating report
headings for each group.) This

option does not apply to the
Search Update command.
When using this option, the
generated results may diﬀer
between the 2200 and
Windows Server and Windows
Client platforms.

T[(x)]

Includes data found on the
type of line you are processing
and on the preceding x type
line. Default = tab line. Do not
use this option with the A, N,
or U options.
A common use for this option
is to include the tab line
preceding the asterisk line
found.

U[(x)]

Includes data found in the
type of line you are processing
and its surrounding data unit.
A data unit consists of a group
of lines, starting at line type x
up to the next line of that
type. Default = tab line. Do
not use this option with the A,
N, or T options.
With this option, you specify
the paragraph boundary as
the x line type. In contrast, the

P option automatically uses
the line type you are
processing as the paragraph
boundary.

Y[(x)]

Searches for dates in the ﬁelds
deﬁned by the date format
parameter line. (See the Y
parameter description.)
Optionally speciﬁes the line
type of the parameter line.
The line type cannot be an R
and defaults to a Y. It should
be changed only if your target
data contains Y line types.
Lines with invalid dates are
not included in the result.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

@

Indicates that @ characters
found on the parameter line
specify a search for space
characters.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

@[(x)]

Indicates that @ characters
found on the parameter line
specify a search for space
characters. You can redeﬁne
the @ character to another
character by using the
optional character in
parentheses.

Ü Ü 2200

/

Searches for a slant (/)
character as data when used
with the / parameter.

Search Parameters
Parameter Description

/

Searches for a slant (/)
character when used with
the / option. Without the /
option, a slant appearing in
a parameter line shortens
that ﬁeld and a partial ﬁeld
is processed.
In the SRH statement, place
the slant within apostrophes
in the parameter subﬁeld to
process a partial ﬁeld.

@

Searches for spaces. Use
with the @ option or your
results may be
unpredictable. Type @ in the
columns of the function
mask where you want to
search for spaces. To ﬁnd a
blank ﬁeld, type @ in the
ﬁrst column of the ﬁeld. See
Searching for Spaces
Example.
Also, this parameter helps
ﬁnd all lines of a speciﬁc

type when used with the @
option. See Searching for All
Characters on a Speciﬁc
Line Type Example.

R

Searches for a range of
data. See Searching for a
Range of Data Example and
Searching for Multiple Data
in Range Example.

Y or x

Identiﬁes the line type of
the date parameter line. Use
Y or the value of x from the
Y option. Place a date
format in the ﬁeld being
searched. The date
parameter line should be
placed after all other
parameter lines. See Finding
Data Within a Range of
Dates Example.

0 - 20

Speciﬁes the numeric date
format. You can also use the
alphabetic date format
rather than a number (for
example, YYMMDD could be
used in place of number 1).
For a complete list of date
formats, see Entering Dates

and Times in the DC
Statement.

2200 Þ Þ

0 - 21

Ü Ü 2200

Speciﬁes the numeric date
format. You can also use the
alphabetic date format rather
than a number (for example,
YYMMDD could be used in
place of number 1). For a
complete list of date formats,
see Entering Dates and Times
in the DC Statement.

Search Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command creates a result containing the lines
found and includes trailer lines, if any.

Note: The Search command considers the following as
trailer lines: an asterisk and period lines are trailer lines
to tab lines; period lines are trailer lines to asterisk lines.
If the speciﬁed data is not found, the search information
lines state that no ﬁnds were made. If you use the D
option, the information lines are omitted but the
heading lines are included in the result.
The following information is included at the top of the
result:
Number of ﬁnds
Number of lines searched
Search mask showing the options and parameters
you speciﬁed
Use the D option to omit this information from the
result.
If you want the headers to appear in the search result,
the *= line must be placed within the ﬁrst eight lines of
the report being searched. If the *= line appears after
line eight, headers do not appear in the search result.

@SRH Statement Outcome
In addition, executing the SRH statement also causes the
following actions to occur:
Loads variables with information as described under the
statement format description.
The statement loads vls with the number of lines
searched and loads the other variables with 0. Then it
gives control to the speciﬁed label.

Search Guidelines
This subtopic describes how to search for
Multiple strings in the same ﬁeld
Multiple strings in diﬀerent ﬁelds
A range of character strings
Certain line types
Data and create an update result for blending changes
back into the report
Data in large, sorted reports
To Search for Multiple Strings in the Same Field
Enter each character string on a separate line under the
ﬁeld heading in the mask. See Searching for Multiple Strings
Example.
To Search for Multiple Strings in Diﬀerent Fields
Enter the character strings under diﬀerent headings on the
same line. Data lines are included in the result whenever all
speciﬁed ﬁelds contain the requested strings. See Searching
for Multiple Strings Example.
To include either one string or the other or both, enter the
character strings under diﬀerent headings and on diﬀerent
lines in the mask. Data lines are included in the result
whenever either of the speciﬁed ﬁelds contains the
requested strings. See Searching for Multiple Data in Range
Example.

To Search for a Range of Character Strings Using
Search
To search for a range of character strings using search, use
the following procedure:
1. Type the characters for the lower end of the range in the
ﬁrst line under the mask in the correct ﬁeld.
2. Type r in the ﬁrst character position in the next line.
3. On the same line as the R parameter, type the
characters for the higher end of the range in the same
ﬁeld as the lower end. Then transmit.
*St.Status.
*Cd. Date .
*==.======.
** ******
800101 <-- Lower end of the range.
r 890520 <-- R parameter in column 1 designating
a range
search; higher end of the range.
See Searching for a Range of Data Example.
To Search for Certain Line Types
Specify that line type as the type to process. For search
parameters, specify a range search of every character in the
character set. See Searching for All Characters on a Speciﬁc
Line Type Example.
R line types should not be used because they conﬂict with
the range (R) parameter.
To Create an Update Result

Use the Search Update command to create an update result.
The SRU statement uses the same ﬁelds and subﬁelds as
the SRH statement.
You can process the update result in the following ways:
Delete the update result lines from the original report
using the Delete command.
Delete the update result lines from the original report
and redisplay the result using the Extract command.
Merge updated lines in the update result into the
original report using the Update command. (For faster
updating, use the Totalize command to ﬁll ﬁelds with
dates, status codes, and so on before calling the Update
command.)
When you are searching multiple reports, using the Search
Update (SU) function, update results are displayed one at a
time so that you can change, update, or delete the data
from the report. When ready to display the next search
update result, press Resume.
To Search for Data in Large, Sorted Reports
If the report is already sorted and you only need to search
on the sorted ﬁeld, you can use the Binary Find command
with the O option instead of the Search command. For large
reports, this is more eﬃcient than using the Search
command. For more details, see BF and @BFN (Binary Find).

@SRH Statement Guidelines
This subtopic describes how to use a run to
Search for multiple strings
Search for a range of character strings
Process update results created with the SRU statement
To Search for Multiple Strings with a Statement
Use a slant (/) to separate multiple parameters. See
Searching for Multiple Strings Example.
To Search for a Range of Character Strings
Use a slant (/) to separate multiple parameters and an R in
the second ltyp subﬁeld to indicate a range search. See
Searching for a Range of Data Example.
To Process Update Results Created with the SRU
Statement
Set up a loop to process the update results individually and
to repeat the SRU statement on the next report in the range
of reports you want to search. (A loop is a sequence of
statements that execute repeatedly until a speciﬁed
condition is met.)

Searching and Updating All Reports in a Drawer
Example
This example searches all reports in a drawer and when a
speciﬁed parameter is found, it deletes the line and
continues to the next report. The run completes after all the
reports are searched.
@sru,<cab>,<drw>,,,,0089 dh 'columns','alias' ,
<cdr>,<delt> .
@del gto lin -1 .
@0089 gto end .

Searching and Updating a Range of Reports
Using the R Option Example
This example displays every report that matches the search
criteria as an update result.
@ldv v1i4=1 .
@010:sru,20,b,,,,100 adhrv1-5000 2-4 ,1234 ,,v1 .
@dsp,-0 .
@upd .
@inc v1 if v1 < 5001 gto 010 .
@100 .

Searching for a Paragraph of Data Example
A paragraph of data is all lines from the speciﬁed line type
processed to the next line type of that kind. This example
searches for a status code of OR on asterisk lines, but
includes in the result all lines, regardless of their content
between the found line and the next occurrence of an
asterisk line.

Searching for a Paragraph of Data using the U
Option Example
Still using the sample database from the two previous
examples, referred to as report3a in the statement, this
example searches for a paragraph of data containing data of
a line type diﬀerent from the line type that deﬁnes the
paragraph. The u option deﬁnes the beginning line type of
the paragraph, which ends with the line before the next
paragraph line type.
Function Mask
u(*)
*St.Status.
*Cd. Date .
*==.======.
** ******
ip
Equivalent Statement
@srh,'report3a' u(*) 2-2 |,ip .
Result
. 1 LINE(S) FOUND OUT OF 14 LINES
.U(*)
***
*ip
. . .
.DATE 18 DEC 92 09:39:44 RID 3A
.
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.

*OR 840110 LS BLACKBOX4
SC 840110 LS BLACKBOX5 675281
IP 831222 LS BLACKBOX5 737582
.Comment line describing item
IP 831216 LS BLACKBOX6 926581
AXX 831202 LS MAUVEBOX0
BXX 831209 LS ITSA-BOX0
SH 831203 LS BLACKBOX6 785367
IP 831216 LS BLACKBOX6 926581
The Search command ﬁnds the ﬁrst IP within the paragraph
beginning with an asterisk and ending before the next
asterisk line. It claims to ﬁnd one line, not three, as it might
appear, because the second and third IP items are simply
part of the paragraph that is being extracted. Also, the
Search command does not ﬁnd the three IP items that
appear before the ﬁrst asterisk line because they are not
part of a paragraph as we deﬁned it.

that encompasses the entire character set. In this example,

character set, from space (designated as @) through tilde
(~). The slant (/) speciﬁes a partial ﬁeld search-process only
the ﬁrst character in the St Cd ﬁeld.

*Cd. Date .

Finding Data Within a Range of Dates Example
This example ﬁnds data within a range of dates. The Y
option indicates the presence of a date format parameter
line and the Y parameter identiﬁes the date format
parameter line. The number 1 in the Status Date ﬁeld
speciﬁes the numeric date format to use.
Function Mask
Y
*St.Status.By. Product
.Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.Produc.Produc. Ship .
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
Plan .Actual. Date .
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.====.
======.======.======.
** ****** ** ********* ****** ****** ***** ****
****** ****** ******
sh 970101
Rsh 001231
Y 1
Equivalent Statement
@srh,'drawerb' y 2-2,5-6
|,sh,970101/r,sh,001231/y,,1 .

Note: If the search parameter is not speciﬁed, the search is
not processed. The statement does not show any error or
display any message, but control passes to the next
statement in the script.
Example

@srh,0,B,2 '' 45-4 |, amco .
Partial Result
. 9 LINE(S) FOUND OUT OF 42 LINES
.
* ****
* amco
. . .
.DATE 02 JUN 92 12:38:12 RID 2B 20 JAN 9
.Production Status Report
*St.Status.By. Product .Serial.Produc.Order.Cust.
*Cd. Date .In. Type .Number. Cost .Numbr.Code.
*==.======.==.=========.======.======.=====.====.
IP 831224 LS BLACKBOX1 436767 84389 AMCO
IP 831225 LS BLACKBOX1 436768 84390 AMCO

@SC (Screen Control)
Use the Screen Control (SC) statement to create menus,
input screens, and overlays using screen commands.
The SC statement has two formats. Use format 1 to directly
specify screen commands, or use format 2 to access a
report containing screen commands (also referred to as a
form).
If you use format 2, a report containing executable screen
commands must exist.

Screen Control Statement Formats
Format 1
@SC[,,,,,,tabp,sn,lab] o scmnd .

Field

Description

tabp

Tab position at which to place
the cursor, where n is the
number of tab positions
forward from the home
position and -n is the number
of tab positions backward from
the home position. Maximum
= 100 tab positions forward or
backward. Default = no
positioning.

2200 Þ Þ

sn

Station number where output
is to be displayed. If you omit
this ﬁeld or specify zero,
output is displayed at the
station executing the run.

lab

Label to go to if the output to
another station cannot be

completed successfully. If you
omit this ﬁeld and the run
cannot be successfully
completed, it is terminated
with an error.

Ü Ü 2200

o

Options ﬁeld. See Screen
Control Options.

scmnd

Screen command or
commands to be executed.
See Screen Commands.

Format 2
@SC,c,d,r[,l,q,tabp,sn,lab] o [fldtxt] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing screen
commands (also referred to as
a form).

l

Line number in the speciﬁed
report at which to start

reading screen control
commands. Default = 2.

q

Number of lines to read in the
speciﬁed report. Default = all
lines to the end of the report.

tabp

Tab position at which to place
the cursor, where n is the
number of tab positions
forward from the home
position and -n is the number
of tab positions backward from
the home position. Maximum
= 100 tab positions forward or
backward. Default = no
positioning.

2200 Þ Þ

sn

Station number where output
is to be displayed. If you omit
this ﬁeld or specify zero,
output is displayed at the
station executing the run.

lab

Label to go to if the output to
another station cannot be
completed successfully. If you
omit this ﬁeld and the run

cannot be successfully
completed, it is terminated
with an error.

Ü Ü 2200

o

Options ﬁeld. See Screen
Control Options.

ﬂdtxt

Field text. Text to be inserted
into unprotected ﬁelds. For
more information see Deﬁne
Attribute (DEF) Command,
Deﬁne Field (DFLD) Command,
and AREA Command. This ﬁeld
is a list of items separated by
commas. The ﬁrst item is
placed in the ﬁrst ﬁeld, the
second item in the second
ﬁeld, and so on.
This ﬁeld is not interpreted
unless the T (ﬁeld text) option
is speciﬁed in the SC
statement.

A Form
A form is a report containing executable SC commands. An
SC form can consist of up to three sections:

The command section, which starts at the beginning of
the form and continues to the ﬁrst END command. See
Screen Commands for more information.
The edit section, which starts after the ﬁrst END
command and continues to the second END command.
See Verifying User Input (EDIT) Command for more
information.
The help section, which begins immediately after the
second END command and extends to the end of the
form. See DSPHELP Action under Special FKEY Actions
for information about context-sensitive help.
Alternative Screen Design Run-SCGEN
You can also design your screens using a menu-driven
screen generator called the SCGEN run. For information on
the SCGEN run, see SCGEN (Screen Control Generator), or
enter apt on the control line, tab to SCGEN, and press Help.
Actions, Commands, and Statements
See the sections listed for details on the actions,
commands, and statements described in the following
information.

Screen Control Options
Option

Description

B

Blink. Changes the less than
(<) and greater than (>) signs
to the left and right blink
characters in all literal text.
The blink characters may vary
from terminal to terminal. See
Special Characters (TIC, SOE,
LB, RB) for information about
blink characters.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C

Center. Enables screen output
(such as menus) designed to
be centered on standard 80character display terminals to
be centered on terminals of
other widths.

Note: An extra ﬁeld is created
when the C option is used with
a screen control screen that
returns input to a run and the
screen width is greater than 80
columns. Once this extra ﬁeld
is created, the ﬁelds and the

variables in the INPUT$
statement do not correspond
and data is placed in the
wrong variables. When this
occurs, either remove the C
option, reset the terminal size
to 80 columns, or redesign the
screen control screen.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C

Center. Enables screen output
(such as menus) designed to
be centered on standard 80character display terminals to
be centered on terminals of
other widths.

Ü Ü 2200

H

Home Cursor Bypass. Bypasses
the initial home cursor (HC)
command that is automatically
performed by the SC
statement before executing
the ﬁrst screen command.
Using this option causes the
initial cursor position to be
unknown. References to

current or relative positions are
not allowed until Screen
Control encounters a command
that establishes an absolute
position (for example, PREP,
FLD,r,c, and DATA,r,c).

I

Interim Output. Resumes the
run automatically when the
output is completed. Input is
not accepted. See also the Q
option.

K

Key Mapping. Causes the
current function key mapping
and input editing to remain in
eﬀect. By default, the SC
statement clears the current
function key mapping and
input editing before executing
the ﬁrst screen command. This
option does not prevent the
bottom screen line from being
erased.

L

Line Control. Enables you to
regain line control on the
previously displayed report,
using the Paint key.

M

Mask. Sends the data, but not

spaces. (If data exists on a
line, only the columns
containing new data are
overwritten. The old and new
data are blended together on
the same line.)

O

Overlay menu. Reprocesses
the screen commands, but
repaints only the text. It
eﬃciently repaints or puts new
data onto a menu that is
already on the screen.

Q

Quick Output. Resumes the run
automatically when the output
is displayed. This option is
similar to the I option except
that the run does not suspend,
pending completion of the
output, but rather continues as
soon as the output is
displayed.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

S

Sends the data including all
spaces. The default method of
sending characters is that if
fewer than six consecutive
spaces appear, the system

sends the data, including
spaces. If six or more spaces
appear, the cursor skips over
the positions where the spaces
would have been placed.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

S

Sends the data including all
spaces. Without the S option,
results are unpredictable for
the diﬀerent terminal types.

Ü Ü 2200

T

Field Text. Inserts values
supplied in the ﬂdtxt ﬁeld into
unprotected screen ﬁelds
created with the FLD and AREA
commands. For more
information, see Field (FLD)
Command and Deﬁne Field
(DFLD) Command. The T
option is valid only when a
report is speciﬁed in the SC
statement (see Format 2). The
statement supplies values to
the FLD and AREA commands

in the order the commands
appear in the report.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

U

Update Control. Overlays a
current report with data (for
example, to display a
message) but enables the user
to perform manual SOE
updates by holding update
control of the report. This
option is meaningful only when
a report is on display.

Note: Some statements cause
update control to be released
without actually removing the
report from the screen.
Statements that process
reports and do screen output
normally release update
control. Update control does
not exist unless both DRW$
and DLINE$ are nonzero.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

U

Update Control. Overlays a

current report with data (for
example, to display a
message) but enables the user
to perform manual SOE
updates by holding update
control of the report. This
option is meaningful only when
a report is on display.

Note: Some statements cause
update control to be released
without actually removing the
report from the screen.
Statements that process
reports and do screen output
normally release update
control. Update control does
not exist unless both DRW$
and DLINE$ are nonzero.
This option also enables the
run user to update the current
report or result (-0), whether it
is currently on display or not.

Ü Ü 2200

X

Causes a screen to be
transmitted after it is
displayed, just as if the user
had pressed Transmit.

Screen Control Outcome
When SC output is displayed, the run suspends until the
user presses Transmit or a function key, unless the
statement options indicate an interim output or a forced
transmit.

Screen Control Reserved Words
Use the reserved words that follow to tailor screens to a site,
user, and terminal. Using reserved words also enhances the
portability of screens.
You can use the following reserved words in forms.

Reserved Description
Word

ADRW$

Alphabetic drawer of the
current result.

AKEY$

Key or key sequence used to
perform the Abort command.

ALERT$

The current site alert
message text.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

CAB$

Cabinet number of the report
or result last processed, or
cabinet number of the report
or result on display when the
run started, or zero if no

report or result was processed
or on display.
CAB$ always contains the
even cabinet number.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

CAB$

Cabinet number of the report
or result last processed, or
cabinet number of the report
or result on display when the
run started, or zero if no report
or result was processed or on
display.
CAB$ contains the odd cabinet
number when one of the
following occurs:
The CS command or any
run displays an odd
cabinet number report (for
example, CS,7,3b or
@dsp,7,b,3).
The user requests a report
in an odd cabinet, using
the rdc format (for
example, 3b7).

If CAB$ is odd, the run is not
allowed to update reports. If
either CAB$ or CAB1$ is odd,
the user is not allowed to
update reports manually.

Ü Ü 2200

CAB1$

Currently active cabinet
number.

2200 Þ Þ

CAB1$

Currently active cabinet
number.
If either CAB$ or CAB1$ is
odd, the user cannot update
reports manually. Business
Information Server runs ignore
CAB1$, so only CAB$ needs to
be odd to prevent updating by
a run.

Ü Ü 2200

DATE0$

Current date in YMMDD
format.

DATE1$

Current date in YYMMDD
format.

DATE2$

Current date in DD MMM YY
format.

Current date in YDDD format.

DATE4$

Current date in YYDDD
format.

DATE5$

Current date in DDMMYY
format.

DATE6$

Current date in MM/DD/YY
format.

DATE7$

Current date in MONTH DD,
YYYY format.

DATE8$

Current date in MMDDYY
format.

DATE9$

Current date in DD/MM/YY
format.

DATE11$

Current date in YYYYMMDD
format.

Current date in DD MMM YYYY
format.

Current date in YYYYDDD
format.

Current date in DDMMYYYY
format.

Current date in MM/DD/YYYY
format.

Current date in MMDDYYYY
format.

Current date in DD/MM/YYYY
format.

format.

DATE21$

Current date in DD-MMM-YY

Current day in DAY format
(MON, TUE, and so on).

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

The current site database
path.

User's department sign-on

User's department name.

Line number of the ﬁrst

2200 Þ Þ

Ü Ü 2200

F1$
through
F10$

Key or key sequence used as
the F1 through F10 function
keys.

FPAGE$

Last explicitly called page of a
Screen Control form.

KKEY$

Key or key sequence used to
obtain keyboard help, if key
mapping help is available on
your system.

LEVEL$

A string of characters
identifying the current
software level. This reserved

is redeﬁned with every
product build.

Error Status Reserved Words
If the run fails while processing an SC statement, the run
loads STAT1$ and STAT2$ with the following information:
STAT1$ contains the column number where the error
occurred. If screen commands are being read from a
report, STAT1$ contains a report column; otherwise, it
contains the character position in the interpreted screen
command string (from the scmnd ﬁeld) of the SC
statement.
STAT2$ contains the report line number where the error
occurred in the screen command report (format 2 only).

Screen Commands
Following is a list of the sections that describe screen
commands:
Field attribute commands (PREP, FLD, DFLD, AREA, DEF,
MSG, ATT)
Text handling commands (DATA, LIST, #PAGE, EMP, TIC,
SOE, LB, RB)
Cursor control commands (HC, PC, CR, TAB)
Setup commands (END, FKEY, BEEP, CP, MODE, OPTS)
Screen editing commands (PD, CS, ED, EUD, EEL, DIL,
DID, IIL, IID, DL, IL, DUP)
Screen printing commands (PRT, PRF, LF, FF)
Use screen commands to perform basic screen operations,
which include positioning the cursor, clearing the screen,
and deﬁning ﬁelds and screen attributes.
You can also use screen commands to design reports that
deﬁne ﬁelds, box data in a speciﬁed area, and map function
keys.

Screen Control Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using screen commands:
To provide a known starting point, the system moves the
cursor to the home position before the ﬁrst screen
command is interpreted.
Screen commands are executed in the order they are
speciﬁed.
When commands are read from a report, a trailing space
on any subﬁeld or a space in column 1 terminates the
scan of the current report line. You can use the
remaining portion of the line for comments.
Not all screen commands produce the same results on
all types of display terminals. Whenever possible, screen
commands that cannot be executed on a particular
terminal are ignored.
By default, the SC statement disables any current
function key mapping before executing the ﬁrst screen
command. Use the K option to keep the current function
key mapping enabled.
Any string of data that is not the name of a valid screen
command is treated as literal text.

Syntax Rules for Screen Commands
Follow these syntax rules when writing screen commands:
Entering Screen Commands on a Line
To enter screen commands on a line
Use semicolons (;) to separate commands.
If a screen command is too long to ﬁt on one report line,
you can continue it on the next line. Any command
subﬁeld that speciﬁes text, such as ﬁeld or message
text, recognizes the reverse slant (\) as the continuation
character when it occurs outside of literal delimiters.
Specifying Screen Coordinates
To specify screen coordinates
If you omit a value, the current cursor position is used.
If you specify a signed value (for example, +3 or -10), it
is assumed to be relative to the current cursor position.
The home position is 1,1 (row 1, column 1).
Specifying Attribute Parameters
Enclose attribute parameters in parentheses. This is
necessary because the attribute parameters together are
actually one subﬁeld. For example: att,(rv,whi/red)
def,1,(pr,rv,tc); att,1
See Using Inline Attributes, Attribute (ATT) Command, and
Deﬁne Attribute (DEF) Command.

Displaying and Processing Literal Text
Literal text often requires special handling in order to be
properly displayed. In many cases, Screen Control
commands are scanned as text by the run interpreter before
actually being processed. This does not apply when using
format 2 with a permanent report.
In the software there are two run interpreters for processing
literal text-parameter and output area. Format 1 uses the
parameter interpreter to scan a run control report for lines
beginning with an at sign (@). Format 2 reads literal text
from a report-a result created in the output area of the run
or a permanent report.
Examples of Using Literal Text
Simple Literal Text
Literal text, such as ﬁeld or message text, that contains
spaces, commas, or semicolons must be enclosed by
delimiters.
Format 1 (statement): Use a double set of literal delimiters,
such as TIC$ and the apostrophe:
@sc '' tic$'Glen Ellyn'tic$ .
Format 2 (output area): Enclose the literal text in single
apostrophes ( ' ) or whatever literal delimiter is in use: ''Glen
Ellyn''
See Special Characters (TIC, SOE, LB, RB).
Format 2 (permanent report):
'Glen Ellyn'

Literal Text Containing Apostrophes
Displaying apostrophes requires using the TIC command to
redeﬁne the Screen Control literal delimiter to some other
character.
Format 1 (statement): Deﬁne the literal delimiter to be a
character other than an apostrophe and use TIC$ to equal
an apostrophe in the literal text. This example statement
deﬁnes the delimiter as % and displays "We've got it!": @sc
'' tic,%;%'We'tic$'ve got it!'% .
Format 2 (output area): Use a sequence of two apostrophes
to produce one apostrophe in your data:
tic,%;%We''ve got it!%
Format 2 (permanent report):
tic,%;%We've got it!%
Literal Text from Run Variables
Follow the general guidelines set for literal text and place
the variable name in the literal text. For example, these
format examples place the value of <who> in the literal
phrase.
Format 1 (statement):
@sc '' tic$'Let '<who>' out'tic$ .
Format 2 (output area):
''Let <who> out''
Format 2 (permanent report): This does not apply.

Complex Text Containing Run Variables and
Apostrophes
Format 1 (statement):
@sc '' tic,%;%'Disa'tic$'s phone number is
'<phone>% .
Format 2 (output area):
tic,%;%Disa''s phone number is <phone>%
Format 2 (permanent report): This does not apply.

Verifying User Input (EDIT) Command
You can use the EDIT command to verify user input. After a
user completes ﬁelds on the form, the editing section of the
form veriﬁes the input before continuing. If any value is in
error, a message is displayed and the cursor is placed in the
ﬁeld that contains the error.

Note: All input ﬁelds must have the tab stop (TS) attribute
for editing to work properly.
One EDIT command can apply to any number of ﬁelds.
2200 Þ Þ

EDIT f Dn date1[/date2]

date1

date.

date2

Parts of an Edit Section
The edit section consists of one or more edit descriptions.
Each edit description consists of an EDIT command followed
by one or more rule statements, which specify how to verify
the ﬁelds.

Screen Control Date Formats
Two-digit year dates must represent dates between 1944
and 2043.
Four-digit year dates must represent dates between 1600
and 2299.
One-digit year dates must represent dates in the years
between -4 and +5 years from the current date. For
example, 1 January 1996 through 31 December 2005 (if the
current day was 1 November 2000).

values are used, the minus sign can be an eighth character.
The values in output ﬁelds are right-justiﬁed, space-ﬁlled. If
the output ﬁeld is too small for the value, it is ﬁlled with
asterisks (*).

Field Attribute Commands
Use ﬁeld attribute commands to deﬁne ﬁelds and their
attributes.
You can place attributes anywhere on the screen. The
attribute you deﬁne extends forward to the immediate left
of the next attribute or to the end of the screen, whichever
occurs ﬁrst.
Prepare Screen for Painting (PREP) Command
Use the Prepare Screen for Painting (PREP) command to
prepare the screen for an input menu by clearing the screen
and assigning an attribute on row 2, column 1.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If you do not specify an attribute, the command assumes
protected input and the background color deﬁned by the
Terminal Deﬁnition Report.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If you do not specify an attribute, the command assumes
protected input and the colors white on black.
Ü Ü 2200
The PREP command is equivalent to this sequence of
commands:
CS;ATT,(attr),2,1.

Options ﬁeld: Border options.
Use any combination of the
following options to create a
border for your ﬁeld:
A - attributes. Assign ﬁeld
attributes even if the ﬁeld is
being boxed. Use this option to
produce a highlighted box
around a ﬁeld. Use only with
the B option.
B - box. Draw a box around the
ﬁeld if the terminal supports
box drawing characters. The
box is drawn inside the
perimeter of the ﬁeld reducing
the eﬀective size of the ﬁeld
by two rows and columns. See
also the A option.
This option is ignored if the
ﬁeld contains text, or if the rsiz
and csiz ﬁelds are not greater
than 1.

F - frame. Frame the ﬁeld with
emphasis. This option is
terminal dependent.
S - sides. Draw lines on the left
and right sides, using
emphasis. This option is
terminal dependent.
T - top. Draw a line at the top
of the ﬁeld, using emphasis.
This option is terminal
dependent.
U - underline. Draw a line
under the ﬁeld, using
emphasis. This option is
terminal dependent.

o

Options ﬁeld: Justiﬁcation
options.
Use one of the following
options to control justiﬁcation
of text in a ﬁeld:
C - center the text in the ﬁeld.
L - left-justify the text in the
ﬁeld.
R - right-justify the text in the
ﬁeld.
V - vary the size of the ﬁeld to
ﬁt the text supplied. Default

(no text) and maximum size is
csiz. The ﬁeld is left-justiﬁed
within the columns bounded
by c and csiz; use this with the
R or C options to alter
justiﬁcation.

o

Options ﬁeld: Miscellaneous
options.
Use the following options to
control other ﬁeld attributes:
E - empty. If the ﬁeld is empty
(no text), do not display it. Use
this option for status ﬁelds
that should only be displayed
if relevant.
P - protect. Two conditions
apply:
If a ﬁeld is not deﬁned with the
PR parameter and is empty
when placed on the screen,
protect it from user input.
If a ﬁeld is deﬁned with the PR
parameter, allow data that is
supplied on the SC statement
through the statement option
T.

attr

Attribute that deﬁnes the

characteristics of the ﬁeld. See
Field Attribute Parameters.
Default = no attributes
assigned.

text

Literal text to be inserted into
the ﬁeld. If you do not specify
text, the data within the
boundaries of the ﬁeld remain
unaltered. If you use the T
option on the SC statement,
text supplied in the ﬂdtxt ﬁeld
of the SC statement is used
instead. (See also the FLD
command P option.)

Outcome
After the statement executes the FLD command, the cursor
is displayed on the last line of the ﬁeld, two columns beyond
the end of the ﬁeld.
Example
This example shows how the FLD command is used to
create the Add Report (AR) input screen. The A, B, and F
options in the ﬁrst FLD command create a highlighted
border if permitted by the terminal type. The second FLD
command places the menu title in the center of the top line
of the border. Execute the AR function to see the result.
prep,(pr,bac)
fkey,1,Resume,rsm
fkey,2,Paint,pnt

fkey,4,Return,formret,7
fkey,8,Help,dsphelp
fkey,10,Quit,^
pc,1,61;'AR'
fld,2,6,6,69,afb,(PR,boc)
fld,2,6,,69,cv,(PR,tc),,' Add Report '
dfld,inp,u,(ts,ai,fc);dfld,,,(co,fc)
area,,3,8,4,65,,(pr,mc)
Report or drawer __inp_________
Title __inp_________________________________
end
Deﬁne Field (DFLD) Command
Use the Deﬁne Field (DFLD) command to deﬁne ﬁeld
characteristics for later use by the AREA command. The
DFLD command supplies all the information necessary to
deﬁne a ﬁeld, except for screen location and ﬁeld size,
which is supplied by the AREA command. After an AREA
command is processed, all ﬁeld deﬁnitions are discarded.
You can deﬁne up to 40 named and 40 unnamed ﬁelds,
using the DFLD command. See Using AREA and DFLD
Commands for examples.
Format
DFLD[,name,o,attr,text]

Field

Description

name

Name of the ﬁeld you are

deﬁning. Maximum = 12
characters.

o

Border options (terminal
dependent):
F - frame. Frame the ﬁeld with
emphasis.
S - sides. Draw lines on the left
and right sides, using
emphasis.
T - top. Draw a line at the top
of the ﬁeld, using emphasis.
U - underline. Draw a line
under the ﬁeld, using
emphasis.
Justiﬁcation options:
C - center the text in the ﬁeld.
L - left-justify the text in the
ﬁeld.
R - right-justify the text in the
ﬁeld.
V - Vary the size of the ﬁeld to
ﬁt the text supplied. Default
(no text) and maximum size is
determined by the length of
the ﬁeld as speciﬁed in the
AREA command data. Use this

with the R or C options to alter
justiﬁcation.
Miscellaneous options:
E - empty. If the ﬁeld is empty
(no text), do not display it. Use
this option for status ﬁelds
that should only be displayed
if relevant.
P - protect. Two conditions
apply:
If a ﬁeld is not deﬁned
with the PR parameter and
is empty when placed on
the screen, protect it from
user input.
If a ﬁeld is deﬁned with
the PR parameter, allow
data that is supplied on
the SC statement through
the statement option T.

attr

Attribute that deﬁnes the
characteristics of the ﬁeld. See
Field Attribute Parameters.
Default = no attributes
assigned.

text

Literal text to be inserted into
the ﬁeld. If you use the T

option on the SC statement,
text supplied in the ﬂdtxt ﬁeld
of the SC statement is used
instead. See also the P option.
If you do not supply text, the
ﬁeld will be blank. The
statement supplies T option
values to ﬁelds in the order
speciﬁed by the AREA
command, not in DFLD
command order.

AREA Command
Use the AREA command to lay out an entire region (area) of
the screen, including both its text and ﬁelds.
The data that deﬁnes the contents of the area begins in
column 1 of the report line immediately following the AREA
command. The text is displayed exactly as it appears. The
locations and sizes of ﬁelds within the area are indicated by
underline characters ( _ ).
The AREA command recognizes reserved words, inline
attributes, and literal delimiters in its data. (See Using Inline
Attributes.) Notes:
The AREA command is valid only if screen commands
are being read from a report.
It is recommended that you use the S option on the SC
statement when using the AREA command.
Prerequisite

The characteristics of the ﬁelds indicated with the AREA
command must be previously deﬁned with DFLD commands.
Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
AREA[,name,r,c,rsiz,csiz,o,attr,fldchar]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
AREA[,,r,c,rsiz,csiz,o,attr,fldchar]
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Field

Description

name

A name identifying the region
on the screen deﬁned by the
area. The name is valid until
the next time the screen is
erased. The system tracks up
to ten named areas at a time.
When a user presses Transmit
or a function key from
anywhere within a named
area, all input from that area,
and only from that area, is
received. If the area is output
from a run,

AREA$ contains the name
of the area from which
input occurs.
FIELD$ contains the ﬁeld
number within the area in
which the cursor is
located.
CURV$/CURH$ contains
the cursor coordinates
relative to the upper left
corner of the area.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

r,c

Cursor position coordinates for
the upper left corner of the
area. See Syntax Rules for
Screen Commands for rules
about specifying screen
coordinates.

rsiz

Vertical area size in rows
(lines). Default = 1.

csiz

Horizontal area size in columns
(characters). Default = 1.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

o

Options ﬁeld.
Border options (use any
combination of the following
options to create a border for
your ﬁeld): A - attributes.
Display the background

the area is being boxed. Use
only with the B option.
B - box. Draw a box around the

box drawing characters. The

perimeter of the ﬁeld. See also
the A option.
The top and bottom rows of
data are incorporated into the
box and the ﬁrst and last
columns are deleted.
F - frame. Frame the area. This
option is terminal dependent.
U - underline. Underline the
area. This option is terminal
dependent.
Miscellaneous option:
D - delimiter. Mark ﬁelds with a
ﬁeld delimiter character only.
Named ﬁelds are not

recognized. Enables you to
place text immediately
following a ﬁeld.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

o

Options ﬁeld.
Border options (use any
combination of the following
options to create a border for
your ﬁeld): A - attributes.
Display the background
attributes of the area even if
the area is being boxed. Use
only with the B option.
B - box. Draw a box around the
ﬁeld if the terminal supports
box drawing characters. The
box is drawn inside the
perimeter of the ﬁeld. See also
the A option.
The top and bottom rows of
data and the ﬁrst and last
columns are overwritten by the
box.
F - frame. Frame the area. This
option is terminal dependent.

U - underline. Underline the
area. This option is terminal
dependent.
Miscellaneous option:
D - delimiter. Mark ﬁelds with a
ﬁeld delimiter character only.
Named ﬁelds are not
recognized. Enables you to
place text immediately
following a ﬁeld.

Ü Ü 2200

attr

Attribute that deﬁnes the
background characteristics of
the area. Default = no
attributes assigned.

ﬂdchar

Character that delimits ﬁelds
within the AREA data. Default
= underline character ( _ ).

Using AREA and DFLD Commands
If a named ﬁeld is indicated in the area data, the
characteristics of the ﬁeld come from the DFLD command
specifying the same ﬁeld name. A named ﬁeld deﬁnition can
be referenced more than once.

The characteristics of unnamed ﬁelds in the area are
supplied by DFLD commands that specify no ﬁeld name. The
ﬁrst unnamed ﬁeld indicated in the area uses the ﬁrst
unnamed deﬁnition; the second unnamed ﬁeld uses the
second unnamed deﬁnition, and so on.
If there are more unnamed ﬁelds indicated than there are
unnamed deﬁnitions, the last unnamed deﬁnition is used to
satisfy the remaining ﬁelds.
After the AREA command is performed, all ﬁeld deﬁnitions
are discarded.
Example
This example uses the DFLD and AREA commands to lay out
an input menu. Note that each ﬁeld begins with at least one
underline character. When named ﬁeld references are
indicated in the area data, the name is included in the size
of the ﬁeld.
The ﬁrst unnamed ﬁeld (Drawer letter) uses the ﬁrst
unnamed deﬁnition; the second unnamed ﬁeld (User-id)
uses the second unnamed deﬁnition. The last menu ﬁeld
(the single-character ﬁeld following the Last Report ﬁeld)
uses the third unnamed deﬁnition. The FLD commands put
the border and title around the menu; this must be done
before the AREA command.
fld,3,9,10,60,afb,(pr,rv,boc)
fld,3,9,,60,cv,(pr,tc),,' Index User '
dfld,num,u,(ts,no,fc)
dfld,date,u,(ts,ai,fc)
dfld,,u,(ts,ao,fc)
dfld,,u,(ts,ai,fc),user$
dfld,,,(co,fc)

area,,4,11,8,56,,(pr,mc)
Number of lines from each report _num_
Drawer letter _
User-id (ALL for all) ____________
Last update start date (DDMMMYY) _date__
Last update end date (DDMMMYY) _date__
First report _num
Last report _num _
end
Deﬁne Attribute (DEF) Command
Use the Deﬁne Attribute (DEF) command to deﬁne a
numbered attribute for subsequent use in other screen
commands. Once a numbered attribute is deﬁned, you can
use it anywhere an attribute is required.
Format
DEF,n,attr

Field

Description

n

Number with which to identify
the attribute (1 through 20).

attr

Attribute to deﬁne. See Field
Attribute Parameters.

Example

This example deﬁnes attribute 1 as having protected input
and a color of white on blue:
def,1,(pr,whi/blu)
Message (MSG) Command
Use the Message (MSG) command to display a message or
prompt line.
Format
MSG,r,o,attr,text

Field

Description

r

Row on which to place the
message.

o

Options:
Blank - Display message text
as given.
B - Blink. Allow blink
characters to be used on nonsystem messages. See Special
Characters (TIC, SOE, LB, RB).
E - Present the text in error
message format. That is,
center the message text on
the line and translate the

greater than (>) and less than
(<) characters to left and right
blink characters L - Left-justify
the message text.
R - Right-justify the message
text.
C - Center the message text.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

attr

Screen attribute for the
message line.
Default = reverse video (RV)
and the user's conﬁgured
system message color as
assigned in the user
registration report.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

attr

Screen attribute for the
message line.
Default = reverse video (RV)
with white characters on a red
background.

Ü Ü 2200

text

Literal text for the message
(up to 79 characters).

Outcome
After the statement executes the MSG command, the cursor
is displayed in column 1 of the line on which the message is
displayed.
Guideline
If you place the MSG command on a line other than the
control line, it overrides any attributes previously deﬁned for
the screen. Consequently, any lines below the line where
the message is placed are no longer protected.
Example
This example uses format 1 to display a message on line 1,
using the text supplied. The default message attribute is
used.
@sc '' msg,1,e,,tic$'<That input is not
valid>'tic$ .
Attribute (ATT) Command
Use the Attribute (ATT) command to place an attribute at
the current or designated cursor position.
Format

ATT,attr[,r,c]

Field

Description

attr

Attribute to be placed. See
Field Attribute Parameters.

r,c

Cursor position where
attribute begins. See Syntax
Rules for Screen Commands
for rules about specifying
screen coordinates.

Example
This example positions the cursor to row 2, column 1, and
places an attribute on the screen having protected input
and a color of white on blue: att,(pr,whi/blu),2,1
Using Inline Attributes
Use inline attributes in DATA and AREA commands to
highlight words or phrases. Create inline attributes using the
tilde (~) character followed by any valid ﬁeld attribute
parameter or followed by the number of a previously
deﬁned attribute. See Field Attribute Parameters for a
complete list of parameters. See Deﬁne Attribute (DEF)
Command for deﬁning attribute numbers.
Examples

In the following example, the attribute parameter follows
the tilde. When the text is displayed, it is displayed as
reverse video and yellow on black, and it starts at the cursor
position of the ﬁrst tilde.
~(rv,yel/bla)SC uses inline attributes.~(whi/bla)
In the following example, DEF commands specify attributes
numbered 1, 2 and 3. The AREA command uses a tilde
followed by attribute number, to indicate which attribute to
apply.
def,1,(pr,mc);def,2,(pr,whi/bla);def,3,
(pr,yel/bla)
.
. (other screen control commands)
.
area,,3,2,4,76,,(pr,mc)
~3Move cursor to desired menu and press
Transmit.~1
~2Accounting Functions~1
_ Payables Menu
_ Receivables Menu

Field Attribute Parameters
Use ﬁeld attribute parameters to determine the following
attributes:
Input control
Intensity
Text input justiﬁcation
Tab stop
Emphasis protection
Color

Note: Field attributes are terminal dependent and may
not be available on all terminals.
Input Control Parameters

Parameter Description

AI

Any input allowed (except
kanji).

AO

Alphabetic input only.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

CO

Cursor only. The user can
move the cursor into the ﬁeld,
but cannot type input. Use to
create menu selection items.
The user can move between
ﬁelds using tab keys, arrow
keys, or the spacebar.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

CO

UTS terminals treat the CO
parameter as an NO
parameter.

Ü Ü 2200

NO

Numeric input only.

PR

Protected-cursor cannot be
placed in ﬁeld.

Kanji Input Control Parameters
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Parameter Description

AIK

Any input, including kanji
(default).

AK

Alpha and kanji input.

KO

Kanji input only.

NK

Numeric and kanji input.

Note: Kanji parameters are meaningful only for terminals
that support kanji.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Intensity Parameters

Parameter Description

BL

Blinking.

LI

Low intensity.

NI

Normal intensity (default).

VO

Video Oﬀ. Use to make text
invisible.

Justiﬁcation Parameter

Parameter Description

RJ

Right-justify data entered by
the user.

Tab Stop Parameter

Parameter Description

TS

Tab stop. Unlike a tab
character, an attribute tab
stop does not require a
screen location, so the
cursor stops at the attribute
position rather than
immediately after it.

Emphasis Protection Parameter

Parameter Description

UE

Unprotected emphasis.
Overrides the normal
protection given to
emphasis. The UE
parameter causes the
emphasis to be destroyed if
the data at that location is
erased or typed over.

Color Parameters
Specify color parameters in pairs, separated by a slant (/).
For example, WHI/RED speciﬁes white characters on a red
background. If you do not specify a color, white characters
on a black background is assumed.
If you want to override the color attributes of menus, input
screens, or overlays generated by the SC statement, you
can set the BlackOnWhite parameter in the MPC.INI ﬁle to
true (1). See the Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server for more information on the MPC.INI ﬁle.

Value

Description

BLA

Black

BLU

Blue

CYA

Cyan

GRE

Green

MAG

Magenta

RED

Red

WHI

White

YEL

Yellow
