Terminal-Deﬁned Color Parameters
The following parameters determine the foreground and
background colors of menus, reports, ﬁelds, or ﬁeld text
deﬁned by the terminal type in the Terminal Deﬁnition

Report or in the User Registration Report. These parameters
free the screen designer from having to choose the color for
each element on the screens.

Parameter Description

BAC

Background color.

BOC

Border color.

TC

Title color.

MC

Menu color (the background
color inside the menu
border).

FC

Field color. Use this
parameter with the input
control parameters (AI, AO,
NO, CO, KO, and PR) to
select a color.

RC

Report color. This parameter
selects either the user's
conﬁgured report or result
color, based on the currently
displayed report (-0).

Text Handling Commands
Use text handling commands to display literal text on the
screen and to substitute characters.
DATA Command
Use the DATA command to display a speciﬁed number of
lines of text data, starting at a speciﬁed screen position.
The text data to display begins in column 1 of the report line
immediately following the DATA command. The text is
displayed exactly as it appears. The DATA command does
not recognize commands and syntax characters within the
text.

Notes:
The DATA command is valid only if screen commands
are being read from a report.
It is recommended that you use the S option on the SC
statement when using the DATA command.
See LIST Command for information about creating lists.
Format
DATA[,r,c,rsiz,csiz,pn,o,attr]

Field

Description

r,c

Cursor position at which to
start displaying the text. See

Syntax Rules for Screen
Commands for rules about
specifying screen coordinates.

rsiz

Number of rows of text to
display (paged text if you
specify the pn parameter). The
DATA command reads one line
of text from your command
report, starting with the line
immediately following the
DATA command. Default = 1.

csiz

Number of columns of text to
display (paged text if the pn
parameter is speciﬁed).
Default = report line length, or
screen width, whichever is
smaller.

pn

Page number. Indicates that
the following report lines
consist of page-formatted
data, and speciﬁes the default
initial page to be processed.
This page number may be
overridden. See DSPFORM
Action and PAGE Action.
Use paged data to handle
multiple menus within the
same report, or for textual

information too lengthy for a
single screen, such as
application documentation.

o

Options:
W - Interpret reserved words,
inline attributes, and literal
delimiters in DATA text. See
Using Inline Attributes.
L - List data. Precede the DATA
command with a LIST
command to specify the
source of the data. See LIST
Command.

attr

Attribute deﬁning the
characteristics of the data.
Default = no attributes
assigned. See Field Attribute
Parameters.

Example
This example is a DATA command followed by two lines of
literal text.
data,5,12,2,45,,,(co,mc,ts) .
To stop the printer, press F1.
To resume printing, press F2.
See #PAGE Identiﬁer for examples of a DATA page.

LIST Command
Use the LIST command with the DATA command to create a
list from a report and display the list on a form. The list can
be rolled forward and backward, using function keys. See
DATA Command.
Format
LIST,r[dc,line] fk,'RollFw',fk,'RollBw'

Field

Description

r[dc]

The report, drawer, and
cabinet containing the list. If
the drawer or cabinet or both
are omitted, the LIST
command assumes the
cabinet number and the
drawer letter of the report
containing the LIST command.
(This enables you to create
forms with maximum
portability.)

line

Report line number where the
list starts. Default = 1. If you
use the T option in the SC
statement, the run uses a
value supplied in the ﬂdtxt
ﬁeld of the SC statement. If
you do not specify the line

ﬁeld, listing begins with the
line following the heading (line
2 if there is no heading).

fk

Number of the function key to
press to roll forward.

'RollFw'

Text to be displayed on the
function key bar.

fk

Number of the function key to
press to roll backward.

'RollBw'

'RollBw' Text to be displayed
on the function key bar.

Examples
The following example illustrates the use of the LIST
command for passing information back to a run.
When a user presses Transmit, the run passes ﬁelds %#
(line number), %4 (ﬁeld 4, product type data) and %8 (ﬁeld
8, customer code data) back to the run, where they are
picked up by a CHG INPUT$ statement and redisplayed on
the screen by the AREA command.
@brk .
prep
fkey,0,,KEY,%#,%4,%8

fkey,10,Quit,^
fld,05,10,12,35,ba,(pr,boc)
fld,05,10,1,35,vc,(pr,tc),,''A LIST''
list,2b0,6 2,'RollFw',3,'RollBw'
data,06,12,10,31,,l,(ts,co,mc)
%2 %4 %3
end
@brk sc,-0,,,1 '' .
@1:chg input$ v1a12,v2a12,v3a12 .
dfld,,lp,(pr,yel/bla)
area,,2,1,,80
Record = ___ Product Type = ________ Cust Code =
__
@brk sc,-0,,,field$ 'tk' v1,v2,v3 .
@gto 1 .
The following example speciﬁes a report from which to take
a list of data, using the LIST command. The DATA command
sets up a screen on which to present the list, and assigns
attributes to the data. Data ﬁelds from report 110A0 are
presented in the following order: ﬁeld 2, ﬁeld 1, ﬁeld 3.
list,110a0 2,'RollFw',3,'RollBw' .
data,5,12,9,45,,l,(co,mc,ts) .
%2 %1 %3
#PAGE Identiﬁer
Use a page identiﬁer to mark the beginning (and end) of a
form page. (See DATA Command.) Format
#PAGE[,n]
where n is the optional page number.
Guidelines

Follow these guidelines when using the #PAGE identiﬁer:
The identiﬁer must begin in column 1 of the report and
the word PAGE must be in uppercase (#page does not
work).
Pages can be numbered or unnumbered. However, only
a numbered page can be used as the initial page. Once
the initial page is displayed, the PAGE action enables
access to any page. See FKEY Command.
Examples
Page data is structured as follows:

SC Format

Section Description

#PAGE,1

Page identiﬁer

Screen
commands

Pretext section

end

End of pretext section

Number of lines
speciﬁed by rsiz
on DATA
command

Page text section
(optional)

Screen
commands

Post-text section
(optional)

#PAGE,2

Page identiﬁer

The following examples illustrate DATA pages.
data, 10,20,5,40,1,w,(pr,mc) <-- Start of page 1
#PAGE,1
fkey,3
fkey,2,'RollFw', page,+
fkey,8,Help,dsphelp
end <-- End of pretext commands
This is the text for page one. <-- Page text (5
lines)
Press F2$ to roll forward.
fld,1,1,1,80,rv,(pr,fc),,'Page 1 of 10'
end <-- End of post-text commands
dspform,11e,1 <-- Context help for this page
#PAGE <-- Start of next page
data,,,0,,1 <-- Start of page 1
#PAGE,1
fkey,4,Return,formret
fkey,8,Help,dspform,215e,1,1,3
fld,12,24,6,55,afb,(pr,boc)
fld,12,24,1,55,vc,(pr,tc),,'Display a Report'
dfld,,u,(ts,co,fc);area,,13,25,4,53,,(pr,mc)

_ Display report (D)
_ Display available named reports (NAMES)
end <-- End of command section
help,1 18
Displays a form to enter the report to be
displayed
help,2
Uses the NAMES run to display a list of names
reports.
#PAGE,2 <-- Start of page 2
How the SC Statement Interprets Paged Data
The SC statement interprets paged data in the following
ways:
Processing of paged data always stops when the next
page identiﬁer is encountered.
If the DATA command speciﬁes a row or column size
(rsiz or csiz) of zero, the pages are assumed to consist
only of one command section (in eﬀect, the pretext
section).
Begin context-sensitive help (help for a form ﬁeld) for a
speciﬁc page after an END command and before the
next page identiﬁer.
FPAGE$ contains the current page number.
Example
In this example, the DATA command displays page 1 unless
the caller speciﬁes a diﬀerent page number. The pn ﬁeld in
the DATA command contains the value for the default page.

data,,,0,,1
#PAGE,1
fld,1,1,,63,,,,' MAPPER Online Documentation'
fld,1,64,,14,,,,'Screen 1 of 1'
(more screen commands)

#PAGE,2
Emphasis (EMP) Command
Use the Emphasis (EMP) command to highlight or
emphasize text. You can place emphasis anywhere on the
screen.
Format
EMP[,p,p,...,p]

Field

Description

p

Emphasis parameter or
parameters, as follows:
Blank - turns oﬀ emphasis
C or | - separates columns.
U or _ - underscores.
- strikes through.

Example
This example underscores the text enclosed in the literal
delimiters.
emp,_; 'This text is underscored'
emp; 'This text has no emphasis'
Emphasis characters are normally displayed as white text
on a black background. Foreground and background colors
are ignored.
Special Characters (TIC, SOE, LB, RB)

Command

Description

TIC[,c | ,n]

Place n current literal
delimiter characters at the
current cursor position.
Default = 1. Redeﬁnes the
literal delimiter character,
which is initially the
apostrophe, to a special
character supplied by c. Do
not use a tilde (~) as a
replacement character.
The TIC command is useful
when apostrophes are used
in text. For example:
@sc '' 'tic,"; "It is
a sunny day."'

tic,"; "It's a sunny
day."
When the contents of the
text to be output is not
known, use the DATA
command instead of
redeﬁning the literal
delimiter.

SOE[,c | ,n]

Place a start-of-entry (SOE)
character at the current
cursor position. The value
supplied for n is the
number of SOE characters
to display. Default = 1. The
value supplied for c is the
special character that you
are using to redeﬁne the
SOE character. The
following example shows &;
as the special character to
represent the SOE
character.
soe,&;;'Enter new
value &;'

LB[,c | ,n]

Place a left blink character
at the current cursor
position. The value
supplied for n is the
number of left blink
characters to display.

Default = 1. The value
supplied for c is the special
character you are using to
redeﬁne the left blink
character. See the RB
command in this table for
an example.

RB[,c | ,n]

Places n right blink
characters at the current
cursor position. Default =
1. The value supplied for c
is the special character you
are using to represent the
right blink. For example:
lb,*;rb,%;msg,1,e,,'*
Operators are <,>,= %'
Produces the output:
< Operators are <,>,=
>

Cursor Control Commands (HC, PC, CR, TAB)
Use cursor control commands to control the position of the
cursor on the screen.

Command

Description

HC

Home Cursor. Moves the

cursor to the home
position.

PC,r,c

Position Cursor. Moves the
cursor to the speciﬁed row
(r) and column (c) position.
When specifying relative
cursor coordinates, the
cursor wraps around the
edges of the screen as if
you were moving the cursor
manually.

CR[,n]

Cursor Return. Moves the
cursor to column 1 of the
next screen line. Repeat n
times. Default = 1.

TAB[,n]

Tab Cursor. Tabs the cursor
forward n positions. Default
= 1. If n is negative, the
cursor tabs backward. After
using the TAB command,
use the HC, PC, FLD, or
MSG command to
reestablish the cursor
location before using any
command that references
the current cursor position.
See Message (MSG)
Command for more
information.

Setup Commands (END, FKEY, BEEP, CP, MODE, OPTS)
Use setup commands to perform a variety of tasks, such as
sounding a beep at the terminal, altering terminal setup,
controlling graphic display modes, overriding current
options, and mapping function keys. (Some of the setup
commands listed in parentheses in the heading, and some
of the tasks just described, may not apply to all systems.)

Command

Description

END

Marks the end of a form
section or page section.

FKEY

Speciﬁes an action to be
performed at the next input
if a particular function key
is pressed. See FKEY
Command.

BEEP

Causes the terminal beeper
to sound once. Use it to
signal the terminal user
that an error or other
special condition has
occurred. After your run
executes the BEEP
command, it places the
cursor at the home position.

You can execute only one
BEEP command for each SC
statement.
Not all terminals support
this functionality.

CP[,p]

Alters the terminal setup
(control page). The CP
command accesses the
terminal setup and inserts
the text string p. Any
special sequence (control
string introducer) required
is inserted automatically.

Note: Be careful when
altering the terminal setup;
it may aﬀect the ability of
the system to communicate
with the terminal. See your
terminal documentation
before using this command.

MODE[,p]

Selects the display mode of
a graphics terminal.
(Nongraphics terminals
ignore MODE command.)
The valid entries for the p
ﬁeld are as follows: A Alpha only display.
G - Graphics only display.

M - Mixed mode display
(graphics and alpha). This
mode is terminal
dependent.

OPTS,[+]o | Overrides a subset of SC
[-]o
statement options that may
be in eﬀect from the calling
run. It enables the screen
command report to specify
a subset of the SC
statement options
independent of the calling
run.
Allowable options are: B, C,
H, K, L, M, S, U. See Screen
Control Options.
If the ﬁrst option character
is a plus (+), the options
are added to the caller's
options; a minus (-)
removes the speciﬁed
options.
This example holds line
control and forces all
spaces to be output. All
other options are
deselected.
opts,ls

FKEY Command
Use the FKEY command to specify an action to be performed
at the next input if a particular function key is pressed. This
is called function key mapping. You can map actions to
function keys F1 through F10 as well as Transmit. If one or
more keys are mapped, the remaining unmapped function
keys are disabled.
Place each FKEY command on a line by itself, beginning in
the ﬁrst column of the report.
The FKEY command also displays a function key bar at the
bottom of the screen, showing the current mapping of
function keys F1 through F10. The current mapping remains
in eﬀect until the next output (see the K option under
Screen Control Options).
Format
FKEY,n,title,action
When using forms with the PAGE action, use the following
format to disable a function key that has key number n.
FKEY,n

Field

Description

n

Function key number (0 =
Transmit).

title

Text to display with the

function key number on the
function key bar. The title
must be six characters or
fewer. If the title contains
spaces, enclose it in
apostrophes. For example:
fkey,8,'Ex hlp',^

action

The action to take when the
function key is pressed. The
action can be any valid
command (control line) input
that a user might enter, such
as a ^ to display the active
screen or an X to sign oﬀ the
software. See Special FKEY
Actions for other possible
actions.

Special FKEY Actions
Within the Function Key (FKEY) command, you can specify
the following FKEY actions. For information, see FKEY
Command.
Display Help (DSPHELP) action (use with the HELP
identiﬁer)
Display Form (DSPFORM) action
Return to Previous Form (FORMRET) action
PAGE action

SELECT action
KEY action
SOE action
You can also use information taken dynamically from the
screen and from LIST data, using $n, %n, $#, and %#. See
Pass Screen and List Values.
DSPHELP Action
Use the DSPHELP action to display context-sensitive help
text (help for a form ﬁeld). The context-sensitive help text
must be in the same report as the FKEY command using the
DSPHELP action and is preceded by the HELP identiﬁer. See
HELP Identiﬁer for more information.
Format
DSPHELP[,o]

Field

Description

o

Option:
1 - Display help for the ﬁeld
where the cursor is currently
positioned (default).

Example

This example displays context-sensitive help for the ﬁeld
where the cursor is positioned when F8 is pressed.
fkey,8,Help,dsphelp
HELP Identiﬁer
Use the HELP identiﬁer to identify help text in your screen
command report and to determine where on the screen to
place the context-sensitive help.
Format
HELP,fld,...fld row ...|

Field

Description

ﬂd,...ﬂd

The ﬁeld number for which
the following context-sensitive
help refers.

row

The row (screen line) on which
to start the help text display.

|

Marker to specify the width of
the HELP window.

Note: The row and | ﬁelds are for the ﬁrst HELP identiﬁer
only.
Example

In this example, the END command separates the
commands used to build the screen from the help text. The
optional DSPFORM speciﬁes another form to display if the
user presses Help while the context-sensitive help (help for
a form ﬁeld) is already displayed, or if no ﬁeld help is
available.
end
dspform,49h,1 help,1 15 |
First Field
This is where the description for the first field
on the
screen is placed. It is freeform text.
help,2
Second Field
This is the text for the second field on the
screen.
help
DSPFORM Action
Use the DSPFORM action to access a report containing
screen commands (also referred to as a form) when a
function key is pressed. Using the DSPFORM action creates a
stack of forms built up in the order in which they are
displayed. The stack grows this way until it is cleared or
rearranged, using the DSPFORM or FORMRET FKEY actions.
The form at the top of the stack is the currently displayed
form.
The forms return stack identiﬁes the form the software
should return to if a FORMRET action is used.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

A stack holds a maximum of 10 forms.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
A stack holds a maximum of 20 forms.
Ü Ü 2200
A form on the stack can include a saved context so that the
form can be returned to the top of the stack or recalled at
any time while a function is still active. The forms that were
stacked on top of it are discarded.
Format
DSPFORM,r[dc],pn,tabp,stack,o [fldtxt]

Field

Description

r[dc]

The report, drawer, and
cabinet containing the form. If
the drawer or cabinet or both
are omitted, DSPFORM action
assumes the cabinet number
and the drawer letter of the
report containing the
DSPFORM action. (This
enables forms to be created
for maximum portability.) The
system begins reading
commands on line 4 of the
report you specify. (Be sure to

take this into account when
entering commands in the
output area.)

pn

The form page number to
display. This is valid only if the
report speciﬁed by rdc uses
paged data. See DATA
Command.

tabp

The tab position n at which to
place the cursor, where n is
the number of tab positions
forward from the home
position and -n is the number
of tab positions backward
from the home position.
Maximum = 100 tab positions
forward or backward. Default
= no positioning.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

stack

Return stack action. Use one
of these values to specify the
action for the return stack:
0 - Puts this form on the top of
the stack (default).

1 - Clears the stack and puts
this form on the bottom.
2 - Marks the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
3 - Saves the context of the
current form so a return
restores the screen as it
currently exists. This is useful
for Help displays.
4 - Does not put an entry on
the return stack for this form.
5 - Overwrites the current
entry on the return stack with
this form.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

stack

Return stack action. Use one
of these values to specify the
action for the return stack:
0 - Puts this form on the top of
the stack (default).
1 - Clears the stack and puts
this form on the bottom.

2 - Marks the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
3 - Saves the current stack
position and then puts this
form on the top of the stack.
The screen itself is not saved.
4 - Does not put an entry on
the return stack for this form.
5 - Overwrites the current
entry on the return stack with
this form.

Ü Ü 2200

o

Deﬁne Field (DFLD) Command.

values to menu ﬁelds.

ﬂdtxt

the form when the T option is
present. See Field (FLD)
Command, Deﬁne Field (DFLD)
Command, and AREA

Command. This ﬁeld is a list of
items, separated by commas.
The ﬁrst item is placed in the
ﬁrst ﬁeld, the second item in
the second ﬁeld, and so on.

Example
This example enables the user to display page 4 of the form
in 202e by pressing Readme (the F6 function key).
fkey,6,Readme,dspform,202e,4
FORMRET Action
Use the FORMRET action to return to a previous form, as
determined by the forms return stack. See DSPFORM Action
for more information on stacking.
Format
FORMRET,stack,o [fldtxt]

Field

Description

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

stack

A value that acts on the
display. Use one of these
values to specify how to act
on the display: 0 - Returns to

the previous entry on the
return stack.
1 - Returns to the start of the
stack and clears the stack.
2 - Returns to a mark
previously set by a DSPFORM
action with a stack value of 2
(see DSPFORM Action).
3 - Returns to a saved context
set by a DSPFORM action with
a stack value of 3. Note that
only one saved context
applies for a user at any time.
4 - Returns to the top entry on
the stack. This is used if the
current command does not
put anything on the stack.
5 - If there is a report on
display, repaints the report
before redisplaying the form.
This is used to clean up a
screen that has several menus
on it.
6 - If there is a report on
display, either repaints it or
does a formret,0.
7 - If there is a report on
display, either repaints it or
does a formret,4.

PAGE Action
Use the PAGE action to page forward or backward in a form,
after the initial page of a form is displayed.
Format
PAGE,p
where p uses a plus (+) to move forward to the next page, a
minus (-) to move back to the previous page, or a number to
go to a particular page. Default = +.
Guidelines
SC statement options in eﬀect at the start of the initial
page remain in eﬀect.
Current function key mapping is retained.
Individual function keys can be mapped or remapped.
2200 Þ Þ
Screen commands preceding the ﬁrst #PAGE identiﬁer
in the report are processed prior to those in the
destination page.
Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Only the screen commands in the destination page are
processed.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Example
In this example, pressing RollFw advances the user to the
next page of paged data.
fkey,2,RollFw,page,+
SELECT Action
Use the SELECT action to select one of a group of actions or
commands, based on the ﬁeld that the cursor is in at the
time the key is pressed.
Format
SELECT

Note: The SELECT command must be in uppercase letters
and the ﬁeld number must not start in column 1.
Example
This example displays form 11e if the cursor is in the ﬁrst
ﬁeld when the user presses Transmit, and returns control to
the run if the cursor is in the second ﬁeld when the user
presses Transmit. FKEY$ contains zero and FIELD$ contains
2.
fkey,0,,SELECT
1,dspform,11e,1,1,0
2,key
KEY Action

Use the KEY action to enable the function key to be passed
directly to a run. This action enables function keys to be
interpreted by the run instead of by the controlling form.
Format
KEY[,text]
where text is the data item or items to be input to the run. If
multiple items are speciﬁed, separate them with commas.
The run captures them with INPUT$.
Reserved Word
FKEY$ can be used by the run to determine which key was
pressed.
Example
This example passes F1 to the run.
fkey,1,'Cont.',key,$1
SOE Action
Use the SOE action to output an SOE character at the
current cursor position.
Format
SOE
Example
In the following example, pressing F3 places an SOE
character at the current cursor position.
fkey,3,SOE,soe

Pass Screen and List Values
You can use unprotected screen ﬁelds and ﬁelds from a
selected LIST data record in FKEY actions, as described in
the following table:

Action

Description

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

$n

Insert the value of screen ﬁeld
n. Field numbering begins with
1 (the ﬁrst ﬁeld on the
screen). If you specify $0, the
system inserts all unprotected
screen ﬁelds as comma
separated items.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

%n

Insert the value of LIST data
ﬁeld n. Specify any ﬁeld in the
LIST report, whether or not it
was displayed to the user. The
value is null if the cursor was
not on a LIST item at the time
the key was pressed.

$#

Number of the ﬁeld that the
cursor was in at the time the
key was pressed. Equivalent
to FIELD$.

%#

Report line number of the
currently selected list record.
Valid only if the user had the
cursor on a list item at the
time the key was pressed. See
LIST Command and DATA
Command.

Examples
This example uses the ﬁeld number as the page for the
DSPFORM command:
dspform,253e6,$#,1
This example starts the run called accts. The items 'Modify',
the value of screen ﬁeld 1, and LIST report ﬁelds 3 and 2 are
passed as input to the run.
fkey,6,'Modify',accts,modify,$1,%3,%2

Screen Editing Commands
Use the screen editing commands described in the following
table to perform on-screen text editing operations.

Action

Description

PD

Protect Display. Protects the
display from the cursor
position to the end of the
display. Using the PD
command does not alter the
text and attributes that you
are planning to display. You
can also use the PD command
to prepare the screen for
overlaying.

CS

Clear Screen. Erases the
entire display and moves the
cursor to the home position.

ED

Erase Display. Erases the
display from the current
cursor position to the end of
the screen.

EUD

Erase Unprotected Display.
Erases the unprotected
portion of the display from the

current cursor position to the
end of the screen. Attributes
and protected ﬁelds are not
erased.

EEL

Erase to End of Line. Erases
from the current position to
the end of the line or ﬁeld,
whichever occurs ﬁrst.
Attributes and protected ﬁelds
are not erased.

DIL[,n]

Delete in Line. Deletes the
unprotected character under
the cursor, shifting
succeeding characters, up to
the end of the ﬁeld or line
(whichever occurs ﬁrst), to the
left. Repeat n times. Default =
1.

DID[,n]

Delete in Display. Deletes the
unprotected character under
the cursor, shifting
succeeding characters, up to
the end of the ﬁeld or display
(whichever occurs ﬁrst), to the
left. Repeat n times. Default =
1.

IIL[,n]

Insert in Line. Inserts a space

at the current cursor position,
shifting the character under
the cursor and all succeeding
characters up to the end of
the ﬁeld or line (whichever
occurs ﬁrst), to the right.
Repeat n times. Default = 1.

IID[,n]

Insert in Display. Inserts a
space at the current cursor
position, shifting the character
under the cursor and all
succeeding characters up to
the end of the ﬁeld or display
(whichever occurs ﬁrst), to the
right. Repeat n times. Default
= 1.

DL[,n]

Delete Line. Deletes the line
on which the cursor is located,
moving all remaining lines up.
Repeat n times. Default = 1.

IL[,n]

Insert Line. Inserts a blank
line at the cursor location,
moving the line on which the
cursor is located and all
remaining lines down. Repeat
n times. Default = 1.

DUP[,n]

Duplicate Line. Duplicates the

line the cursor is on onto the
line directly below it. The
cursor is positioned on the
duplicated line. Repeat n
times. Default = 1.

Screen Printing Commands (PRT, PRF, LF, FF)
Use the screen printing commands described in the
following table to control output to a printer.

Command Description

PRT

Sends protected and
unprotected data to the
attached print device. It
sends all data from the home
position up to and including
the character under the
cursor. The station number
of the device must exist in
the conﬁguration report.

PRF

Sends unprotected data to
the attached print device
(such as COP). The PRF
command is the same as the
PRT command, but it sends
only unprotected data.

LF[,n]

Places n line feed characters
at the current cursor
position. Default = 1. Use
this command with the PRT
and PRF screen commands.

FF

Places a form feed character
at the current cursor
position. Use this command
with the PRT and PRF screen
commands.

SCAT (Scatter Chart)
Use the Scatter Chart (SCAT) run to create a scatter chart. A
scatter chart depicts variable points graphed according to
numeric x and y scales. A scatter chart is displayed on the
screen or drawn on a plotter.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Scatter Chart Control Line Format
SCAT[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

SCGEN (Screen Control Generator)
Use the Screen Control Generator (SCGEN) command to
create input screens and menus, using a menu-driven
interface. With the Screen Control Generator command, you
design screens using a visual layout that resembles the
actual output screen. You can also include ﬁelds in your
screens that are already deﬁned in the data dictionary. The
Screen Control Generator command automatically creates
the Screen Control (SC) code for you to use in your run.
When the screen design is ﬁnal and you press Build, the
screen control code is created and placed into a report in
drawer A of cabinet 0. Your run can then use the SC
statement to access that report.

Screen Control Generator Control Line Format
SCGEN

To Create Screen Text
Place text anywhere on the scratch pad, except directly over
existing ﬁelds or on frame borders. Move the cursor to the
point where you want the text to begin and start typing.
The command automatically updates text on the screen
whenever you transmit or select a new option.
When typing your text, do not use underscores ( _ ),
quotation marks ("), or vertical bars (|). Using these
characters can cause translation problems.
You can use reserved words in the screen text. If you want
the contents of a reserved word to be displayed, type it
normally (for example, 'TIME$') on the screen.

To Change the Display to Black on White
If you want to override the color attributes of menus
generated by the SCGEN command, you can set the
BlackOnWhite parameter in the MPC.INI ﬁle to true (1).
See the Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
for more information on the MPC.INI ﬁle.

@SCH (Schedule Run Statements)
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the Schedule Run Statements (SCH) statement to
establish a time for the execution of a speciﬁed statement.
The SCH statement can queue any of the following
statements for execution at a later time:
Auxiliary (AUX)

Send Report (SEN)

Background Run Send Report to User
(BR)
(SNU)
Print (PRT)

Start (STR)

Remote Run
(RRN)
The SCH statement is useful for starting the operations
performed by the statements mentioned above at a certain
date and time. The operation is queued on the system but
does not start until the date and time speciﬁed.
The SCH statement validates the date and time ﬁelds before
you schedule the run.

Schedule Run Statements Statement Format
@SCH[,date,time] rst .

Field

Description

date

Date to schedule the
queuing function in
YYMMDD (DATE1$) format.
Default = current date.

time

Time of day to schedule the
queuing function in
HHMMSS format. Default =
current time.

rst

Statement to queue: AUX,
BR, PRT, RRN, SEN, SNU,
STR.

Schedule Run Statements Guideline
Scheduled runs do not always start at the exact time they
are scheduled to begin. A scheduled run begins when the
system clock is greater than or equal to the time of the
scheduled run, plus the elapsed time in which the queues
are searched looking for runs to be executed. For more
information on scheduling background runs, see your
administrator.

Schedule Run Statements Examples
This example schedules the current -0 result to be printed
on the current date at 10:00 p.m.: @sch,,220000 prt,-0 .
This example schedules the background run named STATUS,
with input data jun and monthly, for execution at 7:00 p.m.
on November 3, 1996: @sch,961103,190000 br
status,jun,monthly .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

SCHEDULE
Use the Schedule script to set up a script for automatic
execution at scheduled times. You can schedule a script to
execute only once, or daily, weekly, monthly, periodically
throughout the day, or when the system is initialized.
The script must already be registered as a background
script to schedule it using the Schedule script. The Schedule
script is also used to display or delete the scripts that are
scheduled for automatic script execution.

Schedule Control Line Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
SCHEDULE [{script,time,date,stnum,restart,inp1, …
,inp40 | D | H}]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
SCHEDULE [{script,time,date,stnum,restart,inp1, …
,inp35 | D}]
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

script

Name of the script to
schedule.

time

Time you want the script to
start (in format HHMM).

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

date

Date you want the script to
start (in format YYYYMMDD).

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

date

Date you want the script to
start (in format YYMMDD or
YYYYMMDD).

Ü Ü 2200

stnum

Station number that receives
the script completion
message.

restart

Restart the script if
incomplete when Business
Information Server is taken
oﬀ-line? Y or N. Default = N.

inp1, ...
,inpn

The input parameter of the
script being scheduled. On the
OS 2200 platform, Schedule
supports up to 35 parameters.
On other platforms, Schedule
support up to 40 parameters.
Each input parameter can
contain up to 18 characters.

D

Request for displaying or
deleting a script.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

H

Change the maximum number
of lines in the scheduled
scripts report. This option is
used with the fast-access
command format, and is
available to the administrator
only.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

U

Ü Ü 2200

Request for modifying the
credentials for a script.

Schedule Outcome
The script schedules the background script to be executed
on the days and times you request, or displays an
information screen about the currently scheduled script you
speciﬁed, enabling you to remove it from the schedule.
2200 Þ Þ
Administrators can use SCHEDULE (or link to SCHEDULEX) to
add or delete the scheduled registration for any user-id.
On systems conﬁgured to use OS 2200 user-ids, the
SCHEDULE script may be used to modify the user-id,
department, and password for scripts already scheduled for
execution.
See Linking to the SCHEDULE Script Example for more
information.
Ü Ü 2200

@SCN (Screen Size)
Use the Screen Size (SCN) statement to obtain the size of
the user’s screen and main window. Screen Size returns the
vertical and horizontal size of the screen and main window
both in positioning units and row column units.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(14-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.

Screen Size Statement Format
@SCN[,lab]
[pscnv,pscnh,pwinv,pwinh,cscnv,cscnh,cwinv,cwinh]
.

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the SCN
statement fails within the
workstation client.

pscnv

Variable to capture the inside
vertical size of the screen
deﬁned in positioning units.

pscnh

Variable to capture the inside
horizontal size of the screen
deﬁned in positioning units.

pwinv

Variable to capture the inside
vertical size of the main
window deﬁned in positioning
units.

pwinh

Variable to capture the inside
horizontal size of the main

window deﬁned in positioning
units.

cscnv

Variable to capture the inside
vertical size of the screen
deﬁned in row column units.

cscnh

Variable to capture the inside
horizontal size of the screen
deﬁned in row column units.

cwinv

Variable to capture the inside
vertical size of the main
window deﬁned in row column
units.

Note: This variable is similar
to the SCNV$ reserved word
except that SCNV$ includes
the rows that are hidden by
the scroll bars and does not
include the function key area
of the main window.

cwinh

Variable to capture the inside
horizontal size of the main
window deﬁned in row column
units.

Note: This variable is similar
to the SCNH$ reserved word

except that SCNH$ includes
the columns that are hidden
by the scroll bars.

Screen Size Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
The inside size of the screen or main window is the
client displayed area. This size does not include title
bars, task bars, scroll bars, borders, or frames. It also
does not include the area that is hidden except when
using the scroll bars.
When the screen or main window is created in
positioning units but the size is requested in row column
units, the size is either rounded up or down (depending
on the pixel size) to a whole number.

Screen Size Reserved Word
If the SCN statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement receives an error from the workstation client, the
run continues at the label. The STAT1$ reserved word
contains an error status. See STAT1$ Values for possible
values returned in the STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: If a run syntax error is detected, the run will error; it
does not continue at the speciﬁed label.

Screen Size Examples
This example uses the SCN statement to get the screen and
main window sizes in positioning units.
@scn,0101 <pscnv>i5,<pscnh>i5,<pwinv>i5,<pwinh>i5
.
This example uses the SCN statement to get the screen and
main window sizes in row column units.
@scn,0101 ,,,,<cscnv>i3,<cscnh>i3,<cwinv>i3,
<cwinh>i3 .

SEND and @SEN (Send Report)
Use the Send Report (SEND) function or SEN statement to
send an entire report from one station to another station.
You can request that the system notify you when the
receiver accepts the message. The receiver can also send a
message back to you.
The receiving station can be on your system or at another
site.
For the Send Report manual function, the report you want to
send must be on display.

Send Report Control Line Format
SEND [sn,sl,ack?]

Field

Description

sn

Station number of the
receiving station.

sl

Site letter of the receiving
station. (For a list of sites,
press Remote from the active
screen.) Default = local site.

ack?

Request acknowledgment
when the message is
received? Y or N. Default = N.

Send Report Statement Format
@SEN,c,d,r,sn[,sl,ack?,lab] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to send. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

sn

Station number of the
receiving station.

sl

Site letter of the receiving
station. (For a list of sites,
press Remote from the active
screen.) Default = local site.

ack?

Request acknowledgment
when the message is
received? Y or N. Default = N.

lab

Label to go to if the station is
not conﬁgured.

Send Report Outcome
When you execute the Send Report command, the
command sends the report as a result to the speciﬁed
station. When using the Send Report manual function, the
command redisplays the report on the sender's screen,
along with a message indicating that the report was queued
to the receiving station.
When the receiver requests to duplicate the message
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the message is from the same system, the system
creates a report in the cabinet and drawer of the
original report being sent.
If the message is from a remote system, the message
resides in cabinet 0, drawer I. If you duplicate the
message, the duplicate report resides in cabinet 0,
drawer I.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If the message is from the same system, the system
creates a report in the speciﬁed drawer of the cabinet
the user is currently in. (Default is the drawer letter of
the sent report).
If the message is from a remote system, and has more
than 80 characters per line, the message resides in
cabinet 0, drawer I. If the message has 80 characters or
less, it resides in drawer A. If you duplicate the
message, the duplicate report resides in the speciﬁed
drawer of the cabinet the user is currently in. (The

default drawer is I if the result is more than 80
characters, or drawer A if the result is 80 characters or
less).
Ü Ü 2200
If the number of characters in the received message is
greater than the maximum line length, the report lines
are truncated to the maximum line length.

Send Report Examples
This Send Report example sends report 1A0 to station
number 5. With report 1A0 on display, enter send 5
Equivalent Statement
@sen,0,a,1,5 .
This SEN example sends result -2 to station number 22 and
returns an acknowledgment after the message is accepted
(goes to label 99 if the station does not exist):
@sen,-2,22,,y,099 .

@SFC (Set Format Characters)
Use the Set Format Characters (SFC) statement to set a
customized report format for a following output display,
such as that created by a Display Report (DSP) or Output
Mask (OUM) statement. You can also select columns to print
the next time you call the Auxiliary (AUX) or Print (PRT)
statements.

Note: The SFC statement sets up the format for your
current -0 result or the report you specify in the c,d,r ﬁeld
(which then becomes the current result).

Set Format Characters Statement Format
@SFC[,c,d,r] vld .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report on which to set the
format. Default = -0. If you
specify a report on the
statement, it becomes the
current -0 result.

vld

Characters identifying the
columns to include in the
format. You can specify the
columns using a literal,
constant, variable, or any
combination of these.

Set Format Characters Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the SFC statement:
You can restore a format previously saved with a Load
Format Characters (LFC) statement or build your own
customized format by using the SFC statement.
The value in the vld ﬁeld identiﬁes which columns to
include in the format. Use any characters other than
spaces to specify the columns to include; use spaces to
specify the columns to exclude from the display.
Process the newly formatted -0 result before creating
another -0 result.
Note that when you create a new -0 result in a diﬀerent
cabinet and drawer, the system clears the format set by
the SFC statement. A new -0 result in the same cabinet
and drawer retains the format, just as it does in manual
operations.

Set Format Characters Examples
This example indicates that columns 1 through 10 and 16
through 20 are to be displayed in the following report
display.
@sfc 'xxxxxxxxxx xxxxx' .
@dsp,-0 .
This example uses an LFC statement to capture the format
of the current result. It then processes the result and
redisplays it, using the SFC statement to specify the
previous format.
@lfc <format>s80 .
.
. (other processing)
. @sfc <format> .
@dsp,-0 .

@SGGET (Session Global Report Get)
Use @SGGET (Session Global Report Get) run statement to
retrieve a Session Global Report that was saved through the
SGPUT run statement.

Format
@SGGET,name,opts[,lab]

Outcome
If the Session Global Report (name) exists, it will be returned
as your current -0 report and 'STAT1$' will be set to zero. If
the report can not be returned, 'STAT1$' will be set nonzero
and the script will go to the label (lab) if a label is speciﬁed.
If no label is speciﬁed it will continue at the next run
statement.

Reserved Words
The reserved words 'STAT1$' through 'STAT3$' are loaded
with the status of the SGGET operation.

'STAT1$'

=0 if
successful
and the
report is
returned as
your current
-0. If the D
option was
speciﬁed, the
Session
Global report
has been
deleted and
your -0 has
not been
changed.
=1 if the
report name
could not be
found.
=2 if the
report table is
full. (SGPUT
only) =3 if
the script is
not registered
for read-

access to the
drawer of this
report. No -0
report is
created and
the Session
Global report
remains in
the table.

'STAT2$'

The number
of active
reports in the
Session
Global Report
Table after
this command
completes.
This is the
same value
returned in
SGRACT$.

'STAT3$'

The system
error
message
number
corresponding
to the
'STAT1$'
value. (You
can use LSM

to translate
the number
into error
message
text)

Also see reserved words 'SGRACT$' and 'SGRMAX$'.

previously saved through the SGPUT statement.
@sgget,'my_report1' .

report
name.

@SGPUT (Session Global Report Put)
Use @ SGPUT (Session Global Report Put) run statement to
save a copy of a report to the Session Global Report Table.

Format
@SGPUT,c,d,r,name[,lab]

Outcome
A temporary copy (RSL) of the report is placed into the
Session Global Report Table and is identiﬁed by the report
name (name). 'STAT1$' contains the status of the operation.
If the name already exists in the table, it is replaced with
the new report and the old report is discarded. If any errors
occur, the script will go to the label (lab) if a label is
speciﬁed. If no label is speciﬁed, it will continue at the next
run statement.

Reserved Words
The reserved words 'STAT1$' through 'STAT3$' are loaded
with the status of the SGPUT operation.

'STAT1$'

=0 if
successful
and a copy of
the report
was placed in
the Session
Global Report
Table. Your
current -0
report does
not change.
=1 if the
report name
could not be
found (Delete
only).
=2 if the
report table is
full.
=3 if the
script is not
registered for
read-access
to the drawer
of this report
(SGGET only).

'STAT2$'

The number
of active
reports in the
Session
Global Report
Table after
this command
completes.
This is the
same value
returned in
SGRACT$.

'STAT3$'

The system
error
message
number
corresponding
to the
'STAT1$'
value. (You
can use LSM
to translate
the number
into error
message
text).

Also see reserved words 'SGRACT$' and 'SGRMAX$'.

@SHW (Show Control)
Use the Show Control (SHW) statement to temporarily
redisplay an existing control or form. A control can be: BTN
(Button), CBX (Combo Box), EDT (Edit Box), LST (List Box),
PIC (Picture), TXT (Text Box), or WIN (Window). This
command should not be used on DDE conversation handles.
See also @CLS (Close Control) and @HID (Hide Control).
The SHW statement temporarily displays the speciﬁed
control.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Show Control Statement Format
@SHW[,vwh,...,vwh] .
where vwh is the variable that contains the handle of the
control to display. If the user displays a window containing
other controls, the statement displays all controls. Default =
all controls attached to the main window. Maximum = 80
entries.

Show Control Outcome
The SHW statement redisplays the speciﬁed controls or
forms.

Show Control Example
This example displays the controls whose handles reside in
variables <toolist> and <gettool>.
@shw,<toolist>,<gettool> .

does not exist.)

Station number of the printer.

SIGN
Use the Sign run to create signs with large-size text.
To display signs on your monitor, it must be conﬁgured for
color.
A sign is displayed on the screen or drawn on a plotter.
To display a sign that was already created, display the sign
report and enter sign on the control line.

Sign Control Line Format
SIGN[,report]
where report is the sign report in the current cabinet.

Sign Colors
Color

Pen
Stall

Aqua

8

Black

1

2

2

1

Green

4

3

6

Pink

Red

3

Yellow

To plot the chart, place the indicated pen color in the
numbered pen stall. On a four-pen plotter, you can specify
only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Sign Report Fields
Field

Description

color

Color of the screen. Default =
black. See Sign Colors.

Default = no border. See Sign
Colors.

chars

Character height and width
equal? Yes or No. Default =
Yes. If no, the character height

from .5 to 15.75. Default =

Height of the sign, in inches,
from .5 to 12. Default = 8.5.

Spacing between the lines of
text, in inches. Default = .5.

O

Line option. Default = start
new line.
- is continue the previous line.
The continuation is placed on
the same line as the previous
line.
M is add spaces at the
beginning of the next line. For
this option, type the number
of spaces in the CF ﬁeld. The
command ignores remaining
ﬁelds on this line following
this number.

Font number (1 - 30). Default
= 1 or previously speciﬁed
font. If the selected font
causes text to exceed
boundaries or cause errors,
place tab codes on both sides
of the text string.

Underline characters? Y or N.
Default = N.

Size

Font size ratio. Use positive
numbers only. For example,
typing 1 for the ﬁrst line and
1.5 for the second makes the

second line 1-1/2 times taller
than the ﬁrst.

Color

Color of the text. Default =
white. See Sign Colors.

N. To center a continuation
line, use the M option in the O
ﬁeld.

Text. The command ignores
leading and trailing spaces. To
have leading or trailing
spaces, use tab codes.

@SIZ (Control Size)
Use the Control Size (SIZ) statement to obtain the size of a
control. Control Size returns the vertical and horizontal size
of a control speciﬁed in the statement. You can apply
Control Size to all Graphical User Interface (GUI) controls
(BTN, CBX, EDT, LST, PIC, TXT, and WIN).
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.

Control Size Statement Format
@SIZ,vwh[,lab]
[vsiz,hsiz,nvsiz,nhsiz,ovsiz,ohsiz] .

Field

Description

vwh

The handle of the control.

lab

The run continues processing
at the label number if the SIZ
statement fails within the
workstation client; run syntax
errors do not take the label.

vsiz

A variable that contains the
inside vertical size of the
control, in the units deﬁned for
the control.

hsiz

A variable that contains the
inside horizontal size of the
control, in the units deﬁned for
the control.

nvsiz

A variable that contains the
inside vertical size of the

control, in the units not
deﬁned for the control.

nhsiz

A variable that contains the
inside horizontal size of the
control, in the units not
deﬁned for the control.

ovsiz

A variable that contains the
outsize vertical size of the
object, in positioning units.

ohsiz

A variable that contains the
outside horizontal size of the
object, in positioning units.

Notes:
When the control is deﬁned using the Y option, SIZ
provides the dimensions ﬁrst in positioning units, then in
row and column units. The Y option indicates the control
placement is deﬁned in positioning units or row and
column units, and the sizing of the control is deﬁned in
row and columns. This applies to the following controls:
CBX, EDT, LST, and TXT.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(13-1) = 1 if
available for nvsiz, nhsiz, ovsiz, and ohsiz ﬁelds.

Control Size Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
The inside size is the control’s client area. This size does
not include the title bar or the scroll bar.
The outside size is the entire control size. This size
includes the title bar and the scroll bar.
When a control is deﬁned with row and column units and
the text font is a proportional font, the size is based
upon the average character size. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts.
When a control is deﬁned with row and column units, a
whole number is always returned. However, when a
control is deﬁned to be the maximum size (vsiz = 0 and
hsiz = 0) and a border is deﬁned, the control size can be
a fraction of a row or column. In this case, the vertical
and horizontal size are rounded down.

Control Size Reserved Word
If the SIZ statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement receives an error from the workstation client, the
run continues at the label. The STAT1$ reserved word
contains an error status. See STAT1$ Values for possible
values returned in the STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: If a run syntax error is detected, the run will error; it
does not continue at the speciﬁed label.

Control Size Examples
This example uses the WIN statement to create a window.
This window is a child window and is deﬁned to use
positioning units. The window starts at the vertical
positioning unit (800) and the horizontal positioning unit
(400), and extends to the size of the user’s main window.
See @WIN (Deﬁne Window Display) for more information on
the WIN statement.
The SIZ statement returns the size of the child window in
positioning units, in vsiz and hsiz variables. The SIZ
statement also returns the size of the window in row and
columns, in nvsiz and nhsiz variables.
@win,,0800,0400,0,0,,cw 'Child Window' <wind>i6 .
@siz,<wind> <vsiz>i6,<hsiz>i6,<nvsiz>i6,<nhsiz>i6
.
This example uses the WIN statement to create a window.
This window is an overlay window and is deﬁned to use row
and column units. The window starts at row 2 and column 3,
and extends to the size of the user’s main window. See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window Display) for more information on the
WIN statement.
The SIZ statement returns the size of the child window in
row and columns, in vsiz and hsiz variables. The SIZ
statement also returns the size of the window in positioning
units, in nvsiz and nhsiz variables.
@win,,2,3,0,0,,o 'Child Window' <wind>i6 .
@siz,<wind> <vsiz>i6,<hsiz>i6,<nvsiz>i6,<nhsiz>i6
.

This example uses the LST statement to create a list box.
This list box is deﬁned to use row and column units. The list
box starts at row 5 and column 3, and is 10 rows by 15
columns in size. See @LST (Deﬁne List Box) for more
information on the LST statement.
The SIZ statement returns the size of the list box in row and
columns, in lvsiz and lhsiz variables. The SIZ statement also
returns the size of the list box in positioning units, in lnvsiz
and lnhsiz variables. The SIZ statement also returns the
outside dimensions of the list box in positioning units, in
lovsiz and lohsiz variables.
@lst,0,4,15,3,,BIGLIST.TXT, <wind>,5,3,10,15
‘BIGLIST’ <lst>i6 .
@siz,<lst> <lvsiz>i6,<lhsiz>i6,<lnvsiz>i6,
<lnhsiz>i6,<lovsiz>i6,<lohsiz>i6

Sn (Shift Report)
Use the Shift Report (Sn) command to shift the displayed
portion of the report. It is particularly useful for shifting the
report to the left to display columns beyond column 80.
The command displays the shifted report. Note that period
lines do not shift.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The report to shift must be on display.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The report to shift must be on display and in basic, unshifted
format (format 0).
Ü Ü 2200

Shift Report Control Line Format
Sn
where n is the number of columns to shift. To shift to the
right, enter a minus sign followed by the number of columns
to shift.

Note: Do not leave a space between the S and the number
of columns. (For example, enter s10, not s 10.)

Shift Report Guidelines
To shift back to the original display, type the s, a minus sign,
and the number of characters shifted (or a number greater
than the number shifted). For example: s-255
To shift back to basic, unshifted format (format 0), enter f0.

SNOOZE
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Snooze command to return a received alarm
message to the message queue and then have it sent back
to your station at a later time.
The system delays the message for the amount of time you
specify.

Snooze Control Line Format
SNOOZE [time]
where time is the amount of time to delay the message.
Enter just a number for minutes or follow a number with the
letter H for hours, D for days, or W for weeks. You can delay
the message for up to 52 weeks. Default = 1 hour.

Snooze Examples
This example defers the alarm message for one hour:
snooze
This example sends the message again in 30 minutes:
snooze 30
This example sends the message again in two and one-half
hours: snooze 2.5h
This example sends the message again in two days: snooze
2d
Ü Ü 2200

SNU and @SNU (Send Report to User)
Use the Send Report to User (SNU) function or SNU
statement to send an entire report to another user. This
command is similar to the Send Report command, but you
do not need to know the station number of the receiving
user with this command.
For the Send Report to User manual function, the report you
want to send must be on display.

Send Report to User Outcome
Executing the Send Report to User command causes the
following actions to occur:
The command sends the report as a result to the
speciﬁed user. When using the Send Report to User
manual function, the command redisplays the report on
your screen, along with a message indicating that the
report was queued.
If you do not specify a department number, the
command sends the report to all users with the
speciﬁed user-id. The ﬁrst user to acknowledge the
message acknowledges the message for all users with
that same user-id.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
When the receiver requests to duplicate the message,
the system creates a report in the cabinet and drawer of
the original report being sent.
If the number of characters in the received message is
greater than the maximum line length, the report lines
are truncated to the maximum line length.
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0,
drawer I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate
report resides in cabinet 0, drawer I.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

When the receiver requests to duplicate the message,
the report resides in the speciﬁed drawer of the cabinet
the user is currently in (default is the drawer of the sent
report).
If the number of characters in the received message is
greater than the maximum line length, the report lines
are truncated to the maximum line length.
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0,
drawer I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate
report resides in cabinet 0, drawer I. If a message from
an OS 2200 remote site is an 80-character or less result,
it resides in drawer A. If you duplicate the message, the
duplicate report resides in drawer A.
Ü Ü 2200

@SNU Statement Outcome
If the user-id or department does not exist at the local site,
the run goes to the speciﬁed label. For remote sites, the
system returns the message informing you that the user-id
or department is invalid.

Send Report to User Example
This example sends report 1C0 to all users with the user-id
of newuser. With the equivalent statement, the run
continues at label 3 if newuser is not a valid user-id. With
report 1C0 on display, enter snu newuser
Equivalent Statement
@snu,0,c,1,newuser,,,,003 .

SOE Updating and @WRL (Write Line)
Use SOE updating to change information in a report. Use the
Write Line (WRL) statement to update up to 23 lines of a
report.
For the WRL statement, precede all WRL statements with an
Update Lock (LOK) statement unless you are processing a
result. To release update control, enter an Unlock (ULK)
statement following the WRL statement.

SOE Updating In-Report Format
See SOE Updating Guidelines.

and the update date on the

SOE Updating Outcome
The system updates the report with the changes you
requested and the date and time of the update and your
user-id are written in the date line. (With the WRL
statement, you have the option of omitting the date, time,
and user-id update in the date line.)

@WRL Statement Outcome
The statement updates the report, renames that report -0,
and releases any previous -0 result.

SOE Updating Guidelines
To Update a Report
Follow these steps to update a report:
1. Display a report.
2. Type changes on any lines on the screen below the date
line.
3. Position the cursor below the last line you changed, or
at the end of the last line you changed, and transmit.
If You Do Not Want the Changes
If you do not want to keep the changes you have just made,
you have two possibilities:
If you have already transmitted the changes, press
Undo to undo the most recently transmitted changes.
If you have not transmitted the changes, press Paint to
remove the changes from the screen.
To Update a Report Using the SOE Key
Follow these steps to update a report using the SOE key:
1. Position the cursor on the line above or to the left of the
area you intend to change and press SOE.
2. Enter changes following the SOE character.
3. Position the cursor following your changes and transmit.

Use an SOE character to isolate the data you are changing.
For example, suppose you changed data in several places
on the screen but you decide you do not want to make the
changes in the upper part. Enter the SOE character
immediately preceding the data to change to avoid
changing data above it. You can press Paint to see that the
upper part of the report was not updated.
2200 Þ Þ
An SOE character is not required when updating a report;
however, using one is recommended. Using an SOE on your
updates improves the response time because only the
characters between SOE and the cursor are transmitted to
and from the host.
Ü Ü 2200

Using @WRL to Write One Line Example
In this example, the LOK statement locks the report. The
WRL statement writes line 6 as a tab line, with the
characters SH placed in column 2 for two characters. The
ULK statement unlocks the report.
@lok,0,b,3 wrl,0,b,3,6 2-2 |,SH ulk .

lok,0,b,3

Lock report 3B0.

wrl,0,b,3,6 Write line 6 in report 3B0.

2-2 |,SH

Write a tab line with the
characters SH in column 2
for two characters.

ulk

Release update control.

Using @WRL to Write Multiple Lines Example
In this example, the WRL statement writes two tab lines
(lines 6 and 7) in report 3B0. The LOK and ULK statements
lock and unlock the report.
@lok,0,b,3 .
@wrl,0,b,3,6 5-6,77-3 |,930604,FRI/|,930614,MON .
@ulk .

wrl,0,b,3,6

Write lines starting at line
6 in report 3B0.

5-6,77-3

Write six characters
beginning in column 5
and three characters
beginning in column 77.

|,930604,FRI/

In line 6, write 930604 in
the ﬁrst set of columns
and FRI in the second set
of columns.

|,930614,MON In line 7, write 930614 in
the ﬁrst set of columns
and MON in the second
set of columns.

SORT, @SOR (Sort), SORTR, @SRR
(Sort and Replace)
Use the Sort function or the SOR statement to put the lines
of a report in a speciﬁed order.
Use the Sort and Replace Report (SORTR) function or the
SRR statement to put the lines of a report in a speciﬁed
order and replace the sorted data into the original report.
Sort and Replace Report performs the same operation as a
Sort command followed by Replace Report command.
You can use the Iterative Sort (ISOR) run to save and reuse
Sort function masks containing options and parameters and
to create SOR statements.
With the Sort command, you can do the following tasks:
Sort a report by placing a ﬁeld in alphabetical order
(ascending) or reverse alphabetical order (descending).
Sort a report by organizing a ﬁeld in numerical order.
You can use the N parameter to sort numbers containing
decimal points, spaces, plus, and minus signs.
Sort a report using several ﬁelds. For example, you can
sort the St Cd ﬁeld in alphabetical order, and within that
ﬁeld, sort the Product Type ﬁeld.
For the Sort and Replace Report manual function, you must
be the last person to have updated the report. You must use
the Sort and Replace command on a report; it cannot be
used on a result.

Sort and Sort and Replace Control Line Formats
SORT [report f]
SORTR [report f]

Field

Description

report

Report to sort. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to sort
data (enables you to sort data
in ﬁelds beyond column 80 if
you do not already have those
columns on display).

Sort and Sort and Replace Statement Formats
@SOR,c,d,r o cc ltyp,p .
@SRR,c,d,r o cc ltyp,p .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to sort. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Sort and
Sort and Replace Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of ﬁelds to sort.

ltyp

Line type to sort. If you specify
the A option, leave this
subﬁeld blank but enter the
comma.

p

Parameters ﬁeld (see Sort and
Sort and Replace Parameters).

Sort and Sort and Replace Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.
See Sorting All Line Types
Together Example.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C(S)

Distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters. Uppercase letters
precede lowercase letters on
an ascending sort. For
example, Zebra precedes
apple.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C(x)

Alters the sort process based
on the character set order.
Ordinarily, the system
processes the report based on
the character set of the

drawer. The C option allows
you to choose the character
set type on which to base the
sort. Use one of the following:
C(F) - Full character set (FCS).
C(L) - Limited character set
(LCS).
C(S) - Strict comparison;
distinguishes between
uppercase and lowercase
letters.

S

Stabilizes the sort, always
leaving two equal lines in the
same order as they were in the
original report.

Ü Ü 2200

U(x)

Sorts units (paragraphs) of line
type x based on a trailer line
ﬁeld. If (x) is omitted, tab type
lines are the default. The
trailer line type is the report
mask line type. The sort key
comes from the ﬁrst trailer line
of that line type. The unit must
contain at least one sort key
type line; all lines are treated

as trailer lines until a sort key
line is found. This option
cannot be used with the A
option.

2200 Þ Þ

X+

Sorts the data, using the OS
2200 Sort/Merge product (if
installed at your site),
regardless of the report length.

X-

Sorts the data, using the
normal software process,
regardless of the report length.
If you do not specify X+ or X-,
the sort process is determined
by the report length. See the
administrator for more
information.

Ü Ü 2200

Sort and Sort and Replace Parameters
Parameter Description

1-5

Speciﬁes the ﬁelds to sort
and the hierarchy in which
to sort them. The highest
level is 1 and the lowest
level is 5.
For example, if you specify a
1 in the Last Name ﬁeld, the
Sort command lists all last
names in alphabetical order.
If you specify a 1 in the Last
Name ﬁeld and a 2 in the
First Name ﬁeld, the Sort
command lists ﬁrst names
alphabetically within each
group of alphabetized last
names (as in a telephone
directory).

D

Sorts in descending order.
Type the D following the
level number 1 through 5 for
(example, 2d).
See Sorting in Ascending
and Descending Order
Example.

N

Sorts ﬁelds containing
numbers by strict value, and
regardless of alignment in
the ﬁeld. Type the N
following the level number,
(for example, 1n).
See Sorting Numbers
Example.

Yn

Deﬁnes date ﬁelds to be
processed. n is the number
corresponding to the date
format of 0 to 20.
2200 Þ Þ

n is the number
corresponding to the date
format of
0 to 21.
2200 Þ Þ
The Y parameter
immediately follows the
parameter used to identify
the ﬁeld to sort. Invalid
dates are handled as binary
zeros. The software places
binary zeros at the top of
the report for ascending
sorts and at the bottom of
the report for descending

sorts. For a complete list of
date formats, see Entering
Dates and Times in the DC
Statement.

Note: On the OS 2200 system, you can sort ﬁelds totaling
160 characters in width.

Sort Outcome
Executing the Sort command causes the following actions to
occur:
A result is created with ﬁelds sorted as you speciﬁed.
Default = ascending order.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The command sorts the text according to the alphabetic
order of the language used.
Sort and Sort and Replace Report always leaves two
equal lines in the same order as they were in the
original report.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The system places an update lock on the report to
prevent other users from updating it while it is sorting.
The command sorts the text according to the character
set order of the drawer or, the character set order you
specify if you use the C(x) option.
Ü Ü 2200

Sort and Replace Report Outcome
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The system places an update lock on the report to
prevent other users from updating it while it is sorting.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The command sorts the text according to the alphabetic
order of the language used.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The command sorts the text according to the character
set order of the drawer, or the character set order you
specify if you use the C(x) option.
Ü Ü 2200
When using the Sort and Replace Report manual
function, the system redisplays the sorted report,
updating the time and date in the date line. It does not
create a result.

Statement Outcome for Sort and Replace
Report
In addition, executing the SRR statement also sorts and
replaces the original report, renames the report -0, and
releases any previous -0 result.
