@SPI (Stored Procedure Interface)
Use the Stored Procedure Interface (SPI) statement to
execute a speciﬁed stored procedure and return the results.
You can also use the SPI statement to query and return
metadata about stored procedures in a database catalog.
Before using the SPI statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.
You must end the database communication by executing a
Log Oﬀ Relational Database (LGF) statement after all SPI
statements are completed.
With the SPI statement, you can perform the following tasks:
Execute a single stored procedure.
Retrieve a list of stored procedures for a speciﬁed
database.
Retrieve a list of columns with their deﬁnitions for a
speciﬁed stored procedure.
Retrieve a list of parameters with their deﬁnitions for a
speciﬁed stored procedure.

Stored Procedure Interface Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
@SPI[,c,d,lab,db,edsp?,action,wrap,vert] [spsyntax] [vpar1,&,vparn] [typ1,&,typn] .
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
@SPI[,c,d,lab,db,edsp?,action,wrap,vert] [spsyntax[,c,d,r]] [vpar1,&,vparn] [typ1,&,typn] .
Ü Ü 2200

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field and
Subﬁeld

Description

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to hold
the result. Default = 0,A

lab

Label to go to if errors are
detected (STAT1$ <> 0).

db

Database name. The name
is registered in MRIDBA.

edsp?

edsp subﬁeld.
Create a result if an error
occurs?
Y - The result contains the
last statement sent to the
database manager and the
text of the system message.
It also contains the status
code and a descriptive
message.
N - Default. A runtime error
occurs if there is an error
executing this statement.

action

action subﬁeld.
This option determines the
action performed and allows
you to retrieve metadata
about stored procedures in
the speciﬁed database. The
results are stored in the -0
result report. The options
are

E - Default action. Execute a
single stored procedure.
S - Return a list of stored
procedures for a speciﬁed
database.
C - Return a list of columns
for a speciﬁed stored
procedure.
P - Return a list of
parameters for a speciﬁed
stored procedure.

Note: A result is always
generated for a successful
SPI statement. If the stored
procedure does not return a
rowset (for example, a
table), the -0 result will only
contain a title line. If the
stored procedure returns a
rowset, the -0 result will
contain the title line and
column headers. The
number of data lines is
dependent on the stored
procedure function.

wrap

wrap subﬁeld.
T - Truncate the results. The
rows are truncated for the E
action option, if the width of
the rowset exceeds the
width of the result report.
Decimal values that do not
ﬁt within their ﬁeld widths

are also truncated. For
metadata action options (S,
C, P), the ﬁelds are
truncated on the right side.
W - Wrap results on multiple
lines if the result exceeds
the result report width. A
blank line is added to each
header line that is wrapped.
The W value is only valid
with the E action option.
N - Default action. The E
action option displays a
system error message if the
data does not ﬁt within the
line width of the selected
drawer or if a decimal value
does not ﬁt within its column
width. The metadata action
options (S, C, P) display a
system error message if any
ﬁeld does not ﬁt within the
column width.

vert

vert subﬁeld.
This option deﬁnes the
format of the results. This
only applies to the E action
option. The options are
Y - Display results vertically
without column names.
N - Default action. Display
results horizontally with
column names.

Stored
Required if action option is
Procedure not S. If action option is S,
Field
this and subsequent ﬁelds
should not be present.

sp-syntax

The stored procedure syntax
to execute or its name to
retrieve information about.
This ﬁeld contains the string
feed to the database for
executing a stored
procedure. See Action
Subﬁeld for more
information. If the action
option is C or P, this ﬁeld
should contain only the
name of the stored
procedure to retrieve
metadata about.

2200 Þ Þ

c,d,r

Cabinet, drawer, and report
containing stored procedure
syntax. If the syntax is
supplied in the sp-syntax ﬁeld,
then the syntax in the report is
ignored.

Ü Ü 2200

Parameter Valid only for E action
Field
option. Otherwise, this and
subsequent ﬁelds should not
be present. If there are no
parameters for the stored
procedure, this ﬁeld is not
present.

vpar

Up to 78 variables for
parameter passing. Each
variable is associated with a
parameter in the sp-syntax
ﬁeld. The sp-syntax uses the
ODBC Call escape sequence,
which contains ? (question
mark) parameter markers.
There is a one-to-one
association between each
variable (vpar) and each
parameter marker in the
stored procedure syntax.
Any variable can be used as
an input parameter, an
output parameter, or an
input and output parameter.
The type is deﬁned by the
stored procedure and can be
determined from the P
action option. The size of
variables used determines

the space available for
output parameters.
Therefore, you must ensure
that variables deﬁned for
output parameters are large
enough. See Action Subﬁeld
for more information. Any
variable marked as an
output type parameter will
be updated with the values
from the stored procedure
as deﬁned by the stored
procedure.

Parameter This ﬁeld is required if the
Type Field Parameter Field is speciﬁed.

typ

Parameter type speciﬁcation
for each vpar listed in the
Parameter Field. This is a
comma-separated list of
values. The values are
r - return value
i - input parameter (default
value)
o - output parameter
b - both input and output
parameters
These values are used when
binding the parameters for
execution and must match
the deﬁned type for this

stored procedure. The return
value is always an integer. If
any parameters are
identiﬁed as a return value,
it is recommended that the
associated variable be an I
type since an integer is
always returned.

Stored Procedure Interface Reserved Words
STAT1$ captures the status of the action performed. The
status of the Stored Procedure Interface execution is stored
here. Status values of 0 or 6001 (end of records) are
considered normal completion and do not use the error label
branch. Other positive values reﬂect the native error codes
from the database provider for the operation in error.
Special status codes are returned as negative numbers and
include:
-999 = no native error code provided; see error text for
more details.
-1000 = errors detected while retrieving data for metadata
action options. Usually, this occurs when the provider does
not support a schema or method for retrieving the
requested metadata.
The STAT1$ value is not the same as the RETURN-VALUE
returned by the stored procedure. To capture the RETURNVALUE stored procedure, use an appropriate parameter
marker in the sp-syntax. See examples that follow.

Action Subﬁeld
Option

Description

E

Returns the rowset results in
the -0 result report and any
output parameters are loaded
into the corresponding
variables. See examples that
follow. Unlike the SQL
statement, the SPI rowset
results cannot be returned in
the variables unless the
procedure deﬁnes output
parameters. A stored
procedure can have three
types of output depending
upon the procedure itself:
Rowset - stored in the -0
result as a table
Output parameters - stored
in the corresponding
variables (vpar)
Return value - stored in the
corresponding variables
(vpar)

S

Returns a list of stored
procedures from the database

speciﬁed in the db subﬁeld.
The list is returned in the -0
result. Returned ﬁeld (size)
includes
Type (4) - procedure type
(PROC, FN, or Unkn)
SP Name (100) - name of the
stored procedure

C

Returns a list of columns for
the speciﬁed stored procedure
in the -0 result. Returned ﬁelds
(sizes) include
Column Name (30) - name of
the column
Type (16) - data type name as
deﬁned by the provider
Size (7) - the number of
characters for this ﬁeld
DecPt (5) - the number of digits
to the right of the decimal
point (also called scale)
NF (5) - can this ﬁeld be a NULL
- True, False, or Unkn
Length (10) - length; the
number of digits or characters
for the data as deﬁned by the
provider
Precision (10) - precision of the
column; the number of digits in
the number as deﬁned by the
provider

P

Returns a list of parameters for
the speciﬁed stored procedures
in the -0 result. Returned ﬁelds
(sizes) include
Parameter Name (30) - name
of the parameter
Type (16) - data type name as
deﬁned by the provider
Size (7) - the number of
characters for this ﬁeld
DecPt (5) - the number of digits
to the right of the decimal
point (also called scale)
NF (5) - can this ﬁeld be a NULL
- True, False, or Unkn
PType (5) - parameter type; IN,
OUT, INOUT, RV, Unkn
Length (10) - length; the
number of digits or characters
for the data as deﬁned by the
provider
Precision (10) - precision of the
column; the number of digits in
the number as deﬁned by the
provider

Note: The SPI statement always returns a result. There are
three possible results:
A table with one or more rows
A table with zero rows

No table, just a title line (only possible with the E option)

Vertical Subﬁeld
Option Description

Y

Displays the results in a vertical
format with one data value per line.
This format maximizes the
available space for extremely wide
data columns. The format is similar
to the vert Y option for the FCH
statement. The ﬁeld names are
listed followed by each record, with
one value per line. This option is
only available for the execute
operation (action = E). Each record
begins with .RECORD #., where # is
the record number. The following
shows a 3-column, 2-record result:

.COLUMN(S)
*Color
*Size
*Name
*=========================
.RECORD 1:
|yellow
| 23
|Jack
.RECORD 2:
|blue

| 42
|Jane
N

Displays the results in a horizontal
format with one record per line.
This is the default format. The
following shows a 3-column, 2record result:

*Color Size Name
*======.====.===========
|yellow| 23|Jack
|blue | 42|Jane

SP-Syntax
The sp-syntax for the metada actions (P and C) is simply the
stored procedure name. When the action subﬁeld is execute
(E), the sp-syntax is the standard ODBC CALL escape
sequence (see the following example). The following format
allows you to specify parameter markers (?), which
correspond with the vpar variables on the command line.
The square brackets ([...]) show optional ﬁelds.
{[?=]call procedure_name[([parameter][,
[parameter]]...)]}
For example:
{call SalesByCategory('Produce', '1995')} or {?=call
SalesByCategory('Produce', ?)}
In the following Business Information Server script example,
there are two parameter markers. The <p01> variable holds
the RETURN-VALUE and the <p02> variable holds the ﬁrst
parameter passed to/from SalesByCategory. The STAT1$
variable holds the status from executing the SPI statement.
@ldv <sps>='{? = call SalesByCategory(?,
'<tic>1995<tic>')}'
@spi,0,i,0010,<db>,y,e,n,n <sps> <p01>,<p02> r,i
This is a standard syntax and provides great ﬂexibility, by
allowing for a variety of variables and literals when passing
parameters.

Stored Procedure Interface Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the Stored Procedure
Interface statement:
Executing stored procedures is only supported for
databases that have a local driver on a supported
Windows platform. The database can exist on any
machine if the local driver is conﬁgured to access the
database.
When executing a stored procedure, the sp-syntax
should always use the "ODBC Procedure Call Escape
Sequence." Some database providers, such as Oracle,
do not support other syntax formats. While other syntax
formats may work, they are not tested or supported with
the SPI statement.
The returned metadata depends on the data provider
used. Some data providers do not provide all of the
metadata speciﬁed by the ﬁelds for the S, C, and P
options. In the returned results, the missing ﬁelds will be
blank. The following are examples of some diﬀerences:
Microsoft OLEDB provider for Oracle and Oracle
OLEDB provider for Oracle provide signiﬁcantly
diﬀerent lists of stored procedures.
ODBC providers usually do not provide column
metadata, but no errors are provided.
If the procedure is too complex, OLEDB providers
will generate an error for column metadata.
The list of procedure names generated from an SQL
server includes a version number (;num).

Use caution in constructing the sp-syntax. This syntax
uses the standard "ODBC Procedure Call Escape
Sequence," but each data provider supports diﬀerent
parts of the standard syntax. The unaltered sp-syntax is
sent directly to the database. Therefore, you must
construct the correct syntax for the database and the
data provider being used. For example, when retrieving
a record set from an Oracle stored procedure, a diﬀerent
sp-syntax is required depending on how the procedure
was constructed and whether you use the Microsoft data
provider or the Oracle data provider.
When retrieving parameters (P option) and columns (C
option), ODBC providers usually do not require
delimiters around procedure names containing spaces
or special characters.
For example, @ldv,p <spn>s40='Sales By Year' .
When retrieving parameters (P option) and columns (C
option), OLEDB providers usually require delimiters
around procedure names containing spaces or special
characters.
For example, @ldv,p <spn>s40='[Sales By Year]' .
The Microsoft ODBC provider for SQL Server does not
return any column data (C option). The OLEDB provider
returns column data if the procedure is not too complex.
Microsoft SQL Server always returns a RETURN-VALUE,
even if the procedure does not deﬁne one. This is not
true for other databases, such as Oracle. Using the
syntax "{?=call ..." may result in an error if a return
value has not been deﬁned.
With the Microsoft data provider for Oracle, you can only
deﬁne input parameters for Oracle stored procedures

that include a Result set. You cannot deﬁne output
parameters for these stored procedures.
The Stored Procedure Interface statement is restricted
to using the OLEDB and ODBC interfaces. Other
interfaces are not supported.
Stored procedures that return multiple record sets are
not supported.
Multiple parameter sets are not supported.
Only simple textual parameters, supported by Business
Information Server variables, are supported for stored
procedure parameters. Array parameters are not
supported. Binary, blobs, and variant data types are not
supported.
Stored procedures can have a maximum of 78 passed
parameter markers. They are represented as ? (question
marks) in the syntax. Additional parameters can be
passed if they are passed as literal text in the syntax.
2200 Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Executing the Stored Procedure Interface (SPI) from an
OS 2200 or a Linux or UNIX operating system is only
supported for database conﬁgurations that use MRI-toMRI networking to a remote database on a Windows
operating system. Execution of the SPI statement to
local databases is not supported on these platforms.

Ü Ü 2200 Linux/UNIX OS
Note: The following examples use an OLEDB data provider.
The syntax may be diﬀerent for ODBC data providers.

Execute Stored Procedure With One Input
Parameter and a Rowset Returned Example
This example uses the "CustOrderHist" stored procedure
found in the Northwind database on an SQL Server. The
procedure has one input parameter, called "CustomerID."
The procedure syntax is
PROCEDURE CustOrderHist @CustomerID nchar(5)
AS
SELECT ProductName, Total=SUM(Quantity)
FROM Products P, [Order Details] OD, Orders O,
Customers C
WHERE C.CustomerID = @CustomerID
AND C.CustomerID = O.CustomerID AND O.OrderID =
OD.OrderID AND
OD.ProductID = P.ProductID
GROUP BY ProductName
This procedure returns the following results:
Running dbo."CustOrderHist" ( @CustomerID = OCEAN
).
ProductName Total
---------------------------------------- ---------Chef Anton's Gumbo Mix 20
Konbu 5
Lakkalikööri 10
Mozzarella di Giovanni 5
Original Frankfurter grüne Soße 15
Queso Cabrales 30
Raclette Courdavault 2
Sir Rodney's Marmalade 15

Sir Rodney's Scones 12
Tofu 12
Uncle Bob's Organic Dried Pears 6
No more results.
(11 row(s) returned)
RETURN_VALUE = 0
The following Business Information Server script code shows
how this procedure is executed and the results returned.
.SPI Sample Code Freeform Reports
*================================================
=======================
@ ldv,p <uid>s20='sa',<psw>s20='' . Setup
UserID/password
@ ldv,p <db>s20='northwind' . database
@ ldv,p <par1>s5='OCEAN' . CustomerID (input
parameter)
@ ldv,p <spn>s40='{call custorderhist(?)}' .
@.
@ lgn,,y,0,a,<db> '',,<uid>,<psw> . Logon to the
database
@ spi,0,I,020,<db>,y,e <spn> <par1> i . Execute
the SP
@0020:.
@ lgf,,,<db> . log off the database
@ dsp,-0 .
The following data appears in the results report. The title
line includes the procedure name.
. Results of Procedure: {call custorderhist(?)}

Retrieve Deﬁnition Information About the
Stored Procedure Example
This example uses the S, C, and P options to retrieve
information about the stored procedures in the Northwind
database on Microsoft SQL Server. First, the list of stored
procedures is retrieved. Then, the column and parameter
information is retrieved for the "Sales By Year" procedure.
.SPI Sample Code Freeform Reports
*================================================
=======================
@ ldv,p <uid>s20='sa',<psw>s20='' . Setup
UserID/password
@ ldv,p <db>s20='northwind' . database
@ ldv,p <spname>s40=[Sales By Year] . Name of
stored procedure
@.
@ lgn,020,y,0,a,<db> '',,<uid>,<psw> . Logon to
the database
@.
@ spi,0,i,0020,<db>,y,s . Get list of SPs
@ dsp,-0 . Display list of SPs
@.
@ spi,0,i,0020,<db>,y,c <spname> . Get column
info for this SP
@ dsp,-0 . Display results
@.
@ spi,0,i,0020,<db>,y,p <spname> . Get parameter
info for this SP
@ dsp,-0 . Display results
@0020.
@ lgf,,,<db> . log off the database
The following shows a procedure list for the database result:

. Procedure List for Database: northwind
*Type.SP Name
*====.===========================================
=======================...
|FN |CUSTORDERHIST;1
|FN |CUSTORDERSDETAIL;1
|FN |CUSTORDERSORDERS;1
|FN |DT_ADDTOSOURCECONTROL;1
...
The following shows a column description for the procedure
result:
. Column Description for Procedure: [Sales By
Year]
*Column Name .Type .Size .DecPt.NF .Length
.Precision
*=================....================.=======.==
===.=====....=====.==========
|ShippedDate |DBTIMESTAMP | 23| 3|True | 16| 23
|OrderID |I4 | 11| |False| 4| 10
|Subtotal |CY | 21| |True | 8| 19
|Year |WSTR | 30| |True | 30|

Note: Spaces have been removed from the returned column
name and length ﬁelds so the results display properly.
The following shows a parameter description for the
procedure result:
. Parameter Description for Procedure: [Sales By
Year]
*Parameter Name .Type .Size .DecPt.NF
.PType.Length .Precision
*=================....================.=======.==
===.=====.=====....=====.==========

|RETURN_VALUE |I4 | 11| |False|RV | | 10
|@Beginning_Date |DBTIMESTAMP | 0| |True |IN | |
|@Ending_Date |DBTIMESTAMP | 0| |True |IN | |

Note: Spaces have been removed from the returned
parameter name and length ﬁelds so the results display
properly.
The column sizes for the metadata are ﬁxed sizes. It is
possible that databases may return information that
exceeds these column sizes. This is not likely to occur,
except for possibly the Name ﬁelds. In these cases, an error
message is returned if the truncation option is not set. Many
of the ﬁelds (Type, NF, and PType) are converted from their
numeric form to an alpha form. The size ﬁeld is the
maximum length of any character-based data or the
precision of numeric data. Precision is the maximum number
of digits required to hold a number. It does not include
punctuation marks. You must allow space for any
punctuation marks returned in the data. The data types
represent the data type of the provider and not the data
type of the underlying database.
Providing metadata for stored procedures is dependent on
the ability of the provider to return the data. The SPI
statement may try several methods to retrieve the data. If
the data cannot be retrieved, an error may occur. For
example, a complex stored procedure with multiple select
statements may not be able to determine the columns in
the returning rowset. Executing the SPI statement with the C
action results in an error, because the column information
cannot be determined. Or the provider may simply return no
information and no errors even when columns or
parameters do exist. In that case, SPI returns a rowset with
no rows.

Use Multiple Parameters and an Output
Parameter Example
This example executes the "SalesByCategory" stored
procedure (see the Northwind database on Microsoft SQL
Server), which passes two input parameters and one output
parameter (the return value).
The following Business Information Server script shows how
this procedure is called and the parameters are passed.
.SPI Sample Code Freeform Reports
*================================================
=======================
@ ldv,p <uid>s20='sa',<psw>s20='' . Setup
UserID/password
@ ldv,p <db>s20='northwind' . database
@ ldv,p <cat>s8='Seafood' . Category (input
parameter)
@ ldv,p <yr>s4='1998' . Year (input parameter)
@ ldv,p <rv>i6=0 . Return Value
@ ldv,p <spn>s40='{?=call salesbycategory(?,?)}'
.
@.
@ lgn,030,y,0,a,<db> '',,<uid>,<psw> . Logon to
the database
@ spi,0,i,030,<db>,y <spn> <rv>,<cat>,<yr> r,i,i
. Execute the SP
@.
@ if <rv> eq 0 gto 0030 . Check the return value
Return Value is <rv>
@ dsp,-0 .
@030:.
@ lgf,,,<db> . log off the database

. Results of Procedure: {?=call [salesbycategory]
(?,?)}

One Input Parameter with a Result Set Example
This is an Oracle stored procedure example that returns a
result set containing one input parameter. It uses the
Microsoft data provider (either ODBC or OLE DB) for Oracle.
This example assumes that you have the following table and
procedure package.
Here is the Person table.
SSN .FNAME .LNAME
===================.===============.=============
=======
555662222|Sam |Goodwin
555882222|Kent |Clark
666223333|Jane |Doe
123662200|Mary |Goodwin
Here is the Packperson Package Speciﬁcation of the Oracle
stored procedure.
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE PACKPERSON
AS
TYPE tssn is TABLE of NUMBER(10)
INDEX BY BINARY_INTEGER;
TYPE tfname is TABLE of VARCHAR2(15)
INDEX BY BINARY_INTEGER;
TYPE tlname is TABLE of VARCHAR2(20)
INDEX BY BINARY_INTEGER;
PROCEDURE lastname
(inname IN VARCHAR2,
ssn OUT tssn,
fname OUT tfname,

lname OUT tlname);
END packperson;
Here is the Oracle stored procedure package body. It uses
the procedure name of lastname.
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY PACKPERSON
AS
PROCEDURE lastname
(inname IN VARCHAR2,
ssn OUT tssn,
fname OUT tfname,
lname OUT tlname)
IS
CURSOR person_cur IS
SELECT ssn, fname, lname
FROM person
WHERE lname = inname;
percount NUMBER DEFAULT 1;
BEGIN
FOR singleperson IN person_cur
LOOP
ssn(percount) := singleperson.ssn;
fname(percount) := singleperson.fname;
lname(percount) := singleperson.lname;
percount := percount + 1;
END LOOP;
END;
END;
The following Business Information Server Script executes
the procedure lastname in the package packperson.

@. Setup Database
@ ldv,p <db>s20=msora-ole,<uid>s20=scott,
<psw>s20=tiger .
@ lgn,60,y,0,a,<db> '',,<uid>,<psw> . Do the
logon
@ ldv <p1>s7=Goodwin . Input parameter
@. Load the sp-syntax
@ ldv,p <spn>s80='{call packperson.lastname(?,
{resultset 9,ssn,fname,lname})}' .
@ spi,0,i,0060,<db>,y,e,t,n <spn> <p1> i .
Execute it
@. Display the results
@060:dsp,-0 .
The following shows the results of the execution:
. Results of Procedure: {call
packperson.lastname(?,{resultset
9,ssn,fname,lname})}
*SSN .FNAME .LNAME
*===================.===============.============
========
| 555662222|Sam |Goodwin
| 123662200|Mary |Goodwin

@SQ Statement Format
@SQ[,lab] sn .

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the station
table does not exist.

sn

Station number of the printer.

Ü Ü 2200

@SQL (Submit SQL)
Use the Submit SQL (SQL) statement to pass SQL syntax to
the relational database and retrieve data in the variables.
Include the SQL syntax in the statement or store it in a
report. For more information, see @LGN (Log On to
Relational Database).
Before using the SQL statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.
You cannot use the SQL statement to issue a "BEGIN
THREAD...;" when using an extended mode MRIM server,
you must use the LGN statement to log on.

@SQL Statement Format
@SQL[,lab,edsp?,c,d,cstat,db]
'syntax'[,c,d,r,sel,sq]
vstat[,vcol,vretn,vretn,...vretn] .

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

lab

Label to go to if the status in
the vstat variable is other than
normal completion and does
not equal the value of the
cstat subﬁeld.

edsp?

Create a result if an error
occurs? Y or N. The result
contains the last statement
sent to the database manager
and the text of the system

message. It also contains the
status code and column where
the error occurred, if
applicable.
If you supplied a label, the run
continues at the label and the
error information resides in
the -0 result.

c,d

Cabinet and drawer to hold
the result.

cstat

Alternate valid status code. If
the edsp? subﬁeld contains Y,
and you specify a code in this
subﬁeld, the statement does
not create an error result.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

db

Database name. Default =
default database name on
your system.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

db

Database name. Default =
database running with MRIMID
set to 1. See your
administrator for more
information.

Ü Ü 2200

'syntax'

The SQL syntax (ignored if you
specify a report in the c,d,r
subﬁelds).
If the syntax requires
apostrophes to refer to
character data, use a variable
assigned to TIC$. For example:
'...Where LOCATION =
'<tic>'Edina'<tic>';'

2200 Þ Þ

c,d,r

Report containing a list of SQL
statements to be submitted by
the statement. Each statement
must end with a semicolon ( ;
). In each SQL statement, the
maximum number of syntax
characters for the basic mode
MRIM is 7948 when the syntax
is supplied in a report. The

maximum number of syntax
characters for the extended
mode MRIM is 7500 when the
syntax is supplied in a report.
You can send multiple SQL
syntax statements from a
report with a single request.
This can be accomplished by
surrounding the desired
statements with the pound
sign (#), as follows:
#
SQL statement 1;
SQL statement 2;
#
This method can be used to
create stored procedures that
require more than a single SQL
statement. The maximum
number of characters for all of
the statements using this
technique is the same as
previously stated.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

c,d,r

Report containing a list of SQL
statements to be submitted by
the statement. Each statement

must end with a semicolon ( ;
). In each SQL statement, the
maximum number of syntax
characters is 7500 when the
syntax is supplied in a report.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

sel

SQL statement number,
indicating which SQL
statement to execute. Use this
variable when input is from a
report. Default = 1.

sq

Number of SQL statements to
execute. Use this variable
when input is from a report.
Default = ALL.

Each of the following variables corresponds to variables you
specify in the relational database syntax.
The type and length of these variables depends on the type
and length of the ﬁelds in the database. For numeric
columns, use type A, F, or I variables. For character
columns, use type H or S variables. If a type F variable is not
large enough to hold the results, the statement ﬁlls the
variable with asterisks (*). Using incorrect variable types or
lengths may produce unpredictable results.

When you specify type A, F, or I variables to receive data,
the SQL statement right justiﬁes and space-ﬁlls the results
within the variable.
When you use SQL to send or receive data for a numeric
column to a relational database or when determining the
necessary size of a variable, the variable type must be A, F,
or I, and large enough to contain a leading plus (+) or minus
(-) sign. This is necessary because some databases require a
leading sign when sending numeric data.

Field

Description

vstat

Variable to capture the status
of the action performed by the
SQL syntax. Deﬁne this
variable as type A, seven
characters in length (for
example, v1a7).
Normal Completion status
from RSA/RDMS = 0000
"End of Cursor" or Record not
Found for all databases =
6001
Use the "GETERROR..." syntax
to obtain information relating
to other status values.

vcol

Variable to capture the
number of the character

(within the syntax generated
by the SQL statement) where
an error was detected. Deﬁne
this variable as type I, ﬁve
characters in length (for
example, v2i5). If the
database type is MQL and
vstat = 0, the value of vcol is
unpredictable.

vretn

Up to 38 variables to pass
information to or receive
information from the relational
database.

2200 Þ Þ
The combined size of all variables used to return information
from a function must not exceed 511 characters.
Ü Ü 2200

Submit SQL Reserved Words
STAT1$ contains the status of the last syntax sent to the
database manager. STAT2$ contains zero (0). STAT3$
contains one of the following values:
A system message number if an interface error occurred
(for example, the database is inoperative) and you
supplied a label. Use the Load System Message (LSM)
statement to obtain the text of the message.
Zero if the relational database returns an error (for
example, an invalid column or table name) and you
supplied a label.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If STAT1$ variable equals zero, then STAT3$ contains the
number of records processed for an SQL INSERT or
DELETE command for databases such as Oracle,
Microsoft SQL, and Sybase. For other databases, a value
of zero indicates that no records were processed or that
the database does not support the return of this value.
When you INSERT data into an MQL table, the STAT1
value returned is 0.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

Submit SQL Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Use relational database variables in an SQL statement
to send information to the relational database and to
retrieve information from the relational database.
If you use relational database variables, you must place
the syntax in the 'syntax' portion of the SQL statement;
you cannot use the report (c,d,r) method.
The vstat, vcol, and vretn variables contain information
as described unless the statement detects an interface
error, in which case the contents may be unpredictable.
Each SQL statement must begin on a separate line.

Submit SQL Examples
This example uses the SQL statement to submit an insert
table command. The example identiﬁes a report (shown
next) containing SQL syntax for inserting a row into the
houses table.
@sql,099,y,0,a,,agency '',0,a,350 <error>a5,
<column>a5 .

099,y,0,a

Goes to label 99 in case of
an error, creates a -0 result
containing error
information, and places it
in cabinet 0, drawer A.

agency

Speciﬁes the database
name.

''

Field to contain the SQL
syntax. Since the
statement identiﬁes a
report, the syntax is
omitted.

0,a,350

Identiﬁes the report
containing the SQL syntax.

<error>a5

Loads <error> with the

status of the action
performed.

<column>a5 Loads <column> with the
column number where an
error occurred.

Line> 1 Roll> - 308A
.DATE 20 JUN 91 10:07:52 RID 350A 20 JUN 91
MAPPER
.Insert a row
*================================================
======================
INSERT INTO houses
VALUES ('H500','Edina',150,'3 Bedrooms');
..... END REPORT .....
Here is a sample run that provides an example of the SQL
statement, which puts the data into speciﬁc variables. The
run executes until an end-of-data value is found
(<error>=6001). Possible tic translation problems can occur
if this example is cut and pasted into a Business Information
Server script.
@ldv,p <db>s18=testdb2,<id>s20=db2admin,
<psw>s20=db2admin
@ldv,w <tic>h1=tic$
@ldv,p <Fetch>s998='@SQL,199,y,,,6001,<db> '\
<tic>'Fetch next into:1,:2,:3,:4;'<tic>\
' <error>a6,<column>a5,<1>,<2>,<3>,<4>'
@ldv <l>s10=This,<2>s10=is,<3>s250=Cool,
<4>s20=too
@lgn,,,ecab$,edrw$,<db> '',,<id>,<psw> <vdtt>a6

@SQL,199,,,,6001,<db> 'SELECT * FROM cars'
<error>a6,<column>a5
@brk .
The Select Statement Worked
@brk dsp,-0
@brk
*================================================
======================
@001:xqt <Fetch>
[<1>][<2>][<3>][<4>]
@if<error> = 6001 gto 3 . ; .
@002:
@if<error> ne 0,(199) ; gto 1 .
@003:brk dsp,-0
@lgf,,,<db>
@gto end
@199:ldv <stat1>a12=stat1$,<stat2>a12=stat2$,
<stat3>a12=stat3$
@brk
An error occurred!!!
@dsp,-0
@lgf,,,<db>
@gto end
=================================================
======================
Here is a Singleton SELECT Example. In this example,
P1,:P2,:P3,:P4 are the host variables whose return values
are placed into <1>,<2>,<3>,<4>, respectively.
@ldv,p <db>s18=odbc_orac, <id>s20=scott,
<psw>s20=tiger
@ldv,w <tic>h1=tic$
@ldv,p <Fetch>s998='@SQL,199,y,,,6001,<db>
'<tic>'SELECT '\
'HOUSE_NO,LOCATION,PRICE,HOUSES_DESCRIPTION '\

'INTO :P1,:P2,:P3,:P4 FROM Houses WHERE
HOUSE_NO=1'<tic>\
' <error>a6,<column>a5,<1>s4,<2>s15,<3>i3,<4>s25'
@LGN,190,y,0,a,<db> '',,<id>,<psw> <vdtt>a6
@DSP,-0
*================================================
====================
@010: .
@XQT <Fetch>
@BRK .
[<1>][<2>][<3>][<4>]
@BRK .
@190:
@DSP, -0
@LGF,,,<db>

Note: Singleton SELECTs are only supported in Native mode
for Oracle and RDMS databases. They are not supported for
ODBC or OLEDB.
Here is an MRI to RDMS example.
@brk
.Test Tbl
*C1pk .C2int .C3dec2
*==============.============.============
*C |I |D2
*P | |
* |N |N
|C3dec2_is_NULL| 1|
|One | 1| 1
|NineNinetyNine| 999| 9.99
@brk rnm -1
@ldv <sta>a7=-,<col>i3,<dbt>a3
@lgn,0199,y,0,i,RDMS 'UPDATE(DEFERRED)',,
<userid>,<password> <dbt> . ## Logon

@RAM,-0,0199,D,,,,,y,6006,,,RDMS 'MRI.Tbl'
<sta>a7 . ## Drop
@RAM,-1,0199,B,B,,,,y,,,B,RDMS
'MRI.Tbl,MRI.StorageArea' <sta> . ## Create
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='insert into MRI.Tbl values ($p1,
NULL, $p2);' . ## Insert
@ldv <p1>s14='Twelve',<p2>f12.2=12.12
@sql,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,<p1>,
<p2>
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='update MRI.Tbl set C2int=$p1
where C1pk=$p2;' . ## Update
@ldv <p1>i3=12,<p2>s14='Twelve'
@sql,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,<p1>,
<p2>
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='select * into $p1,$p2,$p3 from
MRI.Tbl where C1pk=$p4;'
@ldv <p1>s14,<p2>i12,<p3>f12.2,
<p4>s14='NineNinetyNine' . ## Select
@sql,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,<p1>,
<p2>,<p3>,<p4>
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='select count(*) into $p1 from
MRI.Tbl where C2int>$p2;'
@sql,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,
<rows>i3,<p2>i2 . ## Count
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='declare Z cursor for Select *
from MRI.Tbl where C2int>$p1;'
@SQL,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,<p1>i12
. ## Declare
@SQL,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS 'open Z using $p1;'
<sta>,<col>,<p1> . ## Open
@.

@ldv <j>i2=1 . ## Loop Initialization ##
@0010:if <j> gt <rows> gto 0019 . ; . ## Loop
Check ##
@.
@ldv,p <sql>s99='fetch Z into $p1 $p2, $p3 $p4,
$p5 $p6;' . ## Fetch
@ldv <p1>s14=-,<p2>i12=-,<p3>f12.2=- . receiving
variables
@ldv <i1>i02=0,<i2>i02=0,<i3>i02=0 . indicator
variables: if -1 then Null
@SQL,0199,y,616,h,6001,RDMS <sql> <sta>,<col>,
<p1>,<i1>,<p2>,<i2>,<p3>,<i3>
@if <sta> = 6001 gto 0019 . ; . . 6001 is end-ofcursor
@brk . ## Display
p1=[<p1>] i1=[<i1>] p1 received the primary key
which is never null
p2=[<p2>] i2=[<i2>] If i2=-1 then p2=null.
Numeric nulls become zero.
p3=[<p3>] i3=[<i3>] If i3=-1 then p3=null.
Numeric nulls become zero.
@brk dsp,-0,,,,,,' Result of @SQL fetch into with
indicator variables'
@inc <j>
@gto 0010 . ## Loop Check Again ##
@0019 . ## Loop Exit ##
@SQL,0199,y,616,h,,RDMS 'Drop Cursor Z;' <sta>,
<col> . ## Drop
@LGF,0199,,RDMS . ## Logoff
@gto end . ## End
@0199 ldv,pw <s1>s12=stat1$,<s2>s12=stat2$,
<s3>s12=stat3$ . Error Handling
@rnm -1 . Save the Error report if a Y was in the
<edsp?> field
@brk lsm,<s3> <lsm>s80 . Load System Message in
case the error was from BIS.

sta=<sta> s1=<s1> s2=<s2> s3=<s3>
lsm=<lsm>
@brk add,-0,-1 dsx,-0,,,,,,' MRI Error'

SS (Station-to-Station Message)
Use the Station-to-Station Message (SS) command to send
data or a message to a conﬁgured station. You can send a
maximum of one screen of data.
The command displays a system message on your screen,
indicating that your message or data was queued to the
receiving station.
The command notiﬁes the receiving station that a message
is waiting.
The system continues to attempt to deliver the message
until the receiving user acknowledges the message.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0, drawer
I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides
in cabinet 0, drawer I.
The message from a local site resides in cabinet 0, drawer F.
If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides in
cabinet 0, drawer F.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
The message from a remote site resides in cabinet 0, drawer
I. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides
in cabinet 0, drawer I. If a message from an OS 2200 remote
site is an 80-character or less result, it resides in drawer A. If
you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides in
drawer A.

The message from a local site resides in cabinet 0, drawer
A. If you duplicate the message, the duplicate report resides
in cabinet 0, drawer A.
Ü Ü 2200
If you type y in the Request message acknowledge ﬁeld, the
system sends a message to you indicating that the receiving
user has acknowledged the message.
For information on acknowledging messages, see OK and
@OK (Acknowledge Message).
To send part of a report, that part of the report must be on
display before you request the command.

Note: Do not send system messages or data containing
SOE characters. To send part of a report, enter the Stationto-Station Message command on the control line; requesting
the command through the menus clears the screen.

Station-to-Station Message Control Line Format
SS[,C]
where C clears the screen. Use this option to clear the
screen when you have a menu on display and you want to
send a station to station message. Default = retains the
current screen and requests information at the top of the
screen.

Station-to-Station Message Procedure
To send a station-to-station message
1. In the Message to station ﬁeld, type the station number
of the receiving station.
Typing the site letter of the receiving station is optional.
(To see a list of sites, press Remote from the active
screen.)

2. To request an acknowledgment when the message is
received, tab to the Request message acknowledge ﬁeld
and type Y.

3. Move your cursor below the top line and type your
message, up to one screen.

Note: If you entered SS on the control line with a report
on display, you can also send part or all of the displayed
report. Move the cursor below the part to be sent and
transmit.

4. Transmit.

START and @STR (Start)
Use the Start function or STR statement to collect data in a
report from one or more reports and use the data as input to
a job or process as a job itself.
You can also use it to start jobs that produce ﬁles you can
retrieve into the system with the Retrieve File command.
Windows Þ Þ
For a batch run, Start executes a DOS shell script or a
program. The optional interface name is a short identiﬁer for
the complete path name of the program and is deﬁned by
Agenda Start Registration.
Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
For a batch run, Start executes a Linux or UNIX shell script
or a program. The optional interface name is a short
identiﬁer for the complete path name of the program and is
deﬁned by Agenda Start Registration.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
Start executes a runstream with Executive control language
(ECL) statements as a batch run.
Ü Ü 2200
Data to pass to a job must exist in a report.
Windows Þ Þ

Note: You should avoid using this command to start long
running "daemon" type processes or batch programs due to
the limited number of external program resources
associated with each Business Information Server site. See
the Developer’s Guide for more information on starting BIS
programs from a Windows command prompt.
Ü Ü Windows

Start Control Line Format
START

Start Statement Format
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

If you enter a site, you must

@STR Reserved Words
STAT1$ returns the following values if the run encounters an
error:
1. Start disabled for this system.
2. No @RUN card found.
3. Invalid @RUN card.
4. Improper runstream in report.
5. Include ($INCL$) ambiguity.
6. Include ($INCL$) loop.
7. Includes ($INCL$) illegally nested.
8. Translation ($TRAN$) ambiguity.
9. $INCL$ cabinet security violation.
10. Run not registered for $INCL$ cabinet.
11. Report does not exist in drawer.
12. Report number is higher than highest available.
13. System I/O error.
14. File name not speciﬁed.
15. File unobtainable.
16. Element unobtainable.
17. File cannot be assigned.

18. Attempted to write past end of ﬁle.
19. Unable to register for contingencies.
20. Access permission denied.
STAT2$ contains a line number identifying the system
message. Use a Load System Message (LSM) statement to
read the message. Place the number in STAT2$ in the
msgno subﬁeld of the LSM statement.
Ü Ü 2200

Start Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To pass more than one report or drawer, place the
$INCL$ command in the report containing the runstream
you are starting.
Use the Retrieve File command to retrieve ﬁles
produced by the job.
Windows Þ Þ
In addition to using the Retrieve File command to
retrieve ﬁles produced by the job, you can also use the
following command to execute the software as a
program and pass a ﬁle containing a BPRUN$ command
for the software to execute:
Set Path = <shared-install>\shared;%path%
<mapper-install>\mapper [-ssl] -ffilename
For a description of the ﬁelds, see Using the BPRUN$
Command.
Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
In addition to using the Retrieve File command to
retrieve ﬁles produced by the job, you can also use the
following command to execute the software as a
program and pass a ﬁle containing a BPRUN$ command
for the software to execute:
mapper [-ssl] -ffilename

For trusted mode installation:
Use the logon and psw ﬁelds in the STR statement and
the Start function to specify the log-on and password of
another system user. The started process runs with the
privilege of the speciﬁed login. If you do not specify an
alternate login or password, the process runs with the
privilege of the session user.
For secure mode installation:
An error will be returned if logon and psw syntax are
speciﬁed, and the run will terminate.
The operating system command runs started from a
foreground session execute with the privilege of the
session user.
The operating system command runs started from
background runs execute with the privilege of the
default user.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
In addition to using the Retrieve File command to
retrieve ﬁles produced by the job, you can also use the
following Executive Control Language (ECL) statement
to pass a ﬁle containing a BPRUN$ command for the
software to execute:
@SYM fn,,dev
You can use multiple Symbiont Output File Queuing
(SYM) statements to bring in the data one report at a
time.

Ü Ü 2200

BPRUN$ Command
Insert a BPRUN$ command in the output ﬁle of a job to be
sent to the system to start a run on the system. You can
also use the BPRUN$ command to return data from a job to
the system. To pass data, insert data lines following the
BPRUN$ command.
Include the BPRUN$ command in the ﬁle being sent to the
system. Build the BPRUN$ command into the output of a
job, and start the command in column 1.
Within the output ﬁle, use the following format for the
BPRUN$ command:
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
BPRUN$[,sl,cont] run[,data] user,dept[,pw
c,,d,f,ltyp,lsp? BPERR,edata]
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
BPRUN$[,cont] run[,data]
[[domain\]user],dept[,pw] [c,cpw,d,f,ltyp,lsp?
BPERR,edata]
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

BPRUN$

Batch run command.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

sl

Site letter. Default = local site.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

cont

A continuation character to
indicate that the statement is
continued on the next line.
Use any character that does
not appear elsewhere in the
BPRUN$ command. Also type
this character at the end of
the ﬁrst line of the BPRUN$
command data.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

run

Name of the run to start in the
system. The run must not
contain the following
statements, which
communicate with a terminal:
GOC (when display requested),
GS (when display requested),
OUM, SC, XIT, XUN, graphical
user interface statements.

Since this list includes several
diﬀerent platforms, some
statements may not be
available on your system.
The background run must not
contain the following
statements if they suspend the
run: DSM, DSP, DSX, OUT, SC.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

run

Name of the run to start in the
system. The run cannot
contain any DSG, DSM, DSP,
DSX, GOC (when display
requested), GS (when display
requested), OUM, OUT, REL,
RSI, SC, XIT, or graphical user
interface statements.
You can use the DSG, OUT, and
SC statements if you send the
output to another terminal.

Ü Ü 2200

data

Data passed to the run and
picked up by INPUT$ in the run.

Separate multiple data items
with commas, and do not
include spaces within or
between data items.

domain

An optional domain name used
to qualify the user-ID.

user

User sign-on, if applicable. If
omitted and the BIS
conﬁguration parameter
OSUIDS is non-zero, then the
credentials of the user queuing
the ﬁle will be used.

dept

User department number.

pw

User sign-on password, if
applicable.

c

Cabinet in which to place the
passed data.

2200 Þ Þ

cpw

Cabinet password.

Ü Ü 2200

d

Drawer letter.

f

Format.

ltyp

Line type for tab character
insertion. Type y to replace
spaces with tab characters in
those column positions
deﬁned in report 0 having tab
characters; type a predeﬁned
line type number instead of Y
for a predeﬁned line deﬁned in
report 0.

lsp?

Retain blank lines in the ﬁle? Y
or N. Default = N, delete blank
lines.

BPERR

Name of the run to execute if
an error occurs during the
batch processing. (Call your
administrator to see if your
site has a modiﬁed version of
the BPERR run to handle batch
errors.)

edata

Data passed to the BPERR run
and picked up by INPUT$ in
the BPERR run. Type the
station number to which to
direct BPERR run system
messages as the only data
item if you are using the
standard BPERR run. Separate
multiple data items with
commas and do not include
spaces within or between data
items if you are passing data
to a site deﬁned BPERR run.
If you have questions about
the BPERR run, see your
administrator. The
functionality of the BPERR run
may vary from site to site.

BPRUN$ Outcome
The following actions occur after you send the ﬁle
containing the BPRUN$ command to the system:
The speciﬁed run starts in the system.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If you specify a cabinet and drawer, you can refer to the
lines of data returned (that is, those lines following the
BPRUN$ command) as -0. For information on marking
the end of the data to return, see ENDRD$ Command.
If the syntax of the BPRUN$ command is incorrect, the
system executes the BPERR run and sends the message
to the station you specify in the edata subﬁeld of the
BPRUN$ command. If you do not specify a station in the
edata subﬁeld, it sends the message to MAPCOORD. If
the run started by the BPRUN$ command fails, the
system sends a message to the user-id speciﬁed in the
BPRUN$ command and does not execute the BPERR run.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
If you specify a cabinet, cabinet password, and drawer,
you can refer to the lines of data returned (that is, those
lines following the BPRUN$ command) as -0. For
information on marking the end of the data to return,
see ENDRD$ Command.
In case of an error, the system executes the BPERR run
and sends a message to the system administrator's
station (with the exception of the MGABRT error-Job Was

Aborted), and the station number speciﬁed in the edata
subﬁeld.
Ü Ü 2200
If an RER or RAR is active at the time the batchport run
errors, the error is processed by the error/abort routine,
and the BPERR run will not execute.

Using the BPRUN$ Command
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
To start a run on the system from outside of the system
(and, optionally, send data to the system), use the following
command:
Windows Þ Þ
Set Path = <shared-install>\shared;%path%
<mapper-install>\mapper [-ssl] -ffilename
Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
mapper [-ssl] -ffilename
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

Field

Description

Windows Þ Þ

<sharedinstall>

The fully-qualiﬁed ﬁlename
of the latest installation.

<mapperinstall>

The fully-qualiﬁed ﬁlename
of the latest installation.

This value may be obtained
from prgm$ for the site.

mapper

Executes the software.

Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

mapper

Executes the software
(must be lowercase).

Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

-s

Option (-s) indicating a site
letter follows.

sl

Site letter. Default = local
site.

-f

Option (-f) indicating the
name of a ﬁle to process
follows.

ﬁlename

Complete path name of the
ﬁle containing a BPRUN$

command to send to the
system (case-sensitive).

If the host encounters a BPRUN$ command in the ﬁle, it
sends the ﬁle to the system for processing.
All IBM output ﬁles automatically go to the system, so you
do not need to include a mapper -f statement in the job
stream.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
For an example of a Linux or UNIX shell script returning
output to the system, see Linux or UNIX Shell Script
Returning Output Example.

Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
To start a run on the system from outside of the system
(and, optionally, send data to the system), queue the output
ﬁle containing the BPRUN$ command to the interface with
the following Executive Control Language (ECL) statement:
@SYM fn,,dev

Field

Description

fn

Name of the ﬁle.

dev

Any valid symbiont device
identiﬁcation for the system.
The symbiont device must be
conﬁgured as a batch port
name to the system, and as a
local device to the OS 2200
operating system.

If the host encounters a BPRUN$ command in the ﬁle, it
sends the ﬁle to the system for processing.
For example runstreams, see Transferring Files in System
Data Format (SDF) Example and Starting a Run without
Transferring Data Example.
Ü Ü 2200

ENDRD$ Command
The ENDRD$ command (or another BPRUN$ command)
marks the end of data to include in the report and indicates
to the system that the speciﬁed run is to start.
Enter the ENDRD$ command, starting in column 1 of the
ﬁle.
If the host does not detect an ENDRD$ command, it sends
the entire ﬁle (after the BPRUN$ command).
When using several BPRUN$ commands in a ﬁle, you do not
need to include ENDRD$ commands to mark the end of the
preceding segments of data, since subsequent BPRUN$
commands mark the end of the data.
The host processes all BPRUN$ commands imbedded in a
ﬁle.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

IBM Start Requirements
The IBM binary synchronous communications (BSC) and
system network architecture (SNA) interface capabilities
incorporated into the system let you start IBM job streams
on an IBM mainframe through BSC and SNA protocol.
The bscsym or snasym shell scripts return ﬁles from the IBM
mainframe to the system.
If an error occurs in the job stream and the BPRUN$
command through ENDRD$ command does not get built,
the bscsym or snasym shell script places a BPRUN$
command into the output ﬁle and starts the BPERRIBM run
to send the output to the MAPCOORD user-id at the default
site.
BSC and SNA daemon programs transfer the job streams
and host ﬁles. How often the daemon runs depends on the
entry in the crontabs directory put in by the Linux or UNIX
system administrator during installation. When the daemon
ﬁnishes transferring job streams and receiving output ﬁles
from the IBM host, the host starts any runs speciﬁed by the
BPRUN$ command.
If you want output from the execution of the IBM job stream,
place an IBM ROUTE statement in the job stream. See your
Linux or UNIX system administrator for the remote address
of the BSC and SNA connection.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

Transferring Files in System Data Format (SDF)
Example
In this example, the data processor transfers data to the ﬁle
ﬁle-name in system data format (SDF).
The system searches the ﬁle for the BPRUN$ image. Since
the cabinet and drawer for the returning data are speciﬁed,
the system places the data between the BPRUN$ and
ENDRD$ images in -0, cabinet 0, drawer B. When the
runstream encounters the ENDRD$ command, it starts the
run run-name and passes variables to the run to be picked
up by INPUT$.
@run,a run-id,account/user-id,project-id
@delete,c print-file.
@cat,p print-file.,f///262143
@sym,d print$
@brkpt print$,print-file.
@msg,n ; This statement allows bprun$ to be a
continuation line
bprun$ autret,station newuser,7 0,open,f
@.
@xqt This data is returned to MAPPER
@.
@msg,n ; This statement allows endrd$ to be a
continuation line
endrd$
@.
@xqt This data is not returned to MAPPER
@.
@brkpt print$
@sym,d print$
@sym print-file.,,device-name
@fin

Starting a Run without Transferring Data
Example
The following example starts a run without transferring
data:
@run,a run-id,account/user-id,project-id
@.
@xqt do some work
@.
@delete,c start-run.
@cat,p start-run.,f///262143
@sym,d print$
@brkpt print$,start-run.
@msg,n ; This statement allows bprun$ to be a
continuation line
bprun$ run-name newuser,7
@brkpt print$
@sym,d print$
@sym start-run.,,device-name
@fin
Ü Ü 2200
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

Linux or UNIX Shell Script Returning Output
Example
This is an example of a Linux or UNIX shell script to return
output to the software:
1 cat > /usr/work/newuser/filea<<end*of*input
2 bprun$,a getfile,18 newuser,7,newuser 0,open,a
BPERR,18
3 ======== Line 1 of output file ===========
4 Line 2 of output file
5 Line 3 of output file
6 Line 4 of output file
7 Line 5 of output file
8 endrd$
9 end*of*input
10 mapper -sa -f/usr/work/newuser/filea
The data following the cat command is redirected to the ﬁle
/usr/work/newuser/ﬁlea. All data between the
END*OF*INPUT statements is included in the ﬁle (including
the BPRUN$ and ENDRD$ commands). The ﬁle is then sent
to MAPPER site A through the mapper -f statement.
When this ﬁle is sent to the software, the GETFILE run is
executed.
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
Windows Þ Þ

DOS Shell Script Returning Output Example
This is an example of a DOS shell script to return output to
the software:
. Working example for Windows Server
. Requires 3 runs, getfile, batchcreat, batchexec
.************************************************
***************************
. *** Create the getfile Run and register the
run.
. *** getfile receives the batch port input file
as a -0, and
. *** sends it to the user.
.
@chg input$ <user>s12,<depn>s4 .
@snu,-0,<user>,<depn>
@gto end .
.************************************************
***************************
. *** Create the batchcreat Run,and register the
run.
. *** This run creates a batch port file,
filea.cmd.
. *** that will provide file input to the batch
port run getfile.
.
. *** Invoke with batchrun,userid,dept,passwd,textfile
. *** user,id,dept,passwd is the user of the
getfile Run
. *** textfile is the file to be sent to the user
by the getfile Run
.
@chg input$ <user>s11,<depn>s4,<passw>s6,

<inputfile>s65 .
@ldv,p <user>=<user> .
@ldv,p <depn>=<depn> .
@ldv,p <passw>=<passw> .
@ldv,p <inputfile>=<inputfile> .
@ldv,p <bpath>s80=dbase$'\..\tmp\filea.cmd' .
@brk,0,a .
echo BPRUN$,lrrsd$,\' \' > <bpath>
echo getfile,<user>,<depn>' \' >> <bpath>
echo <user>','<depn>','<passw>' \' >> <bpath>
echo 0,OPEN,A' 'BPERR,stnum$ >> <bpath>
type <inputfile> >> <bpath>
echo ENDRD$ >> <bpath>
@brk .
@str,-0 .
@gto end .
.************************************************
***************************
. *** Create the batchexec Run,and register the
run.
. *** This Run executes the Batch Port file,
filea.cmd at some later time
.
@ldv,p <bpath>s80=dbase$'\..\tmp\filea.cmd' .
@brk .
prgm$'\mapper -s'lrrsd$ -f<bpath>
@brk .
@str,-0 .
@gto end .
Ü Ü Windows

5 //SYSIN DD DUMMY

BPERR,18
9 /*

11 //SYSPRINT DD DUMMY
12 //SYSIN DD DUMMY
13 //SYSUT2 DD SYSOUT=A,DCB=BLKSIZE=133
14 //SYSUT1 DD *
15 ===========FIRST LINE OF FILE ============
16 LINE 1 OF EXAMPLE
17 LINE 2 OF EXAMPLE
18 LINE 3 OF EXAMPLE
19 LINE 4 OF EXAMPLE
20 LINE 5 OF EXAMPLE
21 LAST LINE OF EXAMPLE
22 /*
23 //* CREATE ENDRD$ FOR END OF DATA
24 // EXEC PGM=IEBGENER
25 //SYSPRINT DD DUMMY
26 //SYSIN DD DUMMY
27 //SYSUT2 DD SYSOUT=A,DCB=BLKSIZE=133
28 //SYSUT1 DD *

29 ENDRD$
30 /*
The output from the job stream (including the BPRUN$ and
ENDRD$ commands) is sent to the software. The GETFILE
run is executed on MAPPER site A.

@STN (Station Information)
Use the Station Information (STN) statement to obtain
information about a speciﬁc station. For example, you can
ﬁnd out whether it is conﬁgured as a valid station or verify
that a user is signed on.
Executing this statement causes the following actions to
occur:
The statement loads variables with information about
the station. The run goes to the label if the station or
terminal type does not exist.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The run also goes to the label if a user is not signed on.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
The STN statement does not create a result. Any result
that exists before the STN statement is executed is still
the current (-0) result.

Station Information Statement Format
@STN[,sn,lab
vuser,vdepnm,vdept,vttyp,vvsiz,vhsiz,vaspect,vcol
or,vgraph] .

Field

Description

sn

Station number or terminal
type you want information
about. Default = number of
the station executing the run.
If you specify a terminal type,
vdept contains 0; vuser and
vdepnm contain spaces.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

lab

Label to go to if the station or
terminal type does not exist or
a user is not signed on.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

lab

Label to go to if the station or
terminal type does not exist.

Ü Ü 2200

vuser

Variable to capture the user-id
of the user signed on to the
station. If no user is signed on
to the terminal, spaces ﬁll this
variable. If you specify a
terminal type in the sn
subﬁeld, vuser contains
spaces.

vdepnm

Variable to capture the
department name of the user.
If no user is signed on to the
terminal, spaces ﬁll this
variable. If you specify a
terminal type in the sn
subﬁeld, vdepnm contains
spaces.

vdept

Variable to capture the
department number of the
user. If no user is signed on to
the terminal, this variable is
set to zero. If you specify a
terminal type in the sn
subﬁeld, vdept contains 0.

vttyp

Variable to capture the

terminal type of the station.
These names are identical to
those provided by TTYPE$.

vvsiz

Variable to capture the
conﬁgured vertical screen size
of the station.

vhsiz

Variable to capture the
conﬁgured horizontal screen
size of the station.

vaspect

Variable (F type) to capture
the aspect ratio of the
speciﬁed station. (Same as
the value of ASPECT$.)

vcolor

Variable to capture the
graphics color ﬂag or
terminal. (Same as the value
of COLOR$.)

vgraph

Variable to capture the
graphics type. (Same as the
value of GRAPH$.)

Station Information Example
This example obtains station information for station 100:
@stn,100,199 <user>h11,<dname>h12,<dnum>i4,
<term>h7,\
<vsize>i2,<hsize>i3 .

Provide information about
station 100; go to label
199 if station 100 does
not exist.

Load <user> with the
user-id of the person
currently signed on.

department name.

Load <dnum> with the
department number.

Load <term> with the
terminal type.

<vsize>i2

Load <vsize> with the
vertical screen size.

<hsize>i3

Load <hsize> with the
horizontal screen size.

STOP
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Stop command to stop any or all background runs
being executed under your user id. The Stop command
sends an abort message to the run in the same way a user
might press the abort key to stop an active foreground run.
Use the Register Abort Routine (RAR) statement to register
the background run with an abort routine.
The system terminates the background run you specify.

Stop Control Line Format
STOP[,runname,sn]

Field

Description

runname

Name of the background run
you wish to abort.

sn

Station number that started
the run.

Ü Ü 2200

See START and @STR (Start) for more information.

SU and @SRU (Search Update)
See S and @SRH (Search), SU and @SRU (Search Update)
for more information.

@SUB (Subtotal)
Use the Subtotal (SUB) statement to subtotal data in a
report and format it in the output area of your run.
Since you place the subtotal key variables along with the
variables containing their subtotals in the output area, you
can easily reformat your data at the same time as the
statement computes it.
Enter the variables processed by the SUB statement in the
output area immediately following the SUB statement.

Subtotal Statement Format
@SUB,c,d,r[,lab] o cc ltyp,p vrslts .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to process.

lab

Label to go to if no lines exist
to be subtotaled.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Subtotal
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to process.

ltyp

Line type to process. If you
specify the A option, you can
leave this subﬁeld blank, but
enter the comma.

p

Subtotal parameters ﬁeld (see
Subtotal Parameters). Deﬁne
parameters as you would in a
Totalize (TOT) statement.

vrslts

Variables to capture the
results. Do not use substrings
such as <data>(1-3). Select
the E option to load the ﬁrst
variable with the entry count
for each subgroup.
To initialize a variable to the
size of a ﬁeld, specify the
variable number and variable
type (such as <data>h). If you
use the E option, the variables
capturing the subtotal value
must be fully deﬁned (such as
<data>h3).

Subtotal Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

C

Looks for case changes in the
subtotal key ﬁeld values.
Whenever the value in the
subtotal key ﬁeld changes, a
subtotal is displayed.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

C

Looks for case changes in the
subtotal key ﬁeld values.
Whenever the value in the
subtotal key ﬁeld changes, a
subtotal is displayed.
This option applies to full
character set (FCS) reports
only.

Ü Ü 2200

E

Counts the number of entries
processed for each subtotal
and enters that count in the
ﬁrst deﬁned variable. For
example, if a subtotal includes
four values, the statement
loads the ﬁrst variable with 4.

I

Ignores headings: processes
report even though asterisk
lines or a heading divider line
may not exist.

J(x)

Justiﬁes numeric result values
in variables to x; x is one of
the values listed below.
Leading zeros appear to the
left of a value, nonsigniﬁcant
zeros to the right. Neither
aﬀect the value.
c - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros. Inserts
commas every three digits in
the integer portion of the
result. Right-justiﬁes values.
l - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros. Left-

justiﬁes values.
r - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros. Rightjustiﬁes values.
x - Eliminates leading zeros,
left-justiﬁes values, then ﬁlls
variable with nonsigniﬁcant
zeros.
z - Eliminates nonsigniﬁcant
zeros, right-justiﬁes values,
and ﬁlls variable with leading
zeros.

2200 Þ Þ

*

Ü Ü 2200

Omits all ﬂags that are
otherwise inserted
immediately after the subtotal
result of the operation (but not
the grand total). Subtotals
ﬂagged with an asterisk
indicate that at least one item
processed is blank or
nonnumeric. Subtotals ﬂagged
with a space indicate that all
items processed are valid
numeric data.

Subtotal Parameters
Parameter Description

+

Subtotal a ﬁeld, ﬂagging a
ﬁeld to be multiplied,
divided into, added to, or
subtracted from in
horizontal calculations

-

Subtract (used in horizontal
calculations)

*

Multiply (used in horizontal
calculations)

'/'

Divide (used in horizontal
calculations; include
apostrophes)

=

Load ﬁeld values into this
variable (use with the M
parameter)

A

Average

C

Cumulate data

M

Load the values of this ﬁeld
into the appropriate variable
(use with the = parameter)

S

Subtotal on this subtotal key
ﬁeld (required in all
instances)

S1-S5

Hierarchical subtotal

Subtotal Outcome
Variables contain the subtotal key ﬁeld entries and
subtotals. If you use the E option, the ﬁrst variable contains
the entry count.
Whenever at least one ﬁeld item processed in a vertical
operation contains nonnumeric data or no value (blank), an
asterisk appears next to the resulting value as a ﬂag,
indicating that some of the data was invalid.

Subtotal Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Follow a SUB statement with an output line, since it
creates one line of output for each item subtotaled. Do
not use variable substrings in the output line.
Be sure to place the variable deﬁned for the subtotal
ﬁeld in the output area.
Even though you can predeﬁne the size of a variable to
load in a SUB statement, you can also initialize the
variable to match the size of a corresponding report
ﬁeld. This is especially useful with a named ﬁeld
because the name does not directly specify the ﬁeld
size; it also allows the SUB statement to adjust to a
change in the size of a ﬁeld.

Counting Entries and Subtotaling Example
This example counts the entries used in each subtotal and
loads variables with the subtotal key ﬁeld entries and
subtotal values.
@sub,0,b,5 e 'custcode','producplan' |,s,+
<entries>i6,\
<sub>h,<subtot>i6 .
<sub> <subtot> <entries> ( <-- output line)

0,b,5 e

Process report 5B0 and
use the E option to count
entries.

'custcode' ...
s

Specify the Cust Code
ﬁeld as the subtotal key
ﬁeld.

'producplan'
... +

Specify the Produc Plan
ﬁeld as the ﬁeld to
subtotal.

|

Process tab lines only.

<entries>i6,
<sub>h,
<subtot>i6

Load <entries> with the
entry count, <sub> with
the subtotal key ﬁeld

entry, and <subtot> with
the subtotal value.

Horizontal Calculating and Subtotaling Example
This example subtracts values in the Num2 ﬁeld from those
in the Num1 ﬁeld and adds the result values together when
the value in the Key ﬁeld changes.
Sample Database and Result
Sample database: Resulting output area:
*Key.Num1.Num2.Result. one 4
*===.====.====.======. two 1
one 23 22
one 5 2
two 1 5
two 2 4
two 20 13
Statement and Output Line
@sub,0,a,5 '' 'key','num1','num2','result'
|,s,+,-,\
= <sub>h,,,<subtot>i .
<sub> <subtot> ( <-- output line)

0,a,5 ''

Process report 5A0 and
use no options.

'key' ... s

Specify the Key ﬁeld as
the subtotal key ﬁeld.

'num1' ... +

Specify the Num1 ﬁeld as
the ﬁeld to subtract from.

'num2' ... -

Specify the Num2 ﬁeld as
the ﬁeld to subtract.

'result' |

Specify the Result ﬁeld as
the ﬁeld to hold the
results and process tab
lines only.

<sub>,,,
<subtot>I

Store subtotal key ﬁeld
values in <sub> and
subtotals in <subtot>.

one one 22 two two 9
one two 2
two two 5
two two 4

<sub2>h,<subtot>i .

0,b,5 ''

Process report 5B0 and

'key2' ... s

Specify the Key2 ﬁeld as
another subtotal key ﬁeld.

'Num' ... +

the ﬁeld to subtotal.

|

Process tab lines only.

<sub2>h,
<subtot>i

Load <sub1> with the
ﬁrst subtotal key and load
<sub2> with the second
subtotal key. Load
<subtot> with the
subtotals.

Hierarchical Subtotaling Example
This example assigns a hierarchy to the subtotal key ﬁelds.
It displays subtotals whenever the lower-order subtotal key
changes and displays subtotals of the subtotals whenever
the higher-order subtotal key ﬁeld changes.
Sample Database and Result
Sample database: Resulting output area
*Key1.Key2.Num. a 24
*====.====.===. one 24
one a 22 a 5
one a 2 b 9
two a 5 two 14
two b 5
two b 4
Statement
@sub,0,b,5 '' 'key1','key2','num' |,s1,s2,+
<sub1>h,\
<sub2>h,<subtot>i .
<sub1> <sub2> <subtot> ( <-- output line)

0,b,5 ''

Process report 5B0 and
use no options.

'key1' ... s1

Specify the Key1 ﬁeld as
the higher-order key ﬁeld.

'key2' ... s2

Specify the Key2 ﬁeld as

the lower-order key ﬁeld.

'num' ... +

Specify the Sub ﬁeld as
the ﬁeld to subtotal.

|

Process tab lines only.

<sub1>h,
<sub2>h,
<subtot>i

Load <sub1> and
<sub2> with the subtotal
keys in the Key1 and Key2
ﬁelds, and load <subtot>
with the subtotals.

SV (Save Report Version)
Use the Save Report Version (SV) command to temporarily
save a report or result.
With the SV command, you can
Retain a result while displaying another report.
Retain a result while switching cabinets, so that you can
examine the contents of another cabinet and still have
your saved result available for processing.
The Save Report Version command is especially useful
for saving multiple results.
The command saves the report or result, so that you can
redisplay it using a function key. If you save a result, when
you redisplay it using a function key, a result is displayed; if
you save a report, it is redisplayed as a report. If you update
this report, you are actually updating the original report.
In order to use the SV command, a report must be on
display.

Save Report Version Control Line Format
SV

Save Report Version Guidelines
You can use the Replace Report command to copy a report
to a diﬀerent cabinet. For more information, see REP and
@REP (Replace Report).
However, if you do not know which report or drawer in
which to place the report, use the Save Report Version
command. It enables you to save the report as a result,
switch to the new cabinet, and examine its contents before
saving the report.

Save Report Procedures
Use the following procedure to save a report while you
switch to a diﬀerent cabinet:
1. With the report to save on display, use either the menu
procedure or the control line procedure to execute the
Save Report Version command.
2. Switch to a diﬀerent cabinet, using the Cabinet Switch
(C) command. (Do not use the CS command.)
3. Press Resume to display the saved result.
4. Process the result. For example, to save the result as a
report, use the Replace Report or Duplicate Report
command.

Note: Between steps 1 and 4, do not use any command
that can be resumed or use any run; if you do, the system
may release the saved version.
Use the following procedure to save up to four reports or
results:
1. Use the Save Report Version command to save the ﬁrst
report or result.
2. Display the second report or result and press F2.
3. Display the third report or result and press F3.
4. Display the fourth report or result and press F4.
To redisplay these saved results, press the corresponding
function key.

To release all of the results, enter a caret (^).
The function key bar is redeﬁned. The number of the saved
report or result is displayed with the corresponding function
key (1 through 4), or the word Open is displayed if nothing is
saved. Pressing the Contrl function key redisplays the
original function key deﬁnitions. To regain the Save Report
Version function key bar, press Resume.

SYSTEM and @SYS (System)
Use the System command or SYS statement to display a
result listing the current system activity. To display a new
result reﬂecting the most current activity, press Resume.

Notes:
When debugging non-registered scripts from the
Developer Workshop, the run name appears as the
Report Drawer Cabinet (for example, 65A1) and an
asterisk character appears in the last column of the RUN
NAME ﬁeld.
The SYS statement is only available on Windows Server,
Windows Client, Linux, and UNIX systems.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

System Guidelines
This lock-related information is presented in columns 81
through 154 of the result rid produced by the System
statement. The output format for the ﬁrst 80 columns of the
result rid remains unchanged for compatibility reasons.
The System statement uses drawer D of cabinet 4. This
drawer is generated with a line width of 256 characters. The
zero report now contains the header information for the
System statement. Three formats are deﬁned.
Format 0 presents the current System statement
information.
Format 1 presents the station number and the System
information screen ﬁelds (lock type, lock number, lock
device, Sta read, lock owner, lock drawer, lock report,
and elapsed queue time).
Format 2 presents Run Cntrl Report and Run Line,
replacing Type in the format 0 display.
Since a format switch holds across resumes, it is possible to
track lock type information by switching to format 1 and
continually depressing the resume key.
Since drawer D cabinet 4 is the new default result type for
the System statement and a zero report exists to deﬁne
header information, all System header information has been
removed from the local language rid (4G6). However, user
type (active user and background user) can still be modiﬁed
using the local language rid.

T (Cabinet Table of Contents)
Use the Cabinet Table of Contents (T) command to display a
list of available drawers in your cabinet. Since drawer A is
accessible from all cabinets, it is not included in the list.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
The list of available drawers includes those accessible to
your security group, as registered by the administrator.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Each line in the table of contents describes one drawer and
contains the following items:
Drawer name.
Drawer letter.
Current cabinet number.
W if you have write privileges for the drawer, or R if you
have read-only privileges.

Cabinet Table of Contents Control Line Format
T

Cabinet Table of Contents Guidelines
With the table of contents on display, you can do the
following tasks:
Tab to a selection and transmit to select a drawer. The
command displays a list of reports in the drawer, from
which you can select a report.
Press Tasks to perform processing on the drawer.

TARGET (Target Chart)
Use the Target Chart (TARGET) run to create a target chart
on the screen or plotter. A target chart is a bull's-eye chart
showing data in relation to the center point.
Your terminal must be conﬁgured for graphics.

Target Chart Control Line Format
TARGET[,report]
where report is the chart report in the current cabinet.
Default = current report.

only black, blue, red, or green, but you can put any colors
you want in the pen stalls. If you specify a color not
available on your terminal or plotter, the system selects
another color for it.

Time Line Chart Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this run:
The dates speciﬁed for Sdate and Edate are the
minimum and maximum dates for the chart. The ﬁelds
Minimum Date and Maximum Date enable you to select
a range of dates to show on the chart within the Sdate
and the Edate range.
You can have up to 50 individual time lines on the chart.
If titles begin to overwrite each other, break the data
into two or more charts.

Time Line Chart Procedure
To place a time line on the same line as another, place an
asterisk at the beginning of the data line, as shown in the
following example: * TITLE .SDATE .CDATE .CDATE .CDATE .
EDATE ...
*==============.======.======.======.==
====.======
.Product A ...
*Product B ...
This causes the run to place the time line deﬁned for
Product B on the same line on the chart as the time line
deﬁned for Product A. You can place as many time lines on a
line as space allows.
