@TIP (Tool Tip)
Use the Tool Tip (TIP) statement to deﬁne a tool tip for a
control. The tool tip is displayed when you place your mouse
on the control. Tool tips can be applied to the following GUI
controls: BTN, CBX, EDT, LST, PIC, and TXT.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Notes:
If this session is using Graphical Interface for Business
Information Server or Business Information Server for
Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$
(workstation ﬂag) is equal to one.
Check DWCAP$ for availability: DWCAP$(12-1) = 1 if
available.
For more information on DWCAP$, see the Developer’s
Guide.
The TIP statement displays a tool tip on the screen when
you place your mouse on the speciﬁed control. Tool tips are
for display purposes only; the statement does not return
data.

Tool Tip Statement Format
@TIP,"tip text",vwh[,,lab] .

Field

Description

"tip text" Text for the tool tip window, up
to 256 characters statement.

vwh

Control handle that applies to
the tool tip.

lab

The run continues processing
at the label number if the TIP
statement fails within the
workstation client; run syntax
errors do not take the label.

Tool Tip Reserved Word
If the TIP statement speciﬁes a label in the lab ﬁeld and the
statement receives an error from the workstation client, the
run continues at the label. The STAT1$ reserved word
contains an error status. See Graphical Interface Returned
Status Codes (STAT1$ Values) for possible values returned in
the STAT1$ reserved word.

Note: If a run syntax error is detected, the run will error; it
does not continue at the speciﬁed label.

Tool Tip Example
This example uses the TIP statement for a tool tip on a PIC
statement.
@pic,0,a,3,y,'rose.bmp()' 'Rose' <petals>i6 .
@tip,"A rose by any other name would still be a
rose",<petals> .

"A rose by any
other name would
still be a rose"

Tool tip which
appears when you
place your mouse
over the picture.

<petals>

Handle for the
picture.

TOT and @TOT (Totalize)
Use the Totalize (TOT) function or TOT statement to perform
arithmetic and move operations on ﬁelds in reports.
You can use the Iterative Totalize (ITOT) run to save and
reuse Totalize function masks containing options and
parameters and to create TOT statements.
The following tasks are a few of the operations you can do
with the Totalize command:
Adding, subtracting, multiplying, or dividing values in a
ﬁeld by a constant value.
Computing horizontally and vertically.
Subtotaling ﬁelds when key ﬁelds change.
Changing the line type.
Counting entries.
Copying data from one ﬁeld to another ﬁeld or ﬁelds.
Changing, computing, or copying data in an update
result, then merging changes into the original report.

Totalize Control Line Format
TOT [report f]

Field

Description

report

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

f

Report format in which to
totalize data (enables you to
process data in ﬁelds beyond
column 80 if you do not
already have those columns
on display).

Totalize Statement Format
@TOT,c,d,r[,lab] o cc ltyp,p [vrslts] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

lab

Label to go to if no data on
which to operate is found.

o

Options ﬁeld (see Totalize
Options).

cc

Column-character positions or
names of the ﬁelds to process.

ltyp

Line type to process. If you
specify the A option, you can
leave this subﬁeld blank, but
type the comma following it.

p

Parameters (operators) to use
(see Totalize Parameters).

vrslts

Variables to capture the totals.
If you use this ﬁeld, the
statement does not create a
result.

Totalize Options
Option

Description

A

Processes all line types.

B

Space-ﬁlls duplicate line
occurrences of a subtotal
parameter.

C

Displays a subtotal if the
value in the subtotal key ﬁeld
changes case. For example,
with the C option, if the value
in the key ﬁeld is Rate on the
third line and RATe on the
fourth, a subtotal is shown
after the third line. Use this
option with the S parameter
only.

2200 Þ Þ

C

Displays a subtotal if the value
in the subtotal key ﬁeld
changes case. For example,
with the C option, if the value

in the key ﬁeld is Rate on the
third line and RATe on the
fourth, a subtotal is shown
after the third line. Use this
option with the S parameter
only.
This option applies to full
character set (FCS) reports
only.

Ü Ü 2200

E

Counts entries (see also the R
Option). In the TOT statement,
the ﬁrst variable contains the
entry count.
See Numbering Lines Example.

H

Cumulates horizontally. See
Cumulating with Grand Totals
Example.

I

Ignores the check for headings;
ignores that there are no
heading divider lines in the
report and starts processing on
line 2.

J(x)

Justiﬁes numeric result values
in ﬁelds to x; x is one of the
values listed below. Leading
zeros are displayed to the left
of a value, nonsigniﬁcant zeros
to the right. Neither aﬀect the
value.
c - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros. Inserts
commas every three digits in
the integer portion of the
result, if the ﬁeld is large
enough.
l - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas. Left-justiﬁes values.
r - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas. Right-justiﬁes values.
x - Eliminates leading zeros
and commas, left-justiﬁes
values, and ﬁlls ﬁelds with
nonsigniﬁcant zeros.
z - Eliminates leading and
nonsigniﬁcant zeros and
commas, right-justiﬁes values,
and ﬁlls leading spaces with
zeros.

N

Inserts three blank comment

lines between subtotal groups
and omits the grand total. Can
be used with the S option.

O

Omits all data from the result,
displaying only subtotal and
grand total information. If you
use this option with the S
option, the command places
the vertical subtotal in the
requested ﬁelds, displays the
last line of each subtotal, and
omits the grand total.
See Subtotaling Data Example.

Rn

Rounds numbers to the nearest
n. Use with the E option to
round entry counts. The range
for n is .0000000000000001 to
100000000000.
For example, if you type r.001,
the values are rounded to the
nearest thousandth. If you type
r10000, the values are
rounded to the nearest 10
thousand.
Examples:
With option r.001, the value
8.3224 gives the value of
8.322

With option r.001, the value
8.7719 gives the value of
8.772
With option r10000, the value
12345 gives the value of 1
With option r10000, the value
16345 gives the value of 2
See Rounding Result Values
Example.

S

Places subtotals in vertical
operation ﬁelds. Enables you to
subtotal up to 5 ﬁelds, columns
numbered S1 through S5 in the
function mask. The last line of
each subtotal shows the result.
See Subtotaling Data Example,
Horizontal Computations and
Subtotaling Example,
Combining Two Subtotal Fields
Example, Hierarchical
Subtotaling Example, and
Hierarchical Subtotaling and
Combining Key Example.

=x

Changes the line type indicator
in column 1 to x, where x is an
asterisk, period, or
alphanumeric character. With
the TOT statement, if the only
operation you are performing is

changing the line type, leave
the parameter (p) ﬁeld blank,
but enter two apostrophes,
indicating a blank ﬁeld.

*

Omits all ﬂags that are
otherwise inserted immediately
after the subtotal result of the
operation (but not the grand
total). Subtotals ﬂagged with
an asterisk indicate that at
least one item processed is
blank or nonnumeric.

2200 Þ Þ

*

Omits all ﬂags that are
otherwise inserted
immediately after the subtotal
result of the operation (but not
the grand total). Subtotals
ﬂagged with an asterisk
indicate that at least one item
processed is blank or
nonnumeric.
Subtotals ﬂagged with a space
indicate that all items
processed are valid numeric
data.

Ü Ü 2200

Totalize Parameters
You can perform up to 80 operations in any one parameter
line, and you can use up to two parameter lines.

Parameter Description

+

Add, ﬂag the ﬁeld to operate
on (for example, ﬂag the
ﬁeld to divide into by
placing + in that ﬁeld).

-

Subtract.

*

Multiply.

/

Divide. (In the TOT
statement, enclose the slant
in apostrophes.) Divide by
zero and divide by one
produce the same result, for
example, 5/0 = 5.

=

Total horizontally, ﬁll ﬁelds,
number sequentially.

A

Average. (Blanks are not

considered zeros.) See
Averaging Fields and
Counting Entries Example.

C

Cumulate data or sequence
data lines. See Cumulating
with Grand Totals Example
and Numbering Lines
Example.

D

Delete commas.

E

Count entries and display
the number at the end of
result. See Averaging Fields
and Counting Entries
Example.

I

Insert commas.

M

Copy data. See Copying
Fields.

S

Place subtotals in vertical
operation ﬁelds. The size of
the key ﬁeld can be up to
the length of the report line.

See Subtotaling Data
Example, Horizontal
Computations and
Subtotaling Example, and
Combining Two Subtotal
Fields Example.

S1 - S5

Hierarchical subtotal. The
size of the key ﬁeld can be
up to the length of the
report line.
See Hierarchical Subtotaling
Example and Hierarchical
Subtotaling and Combining
Key Example.

Totalize Outcomes
Executing the Totalize command causes the following
actions to occur:
The command processes the operations and creates a
result.
Results of vertical operations are placed at the end of
the result.
Whenever at least one ﬁeld item processed in a vertical
operation contains nonnumeric data or no value (blank),
an asterisk is placed next to the resulting value as a ﬂag
indicating that some of the data was invalid.
In addition, executing the TOT statement causes the
following actions to occur:
The statement does the requested calculations and
loads the variables with the results. It does not create a
-0 result unless you do not specify variables.
If you use the E option, the ﬁrst variable contains the
entry count.

Totalize Procedures
This topic describes how to
Compute horizontally
Compute horizontally, using constant values
Copy ﬁelds
Fill ﬁelds with speciﬁed data
Display vertical subtotals
Display only the subtotals and grand total
Combine up to ﬁve subtotal key ﬁelds
Subtotal hierarchically
Subtotal hierarchically on combined key ﬁelds
Computing Horizontally
To compute horizontally
1. Type + in the ﬁeld containing the data to add to,
subtract from, multiply, or divide into.
2. Type + in the ﬁeld to add, - in the ﬁeld to subtract, * in
the ﬁeld to multiply by, or / in the ﬁeld to divide by.
3. Type = in the ﬁeld you want to contain the result.
If a ﬁeld is blank and you enter an operator in that ﬁeld, the
Totalize command does not perform calculations on that
ﬁeld.

To receive a grand total at the end of the report, type + in
the line directly below the equal sign in the ﬁeld to be
totaled.

Note: You can perform only one horizontal operation at a
time.
See Multiplying Two Fields Example.
To compute horizontally, using constant values
1. Type the operator (+, -, *, /) and the constant value in
the ﬁeld you want to process. To subtract a constant
value, type a plus sign before the minus sign. For
example, type +-2 to subtract 2 from a ﬁeld.
2. Type = in the ﬁeld you want to contain the result. To
place the result in the same ﬁeld, type = in the line
below the operator in that ﬁeld.

Note: If the constant value includes a decimal, precede the
decimal point with a zero (for example, 0.1).
See Computing Horizontally Example and Computing
Horizontally Using Constant Value Example.
Copying Fields
To copy ﬁelds
1. Type m in the issuing ﬁeld, the ﬁeld to copy.
2. Type = under the ﬁrst asterisk in the receiving ﬁeld, the
ﬁeld you want to contain the data.
The receiving ﬁelds must be the same width as the ﬁeld to
be copied. Add or erase asterisks under the ﬁeld headings
to make the ﬁelds the same size.

See Copying Fields Example.
Filling Fields with Speciﬁc Data
To ﬁll ﬁelds with speciﬁed data,use the = parameter. The =
parameter oﬀsets the speciﬁed data by one character, so
the command considers the following tab in the mask as
part of what you are inserting unless you erase it. See Filling
Fields with Data You Specify Example.
1. Type = under the asterisk of the ﬁrst column in a ﬁeld to
ﬁll.
2. Type the data to be ﬁlled in the ﬁeld.
3. Erase the tab character in the mask that follows the
ﬁeld you are ﬁlling.

Notes:
1. Other ﬁelds cannot have arithmetic or move symbols.
2. To ﬁll a ﬁeld with spaces, type = under the ﬁrst asterisk
in the ﬁeld and erase the next tab character in the
mask.
Displaying Subtotals
To display vertical subtotals, use the S option and the S and
+ parameters to display subtotals vertically at each line
where a speciﬁed ﬁeld, the subtotal key ﬁeld, changes
values.
1. Type s in the options ﬁeld above the mask.
2. Type s in the subtotal key ﬁeld.

3. Type + in one or more ﬁelds you want added. Each time
the value in the key ﬁeld changes, the addition starts
over.
You can also average ﬁelds by using a subtotal key. Simply
use the A parameter instead of the + parameter. The
command averages the data in each group of subtotal keys.
The result includes three lines inserted between each
subtotal key group:
An asterisk line with dashes in the ﬁelds you added or
averaged.
Another asterisk line containing the word SUBTOTAL
below each subtotal key (or part of the word SUBTOTAL
if the key ﬁeld is fewer than eight characters) and the
subtotals or averages under the ﬁelds processed.
A blank period line to separate each subtotal group. Use
the N option to insert three blank lines between groups.
See Subtotaling Data Example and Horizontal Computations
and Subtotaling Example.
To display only the subtotals and grand total, use the O
option with the S option. The last line from each subtotal
key group is included in the result, along with the subtotals
for the ﬁelds.
See Subtotaling Data Example.
Combining Subtotal Key Fields
You can combine up to ﬁve subtotal key ﬁelds so that
whenever the values in any of the ﬁelds change, the
subtotaling starts over. To do this

1. Type s in the options ﬁeld above the mask.
2. Type s under the subtotal key ﬁelds you want to
combine.
3. Type + in one or more ﬁelds you want added. Each time
the values in any of the key ﬁelds change, the addition
starts over.
See Combining Two Subtotal Fields Example.
Subtotaling Hierarchically
To subtotal hierarchically, assign a hierarchy of up to ﬁve
key ﬁelds (S1 through S5). Whenever the value changes in a
lower-level key ﬁeld, such as S2, the subtotal is displayed.
Whenever the value changes in a higher-level key ﬁeld, such
as S1, the subtotal of the previous subtotals is displayed.
If a higher-level key ﬁeld stays the same but a lower-level
key ﬁeld changes, even though a subtotal is displayed, the
subtotaling for the higher-level continues as long as its
value stays the same.
Use the same procedure as other subtotaling operations,
except type s1 through s5 under the key ﬁelds you want to
subtotal hierarchically.
When you use S1 through S5 for subtotal parameters, the
command calculates the subtotals by level. Compare with
multiple sub keys, which always restart the subtotal
whenever the values in any of the key ﬁelds change.
See Hierarchical Subtotaling Example.
To subtotal hierarchically on combined key ﬁelds

Follow the same procedures as for other subtotaling
operations, except place the same hierarchical parameters
in the ﬁelds to combine.
s
* Product . Sub . .Space. Demo . .
* Type . Key . . Req . Quantity . Demo Results .
*=========.=====. . . .
.=====.==========.===============.
s1 s1 s2 +
In this function mask, the ﬁelds Product Type and Sub Key
are combined to form the primary key ﬁeld, and Space Req
is designated as the secondary key ﬁeld.
To see the equivalent statement and a partial result, see
Hierarchical Subtotaling and Combining Key Example.

and the vertical total placed in variables, use the E option
and add variables to the statement.
@tot,'report1c' e 42-7 |,+ <entries>i4,<sum>i7 .

Filling Fields with Data You Specify Example
This example ﬁlls the Product Type ﬁeld with spaces, the
third character of the Space Req ﬁeld with 5, the Demo
Quantity ﬁeld with left-justiﬁed abcd, and the last character
of the Demo Results ﬁeld with 1.
This example uses a vertical bar to indicate tabs not erased
from the function mask and tabs that are included in the
result. Since you did not erase the tab following the string
abcd in the mask, an extra tab is included in the result.
Function Mask
* Product . .Space. Demo . .
* Type . . Req .Quantity. Demo Results .
*=========. . . .
.=====.========.===============.
********* ***** ******** ************* *
|= |= 5 =abcd | =1
Equivalent Statement

Note: The erasing of tabs does not apply in the statement,
and an extra tab will not follow abcd.
@tot,'report1c' '' 2-9,50-5,56-8,79-1 |,=,'=
5',=abcd,=1 .
Partial Result
* Product . .Space. Demo . .
* Type . . Req .Quantity. Demo Results .
*=========. . . .
.=====.========.===============.
| | | 5|abcd | 1

| | | 5|abcd | 1
| | | 5|abcd | 1

total starts over.

Requests a subtotaling
operation.

Parameters Used

s

Identiﬁes the subtotal key
ﬁeld.

+

Identiﬁes the ﬁeld to divide
into.

/

Identiﬁes the ﬁeld to divide by.

=

Identiﬁes the ﬁeld to place
cumulated division results.

Hierarchical Subtotaling and Combining Key
Example
Follow the same procedures as other subtotaling operations,
except place the same hierarchical parameters in the ﬁelds
to combine.
This example combines the Product Type and Sub Key ﬁelds
and designates them as the primary key ﬁeld. The Space
Req ﬁeld is designated as the secondary key ﬁeld.
Function Mask
s
* Product . Sub . .Space. Demo . .
* Type . Key . . Req . Quantity . Demo Results .
*=========.=====. . . .
.=====.==========.===============.
s1 s1 s2 +
Equivalent Statement
@tot,'report1c' s 2-9,12-5,50-5,56-8 |,s1,s1,s2,+
.
Partial Result
* Product . Sub .Produc. Whole . Retail . Sales
.Space. Demo .
* Type . Key . Cost . Sale$ . $$$$ .Commiss. Req
.Quantity.
*=========.=====.======.=======.========.=======.
=====.========.
BLACKBOX1 A 13500 16875 23625 2362.50 100 1
*--------- ----- ----- -------* 100 1

*BLACKBOX1 A 1
.
BLACKBOX2 A 13600 17000 23800 2380.00 110 2
*--------- ----- ----- -------* 110 2
*BLACKBOX2 A 2
.
BLACKBOX3 A 13700 17125 23975 2397.50 120 4
*--------- ----- ----- -------* 120 4
*BLACKBOX3 A 4
.

@TPC (Client Interface)
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the Client Interface (TPC) statement to develop Open
Distributed Transaction Processing clients. The TPC
statement uses the Application Transaction Message
Interface (ATMI) to provide for client/server application
development. The software does all the buﬀer management
and parameter handling necessary to work within the Open
Distributed Transaction Processing environment.
In general, a client calls on services, but does not provide
services to other clients. A service can also use many of the
TPC options to call other services. In this section on the TPC
statement, client refers to an actual client or to a service
that is acting as a client by calling another service.
For more information on Open Distributed Transaction
Processing servers, see Provided Servers.
The Open Distributed Transaction Processing Transaction
Manager must be running.

Client Interface Quick Reference
Enabling an Actual Client to Join an Open Distributed
Transaction Processing Application
@TPC,I[,flags,lab]
config[,size,user,client,password,reserved, extra
data] .
Managing an Open Distributed Transaction
Processing or Global Transaction
@TPC,B[,,lab,timeout] .
@TPC,C[,,lab] .
@TPC,A[,,lab] .
Enabling a Client to Call Services
@TPC,W[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,
input_data output_data] .
@TPC,N[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
@TPC,R,[flags],[lab],service_id [output_data] .
@TPC,M[,,lab] [output_data] .
Enabling a Client to Carry on a Conversation with a
Service
@TPC,U[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .

@TPC,T,[flags],[lab],conversation_id[,c,d,r]
buffer_type[,size,
input_data] .

[output_data] .
@TPC,D,,[lab],conversation_id .

Messages

[client_id],[user_id],
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
@TPC,Q [buffer_type] [type_found] .
Writing a Message in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing Central Event Log

Client Interface Statement Fields
This table describes some of the statement ﬁelds and
subﬁelds that are common regardless of the TPC option you
use. Other ﬁelds, and variations in descriptions of these
ﬁelds, are described under the appropriate option section.

Field

Description

ﬂags

Flag values to send to
Open Distributed
Transaction Processing
(see Client Interface
Flag Values).

lab

Label to go to if an error
occurs (see Client
Interface Reserved
Words).

conversation_id Identiﬁer assigned to
this conversation by a
previous request using
the U option.

c,d,r

A report from which to
read the data deﬁned
for input_data.

service_name

Name of the service
being called. Entries in
this ﬁeld are casesensitive.

service_id

Service_id number
returned in STAT2$ by a
previous request using
the N option.

buﬀer_type

One of the following
buﬀer types to send to
the service. Entries in
this ﬁeld must be
uppercase.
STRING
CARRAY (or X_OCTET)
X_OCTET is the only
buﬀer type supported by
the software in the
X/Open environment. It
is mandatory for
interoperating with 2200
Open Distributed
Transaction Processing.
FML
NULL
MAPPER_REPORT

size

Number of bytes needed
for the extra data. When
using the I option,
default = 500.
When using the other
options, the buﬀer size
is required for STRING,
CARRAY (or X_OCTET),
or MAPPER_REPORT
input_data (not required
for FML or NULL). If you
leave this ﬁeld blank
and ﬁll in the report
(c,d,r), the statement
calculates the buﬀer
size required; otherwise,
default = 500 bytes.

input_data

Data sent as input to the
service being called.

output_data

Data sent as output to
the service being called.

Joining an Open Distributed Transaction
Processing Application
Use the I option to join the run to the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing application speciﬁed by TUXCONFIG.
This is required only if security is used or if the Linux or
UNIX environment variable, TUXCONFIG, needs to be set;
otherwise, the system uses the current value of TUXCONFIG
the ﬁrst time you issue any other TPC statement.
If you are already attached to an Open Distributed
Transaction Processing application, using the I option causes
you to leave the current application before joining the new
application. When a run attached to an Open Distributed
Transaction Processing application exits, it also leaves the
System/T application.
@TPC,I [,flags,lab]
config[,size,user,client,password,reserved, extra
data] .

Field

Description

conﬁg

To set a new environment
variable TUXCONFIG, enter a
value. To use the current
TUXCONFIG setting, enter
two apostrophes (''). Also, a
Business Information Server
workstation client can set a
new WSENVFILE
environment variable by

entering a value in the
conﬁg ﬁeld.

size

Number of bytes needed for
the extra data. Default =
500.

user

Open Distributed Transaction
Processing user name.

client

Open Distributed Transaction
Processing client name.

password

Open Distributed Transaction
Processing password for
level two security.

reserved

Reserved for future use
(group name).

extra data

Extra data added to the
TPINIT buﬀer for level three
security. Enter extra data to
the TPINIT buﬀer in the
same manner as CARRAY
input_data.

Client Interface Options
Use the B option to begin a global transaction, which is
needed only when accessing XA-compliant databases.
@TPC,B[,,lab,timeout] .
where timeout is the time limit for the transaction. If the
transaction does not complete in this amount of time, the
transaction aborts. Default = system default value
conﬁgured by the Open Distributed Transaction Processing
application.
Use the C option to commit a transaction, which is valid only
after using the B option.
@TPC,C[,,lab] .
Use the A option to abort a transaction, which is valid only
after using the B option.
@TPC,A[,,lab] .
Use the W option to call a service and wait for a reply.
@TPC,W[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size, input_data
output_data] .
Use the N option to call a service and continue. Retrieve the
response, using the R option. You can also use this option or
call services that do not return a response. STAT2$ contains
the service-id handling the request.
@TPC,N[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .

Use the R option to retrieve a reply.
@TPC,R,[flags],[lab],service_id [output_data] .
where service_id is the service_id number returned in
STAT2$ by a previous request using the N option.
Use the M option to retrieve values from a previous call,
using the W, R, L, or Q options that were not retrieved on
the original TPC statement and were not already retrieved
by a previous call using the M option.
@TPC,M[,,lab] [output_data] .
Use the U option to begin a conversation with the server
speciﬁed and, optionally, send the initial message.
@TPC,U[,flags,lab,,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
Use the T option to send a message to the conversational
service speciﬁed by conversation_id. The conversation_id
was returned in STAT2$ by a previous command, using the U
option to begin the conversation.
@TPC,T,[flags],[lab],conversation_id[,c,d,r]
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
Use the L option to receive a message from the
conversational service speciﬁed by conversation_id.
@TPC,L,[flags],[lab],conversation_id
[output_data] .
Use the D option to stop the conversation.
@TPC,D,,[lab],conversation_id .

Use the X option to broadcast an unsolicited message to the
registered clients within the system that match all nonwild
card identiﬁers speciﬁed for machine_id, client_id, and
user_id.
@TPC,X[,flags,lab,,c,d,r] [machine_id],
[client_id],[user_id],
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .

Field

Description

machine_id Logical machine identiﬁer.
Default = wild card identiﬁer
that matches all machine_id
values.

client_id

Client name. Default = wild
card identiﬁer that matches
all client names.

user_id

User name. Default = wild
card identiﬁer that matches
all user names.

Use the Q option to retrieve the ﬁrst unsolicited message of
buﬀer_type. The statement puts the unsolicited message
into the current buﬀer. Use the M option to retrieve the data
from the current buﬀer. When that processing is complete,
use the Q option again to retrieve the next unsolicited
message.

@TPC,Q [buffer_type] [type_found] .

Field

Description

buﬀer_type Buﬀer type (STRING,
CARRAY, X_OCTET, FML) of
the message desired. To use
any buﬀer type, type two
apostrophes ('').

type_found Buﬀer type of this message.

Use the J option to write a message in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing central event log.
@TPC,J[,,lab] 'text message' .
where 'text message' is the quoted literal string or a
variable.

Client Interface Guidelines
This topic discusses
Data formatting
Input data
Output data
FML ﬁelds
Data Formatting
The following table describes the types of data translation
provided by the TPC and TPS statements. These use three
buﬀer types provided by Open Distributed Transaction
Processing: STRING, CARRAY (or X_OCTET), and FML.

Type

Description

STRING

Single null terminated
string. If the input is
from a report, the
statement passes all
text following the
heading divider line
(until terminated by
either the end of the
report or a semicolon)
as a single string. The
semicolon must be the

last nonspace
character on the line.

CARRAY (or
X_OCTET)

Assumes the buﬀer is a
series of NULL
terminated strings. If
the input is from a
report, the statement
treats all text following
the heading divider
line (until terminated
by either the end of
the report or a
semicolon) as a single
string. The statement
puts all strings in the
report into the buﬀer
as NULL terminated
strings.

FML

Flexible format that
enables a variety of
data types and
multiple occurrences of
ﬁelds within the buﬀer.

NULL

Format for sending no
buﬀer.

MAPPER_REPORT Format for sending
reports. It is a CARRAY

buﬀer, where the
statement treats each
line of the input report
as a string beginning
with line 2. The
statement sends all
lines in the report.

X_OCTET is the only buﬀer type supported by the software
in the X/Open environment. It is mandatory for
interoperating with 2200 Open Distributed Transaction
Processing.
Input Data from Variables
The following table describes the actions taken for each
type of data.

Type

Description

STRING

The statement expects
one text string, which
it places in the buﬀer
as a NULL terminated
string.

CARRAY (or
The statement places
X_OCTET) and
each value (separated
MAPPER_REPORT by commas) into the
buﬀer as NULL

terminated string, one
after another.

FML

Express FML values in
the following form:
[(fml_ﬁeld)=]value,...
where fml_ﬁeld is one
of the FML format
speciﬁcations. See FML
Fields.
Default = type 5 (NULL
terminated string) and
ﬁeld numbers
generated
automatically starting
with ﬁeld number 1.
To specify multiple
occurrences for the
same ﬁeld, follow this
example:
(RTK)=v1,v2,v3,
(GWO)=v4,v5
The statement places
three occurrences of
ﬁeld RTK and two
occurrences of ﬁeld
GWO into the FML
buﬀer.

Input Data from a Report
The statement ﬁrst accepts input from the command line
and then from the input report. If the buﬀer size is not
speciﬁed in the statement, it is calculated as follows:
(characters-per-line + 1) * number-of-lines-in-the-report The
following table describes the actions taken for each type of
data (buﬀer_type).

Type

Description

STRING

The statement
transfers the input
report into the buﬀer
beginning at the line
following the heading
divider line (until
terminated by either
the end of the report or
a semicolon-the
semicolon must be the
last nonspace
character on the line).

CARRAY (or
X_OCTET)

The statement
transfers the input
report into the buﬀer
as a series of strings
beginning at the line
following the heading
divider line (until
terminated by either

the end of the report or
a semicolon-the
semicolon must be the
last nonspace
character on the line).
The statement puts all
strings in the report
into the buﬀer as NULL
terminated strings.
The statement places
only one trailing space
in the buﬀer on any
lines not terminated
with a semicolon. For
example: xxx
yyy;
zzz
AA
is
xxx yyy;
zzz AA

MAPPER_REPORT The statement
transfers the input
report into the buﬀer
as a series of NULL
terminated strings
beginning with the line
following the date line
of the report and
continuing until the
end of the report. The

statement treats each
line of the report as a
string.

FML

The statement places
any FML ﬁelds
speciﬁed on the
command line without
a value in a list. For
example, it places
(RTK) in a list but not
(RTK)=5.
In the input report,
values for FML ﬁelds
must be delineated by
a beginning and
ending tab code. The
statement reads lines
from the report until it
ﬁnds a value for each
FML ﬁeld in the list.
If the value found is
nonblank, the
statement places it in
the FML buﬀer as an
occurrence of that FML
ﬁeld. To pack trailing
spaces, type a reverse
slant (\) as the last
nonspace character on
a line.

Once the statement
ﬁnds values for all FML
ﬁelds, it repeats the
process starting with
the next line in the
report and continues
until all lines are read.
When the statement
reaches the end of the
report, it places any
nonblank values found
in the FML buﬀer.

Output Data
Output data (output_data) is data that is
Returned from a service to a client
Sent as a request to a service
Received by a listener during a conversation
Output Data in Variables
The following table describes actions taken for each type of
data (buﬀer_type).

Action

Description

STRING

The statement returns a
single text string. If the
string is larger than the

variable, the statement
truncates it.

CARRAY (or
X_OCTET)

The statement returns
multiple text strings (one
string per variable) until
the buﬀer is empty or the
list of variables speciﬁed
is exhausted. If a string is
larger than the receiving
variable, the statement
truncates it.

FML

If you do not use an FML
format speciﬁcation on
the ﬁrst output_data
ﬁeld, the statement
returns values from the
FML buﬀer starting with
the ﬁrst occurrence of
the lowest ﬁeld_id and
continuing until the last
occurrence of the highest
ﬁeld_id. The statement
moves to the next
ﬁeld_id when all
occurrences of a ﬁeld_id
are exhausted.
If the ﬁrst ﬁeld does not
have an FML format
speciﬁcation, a
subsequent FML format

speciﬁcation causes the
run to fail.
To specify multiple
occurrences for the same
ﬁeld, follow this example:
v1=(RTK),v2,v3,v4=
(GWO),v5
The statement places the
ﬁrst three occurrences of
the ﬁeld named RTK into
variables v1, v2, and v3
and the ﬁrst two
occurrences of GWO into
variables v4 and v5.

Output Data in the Output Area
If you do not specify variables for the values returned from a
service, the statement places the values in the output area.
The following table describes actions taken for each type of
data (buﬀer_type).

Action

Description

STRING

The statement places
one text string in the
output area. If the length
exceeds the line length
of the drawer, it wraps

the string to the next
line.

CARRAY (or
X_OCTET)

The statement places
each character string in
the output area as a new
line. If the length
exceeds the line length
of the drawer, it wraps
the string to the next
line.

FML

You must place an output
line following the TPC or
TPS statement, otherwise
the run will fail.
The output line consists
of place holders and
character constants. Use
one of the following
formats to specify a
place holder: (sz) or (*sz)
where sz deﬁnes the size
of the ﬁeld in the output
area. If zero, the
statement uses the size
of the ﬁeld deﬁned in the
FML buﬀer. If sz is
smaller than the actual
FML ﬁeld size, the
statement left-justiﬁes
and truncates the ﬁeld. If

sz is larger than the
actual FML ﬁeld size, the
statement left-justiﬁes
and space-ﬁlls the ﬁeld.
The asterisk (*) in the
format serves as a repeat
ﬂag indicating the last
occurrence of this ﬁeld is
to be repeated until all
occurrences of the other
ﬁelds are retrieved.
If you do not supply a
place holder for a
requested ﬁeld, the
statement adds the value
for that ﬁeld to the end
of the output line using
the default size. Each line
put into the output area
will have the next
available occurrence of
the requested ﬁeld in the
order speciﬁed on the
TPC statement.
If you do not use the
repeat ﬂag in any of the
place holders, the
statement changes the
line type of all lines in
the output area after the
ﬁrst line to asterisk type
(*). If you use the repeat

ﬂag, the line type does
not change.
If an output line exceeds
the line length of the
output area, the
statement wraps the line.

FML Fields
Use one of the following methods to specify an FML ﬁeld.
Field name must be a valid ﬁeld name deﬁned in one
of the ﬁeldtbl ﬁles listed in TUXCONFIG in an Open
Distributed Transaction Processing application. Field
names must begin with an alphabetic character. In the
following example, RTK is the ﬁeld name and value is a
literal or variable specifying the value of RTK.
(RTK)=value
Field id is a combination of the ﬁeld type and ﬁeld
number. In the following example, 40961 is the
combined ﬁeld type and ﬁeld number and value is a
literal or variable specifying the value of the ﬁeld.
(40961)=value
Field type is one of the following:
0-Short
1-Long
2-Character
3-Single precision ﬂoat
4-Double precision ﬂoat
5-NULL terminated string

Field number is an integer value associated with the
ﬁeld when it is deﬁned in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing application environment. In the
following example, 5 is the ﬁeld type, 1 is the ﬁeld
number, and value is a literal or variable specifying the
value of the ﬁeld.
(5,1)=value

Client Interface Examples
This example places all the occurrences of RTK in the ﬁrst
ﬁeld starting at column 2 for ﬁve characters. A constant
ﬁeld containing xyz starts in column 8 for three characters.
All occurrences of GWO are in the second ﬁeld starting at
column 12 for six characters.
The ﬁrst line in the output area is a space type line. The
remaining lines in the output area are asterisk type (*) lines.
The statement places lines in the output area until it
retrieves all occurrences for all ﬁelds. It space-ﬁlls any ﬁelds
where there is no occurrence.
@tpc,w service_id,buffer_type,size,value1 (RTK),
(GWO) .
(5) xyz (6)
This example produces the same result as the ﬁrst example,
except the last occurrence of RTK is repeated on every line
until the statement retrieves the last occurrence of GWO. All
lines in the output area are space type lines.
@tpc,w service_id,buffer_type,size,value1 (RTK),
(GWO) .
(*5) xyz (6)
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@TPS (Server Interface)
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the Server Interface (TPS) statement to develop Open
Distributed Transaction Processing servers. The TPS
statement uses the Application Transaction Message
Interface (ATMI) to provide for client/server application
development. The software does all the buﬀer management
and parameter handling necessary to work within the Open
Distributed Transaction Processing environment.
In general, a server provides a service, but it can also use
the TPC command to call other services. In this section on
the TPS statement, server refers to the operations provided
by the TPS statement, not to the operations when a server
is acting as a client by calling another service.
For more information on Open Distributed Transaction
Processing servers, see Provided Servers.
The server must be conﬁgured in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing Transaction Manager. This statement
is usable only in the MAPPER server (TPMAPPER).

Server Interface Quick Reference
Enabling the Services to Receive and Return
Requests from Clients
@TPS,P,[,lab output_data] .
@TPS,R[,flags,lab,c,d,r] [result_status],
[usercode],
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
@TPS,F[,flags,lab,c,d,r]
service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
@TPS,M[,,lab] [output_data] .
Enabling the Services to Change Their Service Names
Dynamically
@TPS,A[,,lab] 'service_name' .
@TPS,U[,,lab] 'service_name' .
Enabling a Service to Participate in a Conversation
with a Client
@TPS,T[,flags,lab,c,d,r]
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
@TPS,L[,flags,lab] [output_data] .
Writing a Message in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing Central Event Log
@TPS,J[,,lab] 'text message' .

Enabling a Service to Send Unsolicited Messages to

Server Interface Options
Use the P option to suspend the service until activated by
Bulletin Board. This request marks the end of the
initialization stage of the server run. Server execution
begins on the line immediately following this request.
@TPS,P,[,lab output_data] .
Use the R option to return the result of this service request
to the client.

service_name,buffer_type[,size,input_data] .

Use the M option to retrieve values from any buﬀer type.

Use the A option to advertise service_name (a quoted literal
string or a variable) as a service.
@TPS,A[,,lab] 'service_name' .
Use the U option to unadvertise service_name as a service.
The service_name can be a quoted literal string or a
variable.
@TPS,U[,,lab] 'service_name' .
Use the T option to talk (send) during a conversation with
the client.
@TPS,T[,flags,lab,c,d,r]
buffer_type[,size,input_data] .
Use the L option to listen (receive) during a conversation
with a client.
@TPS,L[,flags,lab] [output_data] .
Use the J option to write a message in the Open Distributed
Transaction Processing central event log.

where 'text message'is the quoted literal string or a
variable.

registered clients within the system that match all nonwild
card identiﬁers speciﬁed for machine_id, client_id, and
user_id.

Server Interface Guidelines
