Provided Servers
Note: See the related Software Release Announcement for
information on supported levels.
The Open Distributed Transaction Processing server provides
services that enable a client to access Informix or Oracle
databases. The services provided may be part of a local or
global transaction and have the following in common:
All are called using the W or N options for the TPC
statement.
All expect an FML buﬀer as input to the service. Route =
is used to route data to correct server. Info = data is
needed by the service.
STAT2$ contains database error status.
The following describes the services provided by the Open
Distributed Transaction Processing server.

Service

Description

MPR_COL Retrieve the description of a
table.

MPR_TBL

Retrieve a list of named
reports in the system.

MPR_SQL

Send SQL statements to the

database.

To Retrieve the Description of a Table
Use the MPR_COL service. It returns a description of the
table speciﬁed in the same format as the Data Deﬁnition
Information (DDI) statement. The following example shows a
synchronous call to retrieve a description of the table
gwo.houses.
@tpc,w,,99 'MPR_COL','FML',,(route)=Informix,
(info)='gwo.houses' .

99

Label to pass control to if
an error occurs.

MPR_COL

Name of the service to
call.

FML

Type of buﬀer
(buﬀer_type) to send the
service.

'route'

FML name (used to direct
the request to the correct
service). Use an FML
name if you need datadependent routing.

'info'

FML buﬀer (must be
present for services to

work).

'gwo.houses' Name of the table for
which a description is
retrieved. You can also use
the format
owner_id.tablename or
tablename.

To Retrieve a List of Named Reports
Use the MPR_TBL service. The following example shows a
synchronous call to retrieve a list of all tables that are public
or have an owner_id of gwo.
@tpc,w,99 'MPR_TBL','FML',,(route)=Informix,
(info)='gwo'

99

Label to pass control to if an
error occurs.

MPR_TBL

Name of the service to call.

FML

Type of buﬀer (buﬀer_type)
to send the service.

'route'

FML ﬁeld name (used to
direct the request to the
correct service). Use an FML
name if you need datadependent routing.

'info'

FML buﬀer (must be present
for services to work).

'gwo'

An asterisk (*) to select all
the tables in the database.

An 'owner id' to select only
those tables that have an
owner_id equal to the
speciﬁed owner_id or have a
table attribute of public.

To Send SQL Statements to the Database
Use the MPR_SQL service. This service sends SQL requests
to the Informix database. If you send multiple SQL
statements on a single call, the statement processes all
statements before it returns any results. If a fatal Informix
error occurs, all processing stops and STAT2$ contains the
Informix error code. You can also send multiple Select
statements on an MPR_SQL service call.

Receiving Select Data
If you specify output_data on the TPC statement, the
statement returns the data in variables; otherwise, it places
the data in the output area. Each line in the output area
contains the selected columns from the corresponding row
in the database. If the columns selected are wider than the
line length of the current output area, the statement wraps
the data to the next line. Also, it writes the string END
SELECT DATA at the end of the data returned for each Select
statement.
In the following example, the ﬁrst Break (BRK) statement
clears the output area. The TPC W option with no
output_data speciﬁcation places the results of the SQL
statement in the output area. The second BRK statement
closes the output area and makes it the current result (-0).
@brk
@tpc,w,,99 'MPR_SQL','FML',,(route)='Informix',
(info)='SQL statement' .
@brk

99

Label to pass control to if
an error occurs.

MPR_SQL

Name of the service to call.

FML

Type of buﬀer (buﬀer_type)
to send the service.

'SQL
statement'

SQL statement to send to
the Informix database.

MAPPER Server
The MAPPER Server (MAPSVR) command provides services
that you can use in the Open Distributed Transaction
Processing environment. The following table describes these
services.

Service

Description

MPR_COL

Provides a description of a
report.

MPR_TBL

Provides a list of named
reports available.

MPR_SQL

Provides an SQL interface
into the software using the
MQL statement.

These services accept FML buﬀers with values in the FML
info ﬁeld.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS

@TRC (Trace Relational Syntax)
Use the Trace Relational Syntax (TRC) statement to save the
SQL syntax generated by statements.
Before using the TRC statement, you must ﬁrst establish
communication with a database by using the Log On to
Relational Database (LGN) statement.
The TRC statement saves the SQL syntax generated by MRI
statements in a report. The TRC statement terminates when
the run executes either a Log Oﬀ Relational Database (LGF)
statement or another TRC statement that does not contain
values in the c,d,r subﬁelds.

Trace Relational Syntax Statement Format
@TRC[,c,d,r,db,...,db] .

Notes:
If a cabinet and drawer are not speciﬁed, replace the
ﬁeld with one comma on the OS 2200 system and two
commas on Windows Server, Windows Client, Linux, or
UNIX systems.
2200 Þ Þ
You must specify a full character set (FCS) drawer.
Ü Ü 2200

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report containing the syntax.
If you do not enter values in
these subﬁelds, the statement
terminates the previously
issued TRC statement for the
speciﬁed database.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

db

Database name.

You can specify multiple
database names. For example,
this statement starts saving the
syntax sent to the Salary
database and stops saving the
syntax to the Payroll database:

@trc,0,a,3,salary,,,payroll .
Default = default database
name on your system.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

db

Database name.
You can specify multiple
database names. For example,
this statement starts saving the
syntax sent to the Salary
database and stops saving the
syntax to the Payroll database:

@trc,0,a,3,salary,,,payroll .
Default = database running with
MRIMID set to 1. See your
administrator for more
information.

Ü Ü 2200

Trace Relational Syntax Examples
This example saves SQL syntax in the report identiﬁed in
the statement: @trc,0,a,5,oracle .
This example terminates a previously issued TRC statement:
@trc,,,oracle .

TW (Terminal Width)
2200 Þ Þ
Use the Terminal Width (TW) command to set the display
width of SVT-1120, SVT-1121, or PWT-120 terminals to 80 or
132 columns.
The terminal width must also be set in the control page of
the SVT-1120, SVT-1121, or PWT-120 terminal.
The command toggles the display width between 80 and
132 characters.

Terminal Width Control Line Format
TW {80|132}
where {80³132} is the number of columns for terminal
width.
Ü Ü 2200

@TXT (Deﬁne Text Box)
Use the Deﬁne Text Box (TXT) statement to deﬁne a text box
and display it on the user screen.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.
The TXT statement displays text on the screen with
information. Text boxes are for display purposes only; the
statement returns no data.

Deﬁne Text Box Statement Formats
Format 1
@TXT[,c,d,r,l,q,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,fc/bc,o]
[vth] .
Format 2
@TXT[,"text",vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,fc/bc,o]
[vth] .

Note: In both formats, all tab characters are converted to
spaces.

Field

Description

c,d,r

(Format 1 only.) Report
containing the text to be
displayed.

l

(Format 1 only.) Line in the
report at which to start
reading lines. Default = 1.

q

(Format 1 only.) Number of
lines to read from the report.
Default = 1.

"text"

(Format 2 only.) Text to appear

in the text box. If you do not
specify text, place two
quotation marks in this ﬁeld.
To begin a new line, use a
slant (/) character.
The system translates
variables as follows:
If enclosed in apostrophes,
no translation takes place.
If enclosed in quotation
marks, the statement
translates the variable to
its contents.

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window or text box handle.
Default = main window.
If you specify a window
handle, the system places the
text box in that window, and
all vertical and horizontal
coordinates are relative to that
window.
If you specify a text box
handle, the system assumes
that you are changing that
text box in some way. If you do
not specify a value in a
subﬁeld, the system uses the

original values except for the
following:
To change the data, you
must either change the c,
d, r, l, and q subﬁelds in
Format 1, or change the
text subﬁeld in Format 2.
If you specify any of the
vert, hort, vsiz, and hsiz
subﬁelds, you must specify
all of them because the
system uses zero for the
ones not speciﬁed.
The system uses the
original options except for
the I option, which adds
the input property to the
text box. If the I option is
missing, the input property
from the text is removed.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the text box
using a positioning unit (or row
number). See @WIN (Deﬁne
Window Display) for more
information on sizing units.
When using a row number, the
position is based on the main
run font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts. If
you are specifying a value for

the vertical position, you must
also specify values in the hort,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit or
row number).

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the text
box using a positioning unit (or
column number). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When using a column
number, the position is based
on the main run font. See
@FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you are
specifying a value for the
horizontal position, you must
also specify values in the vert,
vsiz, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 1 (positioning unit or
row number).

vsiz

Vertical size of the text box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). See @WIN
(Deﬁne Window Display) for
more information on sizing
units. When using row
numbers, the size is based on
the text run font. See @FON

(Font) for more information on
fonts. If you are specifying a
value for the vertical size, you
must also specify values in the
vert, hort, and hsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 800 positioning units
(or 1 row).

hsiz

Horizontal size of the text box
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or column numbers). See
@WIN (Deﬁne Window
Display) for more information
on sizing units. When using
column numbers, the size is
based on the text run font. See
@FON (Font) for more
information on fonts. If you are
specifying a value for the
horizontal size, you must also
specify values in the vert, hort,
and vsiz subﬁelds.
Default = 2800 positioning
units (or 7 columns).

fc/bc

Foreground and background
colors for this text box. If you
specify only one color
(omitting the slant), that color
is used for both foreground
and background. See Set

Default Color Codes for the
available colors.

o

Options. See Deﬁne Text Box
Options. Default = L.

vth

Variable to capture the text
box handle. This must be a sixcharacter variable.

Deﬁne Text Box Options
Option

Description

B

Border.

C

Center.

I

Input text. If you click this text
box, the run resumes and is
processed accordingly.

L

Left-justiﬁed.

R

Right-justiﬁed.

S

Send immediately. If you do
not use this option, the
system does not send the
button until the current block
is transferred; this results in
increased performance. If you
do not want the workstation to
wait for the block transfer,
however, you must use the S
option.

T

Displays the text with a
transparent background.
Notes:
If a background color is
speciﬁed with the T
option, the background
color is ignored.
The T option is not
compatible with the B
option or the 3 option.

Y

Allows the sizing of the control
to be in row and column units,
independent of the units used
for placement.

3

Displays the text box with 3D
eﬀects.

Note: The 3 option is not
compatible with the T option.

Deﬁne Text Box Colors
See Set Default Color Codes for a list of valid colors that can
be used with the Deﬁne Text Box statement.

Deﬁne Text Box Guideline
Follow this guideline when using the TXT statement: You can
have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT, LST, PIC,
TXT, and WIN controls concurrently open.

Note: The actual number of handles created can be lower
depending on workstation PC memory conﬁguration and
usage.

Deﬁne Text Box Example
This example uses the TXT statement to display a text box.
@txt,"printing in progress",
<ha1>,02,02,03,22,whi/red,c <print>i6 .

"printing in
progress"

Display the words
"printing in progress" in
the box.

<ha1>

Display the text box in
the window whose
handle is contained in
<ha1>.

02,02

Create the text box at
row 2, column 2 of the
window.

03,22

Make the text box three
lines long and twenty
characters wide.

whi/red

Color the foreground of
the box white, and the
background red.

c

Center the text in the
box.

<print>i6

Place the window handle
for this box in the
variable <print>.

@ULK (Unlock)
Use the Unlock (ULK) statement to release update control on
a report that was previously set by the Update Lock (LOK)
statement.
The statement releases update control so that other users
can update the report.
Use this format:
@ULK .

UNDO
Use the Undo command to restore your data to its state
before the execution of the last command used. For
example, if you delete a line in a report, the Undo command
restores the line. The Undo command is especially useful for
the following tasks:
Restore a report you just deleted.
Undo the last update you made to a report.
Restore a previously displayed result.
You must execute a command for which the Undo command
is available.
Use this format:
UNDO
Or press Undo.

UNIX and @UNX (UNIX Interface)
Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Use the UNIX Interface (UNIX) function or UNX statement to
access the Linux or UNIX operating system and execute
Linux or UNIX commands.
Executing the UNIX manual function causes the following
actions to occur: Terminal or Terminal Emulator
The shell prompt is displayed if you entered the system
from the Linux or UNIX shell.
If you entered the system using the MAPPER log-on, a
Linux or UNIX log-on prompt is displayed.
Workstation
Only the -f option is supported (returns output of a shell
command as a result).
Executing the UNX statement causes the following actions
to occur:
Terminal or Terminal Emulator
If the run user entered the system through the Linux or
UNIX shell, the user can execute any Linux or UNIX
command normally available through that log-on. When
the Linux or UNIX command completes, the run
continues.
If the run user entered the system directly from the
Linux or UNIX log-on prompt (with a log-on of MAPPER),
the run terminates with an error. (The MAPPER log-on

provides access only to the software.) When the
command completes, the run continues.
Workstation
Only the -f option is supported (returns output of a shell
command as a result).
This functionality should be used only by experienced Linux
or UNIX users.

UNIX Interface Control Line Format
UNIX [o cmd args]

Field

Description

o

Options ﬁeld (see UNIX
Interface Options).

cmd

Linux or UNIX command to be
executed; a standard Linux or
UNIX search is done to ﬁnd the
command. If the path to the
command is deﬁned in your
shell variable PATH, the full
path name is not required.

args

Arguments to pass to the
Linux or UNIX command; full
path names are required only
if the Linux or UNIX command
requires them.

UNIX Interface Statement Format
@UNX[,c,d,logon,pw,lab] o cmd [args].

Field

Description

c,d

Drawer into which the result is
to be returned (-f option). For
more details, see Specifying
Reports or Drawers to Process.

logon

Linux or UNIX log-on. Default
= Linux or UNIX log-on of the
current run user.

pw

Logon password. Default =
Linux or UNIX log-on password
of the current run user.

lab

Label to go to if the Linux or
UNIX command fails.

o

Options ﬁeld (see UNIX
Interface Options). Enter
options in lowercase
characters. If you do not
specify options, you must

designate the o ﬁeld with two
apostrophes ('').

cmd

Linux or UNIX command to be
performed. Standard Linux or
UNIX searching is done to ﬁnd
the command. If the path to
the command is deﬁned in
your shell variable PATH, you
do not need to enter the full
path name.
Enclose commands containing
embedded spaces in
apostrophes ('). If you do not
specify a command, designate
its position with two
apostrophes ('').
Business Information Server
cannot determine whether the
Linux or UNIX syntax speciﬁed
in this ﬁeld is valid or if it
encountered an error during
execution. You need to analyze
the generated output to be
sure the execution was
successful.

args

Arguments passed to the
Linux or UNIX command. Enter
full path names only if the
command requires them.
When entering arguments,

enclose the command and
argument in apostrophes.

UNIX Interface Options
Option

Description

-c

With the UNX statement,
clears the screen after the
statement ﬁnishes processing
and returns to the system.
(The manual UNIX Interface
function does this
automatically.)

-f

Returns the output of the
command as a result in
drawer A or, if you request
another drawer using the UNX
statement, the requested
drawer. If you enter a ﬁle
name immediately following
the -f option, the command
places a copy of the output
from the Linux or UNIX
command into the named ﬁle.
(Do not use this option with
the -w option.) Do not use the
-f option when executing an
interactive Linux or UNIX
process, such as vi and ed.

-p

Executes the user's .proﬁle

before executing the Linux or
UNIX command.

-w

With the UNX statement,
enables the run user to press
Resume (or Return, if the
run user is on a Graphical
Interface for Business
Information Server
workstation using Ethernet) to
resume the run once the
command is executed and the
output is displayed. (Do not
use this option with the -f
option.)

UNIX Interface Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To return to the system, press Ctrl-D or type exit and
press the Return key. The active screen is displayed.
Linux and UNIX commands, arguments, and ﬁle names
are case-sensitive. For example, the command LS -L
does not execute the ls -l command.

@UNX Statement Guidelines
In addition, follow these guidelines when using this
statement:
You cannot use the UNX statement or the UNIX
command to access the operating system without
specifying a valid log-on and password in a remote
foreground session.
You may want to use the Refresh Screen (PNT)
statement following a UNX statement to re-establish
screen attributes when the system level command
returns from the Linux or UNIX environment.
Since your user .proﬁle ﬁle is executed when you use
the -p option, the PATH environment variable setting
within your .proﬁle ﬁle must include all directories being
searched so commands you specify can be executed.
For example, if you want to be able to specify shell
commands with UNIX Interface (UNIX/UNX), your PATH
variable in the .proﬁle ﬁle should be set as follows:
PATH=$PATH:/bin;export PATH
If PATH does not include a directory where the command
executable is found, the following error is returned by
the shell: -map: line 1: command: not found where
command is the speciﬁed command.
For trusted mode installation:
To prevent the run from terminating, be sure the UNX
statement contains a valid logon and password for all
users of the run. You may want to prompt the user for a

valid log-on and password before executing the UNX
statement.
If you use UNX statements in runs that are to be
executed from a remote site running on a remote host,
you must specify a valid Linux or UNIX logon and
password for the remote host. (Statements that can
start runs at remote sites include RRN, NRN, NRM, and
STR, using the BPRUN$ command.)
For secure mode installation:
An error is returned if logon and psw syntax are
speciﬁed, and the run terminates.
If the UNX statement is in a run started from foreground,
the requested system level activity runs with the
privilege of the session. If the UNX statement occurs in
background runs, the requested Linux or UNIX Operating
System access runs with privileges of the default user.
If you use UNX statements in runs that are to be
executed from a remote site, the Linux or UNIX
Operating System access runs on the remote host with
the identity of the default user.
If you are running from a terminal or terminal emulator,
the environment variables in your original shell aﬀect
the new shell started by the UNIX function or the UNX
statement. For example: if LD_LIBRARY_PATH is set to
/usr/ucblib in your original shell, a vi command fails in
the new shell started by the UNIX function or UNX
statement.

Sharing Native Files
You may be unable to read, overwrite, or remove a native
(Linux or UNIX) ﬁle that was created by another session
user. The ﬁle may have been created at the system level or
within the Business Information Server context using the
FILE or UNIX command or with the FIL or UNX statement. An
attempt to access the ﬁle for reading or writing can be done
at the system level or within the Business Information
Server context with the RET or UNIX command or with the
RET or UNX statement. Whether another user can access
the ﬁle in diﬀerent modes depends upon the active or
default permissions attached to the ﬁle.
All ﬁles created either at the system level or within the
Business Information Server context receive "rw-r--r"
permissions by default. These permissions allow the owner
to read and write to the ﬁle. Group members and others can
only read the ﬁle. This is because the default umask
provided by the Operating System is 022, which removes
"group" and "other" write permissions. The ﬁle owner can
actively change permissions by doing one of the following:
issuing the system "chmod" command which sets the
access permissions explicitly. This can be done either at
the system level or within the Business Information
Server context using the UNIX command or the UNX
statement. It can be done by all workstation and
terminal emulator users.
modifying the umask for the system session. This can be
done only by terminal emulator users, including the
"mapper" user in Trusted sites.

mapper login: add a umask command to the mlogin
script. This script resides in the M_ROOT/lbin directory.
other logins: add a umask command to the .proﬁle ﬁle,
found in the users home directory.
The argument to the umask is a three-digit octal number
that is bitwise subtracted from octal 666. For example:
umask 002
changes ﬁle creation mode to 664. Thereafter, all ﬁles are
created with "rw-rw-r--" permissions.
Files created by runs initiated by MAPQUE are owned by the
default user/group ("mapper"/"mapper" in Trusted
instances) and receive permissions "rw-rw-r--". (The default
user and group have read and write permissions, but others
have only read permission.) This also applies to scheduled
runs initiated by DISPATCHER. The software uses umask to
actively change the ﬁle creation mode when it starts
MAPQUE and DISPATCHER.

Accessing the Linux or UNIX Shell Examples
This example accesses the Linux or UNIX system shell.
When using the UNX statement, you must enter the
apostrophes, as shown to preserve the positions of options
and commands. In the second UNX example, the logon and
pw subﬁelds are speciﬁed by <runlogon> and <runpw>.

Note: This example does not apply to a workstation.
Control Line Entry
unix
Equivalent Statements
@unx '' '' .
@unx,,<runlogon>,<runpw> '' '' .
To return to the system, press Exit.

Executing a Command Examples
This example executes the cat command on the ﬁle
prod_status and places the output in the tmpﬁle ﬁle. In the
UNX example, the output is also placed in a cabinet 0,
drawer E result.
Control Line Entry
unix -f/tmpfile cat prod_status
Equivalent Statement
@unx,0,e -f/tmpfile 'cat prod_status' .

Listing the Contents of a Directory Examples
This example lists the contents of the /usr/work/newuser
directory. In the UNX example, the output is placed in a
cabinet 0, drawer E result.
Control Line Entry
unix -f ls -l /usr/work/newuser
Equivalent Statement
@unx,0,e -f 'ls -l /usr/work/newuser' .
Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS

UPD and @UPD (Update)
Use the Update (UPD) function or UPD statement to replace
the lines in a report with those displayed in the update
result.
The manual function updates the lines and redisplays the
original report.
The statement updates the lines and turns the update result
into a normal -0 result.
For the Update manual function, an update result from a
previous function must be displayed. For the UPD
statement, the update result is the -0 result.

Update Control Line Format
UPD [psw]
where psw is the password required to perform an update
operation on a write-protected report.

Update Statement Format
@UPD .

Update Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
To modify the lines displayed in the update result before
you execute the Update command, you can
Delete lines in the update result that you do not
want to use as replacement lines in the original
report.
Add lines to the update result. The added lines are
placed at the end of the original report.
Modify the data in the lines of the update result. The
changed lines replace those in the original report.
To remove the lines from the original report and discard
the update result, use the Delete command. For more
information, see DEL and @DEL (Delete).
To remove the lines from the original report and retain
the lines in the update result, use the Extract command.
For more information, see EXT and @EXT (Extract).

@USE (Use Variable Name)
Use the Use Variable Name (USE) statement to associate a
name with a numbered variable.

Use Variable Name Statement Format
@USE name=v[,name=v,...,name=v] .

Field

Description

name

Name to associate with the
variable.

v

Numbered variable to which
you are associating a name. To
deﬁne the variable, include
the type and size.

Use Variable Name Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
Normally, when you assign a name to a variable, you
can refer to it only by the name and not a variable
number. However, if you assign the variable with the
USE statement, you can refer to it by either its name or
number.
As a rule, do not use named and numbered variables
within the same run. There may be certain cases,
however, in which it is necessary. For example, you may
need to call an external subroutine containing
numbered variables from a run that uses named
variables.
You can assign several names with a single USE
statement and optionally initialize the variables at the
same time. If you want to initialize the variable while
naming it, include the variable type and size with the
variable number.

Use Variable Name Examples
This example assigns the name "station" to v100 and
deﬁnes v100 as an integer variable that is ﬁve characters
long: @use station=v100i5 .
This example assigns the name report to v101: @use
report=v101 .

VARIABLE
The Variable command demonstrates how a variable type
aﬀects its contents.
You are prompted to enter a value. The system then loads
that value into a series of variables of diﬀerent types and
displays the contents of those variables.
Use this format:
VARIABLE

VIEW (Create Temporary Format)
Use the Create Temporary Format (VIEW) function to change
the selection of report ﬁelds displayed. The Create
Temporary Format function enables you to place
nonadjacent ﬁelds side by side. For example, you can
display a ﬁeld in columns 20-30 next to a ﬁeld in columns
85-90. A format you create with the Create Temporary
Format function is similar to a permanent format, but it is
for a temporary display only.
To create a permanent format, ask your administrator.
The report in which to create the temporary format must be
on display.
If VIEW is unable to display all the report ﬁelds due to report
width and screen size, the undisplayed ﬁelds cannot be
included in the temporary format.

Note: A workstation must be used to view all the ﬁelds of a
wide report.

VIEW Control Line Format and Procedure
VIEW
Follow this procedure:
1. Type view on the control line and transmit. A mask
containing the report headings is displayed.
2. Type an x in each ﬁeld to include in the new format and
transmit.

@WAT (Wait)
Use the Wait (WAT) statement to suspend the execution of a
run, speciﬁcally in the following situations:
While you wait for a message to be received; note that
the message becomes the current result (-0).
To reduce the impact on the system when you have
extensive logic loops in a run.
To hold an interim display (displayed using DSM, DSP,
OUT, or SC) on the screen long enough to read it.

Wait Statement Format
@WAT[,M,lab] ms .

Field

Description

M

Option that suspends the run
until an outstanding message
is received or until the time
speciﬁed by the ms ﬁeld
elapses.

lab

Label to go to if an
outstanding message is
received. Note that the
message becomes the current
result (-0).

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

ms

Time in milliseconds to
suspend the run. Specify an
integer from 0 to 600000. The
maximum wait time is 600
seconds (600000
milliseconds). The wait time is
expressed in milliseconds.
However, it is performed in

granularity of seconds. For
example:
This statement waits for 1
second:
@WAT 1000 .

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

ms

Ü Ü 2200

Time in milliseconds to
suspend the run. Specify an
integer from 0 to 60,000. The
maximum wait time is 60
seconds (60,000 milliseconds).

Wait Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using the WAT statement:
The M option on the WAT statement enables the run to
stall until an outstanding message is received or until
the wait time is reached. If a message is received and a
label is speciﬁed, the run continues at the label. If a
message is not received within the wait time limit
speciﬁed, the run continues at the next statement.
Do not place any statements on the same line following
the WAT statement. The logic scan of the line terminates
after executing the WAT statement.

Wait Example
This example sends a message and suspends the run until it
receives a response, at which time the run continues at
label 099. If the run does not receive a response within 60
seconds (60,000 milliseconds), a message is displayed at
the top of the screen.
@ ldv,c <nomsg>s80='Message not received' . Load
msgs
@ ldv,c <error>s80='Problem with user-id or
dept.' .
@020:snu,0,b,2,<user>,<dept>,,y,199 . Send a
report
@ wat,m,099 60000 . Wait for user to receive
message
@ sc 'Q' tic,";msg,1,e,,"<nomsg>" . Display
message
@ wat 4000 . Suspend run
@ rel . Release the screen
@099: . Process the response
@ . (other processing based on response)
@ rel . Release the screen
@199:sc 'Q' tic,";msg,1,e,,"<error>" . Error
routine
@ wat 4000 . Suspend run
@ rel . Release the screen

WC and @WDC (Word Change)
Notes:
On a Linux or UNIX system, this command may not be
available because it is part of the Word Processing
feature and might not be installed at your site. If you
cannot successfully invoke this command, see your
administrator.
The Word Change (WC) function is only available on the
2200 system.
Use the Word Change (WC) function or WDC statement to
locate and change words from a list of target words and
replacement words (maximum of 200) in an issuing report
or from target and replacement words you enter on the
control line.
The command creates a result with the target words
changed to the replacement words.
For the Word Change manual function, when using a target
list in a report, the report containing the target list must be
on display; when entering the target and replacement words
with the Word Change call in the control line, the receiving
report must be on display.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If the replacement string must expand beyond the report
width, it wraps to the next line. If it wraps to more than one
line, you receive the following message: (MGMW14) Word
and Phrase Change can only expand one line Ü Ü Windows
Linux/UNIX OS

Word Change Control Line Formats
2200 Þ Þ
WC report
WC target,replace,[...,target,replace,]

Field

Description

report

Receiving report. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.
To specify a named report,
display the target list report
and enter wc *name. The
asterisk (*) indicates you are
requesting a named report.

target

Word to locate.

replace

Word or words to change the
target word to.
Separate the target and
replace words with a comma,
and end the sequence with a
comma. You can enter
multiple sets of target and
replace words.

Ü Ü 2200

Word Change Statement Format
@WDC,ic,id,ir,rc,rd,rr[,l,col,lab] .

Field

Description

ic,id,ir

Issuing report (the report that
contains the list of target
words). For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers
to Process.

rc,rd,rr

Receiving report (the report to
scan for target words).

l

Line number in the receiving
report at which to start
changing words.

col

Column number of the
receiving report at which to
start changing words.

lab

Label to go to if no changes
are made.

Word Change Compared to Locate and Change
The Locate and Change command locates target strings
wherever they appear, even if they are part of another
word. Then it changes the string to what you specify, and if
that causes the line to expand to more than the report or
screen width, all characters beyond the report or screen size
disappear from the report.
The Word Change command, however, locates the words as
you enter them and does not ﬁnd the string if it is part of
another word. If the replacement string causes the line to
expand to more than the report width, the extra characters
wrap to the next line. You can remove the extra spaces with
the ADJ command.

Word Change Issuing Report Format
Follow these guidelines when creating the target (issuing)
report:
Report must include a heading divider line.
Targets and replacement strings must be on tab lines.
Targets can contain only alphanumeric characters A
through Z and 0 through 9, as well as hyphens and
apostrophes. You can use more than one replacement
word and they can contain special characters.
Targets start in column 2, and are followed by a comma
and the replacement words, and then another comma.
Enter one target per line.

Word Change Examples
In this example list, whenever the word information is
located, it is replaced by the words information system;
whenever the word cat is located, it is replaced by dog.
. SAMPLE REPORT
*=========================
|information,information system, (| = tab
character)
|cat,dog,
... END REPORT ...
This example changes words in the receiving report, report
12H0, from the word change list (issuing report) in report
11H0: @wdc,0,h,11,0,h,12 .

@WIN (Deﬁne Window Display)
Use the Deﬁne Window Display (WIN) statement to deﬁne a
window and display it on the user screen. The statement
displays a window of a speciﬁed size at the speciﬁed
location.
This statement requires a workstation session that is using
one of the following clients:
Graphical Interface for Business Information Server
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows
Client

Note: If this session is using Graphical Interface for
Business Information Server or Business Information Server
for Microsoft Windows Client, the reserved WS$ (workstation
ﬂag) is equal to one.

Deﬁne Window Display Statement Format
@WIN[,vwh,vert,hort,vsiz,hsiz,fc/bc,o] caption
[vwh] .

Field

Description

vwh

Variable containing an existing
window handle. If you use this
ﬁeld, all vertical and
horizontal coordinates are
relative to the speciﬁed
window. Default = main
window.
If you specify a window
handle, you place a window
within a window unless you
specify the U option. The U
option implies that you are
changing an existing window
in some way. If you do not
specify a value in the vert,
hort, vsiz, hsiz, fc/bc, or
caption ﬁelds, the system
uses the original values.
Once you deﬁne a window,
you can only change the vert,
hort, vsiz, hsiz, fc/bc, and
caption ﬁelds.

If this subﬁeld contains WND$
(the main window handle), the
statement uses only the
caption ﬁeld. That caption
becomes the caption of the
main window for the duration
of the run. In this case, the
statement ignores vwh, vert,
hort, vsiz, hsiz, fc/bc, and o
speciﬁcations.

vert

Vertical position of the upper
left corner of the window
using a positioning unit (or
row number). When using a
row number, the position is
based on the main run font.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or row number).

Note: Negative vertical
position numbers are allowed
when using the P option
window.

hort

Horizontal position of the
upper left corner of the
window using a positioning
unit (or column number).
When using a column number,
the position is based on the

main run font. See @FON
(Font) for more information on
fonts.
Default = 1 (positioning unit
or row number).

Note: Negative horizontal
position numbers are allowed
when using the P option
window.

vsiz

Vertical size of the window
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or row numbers). When using
row numbers, the size is
based on the main run font.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts.
Default = For a child window
(C option), the window
extends to the bottom of the
parent window. For an
overlapping window (O
option), the window is the size
of the screen.

Note: For the above
mentioned default case (When
absolute size is not mentioned
in @WIN statement), the child
window or overlapped window
size will set to its parents
window size if the size of the

parent window is changed
after creating the window.

hsiz

Horizontal size of the window
speciﬁed in positioning units
(or column numbers). When
using column numbers, the
size is based on the main run
font. See @FON (Font) for
more information on fonts.
Default = For a child window
(C option), the window
extends to the right side of
the parent window. For an
overlapping window (O
option), the window is the size
of the screen.

Note: For the above
mentioned default case (When
absolute size is not mentioned
in @WIN statement), the child
window or overlapped window
size will set to its parents
window size if the size of the
parent window is changed
after creating the window.

fc/bc

Foreground and background
colors for this window. If you
specify only one color
(omitting the slant), the
statement uses that color for

both foreground and
background. See Set Default
Color Codes for the available
colors.

o

Type of window to display. See
Deﬁne Window Display
Options for available options.

caption

Caption for the window.
Enclose captions containing
embedded spaces in
apostrophes ('). If you do not
specify a caption, place
apostrophes in this ﬁeld.
Windows without captions
cannot be moved. The
statement automatically adds
a caption bar when you
specify an F, I, or X option.
Also, the statement
automatically adds a border if
you specify a caption with no
thick frame. The maximum
character length is 255
characters. If this limit is
exceeded, the caption is
truncated to 255 characters.

Note: If DWCAP$(13-1) is not
set, the maximum character
length is 50 characters.

vwh

Variable to capture the new
window handle. This must be
a six-character variable.

Deﬁne Window Display Options
Option

Description

B

Displays a border around the
window. Do not use with the F
option.

C

Creates a child window. This
window cannot move or exist
outside the main window. Do
not use with the O or P
options.
If you do not specify the C, O,
or P options, the child window
is the default.

F

Creates a thick frame around
the window, enabling the user
to expand or contract the
window. Do not use with the B
option.

H

Hides the window from view
until the run invokes a SHW
statement.

I

Enables the user to reduce the
window to icon size.

M

Creates a window with both
horizontal and vertical scroll
bars. The scroll bars are
displayed when some or part
of the controls are not visible
in the window area.

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(15-1) =
1 if available.

O

Creates an overlapped
window. This window exists
wholly outside of the main
window. Do not use with the C
or P options.

P

Creates an overlapped window
that is positioned relative to
the parent window. This
window exists wholly outside
of the main window. To specify
the main window, leave the
vwh ﬁeld blank. Do not use
with the C or O options.

Note: Check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(14-1) =
1 if available.

S

Send immediately. If you do
not use this option, the
system does not send the
window until the current block
is transferred; this results in
increased performance. If you
do not want the workstation to
wait for the block transfer,
however, you must use the S
option.

U

Updates an existing window.
You can change only the vert,
hort, vsiz, hsiz, fc/bc, and
caption ﬁelds. In this case, use
the same variable in both vwh
ﬁelds.

X

Enables the user to enlarge
the window to full size.

W

This window and all controls
speciﬁed within this window
deﬁne their position and size
in positioning units, which are
based on the Windows system
font. A vertical positioning unit
is approximately 800 times
the size of a row. A horizontal
positioning unit is

approximately 400 times the
size of a column.
Use a TrueType font with the
W option. When you choose
large fonts or small fonts
settings, the positioning units
and the size of TrueType fonts
change by the same ratio
(remaining proportional to
each other.) When you use
positioning units and TrueType
fonts, screen presentations
are consistent between PCs.
See @FON (Font) for more
information on fonts.

Notes:
Check DWCAP$ for
availability: DWCAP$(12-1)
= 1 if available.
For more information on
DWCAP$, see the
Developer’s Guide.
If the window is deﬁned
with the W option
(positioning units), the
size of the window
speciﬁes the whole
window size including the
title bar, border, and scroll
bars. Without the W option
(row, column), size

speciﬁes only the client
area of the control and
does not include the title
bar, border, and scroll
bars.

You can have an aggregate limit of 4000 BTN, CBX, EDT,

WL and @WDL (Word Locate)
Notes:
On a Linux or UNIX system, this command may not be
available because it is part of the Word Processing
feature and might not be installed at your site. If you
cannot successfully invoke this command, see your
administrator.
The Word Locate (WL) function is only available on the
2200 system.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
Strings of words on the same line can be located.
Target words may contain a space between them. A
space does not stop the scan.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
Use the Word Locate (WL) function and WDL statement to
locate words from a list of target words (maximum of 200) in
an issuing report or from targets you enter on the control
line.
The Word Locate manual function displays the receiving
report at the line where the ﬁnd was made and places the
cursor just ahead of the found word.
The statement loads variables with line numbers and
column numbers where the ﬁrst ﬁnd occurs.
To continue locating the target or targets, press F1 or type
rsm and transmit.

For the Word Locate manual function, when using a target
list in a report, the report containing the target list must be
on display; when entering the target words with the Word
Locate call in the control line, the receiving report must be
on display.

Line number where the word
was located.

vcol

Column number where the
words starts.

vcolnxt

Column number immediately
following the word.

Follow these guidelines when creating the target (issuing)
report:
Report must include a heading divider line.
Targets must be on tab lines.
Targets can contain only alphanumeric characters A
through Z and 0 through 9, as well as hyphens and
apostrophes.
Targets start in column 2, one word per line.

Word Locate Compared with Locate
The Locate command locates target strings wherever they
appear, even if they are part of another word. The Word
Locate command locates the words as you enter them and
does not ﬁnd the string when it is a part of another word.

Word Locate (WL) Example
2200 Þ Þ
In this example, whenever the word information or cat is
located, the receiving report is displayed at the line where
the ﬁnd was made and the cursor is placed just ahead of the
found word.
. SAMPLE REPORT
*=========================
|information (| indicate tab characters)
|cat
... END REPORT ...
Ü Ü 2200

Word Locate (WDL) Statement Example
This example locates words in the receiving report, report
12H0, from the word locate list (issuing report) in report
10H0, and captures the line number where the word was
located in <line>, the column number in <column>, and
the column number right after the word in <colnext>:
@wdl,0,h,10,0,h,12 <line>i5,<column>i3,<colnext>i3 .

WP and @WPR (Word Process)
Notes:
On a Linux or UNIX system, this command may not be
available because it is part of the Word Processing
feature and might not be installed at your site. If you
cannot successfully invoke this command, see your
administrator.
The Word Process (WP) function is only available on the
2200 system.
Use the Word Process (WP) function or WPR statement to
Execute word processing commands against reports.
Enter interactive word processing. To exit interactive
word processing, press the Exit WP key or type exit
and transmit.
2200 Þ Þ
The report must be full character set (FCS).
Ü Ü 2200
If you do not request a word processing command in the
control line format or statement format, you enter
interactive word processing. If you request a word
processing command, the system processes the report as
indicated by the command and creates a result.

Word Process Control Line Format
2200 Þ Þ
WP [report]
where report is the report to process. For more details, see
Specifying Reports or Drawers to Process.
Ü Ü 2200

Word Process Statement Format
@WPR,c,d,r[,l,lab] wpcmd .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to process. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

l

Line at which to start the
display (interactive).

lab

Label to go to in case of an
error.

wpcmd

Word processing command.
See Word Processing
Commands.

@WPR Reserved Words
If the run encounters an error, it goes to a label in the lab
subﬁeld and loads STAT1$ with the message number (use a
Load System Message [LSM] statement to obtain the text of
the message), and STAT2$ with the line number where the
error occurred in the report being processed.

Word Processing Control Characters
Control
Character

Description

~A and ~a
(Lettering)

Alphabetizes following
item or paragraph with
uppercase or lowercase
letters as speciﬁed (~A
produces uppercase
letters; ~a produces
lowercase letters).

~B and ~b
(Bolding)

Starts bolding at ~B;
ends bolding at ~b.

~C (Center)

Centers preceding text
within the margins,
then starts a new line.

~E (Expand to
Margins)

Expands preceding text
between left and right
margins, then starts a
new line.

~F (Freeze
Data)

Freezes the following
text (leaves it where it

is now displayed on the
line).

~G,n (Figure)

Next n lines contain
ﬁgure (ﬁgure receives
title after PRT and is
displayed in table of
contents).

with a downline loaded
UTS 40 terminal [F15]
key.)

or the number set in
the INDENT parameter.

Left-justiﬁes preceding
text, then starts new
line.

~M (Indented
Paragraph)

Indents following whole
paragraph at ﬁrst tab
set or tab speciﬁed in
the BULLET parameter.

~N (Numbering) Numbers following item
or paragraph.

~n (Document
Numbering)

Produces chapter and
paragraph numbers (up
to 5 levels):
~1 produces 1.
~2 produces 1.1.
~3 produces 1.1.1.
~4 produces 1.1.1.1.
~5 produces 1.1.1.1.1.

~O (Omit from
Print)

Omits (does not print)
text that exists
between two ~O
control characters
when PRINTOFF
parameter is set to Y
(yes, turn oﬀ printing
between ~Os).

Comment line or line

Line)

you insert to change or

(does not print).

Speciﬁes that next

command for an
auxiliary device or
printer (not counted as
text).

~R (Rightjustify)

Right-justiﬁes
preceding text, then
starts new line.

Next Tab Set)

Starts following text at
next available tab
setting.

in the left margin on

Marker)

this line. In adjusted

followed by a space
starting in column 1 of

The following text goes
to the next line. To

between text, use two
consecutive new line
control characters
(~~~~).

~. (Tab Rack)

A tab rack, which
indicates left and right
margins and tab
settings.

(Conditional

cannot ﬁt on the
current page.

Word Processing Commands
Some commands work only while in interactive word
processing. These are marked with an asterisk (*). Singleletter alphabetic commands can be entered in either upperor lowercase.

Note: Interactive word processing is not available for
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows Client,
Business Information Server for Microsoft Windows Server,
or Business Information Server for Linux and the UNIX
Operating System.

Command

Description

ADJ (Adjust)

Paginates and formats
the data according to
the parameters and
control characters in
the report and creates a
result. Execute ADJ
before doing a PRT or
DOC.

ADJDOC (Adjust Combination ADJ and
and Document) DOC command (use
only with documentstyle reports).

2200 Þ Þ

ADJREP*
(Adjust and
Replace)

Combination ADJ and
REP command.

Ü Ü 2200

ADJPRT (Adjust
and Print)

Combination ADJ and
PRT command.

2200 Þ Þ

BACKUP*

Displays a result showing
your report as it was at
the beginning of the
word processing session.
If you want to keep this
version in place of your
latest updated version,
enter rep. To return to
your updated copy, enter
return.

CHG*
(Change)

Changes the speciﬁed
target string to the
replacement string.
Format: CHG
;/target/replacement/

CUT* (Cut)

Enter cut control.

while you are in

the standard method of

the report number and
drawer).

Word Processing Control Parameters
Control parameters format text and modify or control the
use of Word Process commands. Enter control parameters
anywhere in the report heading between the date line and
the heading divider line. You can also use the ~P control
character to change some of these parameters.
Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client Þ Þ
Control parameters that are entered on the same line must
be separated by spaces.
Ü Ü Windows Server Linux/UNIX OS Windows Client
Format

parameter:value

Field

Description

parameter

Control parameter.

:

Tab character or colon
to enable the
parameter.

value

Value to be used for this
parameter.

Parameters

Parameter

Description

APPENDIX

Indicates that the next
chapter break (1) will be
the ﬁrst appendix. A 1
(ﬁrst level) section must
immediately follow the
~P line containing the
APPENDIX parameter.
Format: APPENDIX:
(must be in ~P line)

BULLET

Sets tab set n as the left
margin for indented
paragraphs.
Format: BULLET:n (use
with ~M)

CONTROL

Uses the character ~ or
_ to deﬁne control
characters. Default = ~.
Format: CONTROL:_
(must be in a heading
line)

DOCUMENT

Identiﬁes the report as a
document-style report.
Format:
DOCUMENT:x,y,z,a,b,c
(must be in a heading
line) x - Title pages, Y or
N. Default = Y.

y - Create an index, Y or
N. Default = Y.
z - First section number;
enables you to start
numbering at section 2
or greater Default = 1.
a - Insert page breaks on
all chapter breaks, Y or
N. Default = Y.
b - Level number
through which to force
title to uppercase.
c - Level number through
which to bold titles.

EDITMARK

Uses character x as an
editing mark.
Format: EDITMARK:x
(use with ~ [tildespace])

EZWP

Processes reports that
may not have any
control characters.
Default = N.
Format: EZWP:x (x = Y
or N)

FOOTER

Includes footers (deﬁned
in the report heading) on
the current page. Default
= Y.
Format: FOOTER:x (x = Y
or N)

FORMAL

Identiﬁes the report as a
book-style report (similar
to document).
Format:
FORMAL:x,y,z,a,b,c
(must be in a heading
line) x - Title pages, Y or
N. Default = Y.

y - Create an index, Y or
N. Default = Y.
z - First section number;
enables you to start
numbering at section 2
or greater Default = 1.

a - Insert page breaks on
all chapter breaks, Y or
N. Default = Y.
b - Level number
through which to force
title to upper c - Level
number through which
to bold titles.
~1 titles are centered;
~2 titles are leftjustiﬁed; the numbers of
the subsections (2-5) are
omitted. You can use the
TOC command to create
a table of contents.
Process FORMAL reports
with the DOC command.

GLOSSARY

Indicates the location of
your glossary report.
Format: GLOSSARY:rd (or
name) (must be in a
heading line)

HEADER

Includes headers
(deﬁned in the report
heading) on the current
page. Default = Y. There
is one default header
line that contains the
page number. This

header line starts on the
second page.
Format: HEADER:x (x = Y
or N)

INDENT

Speciﬁes number of
spaces (n) to indent
paragraphs when a ~I is
encountered in the text.
Format: INDENT:n or -n
to move text to left of
left margin

Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

LPP

Sets lines per page (n).
If greater than 60, print
device must be able to
handle over 60 lines per
page.
Format: LPP:n
Default = 60.

Ü Ü Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

LPP

Sets lines per page (n).
If greater than 60, print
device must be able to
handle over 60 lines per
page.
Format: LPP:n
Default = 60, or the
value set for your
system, specifying the
number of lines per
page on auxiliary
devices.

Ü Ü 2200

MARGINS

Speciﬁes where text is
placed within the report
margins (x = L, left; R,
right; E, even; F, freeze
as is; C, center). Control
characters ~L, ~R, ~E,
~F, and ~C override the
MARGINS parameter.
Defaults = left, as long
as you have a tab rack;
otherwise, text remains
frozen.
Format: MARGINS:x

NEWHEAD

Speciﬁes that either the
following lines are new
header lines (*/ in
columns 1 and 2) and
footer lines (*\) from this
point on or the headers
and footers should be
deleted (x = N) from this
point on. You can also
delete headers by
specifying Y but not
deﬁning any new lines
following the NEWHEAD
parameter.
Format: NEWHEAD:x (x
= Y or N) (must be in ~P
line)

NOCR

Speciﬁes standard
updating if x = Y (does
not insert carriage
returns [~~] in blank
lines). Default = N.
Format: NOCR:x (x = Y
or N) (must be in a
heading line)

OUTLINE

Either clears out the
current numbering and
lettering (a space after
the tab in OUTLINE
restarts the outline at 1

and A) or designates the
format of the outline.
Formats: OUTLINE:
(must be in ~P line) and
OUTLINE:NxAxnxax By
designating the format
of the OUTLINE, you
choose which level gets
numbers, uppercase
letters, a second level of
numbers, and lowercase
letters. The N
(uppercase) numbers
the ﬁrst level, A
(uppercase) starts the
uppercase lettering, n
(lowercase) numbers the
second level of
numbers, and a
(lowercase) starts the
lowercase lettering. To
use Roman numerals,
use an R (rather than N)
in the OUTLINE format.
Nx or Rx - Numbering for
level x (or R for Roman
numerals) Ax Uppercase lettering for
level x nx - Numbering
again for level x ax Lowercase lettering for
level x

PACK

Removes extra spaces
from between words.
Default = N. Leaves (or
places) two spaces after
periods, colons, question
marks, and exclamation
points as long as there is
at least one space
following them already.
Format: PACK:x (x = Y or
N)

PAGE

Deﬁnes the ﬁrst page (n)
of the document. Use
PAGE to start the ﬁrst
page at a number other
than 1.
Format: PAGE:n (must be
in a heading line)

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

PAGEBODY

Sets the number of
lines of text (main
body) on a page.
Format: PAGEBODY:n

n may not exceed 60
(or the value of the LPP
parameter), minus the

value of PAGETOP,
minus the number of
header and footer lines
in the report, or minus 1
if you are not using
headers and footers.
Default = 58.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

PAGEBODY

Sets the number of
lines of text (main
body) on a page.
Format: PAGEBODY:n

n may not exceed 60
(or the value of the LPP
parameter, or the value
set for your system),
less the value of
PAGETOP, less the
number of header and
footer lines in the
report, or less 1 if you
are not using headers
and footers. Default =
58.

Ü Ü 2200

PAGETOP

Inserts n blank lines
between the header
lines and the main body
of the report. Default =
0; one page reports are
centered vertically on
the page.
Format: PAGETOP:n

PRINTOFF

Speciﬁes whether or not
data surrounded by ~O
control characters in the
text should be printed.
Default = N, do not turn
printing oﬀ, print the
characters. Use ~O and
PRINTOFF when you are
working with preprinted
forms.
Format: PRINTOFF:x (x
= Y or N)

PRTSPACE

Sets line spacing: n = 1
(single space), n = 2
(double space), and so
on up to n = 4.
Format: PRTSPACE:n

2200 Þ Þ

RELEASE

Allows carets (^) in text
(not supported with an
IBM terminal).
Format: RELEASE:x (x =
Y or N)

SCREEN

Converts control
characters to emphasis
and FCC characters at
entry into interactive
word processing (no
need to press F15).
Format: SCREEN:x (x =
Y or N)

Ü Ü 2200

SECTION

Zeros out numbering
created by ~1 through
~5 and starts
numbering over.
Format: SECTION: (must
be in ~P line)

SPACE

Changes nonbreaking

space character to x
(character is converted
to a space after a PRT
command). Default
character is a grave
accent (` on keyboard).
Format: SPACE:x

SV

Sets the number of lines
(n) to remain on display
at the top of the screen
after each update while
in interactive word
processing.
Format: SV:n (must be
in a heading line)

2200 Þ Þ

UNADS

Inserts special
characters for UNADS
processing.
Format: UNADS:x,[H] (x
= Y or N)

Ü Ü 2200

WCLIST

Deﬁnes the target list
report for the Word
Change or Word Locate
functions. Use
interactive commands
WCL or WLL to execute
these functions while in
interactive word
processing.
Format: WCLIST:rd (or
name) (must be in a
heading line)

WORDWRAP

Speciﬁes whether or
not to wrap the words
from line to line. Default
= Y. If N, lines are
adjusted between
margins, but if there is
room at the end of a
line for the next line to
join it, it is not joined.
Format: WORDWRAP:x
(x = Y or N)

*/ (header lines) The full line of data
following this control
parameter is placed at
the top of each page,
thus becoming a page
header. You can have
up to three header

lines. The line can
contain page
numbering codes, date
codes, time codes, or
other information.

*\ (footer line)

The full line of data
following this control
parameter is placed at
the bottom of each
page, thus becoming a
footer line. You can
have up to two footer
lines.

Word Process Examples
This example adjusts report 22H0, prepares it for printing,
and produces a -0 result. If an error occurs, the run
continues at label 99: @wpr,0,h,22,,99 adjprt .
This example places the run user in interactive word
processing with report 22H0 on display: @wpr,0,h,22 ' ' .

@WRL (Write Line)
See SOE Updating and @WRL (Write Line) for more
information.

X and @XIT (Sign Oﬀ User Session)
Use the Sign Oﬀ user session (X) function or XIT statement
to sign oﬀ and return to the sign-on screen.
Using the XIT statement also terminates the run in which it
is executing before returning to the sign-on screen.

X (Sign Oﬀ User Session) Control Line Format
X

X (Sign Oﬀ User Session) Statement Format
@XIT .

X (Sign Oﬀ User Session) Procedures
To sign oﬀ your user session, using the X command
1. Move the cursor to the control line (or home position if
the active screen is displayed).
2. Type x and transmit to end your user session.
To sign oﬀ your user session, using the SgnOﬀ key
1. Press the Return function key until you reach a screen
with SgnOﬀ on the function key bar.
2. Press SgnOﬀ to end your user session.

@XIT (Sign Oﬀ User Session)
See X and @XIT (Sign Oﬀ User Session) for more
information.

@XQT (Execute)
Use the Execute (XQT) statement to execute one or more
statements, including data for output areas. You can
execute up to 1280 characters of data (that is, up to one
logic line of data).
After interpreting the XQT statement, the system executes
either the data at the speciﬁed label or line number or the
data speciﬁed in the vld ﬁeld. Once the XQT statement is
ﬁnished executing, the run continues execution at the line
following the XQT statement, unless the executed statement
transfers control to a new location (for example, through a
GTO, RUN, or XIT statement).
If the statement being executed fails, the run goes to the
label given on the executing statement. If the executing
statement does not contain a label, the run terminates in
error with the XQT statement, and a system message is
displayed.

Execute Statement Format
@XQT{,lab | vld} .

Field

Description

lab

Label, or line number relative
to the XQT statement, that
contains the statements you
want to execute. When
specifying a relative line
number, use the characters
"lin" before the line number
(for example, @xqt,lin
<lineno> ).
You can use a variable or
constant that contains the
label or line number.

vld

The data you want to execute.
You can specify the data using
variables, literals, constants,
reserved words, or any
combination of these. Be sure
the variables are packed if you
use them in combination with
other kinds of data.

Execute Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this statement:
You can use the lab subﬁeld to execute a line already
containing one or more statements, or you can
formulate the statement to execute within your run,
using variables, for example, containing data captured
from an input screen that you want to include in a
statement.
Do not place other statements on the same line after
the XQT statement. Other statements following it on the
same line are ignored. Put the next statement on a new
line.
If the data you want to execute is a statement, the ﬁrst
character of the data must be an at sign (@); otherwise,
it is treated as data for the output area and the run
continues at the next line in the run control report. (The
at sign [@] tells the interpreter that the information that
follows is a statement.)
You can include any characters in the data you want to
execute except the reverse slant (\). You do not need to
enclose commas (,) or slants (/) in apostrophes.
You can nest XQT statements. That is, one XQT
statement can execute another XQT statement, which,
in turn, can execute another XQT statement, and so on.

Executing from a Report Example
This example executes the statement in <srh2b>: @xqt
<srh2b> .

Executing from a Label Example
This example executes the statement at label 100:
@xqt,100 .

Reading and Executing Line in Another Report
Example
This example reads and executes a line from report number
2 in EDRW$: @rdl,edrw$,2,<line>,099 1-80 <data>s80 .
@xqt <data> .

Executing Line Computed from Menu Selection
Example
This example determines the line to execute from an input
screen: 1. @out,-1,2,10,1,1,,,,5 .
2. @chg v1i2 curv$ +3 if v1 ge 3 &; le 5 gto lin+1 ; \ gto 001
.
3. @xqt,lin v1 .
4. @dsp,-0 .
5. @rel .
6. @srh,-0,,,099 dh 'status date' ,861225 .
7. @srh,-0,,,099 dh 'ship date' ,870614 .
8. @sor,-0 '' 'product type' ,1 .
Explanation
1. Display the menu to the user.
2. Capture, in v1, the user's menu selection, based on the
vertical cursor position, and adjust it to the bias of the
line that will be executed to handle this menu selection.
3. Execute the relative line pointed to by CURV$+3.
4. Display the result of the user's selection.
5. Release the display if the user resumes the run.
6. Execute this line if the user presses Transmit from line
1 of the screen.
7. Execute this line if the user presses Transmit from line
2 of the screen.
8. Execute this line if the user presses Transmit from line
3 of the screen.

XR and @DUP (Duplicate Report)
Use the Duplicate Report (XR) function or DUP statement to
copy a report or result, creating a new report.
The command creates a copy of the existing report. If you
speciﬁed a drawer, the command places the report in the
drawer you speciﬁed; otherwise, it places the report in the
same drawer you are copying from.
The new report has the following characteristics:
Report number is the next available report number in
the drawer.
Current date is displayed in the date line.
Title of the original report is displayed in the new report,
unless you speciﬁed a new title.
If the report being duplicated contains non-displayable
data (binary, encoded, or runtime), the line length must
match the receiving drawer.

Duplicate Report Control Line Format
XR [report,drawer,title]

Field

Description

report

Report to duplicate. Default =
report on display. For more
details, see Specifying Reports
or Drawers to Process.

Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

drawer

Drawer and cabinet to receive
the new report. Default
drawer = drawer of the report
being duplicated. Default
cabinet = current cabinet.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

drawer

Drawer to receive the new
report. (Remember that the
receiving cabinet is always
your current cabinet.) Default
drawer = the same drawer

letter as the report being
duplicated.

Ü Ü 2200
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ

title

Title for the new report (up to
43 alphanumeric characters).
Default = title of the report
being duplicated.

Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ

title

Title for the new report (up to
43 alphanumeric characters).
Default = title of the report
being duplicated.
For information regarding titles
and save dates, see Duplicate
Report Guidelines.

Ü Ü 2200

Duplicate Report Statement Format
@DUP,c,d,r[,rc,rd,DFA <vtitle>]

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report to duplicate. For more
details, see Duplicate Report
Guidelines.

rc,rd

Receiving cabinet and drawer
in which to place the report.

DFA

Add new report to an existing
deferred update sequence.

vtitle

A variable containing the title
of the new report (up to 43
alphanumeric characters).

@DUP Reserved Words
RPT$ and STAT1$ contain the new report number.

Duplicate Report Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
When creating a title for your new report, use a unique
title so you can distinguish it from other reports in the
cabinet.
Windows Linux/UNIX OS Þ Þ
If you use the manual Duplicate Report function to
duplicate a currently displayed result that was created
by a function or run accessing a diﬀerent cabinet, the
new report is created in the other cabinet, unless you
specify a drawer and cabinet in the drawer ﬁeld.
Ü Ü Windows Linux/UNIX OS
2200 Þ Þ
You may duplicate a report into your currently active
cabinet only. Some functions and runs are designed to
display reports or results from other cabinets. If you use
the manual Duplicate Report function to copy these
reports or results, it places them into your current
cabinet. Enter a valid drawer (in your current cabinet) in
the drawer ﬁeld to specify where you want the new
report.
If your system is set up to retain save dates when you
duplicate reports, to avoid having the save date
overwritten by the title, either do not enter a title or
include a save date (@YYMMDD) at the beginning of the
title.
Ü Ü 2200

Duplicate Report Example
This example duplicates report 2B0 and displays the new
report.
Control Line Entry
XR 2b0,,This is the title
Equivalent Statements
@ldv <title>s40='This is the title' .
@dup,0,b,2 <title> .
@dsp,0,b,rpt$ .

Ü Ü Windows

Linux/UNIX OS

Yank Line and @LNY
Use the Yank Line function or LNY statement to copy lines
from a report into a temporary buﬀer. Compare this
command with the Append Line command:
Yank Line command overwrites the buﬀer.
Append Line command adds lines to the end of the
buﬀer.
Executing this command causes the following actions to
occur:
The command copies the speciﬁed lines into the buﬀer.
If the buﬀer does not exist, the command creates it.
If you yank more lines than the report contains, the
command yanks all lines following the request.
For the Yank Line manual function, the report that you are
copying from must be on display.

Yank Line In-Report-Line Format
>][n]Y[b]

Field

Description

>]

SOE character and line change
command.

n

Number of lines to be copied.
Default = 1.

Y

Yank Line call.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100. Default = unlabeled
buﬀer.

Yank Line Statement Format
@LNY,c,d,r,l[,q,b] .

Field

Description

c,d,r

Report from which lines are to
be copied. For more details,
see Specifying Reports or
Drawers to Process.

l

Line number of the ﬁrst line to
copy from the report.

q

Number of lines to copy.
Default = 1. If you specify
more lines than the report
contains, the statement copies
all lines from line l to the end
of the report.

b

Buﬀer label from 1 through
100. Default = unlabeled
buﬀer.

Yank Line Guidelines
Follow these guidelines when using this command:
If you specify a buﬀer label, remember it. You will need
it to copy the buﬀer lines with the Put Line command.
To move the lines from the buﬀer to a report, use the
Put Line command.
For information on buﬀers, see Temporary Buﬀers in
Append Line and @LNA.

Yank Line Procedure
To yank lines
1. Place the cursor at the beginning of the ﬁrst line to be
copied.
2. Press Edit.
3. Press LineCh. The Line Change menu is displayed.
4. Tab to the Yank lines ﬁeld.
5. Enter the number of lines to copy, if more than one.
Default = 1.
6. Transmit.
Or
1. Place the cursor at the beginning of the ﬁrst line to be
copied.
2. Erase to the end of the line.
3. Enter the Yank Line in-report-line format.

Yank Line Example
This example copies lines 12 through 19 (eight lines) from
report 1C0 into buﬀer 33. Position the cursor on line 12,
erase to the end of the line, and enter the following: >]8y33
Equivalent Statement
@lny,0,c,1,12,8,33 .

following table contains status values that can be returned

Namelist Overview
A Namelist contains the name of a deﬁnition report, and a
list containing any combination of reports and drawers,
including ranges of reports and drawers. It is a logical
collection of reports that you can process with many BIS
functions. These functions generally produces a single result
containing output of all the functions. The Namelist can
include individual reports, ranges of reports, entire drawers,
or cabinets.
You can deﬁne a Namelist using any of the following
methods:
Use the NAME run to deﬁne a Namelist in the System
Data Directory. For more information, see NAME.
Use the NAMELIST script to deﬁne a Namelist for manual
use. For more information, see NAMELIST.
Use the @NAMLST command to deﬁne a Namelist on an
ad hoc basis for local use. For more information, see
@NAMLST.
Use the @NAMDMP (Namelist Dump) statement to create a
result showing the contents of a pre-deﬁned Namelist in a
readable form. For more information, see @NAMDMP.
A Namelist consists of the following:
Deﬁnition Report
Body

Namelist Deﬁnition Report
The Namelist Deﬁnition Report gives the Namelist its
structure, which deﬁnes the headers and ﬁelds that apply to
all reports in the Body of the Namelist. Any function that
reads this Namelist creates its result in the Drawer of the
Deﬁnition Report using the headers from the Deﬁnition
Report.
Multiple Namelists can reference a single Deﬁnition Report.

Namelist Body
The body of the Namelist includes all the data lines in all the
reports that are part of the Namelist without internal
divisions or boundaries. Generally, all the reports should
have the same line width as the Deﬁnition Report.
